sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that regulatory uncertainty—not technical capability—is the primary barrier to clinical AI adoption, with nearly all stakeholders calling for clearer federal frameworks, particularly for non-medical device AI that falls outside traditional FDA oversight. The central tension lies between those advocating for minimal federal intervention with market-driven governance versus those demanding rigorous pre-deployment requirements comparable to medical device regulation. The dominant recommendation thrust favors a coordinated, risk-proportionate federal framework that clarifies agency jurisdictions, reduces state-level fragmentation, and establishes baseline governance expectations without prescribing specific technical implementations.

consensusPoints.text: Regulatory uncertainty is the primary barrier to AI adoption. Nearly all commenters identify regulatory ambiguity—not technical limitations—as the central obstacle preventing organizations from confidently adopting AI tools. Onboard AI notes that AI adoption in clinical care is constrained less by technical capability and more by uncertainty about what constitutes reasonable governance, evaluation, and oversight. Health AI Institute observes that legal and compliance teams frequently delay or block deployment not due to known safety risks, but due to uncertainty around accountability, liability, and post-deployment expectations. FERZ AI emphasizes that the fundamental barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not AI capability—it is the inability to prove that an AI system's governance was functioning correctly at the time of any specific decision.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 50 of 56 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: AFSCME argues the RFI never pauses to consider whether evidence supports widespread AI adoption as a net benefit, suggesting uncertainty may be appropriate given immature evidence.

consensusPoints.text: Non-medical device AI represents a critical governance gap. Commenters specifically identify AI tools outside FDA's traditional medical device framework as the most urgent regulatory gap requiring federal attention. Health AI Institute notes a growing share of clinical AI falls outside traditional device definitions—documentation support, care coordination, operational decision support, generative AI embedded in workflows. HIMSS EHR Association calls for a formal federal framework specifically tailored to non-device clinical AI. Ellipsis Health observes that ambiguity about governance structure for non-medical device AI leads to risk aversion, prolonged procurement cycles, and inconsistent decision-making across organizations.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 40 of 56)

consensusPoints.text: State-level regulatory fragmentation creates untenable compliance burdens. Commenters addressing multi-jurisdictional operations describe the emerging patchwork of state AI laws as a significant barrier that federal action should address. Cooperative Exchange identifies regulatory fragmentation as the primary barrier to AI innovation for national clearinghouses—hundreds of state-level AI statutes create inconsistent definitions, disclosure obligations, and governance expectations. SonderMind notes that more than a dozen states introduced legislation regulating psychotherapy AI risks this year alone, with some states' regulatory frameworks making it untenable to offer AI-powered client support agents deployed across nearly every other state. InStride Health observes that requirements governing consent, data use, and clinical accountability vary significantly across states, particularly in mental health and pediatric care.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing multi-jurisdictional operations (approximately 25 of 30 relevant comments)

consensusPoints.text: Cross-agency coordination is essential. Commenters call for improved coordination among HHS agencies (FDA, ONC, CMS, OCR) to reduce conflicting or duplicative requirements. Massive Bio proposes establishing a Healthcare AI Governance Council bringing together FDA, ONC, CMS, OCR, OIG, and external experts to continuously monitor emerging issues and update policies. EHY Consulting emphasizes that HHS-wide coordination across CMS, ASTP/ONC, OCR, CIO/ONS, OGA, and acquisition functions is essential. AAFP recommends that HHS work across agencies and throughout federal government to harmonize existing guidance into a single federal framework.

Risk-proportionate oversight is preferred over one-size-fits-all approaches. Commenters explicitly support tiered or risk-based regulatory frameworks that scale requirements to the risk profile of AI applications. AMA states that regulation of health care AI should follow a risk-based approach—low-risk AI doesn't need stringent pre-market scrutiny while higher-risk AI needs appropriate regulatory scrutiny with guardrails. ACS CAN calls for a clear regulatory framework that differentiates oversight based on risk level. SANCIAN LLC proposes creating a tiered, risk-based AI governance pathway aligned with NIST AI RMF risk tiers.

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Level of Federal Regulatory Intervention
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on how much federal regulatory intervention is appropriate for clinical AI, ranging from minimal market-driven approaches to comprehensive pre-deployment requirements.

positions.label: Minimal Intervention
positions.stance: Allow market-driven governance with federal guidance only. HealthScoreAI, an AI health data company, argues that prescriptive requirements risk creating a ceiling on innovation rather than enabling its full potential. ScriptChain Health, a healthcare AI startup, contends that AI in healthcare should be very close to unregulated by the federal government. Patient advocate Steven Zecola suggests FDA's role should shift from being an overzealous gatekeeper at every step to being an auditor.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily AI vendors and some healthcare startups
keyArguments: Prescriptive requirements risk creating a ceiling on innovation rather than enabling its full potential
Experience with Meaningful Use shows narrow mandates produce compliance without meaningful innovation
Government should set a level playing field and let the market decide rather than selecting technologies

positions.label: Robust Federal Framework
positions.stance: Establish comprehensive pre-deployment requirements. An anonymous psychologist argues that the absence of a federal regulatory framework is not a neutral condition—it is an active risk to patients. Psychotherapy Action Network, representing 6,500+ mental health clinicians, states that federal regulation of AI in healthcare is essential and cannot be left to companies producing/selling products or to individual states. AFSCME, representing 1.4 million public sector workers, contends that the burden of proof should be on AI developers to demonstrate products will do no harm beyond a reasonable level of doubt. CommonSpirit Health, a major health system, argues that every new AI tool in clinical settings should receive scrutiny similar to a medical device undergoing clinical trial.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-18 commenters, primarily patient advocates, labor unions, and some professional societies
keyArguments: The absence of a federal regulatory framework is not a neutral condition—it is an active risk to patients
AI systems should be held to comparable or higher standards than medical devices and licensed clinicians
Burden of proof should be on developers to demonstrate safety before deployment

positions.label: Balanced Middle Ground
positions.stance: Clear baseline expectations without prescriptive mandates. Onboard AI, an AI governance company, proposes a minimal governance baseline with four demonstrable elements, noting the goal is not to define best-in-class practices, but to articulate the least the industry needs to demonstrate responsible AI use. ECRI, an independent nonprofit, states that HHS doesn't need to micromanage innovation but should communicate expectations, support adoption with practical guardrails, and enforce accountability. Advocate Health, a major health system, notes that clear definitions, risk-based frameworks, and coordinated interagency guidance would significantly improve the regulatory environment. The Markkula Center observes that existing statutory authorities are sufficient; the gap is standardization, usability, and the ability of real organizations to operationalize what is already required.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 30-35 commenters, the majority position including most health systems, professional societies, and established AI companies
keyArguments: Goal is not to define best-in-class practices, but to articulate the least the industry needs to demonstrate responsible AI use
HHS should communicate expectations, support adoption with practical guardrails, and enforce accountability without micromanaging
Existing statutory authorities are sufficient; the gap is standardization, usability, and the ability of real organizations to operationalize what is already required

areasOfDebate.topic: Federal Preemption of State AI Laws
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether federal standards should preempt state AI laws or whether states should retain flexibility to implement additional protections.

positions.label: Strong Federal Preemption
positions.stance: Replace state patchwork with uniform federal standards. WellSpan Health calls for federal leadership to replace the patchwork of state AI laws with a preemptive federal standard. ACLA identifies preempting the patchwork of state and local laws regulating healthcare AI as a key priority. ATA Action notes that the current 50-state patchwork of laws, enforcement actions, and private rights of action creates confusion and stifles innovation. Providence argues that hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20-25 commenters, primarily multi-state health systems, national clearinghouses, and AI developers
keyArguments: Hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules
State fragmentation shapes who can deploy AI responsibly at national scale by favoring large, well-resourced organizations
Inconsistent state requirements create uncertainty for developers, increase administrative complexity for clinicians, and delay safe and equitable deployment

positions.label: Preserve State Flexibility
positions.stance: Federal floor with state discretion for additional protections. ACC calls for alignment and interoperability of frameworks without constraining legitimate state flexibility. Providence notes that regulations should outline areas where providers maintain discretion to reject AI tools or apply more stringent vetting standards.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily patient advocates and some professional societies
keyArguments: States may have legitimate reasons for stricter standards based on local populations and needs
Federal preemption could eliminate stronger patient protections in some states
Alignment and interoperability of frameworks without constraining legitimate state flexibility is preferred

areasOfDebate.topic: Classification of Clinical Decision Support vs. Medical Devices
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether current CDS exemptions should be maintained or expanded versus whether more AI tools should require FDA oversight as medical devices.

positions.label: Maintain/Expand CDS Exemptions
positions.stance: Preserve 21st Century Cures Act exclusions for clinician-supervised tools. NeuroFlow urges reaffirming the clinical decision support exemption under the 21st Century Cures Act, arguing that failure to distinguish clinician-supervised CDS from autonomous AI is a primary structural barrier to innovation. SonderMind contends that oversight should be proportionate to risk profile—well-designed clinical AI shouldn't face the same regulatory burden as unvalidated consumer products. HIMSS EHR Association notes that FDA's January 2026 CDS guidance is a helpful step but needs revisiting as AI technologies mature.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-18 commenters, primarily EHR vendors, CDS developers, and some health systems
keyArguments: Section 520(o)(1)(E) appropriately excludes CDS when clinicians can independently review recommendations
Failure to distinguish clinician-supervised CDS from autonomous AI is a primary structural barrier to innovation
Conflating supervised CDS with autonomous systems causes health systems to apply heightened scrutiny designed for autonomous systems to clinician-supervised CDS

positions.label: Expand Device Classification
positions.stance: More AI tools should require FDA oversight. InStride Health notes that many AI tools influence clinical judgment without being regulated as medical devices, creating structural accountability gaps. An anonymous psychologist argues that AI systems operating in clinical settings should be held to comparable or higher standards than medical devices. Psychotherapy Action Network suggests the bar should perhaps be set higher for AI given it is unproven technology. AFSCME questions what assurances patients and health care workers have that tools meet their claimed competencies.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters, primarily patient advocates, labor unions, and some clinicians
keyArguments: Many AI tools influence clinical judgment without being regulated as medical devices, creating structural accountability gaps
AI systems operating in clinical settings should be held to comparable or higher standards than medical devices
Current exemptions may allow harmful tools to reach patients without adequate review

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether private accreditation should play a significant role in AI governance or whether federal standards should be established first.

positions.label: Leverage Private Accreditation
positions.stance: Use existing accreditation infrastructure for AI governance. URAC argues that accreditation is particularly well-situated as an evolving set of requirements and obligations that can adapt to changes in technology, and recommends HHS implement AI accreditation requirements in future rulemaking. ACR suggests HHS could explore future practice accreditation akin to mammography and advanced diagnostic imaging provider accreditation programs. MHA endorses a neutral, standards-based certification and credentialing framework.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters, primarily accreditation organizations and some health systems
keyArguments: Accreditation is particularly well-situated as an evolving set of requirements and obligations that can adapt to changes in technology
Private accreditation has historically supplemented federal regulation effectively
Can provide faster, more flexible oversight than statutory/regulatory approaches

positions.label: Federal Standards First
positions.stance: Government should set requirements before delegating to private bodies. Psychotherapy Action Network argues that government has already decided regulation and oversight are essential in healthcare and should not accept anything less with AI.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily patient advocates and labor representatives
keyArguments: Private accreditation may prioritize industry interests over patient safety
Federal government has responsibility to establish baseline protections
Accreditation should supplement, not replace, federal oversight

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty creating liability exposure and procurement delays; disproportionate burden on health systems to evaluate and govern AI without clear federal standards; state-level fragmentation complicating multi-state operations. Health systems emphasize they are being asked to assume responsibility for AI governance without consistent national standards, predictable payment pathways, or clear liability allocation, as noted by Hackensack Meridian Health.
specificPoints: Federal baseline governance expectations that reduce duplicative institutional review
Provisional clearances with stepwise pathways to full accreditation
Clear distinction between AI that informs care versus AI that autonomously diagnoses/treats
Technical expertise panels including health systems in standard development
CommonSpirit Health argues the concepts involved in clinical care AI are too specific, too important, and too complex to be regulated outside of a technical expertise panel
Providence notes the federal government is best positioned to dictate AI use standards as a multi-state health system operating across seven states
WellSpan Health emphasizes the federal government must prioritize strong incentives rather than punitive regulatory or enforcement postures

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unclear regulatory pathways delaying market entry and diverting capital from product development; classification ambiguity between wellness/administrative tools and regulated medical devices; inability to demonstrate compliance when governance requirements are undefined. Developers emphasize the chilling effect of uncertainty on investment decisions, noting that organizations cannot invest when regulatory requirements are unclear, as stated by FERZ AI, and that uncertainty shapes who can deploy AI responsibly at national scale by favoring well-resourced incumbents over innovative startups, as observed by Cooperative Exchange.
specificPoints: Clear guidance distinguishing regulated devices from exempt CDS
Regulatory sandboxes or pilot programs for novel AI solutions
Safe harbor provisions for AI systems meeting specified governance criteria
Tiered frameworks differentiating administrative AI, clinical decision support, and autonomous systems
Massive Bio proposes regulatory sandboxes or pilot programs where novel AI solutions can be tested with temporary regulatory flexibility
COGA Labs recommends establishing an AI Health Sandbox modeled on UK (MHRA AI Airlock) and Singapore programs

Kooth observes that products with clinical evidence of health improvement face significantly higher regulatory burden, skewing the market toward adopting tools optimized for increasing user engagement rather than verifiable health outcomes

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clinician liability when using AI tools without clear accountability frameworks; lack of standardized governance creating institution-by-institution patchwork; need for post-deployment monitoring as AI performance changes over time. Medical societies emphasize that their members are expected to integrate AI into practice while facing unresolved liability questions, regulatory ambiguity, and potential malpractice insurance impacts, as noted by AMA. They highlight that clinicians in small, independent, rural, and safety-net settings lack resources to evaluate AI tools independently.
specificPoints: Clear definitions of AI tool classes and appropriate clinical use contexts
Continuous improvement lifecycle models rather than one-time approvals
Harmonized national governance approach
Mandatory labeling requirements for transparency
AMA states that a clear, consistent, risk-based approach to regulation (not significant deregulation) is required to enhance trust in AI and move towards clinical integration
ASH notes that individual health systems developing their own internal best practices creates a patchwork of varying standards and uncertainty about how AI should be evaluated and adopted
AAFP warns that deregulation disproportionately affects low-resourced practices and places heavier burden on rural and safety-net health care organizations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety and protection from AI-related harms; transparency about when AI is used in their care; ensuring AI doesn't exacerbate existing healthcare inequities. Advocates emphasize that patients and providers have low trust that AI will be used responsibly, as noted by ACS CAN, and that this trust deficit must be addressed before AI's transformative potential can be realized. They uniquely focus on the patient experience of navigating a mosaic of laws that obscures patient awareness of their rights.
specificPoints: Evidence of efficacy and patient safety prior to deployment
Meaningful disclosure to patients about AI tool use
Effective adverse event reporting and post-approval monitoring
Clear accountability standards when AI causes patient harm

ACS CAN calls for requiring evidence of efficacy and patient safety prior to deployment and promulgating regulations providing clear standards for accountability when AI applications result in patient harm
Patient advocate Steven Zecola argues to let the scientists study science, not the FDA requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Mismatch between static regulatory models and dynamic AI systems; need for post-deployment surveillance as population-level intervention; governance architecture gaps that make compliance instruments meaningless. Researchers emphasize that clinical AI is largely regulated as a discrete product but in practice, it functions as a population-level intervention, as noted by Dr. Bhagavathula. They bring systematic analysis of governance failures, noting that GAO audits found agencies achieved behavioral compliance while governance architecture did not function, as observed by Interstitial Institute.
specificPoints: Post-Deployment Algorithmic Surveillance (PDAS) as cross-agency framework
Shift from product-based to population-based evaluation models
Clinical AI Accountability Architecture standards requiring named responsible parties, measurement specifications, and consequence mechanisms
Federal reference implementations for complex benefit programs
Dr. Bhagavathula argues this is a regulatory science failure, not a technological one

Interstitial Institute states the most consequential barrier to AI adoption is not regulatory burden, reimbursement lag, or technical immaturity—it is the absence of governance infrastructure that would make adoption safe to accelerate
Markkula Center observes the United States does not lack innovation capacity; it lacks predictable, repeatable pathways to deploy innovation responsibly

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Worker safety and job security implications of AI adoption; lack of evidence that AI adoption provides net benefit; need for worker voice in AI governance decisions. Labor representatives uniquely challenge the premise of accelerated adoption, noting that AI firms operate with constant iteration, addressing issues only after real-world problems emerge, while AFSCME members do not get to operate with such a carefree attitude.
specificPoints: Pause adoption acceleration until expert panels assess complexities
Place burden of proof on developers to demonstrate safety
Engage workers in governance discussions
AFSCME argues that at this point in generative AI development, we need clearer understanding of who is overseeing AI incorporation into new health care use cases

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: CLIA regulations predate AI and don't expressly address algorithmic services; uncertainty about whether algorithmic services require FDA clearance for Medicare coverage; need to clarify that digital laboratory information analysis qualifies as laboratory services. Laboratory organizations emphasize that CLIA-certified laboratories already meet high regulatory standards and that CLIA oversight combined with rigorous technical assessment provides sufficient regulatory framework for single-site laboratory algorithmic services, as noted by Cellworks. They cite the ACLA v. FDA victory affirming LDTs as professional services.
specificPoints: Amend CLIA definition of laboratory to include facilities examining digital laboratory information
Clarify that algorithmic examinations are included among laboratory functions
Reinstate Clinical Laboratory Improvement Advisory Committee (CLIAC)
CMS guidance that CLIA plus technical assessment suffices for Medicare coverage eligibility
CCLA states federal regulatory clarity is essential to enable laboratories to adopt AI safely and at scale
ACLA argues reinforcing CLIA as the regulatory framework for clinical laboratory services is essential for advancing AI innovation
Cellworks notes algorithmic services offered lawfully by CLIA-certified laboratories currently lack clear pathways to obtain Medicare coverage

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance architecture vs. behavioral compliance: The Interstitial Institute draws on GAO audit data to distinguish between behavioral compliance (policies, attestations) and governance architecture (structural conditions that make compliance meaningful). They note that all 23 civilian agencies achieved behavioral compliance with AI requirements, yet 15 of 20 AI inventories contained incomplete or inaccurate data. This suggests current approaches may produce compliance theater rather than actual accountability.

Regulatory arbitrage creating perverse incentives: Kooth identifies a counterintuitive market dynamic where developers are incentivized to deploy general-purpose AI with minimal oversight, avoiding more responsible, context-aware designs that trigger medical device regulation. This suggests current classification approaches may inadvertently favor less safe products.

Population-level intervention framing: Dr. Bhagavathula reframes clinical AI as a population-level intervention rather than a discrete product, arguing this requires surveillance approaches similar to pharmacovigilance and public health monitoring rather than one-time approval. This epidemiological lens offers a different regulatory paradigm than device-focused approaches.

Measurement-first vs. decision-making AI distinction: Keith Mountjoy proposes distinguishing between AI designed as decision-makers versus assistive measurement and observability layers, arguing the latter could reduce regulatory friction while providing clinical value. This suggests a potential new classification category.

Defense AI governance parallels: Dr. Eli Talbert, a Senior Data Scientist at U.S. Special Operations Command, applies defense AI governance principles to clinical AI, proposing minimum documentation and logging standards for human-AI interaction. His framework emphasizes authority boundaries between model and clinician and escalation pathways—concepts developed for high-stakes military decision-support that may translate to clinical contexts.

State legislative momentum: Satyadhar Joshi's systematic review of 793 state bills reveals the scale of emerging state-level activity, with 143 bills potentially impacting mental health AI and 20 already enacted across 11 states. This quantifies the fragmentation concern and suggests a narrow window for federal action before state patchwork becomes entrenched.

CLIA as alternative regulatory pathway: Cellworks and laboratory associations argue that CLIA-certified laboratories already meet high regulatory standards and should not require FDA clearance for algorithmic services. They cite Oncotype DX as a successful model: a widely adopted algorithmic diagnostic for breast cancer that now informs approximately 40% of U.S. breast cancer treatment decisions and is Medicare/Medicaid reimbursed nationwide without FDA device clearance.

24-36 month critical window: SANCIAN LLC warns of a narrow 24–36 month window before AI becomes irreversible infrastructure without proper governance embedded, suggesting urgency for federal action before governance gaps become structural features of the healthcare system.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Multi-state health systems including Providence, WellSpan, Advocate Health, and SonderMind consistently emphasize state fragmentation as a primary barrier, while single-state organizations focus more on federal agency coordination.

California-based organizations including CCLA, Kooth, and Markkula Center reference state-specific regulatory experiences, including California's Soluna youth mental health program. States mentioned as having particularly impactful or restrictive AI laws include Illinois, California, and multiple states with mental health chatbot legislation.

Large integrated health systems emphasize need for federal preemption and standardized governance to reduce duplicative institutional review, while small and rural practices represented by AAFP emphasize that deregulation shifts burden to least-resourced organizations.

AI developers consistently favor clearer classification guidance and safe harbors; less concerned about stringent requirements than about uncertainty. Health systems consistently favor federal baseline standards that reduce their governance burden; willing to accept requirements if clearly defined.

Professional societies consistently favor risk-based approaches with post-deployment monitoring and emphasize clinician liability concerns. Patient advocates consistently favor evidence requirements before deployment and are less concerned about innovation speed than safety.
Labor representatives uniquely question whether accelerated adoption is warranted given evidence gaps, as demonstrated by AFSCME's challenge to the premise of the RFI.
Behavioral health AI faces unique challenges due to state-level mental health chatbot legislation and telehealth prevalence, as noted by SonderMind and InStride Health.
Laboratory/diagnostic AI faces unique challenges around CLIA applicability and Medicare coverage pathways, as emphasized by CCLA, ACLA, and Cellworks.
Regulatory asymmetry identified as unintended consequence: Stricter oversight of clinically-validated tools may favor less-governed wellness products, as noted by Kooth.
Incumbent advantage identified as unintended consequence: Uncertainty favors well-resourced organizations that can absorb compliance costs, as noted by Cooperative Exchange and Health AI Institute.
Compliance theater identified as unintended consequence: Behavioral compliance requirements may produce documentation without meaningful accountability, as analyzed by Interstitial Institute.

keyQuotations.quote: AI adoption in clinical care is constrained less by technical capability and more by uncertainty about what constitutes reasonable governance, evaluation, and oversight.

The fundamental barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not AI capability—it is the inability to prove that an AI system's governance was functioning correctly at the time of any specific decision. Without such proof, liability cannot be allocated, regulatory compliance cannot be demonstrated, and patient trust cannot be justified. This is a governance problem, not an AI problem.
Legal and compliance teams frequently delay or block deployment not due to known safety risks, but due to uncertainty around accountability, liability, and post-deployment expectations.
Regulatory ambiguity hampers innovation, promotes distrust in systems, and results in the development of fewer applications of this promising technology.

The advent and proliferation of new healthcare AI tools is often occurring outside the scope of existing laws and regulations, creating situations where patients and providers lack the transparency and knowledge of AI tools used in their care.

AI is no longer experimental in health care and is already embedded across care delivery. Federal policy must therefore evolve from pilot-focused experimentation toward scalable, predictable, and trustworthy deployment.
The central gap is not absence of authority. It is standardization, usability, and the ability of real organizations to operationalize what is already required.
These barriers do not merely slow AI adoption—they shape who can deploy AI responsibly at national scale.
The result is that those members are denied access to a beneficial, clinically-guardrailed tool, not because of a safety concern, but because of regulatory ambiguity.

The most consequential barrier to AI adoption in clinical care is not regulatory burden, reimbursement lag, or technical immaturity. It is the absence of governance infrastructure that would make adoption safe to accelerate.

Policy says review happens. Architecture makes review impossible to bypass. Where the two diverge—and the evidence shows they have diverged systematically, not occasionally—clinical AI tools fall short not because the model failed but because the governance structure surrounding the model was not designed to hold under operational pressure.
HHS should treat AI not as technology to be adopted, but as care infrastructure to be governed.
The goal is not to define best-in-class practices, but to articulate the least the industry needs to demonstrate responsible AI use.

America gains the most when innovation is tested, proven, and trusted, not when shortcomings are discovered post-deployment. These measures do not restrict innovation—they differentiate serious innovators from opportunistic ones.
Efficacy without safety is insufficient.
At present, clinical AI is largely regulated as a discrete product. In practice, it functions as a population-level intervention. This is a regulatory science failure, not a technological one.
AI must function as a clinical support tool—not a black box that introduces new risks or uncompensated workload.

Products with clinical evidence of health improvement face significantly higher regulatory burden... By contrast, general purpose or wellness AI products face less regulatory scrutiny, thereby skewing the market toward adopting tools optimized for increasing user engagement rather than verifiable health outcomes or population health impact.

Experience with Meaningful Use demonstrates that when government mandates narrowly define requirements, vendors tend to meet the letter of the mandate without meaningful innovation. Imposing prescriptive requirements on AI risks creating a ceiling on innovation rather than enabling its full potential.
The absence of a federal regulatory framework is not a neutral condition—it is an active risk to patients.

Patients and providers have low trust that AI will be used responsibly and that health care systems will protect patients from AI-related harms—and this trust is essential for the transformative potential of AI advances to be realized.
AI firms operate with constant iteration, addressing issues only after real-world problems emerge. AFSCME members do not get to operate with such a carefree attitude.
The United States does not lack innovation capacity. It lacks predictable, repeatable pathways to deploy innovation responsibly.
The next wave of healthcare AI will be defined not by better models, but by responsible operationalization at scale.
AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data.

Health systems are being asked to assume disproportionate responsibility for evaluating, governing, and managing AI tools without consistent national standards, predictable payment pathways, or clear liability allocation.
Deregulation disproportionately affects low-resourced practices and places heavier burden on rural and safety-net health care organizations lacking capacity for thorough product assessments.
HHS can responsibly transform AI from a decision-support tool into a scalable, accountable virtual workforce that expands access, strengthens prevention, and sustainably reduces total cost of care.
The future of healthcare data will define the future of healthcare AI. Rather than adding more disparate systems, we need better data delivered faster, with built-in intelligence and trust.

Let the scientists study science, not the FDA requirements. By being forced to have one eye on the finish line, scientists tend to craft their work to satisfy the FDA, rather than the rudiments of a flawed biological process.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of regulatory frameworks, with most stakeholders providing detailed analysis of specific statutory authorities, agency jurisdictions, and practical implementation challenges. Commenters frequently cite specific regulations, prior rulemakings, and empirical evidence to support their positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many comments cite specific examples, GAO audits, state legislative reviews, and real-world implementation experiences. However, quantitative evidence on AI safety outcomes remains limited, with several commenters noting the absence of systematic data on AI-related harms or benefits in clinical settings.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct patient voice in comments; most patient perspectives are mediated through advocacy organizations. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are primarily represented through professional societies rather than direct submissions. International perspectives and comparative regulatory analysis are largely absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High complexity due to overlapping federal agency jurisdictions, evolving state legislative landscape, and fundamental questions about how to classify and regulate AI systems that don't fit traditional regulatory categories. The debate involves technical, legal, ethical, and practical implementation dimensions that resist simple solutions.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate near-universal agreement that liability uncertainty represents a primary barrier to clinical AI adoption, with legal and compliance teams frequently blocking deployments not due to safety concerns but due to unresolved questions about who bears responsibility when AI influences care decisions. The central tension lies between those advocating for shared responsibility frameworks that distribute liability based on control and capability versus those emphasizing that clinicians must retain ultimate accountability while being protected from uninsurable risks they cannot control. Commenters overwhelmingly call for HHS to provide clarity through model contract language, safe harbor provisions, and governance frameworks—viewing federal guidance as essential to breaking the current wait and see paralysis affecting AI adoption across the healthcare ecosystem.

consensusPoints.text: Liability uncertainty is the primary barrier to AI adoption. Nearly all commenters identify liability ambiguity as a critical obstacle preventing beneficial AI deployment. This consensus spans healthcare providers, AI developers, professional associations, and patient advocates. Health AI Institute notes that legal and compliance teams frequently delay or block deployment not due to known safety risks, but due to uncertainty around accountability, liability, and post-deployment expectations. InStride Health observes that organizations prioritizing patient safety must limit experimentation and narrowly scope AI use cases regardless of technical readiness due to liability uncertainty. AAFP states that lack of clearly defined liability model for clinical AI remains significant barrier to adoption.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 55 of 60+ substantive comments)

exceptions.text: While all agree uncertainty is problematic, commenters differ on whether the solution requires new legislation, agency guidance, or industry-developed standards.

consensusPoints.text: Current malpractice frameworks are inadequate for AI-assisted care. Commenters explicitly state that existing legal constructs—designed for human decision-makers—do not adequately address AI's role in clinical workflows. SANCIAN LLC notes that traditional malpractice frameworks assume human decision-makers and AI disrupts these assumptions. AMA observes that use of AI impacting patient-physician medical decision-making does not fall neatly within current legal constructs like product liability approaches. Cyberhill Partners states that current medical malpractice frameworks assume human decision-making and don't account for AI involvement.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 45 of 60+)

consensusPoints.text: Vendor contract practices currently shift disproportionate risk to providers. Healthcare providers and health systems report that AI vendors use contract terms that inappropriately transfer liability to clinical organizations. University of Kansas Health System reports that AI vendors are increasingly capping their liability for data breaches at very low amounts (e.g., one year of service fees), which ignores the massive downstream risk to the health system and patients. AUA notes that contracts may shift indemnification risk to clinicians despite their limited control over AI design or updates. WellSpan Health observes that vendors use opaque contracting practices and black-box data silos that shift disproportionate liability to providers.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and health systems (approximately 20 of 25 provider comments)

consensusPoints.text: Human oversight must be preserved regardless of AI autonomy level. Commenters agree that clinicians should retain final decision-making authority and that AI should inform rather than replace clinical judgment. Massive Bio states that AI must inform but not dictate—explicitly preserving physician autonomy. AAPA emphasizes that healthcare-related AI technology cannot be granted an assumption of veracity without oversight from a licensed clinician. Global Liver Institute notes that AI tools should support, not replace, clinical judgment, and clinicians should retain full responsibility for interpreting and applying AI-generated information.

HHS should provide federal guidance to reduce fragmentation. Commenters explicitly call for HHS to issue guidance, model contract language, or safe harbor provisions rather than leaving liability allocation entirely to state courts or individual negotiations. Onboard AI suggests HHS can play a constructive role by clarifying expectations for reasonable governance, enabling courts, regulators, and organizations to assess whether appropriate care was exercised. Advocate Health recommends federal legislation should set clear, consistent national standards for liability, accountability, transparency, and responsible AI use. SonderMind proposes HHS should convene multi-stakeholder working group to develop model liability allocation frameworks clarifying responsibility based on who controls what.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 40 of 60+)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over how liability should be distributed among clinicians, healthcare organizations, and AI developers when AI influences clinical decisions.

positions.label: Clinician-Centered Accountability
positions.stance: Clinicians retain primary liability as final decision-makers. Most professional medical associations, some AI developers, and EHR vendors support this position. EHRA states that ultimate responsibility for patient care rests with authorizing clinician. Massive Bio notes that clinicians remain the final authority and thus primarily liable. DSI emphasizes that clinicians and organizations remain responsible for clinical decisions even when AI is involved.
positions.supportLevel: Most professional medical associations, some AI developers, EHR vendors
keyArguments: Clinicians are licensed professionals with legal authority and responsibility for patient care
AI tools are advisory; the human must make the final call
Existing professional liability frameworks can accommodate AI as a tool

positions.label: Shared Control-Based Liability
positions.stance: Responsibility should align with control over the AI system. Most health systems, many AI developers, patient advocacy groups, and labor unions support this position. CommonSpirit Health argues that liability for harm or bias caused by AI tool should fall also to the vendor who developed and tested the product, not solely the clinician who deployed it. SonderMind notes that current frameworks risk holding clinicians fully responsible for technical failures they cannot prevent, detect, or fix. MHA states that if a reasonable clinician could not have known that an AI output was erroneous, and it was reasonable to rely on that output based on the developer's representations, the accountability for resulting harm should rest with the AI developer.
positions.supportLevel: Most health systems, many AI developers, patient advocacy groups, labor unions
keyArguments: Clinicians cannot control algorithm design, training data, or vendor updates
Developers possess exclusive knowledge of failure modes and limitations
Holding clinicians liable for technical failures they cannot detect is unjust and uninsurable

positions.label: Developer-Primary Liability
positions.stance: AI developers should bear primary responsibility for system performance. Several physician specialty societies and some patient advocates support this position. AASM argues that AI vendors should bear legal responsibility for performance errors attributable to their algorithms. ACC suggests government should encourage a framework where developers can assume liability for specific medical diagnosis, treatment, and management use cases. ASRS states that developers and vendors of clinical AI systems should bear appropriate responsibility for system performance, maintenance, updates, and known limitations.
positions.supportLevel: Several physician specialty societies, some patient advocates
keyArguments: Developers are best positioned to prevent harm given their knowledge of system design
Industry precedents (e.g., IDx-DR) demonstrate developers can assume liability with insurance
Would lower barriers to clinical adoption

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether and how to provide liability protection for organizations following defined governance practices.

positions.label: Support Safe Harbors
positions.stance: Provide liability protection for organizations implementing defined governance practices. Primarily AI developers, health systems, and some professional associations support this position. BlueHalo suggests considering safe harbors for organizations implementing defined evaluation, monitoring, and oversight practices. AADA recommends establishing safe harbor that clarifies and limits physician liability when AI is used as support tool—applies in cases of documented clinical oversight, compliance protocols, and diagnostics. Dr. Randhawa proposes HHS's internal AI Governance Board should advocate for exploration of time-limited federal safe-harbor mechanisms during declared staffing emergencies.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-20 commenters
keyArguments: Incentivizes adoption of best practices without requiring new legislation
Reduces defensive non-adoption driven by liability uncertainty
Creates predictability for organizations investing in governance infrastructure

positions.label: Cautious Conditional Support
positions.stance: Safe harbors need careful design to avoid undermining accountability. Several academic commenters, patient advocates, and some professional associations hold this position. Kanav Jain warns to avoid governance frameworks that allow responsibility to be laundered through vendor disclaimers or performative human review while the system design prevents meaningful intervention. Markkula Center suggests HHS can convene stakeholders and publish model contractual principles or safe-harbor approaches that distinguish systematic or repeatable model defects from patient-specific clinical judgment.
positions.supportLevel: Several academic commenters, patient advocates, some professional associations
keyArguments: Safe harbors must not allow responsibility laundering through performative compliance
Must distinguish systematic model defects from patient-specific clinical judgment
Should not shield organizations from accountability for genuine negligence

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI use should become part of the standard of care and implications for liability.

positions.label: AI Should Not Define Standard
positions.stance: Using AI should remain optional without creating new liability exposure. Most physician specialty societies and many individual clinicians support this position. ACC argues that policy frameworks should clearly ensure AI/ML-driven solutions are not considered or presumed as the new standard of care. ASRS states that physicians should retain clinical judgment and oversight but not be penalized for reasonable reliance on validated tools or choosing not to use AI where standards unsettled. AASM warns that if AI use becomes standard of care, practices lacking financial resources to adopt may face disproportionate legal exposure.
positions.supportLevel: Most physician specialty societies, many individual clinicians
keyArguments: Practices lacking resources to adopt AI should not face disproportionate legal exposure
Standards should evolve through clinical evidence, not technology availability
Physicians should not be penalized for choosing not to use AI

positions.label: Acknowledge Evolving Standards
positions.stance: Framework must address when AI use becomes expected. Several health systems and some AI developers support this position. MHA recommends addressing evolving standard of care including when omission of proven AI could be considered substandard. Massive Bio notes that following validated AI recommendations could be considered in determining the standard of care.
positions.supportLevel: Several health systems, some AI developers
keyArguments: As AI proves beneficial, failure to use it may constitute substandard care
Framework should address both over-reliance and under-utilization
Professional societies should guide specialty-specific standards

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether liability frameworks should be established through agency guidance or congressional legislation.

positions.label: Guidance-Based Approach
positions.stance: HHS should issue guidance without new legislation. The majority of commenters support this position. Markkula Center notes this would create predictability without requiring new legislation. Eli Talbert suggests clarification could be implemented through ONC interpretive guidance under HTI authority, joint FDA-ONC guidance on hybrid accountability allocation, or CMS Conditions of Participation updates.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (approximately 35-40)
keyArguments: Can be implemented more quickly than legislation
Provides flexibility to adapt as technology evolves
Creates predictability without rigid mandates

positions.label: Legislative Action Needed
positions.stance: Congress must act to establish clear liability frameworks. Several large health systems and some professional associations support this position. Advocate Health argues that federal legislation should set clear, consistent national standards for liability, accountability, transparency, and responsible AI use. Interstella suggests HHS could work with Congress to adjust liability frameworks if needed.
positions.supportLevel: Several large health systems, some professional associations
keyArguments: Fundamental liability allocation requires statutory authority
State-by-state variation creates untenable fragmentation
Guidance alone cannot provide sufficient legal certainty

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Disproportionate liability exposure for AI failures they cannot control or detect; vendor contract practices that shift all risk to providers; uncertainty about standard of care when using or declining AI tools. Providers emphasize the untenable position of being held liable both for relying on AI and for exercising independent judgment against AI recommendations. They highlight the practical reality that high-throughput clinical environments limit meaningful verification of AI outputs.
specificPoints: MHA states that holding healthcare providers liable both for relying on AI and for exercising independent judgment against AI recommendations places them in an untenable position

Providence notes that providers need direction about where accountability should lie when negotiating with national EHR vendors and AI companies who have greater negotiating power than even sophisticated health systems

Proposed solutions include model contract language with realistic indemnification, clear allocation of responsibility based on control, safe harbors for documented governance practices, and federal guidance to level negotiating power with national vendors

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability uncertainty creating wait and see culture that slows adoption; multi-party accountability complexity in AI value chains; need for governance architecture standards to enable market entry. Developers emphasize that liability clarity would actually increase adoption and innovation. Several note that without clear governance boundaries, even beneficial AI tools cannot cross procurement thresholds because institutional buyers lack frameworks to accept accountability.
specificPoints: FERZ AI states that without clear governance boundaries, liability cannot be allocated

Ellipsis Health notes that in the absence of clear guidance, organizations often default to conservative positions regarding limiting AI use or requiring excessive manual review—both tactics create inefficiencies, slow adoption, and diminish the ability to realize positive outcomes

Proposed solutions include shared responsibility frameworks based on control and capability, model governance playbooks and standard contract clauses, certification or accreditation programs that clarify roles, and safe harbors tied to defined governance practices

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protecting physician autonomy and professional judgment; ensuring AI does not create new malpractice exposure beyond existing standards; preventing AI from becoming presumptive standard of care. Medical associations emphasize the no-win position created when physicians face liability for both following and overriding AI recommendations. They stress that physicians cannot be expected to detect algorithmic errors in systems they did not design and cannot inspect.
specificPoints: AMA states that assigning liability to physicians on the basis that they should not depend on AI outputs and must rely on their independent judgment, while also exposing them to penalties for doing so, effectively places them in an untenable, no-win position
ASRS argues that physicians should not be held accountable for errors attributable to algorithm design, training data deficiencies, or vendor-controlled system updates

Proposed solutions include clear federal guidance on liability allocation, developer accountability for algorithmic errors not reasonably detectable by clinicians, protection for physicians who reasonably rely on validated tools, and specialty-specific standards developed with professional societies

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance architecture gaps between policy and operational reality; technical requirements for accountability (audit trails, provenance, verification); distinguishing systematic model defects from clinical judgment errors. Academic commenters provide the most technically detailed analysis of accountability mechanisms, emphasizing that governance policy without governance architecture is merely aspirational. They highlight the need for invocation-time verification, cryptographic audit trails, and reconstructable evidence of decision-making.
specificPoints: Jennifer Bryan notes that real-world patient safety and accountable deployment are not defined by performance metrics alone—they require systems that preserve reconstructable evidence of how and why decisions were made in context

Walt Simmons observes that in documented cases producing most significant harms, organizations had governance policy but lacked governance architecture—structural connections between policy commitments and operational reality that would make commitments binding rather than aspirational

Proposed solutions include minimum governance architecture standards, chain-of-custody documentation requirements, distinction between retrospective attribution and invocation-bound authorization, and evidence infrastructure for procurement, deployment, and insurance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clear accountability when AI tools cause patient harm; appeal rights and recourse pathways for AI-influenced decisions; protection against AI-driven care denials. Patient advocates emphasize that accountability structures must serve patients' ability to seek redress, not just organizational risk management. They highlight concerns about AI being used to deny care while diffusing responsibility.
specificPoints: ACS CAN warns that absent sufficient oversight, AI algorithms can be improperly used to deny care, citing a 2023 Cigna lawsuit
APDA expresses concern over who is legally liable for patient safety, quality of care, and care outcomes when AI tools are used in clinical care

Proposed solutions include clear standards for accountability when AI causes harm, auditability and appeal rights for AI-influenced decisions, regulations holding corporations accountable for AI products, and transparency about AI involvement in care decisions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Multi-stakeholder liability allocation in complex value chains; state-by-state regulatory fragmentation; compounding compliance costs without corresponding incentives. Clearinghouses and data intermediaries highlight the unique challenge of liability allocation when AI-enabled transactions involve multiple parties (data providers, model developers, intermediaries) and determining fault is currently impossible under existing HIPAA contracts.
specificPoints: Cooperative Exchange notes that when an AI-driven transaction fails or is biased, determining fault among data provider, model developer, or clearinghouse intermediary is currently impossible under existing HIPAA contracts

Proposed solutions include Chain of Provenance standards for AI-influenced metadata, federal preemption of fragmented state requirements, and clear allocation of responsibility across multi-stakeholder value chains

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Personal liability exposure for AI failures beyond their control; confusion about how much to rely on or override AI tools; lack of clear governance norms for AI approval and monitoring. Frontline clinicians describe the daily reality of using AI tools without clear guidance on accountability, creating confusion about liability, standards of care, and appropriate reliance.
specificPoints: Dr. Emilie Maxie notes it is often unclear who is responsible if the tool is wrong or misleading, creating confusion around liability, standards of care, and how much clinicians should rely on or override these tools

Proposed solutions include basic governance norms identifying who approves, monitors, and can pause AI tools, clear disclosure and practical evaluation standards, and safeguards protecting both patients and clinicians

noteworthyInsights.insight: Defense AI governance principles apply to clinical settings. Eli Talbert, a Senior Data Scientist at U.S. Special Operations Command, draws parallels between military and clinical AI accountability, noting that accountability clarification reduces uncertainty without altering tort standards—it establishes documentation expectations that support, rather than supplant, existing malpractice and product liability frameworks.

Insurance market already responding to governance gaps. GLACIS Technologies reports that insurance carriers are introducing AI-specific exclusions and requiring governance documentation most organizations cannot produce, suggesting market forces may drive governance adoption faster than regulation.

Hardware-level accountability for agentic AI. Michael Bridges proposes cryptographically signed hardware-resident event logging as a black box solution for AI-controlled medical devices, noting that traditional medical liability relies on auditing what went wrong, but AI-driven devices can produce unreliable or spoofed software logs.

RapidAI stroke triage paradox. Penn Neurosurgery team describes how RapidAI is not intended for clinical diagnosis but remains significant input into clinical decision-making, with the distinction often resting on technicalities like viewing images on mobile device rather than full-resolution radiology workstation.

Autonomous AI liability precedents exist. ACC cites IDx-DR (LumineticsCore) for diabetic retinopathy and Doctronic for automated prescription refills as examples where developers can assume liability for specific medical diagnosis, treatment, and management use cases with liability insurance.

AI-driven care denials with documented harm. Walt Simmons cites Senate investigation finding UnitedHealthcare's nH Predict had error rate of approximately 90% in internal documents with patients dying during or after algorithmic termination of coverage.

Quantified adoption hesitancy. AMA reports that in their AI Physician Sentiment Survey of 1,000+ physicians, respondents identified liability concerns as one of their top concerns when considering implementing these technologies.

Scale of AI-driven denials. ProPublica investigation cited by Walt Simmons documented Cigna's PXDX algorithm denied over 300,000 payment requests in a two-month period with reviewing physicians spending average of 1.2 seconds per claim.

Governance architecture vs. governance policy distinction. Walt Simmons distinguishes between having policies on paper versus structural connections between policy commitments and operational reality that would make commitments binding rather than aspirational.

AI as source of exposure, not neutral tool. Kanav Jain argues that when AI influences clinical action, eligibility, documentation, or resource allocation, it becomes part of the causal chain of harm—governance must reflect this.

emergingPatterns.pattern: State-level fragmentation creating operational challenges. SonderMind reports being actively engaged with state legislatures on proposed bills holding providers fully responsible for all aspects of AI system performance, forcing them to exclude some states from AI offerings. Kooth operates the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for youth in California, navigating unique state requirements. Multi-state health systems like Providence (seven states) and Advocate Health (five states) face compounding complexity from inconsistent requirements.

Facility-type patterns in concerns. Large health systems focus on vendor negotiating power imbalances and need for federal guidance to level playing field. Small and rural practices, emphasized by AAFP and AASM, express concern about disproportionate legal exposure if AI becomes standard of care without resources to adopt. Safety-net providers face disproportionate burdens according to Advocate Health, which specifically notes ambiguities affect federally qualified health centers, rural hospitals, and faith-based or nonprofit programs. Academic medical centers focus more on technical governance architecture and evidence infrastructure requirements.

Specialty-specific patterns in AI adoption and liability concerns. Radiology is most advanced in AI adoption and focused on persistent malpractice risk despite AI assistance. Behavioral health has unique concerns about high-risk situations (suicide risk, duty-to-warn) creating heightened liability stakes. Dentistry emphasizes need for dental-specific standards and simplified compliance templates for smaller practices. Sleep medicine expresses concerns about AI-driven diagnostic determinations affecting long-term therapy decisions and DME prescriptions.

Unintended consequences of liability uncertainty. Multiple commenters note defensive non-adoption where organizations avoid beneficial AI tools entirely rather than accepting unclear liability. Ellipsis Health notes organizations requiring excessive manual review that creates inefficiencies, slows adoption, and diminishes the ability to realize positive outcomes. FERZ AI notes innovation suppression where AI vendors capable of delivering genuine clinical value cannot cross the procurement threshold because institutional buyers lack governance architecture to accept accountability. Current dynamics push all risk to providers while vendors and developers disclaim responsibility. Standard of care paradox emerges where if AI becomes standard of care, practices unable to afford adoption face increased liability for non-use.

keyQuotations.quote: A growing liability vacuum has emerged as AI developers increasingly disclaim responsibility for errors or harm, even as clinicians are expected to rely on these tools in clinical and administrative decision-making.
Legal and compliance teams frequently delay or block deployment not due to known safety risks, but due to uncertainty around accountability, liability, and post-deployment expectations.
The result is a system in which responsibility is concentrated on providers, but the rules governing acceptable AI use remain underdeveloped.

Assigning liability to physicians on the basis that they should not depend on AI outputs and must rely on their independent judgment, while also exposing them to penalties for doing so, effectively places them in an untenable, no-win position.
Holding healthcare providers liable both for relying on AI and for exercising independent judgment against AI recommendations places them in an untenable position.

Current frameworks risk holding clinicians fully responsible for technical failures they cannot prevent, detect, or fix, such as developer bugs, inaccurate AI-generated outputs, or vendor-side data breaches. This creates a chilling effect: providers avoid beneficial AI tools entirely rather than accept uninsurable risk.

AI vendors are increasingly capping their liability for data breaches at very low amounts (e.g., one year of service fees), which ignores the massive downstream risk to the health system and patients.

If a provider utilizes a sophisticated AI product to guide care—a product that has been touted as effective and safe when used appropriately—the liability for harm or bias caused by the tool itself should fall also to the vendor who developed and tested the product, not solely the clinician who deployed it.
Avoid governance frameworks that allow responsibility to be laundered through vendor disclaimers or performative 'human review' while the system design prevents meaningful intervention.

When AI-assisted documentation becomes indistinguishable from documentation produced by a licensed clinician, accountability collapses. Malpractice attribution becomes probabilistic. Informed consent records become unverifiable.

Real-world patient safety and accountable deployment are not defined by performance metrics alone—they require systems that preserve reconstructable evidence of how and why decisions were made in context.

If a reasonable clinician could not have known—or had no reason to know—that an AI output was erroneous, and it was reasonable to rely on that output based on the developer's representations, the accountability for resulting harm to patients should rest with the AI developer.

Health systems are being asked to assume disproportionate responsibility for evaluating, governing, and managing AI tools without consistent national standards, predictable payment pathways, or clear liability allocation.
If providers fear being held accountable for decisions influenced by AI they may be reluctant to implement AI tools in their practice.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types engage substantively with the complexity of liability allocation, providing specific examples, legal analysis, and concrete recommendations. The discourse reflects sophisticated understanding of both technical and legal dimensions of AI accountability.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific examples (IDx-DR precedent, UnitedHealthcare nH Predict investigation, Cigna PXDX algorithm), surveys (AMA Physician Sentiment Survey), and real-world contract experiences. Academic commenters provide technically detailed analysis. Some assertions about liability uncertainty's impact on adoption are based on organizational experience rather than systematic data.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from malpractice insurers who would be key stakeholders in liability framework design. Relatively few comments from smaller physician practices that may face disproportionate burden. Patient voice is present but less prominent than provider and developer perspectives.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves intersection of tort law, professional liability, contract law, federal regulatory authority, and rapidly evolving technology. Commenters acknowledge that existing legal frameworks were not designed for AI-assisted care and that solutions require coordination across multiple domains.

3.themeDescription: Transparency, Explainability, and Disclosure Requirements. This theme encompasses all comments addressing what information about AI systems should be disclosed to clinicians, patients, and regulators, including model documentation, performance reporting, and patient notification. || It includes proposals for standardized AI "model cards" or "nutrition labels," debates about algorithmic explainability requirements, calls for disclosure of training data and validation methods, and discussions of patient consent and notification for AI use

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal near-universal consensus that transparency is foundational to trustworthy AI adoption in healthcare, with stakeholders across all sectors agreeing that current disclosure practices are inadequate and inconsistent. The central tension lies not in whether transparency is needed, but in what form it should take—with debates centering on standardized "model cards" versus flexible disclosure, the appropriate level of technical explainability required, and how to balance vendor intellectual property concerns against clinical and patient needs. The dominant recommendation thrust favors federally-mandated standardized disclosure formats with tiered requirements based on risk level, accompanied by plain-language patient notification standards.

consensusPoints.text: Transparency is a prerequisite for trust and safe AI adoption. Nearly all commenters explicitly identify transparency as foundational to building trust among clinicians, patients, and health systems. This consensus spans healthcare providers, technology vendors, professional associations, and patient advocacy groups. Interstella states that trust is the currency of AI in healthcare, CommonSpirit Health emphasizes that mandatory and detailed transparency is a critical prerequisite for safe, ethical, and effective deployment of AI, and SANCIAN LLC frames transparency as trust infrastructure.

exceptions.text: Several commenters argue that transparency alone is insufficient without enforceable accountability mechanisms—explanations and model cards do not produce accountability without enforceable contestability.

consensusPoints.text: Current disclosure practices are inadequate and inconsistent. A strong majority of commenters describe existing transparency requirements as fragmented, incomparable across vendors, and insufficient for informed decision-making. Trust Publishing Institute notes that current disclosures are inconsistent, incomparable across vendors, and insufficient for informed adoption decisions. Dental Standards Institute observes that most dental AI products rely on proprietary datasets, internal testing protocols, limited transparency into training populations, non-standard reporting of sensitivity/specificity, and inconsistent disclosure of subgroup performance. Markkula Center for Applied Ethics notes that frontline users, purchasers, and health systems often lack easy access to clear, comparable, version-specific information.

exceptions.text: The Markkula Center notes that ONC certification criteria already require extensive transparency through source attributes, but these are frequently difficult to compare across products and not consistently structured for operational decision making.

consensusPoints.text: Patients have a right to know when AI is used in their care. Virtually all patient advocacy groups and most healthcare providers agree that patients should be informed when AI influences their care, though they differ on implementation details. Vikki Small, a safety-net hospital leader, states that patients should be told at the start of an interaction that AI is involved, what it can and cannot do, and how to reach a person at any time. Massive Bio recommends requiring that patients be informed when AI is involved in their care. Providence Health notes that patient focus has been singular: tell me when you are using AI with my information.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually all patient advocacy groups and most healthcare providers (30+ commenters)

consensusPoints.text: Standardized disclosure formats are needed. A strong majority call for standardized disclosure mechanisms, frequently referencing model cards, nutrition labels, or AI Product Facts as models. AORN recommends establishing requirements for standardized reporting of relevant AI model characteristics similar to nutrition facts label or model card. Dental Standards Institute suggests HHS should promote a consistent AI nutrition label disclosure format. AMA suggests a standardized format for disclosures across industry participants, noting that a consistent approach makes it easier for all physicians to understand and compare technologies.

Clinicians need transparency to fulfill professional obligations. Most physician and nursing organizations emphasize that transparency is essential for clinicians to exercise independent judgment, obtain informed consent, and defend clinical decisions. AASM states that clinicians must be able to understand and defend the rationale for clinical decisions, and opaque systems undermine this fundamental professional responsibility. CommonSpirit Health argues that without robust transparency requirements into all non-proprietary elements of a clinically deployed AI tool, health care providers are fundamentally unable to meet their obligations concerning patient consent and notice. ADLM notes that independent evaluation of a system's performance without transparency is challenging and can undermine ongoing quality assurance efforts.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most physician and nursing organizations (15+ professional associations)

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders disagree on how much technical detail about AI reasoning should be disclosed, ranging from full algorithmic transparency to outcome-based approaches.

positions.label: Full Algorithmic Transparency
positions.stance: Require detailed disclosure of how AI systems reach conclusions. Most healthcare providers, patient advocacy groups, and laboratory professionals support this position, including CommonSpirit Health, ADLM, and AASM.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25 commenters
keyArguments: Clinicians cannot exercise meaningful oversight without understanding AI reasoning
Informed consent requires patients understand basis for recommendations
Quality assurance and validation impossible without access to model logic

positions.label: Contextual/Tiered Explainability
positions.stance: Adjust explainability requirements based on risk level and use case. Most AI vendors, some health systems, and behavioral health providers support this position, including SonderMind, Waymark, and AMA.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15 commenters
keyArguments: Full algorithmic transparency may not be technically feasible for all AI types
Vendors have legitimate IP concerns about disclosing proprietary algorithms
Different clinical contexts warrant different levels of explanation

positions.label: Outcome-Based Transparency
positions.stance: Focus on what AI does rather than how it works. Several technology vendors and researchers support this position, including Keith Mountjoy/RTI, Penguin AI, and FERZ AI.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8 commenters
keyArguments: Transparency should focus on interpretable outputs tied to observable signals
Post-hoc explanations can be misleading; focus on provenance and traceability instead
Glass box approaches with traceable outputs preferable to explaining opaque models

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate whether transparency requirements should be federally mandated or industry-led voluntary standards.

positions.label: Mandatory Federal Requirements
positions.stance: Require standardized disclosures through regulation. Most healthcare providers, professional associations, and patient advocates support this position, including CommonSpirit Health, NCPDP, and Global Liver Institute.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 35 commenters
keyArguments: Voluntary approaches have failed to produce consistent, comparable information
Market incentives alone insufficient to drive adequate transparency
Patient safety requires enforceable minimum standards

positions.label: Voluntary/Industry-Led Standards
positions.stance: Support industry-developed standards with federal encouragement. Some technology vendors, accreditation organizations, and EHR vendors support this position, including URAC, AAFP, and EHRA.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10 commenters
keyArguments: Flexibility allows adaptation to rapidly evolving technology
Accreditation can provide market-based accountability
Overly prescriptive requirements may stifle innovation

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders disagree on when patients should be notified about AI use in their care.

positions.label: Broad Notification
positions.stance: Inform patients whenever AI is used in any aspect of care. Patient advocacy groups and some providers support this position, including Global Liver Institute, National MS Society, and Dr. Russell.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15 commenters
keyArguments: Patients have fundamental right to know how their care is delivered
Transparency builds trust and supports shared decision-making
Consistent notification prevents confusion about when AI is involved

positions.label: Risk-Based Notification
positions.stance: Notify patients only for higher-risk or decision-influencing AI. Health systems and some professional associations support this position, including Providence Health, ACR, and AUA.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 12 commenters
keyArguments: Notifying for every AI use (including spell-check) would be impractical and meaningless
Patients need clarity on what AI means before notification is useful
Focus notification on AI that materially influences clinical decisions

areasOfDebate.topic: Protecting Intellectual Property vs. Enabling Oversight
