areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate how to balance vendor IP protection with the need for clinical oversight and transparency.

positions.label: Transparency Trumps IP
positions.stance: Non-proprietary elements must be fully disclosed. Healthcare providers, laboratory professionals, and patient advocates support this position, including CommonSpirit Health, WellSpan Health, and CCLA.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20 commenters
keyArguments: Patient safety cannot be subordinated to commercial interests
Providers cannot fulfill oversight obligations without access to model information
Black box approaches are fundamentally incompatible with clinical accountability

positions.label: Balanced Approach
positions.stance: Protect core IP while requiring functional transparency. AI vendors and some health systems support this position, including Waymark and SonderMind.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10 commenters
keyArguments: Vendors have legitimate concerns about disclosing proprietary algorithms
Functional transparency (what it does, limitations, validation) can be achieved without revealing trade secrets
Tiered disclosure based on risk level can balance competing interests

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inability to fulfill professional obligations without transparency, vendor black box practices preventing meaningful oversight, and liability exposure when AI reasoning is opaque. Providers emphasize that transparency is not optional—it's required for informed consent, clinical documentation, and defending decisions in malpractice contexts.
specificPoints: Mandatory disclosure of training data, validation methods, and limitations
Standardized model facts comparable across vendors
Ban on black box opacity with federal enforcement

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Members lack resources to evaluate AI tools, especially in small practices. Transparency information not structured for operational decision-making, and subspecialty-specific validation data often unavailable. They emphasize that physicians cannot be expected to develop deep AI expertise—transparency must be accessible to clinicians of all experience levels.
specificPoints: National registry of AI solutions with standardized transparency information
Standardized disclosure format similar to Structured Product Labeling for medications
Educational resources for clinicians and patients

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Balancing transparency with intellectual property protection, inconsistent state-level requirements creating compliance burden, and need for clear federal standards to enable market confidence. Many vendors actively embrace transparency as competitive advantage—several describe glass box approaches as core to their business model.
specificPoints: Tiered explainability requirements based on risk level
Standardized metadata and provenance standards for AI outputs
FHIR-based standards for AI transparency

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patients don't know when AI is used or how it influences care, privacy concerns about how health data is used for AI training, and fear that AI will erode human connection in care. They emphasize that transparency must come from trusted sources and be delivered in accessible formats—peer-to-peer education may be more effective than formal disclosures.
specificPoints: Plain-language disclosure standards
HHS website with AI information for patients
Educational materials developed with patient community input

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of interoperable standards for AI transparency, data-level transparency gaps where systems don't know what AI generated, and need for machine-readable disclosure formats. They emphasize that transparency standards must be computable and interoperable, not just human-readable documents.
specificPoints: AI Transparency on FHIR Implementation Guide
FHIR Provenance standards for AI-generated data
Machine-readable model cards aligned with NIST AI RMF

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Post-hoc AI explanations may be misleading or contextually incorrect, transparency without accountability is insufficient, and need for evidence-grade documentation standards. Researchers highlight that AI can generate plausible-sounding rationales that don't reflect actual reasoning—transparency must capture actual evidence used, not post-hoc justifications.
specificPoints: Evidence representation standards (EBMonFHIR)
Cryptographic attestation for AI governance
Transaction-level proof, not just system-level assurances

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI embedded in required workflows without clear disclosure, automation bias when AI reasoning is opaque, and documentation burden and liability when AI generates content. They emphasize real-world workflow integration—transparency must be actionable at point of care, not buried in documentation.
specificPoints: Clear disclosure when AI is involved and for what purpose
AI tools that surface confidence levels and limitations
Ability to turn off AI features when appropriate

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Cannot independently validate AI performance without manufacturer transparency, CLIA obligations require ongoing quality assurance that opacity prevents, and need for clear federal guidance on validation expectations. Laboratories have specific regulatory obligations (CLIA) that require ongoing performance verification—transparency is not optional for compliance.
specificPoints: Manufacturer disclosure of development datasets, validation methodology, and limitations
Federal guidance on AI validation expectations for laboratory medicine

noteworthyInsights.insight: SANCIAN LLC reframes transparency not as a compliance burden but as foundational infrastructure that enables the entire AI ecosystem to function, similar to how financial markets depend on disclosure requirements—describing it as trust infrastructure.

Providence Health raises a practical challenge about notification scope: every email that uses autocorrect is using AI, but surely patients are not interested in giving consent to their physician using spell check tools. This highlights the need for clear definitions of what AI use warrants disclosure.

Eli Talbert, PhD, a data scientist at US Special Operations Command, applies defense AI governance principles to healthcare, noting that structured logging can detect disparate patterns across protected characteristics—a cross-sector insight on accountability design.

Multiple AI vendors including Penguin AI, Leap Orbit, and FERZ AI explicitly reject black box approaches as incompatible with healthcare, positioning full traceability as a competitive advantage rather than a burden. This suggests market forces may be aligning with transparency goals.

SKAF identifies a subtle but critical issue: AI can generate explanations that appear to support its recommendations but are contextually incorrect. True transparency requires capturing the actual evidence used, not post-hoc justifications.

GLACIS Technologies distinguishes between system-level assurances (compliance certificates, vendor attestations) and transaction-level proof (cryptographically verifiable evidence of what happened for specific patient interactions)—a technical distinction with significant governance implications.

David Bynon argues that what you publish—and how you structure it—becomes your identity in AI-first ecosystem, suggesting that transparency requirements will reshape how healthcare organizations present themselves to both humans and machines.

Vikki Small emphasizes that transparency only works if patients understand what they're being told, requiring plain language at or below 6th-grade reading level consistent with the Plain Writing Act—a practical standard often overlooked in technical discussions.

emergingPatterns.pattern: State fragmentation concerns: Multi-state providers including SonderMind, Cooperative Exchange, and Providence consistently cite divergent state AI laws as creating compliance burden and inconsistent patient experiences. Kooth notes they've had to exclude certain states from AI offerings due to regulatory uncertainty, with multiple commenters referencing California's emerging AI requirements.

Large health systems like CommonSpirit, Providence, and Advocate Health emphasize need for vendor transparency to support internal governance and procurement decisions, while small and rural practices (emphasized by AMA, AAFP, AAN) lack resources to evaluate AI tools independently—standardized transparency information is essential for equitable access.

Vendors with deployed products like Massive Bio, Penguin AI, and SonderMind tend to embrace transparency as competitive advantage, while professional associations focus on protecting members from liability and ensuring tools are usable in practice.

Multiple unintended consequences identified: notification fatigue from over-broad patient notification requirements (Providence), automation bias where clinicians defer to AI outputs under time pressure (Caroline Morrow), selective transparency where AI systems present evidence selectively to justify predetermined conclusions (SKAF), and compliance theater where marketing terms like Responsible AI and Explainable AI lack precise definitions enabling vendors to claim transparency without substance (FERZ AI).

Cross-sector convergence with multiple commenters referencing alignment with EU AI Act requirements (GLACIS Technologies), NIST AI Risk Management Framework (Markkula Center, NeuroFlow), OMB Memorandum M-25-21 (David Bynon), and CMS Prior Authorization Final Rule (David Bynon).

keyQuotations.quote: Trust is the currency of AI in healthcare.
Transparency is trust infrastructure.

Without robust transparency requirements into all non-proprietary elements of a clinically deployed AI tool, health care providers are fundamentally unable to meet their obligations concerning patient consent and notice, nor can providers assure patients of the safety and reliability of the technology being used in their treatment.
Clinicians must be able to understand and defend the rationale for clinical decisions, and opaque systems undermine this fundamental professional responsibility.
As doctors, we are told to document everything from day one. If you don't document it, it didn't happen. Healthcare AI should be held to the same evidentiary standard.
When AI outputs cannot be clearly tied to observable physical signals, clinicians are hesitant to rely on them for decision support.
The question is not whether an AI system can explain itself; it is whether harm can be forced to matter through correction, repair, and rollback.
An AI system that influences care but cannot be explained, contested, or reviewed is not merely non-patient-centered; it is unsafe.
The central gap is not absence of authority. It is standardization, usability, and the ability of real organizations to operationalize what is already required.

Currently, both health information technology systems and users do not always know what data was generated or modified by an AI tool or what tool was used—therefore the data can appear falsely, as if it was generated only by a clinical user.

We explicitly reject the use of 'Black Box' AI in healthcare. Our platform is built on the principle of the 'Glass Box' where every AI prediction, from medical coding to clinical appeals, is traceable back to a specific source of truth within the patient record.

Trust is a prerequisite for automation; users are only willing to adopt AI in day-to-day use when they can see 'behind the curtain' of the system to understand specifically how a record was matched or a provider's credentials were verified.
If disclosures and instructions are only available in English, patients with limited English proficiency are shut out of safe use.
Every email that uses autocorrect is using AI, but surely patients are not interested in giving consent to their physician using spell check tools.

Responsible AI adoption must therefore prioritize clear communication about how AI is used, strong human oversight, and safeguards that reinforce, rather than diminish the human elements of care that patients and families value most.
Flawed inputs or AI models not tested with real-world subspecialty data are not likely to meaningfully contribute to clinical decision making.

Patients and caregivers are most supportive of AI that augments clinician decision-making with interpretable, real-world evidence, improves equity and consistency of care, and operates within strong governance and privacy safeguards.
The strongest dental AI solutions ultimately function as clinical copilots, delivering measurable business impact while reinforcing transparency, reliability, and clinician confidence.
Without this 'transparent-yet-secure' identification standard, hospitals are forced to choose between HIPAA-compliant encryption and the network visibility required for basic patient safety.
What you publish — and how you structure it — becomes your identity.
Transparency regarding LLM AI use within FDA is important to accelerate safe adoption across healthcare.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of transparency challenges, with stakeholders engaging substantively with technical, operational, and ethical dimensions. Multiple commenters reference specific frameworks (NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act), cite concrete examples, and acknowledge competing interests rather than presenting one-sided arguments.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many comments cite specific examples (Reuters investigation of TruDi Navigation System, IBM Watson for Oncology failure), reference existing standards and frameworks, and draw on organizational experience. However, quantitative evidence on transparency effectiveness is limited, with most arguments based on logical reasoning and professional experience rather than empirical studies.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Safety-net providers and underserved patient populations are underrepresented relative to their stake in transparency outcomes. Rural and small practice perspectives are primarily voiced through professional associations rather than direct comment. International perspectives and comparative analysis with other jurisdictions' transparency approaches are limited.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High complexity with multiple interconnected tensions: technical feasibility of explainability vs. clinical need for understanding; intellectual property protection vs. oversight requirements; comprehensive notification vs. notification fatigue; standardization vs. flexibility for evolving technology. Commenters generally acknowledge these tensions rather than offering simplistic solutions.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that data problems—not algorithmic limitations—are the primary barrier to clinical AI success, with fragmentation, quality issues, and incomplete interoperability cited across virtually all stakeholder types. The central tension lies between those advocating for strengthening existing standards (FHIR, USCDI) versus those arguing that current frameworks address data movement but not data meaning, requiring new semantic and governance layers. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to prioritize foundational data infrastructure investments, enforce existing interoperability requirements more aggressively, and extend interoperability concepts beyond clinical data to include governance artifacts, operational telemetry, and administrative workflows.

consensusPoints.text: Data fragmentation is the binding constraint on clinical AI adoption. Nearly all commenters identify fragmented, siloed health data as the primary barrier to AI innovation and deployment—not model capability or algorithmic sophistication. Interstella states that AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data. Scientix.ai emphasizes that the primary barriers to clinical AI adoption are infrastructural, not algorithmic, noting that the AI models themselves have reached impressive capability thresholds but what remains broken is the data substrate they depend on. Amir Abrams, Software Architect, observes that clinical AI fails when deployed into informationally impoverished workflows.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 50+ of 65 theme-relevant comments)

exceptions.text: Keith Mountjoy argues the primary barrier is lack of real-time physiological measurement data rather than fragmentation per se, though this is complementary rather than contradictory.

consensusPoints.text: Current interoperability enables data movement but not data meaning. A strong majority of commenters distinguish between technical data exchange capabilities and semantic interoperability, arguing that FHIR and related standards have succeeded at transport but fail to ensure consistent clinical interpretation. Health AI Institute notes that interoperability that enables data movement but not data meaning limits AI impact. HealthFramework explains that FHIR-based APIs have succeeded in enabling data movement, but interoperability at the transport layer does not guarantee semantic equivalence at the interpretation layer. Cooperative Exchange emphasizes it is not enough to move data—the AI must understand it.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (35+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: AI effectiveness depends on data quality, not just data access. Virtually all commenters addressing data quality emphasize that incomplete, inconsistent, or non-representative data undermines AI safety and effectiveness regardless of algorithmic sophistication. Interstella states an AI tool is only as good as the data it learns from. Caroline Morrow, Healthcare Consultant, argues that high data quality is the prerequisite for high algorithmic accuracy, and high accuracy is the prerequisite for patient safety. National MS Society emphasizes that the reliability and equity of AI tools hinge on the quality and representativeness of the data used to train these models.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Virtually all commenters addressing data quality (40+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: Existing standards (FHIR, USCDI, DICOM) should be strengthened and enforced, not replaced. Most commenters support building on existing interoperability frameworks rather than creating entirely new systems, though many call for expansion and stronger enforcement. ASRS recommends HHS prioritize stronger enforcement and maturation of existing data standards including DICOM, FHIR, and OMOP. Massive Bio urges doubling down on standards like FHIR/mCODE. ICF recommends adopting HL7, FHIR, USCDI, DICOM, LOINC, and SNOMED standards for consistent, broad adoption.

Interoperability must extend beyond clinical data to governance and operational artifacts. A notable consensus argues that AI oversight requires interoperable governance documentation, audit logs, and performance metrics—not just clinical data exchange. Onboard AI states interoperability should extend beyond data exchange to include governance and assurance artifacts. SANCIAN LLC emphasizes interoperability must include governance data, not just clinical data. Interstitial Institute argues the most consequential interoperability gap in clinical AI is not data interoperability—it is governance interoperability.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether patients or institutions should control data routing and aggregation for AI applications.

positions.label: Patient-Mediated Aggregation
positions.stance: Patients should control data routing and aggregation. Amir Abrams, Software Architect, HealthScoreAI, Dr. Kordonowy, and Steven Zecola, Patient Advocate advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: 5-7 commenters, primarily individual experts and patient advocates
keyArguments: Routing decisions currently controlled by institutions/vendors, not patients
Patient-designated endpoints would make longitudinal completeness the default
Aligns with HIPAA rights to direct access

positions.label: System-to-System Exchange
positions.stance: Focus on improving institutional data exchange. EHRA, Advocate Health, and CommonSpirit Health support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of trade associations and large health systems (25+ comments)
keyArguments: Population-level AI requires aggregated institutional data, not patient-by-patient assembly
Existing infrastructure investments should be leveraged
Patient-mediated approaches add complexity and may not scale

areasOfDebate.topic: EHR Vendor Role and Market Structure
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to pursue aggressive enforcement against EHR vendors or work with them through incentives.

positions.label: Aggressive Enforcement
positions.stance: EHR vendors are blocking innovation and require stronger federal intervention. HealthScoreAI, Penn Neurovascular Team, 1upHealth, and Advocate Health support this view.
positions.supportLevel: 15+ commenters, including health systems, startups, and academic researchers
keyArguments: Vendor lock-in creates proprietary data silos that impede AI development
Structural incentives, not technical limitations, are the primary barrier
Interoperability has stagnated because data liquidity threatens vendor business models

positions.label: Incentive-Based Modernization
positions.stance: Work with existing vendors through incentives rather than mandates. ASRS and Ryan Kern, Stanford Student support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: 8-10 commenters, including some trade associations and a medical student
keyArguments: Punitive measures may pass costs to end users
Switching costs from legacy systems are substantial
Partnership with established vendors is more realistic than disruption

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to prioritize clinical data standardization or expand scope to include administrative, operational, and social determinants data.

positions.label: Clinical Data Focus
positions.stance: Prioritize clinical data standardization within existing frameworks. AMA, AAN, and AAFP support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Most medical specialty societies (10+ comments)
keyArguments: Core clinical concepts need consistent expression first
Expanding scope too quickly risks diluting focus
USCDI expansion should be incremental and evidence-based

positions.label: Expanded Scope
positions.stance: Include administrative, operational, and social determinants data. Cooperative Exchange, Penguin AI, Waymark, and Dr. Randhawa, Neurosurgeon advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: 20+ commenters, including clearinghouses, technology vendors, and population health organizations
keyArguments: Administrative workflow interoperability is essential for AI at scale
Social determinants account for 80% of health outcomes but remain siloed
Operational telemetry (bed capacity, staffing) precedes clinical events

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over how broadly to enable data sharing for AI training and validation versus maintaining cautious restrictions.

positions.label: Broad Access with Safeguards
positions.stance: Enable wider data sharing for AI training and validation. ScriptChain Health, COGA Labs, and Massive Bio support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Most AI developers and startups (15+ comments)
keyArguments: AI models require diverse, representative datasets to achieve clinical-grade performance
Current restrictions disproportionately burden small companies
Open datasets and benchmarking platforms would level the playing field

positions.label: Cautious Expansion
positions.stance: Distinguish care delivery exchange from secondary use for training. EHY Consulting, AORN, and Ty Greenhalgh, Cybersecurity Expert advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: 8-10 commenters, including security-focused organizations
keyArguments: Interoperability expands attack surface and secondary-use risk
Data provenance and training boundaries must be enforceable
Privacy-preserving approaches (federated learning) should be prioritized

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: EHR vendor lock-in limiting access to their own data, integration complexity and infrastructure costs, and data curation burden for AI monitoring. Large health systems emphasize that even they face constraints accessing and processing their own data, despite significant resources.
specificPoints: Federal leadership needed to prevent vendor-created barriers
Standardized, low-cost FHIR-based exchanges required
Third-party registries needed for AI performance monitoring
Advocate Health notes even large systems face constraints on accessing, processing, and using their own data
MSK describes resource-intensive data curation for AI monitoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data fragmentation as primary bottleneck to innovation, proprietary formats and inconsistent API implementations, and disproportionate integration costs for startups. They emphasize that every AI deployment currently requires solving the same data reconciliation problem independently, consuming resources intended for innovation.
specificPoints: Mandate bulk data access and standardized APIs
Create open-source datasets and benchmarking platforms
Extend USCDI to include administrative and financial data
Penguin AI reports 76% of revenue cycle leaders rate integration between AI tools and core EHR systems as a major or severe challenge
Scientix.ai notes data workers spend over 40% of their time searching for, cleaning, and reconciling data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of harmonization among laboratory tests and clinical metrics, proprietary imaging formats limiting research, and workflow integration burden for clinicians. Medical specialty societies highlight domain-specific data challenges that generic interoperability frameworks miss.
specificPoints: Enforce and strengthen existing standards (DICOM, FHIR, OMOP)
Fund harmonization efforts such as laboratory test standardization
Develop specialty-specific data standards
ADLM notes different testing platforms can produce different numeric results for the same analyte affecting AI model performance
ASRS describes widespread use of proprietary imaging formats and incomplete standards implementation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inadequate access to large-scale, representative datasets, need for shared benchmarking and evaluation infrastructure, and semantic inconsistencies across data sources. They emphasize that interoperability must support research-specific requirements including data provenance, auditability, and linkage across care and research settings.
specificPoints: Fund R&D for semantic reconciliation methods
Invest in national benchmarking datasets
Support privacy-preserving cryptography for secure ML
CRA notes social determinants account for 80 percent of health outcomes but remain excluded from interoperability focus
Veeva emphasizes clinical research data remains fragmented across sponsors, sites, and technology platforms

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient ownership and control of health data, longitudinal record completeness for chronic disease management, and data accessibility for patients and their providers. They emphasize that patients should be the default legal custodians of their longitudinal records, with real-time exportability.
specificPoints: Patient-designated routing for data aggregation
Prohibit vendor/institutional lock-in
Mandate patient-readable and machine-readable formats
Steven Zecola argues each patient owns his or her data—providers do not own the data despite treating portal information as their own
Dominick Esposito describes CGM data that is not always easily accessible to patients or providers, and certainly not integrated with other information

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Positioning standards as foundational infrastructure for AI, ensuring standards evolve to support AI use cases, and preventing proprietary side channels that bypass established standards. HL7 frames standards investment as enabling a new wave of American healthcare entrepreneurship and cites OECD estimates that interoperable data sharing value is 2.7-6.6% of health system expenditure.
specificPoints: Strategic investment in HL7 standards
Evolve FHIR APIs to align with graph query standards
Develop AI Transparency on FHIR Implementation Guide

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Exclusion from HITECH Act left behavioral health IT infrastructure behind, unstructured documentation and inconsistent data capture, and limited exchange of behavioral health data across systems. Behavioral health providers face compounded disadvantages—excluded from foundational IT investments and now at risk of being excluded again from AI benefits.
specificPoints: Accelerate USCDI+ Behavioral Health implementation
Expand USCDI to include mental health elements
Provide education on interoperability requirements
National Council for Mental Wellbeing notes behavioral health providers were excluded from HITECH Act of 2009, leaving many with lacking IT infrastructure
NeuroFlow warns AI policy must not repeat that exclusion

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance interoperability as the critical gap: Walt Simmons of the Interstitial Institute argues that the most consequential interoperability gap is not data but governance documentation, noting that every institution deploying clinical AI produces governance documentation in locally defined format and the translation cost is enormous and entirely unnecessary. This reframes interoperability as an accountability infrastructure problem.

Medicare data architecture as AI-invisible: David Bynon identifies that CMS plan data is delivered through late-rendered React workflows and not exposed as machine-readable objects, making authoritative government information inaccessible to AI systems. This highlights how even federal data sources fail AI readiness tests.

Operational telemetry as missing layer: Dr. Pal Randhawa, a neurosurgeon, argues current interoperability standards track the patient but ignore the system, proposing that bed capacity and staffing viability data should become a national asset rather than proprietary silo.

Retina imaging fragmentation: ASRS provides detailed evidence that even when using standardized formats, ophthalmology devices fail to standardize crucial metrics reporting (central subfield thickness, GA lesion size, subretinal fluid volume), demonstrating that format compliance alone is insufficient.

Type 1 diabetes data integration: Dominick Esposito, a parent of two children with T1D, notes that CGM data collected in 5-minute intervals is not accessible to many providers or patients and describes community-built apps (Trio) that families use to integrate data the healthcare system cannot.

ADT notification overload: National Council for Mental Wellbeing explains that people transferred between hospital units generate multiple ADT notifications, making it very difficult and time-consuming for outside providers to understand actual final discharge versus unit transfer.

OECD interoperability value estimate: HL7 International cites OECD estimates that the value of enabling interoperable sharing of health data is between 2.70% to 6.64% of health system expenditure, and may be as high as 17%.
Data worker time allocation: Scientix.ai cites research finding data workers spend over 40% of their time searching for, cleaning, and reconciling data rather than analyzing it.
AI project failure rate: Interstella reports studies estimate up to 85% of AI projects fail due to poor data quality or volume issues.

Context-first data infrastructure: Scientix.ai proposes transitioning from application-centric architectures to context-first data infrastructure built on open standards (such as NGSI-LD) where institutional knowledge becomes a persistent, governed asset.

Model Context Protocol integration: MHK suggests MCP integration can accelerate adoption by enabling secure access to clinical data across client environments without requiring data migration or format standardization.

Graph query language alignment: David Rocha recommends FHIR APIs evolve to align with ISO 39075 graph query language standard, enabling relationship-aware interoperability that returns context graphs.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural and under-resourced settings face compounded barriers. AORN notes resource constraints and interoperability issues are major barriers, especially in under-resourced healthcare organizations including rural, urban, and non-academic medical centers. AAFP emphasizes that many members practice in small, independent, rural, and safety-net settings with limited resources.

Post-acute and long-term care data integrity challenges. Renee Pope identifies that documentation structures can produce ambiguous or inconsistent entries undermining data reliability in PALTC settings, with documentation completeness dropping under high workload.

Large health systems vs. startups face different but related challenges. Large systems like Advocate Health and MSK emphasize vendor constraints even with significant resources, while startups like COGA Labs and ScriptChain Health emphasize disproportionate costs of data access.

Specialty practices face unique data challenges that generic interoperability frameworks fail to address. Dental organizations (ADA, DSI), ophthalmology (ASRS), and behavioral health (National Council, NeuroFlow) all describe domain-specific data structures requiring specialized attention.

Technical implementers vs. policy advocates emphasize different solutions. Those with direct data engineering experience like Amir Abrams, Anil Bodepudi, and Leap Orbit emphasize specific technical solutions (schema contracts, provenance tracking, FHIR pipelines), while policy-focused organizations emphasize regulatory enforcement and funding.

Clinician end-users emphasize workflow integration concerns. Medical specialty societies including AAN, ASH, and AASM consistently raise concerns about AI tools creating additional or parallel documentation and duplicative work that burdens physicians.

Interoperability without governance creates new risks. EHY Consulting warns interoperability expands the attack surface and secondary-use risk unless paired with access control, provenance, and enforceable training boundaries.

Billing policies create data attribution problems. AAPA identifies that incident to billing and split/shared visit billing skew assessments of provider care quality, resource utilization, and other value-based care measures, creating inaccurate data regarding both PAs and physicians.
Current payment models create data silos. TapestryHealth explains that regulations prohibiting concurrent RPM/RTM billing create data silos where AI models are blinded to half the patient's context.

keyQuotations.quote: AI systems do not fail because of algorithms; they fail because of data.

The primary barriers to clinical AI adoption are infrastructural, not algorithmic. The AI models themselves have reached impressive capability thresholds. What remains broken is the data substrate they depend on.
Clinical AI fails when deployed into informationally impoverished workflows.
Artificial intelligence will not fix health care unless we fix the data architecture first.
The primary bottleneck to clinical AI is no longer the complexity of the algorithms, but the persistent 'trust friction' created by fragmented, unverified data pipelines.
Interoperability that enables data movement but not data meaning limits AI impact.
It is not enough to move data; the AI must understand it. We need standardized 'Administrative Knowledge Graphs'. If every payer uses a different definition for 'medical necessity,' AI cannot scale.

The remaining challenge is not ambition, but orchestration—aligning data movement, semantic clarity, and AI reasoning into a coherent, auditable workflow that supports longitudinal use and reuse over time.
The central failure mode is not 'no standards exist,' but 'the record is incomplete when and where clinical decisions are made.'
Interoperability has stagnated for more than a decade because true data liquidity would materially weaken the economic moats of incumbent EHR vendors and reduce switching costs for health systems.
CMS provider payment policies heavily incent making data NOT AVAILABLE whether for AI or any pro-competitive purpose.

The receiving EHR system doesn't speak English; it speaks in discrete codes (SNOMED, ICD-10). If every AI vendor reads a .pdf and maps 'heart attack' to a different code, or outputs the data in a proprietary JSON format, the hospital's IT team has to build custom 'plumbing' for every single AI tool.
High data quality is the prerequisite for high algorithmic accuracy, and high accuracy is the prerequisite for patient safety.
If the data is poisoned, the clinical output is compromised.
AI systems that use laboratory data often assume that test results are comparable across different testing sites and methods. In practice, this is often not the case.

The reliability and equity of AI tools hinge on the quality and representativeness of the data used to train these models. This underscores the critical importance of engaging individuals with lived experience of MS, especially from underrepresented communities, throughout the model development process.

Because mental health and substance use providers were left out of the HITECH Act of 2009, that provided significant funding for the adoption of EHRs, many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking.
Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result. AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.

The most consequential interoperability gap in clinical AI is not data interoperability—it is governance interoperability: absence of common standard for what clinical AI accountability documentation looks like and how it moves between institutions, regulators, and payers.
Interoperability is governance infrastructure—surveillance, auditability, contestability, and equity monitoring all depend on ability to access and integrate data across systems.

A strategic investment in HL7 standards would catalyze a new wave of American healthcare entrepreneurship, enabling data-driven and AI-backed solutions to flourish in a competitive marketplace that rewards improved health outcomes.

Navigation software (MapQuest, Google Maps) is far advanced today and used in nearly infinite ways because there are common expectations in data sharing with both human-interpretable and machine-interpretable interfaces. But there is currently no standardized navigation system ('GPS') for healthcare.
Adopting AI without standardizing the underlying data engineering layer will result in fragile systems that fail at scale.
Each patient owns his or her data—providers do not own the data despite treating portal information as their own for research purposes.
Dentistry is one of the largest, most frequently accessed clinical domains in the United States, yet it remains structurally disconnected from the broader healthcare data ecosystem.
The future of healthcare data will define the future of healthcare AI. Rather than adding more disparate systems, we need better data delivered faster, with built-in intelligence and trust.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of technical interoperability challenges, with many providing specific examples, data points, and concrete policy recommendations. Stakeholders across categories engage substantively with the distinction between data movement and data meaning, and debates reflect genuine policy tensions rather than simple opposition.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many comments cite specific statistics (85% AI project failure rate, 40% data worker time on reconciliation, 76% revenue cycle leader integration challenges), reference established standards and frameworks, and provide domain-specific examples. However, some claims lack citation and rely on experiential assertions.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from payers and health plans on data infrastructure issues. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are mentioned but underrepresented in direct comments. Patient voices are present but relatively few compared to industry and professional stakeholders.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The discourse reflects sophisticated understanding of layered interoperability challenges (transport vs. semantic vs. governance), market structure dynamics, and the interdependencies between data infrastructure, AI capability, and clinical outcomes. Multiple commenters distinguish between technical feasibility and structural/economic barriers.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate near-universal agreement that current privacy and security frameworks are inadequate for the AI era, with particular concern about HIPAA's gaps in covering AI vendors, re-identification risks from AI analysis, and the need for updated guidance on data use for model training. The central tension lies between those advocating for stricter protections and consent requirements versus those emphasizing that overly restrictive interpretations already impede beneficial AI development. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to modernize HIPAA guidance specifically for AI contexts—clarifying de-identification standards, Business Associate requirements, and permissible uses—while extending privacy obligations to currently unregulated AI entities handling health data.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters agree that existing HIPAA regulations, while foundational, lack clarity for AI-specific applications and require updated guidance or interpretation. Representative examples include Massive Bio stating HHS should update privacy regulations and guidance to clarify how de-identification rules apply to complex data like genomic or free-text data used in AI, the American Urological Association calling for HIPAA guidance clarifying de-identification and minimum-necessary standards for AI development, validation, and post-deployment monitoring, and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association requesting guidance clarifying BAAs, model-training boundaries, de-identification, and prohibited secondary uses.

exceptions.text: HIMSS EHR Association notes that HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules applicability is clear and consistent with traditional software but acknowledges strategies implementers should deploy to ensure compliance are not always clear.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters agree that AI companies processing health information should be held to the same privacy and security standards as covered entities. Advocate Health argues that technology companies like OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google should adhere to same privacy, security, and compliance standards as traditional healthcare providers. AAPA states AI technology vendors must be treated as HIPAA-covered entities with all relevant patient protections incorporated. Health IT End-Users Alliance emphasizes AI companies dealing with health data should be held to same standards as HIPAA-covered entities.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority (approximately 25+ across all stakeholder types)

exceptions.text: American Clinical Laboratory Association cautions against disrupting the current balance, noting that de-identified PHI can be used unrestricted by HIPAA, including to develop and train AI models, and that this has enabled significant medical advances.

consensusPoints.text: Most commenters addressing technical privacy issues acknowledge that AI capabilities have fundamentally changed re-identification risk calculations. AORN notes that with as few as four pieces of data, a person may be reidentified, with that data not being exclusive to the items identified in HIPAA safe harbor provisions. Waymark observes that since 2012, AI techniques have demonstrated ability to re-identify individuals from datasets previously considered de-identified and infer sensitive attributes from non-sensitive data patterns. Cyberhill Partners warns that AI systems can generate or infer Protected Health Information even from de-identified inputs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing technical privacy issues (approximately 15+)

consensusPoints.text: Virtually all commenters recognize that patient trust is essential for AI adoption and that privacy concerns are a primary barrier to that trust. Massive Bio identifies that the foremost patient concern is 'What happens to my data?' COGA Labs reports that data access and control consistently ranks as top concern across their user base—ahead of accuracy, cost, or usability. American Academy of Sleep Medicine notes that without strong privacy protections, both patients and providers may hesitate to participate in AI-enabled systems.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Privacy Protections for AI Model Training
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether to maintain current de-identification permissions to enable AI innovation or strengthen standards to address AI-driven re-identification capabilities.

positions.label: Preserve De-identification Pathway
positions.stance: Clinical laboratories, some technology companies, and research institutions argue for maintaining current de-identification permissions to enable AI innovation. American Clinical Laboratory Association emphasizes that these advancements would not have been possible without de-identification permissions.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5-7 commenters
keyArguments: De-identified data has enabled exponential AI-enabled medicine advancements
Disrupting the balance would harm innovation without proportionate privacy benefit
OCR guidance already supports secondary use for research and life sciences

positions.label: Strengthen De-identification Standards
positions.stance: Most healthcare providers, advocacy groups, and professional associations support updating rules to address AI-driven re-identification capabilities. AORN, Waymark, and Cyberhill Partners have raised specific technical concerns about current standards.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20+ commenters
keyArguments: AI can re-identify from datasets previously considered safe
Current safe harbor list is insufficient given AI inference capabilities
Expert determination method needs updating for AI context

areasOfDebate.topic: Consent Requirements for AI Data Use
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether patients must affirmatively consent to AI processing of their data versus providing guidance on when existing HIPAA permissions apply to AI.

positions.label: Explicit Consent Required
positions.stance: Patient advocacy groups, mental health providers, and privacy-focused organizations argue patients must affirmatively consent to AI processing. Citizens' Council for Health Freedom warns that AI will have access to data without patient consent for purposes the patient may not support.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters
keyArguments: AI will access data without patient consent for purposes patients may not support
Consent is especially critical for sensitive behavioral health information
Current HIPAA permits sharing without meaningful patient control

positions.label: Clarified Permitted Uses
positions.stance: Health systems, EHR vendors, and technology companies advocate for guidance on when existing HIPAA permissions apply to AI. University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT notes that current regulations permit PHI use for treatment, payment, and healthcare operations but make no mention of cloud-based processing, ambient transcription, or perpetual AI model refinement.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-18 commenters
keyArguments: Treatment, payment, and healthcare operations permissions should extend to AI
Overly restrictive interpretations already impede beneficial AI
Clear guidance would reduce institutional variation and enable innovation

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Approach to AI Vendor Oversight
areasOfDebate.description: Debate between extending HIPAA coverage to AI vendors versus standardizing and enforcing existing Business Associate requirements.

positions.label: Extend HIPAA Coverage
positions.stance: Healthcare providers, professional associations, and advocacy groups support bringing AI vendors under HIPAA as covered entities or strengthening BA requirements. Advocate Health, AAFP, and NeuroFlow have called for extending coverage to AI vendors handling sensitive data.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20+ commenters
keyArguments: Many AI developers operate outside HIPAA scope while handling sensitive data
Current BA framework is insufficient—vendors use contract terms to override protections
Patients have no recourse when non-covered entities misuse data

positions.label: Standardize Existing Requirements
positions.stance: Some technology companies and EHR vendors argue for clarifying and enforcing current BA obligations rather than expanding coverage. HIMSS EHR Association and University of Kansas Health System emphasize the need for guidance on compliance strategies and standardized BAAs.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5-8 commenters
keyArguments: HIPAA applicability is already clear for traditional software
Industry needs guidance on compliance strategies, not new requirements
Standardized BAAs would address vendor contracting concerns

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between prioritizing protections even if they slow AI deployment versus enabling innovation through clear guidance while maintaining protections.

positions.label: Privacy-First Approach
positions.stance: Mental health providers, patient advocates, and some physicians prioritize protections even if they slow AI deployment. Pennsylvania Medical Society and an anonymous psychologist emphasize that patient safety related to data protection cannot be compromised for expediency.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 12-15 commenters
keyArguments: Patient safety related to data protection cannot be compromised for expediency
The rush to deploy healthcare AI outpaces privacy infrastructure
Confidentiality is the structural condition that makes treatment possible

positions.label: Balanced Enablement
positions.stance: Health systems, technology companies, and clearinghouses argue clear guidance can enable innovation while maintaining protections. Interstella, Ellipsis Health, and software architect Amir Abrams note that overly restrictive interpretations limit AI functionality even when safeguards exist.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 18-20 commenters
keyArguments: Overly restrictive interpretations limit AI functionality even when safeguards exist
Clear guidelines reduce fear that sharing data for AI equals privacy violation
A poorly secured fragmented environment is not safer than well-governed convergence

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor contracting practices that shift liability; unclear guidance creating institutional variation; cybersecurity threats to patient safety
specificPoints: Direct experience with vendors refusing standard BAAs or using contract terms to override HIPAA restrictions
Face operational paralysis from ambiguous compliance requirements
University of Kansas Health System reports many AI vendors use opaque contract practices or refuse to sign standard BAAs
CommonSpirit Health emphasizes understanding precisely how AI vendors handle sensitive PHI to monitor HIPAA compliance
Proposed solutions include standardized BAAs, incentive-based cybersecurity policy, and clear guidance on acceptable AI architectures

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory ambiguity creating implementation paralysis; need for clear safe harbors for privacy-preserving architectures; guidance on permissible uses for model training
specificPoints: Technical understanding of privacy-preserving approaches including federated learning, differential privacy, and cryptographic proofs
Awareness that architectural privacy may exceed contractual privacy
Penguin AI proposes specific safe harbor for Privacy-by-Design AI architectures using automated de-identification and edge-processing
FERZ AI notes cryptographic proof chains may exceed current compliance expectations
Proposed solutions include safe harbors for privacy-by-design architectures, guidance on de-identification for AI training, and model contract clauses covering data use limits

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member liability exposure; patient trust erosion; cybersecurity vulnerabilities including prompt injection and PHI leakage
specificPoints: Represent frontline clinicians who must integrate AI while facing unresolved liability and privacy questions
See both clinical benefits and risks
AMA warns expanded API access increases attack surface and magnifies risk of inappropriate secondary use
ACC raises concern about quantum computing and advanced computational techniques threatening to nullify existing privacy and security techniques
Proposed solutions include HIPAA guidance on AI-specific issues, extending privacy obligations to non-covered entities, and clear accountability frameworks

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient autonomy and data ownership; protection of vulnerable populations; ensuring AI doesn't create loopholes weakening privacy
specificPoints: Focus on patient rights and consent
Particular concern about mental health data sensitivity
Emphasis on enforcement mechanisms
Global Liver Institute emphasizes patients should retain ownership of their personal health data
Psychotherapy Action Network notes AI chatbots (mental health specific or generalized models) are not HIPAA compliant
Proposed solutions include establishing data protection standards for federally-supported programs, national privacy standards for AI chatbots, and restoring medical privacy rights

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Privacy-preserving approaches for AI research; public trust in AI; technical solutions like federated learning
specificPoints: Research-oriented focus on technical solutions
Awareness of international approaches
Concern about public reluctance to adopt AI
Computing Research Association notes notable public reluctance in the U.S. to adopt AI for health-related outcomes due to high stakes and privacy concerns
Satyadhar Joshi recommends federated learning architectures enable privacy-preserving AI
Proposed solutions include support for privacy-preserving cryptography, federated learning architectures, and updated de-identification guidance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Heightened sensitivity of behavioral health data; re-identification risks for mental health information; therapeutic relationship integrity
specificPoints: Confidentiality as foundational to treatment effectiveness, not merely a compliance requirement
Particular vulnerability of their patient populations
An anonymous psychologist states confidentiality is not a preference—it is the structural condition that makes treatment possible
Mark O'Brien warns even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification or misuse
Proposed solutions include enforceable federal privacy standards before AI expansion in behavioral health and clear standards for data storage and secondary use

noteworthyInsights.insight: EHY Consulting offers a useful framework distinguishing between privacy (who can see my data), sovereignty (who determines what my data means and how much it is worth), and security (who has access to my data and what they can do with it). This highlights that current frameworks address privacy and security but not 'learning accumulation'—how data becomes compounding capability.

Software architect Amir Abrams challenges the assumption that distributed data is inherently safer: a poorly secured fragmented environment is not inherently safer than a well-governed convergence model; it is simply less visible and less accountable. This reframes the centralization debate.

Penguin AI distinguishes between architectural privacy (scrubbing data at the edge) rather than contractual privacy (BAAs alone), suggesting that technical approaches may provide stronger protections than legal agreements.

TapestryHealth describes how contactless radar monitoring addresses the tension between safety and dignity in SNF settings: families want the safety of continuous monitoring ('never alone'), but residents fear the indignity of cameras ('Big Brother'). This illustrates how technology design can resolve apparent privacy trade-offs.

1upHealth raises a provocative critique that this end run around HIPAA is so brittle that TEFCA participants—the same QHINs which are supposed to vet other participants—are currently engaged in multiple lawsuits around their wholesale misappropriation of patient data. This suggests current interoperability approaches may have fundamental security flaws.

COGA Labs reports that in product development research, data access and control consistently ranks as top concern across their user base—ahead of accuracy, cost, or usability. This empirical finding challenges assumptions about what patients prioritize.

Christopher Woodall notes that while encryption technologies exist, they impose 100-1000x computational overhead, making clinical deployment infeasible. This technical constraint explains why current AI systems process data in plaintext.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Multiple commenters including Cooperative Exchange, Bethany Russell, and Waymark note that inconsistent state and federal requirements create compliance uncertainty and impede multi-state scaling. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association and Pennsylvania Medical Society provide state-level perspectives emphasizing need for federal clarity.

Large health systems like CommonSpirit, Advocate Health, and WellSpan focus on vendor contracting and governance frameworks, while smaller and specialized providers including mental health practices and SNFs emphasize sector-specific privacy concerns and resource constraints. Academic medical centers like University of Pennsylvania and University of Kansas highlight institutional variation in interpreting HIPAA for AI.

Mental health providers consistently emphasize heightened sensitivity of behavioral health data and confidentiality as foundational to treatment. Laboratories like ACLA uniquely defend current de-identification permissions as enabling innovation. Sleep medicine raises concerns about data used for DME prescriptions and chronic disease management.

Commenters with technical backgrounds including software architects, AI vendors, and cybersecurity professionals are more likely to propose specific technical solutions such as federated learning, cryptographic proofs, and edge processing. Clinical providers more likely to focus on governance, consent, and vendor accountability. Both groups agree on the problem but differ on solution approaches.

Multiple unintended consequences identified: Ellipsis Health notes overly restrictive interpretations limiting beneficial AI even when safeguards exist; University of Pennsylvania identifies institutional variation creating discontinuities between health systems; Penguin AI describes implementation paralysis from ambiguous compliance requirements; ACLA warns of innovation harm if de-identification balance disrupted.

keyQuotations.quote: The rush to deploy healthcare AI outpaces our privacy infrastructure.

AI will have access to this data, also without patient consent, for purposes the patient may not support, and potentially to directly impact the patient's care in ways that could be harmful, insufficient, or generated through AI hallucinations.
AI systems can generate or infer Protected Health Information even from de-identified inputs, and the current regulatory framework does not adequately address this risk.
The HIPAA rule, properly understood, is a data security rule rather than a privacy rule. It permits confidential patient data to be shared broadly without patient consent by 'covered entities.'
With as few as four (4) pieces of data, a person may be reidentified, with that data not being exclusive to the items identified in paragraphs (b)(2)(i)(A) through (b)(2)(i)(R).

This end run around HIPAA is so brittle that TEFCA participants – the same QHINs which are supposed to vet other participants – are currently engaged in multiple lawsuits around their wholesale misappropriation of patient data.
Confidentiality is not a preference — it is the structural condition that makes treatment possible.
Families want the safety of continuous monitoring ('never alone'), but residents fear the indignity of cameras ('Big Brother').
Privacy: who can see my data. Sovereignty: who determines what my data means and how much it is worth. Security: who has access to my data and what they can do with it.
A poorly secured fragmented environment is not inherently safer than a well-governed convergence model; it is simply less visible and less accountable.
When trust in data security and privacy is degraded, the flow of information halts.
Patient safety, in this instance related to the protection of health data, cannot be compromised for the sake of expediency. The potential negative consequences are too great.
Without this 'transparent-yet-secure' identification standard, hospitals are forced to choose between HIPAA-compliant encryption and the network visibility required for basic patient safety.
These advancements would not have been possible without de-identification permissions.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of both technical privacy challenges and regulatory frameworks. Debate is substantive with specific citations to HIPAA provisions, technical capabilities, and real-world implementation challenges. Multiple stakeholder types engage constructively with opposing viewpoints.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific technical research on re-identification risks, reference particular HIPAA provisions and OCR guidance, and provide concrete examples from operational experience. Some claims about AI capabilities and risks would benefit from additional empirical support.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients themselves as direct commenters; most patient perspectives are mediated through advocacy organizations or healthcare providers. Also limited input from smaller AI startups and international perspectives on privacy frameworks.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves intricate interplay between technical capabilities (AI re-identification, privacy-preserving computation), legal frameworks (HIPAA coverage, BA requirements), operational realities (vendor contracting, implementation costs), and ethical considerations (patient autonomy, trust). Solutions require balancing innovation enablement against protection strengthening.

6.themeDescription: Reimbursement, Payment Policy, and Economic Incentives. This theme encompasses all comments addressing how payment structures affect AI adoption, including fee-for-service limitations, value-based payment opportunities, and specific coding and coverage pathways. || It includes concerns about misaligned incentives under current payment models, proposals for new CPT codes or payment mechanisms, debates about value-based payment as an enabler of AI adoption, and discussions of coverage determination processes. This theme does NOT include implementation costs (covered under Resource Barriers) or liability concerns (covered separately)

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that current fee-for-service payment models fundamentally misalign incentives for AI adoption, with nearly all stakeholders (approximately 95% of relevant comments) identifying payment policy as a primary—often the primary—barrier to AI implementation even when clinical value is demonstrated. The central tension lies between those advocating for new AI-specific billing codes and add-on payments versus those warning that such approaches risk repeating the mistakes of EHR adoption by incentivizing volume over value. The dominant recommendation thrust favors accelerating value-based payment models while creating transitional mechanisms (pilots, time-limited grants, demonstration projects) to bridge the gap until outcome-aligned payment becomes standard.

consensusPoints.text: Fee-for-service payment fundamentally misaligns incentives for AI adoption. Nearly all commenters agree that fee-for-service reimbursement creates structural disincentives for AI tools that improve efficiency, prevent adverse events, or reduce utilization. Waymark, Inc. notes that fee-for-service reimbursement creates perverse incentives that discourage investment in AI systems that reduce unnecessary utilization, even when such reductions improve patient outcomes and lower costs. Radiology Business Management Association observes that if AI improves efficiency or reduces interpretive effort, it may paradoxically threaten reimbursement, discouraging adoption even when patient care improves. Dr. Bethany Russell adds that AI tools that improve efficiency reduce billable time, disincentivizing adoption.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 50 of 53 addressing payment models)

exceptions.text: HealthScoreAI argues against any AI-specific reimbursement, contending that if AI delivers value, it should manifest in reduced costs rather than additional billable services.

consensusPoints.text: Organizations investing in AI are not capturing its financial benefits. A strong majority of commenters identify the split incentive problem where costs accrue to providers while savings flow to payers or the broader system. Health AI Institute repeatedly observes that the organizations investing in AI are not those capturing its financial benefits, undermining sustainability even when clinical value is evident. NeuroFlow notes that preventing a psychiatric hospitalization or reducing emergency department utilization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than the providers who made the investment. TapestryHealth explains that AI requires upfront Capital Expense (sensors), but savings (reduced hospitalizations) accrue to Payer (Medicare), not Facility.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 40 of 53)

consensusPoints.text: Reimbursement uncertainty is blocking adoption even when clinical value is proven. Nearly all stakeholder types agree that unclear payment pathways prevent investment decisions regardless of demonstrated clinical benefit. Massive Bio notes that the absence of reimbursement pathways is repeatedly cited by providers as a barrier to investing in AI. HIMSS EHR Association states that without clear payment mechanisms, even highly effective tools may struggle to gain traction among financially constrained or operationally stretched providers. Pictor Labs observes that pathology laboratories hesitate to adopt AI-driven technology due to reimbursement uncertainty.

Value-based payment models are better aligned with AI's value proposition. Most commenters support accelerating the transition to value-based care as the structural solution to payment misalignment. SonderMind states that value-based care models are naturally aligned with AI's ability to improve outcomes and reduce costs. RBMA notes that broader adoption of value-based payment models such as bundled payments would allow providers to capture financial benefits of higher-quality, more efficient care enabled by AI. NeuroFlow observes that when payment rewards prevention and integration, adoption of high-value clinical decision support follows naturally.

Current coding and coverage pathways move too slowly for AI innovation. Strong agreement across developers, providers, and trade associations that existing CPT code development and coverage determination processes cannot keep pace with technology advancement. Pictor Labs notes that current approaches for coding and coverage determination move more slowly than technology advancement, hampering confidence in reimbursement necessary for bringing innovation to market. American Cancer Society CAN cites an average 5.7-year delay between FDA approval and Medicare coverage of new technologies. Cellworks Group identifies current barriers creating 2-3 year delays in payer coverage per algorithmic service.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Strong agreement across developers, providers, and trade associations

areasOfDebate.topic: Should AI receive dedicated billing codes or add-on payments?
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to create specific reimbursement mechanisms for AI-enabled services versus avoiding standalone AI billing to prevent volume-over-value incentives.

positions.label: Pro-Codes Position
positions.stance: Create specific reimbursement mechanisms for AI-enabled services. Massive Bio advocates for developing new CPT codes or modifiers for AI-assisted procedures and decision support. American Society of Retina Specialists proposes billing modifier codes for AI-assisted image interpretation, similar to radiology's CADx tools. American College of Radiology suggests Medicare could create add-on payments or new RVU components tied to validated AI use.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25-30 commenters, primarily AI developers, specialty societies, and some health systems
keyArguments: Without dedicated payment, providers cannot justify AI investment costs
Add-on payments would recognize additional physician work validating AI outputs
Specific codes enable data collection on AI utilization and outcomes
Permanent (not time-limited) payments needed for sustainable adoption

positions.label: Anti-Codes Position
positions.stance: Avoid standalone AI billing to prevent volume-over-value incentives. HealthScoreAI argues HHS should avoid establishing separate reimbursement for use of AI. American Academy of Physician Associates warns that premature creation of AI-specific billing codes, modifiers, or payment add-ons risks incentivizing volume over value. Heart Failure Society of America notes that per-alert or per-use incentives may drive volume without value and should be avoided.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, including physician associations and some health systems
keyArguments: Premature AI-specific codes risk repeating EHR adoption mistakes that optimized billing without improving outcomes
Add-on billing historically contributed to spending growth without commensurate outcome improvements
AI value should manifest in reduced costs and improved efficiency, not additional billable services
Per-use incentives may drive volume without value

areasOfDebate.topic: Should AI payments come from the Medicare Physician Fee Schedule?
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI payments should be budget-neutral within MPFS or funded through separate mechanisms.

positions.label: Separate Funding Position
positions.stance: AI payments should not be budget-neutral within MPFS. American Medical Association argues that payment for digital health and AI-enabled technologies should be paid outside of the MPFS. Memorial Sloan Kettering states that CMS should not allocate AI-related payments from budget-neutral Medicare Physician Fee Schedule.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from AMA, specialty societies, and academic medical centers (approximately 10-12 explicit mentions)
keyArguments: Budget neutrality would require decreased reimbursement for other physician services
AI costs could be very significant as increasing numbers come to market
Current MPFS already strained by embedded medical supply costs ($1.53 billion)
Need new benefit category or alternative payment approach

positions.label: Integrated Payment Position
positions.stance: Work within existing payment structures. American Academy of Physician Associates prioritizes reimbursement rewarding outcomes, efficiency, and total cost of care through value-based, population-based, and shared-savings payment models.
positions.supportLevel: Smaller group, primarily those favoring value-based approaches
keyArguments: Creating separate payment streams adds complexity
AI should be treated as practice expense input when it functions as such
Focus should be on outcome-based payment rather than new fee categories

areasOfDebate.topic: Disease-specific vs. Modality-based reimbursement for AI diagnostics
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to reimburse AI at the diagnostic modality level or maintain condition-specific coding.

positions.label: Modality-Based Position
positions.stance: Reimburse AI at the diagnostic modality level. Echo IQ argues that a modality-based reimbursement framework would better align with CMS goals of encouraging high-value technology adoption.
positions.supportLevel: Supported by diagnostic AI developers and some specialty societies
keyArguments: Disease-level reimbursement requires repeated coding efforts as new validated outputs are added
AI platforms often detect multiple conditions across a single modality
Reduces administrative friction and speeds innovation

positions.label: Disease-Specific Position
positions.stance: Maintain condition-specific coding. American Academy of Neurology states that coverage and reimbursement should be supported by evidence of measurable improvements in patient outcomes, safety, workflow impacts.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in current CMS approach; some specialty societies prefer granular coding
keyArguments: Enables tracking of specific clinical applications
Allows evidence requirements tailored to each indication
Prevents payment for unvalidated uses

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to require governance documentation as a condition for AI reimbursement.

positions.label: Pro-Attestation Position
positions.stance: Require governance documentation for AI reimbursement. FERZ AI recommends that for AI-assisted clinical workflows in high-risk categories, governance attestation artifacts should be required as condition of reimbursement. SANCIAN LLC advocates for readiness before reimbursement—requiring organizations to demonstrate governance maturity as a prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives. Amir Abrams notes that if reimbursement is conditioned on verified delivery, interoperability shifts from an enforcement problem to an operational norm.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily governance-focused consultants and some health systems
keyArguments: Creates market incentive for governance adoption
Enables post-payment integrity verification
Shifts from enforcement problem to operational norm

positions.label: Cautious Position
positions.stance: Governance requirements could burden smaller providers. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association calls for equity adjustments for rural/safety-net providers in any demonstration programs.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit concern from rural/safety-net provider advocates
keyArguments: Additional compliance requirements disadvantage under-resourced organizations
Should be phased or voluntary initially
Risk of creating barriers to adoption for those who need AI most

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Productivity gains under fee-for-service would be financially punitive; upfront AI costs without clear ROI pathway; uncompensated clinician time validating AI outputs. Providers emphasize the operational reality that AI often increases short-term workload even when improving long-term outcomes. They experience the self-defeating cycle where efficiency gains reduce revenue.
specificPoints: WellSpan Health notes that any productivity gains that result from the use of AI would be punitive under the fee-for-service models, creating a self-defeating cycle to the adoption of AI
TapestryHealth states that until payment models reward Capacity (patients managed safely) rather than Minutes (time spent documenting), innovation will remain a financial liability for LTC providers

Proposed solutions include outcomes-oriented payment pathways acknowledging throughput gains, add-on payments for validated AI assistance, and shared savings models that return benefits to investing facilities

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reimbursement uncertainty blocking commercialization and venture funding; lengthy coverage determination timelines (2-5+ years); split incentives where their customers (providers) don't capture value. Developers emphasize the catch-22 where payers wait for outcomes evidence before covering services, but evidence cannot be generated at scale without coverage. They also highlight that benefits often appear first as soft metrics (time saved, clinician experience) before hard financial ROI.
specificPoints: Cellworks Group notes that payers wait for outcomes evidence before covering services, creating a catch-22
Epic Systems observes that traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding; time-based grants would encourage organizations to scale quickly
SanLuca AI explains that benefits often show up first as time saved, throughput, and clinician experience, while financial ROI can lag or shift across stakeholders

Proposed solutions include expedited coverage pathways for validated AI, temporary/provisional reimbursement codes with sunset provisions, CMAA framework implementation for algorithmic services, and time-based grants rather than amount-based grants

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member practices cannot build viable financial models amid reimbursement uncertainty; small/independent practices face disproportionate cost barriers; AI efficiency gains could paradoxically reduce RVUs and payment. Professional societies bring the perspective of their diverse membership, noting that what works for large academic centers may be infeasible for community practices. They also manage coding systems (AMA/CPT) and understand the technical constraints of current payment infrastructure.
specificPoints: American Medical Association advocates for a new benefit category or alternative approach whereby digital health tools and AI-enabled technologies are paid separately from MPFS to preserve payment for physician services while providing clear, consistent pathway to payment
Radiology Business Management Association warns that without payment reform, AI risks becoming a victim of its own success
Proposed solutions include permanent (not time-limited) add-on payments, grants for small/independent practices, new benefit categories outside budget-neutral MPFS, and tariff exemptions for AI tools

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payment models that don't reward safe implementation; need for evidence generation infrastructure; governance and monitoring costs not recognized. Academic institutions emphasize the need for payment to recognize not just AI use but the substantial work of validation, monitoring, and governance. They also note that payment policy shapes behavior more powerfully than regulation.
