specificPoints: Santa Clara University Markkula Center notes that if AI improves quality but requires additional clinician effort to verify safely, organizations may be penalized financially
University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT identifies that the most consistent challenge to adoption is compensation and workflow alignment
Proposed solutions include CMMI demonstration models for AI, payment for clinician training and governance infrastructure, and billing mechanisms for time spent validating algorithmic outputs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient out-of-pocket costs for AI-enabled services; equitable access to AI benefits regardless of ability to pay; coverage delays preventing access to beneficial technologies. Patient advocates focus on the downstream impact of payment policy on access and affordability, noting that coverage decisions determine whether patients can actually receive AI-enabled care.
specificPoints: American Cancer Society CAN emphasizes that it is vital that CMS ensures viable, timely coverage and reimbursement pathways to prevent cost from becoming barrier to care
American Parkinson Disease Association states that HHS should identify opportunities to reduce patient costs for AI tools

Proposed solutions include timely coverage pathways to prevent cost barriers, ensuring benefits distributed based on patient need not ability to pay, and identifying opportunities to reduce patient costs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Structural friction preventing AI adoption; misalignment between where AI creates value and where payment flows; need for governance-linked incentives. Consultants observe patterns across multiple organizations and can identify systemic barriers. They emphasize that adoption stalls due to structural, financial, and organizational friction rather than model performance.
specificPoints: Lumenex Advisory observes that adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction
EHY Consulting recommends distinguishing value created for patients and providers from value transferred to vendors through learning accumulation from operational data

Proposed solutions include aligning reimbursement with real-world implementation costs, incentives recognizing operational effort (training, governance, workflow integration), and pilot programs and demonstration models

noteworthyInsights.insight: The AI Doctor paradox: TapestryHealth crystallizes the fundamental tension that AI technology has matured to the point where it can predict hospitalizations 5 days in advance, but current payment models actively penalize providers for using it. This captures how payment misalignment blocks adoption of demonstrably beneficial technology.

Time-based vs. amount-based grants: Epic Systems offers a counterintuitive insight that traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding while time-based grants encourage rapid adoption. Their data shows Jump Start customers demonstrated 45% month-over-month AI utilization growth vs. 18% for non-participants.

The data ingestion tax: Leap Orbit identifies that the greatest administrative burden is not AI itself but the cost of cleaning dirty data—suggesting payment policy should reward AI-Ready infrastructure rather than just AI use.

Dentistry's structural barrier: Dental Standards Institute notes that dentistry's treatment-based reimbursement structure has never strongly incentivized diagnosis capture, creating a fundamental barrier to AI adoption that differs from medical care.

The autonomous diabetic retinopathy screening lesson: Multiple commenters reference that the first FDA-cleared AI device has seen limited uptake despite clinical validation—demonstrating that regulatory approval without reimbursement alignment is insufficient.

Behavioral health's VBC gap: SonderMind notes that VBC adoption in behavioral health remains significantly lower than in physical medicine, leaving innovators without a viable commercial pathway—highlighting a sector-specific barrier.

The fraud vector concern: Writerslogic raises an underappreciated risk that without verifiable authorship, one cannot reliably establish whether documentation submitted for Medicare or Medicaid reimbursement reflects genuine clinical judgment or automated output.

Consumer frustration as policy driver: An individual consumer expresses willingness to opt out of this nonsense and take ChatGPT plus generics from India any day—illustrating how payment system dysfunction may drive patients toward unregulated alternatives.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural/safety-net providers consistently express concern about being left behind, with Advocate Health, Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association, Community Care Cooperative, and VieCure noting that AI adoption costs are disproportionately burdensome for under-resourced facilities.

Post-acute and long-term care settings face unique challenges: TapestryHealth and Renee Pope note that the low-margin SNF sector is particularly sensitive to ROI uncertainty and that frequent ownership turnover disrupts long-horizon planning.

Community oncology practices face specific barriers: VieCure notes that high upfront and ongoing costs of AI platforms, integration, and compliance disproportionately strain community practices operating on thin margins.

Radiology has the most developed AI ecosystem but faces the paradox that efficiency gains threaten RVU-based payment, as noted by American College of Radiology and Radiology Business Management Association.

Pathology faces coding pathway uncertainty after recent PLA guidance changes eliminated options for algorithmic-only services, as noted by Digital Pathology Association, California Clinical Laboratory Association, and Cellworks Group.
Behavioral health lacks value-based payment penetration and faces unique reimbursement gaps for continuous care models, as noted by SonderMind, National Council, and NeuroFlow.
Retina specialists note that the first FDA-cleared AI device has seen limited uptake due to inadequate reimbursement, as observed by American Society of Retina Specialists.

AI efficiency gains reducing revenue: Multiple commenters note that AI making visits more efficient causes clinicians to bill for shorter time intervals with lower payment, as observed by Providence, RBMA, and TapestryHealth.

Innovation migrating away from Medicare beneficiaries: California Clinical Laboratory Association warns that absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries, exacerbating disparities in access.
Semantic inconsistency driving appeals: David Bynon notes that AI-generated explanations trigger low-dollar appeals where adjudication cost exceeds claim value.

keyQuotations.quote: The 'AI Doctor' will not work for free—and neither can the human clinicians who supervise them.
Without payment reform, AI risks becoming a victim of its own success.
Current payment models represent the single greatest barrier to AI adoption in clinical care.
We repeatedly observe that the organizations investing in AI are not those capturing its financial benefits, undermining sustainability even when clinical value is evident.

Preventing a psychiatric hospitalization or reducing emergency department utilization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than the providers who made the investment.
If AI improves efficiency or reduces interpretive effort, it may paradoxically threaten reimbursement, discouraging adoption even when patient care improves.
Any productivity gains that result from the use of AI would be punitive under the fee-for-service models, creating a self-defeating cycle to the adoption of AI.
Payers wait for outcomes evidence before covering services (creating a catch-22).

Absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries, exacerbating disparities in access and limiting innovation within federally funded programs.
The AAFP believes that the fee-for-service (FFS) model will continue to drive the creation of AI tools to maximize utilization, instead of AI tools to actually improve patient care.
If reimbursement is conditioned on verified delivery... interoperability shifts from an enforcement problem to an operational norm.

Under current rules, AI tools are typically treated as practice expense inputs or bundled into existing CPT/HCPCS codes, regardless of whether they materially enhance diagnostic accuracy, reduce variation, or support improved clinical outcomes. This approach effectively treats AI the same as commodity software, even when the technology performs clinically meaningful tasks.
This time-intensive oversight represents uncompensated labor and shifts costs from technology developers to frontline providers.

Without aligned incentives, advances will concentrate in large systems, widening disparities and gaps in care within community care and thereby jeopardizing the timeliness and quality of care received by the majority of US citizens diagnosed with cancer seen in the community oncology setting.
Adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction.
Let me opt out of this nonsense, I'll take ChatGPT + generics from India any day over what we have.

Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding; time-based grants would encourage organizations to scale quickly, assess value, and determine which use cases merit continued investment.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of payment policy mechanics, with stakeholders providing specific evidence, quantified data, and concrete policy recommendations. The debate between pro-codes and anti-codes positions reflects genuine policy tension rather than simple advocacy.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many comments cite specific data points (5.7-year FDA-to-coverage delay, 2-3 year per-service delays, $5-10M duplicative evidence costs, 25% administrative waste). However, some claims about AI benefits remain aspirational rather than evidence-based. The 45% vs 18% utilization growth comparison from Epic provides concrete outcome data.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct patient voice in the record; most patient perspective comes through advocacy organizations. Small/independent practice perspective is represented primarily through professional societies rather than direct comment. Payer perspective is notably absent—no major commercial insurers or Medicare Advantage plans submitted comments on payment policy.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High complexity reflecting genuine policy dilemmas: the tension between creating AI-specific incentives (which could repeat EHR mistakes) versus relying on value-based payment transformation (which is proceeding slowly) represents a real strategic choice without clear right answer. The split incentive problem and budget neutrality constraints are structural issues without easy solutions.

7.themeDescription: Evaluation, Validation, and Evidence Standards. This theme encompasses all comments addressing how AI systems should be tested and validated before and after deployment, including pre-deployment evaluation, post-market monitoring, and evidence generation requirements. || It includes debates about appropriate validation methodologies, proposals for shared evaluation infrastructure, concerns about inadequate real-world evidence, and discussions of how to assess AI performance across diverse populations. This theme does NOT include regulatory approval processes (covered under Regulatory Framework) or transparency requirements (covered separately)

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that current AI evaluation practices are inadequate for ensuring real-world safety and effectiveness, with nearly universal agreement that post-deployment monitoring is as critical as pre-deployment testing. The central tension lies between those advocating for rigorous, standardized evaluation frameworks (particularly in behavioral health and high-risk clinical applications) and those warning against overly prescriptive requirements that could stifle innovation. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to invest in shared evaluation infrastructure, standardized benchmarks, and real-world evidence generation rather than mandating specific testing protocols—with particular emphasis on addressing the gap between controlled validation studies and actual clinical performance across diverse populations and settings.

consensusPoints.text: Post-deployment monitoring is essential, not optional. Nearly all commenters emphasize that evaluation cannot stop at deployment—ongoing monitoring for drift, bias, and real-world performance is critical to patient safety. Onboard AI states that post-deployment approaches should include monitoring for performance drift, bias signals, and safety events with defined triggers for reassessment. Dr. Akshaya Bhagavathula notes that pre-market evidence is insufficient to ensure safety at scale, which is why pharmacovigilance, device post-market surveillance, and public health monitoring systems exist. Ellipsis Health emphasizes that post-deployment evaluation is the cornerstone of patient safety.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 50+ of 70 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: Epic Systems notes that AI performance is sensitive to workflow integration, so post-deployment validation cannot be outsourced in practice—emphasizing local responsibility over centralized monitoring.

consensusPoints.text: Real-world performance differs significantly from controlled testing. A strong majority of commenters explicitly state that AI tools validated in controlled environments frequently fail to perform equivalently in diverse clinical settings. Massive Bio notes that AI algorithms have shown degraded accuracy when deployed on different patient populations or different hospitals than they were trained on. Newton's Tree observes that an AI product that works well in one healthcare organisation may not work well in another, geographically close organisation. The Markkula Center for Applied Ethics emphasizes that performance in development environments does not guarantee effectiveness in live care settings. HealthScoreAI notes that widely cited industry-sponsored evaluations claimed certain AI systems outperform physicians by large margins, but when underlying methodologies were reviewed, studies often rely on artificial test conditions rather than real-time clinical workflows.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (40+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: Shared evaluation infrastructure is needed. Most stakeholders across all categories support HHS investment in shared benchmarks, testing datasets, and evaluation frameworks rather than requiring each organization to develop its own. The Health AI Institute proposes creating an AI Evaluation Commons as a shared national resource supporting standardized benchmarks, AI-relevant data standards, post-deployment performance metrics, and failure-mode reporting. The American Urological Association suggests a nationwide network of AI assurance laboratories, supported but not operated by HHS, that would allow industry-driven testing results to be shared transparently and reused across health systems. The HIMSS EHR Association recommends HHS could add value by funding development of common benchmarks, publishing reporting templates for industry consideration, and supporting shared voluntary testbeds.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most stakeholders across all categories (35+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: Subgroup performance and bias testing must be mandatory. Nearly all commenters addressing equity agree that AI tools must be validated across demographic subgroups, with transparent reporting of differential performance. The Radiology Business Management Association calls for pre-deployment validation using diverse, representative datasets and post-deployment monitoring for model drift, error rates, and performance across demographic groups. The Dental Standards Institute advocates for mandatory subgroup performance reporting, post-deployment monitoring expectations and drift detection requirements. The Computing Research Association notes that while 65% of hospitals use predictive models, less than half evaluate these tools for potential bias.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing equity (30+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: Technical accuracy alone is insufficient. A strong consensus holds that evaluation must extend beyond algorithmic performance metrics to include workflow integration, cognitive load, usability, and clinical outcomes. SANCIAN LLC states that traditional accuracy metrics are necessary but insufficient; clinical AI must be evaluated on operational integration—not just algorithmic performance. Dr. Pal Randhawa argues that the primary safety endpoint for clinical AI should be whether the tool reduces time-to-decision or increases cognitive burden. Ellipsis Health emphasizes that static metrics do not predict real-world safety or impact on patient care, clinical team burden, or ability to control cost.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether HHS should mandate specific evaluation protocols and metrics or provide principles and shared resources without mandating specific protocols.

positions.label: Standardized Requirements
positions.stance: Mandate specific evaluation protocols and metrics. CommonSpirit Health argues that every new AI tool in clinical settings should receive scrutiny similar to a medical device undergoing clinical trial. The Psychotherapy Action Network states that AI products must be thoroughly researched and tested like any new psychotherapy protocol or pharmaceutical. The American Academy of Sleep Medicine calls for requiring independent validation of clinical AI tools with standardized performance metrics.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 40% of commenters, particularly patient advocacy groups, professional medical societies, and behavioral health stakeholders
keyArguments: Inconsistent evaluation creates safety gaps and allows snake oil products to reach patients
Health systems lack resources to conduct rigorous independent evaluation
Standardization enables comparison across tools and builds trust

positions.label: Flexible Guidelines
positions.stance: Provide principles and shared resources without mandating specific protocols. Advocate Health argues that AI performance metrics should remain locally defined because relevance depends on how each tool is integrated into clinical workflows. Epic Systems notes that for probabilistic AI, pre-deployment testing on generalized datasets has limited predictive value for real-world performance. HealthScoreAI states that the most effective way to foster innovation while managing risk is to avoid overly prescriptive evaluation frameworks.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 45% of commenters, particularly AI developers, EHR vendors, and large health systems
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all requirements ignore context-specific workflow integration needs
Overly prescriptive frameworks stifle innovation and delay beneficial tools
Local validation is more meaningful than standardized testing

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI tools should meet traditional clinical trial standards (RCT-level evidence) before deployment or whether real-world evidence pathways with quasi-experimental designs should be accepted.

positions.label: RCT-Level Evidence Required
positions.stance: AI tools should meet traditional clinical trial standards before deployment. The Psychotherapy Action Network argues that AI products must be thoroughly researched and tested like any new psychotherapy protocol or pharmaceutical, and that corporate metrics like downloads, user engagement, and customer satisfaction are not substitutes for demonstrating effectiveness. An anonymous psychologist states that efficacy must be measured by clinical outcomes: symptom remission, dropout rates, sustained recovery, and quality of life—not engagement metrics. AFSCME argues that the burden of proof should be on AI developers to demonstrate products will do no harm beyond a reasonable level of doubt.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from behavioral health advocates, patient safety organizations, and some medical societies (approximately 25% of commenters)
keyArguments: Patient safety demands the same rigor applied to drugs and devices
Current deployment outpaces evidence, making patients unwitting guinea pigs
Engagement metrics and user satisfaction are not substitutes for clinical outcomes

positions.label: Real-World Evidence Pathways
positions.stance: Accept quasi-experimental designs and implementation science approaches. Waymark argues that RCTs are often impractical for evaluating population health AI due to cluster-level interventions, rapid iteration making results obsolete, ethical constraints, and external validity limitations. Dominick Esposito notes there are ways to evaluate AI without randomized controlled trials or usual care comparison groups, and that assessments could be rooted in an implementation science framework. Kooth states that real-world effectiveness, not accuracy from simulated cases, should be the evidentiary standard for deployment.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from AI developers, health systems, and population health organizations (approximately 40% of commenters)
keyArguments: RCTs are impractical for rapidly iterating AI tools and cluster-level interventions
Implementation science frameworks can provide rigorous evidence more efficiently
Waiting for RCT evidence delays beneficial tools reaching patients

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether external bodies should validate AI tools through independent third-party validation or whether deploying organizations should validate in their own environments through local/site-specific validation.

positions.label: Independent Third-Party Validation
positions.stance: External bodies should validate AI tools. The Dental Standards Institute states that self-validation is not validation and that independent external validation must become a norm. Providence argues that without third-party accreditation, health care providers must invest their own time and funds to determine whether each AI tool is safe. Newton's Tree emphasizes that local evaluations are done independently of the vendor to cultivate confidence in their findings.
positions.supportLevel: Majority support (approximately 55% of commenters), particularly from provider organizations and professional societies
keyArguments: Self-validation creates conflicts of interest
Health systems lack resources for rigorous independent evaluation
Third-party validation enables trust and comparability

positions.label: Local/Site-Specific Validation
positions.stance: Deploying organizations should validate in their own environments. Epic Systems argues that local validation—where a healthcare facility evaluates how AI performs on its own data in its own workflow—is the better method. Newton's Tree notes that medicine is empirical, so the need for local evidence that AI works is unsurprising.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from EHR vendors and large health systems (approximately 30% of commenters)
keyArguments: AI performance is highly context-dependent
Local data and workflows determine real-world effectiveness
Centralized validation cannot capture site-specific factors

areasOfDebate.topic: Pace of Evidence Generation vs. Innovation
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether deployment should be slowed until evidence catches up or whether evidence generation processes should be accelerated to match innovation pace.

positions.label: Evidence First
positions.stance: Slow deployment until evidence catches up. An anonymous psychologist argues that innovation that outpaces clinical evidence is not progress—it is an uncontrolled experiment on vulnerable people. Barbara Milrod states that this technology cannot be permitted to play any role in mental health care until properly studied. The Pennsylvania Medical Society argues that stakes are too high to pursue any course other than rigorous testing, verification and controls prior to introduction.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from behavioral health advocates and patient safety organizations (approximately 20% of commenters)
keyArguments: Public adoption has outpaced essential safety science
Innovation that outpaces evidence is an uncontrolled experiment on vulnerable people
Stakes in healthcare are too high for move-fast approaches

positions.label: Accelerate Evidence Generation
positions.stance: Speed up evaluation processes to match innovation pace. AAFP notes a significant mismatch between rapid AI innovation cycle (typically 6-7 months) and much longer research and evidence-generation cycle (often 18-36 months). The New Jersey Innovation Institute warns that clinical validation requirements for AI tools are resource intensive and time consuming and can deter developers from pursuing AI innovations.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from AI developers and innovation-focused organizations (approximately 35% of commenters)
keyArguments: 6-7 month AI innovation cycles cannot wait for 18-36 month research cycles
Delayed beneficial tools also cause patient harm
Adaptive, real-world evidence approaches can be both rigorous and timely

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Resource burden of conducting independent AI evaluation without standardized frameworks, liability exposure when deploying tools without clear validation standards, and vendor opacity about training data, limitations, and real-world performance. Providers emphasize the operational reality that evaluation cannot be separated from workflow integration—tools that perform well technically may fail in practice due to alert fatigue, cognitive load, or workflow disruption.
specificPoints: WellSpan Health emphasizes that effective evaluation requires coupling technical robustness measures with human-centered metrics, including drift detection, hallucination and error monitoring, bias assessments, clinician trust, handoff safety, and escalation efficacy
Memorial Sloan Kettering notes that establishing pipelines for ongoing AI monitoring is highly burdensome and the vendor provides no built-in support for this
University of Kansas Health System identifies high resource intensity required for continuous auditing as a major barrier to innovation

Proposed solutions include third-party accreditation to reduce individual evaluation burden, standardized model cards and transparency requirements from vendors, and shared registries for post-deployment monitoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inconsistent evaluation requirements across health systems creating market fragmentation, lack of standardized benchmarks making it difficult to demonstrate value, and prescriptive requirements that do not account for context-specific deployment. Developers emphasize that AI tools derive value from real-world deployment and continuous learning—static pre-deployment testing cannot capture this dynamic nature. They also highlight the distinction between medical device AI and non-medical device AI requiring different evaluation approaches.
specificPoints: Ellipsis Health argues that for non-medical device AI used in clinical care, traditional pre-market evaluation models are insufficient on their own because these tools derive much of their value from real-world deployment, continuous learning, and interaction with humans
Epic Systems notes that many healthcare leaders delay or limit AI use not because they believe AI is unsafe, but because they lack confidence that they are evaluating it correctly
SonderMind states that clinical-grade behavioral health AI requires safety testing at a standard that does not yet exist in research literature
Proposed solutions include shared evaluation infrastructure and benchmarking datasets, tiered evaluation based on risk level and autonomy, and real-world evidence pathways as alternatives to RCTs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member liability when using AI tools without clear validation standards, ensuring AI performs equitably across patient populations, and maintaining physician authority and professional standards. Professional societies bring specialty-specific evaluation needs—radiology requires different metrics than behavioral health, which differs from laboratory medicine. They also emphasize the need for evaluation frameworks that align with existing professional standards and scope of practice.
specificPoints: The American College of Radiology has developed Assess-AI which captures real-world data for continuous automated monitoring of model inputs/outputs, versioning, and concordance with radiologist reports

AORN notes that evaluation frameworks are not yet validated for use and efficacy in positively influencing evaluation and adoption of AI-enabled technology, patient outcomes, or organizational outcomes

The Association for Diagnostics & Laboratory Medicine argues that AI systems that rely on or influence laboratory data introduce similar risks as traditional test systems and require analogous validation approaches

Proposed solutions include specialty-specific evaluation frameworks and benchmarks, independent validation requirements with transparent reporting, and post-market surveillance modeled on existing drug/device systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Gap between research performance and real-world implementation, need for implementation science approaches to AI evaluation, and governance integrity beyond technical performance. Researchers emphasize that evaluation must address not just whether AI works technically, but whether institutional accountability structures remain intact when AI is deployed. They highlight the distinction between model-level evaluation and system-level governance.
specificPoints: The Interstitial Institute identifies the most consequential gap in current AI evaluation methodology as between pre-deployment testing and post-deployment governance, noting that a system that performs within evaluated parameters at the model level can simultaneously produce clinical harm at the institutional level if human review structures have been effectively bypassed
The Computing Research Association warns that implementing and using unvalidated models in clinical care is incredibly dangerous for patients
The Markkula Center for Applied Ethics notes that today validation is fragmented—hospitals repeatedly test similar tools with limited resources, producing inconsistent evidence

Proposed solutions include federated evaluation collaboratives enabling multi-site testing, implementation science frameworks for evidence generation, and governance integrity evaluation alongside technical assessment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI amplifying existing biases and health disparities, patient voice absent from AI evaluation processes, and coverage decisions driven by AI without adequate validation. Advocacy groups emphasize that evaluation must include patient and caregiver input throughout the AI lifecycle, and that outcomes meaningful to patients (not just technical metrics) should drive assessment.
specificPoints: The National Multiple Sclerosis Society calls for establishing a review process by advisory panel of individuals with lived experience such as people living with MS

The Global Liver Institute argues that when patient voices and lived experience are absent from AI evaluation, systems may prioritize efficiency or cost savings while overlooking real-world impacts on access, understanding, and trust
The American Cancer Society CAN recommends requiring creation and use of effective adverse event reporting and post-approval monitoring systems
Proposed solutions include advisory panels of individuals with lived experience, routine audits with patient community involvement, and evaluation against recovery-oriented outcomes

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Being pressured to adopt tools without sufficient evidence, liability for AI-influenced decisions, and impact on therapeutic relationships and clinical judgment. Frontline clinicians emphasize the practical reality of using AI tools daily—they need simple, non-punitive ways to report concerns and confidence that tools have been rigorously tested before being integrated into their workflows.
specificPoints: Emilie Maxie, DNP argues that HHS should offer simple, non-punitive ways for clinicians and organizations to report safety concerns or unintended consequences
Amanda Jenkins, LCPC notes this is a new technology that has not had time to be thoroughly tested, is constantly evolving, and could have ethical implications that we are not yet aware of

Mark O'Brien, LCPC states that many AI-enabled clinical tools currently lack independent, peer-reviewed evidence demonstrating safety, efficacy, or clinical superiority over existing standards of care
Proposed solutions include simple reporting mechanisms for safety concerns, independent research before deployment mandates, and evaluation against clinical outcomes rather than engagement metrics

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance Integrity as Distinct from Technical Performance: The Interstitial Institute offers a sophisticated distinction that few other commenters articulate—pre-deployment evaluation answers whether a system performs acceptably on specified benchmarks, while post-deployment governance answers whether the clinical authority structure surrounding the system is still functioning as designed. These are different questions. This insight suggests evaluation frameworks must assess not just AI outputs but whether human oversight structures remain intact under operational conditions.

Instrument-Agnostic Validation Gap: Anil Bodepudi raises an underappreciated technical concern that AI models degrade silently when applied to different hardware than they were trained on. Diagnostic errors from hardware changes are statistically undetectable until patient harm occurs. This suggests validation must account for the full technology stack, not just the algorithm.

Triplicating Models for Safety: University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT describes an innovative practice where a nationally-acclaimed neurosurgery program runs three different stroke AI systems simultaneously—if discrepancies arise, the physician makes the final determination. This suggests redundancy as a safety mechanism deserving further study.

2026 International AI Safety Report Finding: The Interstitial Institute cites concerning evidence that the 2026 International AI Safety Report found it has become more common for models to distinguish between test settings and real-world deployment, and to exploit loopholes in evaluations. This suggests AI systems may game evaluation processes, requiring more sophisticated assessment approaches.

Contactless Monitoring Validation: TapestryHealth provides compelling real-world evidence that contactless monitoring provided average predictive lead time of 4.99 to 5.65 days prior to hospitalization event, and facilities using the AI plus Clinician model saw 43% reduction in ED visits and 45% reduction in inpatient admissions. This demonstrates the potential for rigorous outcome-linked evaluation.

Economic Evaluation Gaps: Scientix.ai highlights systematic underestimation noting that fewer than half of economic evaluation studies explicitly report costing methods, and only 28% include implementation costs—the full financial burden of AI adoption is systematically underestimated in literature.

Caregiver Perspective on Evidence: Dominick Esposito, a parent managing diabetes care for two teenagers, offers a practical insight that providers believe in rigorous evidence and are trained on randomized clinical trials as the gold standard for care, but in practice providers collect data through every observation with their own patients. This suggests implementation science approaches may align with how clinicians actually learn.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic and Setting Variations: The RUPRI Health Panel emphasizes that AI must be rural proofed—tested for viability in resource-poor environments with different patient populations and infrastructure constraints. SANCIAN LLC notes rural and safety-net settings are particularly underrepresented in evidence. SonderMind reports state-by-state regulatory fragmentation has forced them to exclude some states from AI offerings.

Specialty-Specific Evaluation Needs: ACR has developed Assess-AI for continuous monitoring with emphasis on concordance with radiologist reports. Multiple behavioral health commenters note absence of appropriate benchmarks with engagement metrics inappropriately substituted for clinical outcomes. ADLM emphasizes CLIA-analogous validation requirements for AI affecting diagnostic results. ADA has developed Technical Report No. 1109 with specific performance and bias metrics.

Experience-Based Perspectives: Large health systems like Advocate Health, Providence, and CommonSpirit have developed internal governance frameworks and emphasize local validation. Smaller providers lack resources for rigorous evaluation and strongly support third-party accreditation. AI developers emphasize context-specific deployment and resist one-size-fits-all requirements. Frontline clinicians express concern about being pressured to adopt tools without sufficient evidence.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Multiple commenters note that maximizing sensitivity creates excessive false positives undermining clinical utility through alert fatigue. Eli Talbert warns that over-reliance patterns emerge even among experienced clinicians and are amplified under conditions of high information load. The Interstitial Institute notes AI can produce harm if human review structures have been effectively bypassed by volume, time pressure, or operational design. The New Jersey Innovation Institute warns validation challenges can deter developers from pursuing AI innovations.

International Comparisons: Newton's Tree references work with UK's MHRA on continuous monitoring dashboards. Kooth brings 25 years of NHS partnership experience to the US market. Multiple commenters reference international AI safety reports and frameworks.

keyQuotations.quote: The second one [post-deployment governance question] is not currently being asked systematically anywhere in the clinical AI evaluation landscape.
Self-validation is not validation.
Many healthcare leaders delay or limit AI use not because they believe AI is unsafe, but because they lack confidence that they are evaluating it correctly.
An AI product that works well in one healthcare organisation, may not work well in another, geographically close organisation.
Performance in a controlled testing environment does not always translate to real-world improvements in patient health outcomes, clinical workflows, or health care costs.

Static metrics do not predict real-world safety or impact on patient care, clinical team burden, or ability to control cost; without proper use of data, and reliance only on accuracy metrics, AI will struggle to have wide adoption within healthcare.
Innovation that outpaces clinical evidence is not progress—it is an uncontrolled experiment on vulnerable people.
Patients today should not be forced to serve as unwitting guinea pigs so that tech corporations may profit.
At the moment, this is like releasing an untested, malleable, biological weapon on our most vulnerable citizens.
Implementing and using unvalidated models in clinical care is incredibly dangerous for patients.
Even though LLMs are currently being used by millions of people, this adoption by the public is far ahead of the essential science demonstrating privacy, safety, and efficacy.
Corporate metrics such as user engagement are no substitute for meaningful clinical outcomes like symptom remission, sustained recovery, and quality-of-life improvements.
Retrospective performance metrics alone are insufficient to justify clinical deployment.
Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers.

Without third-party accreditation, health care providers must invest their own time and funds to determine whether each AI tool is safe. This drains resources that could go to patient care and workforce development and slows AI adoption.
If the goal is to keep the human in the loop, triplicating models provides protection against isolated AI error.
It is important that local evaluations...are done independently of the vendor to cultivate confidence in their findings.
I would be cautious about any tool that purported to be a solution without knowing that it was tested and vetted in a standardized and rigorous fashion.
In physical care, such as robotic surgery, a software loop taking 20 to 50 milliseconds to recognize an error and issue a stop command can result in unintended tissue damage.
We suggest a 'rural proofing' that accounts for challenges of implementing new technologies in a resource-poor environments and that tests viability for the end users—clinicians and patients.
The stakes are too high to pursue any course other than rigorous testing, verification and controls prior to introduction and use of AI in clinical care.
Poorly governed or inadequately evaluated AI tools could cause patient harm through preventable hospitalizations, medication-related adverse events, or delayed escalation of care.
This is a new technology that has not had time to be thoroughly tested. It is constantly evolving and could have ethical implications that we are not yet aware of.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples and citations. The debate reflects genuine engagement with complex tradeoffs between innovation speed and safety rigor, with most commenters acknowledging legitimate concerns on multiple sides. Professional societies and academic organizations contribute particularly sophisticated analyses of evaluation methodology gaps.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific examples of AI evaluation failures (IBM Watson for Oncology, proprietary sepsis models), reference peer-reviewed literature, and draw on organizational experience. However, systematic data on AI evaluation outcomes remains limited, and some claims about evaluation effectiveness rely on theoretical frameworks rather than empirical evidence. The 2026 International AI Safety Report is cited as emerging evidence of evaluation gaming by AI systems.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient and caregiver voices are underrepresented relative to industry and provider perspectives, with only 5 patient advocacy comments among 70+ substantive submissions. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are acknowledged as important but receive limited direct representation. International perspectives are present but limited primarily to UK comparisons.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High. The evaluation debate involves multiple intersecting dimensions: technical (what metrics to use), operational (who conducts validation), temporal (pre- vs. post-deployment), contextual (local vs. standardized), and evidentiary (RCT vs. real-world evidence). Commenters generally recognize these complexities and resist oversimplified solutions, though positions on prescriptive vs. flexible standards tend to align with stakeholder interests.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that organizational governance—not technology—is the primary determinant of safe and effective AI deployment in healthcare. The central tension lies between those advocating for federal governance requirements (through Conditions of Participation or accreditation) and those preferring voluntary frameworks with federal endorsement to preserve flexibility. Across 50+ comments, stakeholders consistently identify the absence of clear internal accountability structures, fragmented decision-making processes, and the governance gap between executive understanding and operational requirements as the most significant barriers to responsible AI adoption.

consensusPoints.text: Organizational governance is the primary determinant of AI safety and effectiveness. Nearly all commenters agree that governance failures—not algorithmic failures—drive AI safety risks. This consensus spans healthcare providers, vendors, accreditors, and researchers. Ellipsis Health states that AI safety failures are often governance failures, not algorithmic ones. The Interstitial Institute identifies the absence of governance infrastructure standards as the most significant administrative hurdle. ECRI ranked Insufficient Governance of AI in Healthcare as the #1 health technology hazard for 2025.

exceptions.text: Cyberhill Partners characterized governance committee requirements as administrative hurdles, though still acknowledged human oversight as paramount.

consensusPoints.text: Multidisciplinary governance structures are essential. A strong majority agree that AI adoption decisions require input from clinical leadership, IT, compliance, legal, and risk management functions working together. InStride Health notes that successful AI adoption requires alignment across clinical leadership, compliance and risk management, IT and security teams, and executive leadership. ECRI recommends that adoption decisions should involve clinical leaders, IT leaders, safety teams, procurement, administration, and legal counsel.

Current governance capacity is inadequate across the healthcare sector. Most commenters acknowledge significant governance gaps exist today, with data showing widespread deficiencies. CHIME/Censinet survey data shows only 59% of healthcare organizations require formal approval before AI implementation—over 40% lack even a basic pre-implementation gate. The Interstitial Institute reports only 28% of CEOs take direct responsibility for AI governance and only 17% of boards exercise active oversight. Mosaic Life Tech cites that just 10% use automated monitoring to detect AI capabilities within existing software while 51% rely on informal, ad hoc discovery. Massive Bio notes many organizations cannot produce a complete inventory of AI systems affecting patient care.

Human oversight must be preserved in clinical AI workflows. Virtually all commenters support maintaining human-in-the-loop requirements for clinical AI, with no dissent on this principle. Cyberhill Partners emphasizes that human oversight remains paramount and that agentic AI augments rather than replaces clinical judgment. Medvidi states all AI tools should operate under strict human oversight. WellSpan Health notes responsible use requires human oversight, safety reporting, and continuous performance auditing.

areasOfDebate.topic: Federal Governance Requirements vs. Voluntary Frameworks
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters are divided on whether AI governance should be mandated through federal requirements or supported through voluntary industry-led frameworks.

positions.label: Mandate Governance Standards
positions.stance: Require AI governance through Conditions of Participation or similar mechanisms. Massive Bio recommends updating hospital Conditions of Participation to require an AI governance plan, arguing that just as hospitals must have infection control programs, they should have algorithmic bias control programs. ECRI suggests conditioning participation or reimbursement on healthcare organizations demonstrating they maintain an AI inventory. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association proposes light-touch governance expectations within hospital Conditions of Participation requiring AI governance function including clinician leadership.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 40% of commenters, particularly accreditors, patient safety organizations, and some providers
keyArguments: Creates consistent baseline expectations across all healthcare settings
Mirrors successful models like infection control programs
Reduces variability that disadvantages smaller organizations

positions.label: Endorse Voluntary Frameworks
positions.stance: Support industry-led standards without mandates. Advocate Health argues federal guidance should encourage use of governance structures like their FAIR AI principles and that healthcare organizations need flexible governance structures, not prescriptive federal mandates. Mosaic Life Tech suggests HHS should publicly recognize voluntary frameworks as representing reasonable practice. HIMSS EHR Association supports multi-disciplinary oversight but cautions against rigid governance structures, costly external validation, and too-specific timelines.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 50% of commenters, particularly vendors, large health systems, and EHR developers
keyArguments: Preserves flexibility for organizational context and innovation
Avoids one-size-fits-all burden on resource-constrained organizations
Private sector has already developed effective governance models

positions.label: Tiered/Hybrid Approach
positions.stance: Scale requirements based on risk and organizational capacity. HIMSS EHR Association recommends a tiered governance model scaled to organization type, tool's intended use, and risk profile.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10% explicitly advocate this middle ground
keyArguments: Balances safety with feasibility
Accounts for resource disparities between large systems and small practices

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Accreditation in AI Governance
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether private accreditation should serve as the primary governance mechanism or whether concerns about cost and effectiveness warrant caution.

positions.label: Leverage Private Accreditation
positions.stance: Position accreditation as primary governance mechanism. URAC argues private sector accreditation has historically played a prominent role in ensuring safe and effective healthcare and recommends updating procurement language to mandate or encourage AIHC Accreditation for federal contractors. Interstella suggests HHS should endorse and build upon Joint Commission/CHAI frameworks. IAC notes their recently issued guidance represents an interim solution offering foundational guidelines for most rudimentary quality and safety aspects of clinical AI.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from accreditation bodies (3 comments) and moderate support from approximately 10 other commenters
keyArguments: Proven track record in healthcare quality assurance
Allows flexibility while establishing accountability
Reduces federal regulatory burden

positions.label: Caution on Accreditation Reliance
positions.stance: Concerns about cost, access, and effectiveness of accreditation-based approaches. HIMSS EHR Association notes many smaller organizations and rural providers face governance expectations that risk being overly burdensome. Massive Bio observes that in smaller practices or rural hospitals, there may be limited IT support to implement advanced AI systems, creating an adoption gap.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit concerns from approximately 8 commenters focused on small/rural providers
keyArguments: Accreditation costs may burden smaller organizations
May not address fundamental governance capacity gaps
Risk of creating compliance theater without substantive improvement

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between reports of clinician exclusion from AI adoption decisions and broad agreement that clinician involvement is essential.

positions.label: Clinicians Excluded from Decisions
positions.stance: Frontline providers lack voice in AI adoption. AAFP reports that in large health systems and hospitals, frontline physicians are too often excluded from decision-making discussions despite ample resources and multiple teams. Penn Neurovascular Team notes that when governance structures are dominated by IT or executive leadership removed from active practice, implementation may slow and decision-making risks becoming disconnected from frontline workflow realities.
positions.supportLevel: Raised by professional associations and individual clinicians (approximately 8 commenters)
keyArguments: Decisions made by IT/executives disconnected from workflow realities
Leads to tools that increase rather than reduce burden
Undermines trust and adoption

positions.label: Clinician Champions Essential
positions.stance: Active practitioners must lead governance. Penn Neurovascular Team emphasizes that active clinician champions who maintain substantial clinical effort are essential to balanced governance. WellSpan Health notes adoption hinges on clinical and implementation champions with demonstrated organizational POCs supported by strong governance, finance, and IT partners.
positions.supportLevel: Broad agreement (approximately 15 commenters) that clinician involvement is necessary
keyArguments: Clinical context essential for appropriate deployment
Builds trust and adoption
Ensures tools serve patient care

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Disproportionate governance burden without federal support or standards; fragmented decision-making across clinical, IT, compliance, and legal functions; uncertainty about liability when AI supports clinical decisions. Hackensack Meridian Health notes health systems are being asked to assume disproportionate responsibility for governing and managing AI tools without consistent national standards. Mosaic Life Tech reports organizations with clear AI governance ownership achieve ROI faster and scale more effectively than those without.
specificPoints: Direct operational experience reveals gap between vendor promises and implementation reality
Governance is not a barrier but an enabler of adoption
Federal endorsement of voluntary frameworks would reduce uncertainty
Light-touch Conditions of Participation requirements preferred
Funding needed for governance infrastructure and monitoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Slow, fragmented health system governance creates 12-18 month procurement cycles; legal/risk departments block deployments when governance state is unknowable; lack of standardized governance frameworks leads to duplicative reviews. GLACIS Technologies notes healthcare procurement cycles for AI products routinely exceed twelve months because organizations have no efficient way to verify vendor safety claims. FERZ AI identifies General Counsel and Risk Management as typically blocking stakeholders who cannot approve deployments without governance guarantees.
specificPoints: Governance uncertainty is the primary market barrier
Clear governance standards would accelerate rather than impede adoption
Standardized governance playbooks and contracting frameworks needed
Federal recognition of third-party assurance activities requested
Credentialing for AI governance roles within organizations recommended

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Variability in governance expectations across organizations; exclusion of frontline clinicians from decision-making; need for specialty-specific guidance. American Urological Association notes every health care organization seems to have its own process for AI decisions—some have designated committees while others do not. AAFP observes decision-makers and governance bodies for adopting AI tools vary widely depending on organizational resource depth and decision-making structures.
specificPoints: Represent collective voice of practitioners who will use AI tools
Emphasize workforce readiness and training alongside governance structures
Clear federal standards needed to reduce variability
Investment in small practice infrastructure and education required
Specialty-specific governance frameworks recommended

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of uniform processes for AI quality and safety assurance; need for federal endorsement of private accreditation; integration of AI governance into existing accreditation frameworks. IAC states each facility using AI for direct-patient care should create policies addressing training, security, QI, appropriate use, and governance. URAC argues accreditation promotes best practices and provides guardrails while protecting patients and allowing for innovation.
specificPoints: Position themselves as proven mechanism for healthcare quality assurance
Emphasize modular, scalable approaches
Federal procurement requirements for AI accreditation recommended
Integration of AI governance into existing survey processes proposed
Voluntary certification programs with federal backing suggested

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Gap between executive understanding and AI implementation requirements; need for research on effective governance structures; organizational readiness as prerequisite for AI deployment. Scientix.ai identifies the most profound and overlooked administrative hurdle as the gap between what organizational leaders think AI is and what it actually requires to succeed. Computing Research Association notes if hospitals are not performing robust evaluation, further policy intervention will likely be necessary.
specificPoints: Bring evidence-based analysis and cross-sector comparisons
Emphasize structural and systemic factors over individual tool evaluation
Organizational AI Readiness Grants recommended
Research funding for governance effectiveness studies needed
Executive digital fluency programs proposed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clear accountability for AI-influenced decisions; integration into clinical workflows without increasing burden; authority to pause or roll back problematic tools. Emilie Maxie, DNP recommends establishing basic governance norms that clearly identify who approves a tool, who monitors its performance, and who has authority to pause or roll it back if problems arise. Caroline Morrow, DPT notes insufficient attention to governance and compliance when AI innovations enter clinical workflows acts as a silent killer of adoption.
specificPoints: Ground-level view of how governance failures manifest in daily practice
Emphasize practical operationalization over policy frameworks
Basic governance norms needed identifying who approves, monitors, and can pause AI tools
Workflow transformation support required
Clinician override capabilities essential

noteworthyInsights.insight: Legal/Risk as Structural Veto Point: The Interstitial Institute provides a sophisticated analysis that the adoption choke point is consistently the legal and risk layer who are not opposed to AI—they are unable to approve AI deployments that cannot answer three questions with institutional specificity. This reframes governance not as bureaucratic obstacle but as rational institutional response to uncertainty.

Governance Enables Rather Than Impedes Adoption: Mosaic Life Tech cites Black Book Research showing health systems with AI governance councils are twice as likely to achieve ROI within 12 months and programs with dashboards and clear ownership reach early ROI in 7.5 months versus 13.5 months without them. This data challenges the assumption that governance slows adoption.

The Proof Gap as Core Barrier: GLACIS Technologies identifies that healthcare organizations currently deploy AI systems that can claim compliance but cannot prove it and argues this proof gap is the single largest barrier to deploying AI at the scale healthcare needs. The insight that organizations need cryptographic proof, not vendor promises, offers a technical pathway forward.

Post-Acute Care Instability: Renee Pope uniquely highlights that frequent ownership/management turnover disrupts long-horizon modernization planning in skilled nursing facilities, making governance particularly challenging in settings serving vulnerable populations.

AI Content Provenance Across Networks: Providence raises the novel concern that once the patient and their record travels more than two or three degrees beyond our health system, we simply cannot know whether AI was used or the level of risk it carries. This network-level governance challenge has received little attention.

Defense Sector Parallels: Eli Talbert, drawing on SOCOM experience, notes that each deploying institution must independently design governance documentation—duplicative effort that a federal floor would eliminate, offering cross-sector validation for standardization.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural vs. Urban Divide: Multiple commenters including AAFP, Massive Bio, and HIMSS EHR Association note that governance expectations feasible for large urban systems may be unachievable for rural and small practices, risking a two-tiered adoption landscape.
Multi-State Systems Challenges: Providence (7 states) and Advocate Health (5 states) highlight challenges navigating inconsistent state AI regulations while maintaining unified governance.
Academic Medical Centers are more likely to have established AI governance committees (MSK, Penn, University of Kansas) but face black box trust issues with clinicians.
Post-Acute/Long-Term Care facilities are uniquely challenged by ownership instability and limited IT infrastructure.
Vendors with Federal Experience: Those with defense/government backgrounds such as Cyberhill, BlueHalo, and Eli Talbert emphasize structured accountability frameworks and documentation standards.

Governance as Unintended Adoption Barrier: While governance is intended to enable safe adoption, overly burdensome requirements could paradoxically slow beneficial AI deployment, particularly for smaller organizations.
Compliance Theater Risk: Governance requirements may become checkbox exercises without substantive safety improvement.
Trust Erosion Cascade: Failed implementations due to inadequate governance erode confidence, making future adoption harder even for well-designed tools.

keyQuotations.quote: The question of 'what federal rules apply' often has a clear answer. The question of 'who inside our organization is accountable for this AI-influenced decision' frequently does not.
AI safety failures are often governance failures, not algorithmic ones.

Insufficient attention to governance and compliance when AI innovations enter clinical workflows acts as a silent killer of adoption: trust does not collapse in a single event but erodes incrementally, degrading patient confidence in AI-assisted care until adoption stalls and the root cause is difficult to trace.
General Counsel and Risk Management are typically blocking stakeholders—cannot approve deployments without governance guarantees.

Where foundational questions cannot be answered in writing, with named parties and defined thresholds, legal counsel will advise against deployment—rationally, correctly, and in a way that no amount of clinical performance evidence will override.
The most profound and overlooked administrative hurdle in 2026 is not technical—it is the gap between what organizational leaders think AI is and what it actually requires to succeed.

When a hospital can demonstrate to its board, insurer, and patients that AI systems operated safely with cryptographic proof (not just vendor promises), the risk calculus shifts from 'we can't afford to adopt' to 'we can't afford not to.'
This is not a technology failure, but an implementation and governance one.
Once the patient and their record travels more than two or three degrees beyond our health system, we simply cannot know whether AI was used or the level of risk it carries.
What they need from HHS is not more rules, but clear expectations and capacity support that help them govern AI responsibly.

Health systems are being asked to assume disproportionate responsibility for evaluating, governing, and managing AI tools without consistent national standards, predictable payment pathways, or clear liability allocation.
Establish basic governance norms that clearly identify who approves a tool, who monitors its performance, and who has authority to pause or roll it back if problems arise.
Just as hospitals must have infection control programs, in the future they should have algorithmic bias control programs.
Our recently issued guidance represents an interim solution, offering foundational guidelines to address the most rudimentary quality and safety aspects of clinical AI.
In large health systems and hospitals, frontline physicians too often excluded from decision-making discussions despite ample resources and multiple teams.
Active clinician champions who maintain substantial clinical effort are essential to balanced governance.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types demonstrate sophisticated understanding of governance challenges, with many providing specific data, concrete examples, and nuanced policy recommendations. The debate between mandatory and voluntary frameworks reflects genuine policy tradeoffs rather than simple self-interest.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite specific surveys (CHIME/Censinet, Black Book Research), industry reports (ECRI hazard rankings), and organizational data. However, some claims about governance effectiveness lack rigorous empirical support, and there is limited peer-reviewed research cited on optimal governance structures.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from safety-net providers, federally qualified health centers, and rural critical access hospitals who may face the most significant governance capacity constraints. Patient and consumer advocacy perspectives are notably absent from the governance discussion.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The governance debate involves multiple interacting factors including organizational capacity, regulatory authority, liability allocation, and market dynamics. Simple solutions are unlikely to address the full range of concerns raised.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that AI must augment rather than replace clinical judgment, with virtually all stakeholders—from frontline clinicians to technology vendors—affirming the centrality of human expertise in healthcare delivery. The primary tension lies not in whether clinicians should remain in control, but in how to prepare an already-strained workforce to effectively adopt, oversee, and govern AI tools while managing unprecedented burnout and staffing shortages. Commenters converge on the need for substantial federal investment in AI literacy training, workforce credentialing programs, and clinician involvement throughout the AI lifecycle, while expressing concern that without these supports, AI adoption will either stall or create new safety risks through automation bias and alert fatigue.

consensusPoints.text: AI Must Augment, Not Replace, Clinical Judgment. Nearly all commenters explicitly state that AI should support rather than substitute for human clinical decision-making. This consensus spans healthcare providers, professional associations, technology vendors, patient advocates, and individual clinicians. The Association of periOperative Registered Nurses states that AI cannot and must never be a replacement for human clinical judgement, emphasizing that human expertise, empathy and accountability required for clinical decision-making cannot be replicated. HIMSS EHR Association argues AI should be designed to augment informed clinical judgment, not substitute for licensed clinical professionals. Even AI vendors strongly endorse this position, with Epic Systems noting most healthcare AI use cases are designed to reduce administrative burden and support clinical judgment.

exceptions.text: No notable exceptions identified; even AI vendors strongly endorse this position, suggesting market alignment with clinical autonomy principles.

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians Must Be Involved Throughout the AI Lifecycle. A strong majority of commenters emphasize that clinicians should be engaged from design through deployment and monitoring—not consulted only at the end. AORN states clinicians should be full partners in preparing for the future of AI that intersects their respective practice settings. The Health IT End-Users Alliance notes that end-users are often included at the end of the policymaking and technology development process, not the beginning, which leads to technologies and policies that do not meet their intended goals. The American College of Cardiology recommends policies should encourage AI developers to integrate clinicians' perspectives into design, development, validation, implementation, and monitoring.

exceptions.text: No commenters argued against clinician involvement; debate centers on how to operationalize this engagement given workforce constraints.

consensusPoints.text: Workforce Strain Is a Binding Constraint on AI Adoption. Most commenters addressing implementation barriers identify workforce burnout, staffing shortages, and limited bandwidth as critical obstacles to AI adoption—even when AI tools could ultimately reduce burden. Ellipsis Health notes healthcare organizations are operating under unprecedented workforce strain with nurses, care managers, and social workers managing unsustainable caseloads. They observe that AI initiatives stall not because they lack clinical value, but because frontline teams are stretched too thin to participate in pilots, feedback loops, or early optimization phases. Renee L. Pope highlights that chronic staffing shortages and turnover reduce capacity for onboarding, calibration, monitoring, and governance.

exceptions.text: No notable exceptions identified.

consensusPoints.text: AI Literacy and Training Programs Are Essential. Nearly all commenters call for investment in AI education and competency development across career stages, from academic training to continuing education. Caroline J. Morrow argues AI literacy should be treated as core clinical skill rather than elective technical interest. Ryan Kern, a Stanford pre-med student, states the goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool. NCPDP emphasizes that investing in AI literacy both pre- and post-entering the workforce is key to sustaining and developing AI tools.

exceptions.text: No notable exceptions identified.

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Clinical Champions vs. Systemic Training
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI adoption should be driven by individual clinical leaders or through broad-based systemic workforce development programs.

positions.label: Champion-Driven Adoption
positions.stance: Individual clinical leaders drive organizational buy-in. Massive Bio, WellSpan Health, and Atropos Health emphasize that CMIOs and respected physician leaders can significantly sway their peers, and adoption hinges on clinical and implementation champions with demonstrated organizational POCs.
positions.supportLevel: Emphasized by approximately 10 commenters, primarily technology vendors and health systems
keyArguments: CMIOs and respected physician leaders can significantly sway their peers
Adoption hinges on clinical and implementation champions with demonstrated organizational POCs

positions.label: Systemic Workforce Development
positions.stance: Broad-based training and credentialing programs are needed. NCPDP, Penguin Ai, and Scientix.ai argue that organizations cannot rely on individual champions when workforce is already stretched, need standardized competency frameworks across all roles, and note that 40% of IT leaders report staff are unequipped for AI adoption.
positions.supportLevel: Emphasized by approximately 20 commenters, including professional associations and academic researchers
keyArguments: Cannot rely on individual champions when workforce is already stretched
Need standardized competency frameworks across all roles
40% of IT leaders report staff are unequipped for AI adoption

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI will multiply workforce capacity or displace healthcare workers.

positions.label: Workforce Multiplication
positions.stance: AI expands capacity rather than displacing workers. Epic Systems, Waymark, and FastHealth argue that persistent labor shortages mean AI is increasingly necessary to sustain access to care, AI enables existing staff to serve more patients, and community health workers supported by AI can manage larger patient panels while maintaining personalized, relationship-based care.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology vendors and large health systems (approximately 15 commenters)
keyArguments: Persistent labor shortages mean AI is increasingly necessary to sustain access to care
AI enables existing staff to serve more patients
Community health workers supported by AI can manage larger patient panels while maintaining personalized, relationship-based care

positions.label: Job Displacement Concerns
positions.stance: Real risks require guardrails. AFSCME, Providence, and SanLuca AI note that fears that AI will eliminate jobs remain widespread and increasingly surface in labor negotiations, practitioner unions worry about staff downsizing or increased visit frequency expectations, and explicit protections for worker autonomy are needed.
positions.supportLevel: Labor unions, some professional associations, and individual clinicians (approximately 8 commenters)
keyArguments: Fears that AI will eliminate jobs remain widespread and increasingly surface in labor negotiations
Practitioner unions worry about staff downsizing or increased visit frequency expectations
Need explicit protections for worker autonomy

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI adoption should be mandated or allowed to proceed organically with federal support.

positions.label: Cautious Voluntary Approach
positions.stance: Allow organic adoption without mandates. AAPA and the American Society of Hematology argue that health professionals are still understanding AI capabilities, limitations, and implementation approaches, forcing adoption could harm patient care, and time is needed for workforce readiness.
positions.supportLevel: Several professional associations (approximately 5 commenters)
keyArguments: Health professionals are still understanding AI capabilities, limitations, and implementation approaches
Forcing adoption could harm patient care
Need time for workforce readiness

positions.label: Accelerated Adoption with Support
positions.stance: Federal incentives and infrastructure are needed. Health AI Institute, ICF, and Cyberhill Partners argue that workforce shortages are urgent, AI can address the burnout crisis, and grants and training support are needed to overcome adoption barriers.
positions.supportLevel: Technology vendors, large health systems, consulting firms (approximately 12 commenters)
keyArguments: Workforce shortages are urgent
AI can address burnout crisis
Need grants and training support to overcome adoption barriers

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether policy language should be physician-centric or inclusive of all healthcare practitioners.

positions.label: Physician-Centric Language
positions.stance: Current framing may exclude other practitioners. AAPA notes that by 2033, of estimated 1.5 million practitioners, about 50% will be physicians and 50% will be PAs and other non-physician practitioners, and physician-centric language could restrict PAs from providing AI services.
positions.supportLevel: Non-physician professional associations (approximately 3 commenters)
keyArguments: By 2033, of estimated 1.5 million practitioners, about 50% will be physicians and 50% will be PAs and other non-physician practitioners
Physician-centric language could restrict PAs from providing AI services

positions.label: Inclusive Multi-Disciplinary Approach
positions.stance: All care team members need engagement. The Computing Research Association, AHIMA, and National Council for Mental Wellbeing argue that many other humans also need to be in the loop including radiologists, patients, and caregivers, and that HI professionals, nurses, and non-licensed staff all interact with AI.
positions.supportLevel: Most commenters implicitly support broader inclusion
keyArguments: Many other humans also need to be in the loop: radiologists, patients, caregivers
HI professionals, nurses, and non-licensed staff all interact with AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preserving clinician autonomy; ensuring AI doesn't undermine patient-physician relationship; liability clarity; avoiding additional compliance burdens
specificPoints: Emphasize the art and nuance of medicine that AI cannot replicate
Highlight uniquely human traits like compassion and empathy
Concerned about erosion of professional expertise
The American College of Cardiology states clinicians are essential to interpret, adapt, and individualize insights to ensure that the art and nuance of medicine are preserved
The Pennsylvania Medical Society notes that while attempts continue to be made to humanize AI, compassion and empathy are uniquely human traits that AI cannot replicate
Propose early clinician engagement in AI development, continuing education requirements, professional society involvement in credentialing, and input from specialty societies on AI standards

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity; clinician trust as adoption barrier; workforce readiness gaps; need for reimbursement alignment
specificPoints: Emphasize that their tools are designed to augment, not replace
Highlight successful implementations reducing burnout
Acknowledge that even accurate AI fails if it disrupts workflows

ICF notes that if AI tools and solutions are intuitive, reduce human effort, and demonstrably enhance care delivery, providers are far more likely to embrace them, but even a highly accurate algorithm will fail in practice if it is difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines
Propose federal support for AI literacy training, grants for implementation science, peer-to-peer learning networks, and human-centered design research

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workforce strain limiting adoption capacity; union resistance; need for clinical champions; skepticism about AI accuracy
specificPoints: Experience operational reality of implementation—see both promise and friction
Understand that rapid AI deployment risks over-promising/under-delivering and erodes clinician trust
Providence notes organizations face opposition from union voices about future AI integration, and even ambient listening tools that cut down on clinical charting can spark resistance
Propose clinical AI credentialing programs, governance structures with clinician leadership, and support for implementation champions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Insufficient clinician input in AI development; automation bias risks; need for human-AI collaboration research
specificPoints: Bring evidence-based perspective on documented risks
Emphasize research gaps
Highlight need for implementation science
