The Computing Research Association states AI tools are only worthwhile if they are used by the individuals responsible for patient care
Propose priority research on human-AI interaction, funding for clinical informaticist training, and evidence-based frameworks for measuring collaboration effectiveness

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preserving human connection in care; preventing over-reliance on AI; ensuring personalized care
specificPoints: Focus on relationship quality rather than efficiency metrics
Worried about loss of empathy and individualized attention

The American Parkinson Disease Association expresses concern about providers becoming overly reliant on AI tools and fear that over-reliance would reduce interactions between PD patients and human providers
Propose AI should supplement, not supplant, provider role and maintain meaningful patient-provider interactions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Job displacement; loss of professional autonomy; AI dictating workflows and patient acuity
specificPoints: Only stakeholder explicitly demanding worker override rights and protections against AI-driven scheduling/workflow control
AFSCME states all health care workers must be able to override AI recommendations when the individual believes it's not appropriate for the patient in front of them
Propose explicit guardrails ensuring workers can override AI and prohibitions on AI solely dictating workflows or care plans

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Automation bias; cognitive load; maintaining clinical skills; practical workflow integration
specificPoints: Frontline experience with both promise and friction
Understand day-to-day realities of implementation
Dr. Pal Randhawa, a neurosurgeon, notes a facility may appear fully staffed on paper, yet remain operationally degraded due to unmeasured cognitive load
Propose training on AI limitations, critical evaluation skills, and workflow co-design

noteworthyInsights.insight: Operational Radar concept — Neurosurgeon Dr. Pal Randhawa proposes reframing AI's role from replacing doctors to stabilizing the grid, suggesting AI should predict workforce attrition 30-60 days in advance to allow proactive intervention. This shifts the conversation from clinical decision support to workforce sustainability infrastructure.

Aviation/Energy sector analogy — Multiple commenters draw parallels to high-reliability industries: Successful high-reliability sectors (Aviation, Energy) do not use technology to replace the expert operator; they use it to augment situational awareness and prevent error. This framing may resonate with policymakers familiar with safety-critical systems.

Trust hierarchy insight — Scientix.ai observes that trust in medical AI flows hierarchically: from digital literacy to the AI system, then to physician and institution, reinforcing the physician's intermediary role in building institutional trust. This suggests training investments should focus on clinicians as trust brokers.

Environmental concerns as adoption barrier — InStride Health uniquely notes that clinician concerns about energy intensity and environmental impact of AI technologies can meaningfully limit willingness to adopt or expand AI use—an unexpected barrier not raised by other commenters.

Pre-med student perspective — Ryan Kern, a Stanford junior, offers the future workforce view: The goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool. This pragmatic framing from someone who will practice in an AI-integrated environment is notable.

Parent/caregiver as adoption observer — Dominick Esposito, father of two teenagers with Type 1 diabetes, notes that his sons' endocrinologists' use of AI scribes helped medical community become comfortable with AI—suggesting low-stakes administrative tools may serve as gateway to broader clinical AI acceptance.

Documented burnout relief — Medvidi reports that AI tools helped mitigate provider burnout in context of projected deficit of over 21,000 psychiatrists by 2030, providing concrete evidence of workforce sustainability benefits.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Post-acute and long-term care settings face unique challenges: chronic staffing shortages, high turnover, limited digital literacy, and reduced capacity for governance.
Rural facilities may struggle to meet AI competency standards that urban centers can achieve, as implied by multiple commenters discussing resource disparities.
Multi-state health systems like Providence and SonderMind face regulatory fragmentation that complicates workforce training standardization.
Hospitals with professional management and integrated salaried structures tend to be more proactive in deploying AI.
Small, independent, and safety-net practices have limited resources to evaluate and implement AI.
Behavioral health settings face particular challenges due to exclusion from HITECH Act funding and complex patient populations.
CMIOs and clinical informatics leaders are consistently identified as critical adoption drivers.
Proceduralists/surgeons view AI tools differently than those in credit-based inpatient models versus outpatient settings.
Non-physician practitioners (PAs, nurses, community health workers) are underrepresented in policy language but comprise growing share of workforce.
AI adoption perceived as cost-cutting rather than capacity-multiplying can slow implementation and limit usage.
Rapid deployment without adequate training erodes clinician trust and creates long-term adoption barriers.
Ambient listening tools that reduce documentation burden can paradoxically spark resistance from unions worried about increased visit frequency expectations.
Leadership underestimating specialized roles (information architects, data governance leads) ensures AI tools operate on unreliable data.

keyQuotations.quote: AI cannot and must never be a replacement for a human's clinical judgement. The human expertise, empathy and accountability required for clinical decision-making in practice cannot be replicated.
While attempts continue to be made to 'humanize' AI, compassion and empathy, to name two of many, are uniquely human traits that AI cannot replicate.
Clinicians are essential to interpret, adapt, and individualize these insights to ensure that the art and nuance of medicine are preserved.
AI that adds burden—even if accurate—fails the people it aims to serve.
A facility may appear fully staffed on paper, yet remain operationally degraded due to unmeasured cognitive load.
AI initiatives stall not because they lack clinical value, but because frontline teams are stretched too thin to participate in pilots, feedback loops, or early optimization phases.
By shifting focus from 'replacing doctors' to 'stabilizing the grid,' HHS can transform AI from a disruptive novelty into a stabilizing component of national health security.
The most effective healthcare AI does not seek to replace clinicians or focus only on automation but rather serves as a force multiplier for human care that extends the reach of care teams.
Rather than displacing healthcare workers, AI enables workforce multiplication that expands access to care coordination services.
Even in controlled settings without time pressure, clinicians favored AI-based decision-making systems despite contradictory or clinically nonsensical information.
The goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool.
End-users are often included at the end of the policymaking and technology development process, not the beginning, which leads to technologies and policies that do not meet their intended goals.
AI tools are only worthwhile if they are used by the individuals responsible for patient care.

The central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection; for that reason it is important that HHS promote AI use in clinical care subject to preserving the power of human connection and that human-led care is a core tenet of our health care system.
The 'AI Doctor' will not work for free—and neither can the human clinicians who supervise them.

If AI tools and solutions are intuitive, reduce human effort, and demonstrably enhance care delivery, providers are far more likely to embrace them. Conversely, even a highly accurate algorithm will fail in practice if it is difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines.

The integration of technology into clinical workflows can generate apprehension among clinicians when it overlaps with their scope of care, despite persistent shortages in high-demand specialties like primary care and behavioral health.
All health care workers must be able to override AI recommendations when the individual believes it's not appropriate for the patient in front of them.

Software recommendations will constantly evolve as AI models change, but the laws of physics do not. By standardizing the expectation that safety-critical interventions must happen at the physical energy-delivery layer, HHS can ensure that human clinicians—and not probabilistic software models—always retain the ultimate authority over patient care.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder types engage substantively with the issues, providing specific examples, citing evidence, and offering concrete policy recommendations. The discourse reflects genuine expertise and thoughtful consideration of tradeoffs.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite peer-reviewed research, workforce statistics, and operational experience. Some claims about automation bias and workforce impacts are supported by specific studies. However, some assertions about AI benefits rely more on projected outcomes than demonstrated results.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Labor organizations are significantly underrepresented with only one comment from AFSCME, despite job displacement being a major concern. Patient and caregiver voices are also limited relative to industry and professional association perspectives. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are largely absent or implied rather than directly represented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves interconnected issues of workforce readiness, training infrastructure, professional autonomy, labor relations, and implementation science. Commenters recognize that solutions require coordinated action across multiple domains and that workforce constraints create paradoxical barriers to adopting tools designed to reduce burden.

10.themeDescription: Equity, Bias, and Health Disparities. This theme encompasses all comments addressing how AI affects health equity, including algorithmic bias, disparate impacts, and access disparities. || It includes concerns about AI perpetuating or amplifying existing disparities, proposals for bias detection and mitigation, debates about equity monitoring requirements, and discussions of ensuring AI benefits reach underserved populations. This theme does NOT include data representativeness (covered under Evaluation) or access barriers for small providers (covered under Resource Barriers)

sections.executiveSummary: There is near-universal consensus among commenters that AI in healthcare poses significant risks of perpetuating or amplifying existing health disparities, with strong agreement that bias testing, diverse training data, and ongoing equity monitoring are essential safeguards. The central tension lies between those who view equity requirements as primarily a pre-deployment design challenge versus those who emphasize that disparities emerge post-implementation through differential adoption, trust, and contextual failures—requiring continuous surveillance. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend mandatory bias testing, equity impact assessments, and federal certification standards, with particular emphasis on protecting underserved populations including rural, Medicaid, minority, and safety-net communities.

consensusPoints.text: AI systems can perpetuate or amplify existing health disparities. Nearly all commenters acknowledge that AI trained on historical data risks embedding past inequities at scale. This concern spans all stakeholder types—from academic researchers to frontline clinicians to patient advocacy groups. Global Liver Institute describes how an algorithm significantly under-identified Black patients because it relied on historical health care spending as proxy for need. New Jersey Innovation Institute warns that AI models for predicting patient deterioration may not accurately reflect risk profiles of certain racial groups. ADLM notes that studies of health AI tools have demonstrated such imbalances can cause models to systematically underestimate risk or misclassify disease in certain populations.

exceptions.text: Several commenters argue AI can actually reduce bias compared to human decision-making when properly designed, noting AI can consistently surface signals earlier and reduce risk of unconscious bias versus traditional models.

consensusPoints.text: Bias testing and mitigation should be mandatory requirements. A strong majority of commenters call for required bias testing before deployment, with many advocating for ongoing monitoring post-deployment. Massive Bio argues that just as hospitals must have infection control programs, in the future they should have algorithmic bias control programs. AAFP states that companies providing AI tools must address implicit bias in design with processes for monitoring differential outcomes. Cyberhill Partners recommends HHS establish requirements for bias testing and mitigation, building on Office of Civil Rights' authority under Section 1557.

exceptions.text: No commenters explicitly oppose bias testing requirements, though some business commenters suggest using industry-standard foundation models can reduce but not eliminate bias concerns.

consensusPoints.text: Underserved populations face disproportionate risk of AI-related harm. Nearly all commenters identify specific vulnerable populations at heightened risk, including rural communities, Medicaid beneficiaries, racial/ethnic minorities, patients with limited English proficiency, and those receiving care across multiple fragmented systems. Amir Abrams notes that people who receive care across multiple systems (often due to access patterns, insurance churn, transportation constraints, or regional provider availability) are disproportionately harmed. RUPRI Health Panel observes that technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates disparities that exist. Community Care Cooperative emphasizes that FQHCs care for patients who are, on average, sicker, lower-income, and more socially complex than many other populations.

Diverse and representative training data is essential. Most commenters emphasize that AI models must be trained and validated on demographically diverse datasets to ensure equitable performance. National MS Society calls for use of representative and high-quality data to build AI models reflecting diverse patient populations. RBMA calls for federal certification verifying AI models were trained and validated on comprehensive, demographically diverse datasets. ADLM recommends use of diverse and representative patient datasets in AI model training and validation.

areasOfDebate.topic: When Equity Problems Emerge: Design vs. Deployment
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether equity can be primarily addressed through careful pre-deployment design and testing, or whether many inequities emerge only after implementation and require continuous monitoring.

positions.label: Pre-deployment focus
positions.stance: Equity can be addressed through careful design and testing before deployment. SANCIAN LLC advocates equity impact assessment before deployment, and Interstella recommends algorithmic bias audits should be routine.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 40% of commenters, primarily AI developers and some trade associations
keyArguments: Bias originates in training data and can be detected through pre-deployment testing
Equity impact assessments before deployment can identify potential disparities
Proper design prevents problems from occurring

positions.label: Post-deployment emphasis
positions.stance: Many inequities emerge only after implementation and require continuous monitoring. Dr. Bhagavathula states that disparities associated with AI are frequently attributed to biased training data, but many inequities emerge only after implementation. Satyadhar Joshi calls for ongoing bias audits rather than one-time assessments.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 60% of commenters, particularly academic researchers and safety-net providers
keyArguments: Disparities emerge through uneven adoption, differential trust, resource constraints, and context-specific failures
Performance can degrade over time due to changing conditions
Single pre-deployment tests cannot capture real-world complexity

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether AI developers should bear primary liability for bias in their products, or whether both developers and deployers share accountability.

positions.label: Developer responsibility
positions.stance: AI developers should bear primary liability for bias in their products. CCLA argues the current framework places disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories for potential bias originating in vendor training data or model design.
positions.supportLevel: Several laboratory and clinical associations
keyArguments: Clinicians and laboratories cannot reasonably assess bias in vendor training data or model design
Developers have the technical expertise and access to address bias at the source

positions.label: Shared responsibility
positions.stance: Both developers and deployers share accountability. MHA recommends pre-deployment local validation should include bias analysis. NeuroFlow notes validation requires ongoing assessment of fairness across patient populations.
positions.supportLevel: Most health systems and professional organizations
keyArguments: Local validation is necessary because performance varies by context
Healthcare organizations must monitor outcomes in their specific populations
Both parties have roles in ensuring equitable care

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Approach: Mandatory vs. Voluntary Standards
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether federal regulations should require bias testing and equity monitoring, or whether voluntary certification with incentives is preferable.

positions.label: Mandatory requirements
positions.stance: Federal regulations should require bias testing and equity monitoring. Massive Bio recommends incorporating bias testing into Medicare Conditions of Participation or certification criteria. Waymark calls for clear standards for algorithmic fairness in clinical AI systems including requirements for disparate impact testing.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (approximately 65%), including patient advocacy groups, labor unions, and many providers
keyArguments: Voluntary approaches have not prevented documented harms
Equity is too important to leave to market forces
Consistent standards ensure baseline protections

positions.label: Voluntary certification
positions.stance: Encourage compliance through certification and payment incentives rather than mandates. ICF recommends bias and fairness assessments as part of voluntary certification framework. WellSpan suggests AI Add-on Payments should reward bias mitigation.
positions.supportLevel: Some business commenters and consulting firms (approximately 25%)
keyArguments: Flexibility allows innovation
Incentives can drive adoption without stifling development
One-size-fits-all mandates may not fit all contexts

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability for bias they cannot control; lack of tools to validate AI performance in their specific populations; risk of widening disparities between well-resourced and safety-net providers. CommonSpirit Health emphasizes their special focus on vulnerable and underserved populations. Community Care Cooperative notes FQHCs serve patients sicker, lower-income, and more socially complex than many other populations.
specificPoints: Frontline experience with how AI tools fail in real-world conditions
Direct observation of patient populations underserved by current AI tools
Proposed solutions include local validation requirements, federal certification of bias testing, payment incentives for equity monitoring, and shared infrastructure for under-resourced providers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty; need for clear standards they can design to; competitive disadvantage if bias requirements are inconsistent. Massive Bio describes their focus on diversity in data so that AI recommendations are equitable and inclusive. MHK notes using industry-standard foundation models rather than training proprietary AI models can reduce introduction of training data bias.
specificPoints: Technical understanding of how bias enters AI systems
Experience with bias mitigation techniques
Awareness of trade-offs between model performance and fairness
Proposed solutions include federal certification standards, industry benchmarking tools, support for bias mitigation toolkits, and clear liability allocation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member liability; patient safety; maintaining professional standards; ensuring AI supports rather than undermines clinical judgment. AORN identifies methods for detecting and mitigating bias as priority research area. ACC emphasizes policy actions must ensure biases in data inputs, AI-enabled outputs, and automated systems are minimized or adjusted for.
specificPoints: Represent collective voice of practitioners across specialties
Develop practice standards and guidelines
Bridge between individual practitioners and policymakers
Proposed solutions include research funding for bias detection methods, professional guidelines incorporating equity requirements, and ongoing monitoring with public accountability

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence gaps on equity impacts; need for rigorous evaluation methods; importance of post-deployment surveillance. Dr. Bhagavathula describes witnessing disparities widened quietly over a year when a model's performance degraded due to changes in coding practices. RUPRI Health Panel calls for research to anticipate effects and monitor implementation to identify all consequences.
specificPoints: Scientific expertise in evaluation methodology
Understanding of how bias manifests in complex systems
Experience with population-level analysis
Proposed solutions include equity-specific monitoring requirements, multi-institutional evaluation consortia, and research on AI performance across demographic subgroups

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Algorithmic bias affecting their patient populations; AI amplifying existing care gaps; need for patient voice in AI development. National MS Society calls for review process by advisory panel of individuals with lived experience. Global Liver Institute warns that when AI equates spending with need, it quietly carries inequities forward.
specificPoints: Represent lived experience of patients who may be harmed by biased AI
Understand how disparities manifest in daily care
Advocate for populations often excluded from AI development
Proposed solutions include advisory panels of people with lived experience, routine audits and validation, integration of population health principles, and ensuring AI benefits reach all patients

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Structural barriers to equitable AI; gaps in current frameworks; need for patient-centered approaches. Vikki Small describes direct experience deploying AI in safety-net settings where tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance. Emilie Maxie emphasizes need to understand how AI performance varies by race, language, disability, or care setting.
specificPoints: Deep technical or clinical expertise
Often identify issues overlooked by organizational commenters
Provide detailed implementation insights
Proposed solutions include patient-designated data routing, equity impact assessments, and implementation research in resource-constrained settings

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data fragmentation as equity issue: Amir Abrams, a software architect with clinical informatics experience, makes the compelling point that missingness is not random and often correlates with socioeconomic access patterns—meaning patients who receive care across multiple systems due to insurance churn or transportation constraints are systematically disadvantaged by AI that relies on incomplete records. This reframes data interoperability as an equity imperative.

Spending-as-proxy trap: Global Liver Institute articulates a critical insight that when AI equates spending with need, it quietly carries inequities forward. This captures how seemingly neutral algorithmic choices can embed discrimination—lower historical spending reflects barriers to care, not lower need.

Post-deployment emergence of disparities: Dr. Bhagavathula provides a crucial corrective to the focus on training data bias, noting that disparities associated with AI are frequently attributed to biased training data, but many inequities emerge only after implementation through uneven adoption, differential trust, resource constraints, and context-specific failures. This suggests pre-deployment testing alone is insufficient.

Rural diversion risk: RUPRI Health Panel identifies an underappreciated mechanism for AI-driven disparity: technology that makes care more convenient for well-insured patients may divert them from local providers, threatening the financial viability of safety-net providers who serve all patients regardless of payment source.

Cultural and linguistic grounding gap: Advocate Health observes that even when translation is available, AI models lack cultural, linguistic, and contextual grounding making recommendations often not meaningful or actionable in patients' day-to-day lives. This goes beyond simple language access to deeper issues of cultural competence in AI design.

Infection control analogy: Massive Bio offers a powerful framing that just as hospitals must have infection control programs, in the future they should have algorithmic bias control programs. This positions bias mitigation as a standard patient safety requirement rather than an optional enhancement.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural vs. urban divide: Rural health advocates like RUPRI and VieCure emphasize infrastructure gaps and potential for AI to widen geographic disparities, while urban safety-net providers like Vikki Small and Community Care Cooperative focus on serving diverse, socially complex populations.
State-level concerns: Massachusetts (MHA) and California (CCLA) associations raise specific concerns about patchwork state regulations and liability frameworks.
Large integrated systems like Advocate Health and CommonSpirit have resources to develop internal governance frameworks but identify gaps in AI's ability to address social determinants.
Safety-net providers and FQHCs like Community Care Cooperative and Vikki Small emphasize that AI tools developed in well-resourced settings fail when deployed in their contexts.
Community oncology and specialty practices like VieCure and RBMA worry about concentration of AI benefits in academic medical centers.

Implementation experience correlates with post-deployment focus: Commenters with direct AI deployment experience like Dr. Bhagavathula, Vikki Small, and Advocate Health consistently emphasize that disparities emerge after implementation, while those without such experience focus more on pre-deployment testing.

Technical expertise correlates with nuanced bias understanding: Software architects and AI developers like Amir Abrams and MHK identify specific mechanisms of bias (missingness, prompt injection, RAG context completeness) beyond general concerns about training data.
Financial viability threat to safety-net providers: AI-enabled convenience for well-insured patients may divert revenue from providers serving all patients.
Reimbursement-driven access disparities: Without viable payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries.
Trust erosion: Biased AI experiences may reduce patient engagement with healthcare services broadly, leading to worse outcomes.

keyQuotations.quote: When AI equates spending with need, it quietly carries inequities forward.

Disparities associated with AI are frequently attributed to biased training data, but many inequities emerge only after implementation through uneven adoption, differential trust, resource constraints, and context-specific failures.
Missingness is not random and often correlates with socioeconomic access patterns.

Current clinical AI tools do not appear to incorporate these critical social factors—such as the availability of community resources, individual financial constraints, or the practical feasibility of care recommendations—into their assessments or outputs. These systems may identify a patient as high risk for hospital readmission, but they cannot determine whether that same patient has the means to attend a follow-up appointment or afford necessary prescriptions once insurance benefits are exhausted.
Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers.
Generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning often miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress, producing bland or inaccurate outputs.
Technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates disparities that exist.
AI tools developed without meaningful inclusion of safety-net data risk working well for some populations while failing others—and, in the worst cases, exacerbating existing disparities.
If AI solutions are not designed with inclusivity in mind, marginalized populations may face disparities in access to advanced healthcare technologies.

Without aligned incentives, advances will concentrate in large systems, widening disparities and gaps in care within community care and thereby jeopardizing the timeliness and quality of care received by the majority of US citizens diagnosed with cancer seen in the community oncology setting.
Just as hospitals must have infection control programs, in the future they should have algorithmic bias control programs.
Patient-designated routing improves equity by making longitudinal completeness a default expectation across sites rather than a privilege of being 'in the right health system.'

Absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries, exacerbating disparities in access and limiting innovation within federally funded programs.
It is important to assess whether the use of AI introduces specific risks and harms, such as algorithms that could amplify errors and preexisting biases in the source data.

Technology that facilitates ease of access for well-insured populations may divert encounters away from local providers who serve all patients regardless of source of payment. That diversion could threaten financially viability of local providers.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder types engage substantively with the equity implications of healthcare AI, providing specific examples, citing evidence, and offering concrete policy recommendations. The discourse reflects genuine expertise and lived experience with AI implementation challenges.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific examples of algorithmic bias (e.g., the spending-as-proxy algorithm that under-identified Black patients) and reference studies demonstrating disparate impacts. However, some claims about AI's potential to reduce bias rely more on theoretical arguments than empirical evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct representation from patients themselves, particularly from the underserved populations most at risk of AI-related harm. Most patient perspectives are mediated through advocacy organizations or providers serving these populations. Also limited representation from payers and insurers on equity implications.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - commenters recognize that equity challenges in healthcare AI involve multiple interacting factors including training data bias, post-deployment emergence of disparities, differential adoption and trust, resource constraints, and contextual failures. The debate between pre-deployment and post-deployment focus reflects sophisticated understanding of when and how disparities manifest.

11.themeDescription: Resource Barriers and Implementation Challenges. This theme encompasses all comments addressing practical barriers to AI adoption, including costs, infrastructure requirements, and organizational capacity constraints. || It includes concerns about implementation costs, reports of infrastructure gaps, debates about resource requirements, and discussions of barriers facing smaller and rural providers. This theme does NOT include reimbursement policy (covered separately) or regulatory barriers (covered separately)

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that the primary barriers to AI adoption in healthcare are structural, organizational, and financial—not technical or algorithmic. Across 50 comments representing healthcare providers, trade associations, technology vendors, and researchers, a consistent picture emerges: even clinically validated AI tools fail to achieve adoption because organizations lack the infrastructure, workforce capacity, governance expertise, and capital to implement them. The dominant tension lies between the urgent need for AI adoption to address healthcare system strain and the reality that the organizations most in need—rural, safety-net, small, and independent providers—are least equipped to adopt these technologies. Commenters converge on recommendations for targeted federal financial support, tiered governance requirements, and infrastructure investment, while debating whether adoption should be incentivized or mandated.

consensusPoints.text: Barriers to AI adoption are primarily structural and organizational, not technical. Nearly all commenters explicitly state that the fundamental obstacles to AI adoption lie in organizational capacity, infrastructure, and financial constraints rather than in the technology itself. Representative examples include observations that adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction, and that barriers to AI in healthcare are primarily infrastructural and organizational—not algorithmic.

exceptions.text: A small number of commenters note that integration of emerging technologies like mixed reality or legacy EHR systems does present genuine technical challenges.

consensusPoints.text: Smaller, rural, and safety-net providers face disproportionate barriers. A significant digital divide exists where resource-constrained organizations cannot adopt AI even when it would benefit them most. Data shows 86% of system-affiliated hospitals use predictive AI versus just 37% of independent facilities, and among 18% of health systems with mature AI governance, three-quarters have over $1 billion in net patient revenue. This concern spans trade associations like AAFP, AADA, and AASM, healthcare providers including Providence and Community Care Cooperative, academic researchers from RUPRI and CRA, and technology vendors such as VieCure and COGA Labs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 35 of 50)

consensusPoints.text: Upfront costs create adoption barriers even when long-term ROI is positive. Commenters emphasize the mismatch between immediate implementation costs and delayed or diffuse benefits. Hospital budgets are tight, and AI tools cost money upfront; early adopters of AI for clinical documentation found that while doctors were happier, the financial ROI wasn't immediate. Organizations may incur real costs before seeing offsetting savings, creating financial mismatch that can slow or stall adoption. Additionally, AI requires upfront Capital Expense such as sensors, but savings like reduced hospitalizations accrue to the Payer (Medicare), not the Facility.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing financial barriers (approximately 30 of 50)

consensusPoints.text: AI adoption requires substantial non-technology investments. Successful AI implementation requires workflow redesign, staff training, governance structures, and change management—not just technology procurement. Costs include infrastructure for monitoring, governance, model evaluation, workforce training, and cybersecurity. Organizations underestimate transformation required, leading to failed implementations that erode confidence.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (approximately 25 of 50)

consensusPoints.text: Current ROI metrics inadequately capture AI value. Existing evaluation frameworks fail to capture the full picture of AI costs and benefits. Benefits like reduced physician burnout and improved patient care are soft and difficult to capture meaningfully. Cost-effectiveness studies often fail to clarify who bears costs versus who gains benefits, and studies frequently overlook full lifecycle expenses including integration, cybersecurity, monitoring, revalidation, and governance. Fewer studies fully account for implementation costs including technology infrastructure, staff training, workflow redesign, and ongoing maintenance.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing cost-benefit analysis (approximately 20 of 50)

areasOfDebate.topic: Federal Role in AI Adoption Support
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether the federal government should provide targeted financial support for under-resourced providers or allow market forces to drive AI adoption.

positions.label: Targeted Financial Support
positions.stance: Provide grants, subsidies, and enhanced payments specifically for under-resourced providers. Proponents include Advocate Health, AAFP, Community Care Cooperative, and Mosaic Life Tech.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of commenters (approximately 30 of 50), particularly trade associations and safety-net providers
keyArguments: Market forces alone will widen the digital divide
Regional Extension Center model provides successful precedent (68% vs. 12% Meaningful Use achievement)
Without support, beneficial AI will remain inaccessible to populations most in need

positions.label: Market-Driven Adoption
positions.stance: Allow competitive market forces to drive development and deployment. Proponents include ScriptChain Health and Epic Systems.
positions.supportLevel: Small minority (approximately 3-5 commenters), primarily technology vendors
keyArguments: Government intervention may slow innovation
Market competition drives efficiency and quality
Short-term costs acceptable for long-term benefits

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether governance requirements should be tiered based on organization size and risk level or uniform across all organizations.

positions.label: Tiered/Scaled Governance
positions.stance: Governance expectations should vary based on organization size, resources, and AI risk level. Proponents include HIMSS EHR Association, Mosaic Life Tech, and NeuroFlow.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters addressing governance (approximately 15 of 20)
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all requirements will exclude smaller organizations
Risk-based approach focuses resources where most needed
Prevents governance burden from becoming de facto barrier

positions.label: Uniform Governance Standards
positions.stance: All organizations should meet baseline governance requirements before AI adoption. Proponents include SANCIAN LLC and Renee Pope.
positions.supportLevel: Minority of commenters (approximately 5-7), primarily governance consultants and academic researchers
keyArguments: Patient safety requires consistent standards regardless of organization size
Premature adoption without readiness wastes resources and erodes trust
Governance maturity should be prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI adoption should be encouraged through incentives or required through mandates.

positions.label: Voluntary with Incentives
positions.stance: Encourage adoption through financial incentives and reduced barriers rather than mandates. Proponents include Ellipsis Health, AAPA, and AAFP.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (approximately 25 of 30 commenters addressing this issue)
keyArguments: Organizations experiencing staffing shortages lack bandwidth for mandated implementations
Mandates without adequate support will harm already-struggling providers
Voluntary adoption allows organizations to adopt when ready

positions.label: Mandatory Requirements
positions.stance: Require health systems to implement AI within defined timeframes. ScriptChain Health is a proponent of this position.
positions.supportLevel: Very small minority (1-2 commenters)
keyArguments: Short-term costs outweighed by long-term benefits
Without mandates, adoption will remain too slow
Market incentives insufficient to drive necessary change

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to prioritize foundational data infrastructure investments before AI models or fund both simultaneously.

positions.label: Data Infrastructure First
positions.stance: Invest in foundational data quality and interoperability before AI models. Proponents include Scientix.ai, Leap Orbit, and Interstella.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters, primarily technology vendors and data specialists
keyArguments: Application-first paradigms create compounding integration burden
Data workers spend over 40% of time cleaning and reconciling data
AI models are only as good as underlying data infrastructure

positions.label: Simultaneous Investment
positions.stance: Provide funding for both AI tools and supporting infrastructure simultaneously. Proponents include Providence, RBMA, and ADA.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-20 commenters, primarily healthcare providers and trade associations
keyArguments: Organizations need comprehensive support, not sequential investments
Infrastructure alone won't drive adoption without tool funding
Practical needs include everything from IT closet space to staff training

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Substantial upfront costs with uncertain or delayed ROI, lack of internal IT capacity and governance expertise, and workflow disruption during implementation. Healthcare providers uniquely emphasize the operational reality of implementing AI in clinical settings—the need for physical infrastructure (IT closets, wiring, wireless capabilities), the challenge of maintaining care delivery during transitions, and the burden of continuous monitoring and governance.
specificPoints: Providence emphasizes basic needs like making space in IT closet or adding new rooms for additional wiring and fans
WellSpan Health notes that responsible use requires human oversight, safety reporting, continuous performance auditing - all adding costs
Memorial Sloan Kettering provides detailed experience with FDA-approved mammography AI showing burden of aggregating outputs for monitoring

Proposed solutions include one-time grants for qualifying hospitals (rural and safety net) for IT upgrades, supplemental payments for AI governance and monitoring, and time-limited implementation support and demonstration models

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial barriers for small, independent, and rural member practices, unclear ROI and misaligned incentive structures, and disproportionate burden on under-resourced members. Trade associations uniquely represent the aggregate voice of their membership, often highlighting how policies affect the most vulnerable members rather than well-resourced academic medical centers.
specificPoints: AAFP notes this places heavier burden on rural and safety-net health care organizations lacking capacity for thorough product assessments
AADA observes that financial cost to practices may be prohibitive, particularly for smaller, rural practices
AMA emphasizes that independent practices operate with lean staffing and limited IT capacity yet are expected to navigate increasingly complex health care administration rules

Proposed solutions include federal and state grants for small, independent practices, standardized evaluation frameworks capturing total cost of ownership, tiered governance requirements scaled to organization capacity, and dedicated reimbursement pathways and implementation assistance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: EHR integration costs and custom engineering requirements, regulatory-driven price escalation passed to customers, and unclear reimbursement pathways limiting market development. Technology vendors uniquely highlight the supply-side barriers: how regulatory compliance costs increase product pricing, how lack of interoperability standards forces custom integrations, and how market uncertainty limits investment in innovation.
specificPoints: COGA Labs notes that each health system integration requires custom engineering work that favors large incumbents over innovative startups
Epic Systems observes that costs driven by compute, storage, and model usage are highly variable and difficult for organizations to predict, budget, or govern
Leap Orbit explains that currently every new AI deployment requires custom, site-specific data mapping creating significant training debt

Proposed solutions include enhanced interoperability standards to eliminate bespoke training debt, streamlined regulatory pathways to reduce development costs, federal repository of validated AI use cases with demonstrated ROI, and tariff exemptions for AI tools and technologies

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Underfunded research infrastructure, gap between well-resourced and under-resourced health systems, and lack of published research on implementation costs. Academic commenters uniquely emphasize the research gaps—the lack of longitudinal studies on workforce impacts, insufficient documentation of implementation costs, and the need for evidence-based evaluation frameworks.
specificPoints: CRA/CCC notes the gap between areas with and without AI infrastructure is increasingly difficult to overcome
RUPRI Health Panel identifies lack of published research discussing upfront capital expenditures and integration costs required for AI implementation
Satyadhar Joshi observes that healthcare organizations operate under unprecedented workforce strain
EPSCoR and IDeA program states identified as needing additional resources

Proposed solutions include additional resources for clinical care in EPSCoR and IDeA program states, investment in continuous education and training programs, research on cumulative costs of maintaining AI-enabled infrastructure, and operational readiness assessments before implementation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Organizational readiness gaps beyond technology, failed implementations eroding confidence in AI, and need for governance maturity before adoption. Consultants uniquely emphasize the change management dimension—that successful AI adoption requires leadership alignment, workflow optimization, and cultural readiness that many organizations lack.
specificPoints: SANCIAN LLC advocates readiness before reimbursement—premature adoption without readiness wastes resources and erodes trust
Mosaic Life Tech provides detailed proposal for Regional Extension Center-style technical assistance
Karina Lupercio notes that most significant barriers to AI adoption are not technical—they are organizational, administrative, and incentive-based

Proposed solutions include Organizational AI Readiness Grants, technical assistance models for under-resourced organizations, governance maturity as prerequisite for AI-related payment incentives, and operational readiness assessments before federal capital commitment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Structural barriers reflecting day-to-day realities of safety-net care, limited internal capacity to vet, govern, and monitor AI solutions, and risk of AI tools trained on non-representative data harming their populations. Safety-net providers uniquely emphasize that their barriers are structural, not philosophical—they want to adopt AI but face real constraints that policy must address.
specificPoints: Community Care Cooperative states these barriers are structural, not philosophical, and they reflect the day-to-day realities of operating in safety-net primary care, and with the right federal policy levers, they are addressable

Proposed solutions include leveraging trusted intermediaries and dissemination hubs, ATTUNE-like models pairing developers with safety-net providers, and implementation-focused hubs (Health AI Partnerships)

noteworthyInsights.insight: The Readiness Bind Paradox: Dr. Pal Randhawa, a neurosurgeon with trauma center experience, identifies a critical paradox where adoption is throttled by a Readiness Bind - hospitals lack cognitive bandwidth to adopt tools that don't immediately reduce operational friction. This suggests that AI tools designed to address long-term problems may fail to gain adoption precisely because organizations are too overwhelmed to implement them—even when those tools would ultimately reduce their burden.

The Data Ingestion Tax: Leap Orbit introduces a compelling framing that the greatest administrative burden in clinical AI adoption is the data ingestion tax – the cost of cleaning dirty data. This reframes the barrier from AI is expensive to data preparation is expensive, suggesting that infrastructure investments may yield greater returns than AI tool subsidies.

Regional Extension Center Precedent: Mosaic Life Tech provides detailed evidence that the Regional Extension Center model—which helped 134,000 primary care providers achieve Meaningful Use at rates of 68% vs. 12% for non-participants—offers a proven template for AI governance support. This historical precedent suggests that technical assistance programs can dramatically reduce adoption disparities.

The Capital vs. Operational Mismatch: TapestryHealth articulates a structural problem in the SNF sector where AI requires upfront Capital Expense (sensors), but savings (reduced hospitalizations) accrue to Payer (Medicare), not Facility. This insight applies broadly across healthcare settings and suggests that payment model reform may be as important as direct subsidies.

Dentistry's Infrastructure Gap: The American Dental Association notes that dentistry has not benefited from appropriations to support electronic health record infrastructure development, a prerequisite for AI integration. This highlights how historical policy decisions create path dependencies that affect current AI adoption capacity—and suggests that some sectors may need foundational investments before AI-specific support.

The Vendor Pricing Problem: The American College of Radiology identifies a market dynamic where some AI vendors may capture a disproportionate share of the technical fee associated with a medical procedure, creating substantial financial burdens for facilities and limiting broader adoption. This suggests that even with adequate reimbursement, vendor pricing practices may prevent sustainable adoption.

MSK's Real-World Monitoring Challenge: Memorial Sloan Kettering provides a concrete example of monitoring burden where their FDA-approved mammography AI stores outputs within each patient's exam jacket in PACS with no built-in support to aggregate scores into usable format. This illustrates how even well-resourced institutions struggle with the practical mechanics of AI governance.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Rural providers consistently identified as facing the most severe barriers, including lack of broadband, IT staff, and capital. Multiple commenters from CRA, AAFP, Providence, and RUPRI specifically call out rural communities as requiring targeted support. EPSCoR and IDeA program states identified as needing additional resources. AI governance capacity concentrated in well-resourced health systems, typically in major metropolitan areas.

Facility-Type Patterns: Academic Medical Centers generally have governance infrastructure but still face monitoring burden and vendor limitations. Community Hospitals face moderate barriers and may benefit from health system affiliation. Independent/Small Practices face most severe barriers lacking IT capacity, governance expertise, and capital. Safety-Net/FQHCs face structural barriers but express strong desire to adopt with emphasis on equity concerns. Post-Acute/Long-Term Care faces unique infrastructure challenges including unreliable connectivity, legacy systems, and organizational instability. Specialty Practices face sector-specific challenges with dentistry notably excluded from historical EHR funding.

Experience Correlations: Organizations with AI governance experience like MSK, Providence, and Advocate Health provide more nuanced perspectives on monitoring burden and vendor limitations. Frontline clinicians emphasize cognitive load and workflow disruption. Technology vendors more likely to emphasize interoperability and regulatory streamlining. Trade associations more likely to emphasize member diversity and need for tiered approaches.

Unintended Consequences Identified: AAFP notes that reducing federal certification requirements shifts significant responsibility onto purchasers to evaluate whether tools meet functionality and interoperability standards, disproportionately affecting low-resourced practices. Penn NeuroVERT observes that when compliance requirements are extensive, development and validation costs increase and are passed along as higher pricing. COGA Labs warns that integration costs concentrate AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology. AMA predicts that when administrative overhead expands, consequences are predictable: less time with patients, delayed services, reduced capacity to take new patients.

keyQuotations.quote: Adoption often stalls not because of model performance, but because of structural, financial, and organizational friction.
These barriers are structural, not philosophical, and they reflect the day-to-day realities of operating in safety-net primary care. With the right federal policy levers, they are addressable.

In PALTC, binding constraints are frequently infrastructure, workforce bandwidth, data integrity, and organizational instability—conditions repeatedly identified in long-term care implementation research as barriers to introducing AI-enabled solutions and other digital technologies at scale.
The digital divide is already being reinforced in the AI space, with rural communities lacking IT staff to deploy and configure AI tools.
This concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology.

Community centers have limited resources to adopt new therapies due to the lack of support for information at the point of care. Additionally, they lack access to sophisticated technology support teams to help them adopt new AI technologies.

In some cases, AI vendors may capture a disproportionate share of the technical fee associated with a medical procedure, creating substantial financial burdens for facilities and limiting broader adoption. As a result, while the underlying technology may perform to expectation, real-world deployment may be unsustainable and impractical due to cost recovery problems.
Without clear, predictable financial signals, even highly effective tools may struggle to gain traction among financially constrained or operationally stretched providers.
A facility may appear fully staffed on paper, yet remain operationally degraded due to unmeasured cognitive load.
The greatest administrative burden in clinical AI adoption is the 'data ingestion' tax – the cost of cleaning 'dirty' data.
Dentistry has not benefited from appropriations to support electronic health record infrastructure development, a prerequisite for AI integration.
Readiness before reimbursement—premature adoption without readiness wastes resources and erodes trust.

Organizations experiencing acute staffing shortages often lack the bandwidth to implement new solutions, even when one of the primary benefits is to alleviate long-term burnout and re-balance workload.

Similar model for AI governance could leverage existing infrastructure at HRSA (through FQHCs), SAMHSA, or other agencies to help smaller organizations develop oversight capabilities they currently lack.

Department should assemble a repository of AI use cases with demonstrated ROI, conduct independent research to validate vendor claims, and highlight metrics relevant to healthcare administrators' bottom-line decisions.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples, data citations, and concrete policy recommendations. The discourse demonstrates sophisticated understanding of implementation challenges and systemic barriers.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific data points (e.g., 86% vs. 37% AI adoption rates, HFMA survey findings, Regional Extension Center outcomes), though some claims rely on organizational experience rather than published research. Several commenters note the lack of published research on implementation costs as a gap.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from patients and patient advocacy organizations. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are often represented through trade associations rather than direct comment. Post-acute and long-term care sector underrepresented relative to acute care. International perspectives on implementation challenges absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The analysis reveals interconnected barriers where financial constraints, infrastructure gaps, workforce limitations, and governance requirements compound each other. Solutions require coordinated policy interventions across multiple domains rather than single-point fixes.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that healthcare AI systems require robust auditability infrastructure to maintain accountability, with virtually all 40 commenters agreeing that the ability to reconstruct AI-influenced decisions is essential for patient safety, regulatory compliance, and trust. The central tension lies between those advocating for cryptographic, hardware-level proof systems versus those supporting standardized logging and metadata approaches—a debate about the rigor and technical sophistication required. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend that HHS establish federal baseline requirements for audit trails, provenance tracking, and decision documentation, with particular emphasis on distinguishing AI-generated content from human-authored information in clinical records.

consensusPoints.text: Auditability is foundational to AI accountability in healthcare. Nearly all commenters explicitly identify auditability as essential for responsible AI deployment. This consensus spans business, academic, provider, and individual stakeholders. Representative examples include statements that accountability requires the ability to reconstruct what AI recommended and what humans decided in specific instances, that every AI decision should be auditable for quality and compliance, and that auditability is one of three priority areas HHS should address for AI in clinical workflows.

exceptions.text: One commenter emphasizes that centralized control of all data is neither realistic nor desirable—the goal should be auditability across distributed systems, suggesting the focus should be on reconstructability rather than data consolidation.

consensusPoints.text: AI-generated content must be distinguishable from human-authored information. Commenters emphasize the critical need for metadata and provenance tracking to identify AI involvement in clinical documentation. Currently, both health information technology systems and users do not always know what data was generated or modified by an AI tool or what tool was used—therefore the data can appear falsely, as if it was generated only by a clinical user. Without reliable metadata, learning health systems risk confusing AI-generated content with human-authored information.

Federal baseline standards are needed to reduce duplicative institutional efforts. Commenters call for HHS to establish minimum federal requirements rather than leaving audit standards to individual institutions. Each deploying institution must independently defend ad hoc logging practices—duplicative effort that a federal floor would eliminate. Standardizing expectations for model audit trails would help ensure AI-supported decisions are consistent across industry.

Audit trails must capture clinician override behavior. Commenters addressing clinical decision support agree that logging clinician responses to AI recommendations—including overrides—is essential. Clinician override logging should be part of responsible AI deployment requirements. Structured override monitoring enables evidence-based oversight without imposing blanket restrictions on deployment. Standardized representation of AI outputs in the record should include tool name/version, output, confidence or risk category, and clinician action/override.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing clinical decision support (approximately 20 of 40)

areasOfDebate.topic: Level of Technical Rigor for Audit Evidence
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether audit trails must provide mathematically verifiable, tamper-proof evidence through cryptographic proofs, or whether consistent metadata and logging standards are sufficient to meet accountability needs.

positions.label: Cryptographic Proof Required
positions.stance: Audit trails must provide mathematically verifiable, tamper-proof evidence. FERZ AI argues that deterministic governance generates cryptographic proof bundle capturing inputs, policy state, and verdict for every decision. GLACIS Technologies notes that current audit trails record that requests occurred but don't record which specific controls ran, with what parameters, or why they failed. Michael Bridges proposes that safety violations resulting in shutdown should generate cryptographically signed records at hardware level—completely independent of the AI's operating system.
positions.supportLevel: 6-8 commenters, primarily technology vendors and technical experts
keyArguments: FDA 21 CFR Part 11 requires verifiable electronic records—current AI systems produce outputs, not evidence
Software logs can be spoofed if root environment is compromised
The Replay Test asks: Can any historical decision be reproduced and verified? Can regulator independently confirm what happened?

positions.label: Standardized Logging Sufficient
positions.stance: Consistent metadata and logging standards meet accountability needs. HL7 International's AI Transparency on FHIR Implementation Guide provides implementation guidance for documenting when FHIR resources have been created, updated, or influenced by AI. HIMSS EHR Association notes that standardizing expectations for model audit trails would lead to increased confidence in AI solutions. InterSystems encourages requirements for logging and traceability appropriate to clinical risk.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (~25-30), including most healthcare providers and trade associations
keyArguments: Practical implementation across diverse health IT environments
Builds on existing FHIR and HL7 infrastructure
Achievable without requiring fundamental technology changes

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether authorization and context must be captured at the moment of AI use (invocation-time) or whether recording AI involvement after the fact (post-hoc) meets practical needs.

positions.label: Invocation-Time Capture Essential
positions.stance: Authorization and context must be captured at the moment of AI use. Jennifer Bryan argues that federal guidance should distinguish between retrospective attribution and invocation-bound authorization metadata as separate governance primitives. David Condrey of Writerslogic notes that the key distinction is forensic inference versus prospective proof—every current attribution method is forensic.
positions.supportLevel: 5-7 commenters, primarily technical governance experts
keyArguments: Post-hoc provenance only records associations after the fact, insufficient for accountability
Must capture who was credentialed, acting within scope, and authorized at moment of AI use
Downstream evidence integrity depends on verification at moment of use

positions.label: Post-Hoc Documentation Acceptable
positions.stance: Recording AI involvement after the fact meets practical needs. NeuroFlow states that systems should maintain logs capturing relevant data inputs, decision thresholds, and model version history. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association suggests certification should include lifecycle change logs.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in most provider and vendor comments (~20-25)
keyArguments: Aligns with existing clinical documentation workflows
Less disruptive to implement
Sufficient for most quality improvement and incident review purposes

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether audit requirements should scale with clinical risk level or whether all clinical AI should meet universal minimum audit standards.

positions.label: Risk-Stratified Approach
positions.stance: Audit requirements should scale with clinical risk level. InterSystems advocates for logging and traceability appropriate to clinical risk. Eli Talbert proposes a minimum logging requirement for high-impact AI-enabled CDS.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-20 commenters
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all requirements would burden low-risk applications unnecessarily
Resources should focus on high-impact AI systems
Proportionality enables broader AI adoption

positions.label: Universal Baseline Requirements
positions.stance: All clinical AI should meet minimum audit standards. Kanav Jain argues that decision provenance should be a default output of AI-influenced actions, not an optional feature. ECRI states that systems should meet baseline expectations for traceability including versioning, audit logs, and data provenance.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-15 commenters
keyArguments: Even 'low-risk' AI can have unexpected downstream effects
Consistent standards reduce complexity and confusion
Learning health systems need comprehensive data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Establishing clear technical standards they can build to, ensuring audit requirements don't foreclose innovation, and creating market differentiation through governance capabilities. Vendors emphasize the technical feasibility of sophisticated audit systems and often propose their own solutions as models.
specificPoints: FHIR-aligned AI interaction profiles for standardized audit capabilities
Cryptographic attestation standards for tamper-proof evidence
Model Context Protocol for AI agent interactions
Penguin AI's 'Glass Box' principle where every AI prediction is traceable back to a specific source of truth within the patient record
MHK's audit framework generating 9 report types with 365-day CloudWatch retention and 7-year archive capability

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation within existing workflows, vendor transparency about AI system behavior, and liability protection through documentation. Providers emphasize the operational reality of fragmented systems and the need for audit capabilities that work across vendor boundaries.
specificPoints: Require vendors to provide audit information to end-users
Clear transparency and audit requirements with federal baselines
WellSpan Health's concern about avoiding 'black box' data gaps
Medvidi's Chart Review AI that scans every chart post-visit, identifies compliance gaps, missing documentation, and protocol deviations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Industry-wide consistency in audit standards, building on existing interoperability infrastructure, and avoiding fragmented state-by-state requirements. They emphasize the collective action problem—individual organizations cannot solve audit interoperability alone.
specificPoints: Leverage existing HL7/FHIR standards for AI provenance
Define 'Chain of Provenance' standard for AI-influenced metadata
HL7's AI Transparency on FHIR Implementation Guide
Cooperative Exchange's concern that AI has fallen short where auditability and traceability insufficient to demonstrate transactions were reviewed, approved, or corrected appropriately

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Theoretical soundness of governance frameworks, evidence infrastructure for learning health systems, and ethical implications of AI documentation gaps. They frame auditability as essential infrastructure for scientific advancement, not just compliance.
specificPoints: Governance integrity evaluation frameworks
Open standards for clinical authorship verification
Interstitial Institute's proposal for 'governance integrity evaluation' assessing whether named responsible party is operationally connected to what the system produces

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Technical adequacy of proposed solutions, gap between compliance claims and actual accountability, and precision in governance terminology. They often identify subtle but critical distinctions that other stakeholders overlook.
specificPoints: Seven-element minimum logging requirement
Execution-context metadata that persists through export
Jennifer Bryan's distinction between retrospective attribution and invocation-bound authorization metadata as separate governance primitives

noteworthyInsights.insight: Aviation/finance analogy for evidence infrastructure — GLACIS Technologies draws a compelling parallel: Every industry that scales a powerful technology eventually builds the evidence infrastructure to make it trustworthy at scale—aviation built flight data recorders, financial markets built GAAP, internet security built Certificate Transparency. The question is not whether evidence infrastructure will become standard—it will. The question is whether American institutions build it or import it.

Fragmentation is inevitable; lack of auditability is not — Surgeon and systems advisor Dr. Binita Ashar reframes the problem: This is not primarily a data availability problem. Rather, it is an auditability problem. This insight shifts focus from impossible data centralization to achievable cross-system reconstruction.

Hardware-level 'black box' for AI medical devices — Individual technologist Michael Bridges proposes that safety violations resulting in shutdown should generate cryptographically signed records at hardware level—completely independent of the AI's operating system. This acts as a true, unalterable 'black box' for malpractice adjusters, hospital compliance boards, and the FDA.

Governance integrity vs. performance monitoring distinction — The Interstitial Institute identifies a novel evaluation framework: Governance integrity evaluation would assess whether institutional accountability architecture remains structurally intact under operational conditions—this is evaluable and requires a different assessment framework than performance benchmarking.

Prospective vs. forensic proof distinction — David Condrey articulates a fundamental gap: Every current attribution method is forensic (examines finished document and infers probable authorship), while prospective methods capture evidence during composition that can be verified independently of document content.

Defense AI governance principles applicable to healthcare — Eli Talbert, a defense AI governance expert, brings cross-sector expertise: Current frameworks do not specify how clinician override behavior must be logged, how authority boundaries between model and clinician are operationally defined, or how escalation pathways are monitored over time.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Technical Sophistication Correlates with Audit Rigor Demands — Commenters with deeper technical backgrounds (software architects, cryptography experts, defense AI specialists) consistently advocate for more rigorous audit standards—cryptographic proofs, invocation-time capture, hardware-level logging—while operational stakeholders (providers, administrators) focus on practical logging and metadata standards. This suggests potential tension between technically optimal and operationally feasible solutions.

Vendor vs. User Perspectives on Audit Access — Technology vendors emphasize their audit capabilities as competitive differentiators, while healthcare providers express frustration about 'black box' systems and inability to access audit information. WellSpan Health explicitly recommends requiring vendors to provide audit information to end-users, suggesting current market dynamics don't adequately address user needs.

Standards Organizations Positioned as Bridges — HL7 International and other standards bodies position themselves as essential intermediaries, with HL7 noting its AI Transparency on FHIR Implementation Guide completed first ballot in January 2026. This suggests standards infrastructure is developing but may need federal endorsement to achieve adoption.

Agentic AI Creates New Audit Challenges — Several commenters specifically address autonomous AI agents, noting that traditional audit approaches designed for human-initiated actions may be inadequate. Pair Team proposes Standardized 'On-Behalf-Of' Delegation explicitly linking AI agent's data access to supervising clinician or organization.

Cross-Sector Learning Opportunities — Multiple commenters reference audit standards from other regulated industries—FDA 21 CFR Part 11, CLIA requirements, aviation flight data recorders, financial GAAP—suggesting healthcare AI audit standards could build on established frameworks rather than starting from scratch.

Geographic/Institutional Variation in Readiness — The Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association notes that barriers including safety validation gaps, unclear liability, integration challenges, and funding constraints disproportionately affect smaller and safety-net providers, suggesting audit requirements may need to account for institutional capacity differences.

keyQuotations.quote: Real-world patient safety and accountable deployment are not defined by performance metrics alone—they require systems that preserve reconstructable evidence of how and why decisions were made in context.
This is not primarily a data availability problem. Rather, it is an auditability problem... Fragmentation is inevitable; lack of auditability is not.

Currently, both health information technology systems and users do not always know what data was generated or modified by an AI tool or what tool was used—therefore the data can appear falsely, as if it was generated only by a clinical user.
The question is not whether evidence infrastructure will become standard—it will. The question is whether American institutions build it, or import it.
Chart Review AI flags compliance and quality issues at 100% scale, impossible manually, helping maintain standards without added overhead.
Can any historical decision be reproduced and verified? Can regulator independently confirm what happened?

If a device experiences a safety violation that results in a shutdown or a severed power connection, that event should generate a cryptographically signed record at the hardware level—completely independent of the AI's operating system. This acts as a true, unalterable 'black box' for malpractice adjusters, hospital compliance boards, and the FDA.
Without invocation-time authorization metadata, audit trails may be technically complete yet insufficient to establish accountability when AI systems contribute to documentation or decision support.
Decision provenance should be a default output of AI-influenced actions, not an optional feature.
Trust in AI and data depends not only on algorithms but on surrounding infrastructure that establishes provenance, credibility, and accountability.
Clinical care demands deterministic accountability—ability to trace exactly which data informed a decision and verify it was correct.
Governance should be demonstrable clearly, consistently, and auditably.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of technical and operational challenges. Stakeholders engage substantively with specific implementation details, propose concrete frameworks, and acknowledge tradeoffs. Technical experts provide nuanced distinctions (e.g., forensic vs. prospective proof) while operational stakeholders ground discussion in practical realities.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific regulatory frameworks (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, HIPAA), existing standards (HL7 FHIR), and cross-industry precedents (aviation, finance). Some provide detailed technical specifications and implementation examples. However, empirical evidence of audit system effectiveness in healthcare AI contexts remains limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient and consumer advocacy perspectives are notably underrepresented in this theme. Most comments come from technology vendors, providers, and technical experts. Safety-net providers and smaller healthcare organizations are mentioned as potentially disadvantaged but rarely speak directly. International perspectives on audit standards are largely absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High — The theme involves intersecting technical, legal, operational, and ethical dimensions. Key tensions include cryptographic rigor vs. practical implementation, invocation-time vs. post-hoc capture, and universal vs. risk-stratified requirements. Resolution requires balancing competing stakeholder interests and technical constraints.

13.themeDescription: Patient-Facing AI and Consumer Applications. This theme encompasses all comments addressing AI tools that interact directly with patients, including chatbots, symptom checkers, and patient engagement tools. || It includes concerns about patient-facing AI safety, debates about appropriate uses of patient-facing AI, proposals for consumer protection requirements, and discussions of patient trust in AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal deep tension between the promise of patient-facing AI to expand access and engagement versus serious safety concerns, particularly in mental health applications.
