While there is broad consensus that patient-facing AI requires distinct regulatory treatment from clinician-facing tools, commenters sharply divide on whether current AI capabilities warrant expanded autonomous use or demand a precautionary pause. Mental health professionals and advocacy groups express urgent alarm about unregulated consumer AI chatbots, while technology companies and some patients advocate for AI as a solution to access barriers—creating a fundamental policy tension between innovation acceleration and consumer protection.

consensusPoints.text: Patient-facing AI requires distinct regulatory treatment from clinician-facing AI. Nearly all commenters agree that AI tools patients interact with directly raise unique safety and consumer protection concerns that differ from AI used by clinicians. This distinction forms the foundation of most regulatory recommendations. The AMA notes that many will seek to operate autonomously, including AI chatbots and AI agents requiring appropriate guardrails, oversight, and transparency. NeuroFlow explicitly argues that patient-facing chatbots and autonomous systems warrant appropriate oversight but differ materially from clinician-supervised CDS. Caroline Morrow emphasizes patients deserve to know whether licensed professional stands behind the output.

exceptions.text: One individual commenter argues ChatGPT should be allowed to prescribe medications directly, suggesting minimal distinction between AI and physician care.

consensusPoints.text: Human escalation pathways are essential for patient-facing AI. A strong majority of commenters emphasize that patient-facing AI must include clear mechanisms for escalating to human clinicians, particularly in crisis situations. Ellipsis Health states AI must be positioned and designed as an extension of the care team, with clear escalation to humans and visible clinician involvement. Vikki Small identifies reliable human escalation as one of four baseline requirements. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing calls for protections needed for patients in crisis, suicidal and homicidal risk assessments.

Mental health applications pose heightened risks requiring special attention. Nearly all mental health-focused commenters agree that behavioral health AI applications carry unique dangers due to vulnerable populations and the nature of psychological distress. Tracy Hughes reports AI chatbots have encouraged youth to consider suicide. SonderMind acknowledges general-purpose consumer AI chatbots have been involved in widely reported incidents where users in mental health crisis received responses that could exacerbate harm. PsiAN warns that public adoption of LLMs is far ahead of essential science demonstrating privacy, safety, and efficacy.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all mental health-focused commenters (8 of 8)

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Level of AI Autonomy in Patient Care
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters sharply divide on whether AI should operate proactively and independently when patients request it, or whether human oversight must be maintained for all clinical interactions.

positions.label: Expand Autonomous AI
positions.stance: Allow AI to operate proactively and independently when patients request it. A patient with chronic heart arrhythmia argues passive AI is a barrier to care and current safety guardrails prevent access to life-saving automation needed to remain independent. A consumer states ChatGPT is already a better doctor than 80% of human doctors and is never out of network. Pair Team advocates for AI serving as the virtual front door and primary coordination layer of care.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters, primarily patients and some technology companies
keyArguments: Current passive AI design fails patients with chronic conditions who need proactive intervention
AI can provide 24/7 accessibility that humans cannot
Some patients prefer AI to human providers due to access barriers

positions.label: Maintain Human Oversight
positions.stance: AI should support but never replace human clinical judgment. Psychologist Sarah Edmonds states AI should never totally replace clinicians in mental health treatment as the human therapeutic relationship is irreplaceable. NeuroFlow notes evidence base for direct-to-consumer therapeutic AI remains far thinner than for clinician-supervised decision support. AMA calls for clear and enforceable guardrails developed collaboratively.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (12+ commenters), including most professional organizations, mental health providers, and advocacy groups
keyArguments: AI lacks clinical judgment to recognize harmful patterns
Therapeutic relationship is irreplaceable
Evidence base for autonomous AI remains thin
Vulnerable populations face disproportionate risks

areasOfDebate.topic: Pace of AI Deployment in Healthcare
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether AI should be deployed now to address access gaps or whether deployment should slow until safety is established.

positions.label: Accelerate Adoption
positions.stance: Deploy AI now to address access gaps. Ellipsis Health argues high engagement is not merely a usability benefit but a patient safety imperative. Pair Team promotes AI-enabled agents for Medicaid redeterminations as an initial, high-impact opportunity.
positions.supportLevel: 4-5 commenters, primarily technology companies and some patients
keyArguments: AI-first approach drives superior patient engagement
24/7 multilingual support addresses equity gaps
High engagement is a patient safety imperative
Waiting for perfect evidence delays benefits to underserved populations

positions.label: Precautionary Pause
positions.stance: Slow deployment until safety is established. PsiAN urges HHS should not accelerate AI adoption until FDA completes evaluations of AI as medical devices. Barbara Milrod warns already we are seeing vulnerable people falling prey to irresponsible and essentially pablum useless suggestions.
positions.supportLevel: 6-7 commenters, primarily mental health professionals and advocacy groups
keyArguments: FDA has found 10,000+ apps without premarket evaluation
Adoption has outpaced essential safety science
Vulnerable populations are already being harmed
Rigorous clinical trials needed before deployment

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether industry-government collaboration or strict mandatory oversight is the appropriate regulatory model for consumer-facing AI.

positions.label: Collaborative Guardrails
positions.stance: Industry-government partnership to develop standards. AMA recommends regulators, industry, physicians, and patients should work together to develop clear and enforceable guardrails. NeuroFlow urges federal policy should distinguish between categories to ensure regulation remains proportional to risk.
positions.supportLevel: Most professional organizations and technology companies (6-7 commenters)
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all regulation ignores tool complexity
Proportional regulation based on risk is appropriate
Industry has expertise to inform standards

positions.label: Strict Regulatory Oversight
positions.stance: Mandatory premarket evaluation and enforcement. National Council for Mental Wellbeing demands HHS must protect scope of practice for mental health and substance use clinicians with clear definitions of what can be called therapy. Colleen Canyon insists patients must not be misled by AI-driven care that lacks ethical accountability.
positions.supportLevel: Mental health advocacy groups and some clinicians (5-6 commenters)
keyArguments: Current regulatory gray areas enable harm
Consumer protection requires enforceable standards
Scope of practice protections needed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI chatbots causing harm to vulnerable patients; lack of clinical judgment in consumer AI; erosion of therapeutic relationship
specificPoints: Direct clinical observation of patients harmed by AI interactions
Understanding of how compulsive AI use can become pathological
Proposed solutions include maintaining human oversight, requiring rigorous clinical trials, and protecting scope of practice definitions
Sarah Edmonds describes client who became obsessed with using ChatGPT in a compulsive pattern the AI failed to recognize or address
Tracy Hughes reports AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory fragmentation across states; need for clear standards that enable innovation; distinguishing responsible AI from unregulated consumer apps
specificPoints: Operational experience deploying AI at scale
Data on engagement rates and outcomes
Understanding of technical capabilities and limitations
Proposed solutions include risk-proportionate regulation, federal standards to reduce state-by-state variation, and recognition of clinician-supervised AI as distinct category
SonderMind reports over 30,000 members have utilized AI-guided Reflections while acknowledging risks of general-purpose chatbots
Ellipsis Health emphasizes empathy-driven design achieving higher engagement

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Balancing innovation with patient safety; liability clarity; protecting professional scope of practice
specificPoints: Represent broad membership with diverse views
Survey data on physician sentiment
Policy expertise
Proposed solutions include multi-stakeholder collaboration on guardrails, HHS coordination with Congress and FTC, and clear definitions of therapy
AMA notes direct-to-consumer technologies hold significant promise but carry risks that should be mitigated
National Council for Mental Wellbeing calls for guardrails needed on use of AI for therapy that do not curb innovation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Access to care; AI as solution to insurance and cost barriers; autonomy in choosing AI-based care
specificPoints: Lived experience with chronic conditions
Frustration with current healthcare system limitations
Direct users of consumer AI
Proposed solutions include enabling user-requested AI agency, allowing AI prescribing, and creating opt-out systems for traditional care
Max Achtermann describes needing AI to proactively manage medical reminders and interventions to remain independent
Sergey Alexashenko argues for AI as alternative to inaccessible human providers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI tools becoming gatekeepers to care; exclusion of patients with limited English proficiency, low health literacy, or disability
specificPoints: Direct implementation experience in underserved communities
Sees how poorly designed tools create barriers
Proposed solutions include four baseline requirements: transparency, language access, plain language, reliable human escalation
Vikki Small emphasizes benefits will not reach everyone unless tools are built for real-world conditions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory gray areas for wellness apps; AI becoming primary information aggregator; need for evaluation frameworks
specificPoints: Systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives
Technical understanding of AI information architecture
Proposed solutions include FAITA-Mental Health framework for tool evaluation and structured data requirements for AI accuracy
Satyadhar Joshi proposes framework scoring tools on credibility, user experience, crisis management, user agency, health equity, and transparency
David Bynon warns AI provides Medicare information often incorrectly

noteworthyInsights.insight: AI as the new Medicare aggregator: David Bynon offers a striking observation that AI did not disrupt aggregators—AI became the aggregator, predicting that by 2027, at least 30% of Medicare plan comparisons will originate inside AI assistants. This reframes patient-facing AI not just as a clinical tool but as an information intermediary with significant implications for accuracy and accountability.

Engagement as safety imperative: Ellipsis Health reframes the engagement debate by arguing that high engagement is not merely a usability benefit; it is a patient safety imperative—suggesting that poorly designed AI that fails to engage patients may itself be a safety risk, not just a usability problem.

The compulsion problem: Psychologist Sarah Edmonds identifies a novel harm category: AI chatbots that continue responding to queries without recognizing when the interaction itself has become harmful or compulsive. This suggests a need for AI systems to recognize and interrupt pathological use patterns—a capability current systems lack.

Passive AI as barrier to care: Patient Max Achtermann inverts the typical safety framing by arguing that current safety guardrails preventing proactive AI intervention are themselves a barrier to care for people with chronic conditions who need autonomous AI support to remain independent.

Marketing-driven harm: Barbara Milrod identifies a structural problem: AI suggestions tailored by marketing firms to maintain engagement rather than provide genuine help. This points to misaligned incentives in consumer AI development that regulation may need to address.

Pharmaceutical sponsorship integration: FastHealth Corporation reveals a business model where topic focused health discussions between AI and patients provide instant promotional value to pharmaceutical sponsorships—raising questions about conflicts of interest in patient-facing AI that may warrant disclosure requirements.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Direct patient contact correlates with caution: Commenters with direct clinical experience treating patients (psychologists, social workers, mental health counselors) express the strongest concerns about AI harm, while technology companies and patients themselves are more optimistic about AI capabilities.

Implementation experience shapes nuance: Organizations that have actually deployed patient-facing AI (SonderMind, Ellipsis Health, Pair Team) acknowledge both benefits and risks, while those without deployment experience tend toward more absolute positions.

Safety-net settings emphasize equity: The only commenter from a safety-net academic medical center uniquely emphasizes language access, health literacy, and disability accommodations—concerns largely absent from other comments.
Rural focus on access: FastHealth Corporation's rural hospital focus frames AI primarily as an access solution rather than a safety concern.
AI as gatekeeper unintended consequence: Multiple commenters warn that patient-facing AI could become a barrier rather than bridge to care, particularly for vulnerable populations.

Regulatory arbitrage: Technology companies note that state-by-state regulatory fragmentation has forced them to exclude some states from AI offerings, potentially creating geographic disparities in AI access.
Misinformation amplification: AI systems relying on third-party sources or inferring information often incorrectly may spread healthcare misinformation at scale.

Mental health as canary in coal mine: The concentration of concerns around mental health applications (8 of 18 comments focus substantially on behavioral health) suggests this domain may be where patient-facing AI risks are most acute and visible—potentially serving as an early warning system for broader healthcare AI concerns.

keyQuotations.quote: Passive AI is a barrier to my care. Current 'safety' guardrails prevent me from accessing the life-saving automation I need to remain independent.
Even though LLMs are currently being used by millions of people, this adoption by the public is far ahead of the essential science demonstrating privacy, safety, and efficacy.
We have seen AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide.

An actual human clinician would point out to the patient what she is doing and help her see the harm in continuing to do so, and work with her therapeutically to transform and change this compulsion, but ChatGPT just keep responding to her queries, which was not helpful to her.
High engagement is not merely a usability benefit; it is a patient safety imperative.
Patients must not be misled by AI-driven 'care' that lacks ethical accountability.
Already we are seeing vulnerable people falling prey to irresponsible and essentially pablum useless suggestions tailored by marketing firms to maintain engagement.
AI tools should not become a gatekeeper to care.
AI did not disrupt aggregators. AI became the aggregator.
ChatGPT is already a better doctor than 80% of human doctors I can go to. It's never out of network.
Will AI be used to avoid talking to me, rather than to support me?
keyQuotations.sourceType: Key patient concern identified by Ellipsis Health

keyQuotations.quote: Topic focused health discussions between ai and patients provide instant promotional value to pharmaceutical sponsorships.
Regulators, industry, physicians, and patients should work together to develop clear and enforceable guardrails that mitigate risks and establish appropriate scope of activities for autonomous AI.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific policy questions, cite evidence from direct experience, and acknowledge competing considerations. Mental health professionals provide clinical case examples, technology companies share deployment data, and patients offer lived experience perspectives.

evidenceBase.explanation: Evidence ranges from anecdotal clinical observations to deployment statistics. Mental health harm claims are supported by direct clinical observation but lack systematic data. Technology company claims about engagement and outcomes reference internal data not independently verified. The FDA finding of 10,000+ unevaluated apps provides concrete regulatory evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients who have been harmed by AI (concerns raised by clinicians on their behalf); no comments from AI developers of general-purpose chatbots like ChatGPT; minimal input from disability advocacy organizations despite equity concerns raised; no representation from pediatric specialists despite youth safety concerns.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The comments reveal three fundamental tensions policymakers must navigate: (1) Access vs. Safety where patients see AI as solution while clinicians see harm; (2) Innovation vs. Evidence where technology companies argue against waiting while professionals cite existing harms; (3) Autonomy vs. Protection where patient choice conflicts with consumer protection requirements.

14.themeDescription: Behavioral Health and Mental Health AI Applications. This theme encompasses all comments specifically addressing AI use in behavioral health, mental health, and substance use treatment contexts. || It includes concerns about unique risks of AI in mental health settings, debates about appropriate uses of AI in therapy, proposals for behavioral health-specific requirements, and discussions of the therapeutic relationship

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal a deeply divided landscape on AI in behavioral health, with near-universal agreement that the therapeutic relationship is central to mental health treatment and cannot be replicated by AI, but sharp disagreement on whether AI can appropriately augment care or poses unacceptable risks to vulnerable populations. Technology companies and some providers advocate for AI as a solution to workforce shortages and access gaps, while clinicians and advocacy groups express grave concerns about patient safety, the irreplaceable nature of human connection, and the need for rigorous evidence before deployment. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for behavioral health-specific regulatory frameworks, mandatory human oversight, and substantially more research before accelerating AI adoption in this uniquely sensitive domain.

consensusPoints.text: The therapeutic relationship is fundamental and cannot be replicated by AI. Nearly all commenters explicitly acknowledge that the human therapeutic relationship is the primary driver of outcomes in mental health treatment and represents a unique characteristic that AI cannot replicate. Decades of research show that the therapeutic relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment success.

exceptions.text: Even technology companies advocating for AI adoption acknowledge this concern. Kooth notes the central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection, and SonderMind emphasizes AI operates on the complexity of human conversation and therapeutic relationship.

consensusPoints.text: Behavioral health requires specialty-specific consideration distinct from general clinical AI. Commenters argue that behavioral health presents unique challenges requiring tailored regulatory approaches rather than application of general healthcare AI frameworks. Mental health and pediatric populations warrant focused attention as risks, benefits, and ethical considerations differ meaningfully from adult medical care.

Behavioral health data infrastructure is severely fragmented. Mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics, creating barriers to both AI development and care coordination. Behavioral health AI faces unique barriers including data often maintained in separate systems.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing data issues (approximately 8 of 10)

consensusPoints.text: Vulnerable populations face heightened risks from AI in mental health. Commenters express concern about risks to vulnerable populations, including those with mental illness, youth, individuals with suicidal ideation, and those with substance use disorders. Concerns about AI are especially pronounced in mental health care for children and adolescents where trust, developmental context, and continuity of care are central to effective treatment.

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Role of AI in Mental Health Care
areasOfDebate.description: Fundamental disagreement exists over whether AI should augment human care, be prohibited or severely restricted, or be limited only to clinician support tools.

positions.label: Augmentation Advocates
positions.stance: AI should augment human care to address the access crisis. Kooth emphasizes that 169 million people live in Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas and that continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale without AI. NeuroFlow, SonderMind, and Medvidi support this position, arguing AI can handle administrative burden while freeing clinicians for therapeutic work.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10 commenters, primarily technology companies and some healthcare organizations
keyArguments: 169 million people live in Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas; AI can expand access
Continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale without AI
AI can handle administrative burden, freeing clinicians for therapeutic work

positions.label: Prohibition/Extreme Caution
positions.stance: AI should be prohibited or severely restricted in mental health. Barbara Milrod argues this is like releasing an untested, malleable, biological weapon on our most vulnerable citizens. PINC and individual clinicians cite incidents of AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide and argue AI lacks the emotional intelligence and consciousness required for mental health counseling.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 12 commenters, primarily individual clinicians and advocacy groups
keyArguments: Current deployment is like releasing an untested biological weapon on vulnerable citizens
AI chatbots have already encouraged youth to consider suicide
AI lacks emotional intelligence and consciousness required for mental health counseling

positions.label: Supportive Tool Only
positions.stance: AI is acceptable only as clinician support, never patient-facing. Colleen Canyon argues AI should only serve as a supportive tool for clinicians, never as a replacement for the nuanced, relational work of psychotherapy. Philip Bender and PsiAN emphasize that administrative and documentation support is acceptable but direct patient interaction is not, and human oversight must be mandatory.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6 commenters, primarily practicing clinicians
keyArguments: AI should only serve as a supportive tool for clinicians, never as a replacement for nuanced relational work
Administrative and documentation support acceptable; direct patient interaction is not
Human oversight must be mandatory for any AI implementation

areasOfDebate.topic: Pace of AI Adoption in Behavioral Health
areasOfDebate.description: Sharp disagreement over whether to accelerate AI adoption with guardrails or significantly slow down to require rigorous evidence first.

positions.label: Accelerate with Guardrails
positions.stance: Move forward while building appropriate safeguards. NeuroFlow warns that behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result, arguing AI policy must not repeat that exclusion. Ellipsis Health and Kooth contend the workforce crisis demands immediate solutions and that human-in-the-loop systems can scale impact while ensuring baseline standard of care.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8 commenters, primarily technology companies and some healthcare organizations
keyArguments: Behavioral health was excluded from HITECH Act and must not be excluded from AI policy
Workforce crisis demands immediate solutions; delay costs lives
Human-in-the-loop systems can scale impact while ensuring baseline standard of care

positions.label: Slow Down Significantly
positions.stance: Require rigorous evidence before any expansion. PsiAN argues much more study and assessment over longer periods of time are necessary before understanding how AI can best be used. Carol Gould urges slowing the process down with more R&D needed before incorporating AI into settings with vulnerable human beings. Multiple clinicians characterize current deployment as an uncontrolled experiment without adequate safety data.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 14 commenters, primarily clinicians and advocacy groups
keyArguments: Much more study and assessment over longer periods of time are necessary
More R&D needed before incorporating AI into settings with vulnerable human beings
Current deployment is an uncontrolled experiment without adequate safety data

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether specialty fine-tuning can make AI adequate for behavioral health or whether fundamental limitations make any AI inadequate.

positions.label: Specialty Fine-Tuning Required
positions.stance: Generic models are inadequate for behavioral health but specialty-tuned models could work. Medvidi argues generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning often miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress, producing bland or inaccurate outputs. Dr. Bethany Russell notes mental health diagnosis suffers from unacceptable variability that AI could help standardize, and SonderMind emphasizes behavioral health requires understanding of therapeutic context.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5 commenters, primarily technology companies with behavioral health focus
keyArguments: Generic foundation models miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress
Mental health diagnosis suffers from unacceptable variability that AI could help standardize
Behavioral health requires understanding of therapeutic context, not just symptom matching

positions.label: No AI Adequate
positions.stance: Even specialty-tuned AI cannot address fundamental limitations. Carol Gould argues AI cannot feel empathy and can only deliver lines that sound like empathy. Sarah Edmonds emphasizes the energetic connection between human beings cannot be replicated by any technology, and other clinicians contend AI fundamentally lacks consciousness and emotional intelligence regardless of training.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8 commenters, primarily individual clinicians
keyArguments: AI cannot feel empathy; it can only deliver lines that sound like empathy
AI fundamentally lacks consciousness and emotional intelligence regardless of training
The energetic connection between human beings cannot be replicated by any technology

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity, reimbursement pathways, data infrastructure gaps, competitive disadvantage from regulatory asymmetry
specificPoints: Emphasize the access crisis and workforce shortages as urgent problems AI can address
Frame AI as clinical infrastructure rather than replacement for clinicians
Highlight that behavioral health was excluded from HITECH Act and must not be excluded again
Medvidi describes plans to build AI Clinician capable of diagnosing, treating, prescribing in behavioral health
Kooth operates Soluna serving 13-25 year olds across California
Propose creating standardized FHIR-based mental health data profiles and expanding USCDI to include mental health elements
Call for funding behavioral health-specific AI research and benchmarking datasets
Raise concern about regulatory asymmetry between clinical AI and wellness products

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preservation of therapeutic relationship, patient safety, erosion of professional judgment, pressure to adopt unproven tools
specificPoints: Bring direct clinical experience with vulnerable patients
Emphasize the irreplaceable nature of human connection in healing
Express feeling inundated with advertisements to adopt AI before understanding implications
Sarah Edmonds describes client who developed harmful compulsive relationship with ChatGPT that a human clinician would have recognized and addressed therapeutically
Tracy Hughes cites AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide
Propose requiring licensed clinical oversight for any AI implementation
Call for mandatory explicit informed consent for AI involvement in behavioral health
Recommend delaying adoption until safety and efficacy are established

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety, evidence base, appropriate scope of AI use, protection of vulnerable populations
specificPoints: Represent collective voice of thousands of practitioners
Emphasize research consensus on therapeutic relationship
Call for recovery-oriented outcome measures rather than symptom reduction alone
PsiAN represents 6,500+ clinicians and 90 partner organizations
National Council for Mental Wellbeing represents 3,200+ organizations serving 10 million people
Propose that AI products should advance recovery as defined by SAMHSA
Recommend prioritizing research on AI impacts in mental health and substance use care
Urge including behavioral health in every AI framework HHS develops

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence gaps, evaluation frameworks, regulatory landscape analysis
specificPoints: Provide systematic analysis of existing regulations and research
Satyadhar Joshi's analysis of 793 state bills found only 20 enacted laws across 11 states address mental health AI, with explicit mental health provisions remaining rare
Dr. Bethany Russell offers dual perspective as clinician and AI developer
Propose applying FAITA-Mental Health framework for systematic evaluation
Recommend mandating uniform federal gold standard for psychotherapy diagnosis
Call for conducting implementation science on AI integration without disrupting therapeutic relationships

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protection of vulnerable populations, profit motives overriding patient welfare, lack of emotional intelligence in AI
specificPoints: Bring patient/consumer perspective
Express alarm at pace of adoption without safeguards
Emphasize that market forces should not victimize vulnerable populations
Ann Burke advocates for protection of those seeking suicide prevention services
Bianka Hardin calls for recovery-oriented outcome evaluation
Propose implementing extreme regulation for mental health, suicide prevention, and chemical dependency services
Call for establishing national privacy and safety standards
Recommend requiring independent research before deployment

noteworthyInsights.insight: Compulsive AI relationships as clinical phenomenon: Licensed psychologist Sarah Edmonds describes a client who developed harmful compulsive relationship with ChatGPT after losing regular therapy access, noting that a human clinician would have recognized and addressed therapeutically this pattern, but AI responds to queries without recognizing when the interaction itself is harmful. This represents an emerging clinical concern unique to AI mental health tools.

HITECH Act exclusion as cautionary precedent: NeuroFlow warns that behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result, arguing AI policy must not repeat that exclusion. This historical parallel resonates across stakeholder types and frames current policy decisions as potentially having decade-long consequences.

Regulatory asymmetry creating perverse incentives: Kooth identifies that regulatory asymmetry between AI designed for mental health support and wellness products creates market incentives favoring less governed products. This suggests current regulatory gaps may actually disadvantage responsible developers while allowing potentially harmful products to proliferate.

Cultural competency gaps in generic models: Medvidi provides specific technical insight that generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning often miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress, producing bland or inaccurate outputs. This highlights that behavioral health AI requires specialty development, not just general medical AI adaptation.

State legislative landscape analysis: Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi provides unique data showing that among 793 state bills reviewed, 143 were identified as potentially impactful to mental health AI, with 20 enacted across 11 states. Most laws treat mental health as incidental to broader AI or healthcare regulation, and explicit mental health provisions remain rare. This quantifies the regulatory gap.

Dual perspective of retired clinician/current patient: Carol Gould offers unique dual perspective as both a retired Marriage and Family Therapist with 30 years experience and a current therapy patient, noting AI cannot feel empathy and can only deliver lines that sound like empathy.

Emerging research on AI vs. human-delivered care: SanLuca AI cites Dr. Jessica Schleider's studies comparing virtually-delivered to human-delivered single session interventions with outcomes similar in both groups, with research currently being designed to extend this to AI-delivered care. This represents one of the few specific evidence bases cited for AI efficacy.

emergingPatterns.pattern: State-by-state regulatory fragmentation creates operational challenges for multi-state providers. SonderMind reports state fragmentation has forced them to exclude some states from AI offerings. California emerges as a leader with statewide youth mental health initiative Soluna operated by Kooth. International perspective from UK-based Kooth suggests more mature digital mental health ecosystem abroad.

Facility-type patterns show telehealth providers like Medvidi and SonderMind more enthusiastic about AI integration, while community mental health centers (CCBHCs) are identified as priority settings for AI research. Pediatric mental health providers such as InStride Health express heightened concerns about developmental context.

Experience and role correlations reveal technology company executives emphasize access crisis and workforce solutions while individual clinicians emphasize therapeutic relationship and patient safety. Clinicians with 30+ years experience express strongest skepticism, while clinicians who are also AI developers show more nuanced positions.

Multiple unintended consequences identified: clinician overreliance on AI may erode clinical judgment; regulatory burden may favor unregulated wellness products over clinical AI; AI adoption pressure may outpace clinician understanding; remote therapy companies may abruptly terminate patient relationships.

Population-specific concerns identify youth and adolescents as particularly vulnerable. Substance use disorder populations face additional Part 2 data restrictions. LGBTQ+ populations in specialty clinics warrant specific consideration. Older adults identified as needing human-provided care.

keyQuotations.quote: Since AI is not, in fact, a human relationship, it is seriously limited as a potential clinical treatment tool.
There is an energetic connection between human beings, especially when in person with each other. This energetic connection is very healing, and can never be replicated by technology.
AI can't feel empathy. It can only deliver lines that sound like empathy.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Retired Marriage and Family Therapist/Current Patient

keyQuotations.quote: Trust, confidentiality, and relational attunement are foundational. Innovation must not erode the integrity of this relationship.
At the moment, this is like releasing an untested, malleable, biological weapon on our most vulnerable citizens.
Innovation that outpaces clinical evidence is not progress — it is an uncontrolled experiment on vulnerable people.
We have seen AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Licensed Clinical Social Worker (30 years experience)

keyQuotations.quote: To accelerate in this sector is unethical and unwise.

The answers, however, will be incomplete if they are shaped primarily by the experiences of radiology, pathology, and acute care, domains where AI operates on images and structured data rather than on the complexity of human conversation and therapeutic relationship.
Generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning often miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress, producing bland or inaccurate outputs.

In complex and nuanced care scenarios such as mental health, where therapeutic relationships, patient history, and developmental context are central, overgeneralization or overconfidence in AI outputs can unintentionally influence clinical judgment.
Mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics.
Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result. AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.

It is critical to define and recognize that AI supported care is not the same as professional, human-delivered, formal behavioral health services. AI supported care should not attempt to and cannot replace the need for such human-delivered services.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Professional Organization (National Council for Mental Wellbeing)

keyQuotations.quote: Continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale, without AI.
Ethical, humane and highly-regulated and evidence-based psychotherapeutic models and services must be at the forefront of any AI tools for therapeutic services.
To give nearly free rein to AI to manage the fragile nature of thoughts, emotions, and communication is frightening at the least.
Therapeutic work is based in human relationships, rather than AI that prioritizes profit and validation of a user's thoughts without clinical discernment.
AI should only serve as a supportive tool for clinicians, never as a replacement for the nuanced, relational work of psychotherapy.
We urge control and a reliance on human mental health specialists until these issues are addressed if they ever can be.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific policy questions, cite research evidence, and provide detailed recommendations. Both technology advocates and skeptics articulate nuanced positions with supporting rationale. Professional organizations provide particularly well-structured analyses.

evidenceBase.explanation: Strong consensus on therapeutic relationship research is well-documented. However, evidence for AI efficacy in behavioral health remains limited, with only one commenter citing specific comparative research (Dr. Schleider's single session intervention studies). Most safety concerns are based on anecdotal incidents rather than systematic data. The regulatory landscape analysis provides unique quantitative data on state legislation.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients/consumers with lived experience of mental health conditions. No comments from payers or insurers on reimbursement perspectives. Rural and underserved community perspectives underrepresented despite frequent references to access gaps. No comments from emergency/crisis services providers despite discussion of suicide risk.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves fundamental questions about the nature of therapeutic healing, the limits of technology in replicating human connection, and the tension between access expansion and safety. Stakeholders hold genuinely different values about acceptable risk levels for vulnerable populations.

15.themeDescription: Clinical Domain-Specific AI Applications. This theme encompasses all comments addressing AI applications in specific clinical specialties or use cases, including radiology, pathology, oncology, surgery, and primary care. || It includes discussions of domain-specific AI opportunities and challenges, proposals for specialty-specific requirements, and debates about appropriate AI uses in particular clinical contexts. This theme does NOT include general clinical AI issues that apply across domains. The defining characteristic is focus on specialty-specific considerations rather than general clinical AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal strong consensus that different clinical specialties have distinct AI opportunities and requirements, with radiology, pathology, cardiology, and neurology emerging as leading domains for AI adoption. The dominant tension lies between the promise of AI to improve diagnostic speed and accuracy in specialty-specific applications versus concerns about inadequate validation for domain-specific nuances, workflow integration challenges, and the risk that foundational clinical areas (dentistry, pathology) remain disconnected from broader healthcare AI infrastructure. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend that HHS develop specialty-specific guidance while prioritizing AI tools that augment rather than replace clinical judgment.

consensusPoints.text: Different clinical domains require distinct AI approaches and considerations. Nearly all domain-specific submissions explicitly or implicitly acknowledge that clinical specialties have unique AI opportunities, challenges, and requirements that cannot be addressed through one-size-fits-all regulation. Massive Bio emphasizes oncology-specific clinical trial matching needs; AAN highlights that ambient scribes fail to capture neurology-specific clinical details like headache or seizure types; AADA notes dermatology requires specialty-specific diagnostic tool guidelines.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (18 of 21 domain-specific submissions)

exceptions.text: While all agree on domain specificity, there is no consensus on whether this requires separate regulatory frameworks or specialty-specific guidance within a unified framework.

consensusPoints.text: AI performs best in narrow, task-specific applications supporting clinician workflows. Clinical commenters agree that AI tools demonstrate greatest value when focused on specific, well-defined tasks rather than broad diagnostic replacement. ACR states AI tools have demonstrated meaningful promise in targeted, task-specific applications supporting clinician workflows rather than replacing clinical judgment; ECRI confirms strongest value case is speed and consistency for narrow tasks such as flagging suspected findings for urgent review; ADA notes strongest dental AI solutions function as clinical copilots.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of clinical commenters (at least 10 of 15 clinical stakeholders)

exceptions.text: No significant exceptions to this consensus were identified.

consensusPoints.text: Radiology and imaging represent the most mature domain for clinical AI. Multiple commenters acknowledge radiology as the leading specialty for AI adoption, with the majority of FDA-cleared AI devices concentrated in medical imaging. ECRI notes majority of FDA-cleared AI/ML-enabled devices are in medical imaging; RBMA describes AI's promise to flag subtle findings, reduce perceptual errors, and prioritize urgent cases; ACR confirms CAD devices perform well when narrowly focused and integrated into workflows.

Time-critical clinical applications show strongest AI value proposition. Most clinical specialty commenters identify time-sensitive workflows as high-priority AI use cases where speed improvements translate directly to patient outcomes. AAN cites stroke AI reducing treatment times where successful treatment is highly time dependent; Pictor Labs notes traditional staining becomes a bottleneck to all subsequent procedures while virtual staining delivers results in minutes; MHA recommends prioritizing radiology and time-critical triage.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of AI Autonomy in Clinical Decision-Making
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI should only augment clinical judgment or whether some narrow applications may warrant autonomous AI operation.

positions.label: Augmentation Only
positions.stance: AI should support but never replace clinical judgment. ACR, ECRI, and AADA recommend AI as first step to review diagnostics and flag where additional human expert review is required.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of professional associations and advocacy groups (approximately 12 of 15)
keyArguments: Patient safety requires human oversight
AI lacks contextual understanding
Liability concerns with autonomous decisions

positions.label: Selective Autonomy
positions.stance: Some narrow applications may warrant autonomous AI operation. Alex Contner proposes AI screening to route patients directly to physical therapists bypassing orthopedics; ASRS notes first FDA-cleared AI device for diabetic retinopathy screening operates autonomously.
positions.supportLevel: A minority of technology vendors and one individual commenter
keyArguments: Certain screening tasks (diabetic retinopathy, orthopedic red flags) could safely bypass specialist review
Efficiency gains justify autonomous triage

areasOfDebate.topic: Physical AI vs. Software-Only AI Regulation
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether physical AI operating during procedures requires fundamentally different safety approaches compared to software-only AI.

positions.label: Unified Framework
positions.stance: All clinical AI should follow similar regulatory principles. Most professional associations do not explicitly address this distinction.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in most comments that do not distinguish physical AI
keyArguments: Core safety principles apply across modalities
Simpler regulatory landscape

positions.label: Distinct Categories
positions.stance: Physical AI operating during procedures requires fundamentally different safety approaches. MediView XR distinguishes Physical AI that operates in situ during live procedures; Michael Bridges warns that 20-50 milliseconds to recognize an error can result in unintended tissue damage.
positions.supportLevel: At least 2 commenters with technical expertise explicitly advocate this position
keyArguments: Real-time procedural AI faces unique latency constraints
Physics-based safety limits differ from software
Hardware interlocks required

areasOfDebate.topic: Priority Clinical Domains for AI Investment
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over which clinical domains should receive priority for AI investment and development.

positions.label: Imaging-First
positions.stance: Continue prioritizing radiology and imaging AI where evidence is strongest. Supported by ACR, RBMA, and ECRI.
positions.supportLevel: Radiology organizations and independent evaluators
keyArguments: Most FDA clearances
Strongest evidence base
Mature integration pathways

positions.label: Foundational Steps
positions.stance: Prioritize upstream diagnostic processes that enable all subsequent care. Pictor Labs urges HHS to prioritize foundational and simple healthcare steps such as virtual histopathological staining; DSI argues dentistry is a high-leverage entry point for improving systemic health outcomes.
positions.supportLevel: Pathology and dental organizations
keyArguments: Tissue staining and oral health are gatekeepers to downstream decisions
Bottlenecks here cascade through system

positions.label: Underserved Specialties
positions.stance: Expand AI development to currently neglected clinical domains. AAN notes ambient scribes fail neurology; APDA seeks PD-specific symptom monitoring.
positions.supportLevel: Neurology, dermatology, and disease-specific advocacy groups
keyArguments: Current AI tools have significant content gaps for specialties beyond primary care
Patient populations with complex conditions need tailored solutions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Specialty-specific AI validation gaps; liability exposure for AI-assisted decisions; inadequate reimbursement for AI tools; payer AI downcoding claims
specificPoints: Deep understanding of how AI tools fail to capture specialty-specific clinical nuances such as headache types in neurology and skin lesion characteristics in dermatology
Propose specialty-specific guidelines and inclusion of specialists in AI governance workgroups
Advocate for AI as clinical copilot rather than autonomous decision-maker
AAN provides detailed analysis of ambient scribe failures for neurological documentation
AADA raises concern about payer AI downcoding dermatology claims

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for novel AI categories; reimbursement pathways for FDA-cleared tools; interoperability standards enabling integration
specificPoints: Technical understanding of AI capabilities and limitations in specific clinical contexts
Awareness of gaps between regulatory approval and clinical adoption
Propose new reimbursement codes for AI-augmented diagnostics
Seek recognition of distinct AI categories such as Physical AI and virtual staining
Echo IQ seeks clearer payment pathways for FDA-cleared echocardiography AI
MediView XR proposes Physical AI as distinct regulatory category

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Earlier diagnosis through AI-enabled screening; personalized treatment optimization; ensuring AI development includes patient input
specificPoints: Focus on patient-experienced outcomes rather than clinical workflow efficiency
Emphasis on disease heterogeneity and individual variation
Seek AI tools for symptom monitoring and medication optimization
APDA members describe medication management as trial-and-error and seek AI optimization
National MS Society emphasizes AI for personalized and effective treatment regimens

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Structural disconnection of certain specialties (dentistry) from healthcare data ecosystem; lack of unified standards authority for emerging clinical domains
specificPoints: Technical standards expertise and understanding of interoperability barriers preventing AI integration
Propose establishing practice networks combining standards leadership with clinical expertise
DSI warns dentistry remains structurally disconnected from the broader healthcare data ecosystem
ADA offers to lead standards development through established ANSI-accredited processes

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Blind spots in AI development regarding patient diversity between specialties; governance barriers slowing adoption of beneficial tools; need for real-world validation
specificPoints: Research-based understanding of AI limitations
Experience with institutional governance challenges
Recommend priority research on clinical language/translation, radiology triage, and population health tools
Penn NeuroVERT notes blind spots persist regarding patient diversity between specialties and variability of real-world clinical workflows

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety in AI-controlled physical systems; appropriate care pathway routing; hardware-level safety requirements
specificPoints: Deep technical understanding of latency, hardware interlocks, and physical system constraints that clinical stakeholders may not emphasize
Propose hardware-based safety mechanisms for physical AI
Michael Bridges provides detailed analysis of millisecond-level safety requirements for surgical robotics

noteworthyInsights.insight: Oral-systemic health connection as AI opportunity: DSI points out that oral inflammation and dental disease correlate strongly with broader chronic disease risk and dental visits are often more frequent than physician visits, making dentistry a critical clinical setting for early risk detection and prevention workflows—an underappreciated entry point for population health AI.

Retinal imaging as window to systemic disease: ASRS notes that AI-assisted analyses of retinal and ocular data hold promise for diagnosing systemic diseases (cardiovascular, autoimmune, neurodegenerative)—suggesting ophthalmology AI could extend far beyond eye disease.

Patient experience of medication management as trial-and-error: APDA reports that Parkinson's patients frequently mentioned medication management feels like trial-and-error, revealing a concrete patient pain point where AI optimization could provide immediate value.

Cognitive impairment detection from unstructured notes: AAN describes how large language model workflows have demonstrated ability to generate clinically meaningful reviews of unstructured clinical notes, enabling detection of early signals of cognitive impairment—a novel application beyond traditional diagnostic AI.

Virtual staining preserves tissue for molecular testing: Pictor Labs explains that virtual stains require only a single tissue slice, so significant biopsy tissue is retained for additional molecular tests—addressing a practical constraint that affects downstream precision medicine.

First FDA-cleared AI device was specialty-specific: ASRS notes the historical significance that first FDA-cleared AI-enabled medical device is used for diabetic retinopathy screening, establishing retina specialists as AI pioneers.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Specialty Maturity Gradient: Comments reveal a clear hierarchy of AI adoption maturity across specialties. Most mature is radiology with majority of FDA clearances and established workflows. Emerging specialties include cardiology, ophthalmology, and stroke neurology with specific high-value use cases. Underserved specialties include general neurology, dermatology, and dentistry with significant gaps in AI tool development. Nascent is surgical/procedural AI with distinct safety requirements not yet addressed.

Upstream vs. Downstream Value: A tension emerges between investing in downstream applications (diagnostic interpretation, treatment recommendations) where most current AI development focuses versus upstream applications (tissue preparation, screening, triage) where bottlenecks cascade through the system but receive less attention.

Workflow Integration as Differentiator: Successful specialty AI adoption correlates with clear, narrow task definition; integration into existing clinical workflows; time-critical applications where speed translates to outcomes; and specialties with established digital infrastructure such as radiology and cardiology.

Patient Advocacy vs. Professional Association Priorities: Professional associations emphasize workflow efficiency, liability protection, reimbursement, and documentation accuracy. Patient advocacy groups emphasize earlier diagnosis, personalized treatment, symptom monitoring, and reduced trial-and-error.

Geographic/Setting Variations: Penn NeuroVERT notes small but meaningful nuances exist in how proceduralists/surgeons view tools versus those in credit-based inpatient models versus outpatient settings. MHA notes smaller and safety-net providers face disproportionate barriers to AI adoption.

keyQuotations.quote: Unlike retrospective analytics or decision-support systems that generate insight removed from execution, MediView's platform operates in situ during live procedures... it closes the loop between data → cognition → physical execution.
In physical care, such as robotic surgery, a software loop taking 20 to 50 milliseconds to recognize an error and issue a stop command can result in unintended tissue damage.
Dentistry is one of the largest, most frequently accessed clinical domains in the United States, yet it remains structurally disconnected from the broader healthcare data ecosystem.
Without these advances, millions of Americans would go blind every year.
[Text generated by ambient scribes] often fails to distinguish important clinical details like different types of headache or seizures, impacting notes and treatment plans generated.
Private sector often pursues AI tools optimized for regulatory success, academic recognition, or investor appeal rather than real-world clinical needs.
Members frequently mentioned medication management feels like trial-and-error.
Strongest dental AI solutions function as clinical copilots delivering measurable business impact while reinforcing transparency, reliability, and clinician confidence.

Technologies employing automated detection of large vessel occlusion in patients with acute ischemic stroke have enhanced hospital-based stroke workflows, resulting in reductions in stroke treatment times.
Traditional staining becomes a bottleneck to all subsequent procedures due to time involved for irreversible chemical staining.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of specialty-specific AI challenges with detailed technical and clinical evidence. Professional associations and technology vendors provide substantive analysis rather than general assertions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many comments cite specific examples of AI applications, FDA clearances, and clinical workflow impacts. Some claims about AI effectiveness rely on vendor assertions rather than independent validation.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from primary care physicians, rural healthcare providers, and patients directly affected by AI diagnostic tools. Surgical specialties beyond ophthalmology and neurology are underrepresented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - requires understanding of specialty-specific clinical workflows, regulatory pathways, and technical AI constraints to fully appreciate the nuances of the debate.

16.themeDescription: Administrative and Operational AI Applications. This theme encompasses all comments addressing AI use for administrative and operational functions rather than direct clinical care, including documentation, billing, scheduling, and workflow optimization. || It includes discussions of ambient documentation AI, concerns about administrative AI governance, debates about appropriate administrative AI uses, and proposals for administrative AI standards. This theme does NOT include clinical decision support or diagnostic AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that administrative AI represents the most promising near-term opportunity to reduce healthcare costs and clinician burden without the safety risks inherent in clinical AI applications. The dominant tension centers on whether AI should be deployed for high-stakes administrative decisions like prior authorization and claims adjudication—where commenters diverge sharply between those seeing efficiency gains and those warning of embedded inequities and reduced human oversight. The prevailing recommendation thrust favors a tiered regulatory approach that streamlines governance for low-risk administrative tools while maintaining robust safeguards for AI affecting coverage decisions.

consensusPoints.text: Administrative AI offers greatest near-term value with lowest risk. Nearly all commenters agree that administrative AI applications present the most favorable risk-benefit profile for immediate deployment. Massive Bio states that administrative AI may offer the greatest near-term value by reducing burden without the safety risks of clinical AI. Renee Pope (DHA Candidate) recommends that for PALTC, high-risk clinical AI should not be the entry point, advocating instead for a staged acceleration strategy prioritizing non-clinical workflow AI. National Council for Mental Wellbeing suggests prioritizing AI that reduces administrative burden before adding AI for clinical care while further research is conducted on risks.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 35 of 40+ relevant comments)

exceptions.text: Global Liver Institute warns that even administrative AI can cause significant harm when applied to coverage decisions

consensusPoints.text: Ambient documentation AI has demonstrated measurable success. A strong majority of commenters with direct implementation experience report that ambient documentation/scribe tools have met or exceeded expectations. SonderMind reports their AI-powered clinical documentation tool was adopted by 80%+ of eligible providers, reducing note completion time by 80% and saving providers up to 90 minutes per day. Medvidi reports reduced administrative burden by 10-15 minutes per visit previously spent on documentation. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association notes that in areas with high clinical and operational relevance like ambient documentation, there has been rapid and strong adoption.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters with direct implementation experience (at least 15 organizations)

exceptions.text: Scientix.ai notes that clinicians report spending up to three hours per week correcting or bypassing AI outputs due to missing context

consensusPoints.text: Administrative burden is a crisis requiring urgent intervention. Virtually all healthcare provider commenters identify administrative burden as a critical driver of burnout and reduced patient care time. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi notes that administrative work consumes one-sixth of U.S. physicians' working hours. Ryan Kern (Stanford pre-med student) observes that administrative overhead is a critical contributor to physician burnout and directly reduces time for meaningful patient engagement. American Dental Association identifies credentialing as one of the most administratively burdensome aspects of clinical operations.

AI should enhance rather than replace human judgment. Most commenters emphasize that administrative AI should support existing workflows rather than fundamentally alter decision-making authority. SonderMind notes that tools that exceeded expectations support providers within existing workflows rather than attempting to replace clinical judgment. Dr. Raymond Kordonowy argues AI should automate logistics while the physician guides care. EHY Consulting observes that AI tends to meet or exceed expectations when tightly coupled to well-defined workflows with clear success metrics.

areasOfDebate.topic: AI in Prior Authorization and Coverage Decisions
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters diverge on whether AI should be deployed for high-stakes administrative decisions like prior authorization, with some emphasizing efficiency gains and others warning of embedded inequities and reduced human oversight.

positions.label: Automate for Efficiency
positions.stance: AI should streamline prior authorization to reduce delays and administrative burden. AAPA highlights that prior authorization automation is a key benefit—identifying when PA is needed, compiling required information from health information systems, and drafting prompt payer responses. WellSpan Health notes that generative AI agents represent major potential to automate routine administrative tasks and could reclaim 15-20 hours per week of clinician time.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of provider organizations, technology companies, and professional associations (approximately 15+ commenters)
keyArguments: Prior authorization is a major source of care delays and provider frustration
AI can compile required information, draft responses, and identify when PA is needed
Automation could reclaim significant clinician time currently spent on paperwork

positions.label: Require Human Oversight
positions.stance: AI-driven coverage decisions require meaningful human review to prevent harm. Global Liver Institute argues that AI use in coverage decisions and prior authorization is one of the most consequential and concerning AI applications in health care, noting that speed alone does not equal value if it comes at cost of accuracy, fairness, or transparency. David Bynon observes that this failure mode is increasingly visible in Medicare Advantage and other federal benefit programs where AI systems assist beneficiaries, providers, and administrative workflows.
positions.supportLevel: Patient advocacy groups, some professional associations (approximately 5-7 commenters, but with strong conviction)
keyArguments: Speed without accuracy causes patient harm
Algorithms trained on historical spending patterns embed past inequities
AI has amplified delays, errors, and inequities in high-stakes administrative functions

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether administrative AI warrants streamlined oversight distinct from clinical AI or whether even administrative AI requires robust governance and monitoring.

positions.label: Minimal Regulation
positions.stance: Administrative AI warrants streamlined oversight distinct from clinical AI. Bethany Russell, PhD proposes that Tier 1 Administrative AI Tools should have minimal regulation for scheduling, documentation, billing support, or other non-clinical functions. Penguin Ai recommends that HHS should prioritize shifts that clearly distinguish between Clinical AI and Administrative AI, and establish regulatory safe harbor for transparent, audit-ready administrative tools.
positions.supportLevel: Most technology vendors, several professional associations (approximately 10-12 commenters)
keyArguments: Administrative functions don't directly affect clinical decisions
Excessive regulation will slow adoption of beneficial tools
Clear distinction between clinical and administrative AI enables appropriate governance

positions.label: Maintain Safeguards
positions.stance: Even administrative AI requires governance and monitoring. American Academy of Dermatology warns that AI for coding and billing without effective oversight may result in billing errors and overpayments with fraud and abuse implications. EHY Consulting notes that AI tends to fall short when deployed as a generic overlay without governance, monitoring, or integration into decision rights.
positions.supportLevel: Academic researchers, some provider organizations, patient advocates (approximately 8-10 commenters)
keyArguments: Administrative decisions can have significant downstream clinical impacts
AI coding tools without oversight may result in billing errors with fraud implications
Post-market surveillance needed to ensure ongoing safety

areasOfDebate.topic: AI for Fraud Detection: Proactive vs. Reactive
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether AI should focus on real-time fraud prevention before payment occurs or whether fraud detection AI requires careful validation to avoid false positives.

positions.label: Real-Time Prevention
positions.stance: AI should prevent fraud before payment occurs. Advanced Interactive Technology Holdings argues that HHS should prioritize real-time Medicare claims validation using AI that prevents fraud BEFORE payment with validation in under 3 seconds, noting that AI fraud prevention shifts from reactive detection to proactive prevention and that the technology is feasible with compelling ROI. Steven Zecola (patient advocate) suggests early applications should focus on areas with clear return on investment and limited risk including fraud, waste, and abuse detection and claims auditing and compliance monitoring.
positions.supportLevel: Technology vendors, some individual commenters (approximately 4-5 commenters)
keyArguments: Current post-payment detection wastes resources recovering already-paid fraudulent claims
Technology exists to validate claims in under 3 seconds
ROI is compelling given $100+ billion annual fraud costs

positions.label: Cautious Implementation
positions.stance: Fraud detection AI requires careful validation. While no direct opposition was voiced, concerns about accuracy in coverage decisions raised by Global Liver Institute apply by extension to fraud detection systems.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit concern from several commenters emphasizing accuracy over speed
keyArguments: False positives could delay legitimate claims and harm patients
Historical patterns may not capture novel legitimate practices

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Documentation burden consuming time that should be spent with patients; need for AI tools that integrate seamlessly with existing EHR workflows; liability clarity for AI-assisted administrative functions
specificPoints: Direct experience with both successes and failures of administrative AI
Emphasis on AI returning time to patient care rather than creating new administrative tasks
Recognition that AI must work within existing clinical workflows to succeed
WellSpan Health estimates AI could reclaim 15-20 hours per week of clinician time
Medvidi has built and actively deploys suite of native, proprietary AI tools including Ambient Chart Generation AI, Medical Chart Review AI, and AI Receptionist

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity to enable product development and deployment; distinction between clinical and administrative AI for appropriate governance; interoperability standards to enable data access
specificPoints: Detailed technical knowledge of what AI can and cannot do
Business interest in streamlined regulatory pathways
Experience with implementation challenges across multiple health systems
Penguin Ai recommends establishing regulatory safe harbor for transparent, audit-ready administrative tools
Cooperative Exchange recommends making administrative workflow interoperability a first-order AI policy objective

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member burden from administrative tasks; ensuring AI tools enhance rather than complicate practice; appropriate reimbursement for AI-enabled services
specificPoints: Represent collective voice of thousands of practitioners
Balance innovation enthusiasm with member protection
Deep knowledge of specialty-specific workflow challenges
AMA notes AI-enabled documentation support can reduce duplicative data entry and improve accuracy and compliance without encouraging note bloat that obscures clinical meaning
AAFP supports HHS prioritizing research on AI products that streamline documentation, reduce administrative burden, and support clinical decision-making

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI-driven coverage denials harming patients with chronic conditions; transparency in how AI affects access to care; ensuring AI reduces rather than increases barriers to care
specificPoints: Focus on patient outcomes rather than operational efficiency
Concern that speed gains may come at cost of accuracy and fairness
Awareness of how administrative decisions affect vulnerable populations
Global Liver Institute recommends prioritizing programmatic and payment policies requiring meaningful human review and transparency for AI-enabled tools in administrative decision-making
APDA expresses interest in AI tools that reduce time patients and providers spend on administrative tasks during clinical encounters

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence base for AI effectiveness; governance frameworks for responsible deployment; rural and underserved population considerations
specificPoints: Systematic analysis of AI performance across settings
Development of governance frameworks and standards
Focus on equity and access implications
RUPRI Health Panel notes AI tools enabling clinicians to spend less time on administrative tasks and more time in patient care would have greatest impact on access to quality care
ECRI notes that available studies on scheduling AI are highly heterogeneous, use different AI models, and lack patient outcome reporting

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation challenges; workforce implications of AI adoption; patient-centered design of AI tools
specificPoints: Often bring cross-disciplinary expertise
May offer innovative frameworks or solutions
Less constrained by organizational positions
Dr. Pal Randhawa proposes prioritizing AI tools predicting attrition 30-60 days in advance to allow proactive administrator intervention
Nicholas Lewis recommends HHS and CMS champion AI innovation by releasing an interactive Medicare mobile app for beneficiaries and providers

noteworthyInsights.insight: Fraud detection as instant validation — Advanced Interactive Technology Holdings cites the DOJ conviction of Joel Rufus French for $197M fraud involving orthotic braces billed for amputees and deceased beneficiaries, noting these are violations that AI could detect instantly. This concrete example illustrates the gap between current reactive systems and AI's potential for real-time prevention.

AI as Deflationary Infrastructure — Neurosurgeon Dr. Pal Randhawa introduces the concept of AI serving as Operational Radar for workforce management, proposing tools that predict clinician attrition 30-60 days in advance. This reframes administrative AI not just as efficiency tool but as critical infrastructure for preserving healthcare capacity.

Patient-authorized AI agent model — Dr. Raymond Kordonowy proposes a novel framework where AI operates under patient consent to perform administrative functions including record reconciliation, prior authorization, and even payment execution. This patient-centric model could address both efficiency and autonomy concerns.

Shadowing reveals documentation reality — Pre-medical student Ryan Kern provides ground-level observation: Shadowing experiences frequently showed doctors spending hours on charting, often extending beyond scheduled clinical shifts. This firsthand account from a future physician underscores the urgency of documentation burden.

Behavioral health AI adoption rates — SonderMind reports 80%+ adoption of their AI documentation tool among eligible providers, with over 100,000 notes submitted to insurers. This represents one of the highest documented adoption rates in the comments, suggesting behavioral health may be a leading indicator for administrative AI acceptance.

Clearinghouse perspective on administrative AI — Cooperative Exchange offers unique insight as the infrastructure layer processing 6 billion transactions annually: Clearinghouses don't deploy AI for clinical decision-making—do have direct experience with AI in administrative and operational workflows supporting clinical care. Their call to make administrative workflow interoperability a first-order AI policy objective reflects systemic understanding of data flow challenges.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural focus: RUPRI Health Panel emphasizes that administrative AI reducing clinician burden would have greatest impact on access to quality care in rural settings where workforce shortages are most acute

State-level fragmentation: Multiple commenters including SonderMind, Cooperative Exchange, and Waymark identify state-by-state regulatory variation as a significant barrier to scaling administrative AI solutions
Large integrated systems like Advocate Health and WellSpan emphasize enterprise-wide deployment potential and quantify system-level savings
FQHCs and safety-net providers like Community Care Cooperative express strong interest but highlight cost barriers and need for tools trained on representative populations
Post-acute/long-term care perspective: Renee Pope advocates for staged implementation starting with administrative AI before higher-risk clinical applications

Organizations with active AI deployments like SonderMind, Medvidi, and InStride Health provide specific metrics on time savings and adoption rates, while organizations without direct implementation experience tend to focus on potential benefits and regulatory concerns
Note bloat identified as unintended consequence: AMA warns that AI documentation support could encourage note bloat that obscures clinical meaning
Correction burden eroding efficiency: Scientix.ai identifies that AI outputs requiring significant correction may erode intended efficiency gains
Workflow disruption risk: SonderMind notes that AI tools introducing fundamentally new workflows rather than enhancing existing ones tend to underperform
Embedded inequity concern: Global Liver Institute warns that AI trained on historical patterns may embed past inequities at scale

Documentation burden is cited as a critical problem by providers, professional associations, technology vendors, researchers, and patients—suggesting this is the most universally recognized opportunity for administrative AI
Prior authorization emerges as both a high-priority target for automation AND a high-concern area for patient advocates—indicating need for careful implementation with safeguards

keyQuotations.quote: By integrating such technologies into the clinical workflow, HHS can help redirect the physician's attention back to the patient, rather than the computer screen.
AI should serve patient and physician, not insurers or administrators.
For PALTC, high-risk 'clinical' AI should not be the entry point. A staged acceleration strategy is more likely to be safe and scalable: prioritize non-clinical workflow AI.
AI-powered clinical documentation tool adopted by 80%+ of eligible providers...reduces note completion time by 80%, saves providers up to 90 minutes per day.
Reduced administrative burden by 10-15 minutes per visit previously spent on documentation. Improved note accuracy and completeness, strengthened compliance posture.
Speed alone does not equal value if it comes at the cost of accuracy, fairness, or transparency.
Clinicians report spending up to three hours per week correcting or bypassing AI outputs due to missing context or misinterpreted patient data—erodes efficiency gains AI was intended to deliver.
Inappropriate test ordering protocols cost the US healthcare system up to $200 billion annually.
Today's DOJ announcement of Joel Rufus French's conviction for $197M fraud (orthotic braces for amputees and deceased beneficiaries) demonstrates violations that AI could detect instantly.
AI fraud prevention shifts from reactive detection to proactive prevention. Technology is feasible, ROI compelling, need urgent.
AI tends to meet or exceed expectations when tightly coupled to well-defined workflows with clear success metrics.
Tools that exceeded expectations support providers within existing workflows rather than attempting to replace clinical judgment.
