Could reclaim 15-20 hours per week of clinician time...at scale, could automate 1-2 billion hours annually of administrative work.
Administrative work consumes one-sixth of U.S. physicians' working hours, underscoring potential value of AI-enabled workflow optimization.

HHS should prioritize shifts that clearly distinguish between Clinical AI (diagnostic/treatment) and Administrative AI (operational/revenue cycle). Establish regulatory 'safe harbor' for transparent, audit-ready administrative tools.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples and quantitative claims. Debate is constructive with clear articulation of competing values and priorities.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple organizations cite specific implementation metrics and outcomes. However, ECRI notes that available studies on scheduling AI are highly heterogeneous, use different AI models, and lack patient outcome reporting, suggesting evidence gaps in some areas.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from patients and beneficiaries; only 3 patient advocacy group comments identified. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives underrepresented relative to large integrated health systems.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - commenters recognize nuanced tradeoffs between efficiency and accuracy, speed and fairness, innovation and oversight. Multiple stakeholders acknowledge that administrative AI affecting coverage decisions requires different treatment than pure operational tools.

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that federal research investment should pivot from algorithmic development toward implementation science, real-world evaluation, and governance infrastructure. The dominant tension lies between those advocating for diversified, startup-friendly funding mechanisms versus those emphasizing rigorous, multi-site validation studies. Across 70+ comments, stakeholders consistently argue that the limiting factor in clinical AI adoption is not model capability but rather the evidence, infrastructure, and institutional capacity to deploy AI safely at scale—making federal research investment essential for closing these gaps.

consensusPoints.text: Implementation Science Should Be Prioritized Over Model Development. Nearly all commenters agree that federal research has overemphasized algorithmic performance in controlled settings while neglecting real-world deployment, workflow integration, and post-deployment monitoring. A Software Architect with clinical informatics experience advocates to fund implementation science, not just model research. SANCIAN LLC, an AI governance consultancy, notes there is plenty of research on models but not enough on adoption, outcomes, and workflow impact. Santa Clara University Markkula Center for Applied Ethics identifies the most urgent research gaps are in implementation science. NeuroFlow observes that most existing studies measure algorithmic performance rather than patient-level outcomes.

exceptions.text: One commenter argues drug discovery represents the highest-impact dimension for AI, suggesting basic research still has a critical role.

consensusPoints.text: Real-World, Multi-Site Evaluation Is Essential. A strong majority of commenters call for pragmatic trials, longitudinal studies, and multi-site evaluations that capture performance across diverse populations and settings. ECRI states that large, multicenter studies comparing AI with traditional methods and reporting patient-oriented outcomes are needed. Heart Failure Society of America recommends HHS support pragmatic, multi-site randomized trials and post-deployment monitoring. Kooth emphasizes research should reflect how AI performance varies across populations, care settings, and contexts. Several commenters note that synthetic data and retrospective AUC studies are useful only at early prototype stages.

Public-Private Partnerships and Cooperative Agreements Are Preferred Mechanisms. Most stakeholders endorse CRADAs, cooperative agreements, and public-private partnerships as effective vehicles for translating research into deployable solutions. BlueHalo notes that R&D, CRADAs, and other public-private partnerships play a critical role in translating AI capabilities from concept to sustained use. MediView XR states public-private partnerships can accelerate evidence generation and responsible adoption. California Clinical Laboratory Association recommends HHS fund public-private partnerships, CRADAs, and grant programs.

Equity and Bias Research Must Be Embedded in Federal Priorities. A clear majority emphasize that research must address AI performance across race, ethnicity, socioeconomic status, and underserved populations. A safety-net hospital digital health leader argues federal grants and contracts should prioritize tools developed with and validated in diverse populations including Medicaid beneficiaries, people with disabilities, limited English proficiency populations, and safety-net settings. An independent researcher identifies research priorities for equity including performance monitoring across race, ethnicity, age, language, and socioeconomic status. SonderMind calls for research into ensuring AI models don't perpetuate existing disparities in mental health diagnosis and treatment.

Behavioral Health AI Requires Dedicated Research Investment. Nearly all behavioral health stakeholders agree that mental health and substance use AI applications face unique evidence gaps and safety considerations warranting targeted federal investment. National Council for Mental Wellbeing states more research is needed to understand implications and risks in applying AI in mental health and substance use clinical care, particularly considering cases of AI induced psychosis, exacerbated depression symptoms, suicidality, and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation. SonderMind recommends HHS fund studies testing LLMs in behavioral health scenarios to ensure they cannot be manipulated into encouraging self-harm. Medvidi proposes launching grant programs, cooperative agreements, and challenge competitions focused specifically on mental health AI.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all behavioral health stakeholders (10+ comments)

areasOfDebate.topic: Funding Distribution: Diversified vs. Concentrated Investment
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether federal funding should be distributed across many early-stage efforts like venture capital or concentrated in large-scale validation studies.

positions.label: Venture Capital Model
positions.stance: Distribute small awards across many early-stage efforts. HealthScoreAI argues that if HHS seeks to foster genuine innovation in AI, it should consider an approach more analogous to a diversified venture capital model—funding a large number of early-stage efforts with relatively small awards. A mental health clinician/developer suggests not only funding the large developers but taking a VC approach and offering seed money to thousands of companies in the form of tax credits. ScriptChain Health notes that most R&D funding has gone to nonprofits and academic institutions which yields poor results.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25-30% of commenters, primarily startups and small businesses
keyArguments: Traditional grants favor organizations skilled at grant-writing, not breakthrough innovation
Government cannot predict which approaches will prove transformative
Small awards allow promising ideas to emerge organically before larger investments

positions.label: Rigorous Multi-Site Studies
positions.stance: Concentrate resources in large-scale validation. Cellworks Group proposes HHS establish an Algorithmic Diagnostics Validation Initiative with $50-100M annual funding. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association recommends funding SBIR-style pilots, cooperative agreements, and CRADAs to generate generalizable, real-world evidence across diverse populations and sites. ECRI emphasizes that large, multicenter studies comparing AI with traditional methods and reporting patient-oriented outcomes are needed.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 50-55% of commenters, primarily academic institutions, professional associations, and established health systems
keyArguments: Single laboratories cannot fund multi-institutional prospective studies
Evidence gaps create catch-22 where payers wait for outcomes evidence
Fragmented small studies don't produce generalizable evidence

areasOfDebate.topic: Research Focus: Governance Infrastructure vs. Clinical Capability
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether federal research should prioritize governance and accountability architecture or clinical outcome-improving applications.

positions.label: Governance First
positions.stance: Prioritize accountability architecture research. The Interstitial Institute argues HHS should prioritize governance infrastructure research—not AI capability research, stating the limiting variable in clinical AI adoption is not quality or capability of available AI systems but institutional capacity to deploy those systems within accountability architectures. Onboard AI recommends HHS prioritize research on governance, accountability, and monitoring models.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20-25% of commenters, primarily governance consultants and think tanks
keyArguments: Limiting variable is institutional capacity to deploy safely, not AI capability
Patient safety research transformed hospitals; same transformation needed for AI governance
Cost-benefit literature is methodologically immature and omits governance costs

positions.label: Clinical Capability
positions.stance: Prioritize outcome-improving applications. AORN states all research should prioritize improvement of patient outcomes and clinician user experience with other outcomes recognized as secondary. A patient advocate with Parkinson's argues the biggest benefit to healthcare industry performance from AI is achievable from drug discovery.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 60-65% of commenters, primarily clinicians, health systems, and patient advocates
keyArguments: Research should be designed to have direct, positive impact on patients
Drug discovery and clinical decision support offer highest-impact opportunities
Patient outcomes and clinician experience should be primary endpoints

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether grants should be structured as fixed-period unlimited scaling or traditional fixed-amount milestone-based funding.

positions.label: Time-Based Grants
positions.stance: Fixed period, unlimited scaling. Epic Systems proposes that traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding, while time-based grants would encourage organizations to scale quickly, assess value, and determine which use cases merit continued investment.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly proposed by 1 major commenter (Epic), implicitly supported by several others
keyArguments: Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding
Time-based grants encourage rapid assessment of value
Organizations can discontinue low-value AI without ongoing government spending

positions.label: Traditional Grants
positions.stance: Fixed amount, milestone-based. Most academic commenters assume traditional structures without explicitly debating alternatives.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit preference of most academic and research commenters
keyArguments: Rigorous evaluation requires adequate time regardless of scaling speed
Milestone-based accountability ensures quality

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether government should develop and own AI infrastructure or enable private sector leadership.

positions.label: Government-Developed Platforms
positions.stance: Federal ownership of AI infrastructure. Nicholas Lewis suggests an effective way to promote innovation and effective usage of AI in the medical field would be for HHS and CMS to champion the cause by releasing an interactive Medicare mobile app.
positions.supportLevel: Small minority (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Provides program-controlled platform for future integration
Ensures public interest alignment

positions.label: Private Sector Leadership
positions.stance: Government enables, private sector builds. HealthScoreAI notes government agencies are not well positioned to predict which technical approaches or use cases will prove transformative. ScriptChain Health recommends allowing competitive market to drive development, deployment, and iteration at healthcare's required speed.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (approximately 80%)
keyArguments: Government not well-positioned to predict transformative approaches
Competitive markets drive development at healthcare's required speed
Federal role is enabling infrastructure, not building applications

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence gaps preventing confident AI deployment, lack of research on workflow integration and clinician burden, and insufficient funding for safety-net and rural settings. They emphasize that research must account for operational realities—staffing constraints, EHR integration challenges, and the need for AI to reduce rather than add cognitive load.
specificPoints: Multi-site testbeds and benchmark registries needed
Targeted grants for small and rural providers
Research on AI's impact on clinician workload and burnout

Advocate Health calls for strategic investments focusing on research into AI models addressing social determinants of health and structural inequities, and pilot programs testing AI applications in under-resourced settings including rural and safety net providers

Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association recommends funding SBIR-style pilots, cooperative agreements, and CRADAs to generate generalizable, real-world evidence across diverse populations and sites, prioritizing community, rural, and safety-net settings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty slowing market development, lack of standardized evaluation frameworks, and data access barriers and interoperability challenges. They emphasize that stable reimbursement and clear regulatory expectations are prerequisites for R&D investment, and highlight that startups face disproportionate barriers compared to established players.
specificPoints: CRADAs for standardized validation datasets
Prize competitions for system-agnostic tools
Federated learning infrastructure accessible to small companies
COGA Labs proposes creating a permanent, HIPAA-compliant federated learning network accessible to small companies, not just academic medical centers
ScriptChain Health recommends funding open-access datasets and benchmarking platforms that level the playing field for startups and established companies

California Clinical Laboratory Association notes AI research and innovation in clinical laboratories advance most effectively when reimbursement is stable and regulatory expectations are clear, consistent, and technology-neutral

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring research aligns with practical clinician needs, protecting specialty-specific interests (radiology, dermatology, urology, etc.), and addressing underserved research areas (pediatrics, rare diseases, behavioral health). They bring deep domain expertise and emphasize that HHS should work closely with professional associations to ensure federally funded R&D addresses real clinical workflows.
specificPoints: Specialty-specific research priorities
Prioritized funding for pediatrics and rare diseases
Collaboration with professional societies on evaluation frameworks
American College of Radiology states HHS agencies should work closely with professional associations to ensure federally funded R&D aligns with practical clinician needs
American College of Radiology notes research into AI for pediatrics and rare diseases should receive prioritized funding opportunities from NIH/ARPA-H
National Council for Mental Wellbeing recommends prioritizing research on impacts and implications of clinical decision-supported AI use in mental health and substance use care

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Underfunded research infrastructure, lack of access to compliant computing environments, and need for industry-academia partnerships. They emphasize methodological rigor, the importance of in-context evaluation, and the need for clinicians to be key stakeholders in AI application development.
specificPoints: Regional or federated evaluation collaboratives
Industry-academia partnerships leveraging university training expertise
Development and implementation trajectory evaluation frameworks

Computing Research Association encourages industry-academia partnerships across AI and clinical care, noting university researchers have expertise in training and credentialing that private sector could benefit from
Santa Clara University Markkula Center recommends HHS, including NIH and AHRQ, support regional or federated evaluation collaboratives through cooperative agreements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance infrastructure gaps as primary adoption barrier, methodological immaturity of cost-benefit literature, and need for accountability architecture research. They argue that the limiting factor is not AI capability but institutional capacity to deploy within accountability structures, and emphasize governance failure taxonomy and interoperability standards.
specificPoints: Clinical AI governance architecture research
Governance failure taxonomy analogous to aviation safety
Governance interoperability standards development

The Interstitial Institute argues patient safety research transformed hospital operations by making safety failures measurable and governable, and the same transformation is needed for clinical AI governance
EHY Consulting identifies priority research areas including methods for post-deployment monitoring, drift detection, and change control in heterogeneous clinical settings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring AI benefits reach underserved populations, accelerating research for specific conditions (Type 1 diabetes, Parkinson's, cancer), and patient safety in mental health AI applications. They bring urgency and personal stakes, emphasizing that current research pace is inadequate for patients with serious conditions.
specificPoints: Prioritize research for hard-to-solve illnesses
Support screening and early detection applications
Ensure patient-centered outcome measures
A parent/caregiver urges prioritizing Type 1 diabetes space for AI research exploration, noting that crashing into T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones

A patient with Parkinson's notes a small fraction of basic research initiatives pass through the Valley of Death and make it to clinical trial, with average length of approximately 15 years per endeavor

noteworthyInsights.insight: Aviation safety taxonomy as model for AI governance: The Interstitial Institute proposes a governance failure taxonomy research program analogous to aviation safety incident taxonomy, arguing that patient safety research transformed hospital operations by making safety failures measurable and governable, and the same transformation is needed for clinical AI governance.

Navigation software analogy for evidence standards: Scientific Knowledge Accelerator Foundation draws parallel between GPS/mapping software evolution and healthcare AI, noting that navigation software is far advanced today and used in nearly infinite ways because there are common expectations in data sharing with both human-interpretable and machine-interpretable interfaces, but there is currently no standardized navigation system (GPS) for healthcare.

Defense AI governance principles applicable to healthcare: A Senior Data Scientist from U.S. Special Operations Command applies defense AI accountability frameworks to clinical settings, recommending research to measure longitudinal override patterns in deployed systems and identify escalation pathway shifts following AI integration.

Parent managing Type 1 diabetes for two teenagers provides visceral perspective on research urgency, noting that not everyone who could develop T1D is tested and crashing into T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones. Advocates for AI research supporting early antibody detection.

Patient with 24 years of Parkinson's disease brings economist's perspective to research priorities, noting that a small fraction of basic research initiatives pass through the Valley of Death and make it to clinical trial with average length of approximately 15 years per endeavor. Argues drug discovery represents highest-impact AI application.

Systematic review findings on cost-benefit methodology: The Interstitial Institute cites two 2025 systematic reviews demonstrating that majority relied on static modeling assumptions that overstated benefits and underreported indirect costs, and lifecycle costs were consistently omitted with fewer than half reporting explicit costing methods and none addressing governance infrastructure costs.

NAIRR precedent for ethics integration: Mitchell Berger documents that NAIRR's 2022 interim and 2023 final reports both emphasized ethical issues and called for creation of ethics advisory board to guide AI research, providing precedent for HHS collaboration with bioethics centers.

Pilot-to-Standard approach: Leap Orbit proposes investing in Implementation Science through national or regional technical communities with mission to pilot and fine-tune FHIR standards ensuring they are AI ready before wider implementation.

SummaryOfNetEffect Profile for shared decision-making: Scientific Knowledge Accelerator Foundation has developed a FHIR-based structure enabling specification of population, intervention, comparator, outcomes with risk estimates, and net effect calculations—with working example available online.

Deterministic governance with cryptographic audit trails: FERZ AI proposes research into deterministic governance methods, cryptographic audit infrastructure, and causal inference for clinical AI as alternative to probabilistic approaches.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural focus: Multiple commenters emphasize that rural and safety-net providers face disproportionate barriers to participating in AI research and adoption.

State-level fragmentation concerns: Several commenters note that emerging state AI requirements in Colorado and California create compliance complexity that federal research coordination could help address.
Large health systems emphasize governance infrastructure, multi-site evaluation, and integration with existing research programs.
Small/startup companies emphasize data access barriers, grant mechanism reform, and need for level playing field.
Safety-net providers emphasize equity validation, resource constraints, and need for shared infrastructure.
Frontline clinicians prioritize cognitive load reduction, workflow integration, and patient outcome measures.
Governance specialists prioritize accountability architecture, failure taxonomy, and interoperability standards.
Technology developers prioritize regulatory clarity, data access, and reimbursement stability as prerequisites for R&D investment.

Unintended consequence identified: Grant structure incentivizing slow scaling. Epic Systems notes that traditional grants incentivize organizations to scale slowly to extend funding rather than rapidly assess value.
Evidence catch-22 identified: Cellworks Group notes that payers wait for outcomes evidence before covering services while single laboratories cannot fund multi-institutional prospective studies.
Model collapse risk: Scientix.ai warns of model collapse where AI models degrade when trained on recursively generated synthetic data.

keyQuotations.quote: Fund implementation science, not just model research.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Software Architect/Former Director of Medical Informatics

keyQuotations.quote: Plenty of research on models, not enough on adoption, outcomes, and workflow impact. Most studies evaluate controlled settings; evidence on real-world implementation is thin.
The largest evidence gap is not whether AI can work but how it works when deployed at scale across diverse settings and populations.
Patient safety research transformed hospital operations by making safety failures measurable and governable. The same transformation is needed for clinical AI governance.

HHS should prioritize governance infrastructure research—not AI capability research. Limiting variable in clinical AI adoption is not quality or capability of available AI systems—it is institutional capacity to deploy those systems within accountability architectures that make them safe to scale.

Traditional grants incentivize slow scaling to extend funding; time-based grants would encourage organizations to scale quickly, assess value, and determine which use cases merit continued investment.

Most R&D funding has gone to nonprofits and academic institutions which yields poor results. There needs to be a strong alliance between the private sector and public sector with an accelerated approval process instead of waiting 9-12 months to know if you are approved.
Not only fund the large developers, take VC approach and offer seed money to thousands of companies in the form of tax credits, some companies might just 'hit it out of the park.'

All research should prioritize improvement of patient outcomes and clinician user experience with other outcomes recognized as secondary (eg, cost savings, efficiency). In other words, the research should be structured so that the aim is always to improve the human (ie, patient and clinician) experience above other perceived improvements.
Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested. And, more unfortunately, 'crashing into' T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones.

More research is needed to understand implications and risks in applying AI in mental health and substance use clinical care—particularly in light of considering cases of AI induced psychosis, exacerbated depression symptoms, suicidality, and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation.
Clinical-grade behavioral health AI requires safety testing at a standard that doesn't yet exist in research literature.
Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers.
AI research and innovation in clinical laboratories advance most effectively when reimbursement is stable and regulatory expectations are clear, consistent, and technology-neutral.
The path to accelerating AI adoption is not prescriptive regulation but evidence infrastructure that gives healthcare organizations confidence to deploy.

Navigation software is far advanced today and used in nearly infinite ways because there are common expectations in data sharing with both human-interpretable and machine-interpretable interfaces. But there is currently no standardized navigation system ('GPS') for healthcare.

By investing in trustworthy AI R&D, HHS will foster the foundational 'social license' for AI in healthcare—building public trust and providing the guardrails so innovation can proceed more rapidly without undue risk.
Crucial for ASTP/ONC to partner more closely with private sector coalitions on AI—both financially and intellectually.
A small fraction of basic research initiatives pass through the 'Valley of Death' and make it to clinical trial—average length of this phase is approximately 15 years per endeavor.

While we anticipate rapid acceleration due to AI in areas such as drug discovery and care delivery, we believe that clinical trials and clinical research will continue to be a bottleneck in the process of getting treatments to patients faster.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of research funding mechanisms, evidence generation challenges, and implementation barriers. Stakeholders cite specific systematic reviews, propose detailed funding structures, and engage substantively with tradeoffs between different approaches.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite peer-reviewed systematic reviews and specific data points. However, many recommendations are based on professional experience and logical argument rather than empirical evidence. The strongest evidence base appears in comments addressing cost-benefit methodology limitations and behavioral health safety concerns.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients and caregivers (only 5 comments), rural providers, and international perspectives. Small and medium-sized health systems appear underrepresented relative to large academic medical centers and technology companies.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High complexity with multiple interconnected debates around funding mechanisms, research priorities, and infrastructure needs. The discourse reveals genuine tensions between innovation speed and rigor, between distributed and concentrated funding, and between governance and capability research priorities.

18.themeDescription: Patient and Caregiver Perspectives. This theme encompasses all comments addressing patient and caregiver concerns, preferences, and experiences with AI in clinical care. || It includes discussions of patient trust concerns, reports of patient preferences, debates about patient involvement in AI governance, and recommendations for patient-centered AI design

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal a strong consensus that patient trust is foundational to successful AI adoption in healthcare, with nearly all commenters—regardless of stakeholder type—acknowledging that patient concerns about privacy, transparency, accountability, and the preservation of human connection must be addressed for AI to achieve its transformative potential. The central tension lies between patients' desire for AI's benefits (faster access, reduced administrative burden, personalized care) and their fears about losing the human elements of healthcare that they value most. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend that HHS establish clear governance frameworks, facilitate patient involvement throughout the AI lifecycle, and develop standardized communication tools to build and maintain trust.

consensusPoints.text: Patient trust is essential for AI adoption and must be treated as a core success metric. This consensus spans business, academic, advocacy, provider, and trade association perspectives. Health AI Institute states that patient trust and transparency are essential to durable AI adoption. SANCIAN LLC argues HHS should make patient trust a core success metric—not a soft outcome, but the foundation of sustainable adoption. ECRI emphasizes that public trust is not a barrier to innovation—it is a prerequisite for success at scale. American Cancer Society CAN notes that patients and providers have low trust that AI will be used responsibly, and this trust is essential for the transformative potential of AI advances to be realized.

exceptions.text: While trust is universally acknowledged as important, commenters differ on whether current trust levels are adequate and what mechanisms will most effectively build trust.

consensusPoints.text: Privacy and data security are the most frequently cited patient concerns, making it the single most commonly mentioned issue across all stakeholder types. AORN notes patient concerns include privacy such as ambient listening technology being used to identify or scrutinize patients or caregivers. Cyberhill Partners reports patients are concerned about privacy and fear of health data misuse or breach. American Society of Hematology observes patients show hesitancy toward AI tools, citing privacy concerns about how protected health data is stored and used. Citizens' Council for Health Freedom warns that AI will have access to patient data without patient consent, for purposes the patient may not support.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (30+ of 52)

consensusPoints.text: Patients want AI to augment—not replace—human clinical judgment and relationships. Patients value the human elements of care and fear AI could diminish therapeutic relationships. HealthScoreAI notes patients overwhelmingly want to be cared for by physicians who demonstrate empathy, shared experience, and understanding of their personal circumstances, values, and fears. Waymark reports patients generally support AI that augments rather than replaces human clinical judgment. InStride Health highlights that concerns are especially pronounced in mental health care for children and adolescents where trust, developmental context, and continuity of care are central to effective treatment.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing this topic (25+ of 52)

consensusPoints.text: Patients want AI to address practical access and administrative barriers. Patients prioritize AI applications that reduce friction, wait times, and administrative complexity. Ellipsis Health states that from the patient and caregiver perspective, the promise of AI is not abstract innovation—it is relief from the daily friction, gaps, and delays that characterize much of modern healthcare. EHY Consulting notes patients and caregivers often prioritize improvements that reduce friction, shorten wait times, improve communication, and increase transparency. SanLuca AI reports patients and caregivers want faster access, fewer delays, clearer care plans, reduced paperwork, and proactive outreach.

Patient involvement in AI design and governance is necessary. Patients should be engaged throughout the AI lifecycle, from development through post-market evaluation. National MS Society emphasizes that individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease and HHS and AI developers should meet with patients and carepartners early and often throughout development. Global Liver Institute notes patients are rarely meaningfully involved in design, governance, or evaluation of AI tools. ICF recommends patients should be positioned at center of AI design and evaluation.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (18+ of 52)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to maximize patient choice in AI-driven care or prioritize safety and oversight before patient exposure.

positions.label: Maximize Patient Choice
positions.stance: Allow patients to opt into or request AI-driven care, including proactive AI agency. Max Achtermann, an individual with chronic heart arrhythmia, argues that passive AI is a barrier to his care. Sergey Alexashenko, a healthcare consumer, states he would take ChatGPT plus generics from India any day and wants to opt out of current restrictions.
positions.supportLevel: Small but vocal minority (2-3 commenters), primarily individual patients
keyArguments: Current safety restrictions are paternalistic
Patients with chronic conditions need proactive AI assistance
Patients should have the right to choose AI alternatives to traditional care

positions.label: Prioritize Safety Oversight
positions.stance: Maintain rigorous testing and human oversight before patient exposure. AFSCME argues patients today should not be forced to serve as unwitting guinea pigs so that tech corporations may profit. CJM Strategic Consulting warns that reducing oversight prematurely risks high-profile failures that would set back public confidence.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of commenters (35+), including providers, advocacy groups, and trade associations
keyArguments: Patients should not be unwitting guinea pigs
Trust requires demonstrated rigor
Premature adoption risks high-profile failures that set back public confidence

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI should only inform physician judgment or can appropriately deliver some care functions directly to patients.

positions.label: Decision Support Only
positions.stance: AI should inform but never replace physician judgment. Pennsylvania Medical Society argues that attempting to utilize AI to provide care directly is inherently a disservice to the patient. HealthScoreAI notes that AI can simulate empathy through language and tone, but it cannot genuinely experience empathy, shared suffering, or moral responsibility.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of medical societies, providers, and advocacy groups (25+ commenters)
keyArguments: Trust is relational, not technical
AI cannot genuinely experience empathy
The physician-patient relationship must remain primary

positions.label: AI as Care Extender
positions.stance: AI can appropriately deliver some care functions directly to patients. Sergey Alexashenko, as a patient and consumer, strongly prefers AI healthcare over traditional physician-mediated care. Max Achtermann advocates that federal policy should enable User-Requested AI Agency.
positions.supportLevel: Minority of business commenters and some individual patients (5-7 commenters)
keyArguments: AI can expand access in underserved areas
Patients want faster, more convenient care
AI can address workforce shortages

areasOfDebate.topic: Generational and Demographic Differences in AI Acceptance
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI acceptance varies significantly by demographics and how policy should respond.

positions.label: Acknowledge Variation
positions.stance: AI acceptance varies significantly by age, digital literacy, and cultural background. HealthScoreAI notes AI may be perceived as acceptable or helpful by younger, more technologically fluent patients while the same tools may generate anxiety or mistrust among older populations. Kooth reports that a central concern from youth is loss of human connection.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (6-8) explicitly note demographic differences
keyArguments: Older adults and culturally tight-knit communities may find AI impersonal or unsettling
Younger, tech-fluent patients are more receptive
One-size-fits-all approaches will fail

positions.label: Universal Standards
positions.stance: Apply consistent patient protections regardless of demographics. This position is implicit in most regulatory recommendations with few commenters explicitly arguing against demographic tailoring.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in most regulatory recommendations
keyArguments: All patients deserve the same protections
Demographic targeting could introduce new inequities

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Patient Notification and Consent
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether patients should be informed whenever AI is used or only for consequential applications.

positions.label: Broad Notification
positions.stance: Patients should be informed whenever AI is used in their care. AASM notes many patients are unaware when AI tools are used in their care or how algorithmic outputs influence clinical decision-making.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters addressing this issue (15+ commenters)
keyArguments: Transparency builds trust
Patients have a right to know
Lack of disclosure undermines confidence

positions.label: Risk-Proportionate Notification
positions.stance: Notification should be calibrated to the significance of AI's role. Providence notes that every email that uses autocorrect is using AI, but surely patients are not interested in giving consent to their physician using spell check tools.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (4-6) note practical challenges with universal notification
keyArguments: AI is ubiquitous including spell-check
Patients do not want to consent to trivial uses
Notification should focus on consequential applications

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining the physician-patient relationship; ensuring AI supports rather than replaces clinical judgment; addressing patient privacy fears; managing patient expectations about AI capabilities
specificPoints: Providers emphasize the relational nature of trust and the importance of human oversight in high-stakes decisions
They report direct experience with patient hesitancy and questions about AI
Emilie Maxie, DNP states that building patient trust requires clear communication about when AI is used, appropriate patient notice, and accessible ways to raise concerns or request review
WellSpan Health notes patients want to understand why AI recommended particular treatment
Proposed solutions include clear communication materials for patients, standardized consent templates, educational resources for both clinicians and patients, and human oversight requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety; liability clarity; preserving professional autonomy; ensuring AI does not erode the clinician-patient relationship
specificPoints: Medical societies emphasize the need for educational resources and clear disclosure standards
They report member experiences with patient hesitancy and privacy concerns

American Urological Association notes patients generally support AI when it enhances clinical care while respecting consent, privacy, and dignity, and failure to meet these expectations may undermine trust even in effective tools
American Society of Hematology suggests resources helping clinicians communicate how data are used and protected may improve patient trust
Proposed solutions include federal disclosure standards, patient-facing education, transparent accountability frameworks, and patient opt-out options

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient involvement in AI governance; equity and bias; transparency; protection of vulnerable populations including those with chronic disease, cancer, Parkinson's, and liver disease
specificPoints: Advocacy groups emphasize that patients and caregivers are experts on their own conditions and must be engaged throughout the AI lifecycle
They highlight concerns about AI-driven coverage denials and loss of human connection

National MS Society states individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease and capturing lived experiences of diverse individuals ensures technologies are meaningful and reflect patient needs
American Parkinson Disease Association expresses concern about providers becoming overly reliant on AI tools reducing interactions between PD patients and human providers
Proposed solutions include patient-focused product development meetings, stakeholder feedback opportunities, patient input on priority use cases, and patient-centered metrics

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity; patient trust as a market enabler; privacy and data security; demonstrating value to patients
specificPoints: Businesses emphasize that patient trust is essential for market adoption
They report user research showing demand for objective measurement, transparency, and faster access
Massive Bio states patient trust is essential for AI adoption and patient concerns must inform policy
COGA Labs reports users express frustration with subjective self-report tools and they actively seek AI tools that provide objective, data-driven indicators
Proposed solutions include patient experience metrics, transparent AI methodology, privacy-preserving technologies, and patient consent frameworks

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient-centered AI design; transparency; trust; equity; governance frameworks
specificPoints: Academics emphasize the need for research into patient understanding and trust
They highlight the importance of contestability and accountability as regulatory requirements
Dr. Akshaya Bhagavathula argues an AI system that influences care but cannot be explained, contested, or reviewed is not merely non-patient-centered but is unsafe
Computing Research Association notes patients and caregivers want to know what is going to happen to them and what they can do about it
Proposed solutions include research into patient experience and trust, patient ownership of health data, and AI tools that answer patients' key questions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reliability of AI tools; access to care; autonomy; cost; the burden of daily health decisions
specificPoints: Individual patients and caregivers provide the most direct, emotionally resonant accounts of living with chronic conditions and navigating the healthcare system
They express both hope and caution about AI

Dominick Esposito, a caregiver, describes facing a dichotomy between desperately wanting AI to relieve daily decision stress for his children with Type 1 diabetes while being cautious about any tool without rigorous testing and vetting
Max Achtermann argues passive AI is a barrier to his care
Proposed solutions include rigorous testing and vetting, patient choice, opt-out mechanisms, and AI tools that relieve daily decision burden

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance as a communicable promise: Walt Simmons argues that patients do not need to understand how AI works—they need to know a named institutional party is accountable, there is a metric, and there is a trigger requiring action when it is not met. This reframes patient trust as a governance condition, not a technical specification.

The dichotomy of caregiver hope and caution: Dominick Esposito, a father of two teenagers with Type 1 diabetes, captures the tension many caregivers feel between desperately wanting AI to relieve daily decision stress and being cautious about any tool without rigorous testing and vetting.

Safety vs. surveillance in elder care: TapestryHealth highlights a unique tension where families want the safety of continuous monitoring but residents fear the indignity of cameras. This illustrates how patient and caregiver preferences can conflict, and how privacy-preserving AI such as contactless radar can address both.

AI as a barrier, not a benefit: Max Achtermann, a patient with chronic heart arrhythmia, argues that current safety guardrails are paternalistic and prevent him from accessing the life-saving automation he needs to remain independent. This is a rare but powerful counterpoint to the dominant narrative of caution.

Trust erosion is incremental: CJM Strategic Consulting warns that trust does not collapse in a single event but erodes incrementally, degrading patient confidence until adoption stalls and root cause is difficult to trace.

Patients as experts on their own disease: The National MS Society emphasizes that individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease—a framing that elevates patient input from a courtesy to a necessity.

Low baseline expectations: Interstella cites data showing only approximately 19% of adults expect AI to improve care affordability or relationship with providers, suggesting a need to raise expectations by delivering and publicizing wins.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Condition-Specific Concerns: Patients with chronic conditions including diabetes, Parkinson's, MS, and cancer express both the greatest hope for AI to reduce daily decision burden, improve access, and personalize care, and the greatest concern about losing human connection and personalized attention. Mental health and pediatric populations are flagged as requiring special safeguards due to the centrality of trust, developmental context, and continuity of care.

Generational and Digital Literacy Divides: Multiple commenters note that older adults and those with limited digital literacy may be more anxious or mistrustful of AI, while younger, tech-fluent patients may be more receptive—but even youth express concern about loss of human connection.

Facility-Type Patterns: Long-term care and post-acute settings face unique tensions between safety monitoring and patient dignity and privacy. Rural and underserved areas are cited as potential beneficiaries of AI-expanded access, but also as populations with lower digital literacy and infrastructure.

Unintended Consequences: Several commenters warn that if patients do not trust AI-assisted care, they will avoid providers who use it, withhold information, or seek redundant second opinions—undermining the cost savings and outcome improvements AI promises. Overly broad notification requirements could lead to consent fatigue and undermine meaningful transparency.

keyQuotations.quote: If patients do not trust AI-assisted care, they will avoid providers who use it, withhold information from AI-enabled systems, or seek redundant second opinions, all of which undermine the cost savings and outcome improvements that clinical AI promises.

Individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease. These individuals, along with their carepartners and loved ones, hold a wealth of information that can inform the design of novel therapeutics and emerging technologies to support care delivery.
AI can simulate empathy through language and tone, but it cannot genuinely experience empathy, shared suffering, or moral responsibility.
What patients are asking for is governance architecture that healthcare institutions currently lack vocabulary to promise and frameworks to build.

I face this dichotomy. On the one hand, I desperately would love for someone to harness the power of AI...to develop a tool to support my children so that some stress is relieved from them and my wife daily on making decisions. On the other hand, I would be cautious about any tool that purported to be a solution without knowing that it was tested and vetted in a standardized and rigorous fashion.
Families want the safety of continuous monitoring ('never alone'), but residents fear the indignity of cameras ('Big Brother').
Passive AI is a barrier to my care.
Can we have an efficient process to vet tools that is timely, patient-centered, and cost-efficient to provide families confidence that tools in clinical care settings are safe and reliable?
Public trust is not a barrier to innovation; it is a prerequisite for success at scale.
From the patient and caregiver perspective, the promise of AI is not abstract innovation—it is relief from the daily friction, gaps, and delays that characterize much of modern healthcare.
An AI system that influences care but cannot be explained, contested, or reviewed is not merely non-patient-centered; it is unsafe.
Let the people choose.
It is established that people with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day. We would love a tool to relieve the stress of making these decisions.
There is significant concern about batch denials caused by automation.

Responsible AI adoption must therefore prioritize clear communication about how AI is used, strong human oversight, and safeguards that reinforce, rather than diminish the human elements of care that patients and families value most.
Every email that uses autocorrect is using AI, but surely patients are not interested in giving consent to their physician using spell check tools.

Seeking care at an accredited organization can provide peace of mind that the organization has developed processes to promote appropriate use of confidential information and support quality patient care when using AI.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with patient and caregiver perspectives, drawing on surveys, patient council feedback, clinical experience, and personal narratives. Stakeholders across all types acknowledge the centrality of patient trust and provide specific, actionable recommendations.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite patient surveys, member reports, user research, and specific examples. Some commenters reference published studies such as AMA surveys and Senate investigations. Individual patient and caregiver comments provide direct lived experience evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Direct patient and caregiver voices are underrepresented relative to organizational commenters. Only 5 of 52 comments come from individual patients or caregivers. Perspectives from patients with limited digital literacy, non-English speakers, and those in rural or underserved areas are largely absent or represented only through proxy organizations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High. The theme involves multiple intersecting concerns including privacy, trust, autonomy, equity, and human connection, with significant variation by patient population, condition, and demographic factors. Tensions exist between patient autonomy and safety, between transparency and consent fatigue, and between caregiver and patient preferences.

19.themeDescription: Ethical Frameworks and Bioethics Considerations. This theme encompasses all comments addressing ethical principles, bioethics frameworks, and moral considerations for AI in clinical care. || It includes discussions of ethical principles for AI, proposals for bioethics advisory structures, debates about ethical implications of AI, and recommendations for ethical governance

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments on ethical frameworks for AI in clinical care reveal broad consensus that ethical principles must be foundational—not afterthoughts—to AI deployment, with particular emphasis on preserving uniquely human qualities in healthcare. While commenters universally support ethical governance, they diverge on implementation mechanisms, with some advocating for restoration of federal bioethics advisory structures and others emphasizing distributed ethical responsibility across institutions. The dominant thrust is cautious optimism: stakeholders see AI's potential but insist that ethical frameworks must keep pace with technological advancement.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters emphasize that ethical considerations should guide AI policy from the outset rather than being addressed after deployment. Representative examples include calls for ethical, humane and highly-regulated psychotherapeutic models to be at the forefront of any AI tools, assertions that innovation must serve patients rather than outpace ethical and regulatory frameworks, and recognition that when integrated ethically and responsibly AI can improve outcomes but when ineffectively integrated has potential to do harm.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (6 of 7 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: While all agree ethics should be foundational, commenters differ on whether current frameworks are adequate or require new structures.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of healthcare-focused commenters, particularly provider organizations, emphasize that compassion, empathy, and human judgment are irreplaceable elements of clinical care. The Pennsylvania Medical Society argues that compassion and empathy are uniquely human traits that AI cannot replicate, and further notes physicians employ uniquely human attributes including compassion, empathy, intelligence, acumen, training and experience that AI cannot replicate.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of healthcare-focused commenters

exceptions.text: Technology-focused commenters acknowledge this concern but focus more on governance structures than philosophical distinctions.

consensusPoints.text: All commenters expressing a position on implementation pace advocate for deliberate, evidence-based deployment rather than rapid adoption. AFSCME warns against letting hype and boosterism blind us from potential pitfalls. AORN emphasizes that risk mitigation should be prioritized with nimble and responsive strategies. A mental health counselor argues that technological progress in healthcare should be deliberate, evidence-based, and grounded in enforceable protections.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All commenters expressing a position on implementation pace

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether to restore high-level federal bioethics oversight or encourage distributed ethical responsibility across institutions.

positions.label: Restore Presidential Commission
positions.stance: Establish or restore high-level federal bioethics oversight. A bioethics policy expert provides detailed historical analysis showing precedent exists from pre-2017 commissions that addressed complex topics. NAIRR reports called for an ethics advisory board, and such a commission would provide authoritative guidance across agencies.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly advocated by 1 commenter with detailed historical analysis; implicitly supported by labor union calling for outside expert panels
keyArguments: Historical precedent exists from pre-2017 commissions that addressed complex topics
NAIRR reports called for ethics advisory board
Provides authoritative guidance across agencies

positions.label: Distributed Ethical Responsibility
positions.stance: Encourage diverse institutions to develop their own ethical frameworks. Private companies, academic institutions, faith-based entities, and nonprofits should implement their own ethical programs, allowing context-specific approaches.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed as complementary approach by 1 commenter
keyArguments: Private companies, academic institutions, faith-based entities, and nonprofits should implement their own ethical programs
Allows context-specific approaches

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to address the full spectrum of ethical issues comprehensively or prioritize domain-specific frameworks for sensitive applications.

positions.label: Comprehensive Framework
positions.stance: Address full spectrum of ethical issues including data ownership, privacy, bias, job impacts, and dual-use concerns. A bioethics expert lists 15+ distinct ethical concerns including data ownership disputes, privacy violations, potential re-identification, biased algorithms, governance gaps, unequal access, job displacement, damaged provider-patient relationships, and dual uses/misuses that cannot be addressed in isolation.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters advocate broad scope
keyArguments: Multiple interconnected ethical issues including data ownership, privacy, re-identification, biased algorithms, governance, access, provider-patient relationships, long-term evaluation, and dual uses/misuses
Cannot address issues in isolation

positions.label: Domain-Specific Focus
positions.stance: Prioritize ethical frameworks for particularly sensitive applications, especially mental and behavioral health where ethical stakes are uniquely high and therapeutic relationships require special protection.
positions.supportLevel: 2 commenters emphasize mental/behavioral health as requiring heightened attention
keyArguments: Ethical stakes are uniquely high in behavioral health and psychotherapy
Therapeutic relationships require special protection

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preservation of therapeutic relationships; maintaining human judgment in care; ensuring AI serves rather than replaces clinical expertise. They emphasize lived experience of patient care and the irreplaceable nature of human connection in healing.
specificPoints: Evidence-based implementation with enforceable protections
Ethical frameworks that recognize limits of AI in human care
A mental health counselor warns that science and safeguards have not kept pace with AI implementation speed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member readiness; practice standard development; balancing innovation with patient safety. They serve as a bridge between individual practitioners and policy, developing operational guidance such as AORN's forthcoming AI integration guideline.
specificPoints: Physician leadership in AI governance
Nimble risk mitigation strategies
Ethical practice standards
AORN describes an evidence-informed position that is simultaneously optimistic and cautious

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Worker impacts; ensuring inclusive progress; avoiding hype-driven policy. AFSCME represents hundreds of thousands of health care workers across clinics, nursing homes, and hospitals and emphasizes collective benefit.
specificPoints: External expert panels needed
Prudent, responsible action required
Avoiding boosterism
Emphasis on getting there together as a collective

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Educational competencies; systematic analysis of ethical challenges. Draws on international frameworks including WHO guidance and emphasizes training and competency development.
specificPoints: Educational curricula emphasizing ethical reasoning
Critical evaluation skills alongside technical proficiency
WHO emphasis on ethics and governance guidance for large multi-modal models

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Comprehensive ethical framework; federal advisory structures; historical precedent. Deep knowledge of federal bioethics infrastructure history with systematic cataloging of ethical issues.
specificPoints: Restore Presidential bioethics commission
Involve HHS bioethics staff
Collaborate with non-federal ethics centers
Detailed historical analysis of pre-2017 bioethics commissions addressing cloning, whole genome sequencing, neuroscience, and synthetic biology

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Quality and ethics of AI-enabled mental health services; evidence-based approaches. End-user focus on receiving ethical care.
specificPoints: Ethical standards scaling with demand
Highly-regulated services
Calls for ethical, humane and highly-regulated therapeutic AI

noteworthyInsights.insight: A bioethics expert provides detailed history of Presidential bioethics commissions addressing topics from cloning to pandemics/emergency preparedness, suggesting AI ethics could fit within established governance models rather than requiring entirely new structures.
AORN articulates a nuanced evidence-informed position that is simultaneously optimistic and cautious—a framing that may offer a model for balanced policy development.

An anonymous commenter raises the often-overlooked point that ethical standards must scale proportionally with growing demand for mental health services—suggesting static frameworks may be inadequate for rapidly expanding AI applications.
An independent researcher notes WHO has emphasized need for ethics and governance guidance for large multi-modal models, suggesting opportunity for international coordination on ethical standards.
AFSCME's phrase 'get there together' reframes AI ethics as a matter of inclusive progress rather than simply risk mitigation—a perspective that may resonate with equity-focused policymakers.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Provider organizations emphasize irreplaceable human qualities and physician authority while labor organizations emphasize worker inclusion and skepticism of hype. Academic and policy experts emphasize governance structures and systematic frameworks while individual practitioners emphasize pace concerns and domain-specific risks.
Mental health and behavioral health applications receive heightened ethical scrutiny from multiple commenters, suggesting this domain may require specialized ethical frameworks.
Multiple commenters from different sectors identify absence of adequate federal bioethics infrastructure as a structural problem, not just a policy preference.
Consistent tension across comments between AI's rapid advancement and slower pace of ethical/regulatory framework development.

Unintended consequences identified: using AI to provide care directly rather than support clinicians could run counter to the intent of employing AI in health care, and hype-driven adoption could leave workers and patients behind.

keyQuotations.quote: AI ethics is a set of values, principles, and techniques that employ widely accepted standards of right and wrong to guide moral conduct in the development and use of AI technologies.
Innovation must serve patients—not outpace the ethical and regulatory frameworks designed to safeguard them.
While attempts continue to be made to 'humanize' AI, compassion and empathy, to name two of many, are uniquely human traits that AI cannot replicate.
We must not let hype and boosterism blind us from potential pitfalls and the need for prudent, responsible actions today so that we may all get there together.
Ethical, humane and highly-regulated and evidence-based psychotherapeutic models and services must be at the forefront of any AI tools for therapeutic services.
When integrated ethically and responsibly, AI can improve patient outcomes, patient experience, and healthcare worker experience; when ineffectively integrated, AI has potential to do harm.
Risk mitigation should be prioritized and strategies must be nimble and responsive to the ever-evolving AI risk landscape—both known and unanticipated risks.
Technological progress in healthcare should be deliberate, evidence-based, and grounded in enforceable protections.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate thoughtful engagement with ethical principles, historical precedent, and practical implementation concerns. Multiple stakeholders provide substantive analysis rather than superficial positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Comments draw on historical precedent, international frameworks like WHO guidance, and professional experience. However, empirical evidence on AI ethics outcomes is limited given the emerging nature of the field.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Technology industry and AI developer perspectives are notably absent from this theme. Patient advocacy organizations beyond individual commenters are also underrepresented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - commenters grapple with fundamental tensions between innovation and protection, comprehensive versus domain-specific frameworks, and centralized versus distributed ethical governance.

20.themeDescription: Drug Discovery, Clinical Trials, and Research Applications. This theme encompasses all comments addressing AI use in drug discovery, clinical trial design and execution, and biomedical research. || It includes discussions of AI for drug development, concerns about AI in clinical trials, debates about research applications of AI, and proposals for research AI governance

sections.executiveSummary: Commenters demonstrate strong consensus that AI holds transformative potential for accelerating drug discovery and improving clinical trial efficiency, with stakeholders across sectors—from patient advocates to life sciences companies to professional medical societies—expressing optimism about AI's capacity to compress development timelines and reduce costs. The central tension lies between enthusiasm for AI's research potential and concerns about regulatory frameworks that may either fail to keep pace with innovation or inadvertently create barriers to data sharing and research collaboration. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on standardizing data infrastructure, expanding interoperability frameworks to research contexts, and creating clearer regulatory pathways that enable AI validation while maintaining scientific rigor.

consensusPoints.text: AI Can Significantly Accelerate Drug Discovery and Development. Nearly all commenters express strong agreement that AI has substantial potential to speed drug discovery and reduce development costs. Massive Bio reports AI-powered patient recruitment tools improve enrollment rates by 65% on average and predictive models achieving approximately 85% accuracy in trial outcomes. Patient advocate Steven Zecola emphasizes AI identifies potential treatments much faster than traditional methods and can dramatically increase experiments by studying up to a trillion interactions between variables. The American Dental Association notes AI/ML capabilities compress historically resource-intensive cycles by reducing wet-lab iterations and shift discovery from intuition-driven trial-and-error to predictive, data-driven experimentation.

exceptions.text: Veeva Systems cautions that despite acceleration in drug discovery, clinical trials and clinical research will continue to be a bottleneck in the process of getting treatments to patients faster.

consensusPoints.text: Data Standardization and Interoperability Are Essential Prerequisites. A strong majority of commenters identify fragmented, non-standardized data as a critical barrier to realizing AI's research potential. Veeva Systems observes clinical research data remains fragmented across sponsors, sites, and technology platforms and that clinical research technologies remain highly fragmented compared to routine clinical care. ASRS states data standardization is critical for clinical research and that AI aggregating and analyzing standardized clinical data may unlock answers for treating, curing, or preventing blinding retinal disease. Massive Bio recommends connecting clinical care data with clinical trial and research data through standardized criteria to enable AI to automate trial matching at scale.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (8 of 11)

consensusPoints.text: AI Can Improve Clinical Trial Design, Recruitment, and Execution. Most commenters addressing clinical trials agree AI can enhance multiple phases of the trial lifecycle. ACS CAN states AI applications can enhance almost all areas of clinical trial lifecycle. Steven Zecola recommends AI for enhancing trial design and participant stratification, monitoring safety and efficacy in near real-time, and reducing administrative and compliance costs. ICF notes interoperable data environments enable clinicians and researchers to confidently use AI tools in diagnostics, pediatric oncology, and chronic disease research.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing clinical trials (7 of 11)

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Framework Approach for AI in Research
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether AI in research requires fundamental regulatory restructuring or incremental enhancement of existing frameworks.

positions.label: Transformative Overhaul
positions.stance: Fundamentally restructure approval processes to match AI capabilities. Patient advocates argue current phased trial structure undermines AI's potential, that real-time safety monitoring enables collapsed trial phases, and that existing processes result in glacial pace of development.
positions.supportLevel: 1-2 commenters (patient advocate perspective)
keyArguments: Current phased trial structure undermines AI's potential
Real-time safety monitoring enables collapsed trial phases
Existing processes result in glacial pace of development

positions.label: Incremental Enhancement
positions.stance: Improve existing frameworks while maintaining core regulatory structures. Professional organizations and businesses argue clear guidance within existing structures can facilitate responsible use, that regulatory clarity is needed on model updates and revalidation, and that trial integrity should be maintained through documentation standards.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (6-7), particularly professional organizations and businesses
keyArguments: Clear guidance within existing structures can facilitate responsible use
Need regulatory clarity on model updates and revalidation
Maintain trial integrity through documentation standards

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether NIH should maintain or strengthen controlled-access requirements versus liberalizing access for research advancement.

positions.label: Restrict for Privacy
positions.stance: Maintain or strengthen controlled-access requirements. This position emphasizes patient privacy protection and data security concerns.
positions.supportLevel: Implied by NIH's proposed policy (not directly advocated in comments)
keyArguments: Patient privacy protection
Data security concerns

positions.label: Liberalize for Research
positions.stance: Withdraw or significantly modify restrictive policies. ASRS and others argue the proposed policy has potential chilling effect on imaging-based clinical research and AI development, and that researchers already share data through professional portals.
positions.supportLevel: At least 1 professional society explicitly; others implicitly through data sharing advocacy
keyArguments: Proposed policy has potential chilling effect on imaging-based clinical research and AI development
Researchers already share data through professional portals

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of TEFCA and Interoperability Frameworks
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether TEFCA should be expanded to explicitly include research exchange purposes or maintain focus on clinical care.

positions.label: Expand to Research
positions.stance: Explicitly include research exchange in TEFCA. Veeva Systems and ICF argue this would enable secure, standardized access to clinical data for research activities embedded in care delivery including cohort identification, trial feasibility, and evidence generation.
positions.supportLevel: Multiple business and consulting commenters
keyArguments: Would enable secure, standardized access to clinical data for research activities embedded in care delivery
Supports cohort identification, trial feasibility, and evidence generation

positions.label: Maintain Clinical Focus
positions.stance: Keep TEFCA focused on clinical care exchange. This implicit position suggests research purposes may introduce additional complexity and privacy concerns.
positions.supportLevel: Not explicitly advocated in these comments
keyArguments: Research purposes may introduce additional complexity and privacy concerns

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for AI validation; data fragmentation across research ecosystem; bottlenecks in clinical trial processes. Companies like Massive Bio, Veeva Systems, and Waymark emphasize operational realities of deploying AI at scale and identify specific technical barriers including fragmented platforms and lack of certification standards for research systems.
specificPoints: Expand TEFCA to research purposes
Establish government certification/conformance programs for clinical research systems
Create real-world evidence pathways accepting quasi-experimental designs
Massive Bio's Reticulum Nexus platform demonstrates 65% enrollment improvement
Waymark identifies causal AI systems predicting individual treatment effects as high-potential tools

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for AI model updates during trials; data standardization for specialty-specific research; potential chilling effects of restrictive data policies. Organizations including ASRS, SITC, ADA, and ACS CAN bridge clinical practice and research, emphasizing specialty-specific applications in retinal imaging, cancer immunotherapy, and dental AI, while highlighting existing professional data-sharing infrastructure.
specificPoints: NIH guidance for responsible AI use in federally sponsored research
Investment in AI-driven biomarker discovery
Clear expectations for revalidation and post-deployment monitoring
ASRS's Retina Image Bank serves as model for professional society data sharing
ADA Forsyth Institute's physics-based digital twins demonstrate specialty innovation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Pace of drug development for chronic/rare diseases; cost burden of current treatment paradigms; regulatory processes that may undermine AI's transformative potential. Steven Zecola's 24 years living with Parkinson's disease provides visceral understanding of the glacial pace of R&D, while his economics background enables cost-benefit analysis of current versus AI-enabled approaches.
specificPoints: Collapse discrete trial phases into single elongated trial with real-time safety monitoring
Fundamental regulatory restructuring rather than incremental enhancement
FAP treatment costs range from $10k-$300k depending on stage versus AI-generated treatment showing large, sustained reduction in polyps with no significant safety concerns

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Biomarker validation for preventative care; reproducible science through standardized data; cross-institution research collaboration. Dr. Russell and ICF emphasize the dual role as clinician and AI developer, with focus on precision prevention algorithms integrating multiple data types.
specificPoints: Priority research investment in biomarker identification
Population health surveillance systems
Streamlined multi-omic and clinical data integration
Treatment matching algorithms predicting optimal antidepressant responses

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Translating policy into operational requirements; enabling cross-institution research. ICF brings 50+ years supporting federal health agencies with expertise in bridging policy and implementation.
specificPoints: Standardized data approaches supporting reproducible science
Expanded interoperability for research contexts
Experience translating policy for HHS divisions

noteworthyInsights.insight: Patient advocate Steven Zecola proposes collapsing Phase I, II, and III trials into a single continuous trial with real-time AI safety monitoring—a fundamental restructuring that challenges conventional drug development paradigms while potentially addressing his concern that simply relying on AI for drug discovery while subjecting advances to current approval process would undermine the technology.

Veeva Systems draws a striking comparison: clinical care has EHR certification establishing baseline expectations, but clinical research systems lack equivalent standards, creating a structural disadvantage for research AI development that could be addressed through government certification programs.

ASRS highlights that researchers already share imaging data through professional portals like the Retina Image Bank, suggesting existing infrastructure that could be leveraged rather than replaced—and that restrictive policies may disrupt functioning systems.

Waymark identifies specific high-potential AI categories including causal AI systems predicting individual treatment effects rather than just population-level predictions and federated learning systems enabling multi-site model training without data sharing—pointing to technical approaches that could address both efficacy and privacy concerns simultaneously.

Steven Zecola provides detailed cost data for FAP treatment ($10k+ for early detection, $37k+ for late detection, up to $300k for metastatic colorectal cancer) contrasted with AI-generated treatment showing large, sustained reduction in polyps with no significant safety concerns—offering policymakers tangible economic stakes.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder Type Correlates with Regulatory Philosophy: Technology companies and consulting firms tend to advocate for infrastructure improvements (interoperability, certification) within existing frameworks, patient advocates push for more fundamental regulatory restructuring, and professional societies seek clarity and guidance while preserving research flexibility.

Specialty-Specific AI Applications Drive Engagement: Commenters with specialty focus (retinal disease, oncology, dental, mental health) emphasize domain-specific AI applications and data needs, while cross-cutting infrastructure concerns (interoperability, standards) unite diverse specialties.

Tension Between Speed and Safety Pervades Discussion: Multiple commenters acknowledge trade-offs between accelerating development and maintaining rigorous validation, with proposed solutions attempting to thread this needle through real-time monitoring, continuous learning systems, and RWE pathways.
