consensusPoints.text: Inconsistent state requirements force design to most restrictive interpretation. Multiple commenters specifically note that regulatory fragmentation forces organizations to either design to the strictest state standard or exclude certain markets entirely. InStride Health reports states like Illinois impose stricter standards that often necessitate designing to the most restrictive interpretation or managing complex state-specific implementations. American Dental Association notes state-specific privacy, disclosure, consent, and oversight rules force AI developers to operate under the toughest requirements, increasing operational complexity. SonderMind reveals regulatory ambiguity has forced exclusion of some states from AI client support agents.

exceptions.text: No notable exceptions identified

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters diverge on whether federal standards should comprehensively preempt state AI requirements or establish a federal floor while preserving state authority over clinical practice.

positions.label: Full Federal Preemption
positions.stance: Federal standards should comprehensively preempt state AI requirements. This position is supported primarily by trade associations, national clearinghouses, and multi-state providers. University of Kansas Health System calls for comprehensive national privacy and AI law that preempts state requirements. ACLA identifies preempting patchwork of state and local laws regulating healthcare AI as a key priority. HL7 International supports federal preemption of conflicting state laws to prevent undue burden.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters
keyArguments: Eliminates compliance complexity for organizations operating across jurisdictions
Prevents 'death by a thousand audits' through unified certification frameworks
Ensures consistent patient protections regardless of state

positions.label: Harmonization with Flexibility
positions.stance: Federal floor with preserved state authority over clinical practice. This position is supported by major health systems and professional societies. Advocate Health argues states should retain ability to regulate clinical practice but should operate within federal framework preventing conflicting or overly restrictive rules. American College of Cardiology emphasizes need for alignment and interoperability of frameworks without constraining legitimate state flexibility. Waymark recommends establishing federal floor for healthcare AI governance that provides regulatory certainty while preserving state authority.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5-7 commenters
keyArguments: States have legitimate authority over clinical practice and local healthcare needs
Federal floor provides certainty while allowing states to address unique populations
Avoids constitutional federalism conflicts

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether state legislation primarily creates obstacles to AI deployment or can serve as valuable governance partners complementing federal action.

positions.label: States as Burden
positions.stance: State legislation primarily creates obstacles to beneficial AI deployment. SonderMind notes many state laws inadvertently prohibit safety features like AI-enabled crisis detection. Cooperative Exchange describes how divergent state laws introduce inconsistent definitions, terminology, and thresholds for what constitutes AI use.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (approximately 12-14)
keyArguments: State laws often inadvertently prohibit beneficial safety features
Inconsistent definitions make unified AI model training impossible
Compliance costs disproportionately burden smaller innovators

positions.label: States as Partners
positions.stance: State frameworks can complement federal action through partnership models. FastHealth Corporation proposes a state-led Health Command framework representing a structured approach where states establish governance while proposing federal partnership with HHS, citing Alabama SB97 and Kentucky SB175 as models.
positions.supportLevel: 1-2 commenters
keyArguments: State-led frameworks can establish governance structures that partner with federal agencies
Safe harbor pathways for state-guided AI systems could enable innovation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Operational complexity of managing different requirements across states; inability to deploy beneficial AI tools uniformly; resource drain from compliance activities. Providence operates across 7 states and notes hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules. SonderMind operates in all 50 states and has been forced to exclude some states from AI offerings. Advocate Health operates under three different names across 6 states.
specificPoints: Direct experience navigating conflicting requirements across specific states
Can quantify the operational impact of fragmentation
Propose federal preemption or strong harmonization with clear delineation of state vs. federal authority
Seek safe harbors for federally-compliant systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member compliance burdens; innovation stifling; inconsistent technical requirements. Cooperative Exchange proposes a Common Audit Framework where federal certification satisfies all state requirements. HL7 International references December 2025 Executive Order as model for preemption approach.
specificPoints: Industry-wide view of how fragmentation affects entire sectors
Technical expertise on standards harmonization
Propose federal preemption and NIST-aligned certification frameworks
Recommend expert panels to develop federal standards

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient access to beneficial AI tools; equity implications of geographic variation; patient awareness of rights across state lines. ACS CAN warns fragmentation potentially exacerbates inequity in access to effective AI applications and notes problems when patient is being treated in a state different from their residence. PsiAN calls for clear, science-based federal regulations to define how all AI chatbots can ensure privacy, safety, and efficacy nationwide.
specificPoints: Focus on how regulatory fragmentation affects patients, not just providers
Concern about gaps in protection alongside compliance burdens
Propose science-based federal regulations and consistent patient protections nationwide

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Compliance complexity for research and clinical implementation; gaps in patient protections despite proliferation of laws. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi analyzed 793 state bills, finding 143 potentially impactful to mental health AI with 20 enacted across 11 states, noting this patchwork creates compliance complexity for developers while potentially leaving gaps in patient protections.
specificPoints: Systematic analysis of state legislative landscape
Coordination with state partners on implementation
Propose federal-state alignment and coordinated approaches

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inability to train unified AI models on national datasets; logic-forking requirements; audit proliferation. Cooperative Exchange, processing 6+ billion transactions annually, describes how fragmentation prevents training foundational models on unified national datasets, diluting the predictive power of AI in administrative workflows.
specificPoints: Process billions of transactions across all jurisdictions
See fragmentation's impact on foundational AI infrastructure
Propose federal certification that satisfies all state/payer audits
Seek common definitions and terminology

noteworthyInsights.insight: Perverse incentive structure identified — SonderMind points out that companies most committed to responsible multi-state deployment bear highest compliance burden while companies with less rigorous safety practices face fewer constraints. This suggests current fragmentation may actually reward less responsible actors.

Safety features prohibited by well-intentioned laws — SonderMind reveals that many state laws inadvertently prohibit safety features like AI-enabled crisis detection, demonstrating how state legislation designed to protect patients can have the opposite effect.

Quantified legislative landscape — Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi provides rare systematic data: among 793 state bills reviewed, 143 were identified as potentially impactful to mental health AI, with 20 enacted across 11 states. Notably, most state laws treat mental health as incidental to broader AI or healthcare regulation; explicit mental health provisions remain rare.

State partnership model proposed — FastHealth Corporation offers a contrarian view, citing Alabama SB97 and Kentucky SB175 as examples of state-led Health Command framework that could partner with HHS rather than be preempted by it.

ERISA analogy introduced — Waymark suggests HHS consider whether ERISA-style preemption of inconsistent state AI requirements is appropriate, providing a concrete legal model for how preemption could be structured.

Logic-forking crisis concept — Cooperative Exchange introduces this term to describe how national clearinghouses must localize AI models to accommodate varying state definitions, preventing unified model training—a technical consequence of fragmentation not widely understood.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Specific states repeatedly cited as problematic include Illinois (InStride Health, SonderMind), Colorado (Waymark, SonderMind), California (Waymark, FastHealth), and Texas (Waymark). States cited as potential models include Alabama and Kentucky (FastHealth) for state-federal partnership frameworks. Multi-state providers cluster concerns, with organizations operating in 6+ states like Providence, Advocate Health, and SonderMind expressing most urgent need for harmonization.

Organizational Scale Patterns: Large multi-state systems emphasize operational burden and resource diversion. National infrastructure operators like clearinghouses emphasize technical impossibility of unified AI development. Smaller innovators are described as disproportionately burdened by compliance costs. Trade associations provide industry-wide perspective on innovation suppression.

Sector-Specific Patterns: Mental/behavioral health appears particularly affected, with multiple commenters including SonderMind, InStride Health, PsiAN, and Satyadhar Joshi noting this sector faces especially complex state requirements around consent, data use, and clinical accountability. Pediatric care identified as additional complexity layer. Administrative/claims processing faces unique logic-forking challenges.

Unintended Consequences: State laws designed to protect patients inadvertently prohibit beneficial safety features. Compliance burden advantages large incumbents over innovative smaller companies. Fragmentation creates gaps in patient protection even as it multiplies compliance requirements. Patients may lose access to beneficial tools not due to safety concerns but regulatory ambiguity.

Temporal Urgency: Multiple commenters note accelerating state legislative activity. SonderMind reports more than a dozen states introduced legislation regulating psychotherapy AI risks this year alone. Specific deadlines cited include Colorado's AI Act effective June 30, 2026. Providence notes many states have taken legislative action sensing federal government intended this to be a state issue.

keyQuotations.quote: National clearinghouses face a 'logic-forking' crisis where AI models must be localized to accommodate varying state definitions of 'automated decision-making'. This prevents the training of foundational models on unified national datasets, diluting the predictive power of AI in administrative workflows.
The current 50-state patchwork of laws, enforcement actions, and private rights of action creates confusion and can stifle innovation.

The current environment of state-specific privacy, disclosure, consent, and oversight rules which often have more restrictions than federal privacy and security rules, forces AI developers to operate under the toughest requirements, increasing operational complexity.
The result is that those members are denied access to a beneficial, clinically-guardrailed tool, not because of a safety concern, but because of regulatory ambiguity.
The patchwork of state-level consent rules has paved an unreasonable pathway for both patients and providers to be confident in when consent is needed and how to appropriately secure it.

If a clearinghouse's AI infrastructure is certified by a NIST-aligned federal body, that certification should satisfy all state and payer-specific audit requirements, eliminating the 'death by a thousand audits' currently stifling innovation.

Consent for the use of AI, transparency, accountability, product liability and regulatory authority are all issues that need to be resolved at a federal level to avoid a patchwork of state regulations.
Hospital resources should not be futilely spent on compliance with state schemes that will eventually be preempted by federal rules.

States should retain ability to regulate clinical practice but should operate within federal framework preventing conflicting or overly restrictive rules. This is essential for multistate health systems and nationwide AI development.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of federalism issues, provide specific examples and evidence, and engage constructively with policy tradeoffs. Multiple commenters offer concrete legislative models and quantified data to support their positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters provide substantial evidence including specific state laws cited (Colorado AI Act, Illinois requirements), quantified legislative analysis (793 bills reviewed), operational data (6+ billion transactions), and concrete examples of market exclusion and compliance challenges.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from state regulators, consumer advocacy groups focused on privacy rights, and smaller healthcare providers who may lack resources to comment. The perspective of states that have enacted AI legislation is notably absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves constitutional federalism questions, technical AI development constraints, multi-stakeholder coordination challenges, and competing values of innovation versus local flexibility

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate near-universal consensus that a significant governance gap exists for AI tools influencing clinical care outside FDA medical device jurisdiction, with 45+ organizations and individuals identifying this as a critical barrier to both innovation and patient safety. The central tension lies between those advocating for voluntary, industry-led certification frameworks versus those calling for enforceable federal standards, with most stakeholders seeking a middle path of clear federal guidance that enables rather than mandates compliance. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on HHS—particularly ASTP/ONC—establishing baseline governance expectations, model contract language, and accountability frameworks that can scale across the diverse landscape of non-device clinical AI.

consensusPoints.text: A significant governance gap exists for non-device clinical AI. Nearly all commenters explicitly acknowledge that AI tools influencing clinical care but falling outside FDA jurisdiction represent a growing category with inadequate oversight. Representative examples include observations that many AI tools are neither regulated medical devices nor purely administrative systems, yet they shape how clinicians interpret information, prioritize tasks, and frame clinical decisions. Documentation support, care coordination, operational decision support, and generative AI embedded in workflows lack clear regulatory pathways.

exceptions.text: HealthScoreAI expresses caution about expanding oversight, arguing the historical consumer-awareness model has worked and HHS should be cautious in prioritizing large-scale evaluation programs focused on non-medical devices.

consensusPoints.text: Regulatory uncertainty is blocking adoption and innovation. A strong majority of commenters identify unclear governance expectations—not safety concerns per se—as the primary barrier to responsible AI deployment. Legal and compliance teams delay or block deployment due to uncertainty, not known safety risks. Organizations cannot invest when regulatory requirements are unclear for non-device AI, and this gap is preventing developers from bringing more tools to market.

Liability and accountability allocation is fundamentally unclear. Nearly all commenters addressing liability agree that existing legal frameworks fail to adequately assign responsibility when AI influences clinical decisions. Risk scores or AI-generated summaries may shape how urgently a patient is evaluated or what information a clinician focuses on, yet it is often unclear who is responsible if the tool is wrong or misleading. Vendor contracts typically disclaim clinical liability while clinicians and provider organizations remain fully responsible for care decisions.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing liability (38 of 46)

consensusPoints.text: HHS/ASTP/ONC should take a leadership role. Most commenters specifically call on HHS—often naming ASTP/ONC—to establish governance expectations for non-device AI, including publishing cross-agency governance principles and helping fill this gap via federal standards or recommendations for shared safety, effectiveness, risk-appropriate monitoring, and privacy/security responsibilities.

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Approach: Voluntary vs. Mandatory Frameworks
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters are divided on whether governance frameworks for non-device clinical AI should be voluntary, mandatory, or a hybrid approach based on risk levels.

positions.label: Voluntary Certification
positions.stance: Supporters including most business stakeholders and several trade associations argue for voluntary certification that preserves innovation flexibility while establishing baseline expectations. Organizations like Massive Bio, SanLuca AI, and URAC advocate for approaches mirroring the successful EHR certification model under 45 CFR Part 170, allowing market-driven adoption without creating compliance barriers for smaller organizations.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 60% of commenters
keyArguments: Preserves innovation flexibility while establishing baseline expectations
Mirrors successful EHR certification model under 45 CFR Part 170
Allows market-driven adoption without creating compliance barriers for smaller organizations

positions.label: Enforceable Standards
positions.stance: Patient safety organizations like ECRI and clinical professional associations including AORN argue that voluntary frameworks have historically failed to ensure adequate protections. The Psychotherapy Action Network suggests HHS should not accelerate AI adoption until FDA completes evaluations of AI as medical devices.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25% of commenters
keyArguments: Voluntary frameworks have historically failed to ensure adequate protections
Patient safety requires enforceable accountability, not optional best practices
Creates level playing field and prevents race to bottom

positions.label: Hybrid/Tiered Approach
positions.stance: Commenters including Dr. Bethany Russell, Medvidi, and HIMSS EHR Association explicitly advocate for risk-proportionate frameworks, arguing that one-size-fits-all approaches are inappropriate given the diversity of AI applications and that risk stratification allows appropriate oversight without stifling low-risk innovation.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15% of commenters
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all inappropriate given diversity of AI applications
Risk stratification allows appropriate oversight without stifling low-risk innovation

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate which entity should lead governance efforts for non-device clinical AI, with options including HHS/ONC, FTC, or private sector accreditation organizations.

positions.label: HHS/ONC Leadership
positions.stance: A strong majority supports HHS/ONC leadership, arguing that ONC has existing certification infrastructure and health IT expertise. Commenters like SANCIAN LLC and Cyberhill Partners note ONC can leverage Conditions of Participation and existing regulatory touchpoints as a natural extension of its health IT governance role.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (approximately 35 of 46 commenters)
keyArguments: ONC has existing certification infrastructure and health IT expertise
Can leverage Conditions of Participation and existing regulatory touchpoints
Natural extension of health IT governance role

positions.label: FTC for Consumer AI
positions.stance: The American Medical Association argues that FTC would be the most appropriate regulatory body to address transparency issues related to consumer protection for non-device AI, given its consumer protection mandate being better suited for direct-to-consumer tools.
positions.supportLevel: Limited explicit support (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Consumer protection mandate better suited for direct-to-consumer tools
FDA authority gaps require alternative federal agency involvement

positions.label: Private Sector Accreditation
positions.stance: Organizations including Providence, WellSpan Health, and accreditation bodies argue for private sector accreditation with deemed status similar to 42 CFR Part 488, noting faster adaptation than federal rulemaking, reduced government burden, and proven models in other healthcare contexts.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters
keyArguments: Faster adaptation than federal rulemaking
Reduces government burden while maintaining standards
Proven model in other healthcare contexts

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Governance: Clinical vs. Administrative AI
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether governance frameworks should broadly include administrative AI or narrowly focus on clinical-impact AI tools.

positions.label: Broad Scope
positions.stance: Commenters including Penguin AI and Cooperative Exchange argue that administrative AI such as scheduling, coding, and prior authorization indirectly affects care, governance gaps exist across the spectrum, and artificial distinctions create loopholes. They note AI in clearinghouse environments is often embedded within routing, validation, and exception logic.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20 commenters
keyArguments: Administrative AI indirectly affects care
Governance gaps exist across the spectrum
Artificial distinctions create loopholes

positions.label: Narrow Clinical Focus
positions.stance: Commenters like Onboard AI emphasize prioritizing clinical decision-influencing tools, arguing that limited regulatory resources should focus on highest-risk applications, administrative AI poses a different and lower risk profile, and overly broad scope could stifle beneficial automation.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10 commenters
keyArguments: Limited regulatory resources should focus on highest-risk applications
Administrative AI poses different (lower) risk profile
Overly broad scope could stifle beneficial automation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability exposure when using AI tools without clear standards of care, lack of guidance on appropriate clinical oversight and override documentation, and vendor contracts that disclaim liability while providers bear full responsibility. Frontline clinicians like Dr. Emilie Maxie emphasize the practical reality that AI outputs shape clinical attention and prioritization even when not making explicit recommendations—creating de facto influence without corresponding accountability.
specificPoints: Clear federal expectations for disclosure and governance norms are needed
Safe harbors for reasonable reliance on validated tools would help
Model contract language protecting providers is essential

Significant uncertainty exists in community health settings regarding liability when clinicians rely on AI recommendations that result in harm or decline to follow recommendations and harm occurs, as noted by Advocate Health

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty blocking investment and market entry, inconsistent evaluation requirements across health system customers, and competitive disadvantage for responsible developers who build governance into products. Developers like Kooth emphasize that unclear rules create perverse incentives—companies avoiding medical device classification can deploy with minimal oversight while responsible developers face higher barriers.
specificPoints: Voluntary certification with clear criteria is preferred
Model contract language and safe harbors would enable market growth
Federal preemption of state patchwork is needed

Health systems often struggle with unclear liability and accountability frameworks, uncertainty around appropriate clinical oversight, and inconsistent internal review processes, as noted by Ellipsis Health

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member liability exposure and malpractice implications, lack of standards for evaluating AI tools before adoption, and privacy and cybersecurity gaps for AI vendors outside HIPAA. Professional associations like AMA and AUA emphasize the need for guidance that protects their members while enabling beneficial innovation—seeking clarity on standards of care when AI is involved.
specificPoints: HHS guidance on liability allocation is needed
Strengthened privacy requirements for AI vendors are essential
Post-market surveillance frameworks should be established
Lack of regulatory standards for AI impacting clinical care results in physicians potentially shouldering entirety of risk of harm

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance frameworks that enable rather than impede research and innovation, need for evaluation infrastructure and representative datasets, and architectural foundations for accountability. Researchers like BlueHalo emphasize the need for governance infrastructure that makes existing legal frameworks functional—focusing on structural solutions rather than just compliance requirements.
specificPoints: Reference architectures for non-device AI governance are needed
A Clinical AI Accountability Registry should be established
Deterministic semantic substrates for algorithmic transparency would help

For non-medical device AI, the primary challenge is not the absence of innovation, but the lack of environments and representative datasets in which evaluation, monitoring, governance, and interoperability practices can be developed, tested, and refined

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety and protection from unvalidated AI tools, ensuring FDA completes device evaluations before HHS accelerates adoption, and privacy protections for sensitive behavioral health data. Advocacy groups like the Psychotherapy Action Network express the most caution about AI adoption, emphasizing that governance should follow rather than precede safety validation.
specificPoints: Pause AI acceleration until FDA evaluations complete
Uniform federal standards consistent with FDA determinations are needed
FDA has found over 10,000 applications already widely available to consumers without appropriate premarket performance evaluations, risk management, or post-market evaluations

noteworthyInsights.insight: Walt Simmons of the Interstitial Institute offers a sophisticated analysis reframing the governance gap as architectural rather than merely regulatory: The novel issue is not liability, indemnification, privacy, or security individually—it is what happens when an AI system occupies the clinical decision space between physician and patient without any of those legal frameworks being able to reach where decisions are actually being made.

Dr. Eli Talbert, a Senior Data Scientist at U.S. Special Operations Command submitting in personal capacity, draws parallels between military and clinical AI governance, noting that within the non-device space, a structural gap remains—current frameworks do not specify how clinician override behavior must be logged, how authority boundaries are operationally defined, or how escalation pathways are monitored over time. His defense experience suggests healthcare can learn from high-stakes military AI accountability frameworks.

HealthScoreAI raises an underappreciated concern about data classification transformation risk: Consumer-generated data originating outside HIPAA framework can be transformed into PHI through association with covered entity, making it inappropriate and unsafe to allow designated entities to move data back and forth between PHI and consumer data to avoid regulatory obligations. This highlights how non-device AI can create regulatory arbitrage opportunities.

Cooperative Exchange, representing 90% of the healthcare clearinghouse industry, offers a unique perspective that AI in clearinghouse environments is often embedded within routing, validation, and exception logic rather than deployed as a discrete tool. They propose recognizing clearinghouses as governance infrastructure for AI audit trails and compliance verification.

ACLA and Cellworks argue that CLIA-certified laboratory algorithmic services demonstrate an existing governance model for non-device AI, noting that CLIA plus rigorous technical assessment through MolDx and NYSDOH establishes rigorous safeguards sufficient for governance without an FDA pathway. The recent court decision vacating FDA regulation of laboratory developed tests adds legal weight to this argument.

David Bynon proposes a technical solution addressing the gap between regulatory data and AI interpretation: Effective AI governance in healthcare will not be achieved solely by constraining model behavior. It also requires strengthening the semantic signals those models consume. His concept of a Deterministic Semantic Substrate addresses this infrastructure gap.

emergingPatterns.pattern: State patchwork concerns are raised by multiple commenters including SonderMind, Waymark, and Providence, who describe operational challenges from inconsistent state AI regulations. SonderMind notes that regulatory ambiguity has forced exclusion of some states from AI client support agents. Multi-state health systems like Providence operating in 7 states and Advocate Health in 5 states emphasize the need for federal preemption or harmonization.

Large integrated systems including Providence, Advocate Health, WellSpan, and Hackensack Meridian have developed internal AI governance frameworks but seek federal validation and standardization. Smaller and safety-net providers are described as lacking capacity for rigorous AI evaluation, creating equity concerns. WellSpan notes that benefits are not confined to well-resourced academic medical centers only if governance frameworks are accessible.

Behavioral and mental health providers including InStride Health, SonderMind, Medvidi, Kooth, NeuroFlow, and the Psychotherapy Action Network express heightened concerns about sensitivity of mental health data, liability for suicide risk alerts, and appropriateness of AI in therapeutic relationships. Radiology through ACR has the most mature AI governance infrastructure through Data Science Institute programs. Dentistry through ADA notes unique challenges for dental practices making complex technology decisions.

Responsible developer disadvantage is identified as an unintended consequence where companies building governance into products face higher barriers than those avoiding device classification. Compliance theater risk emerges where without clear standards, organizations may implement superficial governance that does not address real risks. Innovation concentration may result as regulatory uncertainty favors large, well-resourced organizations over innovative startups.

keyQuotations.quote: Risk scores or AI-generated summaries may shape how urgently a patient is evaluated or what information a clinician focuses on, yet it is often unclear who is responsible if the tool is wrong or misleading.
The result is a system in which responsibility is concentrated on providers, but the rules governing acceptable AI use remain underdeveloped.

Non-medical devices that use AI can shape clinical decisions at scale while escaping the normal checks that protect patients. HHS does not need to micromanage innovation, but it should communicate expectations, support adoption with practical guardrails, and enforce accountability so the systems used in care are measurable, monitorable, and honest about what they are not able to do in a way that end users can understand.
Legal and compliance teams delay or block deployment due to uncertainty, not known safety risks.

For non-medical device AI, the primary challenge is not the absence of innovation, but the lack of environments and representative datasets in which evaluation, monitoring, governance, and interoperability practices can be developed, tested, and refined in real-world conditions.
This gap is preventing developers from bringing more tools to market.

What HHS can do that no existing legal framework can do: define where the authority boundary sits. Specify which clinical outputs require human decision authority as a structural prerequisite, not as an audit finding after the fact.
Vendor agreements frequently designate the clinician as the final authority, yet in high-throughput environments, time pressure can limit meaningful verification.

The novel issue is not liability, indemnification, privacy, or security individually—it is what happens when an AI system occupies the clinical decision space between physician and patient without any of those legal frameworks being able to reach where decisions are actually being made.
Developers incentivized to deploy general-purpose AI with minimal oversight, avoiding more responsible designs that trigger medical device regulation.
The result is a democratization of access to high-quality AI, ensuring benefits are not confined to well-resourced academic medical centers.
Effective AI governance in healthcare will not be achieved solely by constraining model behavior. It also requires strengthening the semantic signals those models consume.
Providence fully supports a nationally recognized accreditation path for AI tools... financial and administrative burden should not fall to federal government.
A clinician receiving an AI-generated urgent alert faces genuine questions about what the standard of care requires.

Within the non-device space, a structural gap remains—current frameworks do not specify how clinician override behavior must be logged, how authority boundaries are operationally defined, or how escalation pathways are monitored over time.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of the regulatory landscape, with detailed technical and legal analysis. Most commenters provide specific recommendations with supporting rationale, and there is substantive engagement with the complexity of governing AI that falls between traditional regulatory categories.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific operational experiences, existing regulatory frameworks like 45 CFR Part 170 and CLIA, and concrete examples of governance challenges. However, quantitative data on AI-related harms or benefits in the non-device space remains limited, with most evidence being experiential or analogical.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Consumer and patient advocacy perspectives are underrepresented, with only 2 advocacy group comments identified. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are mentioned by others but not directly represented. International perspectives on non-device AI governance approaches are largely absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves intersecting regulatory jurisdictions (FDA, ONC, FTC, state regulators), multiple stakeholder categories with competing interests, and fundamental questions about the nature of clinical decision-making when AI is involved. The architectural framing offered by some commenters suggests the challenge extends beyond traditional regulatory solutions.

1.7.themeDescription: Adaptive and Continuously Learning AI Governance

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that traditional static approval models are fundamentally inadequate for AI systems that evolve over time, with virtually all stakeholders agreeing that post-deployment monitoring, version control, and change notification are essential governance requirements. The central tension lies between enabling beneficial AI adaptation (which improves performance as populations and care patterns evolve) and maintaining predictable, auditable systems that clinicians can trust and regulators can oversee. Commenters converge on recommending lifecycle-based governance frameworks with defined triggers for re-validation, transparent change logs, and mechanisms for detecting performance drift—though they diverge on whether developers should have flexibility to make updates under pre-approved protocols or face stricter re-review requirements.

consensusPoints.text: Static approval models are fundamentally inadequate for adaptive AI. Nearly all commenters explicitly or implicitly acknowledge that traditional one-time approval frameworks fail to address AI systems that learn or update over time. Representative examples include an Academic Epidemiologist stating that static approval models are incoherent when systems are designed to evolve, an Independent Researcher noting that frameworks treating AI tools as static products fail to account for their dynamic, service-oriented nature, and NeuroFlow observing that traditional device validation assumes a static intervention while AI tools that continue learning in deployment require frameworks for detecting performance drift.

exceptions.text: One commenter from Interstella notes that static models can avoid continuous learning challenges but sacrifice performance as populations and care delivery patterns evolve.

consensusPoints.text: Post-deployment monitoring is essential, not optional. A strong majority of commenters call for mandatory ongoing monitoring after AI deployment, treating it as a safety requirement rather than a best practice. Commenters emphasize that continuity, change-management, and post-deployment monitoring should be treated as safety requirements, that many AI deployments lack mechanisms for continuous monitoring once tools are introduced into clinical workflows, and that without monitoring, generative AI can propagate errors throughout a patient's medical record, ultimately impacting downstream clinical decisions, quality reporting, and patient trust.

Transparent change logs and version control are necessary. Nearly all commenters addressing governance mechanisms agree that AI systems require clear versioning, change documentation, and notification of updates. Recommendations include requiring transparent change logs, ensuring independent auditability of adaptive systems, setting expectations for continuous learning and model updates including material-update notifications, change logs, and re-validation requirements, and model contract clauses covering versioning and advance notice of model updates.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing governance mechanisms (15+ of 24)

consensusPoints.text: Silent model updates pose unacceptable risks. Most commenters specifically identify undisclosed or silent updates as a critical governance failure. Concerns include that silent model updates can alter AI behavior without triggering regulatory re-review, that unannounced model changes can alter behavior without warning undermining clinical governance, training, and auditability, and that silent updates and model drift can alter performance without notice.

areasOfDebate.topic: When Should Updates Trigger Regulatory Re-Review?
areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders disagree on whether developers should have flexibility to make updates under pre-approved protocols or face stricter re-review requirements for changes to adaptive AI systems.

positions.label: Developer Flexibility
positions.stance: Allow updates under pre-approved protocols without new submissions. Massive Bio, Pair Team, and Interstella argue that FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plans provide a proven model for enabling updates within validated bounds, and that requiring resubmission for each change would stifle beneficial improvements and create regulatory bottlenecks.
positions.supportLevel: 4-5 commenters, primarily AI developers and technology companies
keyArguments: FDA's Predetermined Change Control Plans (PCCPs) provide a proven model for enabling updates within validated bounds
Requiring resubmission for each change would stifle beneficial improvements and create regulatory bottlenecks
Developers can maintain performance against approved benchmarks while incorporating new data

positions.label: Stricter Re-Validation
positions.stance: Require defined triggers for mandatory re-review. The American Academy of Sleep Medicine, ECRI, and Dr. Bhagavathula argue that material changes in algorithm performance must be disclosed and reviewed, that clinicians need stable, predictable tools whose characteristics are reproducible, and that without clear triggers, adaptation becomes experimentation embedded within routine care.
positions.supportLevel: 8-10 commenters, primarily medical professional societies, safety organizations, and academics
keyArguments: Material changes in algorithm performance must be disclosed and reviewed
Clinicians need stable, predictable tools whose characteristics are reproducible
Without clear triggers, adaptation becomes experimentation embedded within routine care

areasOfDebate.topic: Who Bears Responsibility for Monitoring Adaptive Systems?
areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate whether developers should maintain primary responsibility for monitoring or whether monitoring requires multi-stakeholder involvement.

positions.label: Developer-Led Monitoring
positions.stance: Developers should maintain primary responsibility for monitoring adaptive systems. Massive Bio, Pair Team, and NeuroFlow emphasize that developers have technical expertise to detect drift and validate performance, that automated drift detection algorithms can alert to distribution shifts, and that developer-led evaluation protocols could be FDA-reviewed and approved.
positions.supportLevel: 5-6 commenters, primarily technology companies
keyArguments: Developers have technical expertise to detect drift and validate performance
Automated drift detection algorithms can alert to distribution shifts
Developer-led evaluation protocols could be FDA-reviewed and approved

positions.label: Shared Responsibility
positions.stance: Monitoring requires multi-stakeholder involvement. HIMSS EHRA, the American Urological Association, and the Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association argue that health systems need mechanisms to audit model behavior and track changes, that clinician time spent on monitoring should be recognized in payment, and that industry needs best practices for shared responsibilities for updates, logic and data transparency, ongoing training and performance monitoring.
positions.supportLevel: 6-8 commenters, primarily trade associations and health systems
keyArguments: Health systems need mechanisms to audit model behavior and track changes
Clinician time spent on monitoring should be recognized in payment
Industry needs best practices for shared responsibilities for updates, logic and data transparency, ongoing training and performance monitoring

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Define Acceptable Drift Thresholds?
areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate whether acceptable drift should vary by clinical risk level or whether standardized field-wide thresholds are needed.

positions.label: Risk-Based Thresholds
positions.stance: Acceptable drift should vary by clinical risk level. Waymark and ATA Action argue that higher-risk applications warrant stricter thresholds and more frequent monitoring, and that frameworks should define acceptable drift thresholds, monitoring requirements, and revalidation triggers based on clinical risk.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters
keyArguments: Higher-risk applications warrant stricter thresholds and more frequent monitoring
Frameworks should define acceptable drift thresholds, monitoring requirements, and revalidation triggers based on clinical risk

positions.label: Standardized Thresholds
positions.stance: Need for consistent, field-wide standards. NeuroFlow notes this is an area where the field lacks consensus and that federal investment in methodology would benefit all stakeholders.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters
keyArguments: This is an area where the field lacks consensus
Federal investment in methodology would benefit all stakeholders

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory flexibility to enable beneficial updates; clear pathways for continuous improvement; avoiding submission requirements for every change
specificPoints: Emphasize technical solutions like automated drift detection, averaging across multiple tries for non-deterministic outputs, and real-time anomaly flagging
Propose expanding PCCPs, authorizing developer-led evaluation protocols, and implementing governance drift detection systems
Massive Bio describes Radiant Core for real-time anomaly supervision
FERZ AI proposes cryptographic audit trails capturing policy state at decision time

stakeholderPerspectives.stakeholderType: Medical Professional Societies and Trade Associations
stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clinician liability for AI outputs that change over time; need for stable, predictable tools; uncompensated oversight burden
specificPoints: Emphasize that variability from algorithm updates complicates longitudinal care, undermines quality measurement, and creates documentation challenges
Propose clear regulatory guidance on when updates require renewed review, recognition of clinician monitoring time in payment, and defined triggers for re-evaluation or withdrawal
The American Academy of Sleep Medicine notes algorithms producing different outputs for same clinical scenario undermines confidence
The American Academy of Neurology warns generative AI can propagate errors throughout a patient's medical record

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Scientific rigor in post-deployment surveillance; treating performance drift as a safety signal; process-based accountability
specificPoints: Frame adaptive AI governance as requiring fundamental shift from product-based to process-based regulation

Propose Post-Deployment Algorithmic Surveillance (PDAS) framework, predefined learning boundaries, mandatory rollback mechanisms, and continuity-aware design treating rupture as ethically consequential risk
Dr. Bhagavathula warns that without safeguards, adaptation becomes experimentation embedded within routine care
The Markkula Center cites Reuters investigation into TruDi Navigation System adverse events after AI capabilities were introduced

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation challenges; integration with clinical workflows; resource constraints for monitoring
specificPoints: Emphasize that purchasing and integration cycles extend well beyond a year, during which clinical practices, patient populations, and data environments change
Propose version-specific safety materials, standardized model-card artifacts, and expectations for material-update notifications
The Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association calls for change logs and re-validation requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Auditability and incident investigation; accountability gaps; retrospective analysis capability
specificPoints: Focus on forensic challenges—what would the model have said on the date of the incident may be unknowable
Propose model contract clauses covering versioning and trigger re-validation for model updates, retrieval source changes, prompt/template changes, or workflow integration changes
ECRI provides comprehensive list of re-validation triggers
SANCIAN emphasizes provenance tracking for data lineage and model versioning

noteworthyInsights.insight: Temporal governance as forensic challenge — FERZ AI introduces the concept that AI systems may be updated between deployment and adverse event, making retrospective analysis impossible without infrastructure that captures policy state at decision time. This reframes governance as requiring temporal snapshots, not just current-state documentation.

Continuity as safety-relevant design target — Renee Pope proposes treating continuity as a measurable safety property and rupture (from updates, outages, or drift) as ethically consequential risk. This framework suggests HHS could support research treating continuity/rupture safeguards as testable safety criteria.

Real-world adverse event evidence — The Markkula Center cites a Reuters investigation of Johnson & Johnson subsidiary Acclarent's TruDi Navigation System, where after AI capabilities were introduced, FDA received substantially larger number of unconfirmed malfunction and adverse event reports, with allegations that surgeons were misinformed about instrument location during sinus procedures. This provides concrete evidence of update-related harms.

Agentic drift as semantic problem — David Bynon introduces agentic drift as AI systems diverging from authoritative policy intent when operating without persistent, verifiable semantic memory substrate. His solution—requiring AI to retrieve from machine-readable policy rather than infer from narrative text—offers a novel technical approach to maintaining fidelity over time.

Non-determinism management strategies — Medvidi uniquely addresses the challenge that different results can be obtained for same input by proposing practical strategies like averaging across multiple tries—a technical approach not mentioned by other commenters.

Static models as deliberate choice — Waymark notes that static models avoid this issue but sacrifice performance as populations and care delivery patterns evolve, highlighting the genuine trade-off between governance simplicity and clinical effectiveness.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder Type Correlates with Flexibility Preferences — AI developers and technology companies consistently favor flexible frameworks (PCCPs, developer-led protocols) that enable updates within pre-approved bounds, while medical professional societies and safety organizations consistently favor stricter triggers and mandatory re-review. This pattern suggests regulatory solutions may need to balance innovation enablement with clinical predictability.

Governance Sophistication Varies by Organization Type — Large technology companies describe sophisticated internal monitoring systems (Massive Bio's Radiant Core, FERZ AI's cryptographic audit trails), while medical professional societies like the American Academy of Neurology emphasize their members lack robust health IT support for vetting AI tools. This gap suggests governance requirements may need tiered implementation support.

Temporal Dimension Increasingly Central — Multiple commenters independently identify the same core insight: the AI approved today may not be the AI operating tomorrow. This temporal framing appears across academics, developers, safety organizations, and clinicians, suggesting strong foundation for lifecycle-based regulatory approaches.

Contracting as Underexplored Governance Lever — Several commenters including ECRI, HIMSS EHRA, and EHY Consulting identify contractual mechanisms as critical but underdeveloped. Model contract clauses, performance warranties, and indemnification frameworks represent non-regulatory governance tools. Industry-led best practices development may complement regulatory approaches.

Payment Recognition as Enabler — The American Urological Association uniquely calls for recognizing clinician time spent on required post-deployment monitoring in payment. This suggests monitoring mandates without corresponding payment reform may face implementation barriers.

keyQuotations.quote: Static approval models are incoherent when systems are designed to evolve.
Without safeguards, adaptation becomes experimentation embedded within routine care.
'What would the model have said on the date of the incident?' may be unknowable.
Without monitoring, [generative AI] can propagate errors throughout a patient's medical record, ultimately impacting downstream clinical decisions, quality reporting, and patient trust.
Healthcare providers require stable, validated tools whose performance characteristics are predictable and reproducible.

Continuity, change-management, and post-deployment monitoring should be treated as safety requirements... workflow drift, updates, outages, and data shifts can degrade performance and worsen inequities if monitoring is weak.
AI systems may be updated between deployment and adverse event—without governance infrastructure capturing policy state at decision time, retrospective analysis is impossible.
Monitor for data drift—automated drift detection algorithms can alert if the input data distribution shifts significantly.
Regulation must shift toward process-based accountability.
This is an area where the field lacks consensus.

Frontline users, purchasers, and health systems often lack easy access to clear, comparable, version-specific information describing what changed, what evidence supports the current version, contexts in which performance is validated, and conditions necessary for safe use.
Static models avoid this issue but sacrifice performance as populations and care delivery patterns evolve.
Traditional contracting models don't adequately address performance warranties, update obligations, or risks from model drift and re-training.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of the technical and governance challenges posed by adaptive AI systems. Arguments are well-reasoned with specific examples, and stakeholders engage substantively with the core tension between enabling beneficial adaptation and maintaining predictable, auditable systems.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite specific real-world examples including the Reuters investigation of the TruDi Navigation System adverse events. Technology companies describe concrete technical solutions. However, quantitative data on drift thresholds and monitoring effectiveness remains limited, with one commenter noting this is an area where the field lacks consensus.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Payment and reimbursement perspectives are underrepresented, with only the American Urological Association addressing the need to recognize clinician monitoring time in payment. Patient and consumer advocacy perspectives are notably absent from the discussion of adaptive AI governance.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High — The theme involves intersecting technical, regulatory, clinical, and contractual dimensions. Stakeholders recognize genuine trade-offs between governance simplicity and clinical effectiveness, and between innovation enablement and safety assurance.

10.1.themeDescription: Algorithmic Bias Detection and Mitigation Approaches. This sub-theme addresses concerns about bias in AI algorithms and approaches to identifying and addressing bias. || It includes concerns about AI trained on biased historical data, proposals for bias auditing requirements, debates about appropriate bias metrics, and discussions of technical approaches to bias mitigation

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types—healthcare providers, technology companies, trade associations, and patient advocacy groups—that algorithmic bias in healthcare AI poses serious risks and requires systematic detection and mitigation. The central tension lies not in whether to address bias, but in how to allocate responsibility between AI developers and healthcare deployers, and whether bias testing should be mandatory or voluntary. Commenters overwhelmingly favor ongoing monitoring over one-time assessments, with strong support for diverse training datasets, demographic subgroup analysis, and federal certification or guidance frameworks.

consensusPoints.text: AI trained on biased historical data perpetuates healthcare disparities. Nearly all commenters acknowledge that AI systems can inherit and amplify biases present in training data, particularly affecting racial minorities and underserved populations. Massive Bio notes that an algorithm widely used to prioritize patients for care management systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients by using healthcare spending as a proxy for illness. Global Liver Institute observes that when AI equates spending with need, it quietly carries inequities forward. National MS Society warns that algorithms could amplify errors and preexisting biases in source data.

exceptions.text: MHK suggests that using industry-standard foundation models rather than proprietary training may reduce bias introduction, though acknowledges residual concerns remain.

consensusPoints.text: Diverse and representative training datasets are essential. A strong majority of commenters explicitly call for AI models to be trained and validated on demographically diverse patient populations. AAFP supports use of diverse and representative datasets. ADLM recommends using diverse and representative patient datasets in AI model training and validation. New Jersey Innovation Institute urges developing AI with diverse datasets and thorough validation in real-world settings.

exceptions.text: RBMA notes that even with good intentions, validation may be incomplete, questioning whether AI in mammography was trained on women across all breast density categories, varied risk profiles, and diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds.

consensusPoints.text: Bias testing should include demographic subgroup analysis. Most commenters specifically recommend stratifying AI performance metrics by demographic factors such as race, age, gender, and socioeconomic status. Interstella recommends including subset analysis to detect biases across races, ages, and genders in validation. SANCIAN LLC calls for bias testing across demographic subgroups as part of pre-deployment validation. Penguin Ai advocates using subgroup performance metrics to detect and correct bias.

Ongoing monitoring is preferred over one-time assessments. A clear majority explicitly advocate for continuous bias auditing rather than single pre-deployment evaluations. Ellipsis Health supports ongoing bias audits rather than one-time assessments. Satyadhar Joshi recommends continuous monitoring architectures with bias monitoring subgroup analysis. Interstella states that algorithmic bias audits should be routine.

areasOfDebate.topic: Mandatory vs. Voluntary Bias Testing Requirements
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether bias testing and mitigation should be required as a condition of deployment or certification, or encouraged through voluntary frameworks and incentives.

positions.label: Mandate Requirements
positions.stance: Require bias testing and mitigation as a condition of deployment or certification. Supported by most advocacy groups and several healthcare providers including Massive Bio, Cyberhill Partners, and Bethany Russell.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 60% of commenters (18 of 30)
keyArguments: Bias can go unnoticed until widespread harm occurs, as noted by Global Liver Institute
Voluntary approaches create inconsistent protections across populations
Section 1557 of the ACA provides existing legal authority, as argued by Cyberhill Partners

positions.label: Voluntary/Incentive-Based
positions.stance: Encourage bias testing through certification, payment incentives, or guidance rather than mandates. Primarily supported by technology companies and some trade associations including ICF, WellSpan Health, and MHK.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25% of commenters (7-8 of 30)
keyArguments: Flexibility allows adaptation to different use cases and contexts
Payment incentives can drive adoption without regulatory burden
Voluntary certification can establish market differentiation

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether vendors should bear significant responsibility for bias in their products or whether existing liability structures with enhanced transparency requirements are adequate.

positions.label: Shared Developer-Deployer Liability
positions.stance: Vendors should bear significant responsibility for bias in their products. Strongly supported by healthcare providers and laboratories including CommonSpirit Health and CCLA.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from healthcare providers and laboratories (8 of 30 explicitly address this)
keyArguments: Bias may originate in vendor training data or model design
Healthcare organizations lack visibility into proprietary algorithms
Vendors must provide immediate disclosure of uncovered bias

positions.label: Current Framework Adequate
positions.stance: Existing liability structures with enhanced transparency requirements may be sufficient. Implicit in several technology company comments; no explicit defenders of current allocation.
keyArguments: Manufacturer transparency about training data can enable informed deployment decisions
Local validation by deployers catches context-specific issues

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether bias metrics should focus on parity-based measures ensuring equal performance across demographic groups, or whether metrics should vary by use case and clinical context.

positions.label: Parity-Based Metrics
positions.stance: Focus on equal performance across demographic groups with no statistically significant differences in sensitivity/specificity. Supported by Waymark and MHK.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate explicit support (5-6 commenters)
keyArguments: No statistically significant differences in sensitivity/specificity across groups
Parity metrics provide clear, measurable standards

positions.label: Context-Specific Metrics
positions.stance: Metrics should vary by use case and clinical context, incorporating social determinants of health. Supported particularly by clinical organizations including ADA, New Jersey Innovation Institute, and ECRI.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support (6-7 commenters)
keyArguments: Different clinical applications require different fairness definitions
Social determinants of health and contextual factors must be incorporated

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability exposure for bias originating in vendor products, lack of transparency about training data and model limitations, and resource constraints for conducting local bias validation. Providers emphasize the operational reality that they cannot fully evaluate proprietary algorithms and need vendor accountability.
specificPoints: CommonSpirit Health argues that liability for harm or bias caused by AI tool should be shared with vendor
WellSpan Health proposes that AI Add-on Payments should reward bias mitigation
Providers call for shared liability frameworks with vendors, mandatory vendor disclosure of training populations, and payment incentives for bias mitigation efforts

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Need for clear, consistent standards across jurisdictions, balancing innovation speed with thorough validation, and technical challenges of bias detection in complex models. Several companies highlight that using established foundation models may reduce bias compared to proprietary training, while acknowledging residual concerns.
specificPoints: MHK notes that using industry-standard foundation models rather than training proprietary AI models independently can reduce introduction of training data bias
Interstella proposes an AI Audit Kit any hospital can use
Companies advocate for federal certification frameworks, standardized bias testing toolkits, and research funding for bias detection techniques

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member readiness to evaluate and implement AI responsibly, protecting clinical autonomy while leveraging AI benefits, and ensuring AI tools work across diverse practice settings. Professional societies emphasize the connection between explainability and bias detection—clinicians need to understand AI reasoning to identify problematic patterns.
specificPoints: AORN emphasizes that explainability is crucial for bias detection
ADA references their Technical Report No. 1109 defining consistent performance and bias metrics for pre and post deployment evaluations and reporting
Societies support research prioritization for bias detection methods, standards development through professional bodies, and pre-deployment validation requirements including bias analysis

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Historical inequities being perpetuated at scale, lack of patient voice in AI development, and invisible harm to vulnerable populations. Advocacy groups uniquely emphasize engaging individuals with lived experience throughout model development, not just in testing.
specificPoints: National MS Society states that developers must engage individuals with lived experience, especially from underrepresented communities, throughout model development
Groups advocate for advisory panels of individuals with lived experience, routine audits with accountability measures, and representative data reflecting diverse patient populations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Scientific rigor in bias evaluation methodologies, gaps in current validation datasets, and need for ongoing research into detection techniques. Researchers highlight that underrepresentation in training data may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower disease prevalence—a critical distinction for interpreting AI outputs.
specificPoints: NeuroFlow observes that research should actively correct for gaps in historical training data that may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower prevalence
Researchers advocate for continuous monitoring architectures, research actively correcting for historical data gaps, and stratification by clinically relevant patient factors

noteworthyInsights.insight: NeuroFlow makes the critical observation that research should actively correct for gaps in historical training data that may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower prevalence. This insight suggests that simply training on historical data perpetuates diagnostic disparities, not just treatment disparities.

MHK offers a nuanced technical perspective that using industry-standard foundation models (Anthropic Claude, Google Gemini) rather than training proprietary AI models independently can reduce introduction of training data bias, while acknowledging residual concerns including prompt injection, cross-tenant data access, decision confidence fairness, and RAG context completeness.

AORN uniquely emphasizes that explainability is crucial for bias detection, suggesting that black-box AI systems may be inherently harder to audit for fairness—a connection not explicitly made by other commenters.

ADA references their Technical Report No. 1109, which defines consistent performance and bias metrics for pre and post deployment evaluations and reporting, demonstrating that professional societies are already developing practical frameworks that could inform federal guidance.

Multiple commenters cite the same widely-known algorithm that used healthcare spending as a proxy for illness, systematically disadvantaging Black patients. This case has become a touchstone example illustrating how seemingly neutral technical choices can embed discrimination.

ADLM provides a useful taxonomy distinguishing demographic underrepresentation/skewed clinical data from technical variability/context mismatch as distinct bias-related risks in laboratory medicine, suggesting different mitigation strategies may be needed.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural populations are specifically identified as underrepresented in validation datasets by NeuroFlow. State-level variation in AI requirements creates compliance challenges for multi-state deployers according to Waymark. Safety-net providers face disproportionate resource constraints for bias evaluation as noted by MHA.

Specialty-specific concerns vary significantly: behavioral health providers like Medvidi and Advocate Health express particular concern about cultural and linguistic bias in interpreting emotional expressions; RBMA questions whether mammography AI was validated across breast density categories and racial backgrounds; ADLM raises unique technical variability concerns when AI trained in one context is deployed in another; Massive Bio focuses on diversity in clinical trial matching algorithms.

Smaller organizations lack resources to conduct thorough bias evaluations, creating need for standardized toolkits as proposed by Interstella and MHA. Large health systems like Advocate Health are developing internal frameworks such as FAIR-AI while smaller providers depend on vendor assurances. Technology companies with healthcare-native expertise like Penguin Ai and MHK express more confidence in bias mitigation approaches than general-purpose AI vendors.

Several unintended consequences are identified: using healthcare spending as a proxy for clinical need systematically disadvantages populations who historically received less care; one-time bias assessments may miss drift as AI systems encounter new populations or contexts over time; placing liability solely on deployers may reduce vendor incentive to address bias proactively; AI recommendations that appear clinically sound may be culturally inappropriate or unactionable for specific patient populations.

Several commenters distinguish between pre-deployment and post-deployment bias concerns, suggesting different evaluation approaches are needed at each stage. There is recognition that bias can emerge or worsen over time as patient populations shift or AI systems are applied to new contexts.

keyQuotations.quote: When AI equates spending with need, it quietly carries inequities forward.

An algorithm widely used to prioritize patients for care management systematically underestimated the health needs of Black patients—used healthcare spending as a proxy for illness, and historically less money was spent on Black patients.
It is important to assess whether the use of AI introduces specific risks and harms, such as algorithms that could amplify errors and preexisting biases in the source data.
Bias in AI systems can go unnoticed until harm is widespread.
Healthcare data remains 'fragmented, biased, and often a mess.'
Generic foundation models without behavioral health fine-tuning often miss sarcasm, contextual history, or cultural expressions of distress, producing bland or inaccurate outputs.
Most AI systems trained predominantly on English language data and interactions from majority populations... models lack cultural, linguistic, and contextual grounding.
Current validation datasets often underrepresent rural populations, Veterans, and communities of color.
Bias may originate in vendor training data or model design, yet current framework places disproportionate responsibility on clinicians and laboratories.
Liability for harm or bias caused by AI tool should be shared with vendor.
Explainability is crucial for bias detection.
Research should actively correct for gaps in historical training data that may reflect underdiagnosis rather than lower prevalence.
AI systems should demonstrate no statistically significant differences in sensitivity/specificity across demographic groups or have documented plan to address disparities.
While implicit bias cannot always be eliminated, companies should have standard processes to identify and mitigate AI tool learning those same biases.
AI in mammography—were models trained on women across all breast density categories, varied risk profiles, and diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds?
In medicine, biases can lead to inaccurate diagnosis and patient harm or death.
Biased algorithms can lead to misdiagnosis, inappropriate treatment plans, or inadequate access for marginalized populations.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder types engage substantively with the technical and policy dimensions of algorithmic bias. Arguments are generally well-supported with specific examples, and there is meaningful engagement with tradeoffs between competing approaches. The widely-cited healthcare spending proxy algorithm serves as a shared reference point that grounds abstract discussions in concrete harm.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific examples of algorithmic bias, reference existing standards and frameworks (such as ADA Technical Report No. 1109), and draw on documented cases of AI systems producing disparate outcomes. However, quantitative evidence on the effectiveness of various mitigation approaches remains limited, with most recommendations based on principles rather than empirical validation.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Individual patients and community members are notably underrepresented in the comments, with perspectives primarily coming from organizations. Rural healthcare perspectives are mentioned as underrepresented in AI validation but are also underrepresented among commenters. Small healthcare practices and safety-net providers are referenced but few submitted direct comments.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves technical questions about bias metrics and detection methods, legal questions about liability allocation, policy questions about mandatory versus voluntary frameworks, and ethical questions about fairness definitions. Commenters generally acknowledge this complexity and avoid oversimplified solutions.

10.2.themeDescription: Equity Monitoring and Disparate Impact Assessment

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that equity cannot be ensured through AI design alone and must be measured continuously post-deployment, with virtually all 19 commenters supporting some form of ongoing equity monitoring. The central tension lies not in whether to monitor, but in how—specifically around which metrics, subgroups, and enforcement mechanisms should be required. Commenters spanning healthcare providers, technology companies, trade associations, and advocacy groups converge on the need for stratified performance monitoring across demographic groups, with particular emphasis on detecting disparities that emerge only after real-world deployment.

consensusPoints.text: Equity requires continuous monitoring, not one-time assessment. Nearly all commenters explicitly or implicitly endorse the principle that equity cannot be ensured through design alone and must be measured continuously after deployment. Dr. Bhagavathula notes that equity cannot be ensured through design alone and must be measured continuously, as many inequities emerge only after implementation through uneven adoption, differential trust, resource constraints, and context-specific failures. Ellipsis Health calls for ongoing bias audits rather than one-time assessments and notes that equitable AI requires evaluation beyond controlled datasets. Massive Bio advocates for continual auditing for fairness during post-deployment monitoring.

exceptions.text: No commenter explicitly opposes continuous monitoring, though some business commenters such as SANCIAN LLC emphasize pre-deployment equity checks, suggesting a both/and rather than either/or approach.

consensusPoints.text: Performance must be stratified by demographic subgroups. A strong majority specifically call for stratified or disaggregated performance monitoring across demographic categories including race, ethnicity, age, language, socioeconomic status, and geography. Ellipsis Health calls for performance monitoring across race, ethnicity, age, language, and socioeconomic status. Waymark states that MCOs using AI systems should implement continuous monitoring across demographic subgroups. BlueHalo notes that AI evaluation must account for variation across patient populations, care settings, and operational contexts, requiring access to diverse, representative data and ability to stratify performance by relevant factors.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority (at least 12 of 19 commenters)

exceptions.text: Some commenters focus on specific subgroups relevant to their domain rather than comprehensive demographic stratification. Vikki Small emphasizes language groups while NeuroFlow emphasizes behavioral health populations.

consensusPoints.text: Aggregate metrics can mask disparities. Multiple commenters explicitly warn that relying on aggregate performance metrics risks obscuring inequities affecting specific populations. BlueHalo warns that AI tools may perform well for certain populations or settings while underperforming for others, and evaluation approaches relying on aggregate metrics risk obscuring disparities and masking context-specific failures. Dr. Bhagavathula describes how a model's performance gradually degraded due to changes in coding practices within the EHR with no routine monitoring in place, and disparities widened quietly over a year.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether equity checks should primarily occur before deployment or through continuous post-deployment monitoring.

positions.label: Pre-deployment focus
positions.stance: Equity checks should be embedded early, before deployment. SANCIAN LLC recommends equity impact assessment before deployment with equity checks embedded early. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi calls for AI-EQUITYClear assessment as equity impact evaluation before deployment.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters emphasize this approach
keyArguments: Prevents harm before it occurs
Establishes baseline expectations
More cost-effective than remediation

positions.label: Post-deployment emphasis
positions.stance: Real disparities emerge only after implementation. Dr. Bhagavathula notes many inequities emerge only after implementation. Ellipsis Health calls for stress-testing AI performance in populations with low digital literacy or limited access.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (15+)
keyArguments: Pre-deployment testing cannot capture real-world complexity
Disparities emerge from uneven adoption, differential trust, and context-specific failures
Continuous monitoring catches drift

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether equity monitoring should be mandated through regulation, incentivized through payment models, or left as process-based expectations.

positions.label: Mandatory requirements
positions.stance: Equity monitoring should be required through regulation or certification. Massive Bio recommends incorporating bias testing into Medicare Conditions of Participation or certification criteria. ADLM calls to mandate ongoing performance monitoring.
