Interstella requests guidance or requirements for federal healthcare programs covering algorithmic bias assessments.
positions.supportLevel: At least 8 commenters explicitly call for mandatory approaches
keyArguments: Voluntary approaches insufficient
Ties accountability to compliance
Creates level playing field

positions.label: Incentive-based approaches
positions.stance: Tie equity monitoring to payment models and value-based care. ICF recommends gating shared savings/benchmarks on monitored performance and equity checks and states equity monitoring should be tied to payment incentives in value-based models.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters propose this approach
keyArguments: Aligns financial incentives with equity goals
Leverages existing payment infrastructure
More flexible than mandates

positions.label: Process-based expectations
positions.stance: Companies should have monitoring processes without strict mandates. AAFP recommends that when applicable, companies should have processes for monitoring differential outcomes—notably softer language than mandatory requirements.
positions.supportLevel: At least 1 major trade association
keyArguments: Allows flexibility for different contexts
Avoids one-size-fits-all burden

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether monitoring requirements should include specific quantitative triggers or remain flexible standards.

positions.label: Quantitative triggers
positions.stance: Specific thresholds should trigger corrective action. Vikki Small proposes that if fallback or escalation rates exceed 20% for any language group for two consecutive months, require corrective action plans and pause high-risk pathways until issues are resolved.
positions.supportLevel: 1 commenter provides explicit thresholds
keyArguments: Creates clear accountability
Removes ambiguity
Enables enforcement

positions.label: Flexible standards
positions.stance: Monitoring requirements without rigid numerical thresholds. Most commenters call for monitoring without specifying exact trigger points.
positions.supportLevel: Most commenters (17+) do not specify numerical thresholds
keyArguments: Context-dependent
Allows for clinical judgment
Avoids gaming

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Need for standardized approaches to validation and bias detection; regulatory clarity to enable scaling; recognition that aggregate metrics mask disparities
specificPoints: Emphasize operational feasibility and the need for real-time surveillance infrastructure
Several offer proprietary frameworks such as SANCIAN's AI-EQUITYClear™ and Interstella's DRaaS
Medvidi proposes continuous monitoring dashboards
Ellipsis Health calls for stress-testing in underserved populations
Interstella notes leading centers deploying dozens of models need standardized approaches
Waymark serves 175,000+ high-risk Medicaid members and calls for MCOs to implement continuous demographic monitoring
NeuroFlow notes behavioral health prevalence, access patterns, and treatment engagement vary significantly across demographic and geographic groups

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member capacity to implement monitoring; need for testing infrastructure; liability clarity
specificPoints: Represent diverse membership with varying resources
Emphasize that smaller, rural, and safety-net providers face disproportionate burden
AUA calls for HHS-supported testing infrastructure for comparing performance, subgroup equity, calibration, and drift across settings
MHA recommends multi-site pragmatic trials with subgroup analyses
AAFP represents 128,300 family physicians, many in small, independent, rural, and safety-net settings with limited resources

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Scientific rigor of monitoring approaches; evidence base for equity assessment; real-world evidence generation
specificPoints: Bring methodological expertise in causal inference, population health, and evaluation science
Emphasize that disparities can emerge silently without proper surveillance
Dr. Bhagavathula calls for mandatory stratification by key subgroups
ECRI proposes independent programs evaluating equity of performance
Joshi recommends structured reporting of algorithmic harms with mechanisms to capture disparity amplification
Dr. Bhagavathula provides concrete example of model degradation going undetected for a year

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation at point of care; detecting errors affecting specific patient groups; workflow integration
specificPoints: Frontline users who see AI impacts on patients daily
Understand clinical context that aggregate data misses
Emilie Maxie recommends monitoring whether certain groups experience more errors or missed care
Vikki Small calls for disaggregating monitoring by language group with specific thresholds
Vikki Small works at a safety-net academic medical center serving patients with limited English proficiency, low health literacy, and uneven digital access

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unintended consequences affecting vulnerable populations; need for patient community involvement in algorithm examination
specificPoints: Centers patient voice and lived experience
Emphasizes cross-functional collaboration
National MS Society calls for routine audits with academia and patient community involvement
Recommends integrating population health principles into AI development
Represents approximately 1 million Americans living with MS who could benefit from AI-enhanced diagnosis but could also be harmed by biased algorithms

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Health equity research gaps; mental health disparities; implementation in safety-net settings
specificPoints: Bring personal experience implementing AI in resource-constrained environments
Dr. Russell proposes health equity research ensuring AI tools reduce rather than exacerbate disparities
Vikki Small provides specific quantitative thresholds for corrective action

noteworthyInsights.insight: Specific quantitative threshold proposal: Vikki Small, a safety-net health system leader, provides the only concrete numerical threshold in the comments, proposing that if fallback or escalation rates exceed 20% for any language group for two consecutive months, corrective action plans should be required and high-risk pathways paused until issues are resolved. This specificity stands out against more general calls for monitoring.

Payment model integration opportunity: ICF uniquely connects equity monitoring to existing CMS payment infrastructure, proposing to gate shared savings/benchmarks on monitored performance and equity checks in specific programs like LEAD and Kidney Care Choices. This leverages existing accountability mechanisms rather than creating new ones.

Real-world degradation timeline: Dr. Bhagavathula provides rare concrete evidence of monitoring failure, describing how disparities widened quietly over a year due to EHR coding practice changes with no routine monitoring in place. This specific timeline illustrates the urgency of continuous monitoring.

Language as equity indicator: Vikki Small uniquely emphasizes language-based monitoring, proposing that completion, fallback, and escalation rates disaggregated by language group serve as early warning indicators of uneven performance. This operational metric could be more immediately actionable than demographic stratification.

Patient community involvement: The National MS Society uniquely calls for HHS to work cross-functionally with academia and patient community to routinely examine algorithms and data, centering patient voice in monitoring rather than treating it as purely technical exercise.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic and Setting Variations: Safety-net and resource-limited settings face disproportionate implementation challenges. Multi-state Medicaid managed care deployments such as Waymark highlight need for consistent approaches across jurisdictions. Rural care explicitly mentioned as requiring equity-specific monitoring.

Domain-Specific Monitoring Needs: Behavioral health faces unique challenges due to variable prevalence and access patterns across demographics. Oncology clinical trial matching requires fairness auditing. CLIA-regulated laboratory settings need specific monitoring guidance. Maternal health explicitly identified as requiring equity-specific monitoring.

Stakeholder Role Correlations: Technology companies emphasize operational feasibility and infrastructure needs. Trade associations balance member diversity and resource constraints. Academics focus on methodological rigor and evidence generation. Frontline providers emphasize practical detection of patient-level harms. Advocacy groups center patient voice and community involvement.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Monitoring requirements without infrastructure support could widen gap between well-resourced and safety-net institutions. Aggregate compliance metrics could create false assurance while subgroup disparities persist. One-time assessments may satisfy regulatory requirements while missing emergent disparities.

Convergence Across Unlikely Allies: Business interests seeking regulatory clarity and advocacy groups seeking patient protection align on need for mandatory monitoring. Academic researchers and technology companies agree on limitations of aggregate metrics. Large health systems and small practices both express concern about implementation capacity, though from different resource positions.

keyQuotations.quote: Equity cannot be ensured through design alone—must be measured continuously... Many inequities emerge only after implementation through uneven adoption, differential trust, resource constraints, and context-specific failures.

AI tools may perform well for certain populations or settings while underperforming for others—evaluation approaches relying on aggregate metrics risk obscuring disparities and masking context-specific failures.
A model's performance gradually degraded due to changes in coding practices within the EHR. No routine monitoring was in place. Disparities widened quietly over a year.
If fallback or escalation rates exceed 20% for any language group for two consecutive months, require corrective action plans and pause high-risk pathways until issues are resolved.
Leading centers deploying dozens of models need standardized approaches for validation, bias detection, and real-time surveillance.
Behavioral health prevalence, access patterns, and treatment engagement vary significantly across demographic and geographic groups.
HHS should work cross-functionally with academia and patient community to routinely examine algorithms and data.
Equity monitoring should be tied to payment incentives in value-based models.

Performance monitoring across race, ethnicity, age, language, and socioeconomic status... Stress-testing AI performance in populations with low digital literacy or limited access... Ongoing bias audits rather than one-time assessments.
After deployment, monitor whether certain groups experience more errors or missed care.
Unintended consequences could affect certain populations without ongoing monitoring.
Ongoing monitoring must be disaggregated by language group to detect uneven performance.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate substantive engagement with specific policy mechanisms, provide concrete examples and evidence, and offer constructive recommendations. The discourse moves beyond general principles to operational specifics, with multiple commenters citing real-world implementation experience.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters provide concrete evidence including specific case studies of monitoring failures, quantitative threshold proposals, and references to existing frameworks. However, much of the evidence is anecdotal or based on organizational experience rather than peer-reviewed research.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups (only 1 of 19 commenters), rural healthcare providers, and smaller technology vendors. The perspective of patients themselves is largely absent, represented only through advocacy organizations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves multiple intersecting dimensions including timing of assessment, enforcement mechanisms, threshold specificity, and domain-specific considerations. Stakeholders bring diverse operational contexts that complicate one-size-fits-all solutions.

10.3.themeDescription: Access Disparities and Digital Divide in AI Benefits. This sub-theme addresses concerns about unequal access to AI benefits across different populations and settings. || It includes concerns about AI benefits concentrating in well-resourced settings, proposals for ensuring underserved populations benefit from AI, debates about digital literacy and technology access barriers, and discussions of how to prevent AI from widening existing disparities. This excludes algorithmic bias and focuses on access to AI benefits. The key distinction is focus on who can access AI rather than whether AI treats people fairly

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that AI adoption patterns risk widening rather than narrowing healthcare disparities, with particular concern for rural, safety-net, and resource-constrained settings being left behind. The central tension lies between AI's acknowledged potential to democratize access to advanced care and the structural barriers—financial, infrastructural, and digital literacy-related—that may concentrate benefits among well-resourced institutions and populations. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend targeted federal support, inclusive design requirements, and reimbursement reforms to ensure equitable AI access, though they differ on whether market-based solutions or direct government intervention should lead.

consensusPoints.text: AI adoption will concentrate in well-resourced settings without intervention. Nearly all commenters express concern that without targeted policy action, AI benefits will flow disproportionately to large, urban, well-funded health systems while smaller, rural, and safety-net providers fall further behind. Advocate Health notes that without targeted reimbursement policies or federal financial support, under-resourced organizations cannot adopt emerging AI technologies and disparities in access to advanced clinical tools may widen. HIMSS EHR Association observes that a digital divide already exists specific to health IT between larger, more resource-rich healthcare systems and providers working in smaller ambulatory practices. Massive Bio warns that large health systems with resources charge ahead, while smaller providers lag behind, creating an adoption gap.

exceptions.text: No commenters argued that market forces alone would ensure equitable distribution of AI benefits.

consensusPoints.text: AI has genuine potential to expand access to underserved populations if properly designed and deployed. Commenters acknowledge AI's potential to bridge geographic, linguistic, and resource gaps. A Stanford pre-med student notes that AI holds immense potential for underserved and rural populations where healthcare resources are strained. Kooth highlights that AI has extraordinary potential to expand mental health care access given current shortage where as many as 169 million people live in federally designated Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas. MediView XR observes that physical AI can expand access to subspecialty expertise across rural, underserved, and resource-constrained settings through telemedicine and remote expert guidance.

Multiple populations face compounding access barriers including limited English proficiency, low digital literacy, disability, transportation constraints, and insurance instability. Ellipsis Health notes that rural populations, older adults, individuals with mobility limitations, and those with behavioral health needs often face disproportionate access challenges. Amir Abrams observes that people who receive care across multiple systems due to access patterns, insurance churn, transportation constraints, or regional provider availability are disproportionately harmed. Dr. Bethany Russell identifies that patients face access barriers including long wait times, geographic limitations, cost constraints, and language barriers.

areasOfDebate.topic: Federal Role in Ensuring Equitable Access
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether government should actively fund, subsidize, and mandate equitable AI deployment versus creating conditions for market solutions through pricing transparency and open-source support.

positions.label: Direct Intervention
positions.stance: Government should actively fund, subsidize, and mandate equitable AI deployment. This position is supported particularly by safety-net providers, advocacy groups, and rural health experts including VieCure, MHA, and RUPRI Health Panel.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 70% of commenters (17 of 25)
keyArguments: Market forces alone will not correct structural inequities
Targeted grants and technical assistance are essential for resource-constrained settings
Federal grants should prioritize tools developed with and validated in diverse populations

positions.label: Market-Enabling Policies
positions.stance: Government should create conditions for market solutions through pricing transparency and open-source support. This position is held primarily by technology companies including Interstella and WellSpan Health.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 20% of commenters (5 of 25)
keyArguments: Pricing transparency and reference pricing can prevent cost barriers
Open-source and public-utility AI reduces dependence on proprietary solutions
Deemed status models can democratize access without direct subsidies

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI tools must be built from the ground up with underserved populations in mind versus adapting existing AI tools for underserved settings through deployment support.

positions.label: Equity-First Design
positions.stance: AI tools must be built from the ground up with underserved populations in mind. This position is supported especially by safety-net providers and patient advocates including Vikki Small, AAFP, and NJ Innovation Institute.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (14 of 25 commenters)
keyArguments: Tools validated only in well-resourced settings perform poorly elsewhere
Language access and low-friction interfaces are essential, not optional
Participatory research models should engage communities directly

positions.label: Adaptation Approach
positions.stance: Existing AI tools can be adapted for underserved settings through deployment support. This minority view is held primarily by larger health systems and some vendors including WellSpan Health and Advocate Health.
positions.supportLevel: Minority view (4 of 25 commenters)
keyArguments: Deemed status and certification can extend validated tools to smaller settings
Technical assistance can bridge implementation gaps

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centered on whether payment reform is essential for ensuring AI benefits reach vulnerable populations.

positions.label: Payment Reform Essential
positions.stance: Without viable reimbursement pathways, AI benefits will bypass vulnerable populations. This position is nearly universal among healthcare providers and trade associations including CCLA, AAFP, and Advocate Health.
positions.supportLevel: Nearly universal among healthcare providers and trade associations (12 of 25 commenters)
keyArguments: Absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries
High upfront costs make AI inaccessible to independent, safety-net, or rural physicians

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial burden of AI implementation; risk of being left behind; liability uncertainty. Direct operational experience reveals that implementation costs and governance requirements disproportionately burden smaller systems.
specificPoints: Advocate Health notes that the financial burden of implementing LLM-based solutions is especially prohibitive for small, rural, and resource-constrained organizations
MHA warns that AI adoption may flow to larger, urban, better-resourced healthcare providers
Proposed solutions include deemed status models, CMMI demonstrations with equity adjustments, and targeted grants for rural/safety-net settings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity; reimbursement pathways; ability to scale solutions to underserved markets. Many frame their business models around democratizing access and see market opportunity in serving underserved populations if policy barriers are removed.
specificPoints: Massive Bio's mission is to democratize access to advanced treatments
Ellipsis Health promotes voice-based and low-friction AI modalities to reduce reliance on screens and apps
Proposed solutions include open-source AI development, pricing transparency, voice-based and low-friction modalities, and multilingual support

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member capacity to adopt AI; competitive disadvantages for smaller practices; workforce recruitment implications. They represent aggregate concerns across diverse membership, highlighting how access disparities affect entire sectors.
specificPoints: AAFP warns that limited access could make clinician recruitment even harder at these practices
CCLA notes AI diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries without payment pathways
Proposed solutions include participatory research models, prioritized funding for community/rural/safety-net settings, and viable coding and payment pathways

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence gaps for underserved populations; need for inclusive research design; rural infrastructure limitations. They bring systematic analysis and historical perspective on technology adoption patterns.
specificPoints: RUPRI Health Panel observes that technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates disparities that exist
Satyadhar Joshi recommends stress-testing AI performance in populations with low digital literacy
Proposed solutions include including rural populations and high-comorbidity beneficiaries in datasets and developing AI addressing health disparities

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient-level barriers; rare disease populations; safety-net implementation realities. They bring lived experience and frontline implementation insights often missing from organizational comments.
specificPoints: Steven Zecola advocates for rare disease patients who have near 0% chance of relief under current regulatory procedures
Vikki Small describes how tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance
Proposed solutions include patient-designated data routing, AI grants for rare disease research, language access requirements, and validation in diverse populations

noteworthyInsights.insight: Data fragmentation as equity issue: Software architect Amir Abrams makes the sophisticated point that fragmentation and missingness are not evenly distributed—patients who receive care across multiple systems due to insurance churn or transportation constraints face worse AI performance because their records are incomplete. This reframes data interoperability as an equity imperative.

Rare disease populations as AI opportunity: Patient advocate Steven Zecola highlights that 33 million Americans with rare diseases have near 0% chance of relief under current regulatory procedures, positioning AI as potentially transformative for populations the current system has failed.

Technology as double-edged sword: RUPRI Health Panel offers the counterintuitive insight that AI improving access for well-insured patients could actually harm rural health systems by diverting volume from local providers who cross-subsidize care for uninsured patients.

Governance capacity as barrier: NeuroFlow identifies that variability in institutional review expectations disadvantages smaller health systems and safety-net providers lacking dedicated AI governance infrastructure—suggesting that even well-intentioned oversight requirements can widen disparities.

Mental health shortage scale: Kooth quantifies the access crisis noting that as many as 169 million people live in federally designated Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas, making the case for AI as essential rather than optional for mental health access.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural vs. urban divide is the dominant geographic concern, mentioned by 14 of 25 commenters. Rural commenters emphasize infrastructure limitations, workforce shortages, and financial viability threats. Urban safety-net providers emphasize linguistic diversity and digital literacy barriers. Regional health systems like WellSpan in Pennsylvania/Maryland and MHA in Massachusetts bridge both concerns.

Large integrated systems like Advocate Health and WellSpan acknowledge their relative advantages while advocating for smaller peers. Community and independent practices are consistently identified as most at risk of being left behind. Safety-net academic medical centers face unique challenges serving diverse populations with limited resources. Community oncology is specifically highlighted by VieCure as delivering majority of U.S. cancer care but lacking AI resources.

Frontline implementers like Vikki Small and WellSpan provide most concrete examples of access barriers. Technology developers tend to emphasize AI's potential while acknowledging need for inclusive design. Policy researchers like RUPRI and Satyadhar Joshi offer most systematic analysis of disparity mechanisms. Patient advocates bring urgency and lived experience often missing from organizational comments.

Unintended consequences identified: Governance requirements intended to ensure safety may inadvertently exclude smaller providers lacking compliance infrastructure. AI-enabled access improvements for some populations may financially destabilize providers serving all patients. English-only AI tools may create new barriers even while solving others. Reimbursement gaps may redirect innovation away from populations most in need.

keyQuotations.quote: Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers.

A digital divide already exists specific to health IT between larger, more resource-rich healthcare systems and providers working in smaller ambulatory practices. We believe these smaller practices can measurably benefit from these technologies; supporting their adoption of these tools should be a priority.
Technology can be an equalizer but often first exacerbates disparities that exist.

Absent viable coding and payment pathways, innovative AI-enabled diagnostics may migrate away from Medicare beneficiaries, exacerbating disparities in access and limiting innovation within federally funded programs.
The result is a democratization of access to high-quality AI, ensuring benefits are not confined to well-resourced academic medical centers.
If disclosures and instructions are only available in English, patients with limited English proficiency are shut out of safe use.

The 33 million people with one or more of the 7000 rare diseases now have a near 0% chance of relief under current regulatory procedures, and their plight must be considered while the contours of biology are examined.
Patient-designated routing improves equity by making longitudinal completeness a default expectation across sites rather than a privilege of being 'in the right health system.'

Technology that facilitates ease of access for well-insured populations may divert encounters away from local providers who serve all patients regardless of source of payment. That diversion could threaten financially viability of local providers.
If AI solutions are not designed with inclusivity in mind, marginalized populations may face disparities in access to advanced healthcare technologies.
AI-first approach drives superior patient engagement through 24/7 accessibility, multilingual support, and hyper-personalized outreach extending care beyond traditional clinic constraints.
Physical AI can reduce unnecessary patient transfers by bringing expertise to underserved locations.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of structural barriers and provide concrete examples from operational experience. Arguments are well-supported with specific evidence and acknowledge complexity of the issues.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific statistics, operational experience, and concrete examples. Some quantitative data provided, such as 169 million people in Mental Health Professional Shortage Areas and 33 million rare disease patients. However, some claims rely on general assertions about market dynamics.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct representation from patients themselves, particularly those in underserved communities. Most comments come from organizations and providers rather than the populations most affected by access disparities. Rural community voices are represented primarily through research organizations rather than direct community input.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - commenters identify multiple interacting factors including financial barriers, infrastructure limitations, governance capacity, reimbursement structures, and design choices that compound to create access disparities. Several commenters identify counterintuitive dynamics such as how improving access for some may harm others.

11.1.themeDescription: Implementation Costs and Return on Investment Uncertainty

sections.executiveSummary: There is near-universal consensus among commenters that high upfront implementation costs and uncertain ROI represent critical barriers to AI adoption in healthcare, with particular concern for small, rural, and safety-net providers. The central tension lies between the documented potential for AI to generate significant long-term value and the structural misalignment where implementation costs fall on providers while savings often accrue to payers or the broader system. Commenters strongly advocate for federal research to standardize total cost of ownership measurement, financial support mechanisms for under-resourced providers, and payment model reforms that align incentives across stakeholders.

consensusPoints.text: High upfront costs are a primary barrier to AI adoption. Nearly all commenters explicitly identify significant upfront investment requirements as a major obstacle to AI implementation. This concern spans all stakeholder types—from large health systems to small practices, from EHR vendors to specialty medical societies. Integrating validated AI tools requires significant upfront and ongoing investments including software, licensing fees, IT infrastructure, cybersecurity protections, workflow redesign, staff training, and maintenance.

exceptions.text: Epic Systems notes that many applications of generative AI in healthcare do present sufficient ROI, though they acknowledge organizations struggle to predict and budget for variable costs.

consensusPoints.text: ROI is difficult to quantify and often uncertain. A strong majority of commenters emphasize that demonstrating clear financial returns from AI investments remains challenging, creating a fundamental barrier to securing organizational buy-in. The lack of hard ROI for most AI tools in clinical care today, unclear ROI projections making business cases difficult to build, and practices being deterred without clear evidence of return on investment are commonly cited concerns.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (22 of 34)

exceptions.text: SanLuca AI reports that imaging/screening triage and radiology support shows strong ROI when it reduces second reads or speeds throughput, suggesting ROI clarity varies significantly by use case.

consensusPoints.text: Cost-effectiveness studies systematically underestimate true implementation costs. Most commenters addressing research methodology agree that existing economic evaluations fail to capture the full financial burden of AI adoption. Fewer than half of economic evaluation studies explicitly report costing methods, and only 28% include implementation costs. Studies frequently overlook full lifecycle expenses including integration, cybersecurity, monitoring, revalidation, and governance, and rarely address model updates over time or how financial impacts are distributed across stakeholders.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing research methodology (8 of 34)

consensusPoints.text: Small, rural, and safety-net providers face disproportionate barriers. Nearly all commenters discussing equity agree that resource-constrained organizations are least able to absorb implementation costs, risking a widening digital divide. High upfront and ongoing costs may make AI tools inaccessible to independent, safety-net, or rural physicians, which could negatively impact patient care. For many physician practices, particularly small, independent, and solo practices, costs are substantial and may be prohibitive.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters discussing equity (12 of 34)

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether AI can demonstrate clear financial value, with vendors generally more optimistic than providers.

positions.label: ROI Is Achievable
positions.stance: AI can demonstrate clear financial value in specific applications. Epic Systems, SanLuca AI, and Scientix.ai argue that specific use cases like imaging triage show measurable throughput gains, downstream benefits create real value even if indirect, and systematic reviews show 98% of economic evaluations conclude AI is cost-effective.
positions.supportLevel: A minority of commenters (4-5), primarily AI vendors and one EHR company
keyArguments: Specific use cases like imaging triage show measurable throughput gains
Downstream benefits (reduced burnout, retention) create real value even if indirect
Systematic reviews show 98% of economic evaluations conclude AI is cost-effective

positions.label: ROI Remains Unproven
positions.stance: Current evidence is insufficient to justify investment. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association, National Council for Mental Wellbeing, and Massive Bio represent the majority view that benefits are often soft rather than hard financial returns, early adopters found limited financial ROI initially, and literature is fragmented and inconsistent.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of commenters (20+), including most provider organizations and trade associations
keyArguments: Benefits are often 'soft' (clinician satisfaction) rather than hard financial returns
Early adopters found 'limited financial ROI' initially
Literature is fragmented and inconsistent

areasOfDebate.topic: Where the Burden of Implementation Costs Should Fall
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether costs should be redistributed through shared savings models or addressed through federal financial support.

positions.label: Shared Savings Models
positions.stance: Benefits should be redistributed to those bearing costs. TapestryHealth, Cooperative Exchange, and NeuroFlow argue that the current structure creates a split incentive where facilities invest but payers capture savings, SNFs operate on fixed per-diem creating a Capital vs. Operational mismatch, and fee-for-service models dampen adoption incentive.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily providers and clearinghouses
keyArguments: Current structure creates 'split incentive' where facilities invest but payers capture savings
SNFs operate on fixed per-diem creating 'Capital vs. Operational' mismatch
Fee-for-service models dampen adoption incentive

positions.label: Federal Financial Support
positions.stance: Government should subsidize adoption. American Academy of Dermatology Association, Heart Failure Society of America, and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association argue that grants would enable small practices to participate, time-limited implementation support reduces friction, and funding for soft ROI tools could accelerate adoption.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters, including trade associations and specialty societies
keyArguments: Grants would enable small practices to participate
Time-limited implementation support reduces friction
Funding for 'soft ROI' tools could accelerate adoption

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Account for Hidden Costs
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether to emphasize physician opportunity costs or focus on standardizing total cost of ownership measurement.

positions.label: Include Opportunity Costs
positions.stance: True costs include clinician time at revenue-generating rates. HealthScoreAI and American Society of Retina Specialists argue that physician time for prompting, supervising, validating, and correcting AI outputs should be valued at $15-30 per minute based on revenue-generating capacity, and when opportunity costs are included, many AI tools fail to demonstrate economic value.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters, primarily those with economic analysis expertise
keyArguments: Physician time for prompting, supervising, validating, and correcting AI outputs should be valued at $15-30 per minute based on revenue-generating capacity
When opportunity costs included, many AI tools fail to demonstrate economic value

positions.label: Total Cost of Ownership
positions.stance: Standardize lifecycle cost measurement. American Urological Association, Scientix.ai, and WellSpan Health argue that costs must include integration, security, monitoring, clinician oversight, vendor fees, and governance, and that current application-first paradigms create compounding integration burden.
positions.supportLevel: 6-8 commenters, including trade associations and researchers
keyArguments: Must include integration, security, monitoring, clinician oversight, vendor fees, and governance
Current 'application-first' paradigms create compounding integration burden

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member practices cannot justify investment without clear ROI; per-use/subscription pricing creates ongoing financial burden; small and independent practices face prohibitive barriers. RBMA emphasizes that AI costs may increase practice expenses through licensing, integration, validation, cybersecurity, and compliance requirements not well reflected in current reimbursement formulas.
specificPoints: Federal grants needed for small practices
Standardized ROI studies required
Tariff exemptions for AI tools proposed
Dedicated reimbursement pathways needed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Upfront capital requirements without clear returns; ongoing costs for monitoring, auditing, and validation unfunded; savings accrue to payers rather than facilities. UPenn NeuroVERT reveals the cyclical barrier—demonstrating downstream savings requires implementation before robust data can be generated.
specificPoints: Payment models should share savings with facilities
Technology adoption incentives should be embedded into CMMI models
Infrastructure-style payments for AI deployment needed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Healthcare organizations' thin margins limit technology investment; variable compute/storage costs difficult to predict; regulatory compliance requirements increase development costs passed to customers. SanLuca AI acknowledges that benefits often appear first as time saved, throughput, and clinician experience, while financial ROI can lag or shift across stakeholders.
specificPoints: Vendor-agnostic ROI repository needed
Independent research to validate vendor claims
Address MLR limitations for payer technology investments

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payment models don't account for trust, education, and governance work; organizations struggle to justify investment without reimbursement pathways; fully burdened costs systematically underestimated. HealthScoreAI emphasizes that realistic assessment must account for fully burdened cost of AI use, not just direct (often subsidized) cost of technology.
specificPoints: Align reimbursement with real-world implementation costs
Recognize operational effort in incentive design
Research economic impact of burden reduction

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Literature on AI cost impact is fragmented and inconsistent; complex approval processes are lengthy and costly; compliance requirements drive up pricing. Satyadhar Joshi highlights that current payment models may not adequately capture long-term savings from preventive AI applications.
specificPoints: Research priorities should include total cost of care impact over time
Attribution models and labor cost comparisons needed
Funding for testing and certification processes

noteworthyInsights.insight: Hidden opportunity costs dwarf visible expenses. HealthScoreAI calculates that physician time for AI oversight should be valued at $15-30 per minute based on revenue-generating capacity, meaning when opportunity costs are included, many AI tools fail to demonstrate economic value. This reframes the ROI calculation significantly.

The true ROI is often invisible to CFOs. Epic Systems observes that AI scribe technology's real value isn't in direct cost savings but in happier clinicians less likely to burn out or leave, reducing recruitment and onboarding costs—benefits that don't appear on traditional ROI spreadsheets.

98% cost-effectiveness finding masks methodological gaps. Scientix.ai cites systematic reviews showing 98% of economic evaluations conclude AI is cost-effective, yet notes fewer than half explicitly report costing methods, and only 28% include implementation costs—suggesting the optimistic findings may be systematically biased.

Diabetic retinopathy screening serves as a cautionary tale. American Society of Retina Specialists notes that despite being the first FDA-cleared AI device, diabetic retinopathy screening has seen limited uptake because CMS is reluctant to reimburse equipment start-up costs—demonstrating how reimbursement gaps can stall even clinically validated tools.

Infrastructure operators bear hidden costs. Cooperative Exchange highlights that clearinghouses often absorb ongoing costs for integration, security controls, monitoring, audit support, and regulatory compliance while benefits (reduced friction) are often realized by other parties in the ecosystem—a stakeholder perspective rarely considered in AI adoption discussions.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural vs. urban divide: Multiple commenters including AAFP, Advocate Health, and AASM emphasize that rural and small practices face disproportionate barriers, suggesting geographic disparities in AI adoption are likely to widen without intervention.

Regional health system perspective: Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association provides state-level insight that lack of hard ROI for most AI tools in clinical care today is driving cautious adoption even among well-resourced systems.

FQHCs and safety-net providers face unique challenges: Community Care Cooperative emphasizes that FQHCs serve populations at highest risk of being harmed by AI tools trained on non-representative data while facing the greatest cost barriers.
SNFs and post-acute care face sector-specific mismatches: TapestryHealth identifies a Capital vs. Operational mismatch where fixed per-diem payments prevent infrastructure investment.
Community oncology faces particular strain: VieCure notes that thin-margin practices in community oncology face particular strain from high upfront and ongoing costs.

Vendors vs. providers diverge on ROI: AI vendors tend to be more optimistic about ROI potential, while providers and trade associations express greater skepticism—suggesting potential misalignment in market expectations.

Regulatory compliance driving up costs: UPenn NeuroVERT warns that when compliance requirements are extensive, development and validation costs increase and are passed along as higher pricing—suggesting well-intentioned regulations may inadvertently worsen cost barriers.

Application-first paradigm creating technical debt: Scientix.ai identifies that current approaches create compounding integration burden where complexity and cost of connecting disparate systems consume capital and time intended for innovation.

keyQuotations.quote: Less 'pajama time' for providers, however, does not by itself offset the cost of the technology. The true ROI of AI scribes appears downstream: happier clinicians are less likely to burn out or leave, reducing recruitment and onboarding costs.
Without clear, predictable financial signals, even highly effective tools may struggle to gain traction among financially constrained or operationally stretched providers.

In some cases, AI vendors may capture a disproportionate share of the technical fee associated with a medical procedure, creating substantial financial burdens for facilities and limiting broader adoption.
AI requires upfront Capital Expense (sensors), but savings (reduced hospitalizations) accrue to Payer (Medicare), not Facility.
'Split incentive' prevents CIOs from securing funding for AI infrastructure.

In fee-for-service models, cost reductions often accrue to payers rather than providers—dampens adoption incentive for clinical organizations bearing implementation cost without capturing financial benefit.

Fully burdened cost includes time physicians spend prompting, supervising, validating, and correcting AI outputs—physician time should be valued based on revenue-generating capacity rather than salary alone... In many specialties translates to effective cost of approximately $15-30 per minute.

Fewer than half of economic evaluation studies explicitly report costing methods, and only 28% include implementation costs—full financial burden of AI adoption is systematically underestimated in literature.
Demonstrating downstream savings, improved throughput, or reduced complication rates often requires implementation before robust data can be generated—creates cyclical barrier.

Financial burden of implementing LLM-based solutions is substantial including vendor licensing fees, internal development costs, technical infrastructure, ongoing monitoring and governance... Especially prohibitive for small, rural, and resource-constrained organizations.
High upfront and ongoing costs may make AI tools inaccessible to independent, safety-net, or rural physicians, which could negatively impact patient care.
Additional federally supported economic studies would help providers model total cost of ownership, reimbursement pathways, and long-term ROI.
The Department could help document ROI data in a way providers can rely and act on.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of implementation economics, with detailed analysis of cost structures, stakeholder incentive misalignments, and methodological limitations in existing research. Multiple stakeholder types provide complementary perspectives that enrich the overall discourse.

evidenceBase.explanation: While commenters cite systematic reviews and specific examples, much of the evidence is anecdotal or based on organizational experience rather than rigorous empirical studies. The acknowledged gap in cost-effectiveness research methodology (only 28% including implementation costs) reflects broader evidence limitations in this area.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from payers who capture many of the savings discussed; minimal representation from patients or patient advocacy groups; few comments from rural providers directly despite frequent discussion of their challenges; no international comparative perspectives on implementation cost structures.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves multi-stakeholder economic dynamics, structural incentive misalignments, hidden cost categories, and cyclical barriers that create interdependent challenges requiring coordinated policy responses across payment reform, research funding, and regulatory streamlining.

11.2.themeDescription: Technical Infrastructure and IT Capacity Requirements. This sub-theme addresses technical infrastructure requirements for AI adoption, including computing resources, connectivity, and IT staffing. || It includes concerns about inadequate IT infrastructure, reports of broadband access limitations, debates about technical capacity requirements, and proposals for infrastructure support

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that inadequate technical infrastructure—not algorithmic limitations—represents the primary barrier to healthcare AI adoption, with particular concern for rural, behavioral health, and community practice settings that lack IT staffing, broadband access, and modern EHR systems. The central tension lies between the urgent need for infrastructure modernization and the reality that organizations most likely to benefit from AI are least equipped to implement it. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend federal funding support, standardized APIs, and targeted technical assistance rather than purely regulatory approaches.

consensusPoints.text: Infrastructure limitations, not AI algorithms, are the primary barrier to adoption. Nearly all commenters identify technical infrastructure as a fundamental obstacle to AI implementation, with multiple stakeholders including Scientix.ai, AORN, and Penguin Ai explicitly stating this is more critical than the AI technology itself.

exceptions.text: While all agree infrastructure is critical, commenters differ on whether the solution is primarily funding (Providence), technical standards (AMA), or workforce development (Scientix.ai).

consensusPoints.text: Limited IT staffing capacity is a widespread barrier across organization types, spanning small practices, rural hospitals, community centers, and even larger health systems. Massive Bio notes smaller practices and rural hospitals have limited IT support, WellSpan Health identifies insufficient local IT capacity as an administrative hurdle, and Scientix.ai reports 40% of IT leaders say staff are unequipped for AI adoption.

EHR integration challenges create systemic friction. Most commenters identify legacy EHR systems and lack of standardized integration as major technical barriers, with COGA Labs noting each health system integration requires custom engineering work, ADA observing many AI technologies do not integrate seamlessly with existing systems, and Penguin Ai reporting 76% of revenue cycle leaders rate EHR integration as a major or severe challenge.

Behavioral health and dental sectors face compounded disadvantage due to HITECH exclusion. All commenters from these sectors cite exclusion from HITECH Act funding as creating a persistent infrastructure gap. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing notes behavioral health providers' IT infrastructure has been lacking since the 2009 exclusion, NeuroFlow warns AI policy must not repeat that exclusion, and ADA states dentistry has not benefited from EHR infrastructure appropriations.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All commenters from behavioral health and dental sectors (3 of 3)

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether direct government grants or market-based incentives are the better approach to addressing infrastructure gaps.

positions.label: Direct Grants
positions.stance: Providence, Epic Systems, and RUPRI Health Panel advocate for one-time or time-limited grants for infrastructure upgrades, arguing that rural and safety-net hospitals cannot self-fund and infrastructure costs scale before productivity gains materialize.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of healthcare providers and trade associations (6 commenters)
keyArguments: Rural and safety-net hospitals cannot self-fund infrastructure upgrades
Infrastructure costs scale before productivity gains materialize
Fixed-period grants allow organizations to reach sustainability

positions.label: Market Incentives
positions.stance: COGA Labs, Penguin Ai, and AMA favor incentivizing vendors and systems to reduce integration costs through standardization, arguing that standardized API marketplaces would reduce per-integration costs and market-based solutions scale better than grants.
positions.supportLevel: Most AI vendors and technology companies (5 commenters)
keyArguments: Standardized API marketplaces would reduce per-integration costs
Prize competitions could spur system-agnostic tools
Market-based solutions scale better than grants

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether to prioritize real-time data streaming capabilities or focus first on ensuring data quality and governance.

positions.label: Prioritize Real-Time
positions.stance: Technology vendors and researchers including Interstella and Satyadhar Joshi argue AI requires near-instantaneous data access, noting that predictive analytics and decision support cannot function with stale data.
positions.supportLevel: Technology vendors and researchers (3 commenters)
keyArguments: Predictive analytics and decision support cannot function with stale data
Quality measures powered by AI fail if data arrives after point of care

positions.label: Data Quality First
positions.stance: Scientix.ai and Renee Pope emphasize ensuring data integrity before optimizing speed, arguing that inconsistent data quality undermines AI regardless of latency.
positions.supportLevel: Implied by several commenters emphasizing data governance (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Inconsistent data quality undermines AI regardless of latency
Governance and validation layers must precede real-time capabilities

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether to define specific performance standards for clinical AI or allow flexible guidelines that organizations can adapt.

positions.label: Specific Standards
positions.stance: Academic researcher Satyadhar Joshi proposes precise technical benchmarks including data ingestion with latency under 100ms, throughput over 10K events per second, model serving with inference time under 500ms and 99.9% availability.
positions.supportLevel: Academic researchers (1-2 commenters)
keyArguments: Clear standards enable consistent evaluation
Specific latency and throughput requirements ensure clinical safety

positions.label: Flexible Guidelines
positions.stance: RUPRI Health Panel, MHA, and most healthcare providers implicitly support allowing organizations to determine appropriate technical specifications, arguing that one-size-fits-all standards ignore organizational diversity.
positions.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and trade associations (implicit in 8+ comments)
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all standards ignore organizational diversity
Rural facilities cannot meet urban technical standards

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Insufficient IT staffing, infrastructure costs scaling before ROI, and lack of funding support for technology upgrades. Providence notes AI requires more powerful wireless internet and Bluetooth capabilities, and at a basic level requires making space in IT closets or adding new rooms for additional wiring and fans.
specificPoints: Operational experience reveals that even well-resourced health systems face bandwidth constraints for AI governance and monitoring
Infrastructure needs extend to physical space including IT closets, wiring, and cooling
Proposed solutions include one-time grants for qualifying hospitals, time-limited infrastructure cost coverage, and technical assistance programs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Custom integration costs for each health system, lack of standardized APIs, and fragmented EHR ecosystem. COGA Labs notes small AI companies face prohibitive per-integration costs that lock out smaller innovators.
specificPoints: Vendors must spend resources becoming compatible with dozens of different EHR ecosystems
Proposed solutions include standardized API marketplaces, prize competitions for system-agnostic tools, and streaming data standards
Epic reports Jump Start customers use 17 times more AI resources than non-Jump Start customers of comparable size when infrastructure costs are covered

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member organizations, especially small, rural, and independent practices, lack IT capacity. AAFP highlights lack of broadband access in rural and underserved communities limits patients' and physicians' ability to engage with digital solutions including AI-enabled tools.
specificPoints: Represent diverse membership with varying technical readiness
Emphasize that infrastructure gaps will widen health equity disparities if not addressed
Proposed solutions include enhanced interoperability standards, funding for data exchange, and incentives for modern technology infrastructure

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Underfunded research infrastructure, lack of compliant computing environments, and gap between AI-ready and AI-lacking institutions. CRA warns the gap between areas with and without AI infrastructure is increasingly difficult to overcome.
specificPoints: Focus on systemic research infrastructure needs such as NAIRR-Secure
Provide specific technical benchmarks for clinical AI architectures
Proposed solutions include investment in compliant infrastructure and research priorities that include infrastructure readiness as prerequisite to safe AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Broadband access, lack of data analysts and technical personnel, and equipment and software support gaps. RUPRI quantifies that up to 45 million Americans lacked availability of quality broadband services as of March 2024.
specificPoints: Without government aid, rural healthcare systems may not benefit as much from AI implementation compared to urban areas due to lack of infrastructure capacity
Recognition that without intervention, rural areas will not benefit equally from AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Unreliable connectivity, unmapped Wi-Fi coverage in resident care areas, and legacy call-light systems with limited auditability. Renee Pope notes environmental and operational conditions in PALTC settings prevent always available digital workflows.
specificPoints: Infrastructure readiness directly shapes whether AI can be implemented safely
AI has performed better in environments with robust infrastructure and consistent data governance
In infrastructure-limited and data-inconsistent settings, tools tied to unreliable inputs or connectivity often fall short or create unacceptable risk

noteworthyInsights.insight: Infrastructure as trust prerequisite: Scientix.ai argues that fragmented systems, informal workarounds, and absence of deterministic validation layers prevent AI from earning clinician trust—suggesting that infrastructure investment is not just operational but essential for clinical acceptance.

Dramatic usage differential with cost coverage: Epic Systems provides striking evidence that Jump Start customers use 17 times more AI resources than non-Jump Start customers of comparable size when infrastructure costs are covered, suggesting that cost barriers—not lack of interest—suppress AI adoption.

HITECH exclusion creating compounding disadvantage: Multiple behavioral health commenters warn that the 2009 HITECH exclusion created infrastructure gaps that now compound AI adoption challenges, with NeuroFlow explicitly cautioning that AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.

Specific technical benchmarks proposed: Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi offers unusually specific technical requirements including data ingestion with latency under 100ms and throughput over 10K events per second, model serving with inference time under 500ms and 99.9% availability—providing concrete standards for policymakers to consider.

Environmental conditions in PALTC settings: Renee Pope uniquely highlights that post-acute and long-term care settings face environmental and operational conditions that prevent always available digital workflows—a perspective often missing from acute care-focused discussions.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural-urban divide is the dominant geographic pattern, with rural organizations facing compounded challenges including broadband limitations, IT staffing shortages, and lack of technical support teams. RUPRI quantifies that up to 45 million Americans lacked availability of quality broadband services as of March 2024. Multiple commenters warn that without targeted intervention, AI benefits will accrue disproportionately to urban, well-resourced systems.

Facility-type patterns show small and independent practices face lean staffing and limited IT capacity, community and rural practices lack sophisticated technology support teams, behavioral health and dental face compounded disadvantage from HITECH exclusion, post-acute and long-term care faces unique environmental challenges, and large health systems still face bandwidth constraints for AI governance despite greater resources.

Stakeholder role correlations show AI vendors emphasize standardization and market-based solutions, healthcare providers emphasize funding and grants, trade associations bridge both perspectives advocating for member diversity, and researchers focus on systemic infrastructure and specific technical standards.

Unintended consequences identified include equity gaps widening if infrastructure support is not targeted to under-resourced organizations, AI tools becoming another separate system if integration challenges are not addressed, premature AI deployment in infrastructure-limited settings creating unacceptable risk, and small innovators being locked out by per-integration costs potentially reducing competition and innovation.

keyQuotations.quote: The primary barriers to clinical AI adoption are infrastructural, not algorithmic.
If an AI can't plug easily into the EHR or workflow, it may end up as another separate system.
Without government aid, rural healthcare systems may not benefit as much from AI implementation compared to urban areas due to lack of infrastructure capacity.
Gap between areas with and without AI infrastructure increasingly difficult to overcome.

Because mental health and substance use providers were left out of the HITECH Act of 2009, that provided significant funding for the adoption of EHRs, many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking.
Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result. AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.
40% of IT leaders report staff are unequipped for AI adoption.
76% of revenue cycle leaders rate integration between AI tools and core EHR systems as a major or severe challenge.
Jump Start customers use 17 times more AI resources than non-Jump Start customers of comparable size.

AI has performed better in environments with robust infrastructure and consistent data governance; in infrastructure-limited and data-inconsistent settings, tools tied to unreliable inputs or connectivity often fall short or create unacceptable risk.
AI-powered technology requires more powerful wireless internet and Bluetooth capabilities... at basic level: making space in IT closet or adding new rooms for additional wiring and fans.

Clinical data often ingested in batch processes with lag times of days or weeks rather than real-time streams. This stifles AI tools requiring up-to-date information for decision support or predictive analytics.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples and data points. Multiple stakeholders cite statistics and offer concrete policy recommendations rather than vague assertions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite specific statistics (45 million Americans lacking broadband, 76% of revenue cycle leaders rating integration as major challenge, 40% of IT leaders reporting staff unequipped, 17x usage differential with cost coverage). Some claims are based on organizational experience rather than published research.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from patients or patient advocacy organizations. Post-acute and long-term care perspective represented by only one commenter. No direct input from EHR vendors other than Epic. Rural perspectives primarily represented through trade associations rather than direct rural provider comments.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - the issue involves interconnected technical, financial, workforce, and policy dimensions that vary significantly by organization type, geography, and healthcare sector.

11.3.themeDescription: Small, Rural, and Safety-Net Provider Capacity Constraints

sections.executiveSummary: There is near-universal consensus across all stakeholder types that small, rural, and safety-net healthcare providers face structural barriers to AI adoption that threaten to widen existing healthcare disparities. Commenters spanning academic researchers, major health systems, trade associations, and technology vendors agree that without targeted federal intervention—including grants, technical assistance, and scaled governance requirements—AI benefits will concentrate in well-resourced settings while vulnerable populations are left behind. The central tension lies not in whether support is needed, but in how to structure it: through direct funding, regulatory relief, shared infrastructure, or market-based solutions.

consensusPoints.text: AI adoption will concentrate in well-resourced settings without targeted intervention. Nearly all commenters explicitly warn that current market dynamics will exclude smaller and rural providers from AI benefits. This concern transcends stakeholder type, uniting competitors, regulators, and advocates. Massive Bio, Epic Systems, and VieCure all emphasize that without appropriate support, AI could widen rather than narrow the digital divide in healthcare. Mosaic Life Tech cites that 86% of system-affiliated hospitals use predictive AI versus just 37% of independent facilities, and among health systems with mature AI governance, three-quarters have over $1 billion in net patient revenue.

Resource constraints are structural, not philosophical. A strong majority of commenters emphasize that barriers facing vulnerable providers are fundamental infrastructure and capacity issues—not resistance to innovation or lack of interest. Community Care Cooperative notes these barriers reflect the day-to-day realities of operating in safety-net primary care. Renee Pope highlights that in post-acute and long-term care, binding constraints are frequently infrastructure, workforce bandwidth, data integrity, and organizational instability. ScriptChain Health observes that smaller institutions lack processes for AI adoption and don't know how to move forward with clinical AI.

Federal grants and targeted funding are essential. Virtually all commenters proposing solutions call for some form of direct federal financial support, though they differ on mechanisms. University of Kansas Health System recommends targeted funding, tax credits, and group purchasing options for equitable access to AI tools. Advocate Health proposes supplemental payments or grant-based support for under-resourced providers. American Academy of Dermatology suggests federal and state grants to small, independent practices seeking to implement AI systems.

Governance requirements must scale to organizational capacity. Most commenters addressing governance agree that one-size-fits-all requirements would disproportionately burden smaller organizations. HIMSS EHR Association notes that many smaller organizations and rural providers face governance expectations that risk being overly burdensome. Mosaic Life Tech emphasizes that for small and mid-market organizations, community hospitals, and safety-net providers, the shift from federal certification to organizational responsibility creates a governance burden they lack capacity to meet. University of Kansas Health System observes that accreditation and auditing processes are too resource-intensive for under-resourced providers.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters agree support is needed but differ on whether direct federal grants, shared infrastructure/open-source solutions, or regulatory relief represents the best approach.

positions.label: Direct Federal Grants
positions.stance: Strong support from trade associations and safety-net providers including American Academy of Family Physicians, American Academy of Dermatology, and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association. Proponents cite the proven precedent of the Regional Extension Center program which achieved 68% vs 12% Meaningful Use achievement rates.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (20+ commenters)
keyArguments: Proven precedent with Regional Extension Center program achieving 68% vs 12% Meaningful Use achievement
Directly addresses capital constraints that market solutions cannot overcome
Can be targeted to specific provider types and geographic areas

positions.label: Shared Infrastructure
positions.stance: Moderate support from technology companies and researchers including Interstella, Epic Systems, and SANCIAN LLC who emphasize sustainable ecosystem development over one-time support.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support (8-10 commenters)
keyArguments: Reduces ongoing costs versus one-time grants
Creates sustainable ecosystem rather than temporary support
Enables smaller organizations to access enterprise-grade tools

positions.label: Regulatory Relief
positions.stance: Moderate support from innovation-focused organizations including New Jersey Innovation Institute and HIMSS EHR Association who argue complex approval processes disproportionately burden resource-constrained organizations.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support (6-8 commenters)
keyArguments: Complex approval processes disproportionately burden resource-constrained organizations
Tiered governance reduces compliance costs
Faster pathways for low-risk applications

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Intermediaries and Existing Networks
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to leverage existing hospital alliances and networks or create new federal technical assistance infrastructure.

positions.label: Leverage Existing Networks
positions.stance: Rural-focused organizations including RUPRI Health Panel and FastHealth Corporation argue that rural providers already operate through networks that can aggregate purchasing power and state-coordinated approaches ensure rural access through existing relationships.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (5-7)
keyArguments: Rural providers already operate through networks that can aggregate purchasing power
State-coordinated approaches ensure rural access through existing relationships
Reduces need for individual provider capacity building

positions.label: New Federal Infrastructure
positions.stance: Mosaic Life Tech and Community Care Cooperative advocate for new federal technical assistance infrastructure, citing the REC precedent and arguing existing networks may lack AI expertise.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (6-8)
keyArguments: Existing networks may not have AI expertise
Federal coordination ensures consistent quality
Can leverage HRSA, SAMHSA, or other agency infrastructure

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between centralized deemed status models that reduce burden versus local validation approaches that account for context-specific performance.

positions.label: Centralized Deemed Status
positions.stance: Health systems including WellSpan Health and American Urological Association support centralized validation to reduce duplicative work and democratize access to high-quality AI for organizations that cannot afford internal evaluation capacity.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (5-6)
keyArguments: Reduces duplicative validation work across organizations
Smaller providers cannot afford internal evaluation capacity
Democratizes access to high-quality AI

positions.label: Local Validation with Support
positions.stance: EHR vendors including Epic Systems and health systems like Advocate Health argue that AI performance varies organization to organization and post-deployment validation cannot be fully outsourced because local context matters for safety.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters (4-5)
keyArguments: AI performance varies organization to organization
Post-deployment validation cannot be fully outsourced
Local context matters for safety

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability ambiguity affecting safety-net partners; inability to donate technology due to Stark/AKS constraints; need for sustainable funding mechanisms. Large systems see themselves as potential enablers of smaller provider adoption but feel legally constrained from helping.
specificPoints: University of Kansas Health System notes current Stark and Anti-Kickback laws deter larger health systems from donating cybersecurity and AI technology to smaller, under-resourced partners
Providence invested $1.9 billion in community benefit in 2024
Proposed solutions include updating Stark/AKS safe harbors for technology donations, one-time grants for rural/safety-net IT upgrades, and requiring EHR vendors to include baseline AI functionality

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workflow disruption with limited staffing; tools validated elsewhere performing poorly in their settings; limited capacity to vet and monitor AI. These providers emphasize that AI strategies designed for well-resourced systems do not translate effectively to their settings.
specificPoints: Community Care Cooperative supports 50+ FQHCs across 15 states and emphasizes FQHCs serve 14% of U.S. population with small share of federal funding
Vikki Small describes direct experience at Brooklyn safety-net academic medical center where tools built and validated in well-resourced systems perform poorly
Proposed solutions include trusted intermediaries and dissemination hubs, implementation research in their specific settings, and shared infrastructure without prohibitive licensing costs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Financial barriers for independent practices; administrative burden expansion; lack of bargaining leverage with vendors; potential liability if AI becomes standard of care. These associations represent frontline clinicians who will use AI daily and emphasize that many members practice in small, independent, rural settings.
specificPoints: AMA notes independent practices lack bargaining leverage to negotiate robust contractual protections
AAFP represents 128,300 family physicians, many in small/rural settings

Proposed solutions include grants for small practices, simplified governance templates, interoperable solutions to minimize custom integration costs, and educational resources specifically for small practices

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Digital divide widening; smaller organizations unable to afford post-deployment validation; governance burden falling on organizations without capacity. Vendors acknowledge their tools require local validation but recognize resource constraints.
specificPoints: Epic released open-source AI Trust and Assurance Suite but reports many lower-resourced organizations report staffing and time constraints remain barriers
Proposed solutions include targeted grant programs scaled to organization size, tiered governance models, and open-source tools and shared infrastructure

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Disproportionate barriers to market entry; integration requirements favoring incumbents; HIPAA/data access costs prohibitive for small companies. These companies see themselves as potential solution providers for underserved markets but face structural barriers.
specificPoints: COGA Labs states this concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology
Proposed solutions include federated learning networks accessible to small companies, expedited pathways for low-risk applications, and an open department for AI adoption guidance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Rural and safety-net settings underrepresented in evidence; gap between areas with and without AI infrastructure increasingly difficult to overcome; in-house validation happening only at better-resourced systems.
specificPoints: Computing Research Association notes models built in-house more likely to use local data for validation, but in-house systems typically happen at better-resourced health systems
Proposed solutions include support for shared testbeds for rural/safety-net settings, additional resources for EPSCoR/IDeA states, and cooperative R&D agreements for database access

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Post-acute/long-term care faces distinct foundational barriers; payment models create financial liability for innovation; frequent ownership turnover disrupts modernization. These advocates emphasize that PALTC/SNF populations differ significantly from ambulatory or acute settings.
specificPoints: TapestryHealth monitors 80,000+ patients in elder care and notes current payment models reward minutes not capacity
Renee Pope notes frequent ownership/management turnover disrupts long-horizon modernization planning
Proposed solutions include sector-specific accreditation standards, payment model reform to share savings with facilities, and prize competitions for PALTC-specific tools

noteworthyInsights.insight: Regional Extension Center precedent offers proven model: Mosaic Life Tech provides compelling historical evidence that federal technical assistance works—the REC program achieved 68% Stage 1 Meaningful Use among participants versus just 12% among non-participants, assisting approximately 134,000 primary care providers. This suggests a similar model for AI governance could be highly effective.

Stark/AKS laws creating unintended barriers: University of Kansas Health System identifies an unexpected regulatory obstacle—current fraud and abuse laws actually prevent larger health systems from donating technology to smaller partners, even when they want to help strengthen sector-wide resilience.

Open-source tools insufficient without capacity support: Epic Systems provides a reality check on technology-only solutions—even after releasing free, open-source AI validation tools, many lower-resourced organizations report staffing and time constraints remain barriers. This suggests infrastructure alone won't solve the problem.

Hub-and-spoke model preserves local autonomy: Massive Bio describes an operational model that enables national scale without centralized data control—preserves privacy and local clinical autonomy while connecting community oncology practices, academic centers, and patient advocacy organizations. This offers a potential template for rural AI deployment.
