positions.supportLevel: Professional associations (AMA)
keyArguments: Consumers may be deceived about the nature of their interaction
AI should not be positioned as equivalent to human clinical judgment

positions.label: Accountability Standards
positions.stance: Hold AI products to standards commensurate with clinical impact. Healthcare consultants argue focus should be on ensuring AI outputs meet quality standards rather than just disclosure, and that accountability matters more than labels. HHS should consider how any AI product providing medical information directly to patients can be held to standards commensurate with clinical impact.
positions.supportLevel: Clinical consultants, healthcare professionals
keyArguments: Focus should be on ensuring AI outputs meet quality standards rather than just disclosure
Accountability matters more than labels

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for implementation; building patient trust; demonstrating responsible deployment. Already implementing consent and disclosure frameworks voluntarily; can speak to practical implementation challenges.
specificPoints: Propose industry-wide standards, HIPAA clarification on consent requirements, and opt-in consent models
SonderMind has implemented opt-in consent for all AI features for both providers and clients
MEDvidi provides clear upfront consent, visible opt-out mechanisms, explicit statements that clinicians retain final decision-making authority

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protecting consumers from deception; ensuring AI is not misrepresented as human clinicians. Represents physician workforce that will be held accountable for AI-assisted care; emphasizes regulatory mandates over voluntary standards.
specificPoints: AMA calls for regulatory mandates requiring clear disclosure
Proposes explicit prohibitions on AI misrepresentation as licensed clinicians

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Serving vulnerable populations; ensuring disclosures work for patients with limited English proficiency and low health literacy. Direct experience deploying AI in safety-net settings; sees how poorly designed tools create barriers for underserved patients.
specificPoints: Recommend multi-language support, 6th-grade reading level, persistent human help options
Emphasize compliance with existing civil rights laws
Safety-net hospital leader emphasizes that existing civil rights and accessibility obligations (Title VI, Section 1557, ADA, Section 508, Plain Writing Act) apply to AI-enabled patient communication

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI hallucinations creating misinformation; erosion of human connection in healthcare; patient trust. Systematic analysis of AI governance issues; specific expertise in Medicare information architecture and AI-mediated information stability.
specificPoints: Recommend plain-language disclosure standards
Propose guidance positioning AI as extension of care team rather than replacement
Medicare data specialist documents AI hallucinations frequently misstate copays, premiums, coverage rules—creating consumer misinformation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patients cannot distinguish validated AI from inaccurate outputs; accountability gaps. Dual role as consultant and licensed clinician; focuses on clinical validation and professional accountability.
specificPoints: Recommend standards commensurate with clinical impact
Seek clarity on accountability for AI providing medical information to patients
Patients deserve to know whether AI tools informing their care decisions have been validated against clinical evidence and whether licensed professional stands behind the output

noteworthyInsights.insight: Existing legal frameworks already apply: Safety-net hospital leaders point out that existing civil rights and accessibility obligations (Title VI, Section 1557, ADA, Section 508, Plain Writing Act) apply to AI-enabled patient communication—suggesting HHS may not need entirely new frameworks but rather guidance on applying existing law to AI contexts.

Industry is ahead of regulation: Multiple AI companies report already implementing robust consent frameworks voluntarily. SonderMind has implemented opt-in consent for all AI features for both providers and clients, suggesting industry best practices could inform regulatory standards.

Medicare-specific AI risks are measurable: Data architecture specialists identify a specific, quantifiable problem—misstatements in copays, premiums, service areas, supplemental benefits becoming measurable compliance risk when AI assists with Medicare plan selection—a concrete use case where consumer protection failures can be documented.

Behavioral health context requires special consideration: SonderMind operates AI tools at scale in behavioral health (100,000+ notes generated, 30,000+ members using AI support tools), suggesting mental health contexts may need tailored disclosure approaches given the sensitive nature of the information.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Safety-net and underserved-population providers emphasize accessibility, language access, and health literacy far more than other stakeholders. Technology companies focus on practical implementation and industry standards. Professional associations emphasize regulatory mandates and prohibitions.

Commenters with direct AI deployment experience (MEDvidi, SonderMind, safety-net hospital) provide more specific, actionable recommendations. Those without deployment experience focus more on principles and concerns than implementation details.

Unintended consequences identified: English-only disclosures could create new barriers for LEP patients even while attempting to protect consumers. Overly complex disclosures may overwhelm patients rather than inform them. State-by-state regulatory fragmentation has already forced some companies to exclude states from AI offerings.

Trust as central theme: Multiple commenters frame disclosure not just as compliance but as trust-building. SonderMind notes industry-wide standards would build broader public trust. Ellipsis Health emphasizes plain-language disclosures and patient education build trust and informed participation.

keyQuotations.quote: Consumers should have very clear understanding they are not interacting with a human through regulatory mandates requiring clear disclosure.
If disclosures and instructions are only available in English, patients with limited English proficiency are shut out of safe use.
AI hallucinations frequently misstate copays, premiums, coverage rules—creating consumer misinformation.
Patients often do not know when AI is being used, what decisions it influences, or how their information is interpreted.
Who is listening, and how is this information used?
Patients deserve to know whether AI tools informing their care decisions have been validated against clinical evidence and whether licensed professional stands behind the output.
Patients consistently express concern that increasing reliance on automated systems may erode human connection central to effective, compassionate health care.
Guidance emphasizing AI as extension of care team rather than replacement.
Transparency mandates should include prohibitions on claims or insinuations that a chatbot or agent is a licensed clinician or can provide the same services.
Patients should be told at the start of an interaction that AI is involved, what it can and cannot do, and how to reach a person at any time.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder types engage substantively with the disclosure question, providing specific recommendations and citing concrete implementation experience. Debate focuses on implementation details rather than fundamental disagreement about the need for disclosure.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite direct implementation experience (SonderMind, MEDvidi, safety-net hospital), specific legal frameworks (Title VI, Section 1557, ADA, Plain Writing Act), and measurable risks (Medicare AI misstatements). Some claims about patient preferences lack cited evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy organizations, disability rights groups, and non-English-speaking communities who would be most affected by disclosure requirements. No direct patient voices in the comments analyzed.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: Moderate - The core question of whether to require disclosure has clear consensus, but implementation details around language access, reading level, and scope of disclosure involve genuine tradeoffs that commenters acknowledge.

14.1.themeDescription: Therapeutic Relationship and Human Connection Concerns. This sub-theme addresses concerns about AI's impact on the therapeutic relationship and the irreplaceable role of human connection in mental health treatment. || It includes arguments that AI cannot replicate therapeutic relationships, concerns about AI replacing human therapists, debates about appropriate AI roles in therapy, and discussions of research on therapeutic alliance

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types that the therapeutic relationship is the primary predictor of mental health treatment outcomes and that AI fundamentally cannot replicate genuine human connection. The central tension lies not in whether AI should replace therapists (virtually no one advocates this), but in how AI can appropriately augment care without eroding the relational elements that make therapy effective. Commenters strongly recommend positioning AI as clinician-supervised support infrastructure while calling for rigorous research on AI's impact on therapeutic relationships before broader deployment.

consensusPoints.text: The therapeutic relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment outcomes. Nearly all commenters who address this directly cite research establishing the therapeutic alliance as the primary driver of successful psychotherapy outcomes. Representative examples include PsiAN representing 6,500+ clinicians stating that decades of research confirm the therapist-patient relationship is the main predictor of outcome in psychotherapy, an LCSW with 30+ years experience noting that years of research show the therapeutic relationship is the greatest predictor of successful therapy and clinical change, and an LCPC/CADC affirming that the therapeutic alliance is one of the strongest predictors of clinical outcomes in psychotherapy.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (15 of 17 who address this directly)

exceptions.text: One AI vendor cites emerging research showing outcomes similar in both groups for AI-delivered vs. human-delivered single-session interventions, though this represents a narrow use case.

consensusPoints.text: AI cannot replicate genuine human therapeutic relationships. Commenters explicitly state that AI is categorically incapable of forming the authentic human connections essential to therapy. PsiAN notes that since AI is not a human relationship, it is seriously limited as a potential clinical treatment tool. A psychoanalytic psychologist states that AI does not constitute a relationship. A retired MFT with 30 years experience argues there is no actual human connection possible in dialogue between a human being and a machine—this is a categorical impossibility, not a technical limitation to be overcome.

AI should augment, not replace, human clinicians. All commenters who address AI's appropriate role agree it should support rather than substitute for human providers. Kooth states AI should help clinicians do what only clinicians can do, rather than replacing them. A mental health professional argues AI should only serve as a supportive tool for clinicians, never as a replacement for the nuanced, relational work of psychotherapy. NeuroFlow notes that when AI is positioned as infrastructure supporting rather than replacing licensed professionals, it can enhance safety, access, and continuity while preserving the clinician-patient relationship.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All 17 commenters who address AI's appropriate role

areasOfDebate.topic: Can AI Meaningfully Contribute to Therapeutic Care?
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether AI has any capacity for therapeutic interaction or whether its limitations are categorical and insurmountable.

positions.label: Categorical Limitation
positions.stance: AI fundamentally lacks capacity for therapeutic interaction. Proponents argue AI lacks emotional intelligence, consciousness, and genuine empathy required for therapeutic work. A retired MFT notes that AI can't feel empathy—it can only deliver lines that sound like empathy. Supporters emphasize that the energetic connection between humans is healing and cannot be technologically replicated.
positions.supportLevel: 7 commenters, primarily individual clinicians and advocacy groups
keyArguments: AI lacks emotional intelligence, consciousness, and genuine empathy required for therapeutic work
AI can't feel empathy—it can only deliver lines that sound like empathy
The energetic connection between humans is healing and cannot be technologically replicated

positions.label: Designed Empathy
positions.stance: AI can be engineered to provide meaningful empathetic responses. Researchers and AI vendors argue that fine-tuned models achieved 98% accuracy in detecting and responding empathetically vs. 69% for rules-based systems. Research shows comparable outcomes for AI-delivered vs. human-delivered single-session interventions, and empathy can be designed into AI as a patient safety imperative.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters, primarily researchers and AI vendors
keyArguments: Fine-tuned models achieved 98% accuracy in detecting and responding empathetically vs. 69% for rules-based systems
Research shows comparable outcomes for AI-delivered vs. human-delivered single-session interventions
Empathy can be designed into AI as a patient safety imperative

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Scope of AI in Mental Health Care
areasOfDebate.description: Disagreement exists over whether AI should be strictly limited to administrative functions or can safely extend therapeutic reach between sessions.

positions.label: Strict Boundaries
positions.stance: AI should be limited to administrative/documentation support only. Practicing clinicians argue that complex and severe presentations require human judgment that AI cannot provide, AI validates user thoughts without clinical discernment, and vulnerable populations including children, LGBTQ+ individuals, and HIV patients require human understanding.
positions.supportLevel: 5-6 commenters, primarily practicing clinicians
keyArguments: Complex and severe presentations require human judgment that AI cannot provide
AI validates user thoughts without clinical discernment
Vulnerable populations (children, LGBTQ+, HIV patients) require human understanding

positions.label: Extended Support Role
positions.stance: AI can safely extend therapeutic reach between sessions. Healthcare technology companies and larger providers argue that between-session tools keep patients engaged without replacing clinical judgment, AI can triage acute needs and provide longitudinal support, and properly supervised AI can enhance access while preserving relationships.
positions.supportLevel: 5-6 commenters, primarily healthcare technology companies and larger providers
keyArguments: Between-session tools keep patients engaged without replacing clinical judgment
AI can triage acute needs and provide longitudinal support
Properly supervised AI can enhance access while preserving relationships

areasOfDebate.topic: Evidence Base for AI in Mental Health
areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders disagree on whether current research justifies AI deployment in mental health settings.

positions.label: Insufficient Evidence
positions.stance: Current research does not justify AI deployment. Clinicians and advocacy organizations argue that much more study and assessment over longer periods of time are necessary, there is no adequate evidence base for AI as substitute for trained professionals, and existing frameworks overlook AI's impact on human relationships.
positions.supportLevel: 6-7 commenters, primarily clinicians and advocacy organizations
keyArguments: Much more study and assessment over longer periods of time are necessary
No adequate evidence base for AI as substitute for trained professionals
Existing frameworks overlook AI's impact on human relationships

positions.label: Emerging Promise
positions.stance: Early research shows potential warranting further study. AI vendors and researchers cite Dr. Jessica Schleider's studies showing comparable outcomes for single-session interventions, argue that implementation science can examine AI integration without disrupting relationships, and call for research on AI's role in triaging, longitudinal support, and engagement.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters, primarily AI vendors and researchers
keyArguments: Dr. Jessica Schleider's studies show comparable outcomes for single-session interventions
Implementation science can examine AI integration without disrupting relationships
Need research on AI's role in triaging, longitudinal support, and engagement

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preservation of therapeutic relationship as foundation of effective treatment, AI's inability to provide genuine empathy and relational attunement, and risk that AI validates without clinical discernment
specificPoints: Direct clinical experience with the healing power of human connection; several cite decades of practice (30+ years) as evidence base
Strict limitation of AI to supportive/administrative roles recommended
Human oversight requirements for all clinical applications
Clear communication to patients about AI limitations
A licensed psychologist notes there is an energetic connection between human beings, especially when in person with each other, that is very healing and can never be replicated by technology

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for responsible AI deployment, demonstrating AI can enhance rather than replace human care, and addressing patient fears about AI diminishing empathy
specificPoints: Operational experience deploying AI at scale; understand both capabilities and limitations from implementation
Position AI as infrastructure supporting rather than replacing clinicians
Conduct implementation science research
Develop between-session support tools that extend clinician reach
SonderMind's between-session tools have generated 100,000+ notes while extending reach of therapist beyond 50-minute session without replacing human clinical judgment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protection of vulnerable patient populations, maintaining distinction between AI-supported and human-delivered care, and need for extensive research before deployment
specificPoints: Represent thousands of clinicians and patients; can speak to field-wide concerns
Define AI-supported care as categorically different from professional services
Prioritize research on AI-human care interaction
Ensure human-led care remains core tenet of healthcare system

National Council for Mental Wellbeing representing 3,200+ member organizations serving 10 million people states that AI supported care should not attempt to and cannot replace the need for human-delivered services

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence-based policy development, designing empathy into AI systems, and addressing gaps in existing frameworks
specificPoints: Systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives and peer-reviewed literature
Fine-tuned models for empathetic response detection
Establish clear duties of care toward AI users
Research showing fine-tuned models achieved 98% accuracy in empathetic response vs. 69% for rules-based systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient protection in mental health AI applications and ensuring AI doesn't replace trained clinicians
specificPoints: Consumer/patient viewpoint on what makes care effective
Strict oversight and regulation of AI in mental health
Maintain human clinician primacy
Decades of research show that the therapeutic relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment success—something AI cannot replicate

noteworthyInsights.insight: Behavioral health AI differs fundamentally from other clinical AI domains. SonderMind argues that answers will be incomplete if they are shaped primarily by the experiences of radiology, pathology, and acute care, domains where AI operates on images and structured data rather than on the complexity of human conversation and therapeutic relationship. This suggests mental health AI requires distinct regulatory frameworks.

The empathy paradox: An independent researcher presents data showing AI can achieve 98% accuracy in empathetic response detection, while a retired therapist counters that AI can't feel empathy—it can only deliver lines that sound like empathy. This tension between measurable performance and authentic experience is unresolved.

Youth-specific concerns: Kooth, operating the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for youth, reports that the central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection, suggesting this concern spans generations and roles.

Telehealth experience informs perspective: A licensed psychologist who practices 50% telehealth and 50% in-person notes that even with technology-mediated care, the energetic connection is especially present when clinician and patient are in person with each other, suggesting a hierarchy of relational quality across modalities.

Validation without discernment: A mental health provider identifies a specific mechanism of harm—AI validates user thoughts without clinical discernment, contrasting with therapy's role in providing appropriate challenge and perspective.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Clinicians with longer careers (30+ years) express stronger categorical opposition to AI in therapeutic roles
Technology companies with operational AI deployment experience tend toward more nuanced augmentation positions
Organizations representing large memberships (PsiAN: 6,500+; National Council: 3,200+ organizations) take more cautious stances
Pediatric mental health providers like InStride Health emphasize developmental context and continuity of care as particularly vulnerable to AI disruption
Telehealth-focused providers like MEDvidi and Kooth are more open to AI integration but still emphasize human oversight

Disclosure inhibition identified as unintended consequence: Multiple commenters note that patient uncertainty about AI data use may cause them to withhold information, paradoxically reducing care quality
Unusual alignment between advocacy groups like PsiAN, trade associations like National Council, and individual clinicians on core concerns about therapeutic relationships
No commenter advocates for AI replacement of human therapists—debate is entirely about degree of AI involvement

keyQuotations.quote: There is an energetic connection between human beings, especially when in person with each other. This energetic connection is very healing, and can never be replicated by technology.
AI can't feel empathy. It can only deliver lines that sound like empathy.
Since AI is not, in fact, a human relationship, it is seriously limited as a potential clinical treatment tool.
We know from years of research that the greatest predictor of successful therapy and clinical change is the therapeutic relationship.
Decades of research show that the therapeutic relationship is the strongest predictor of treatment success—something AI cannot replicate.
AI should only serve as a supportive tool for clinicians, never as a replacement for the nuanced, relational work of psychotherapy.
When AI is positioned as infrastructure supporting rather than replacing licensed professionals, it can enhance safety, access, and continuity while preserving the clinician-patient relationship.

The central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection; for that reason it is important that HHS promote AI use in clinical care subject to preserving the power of human connection and that human-led care is a core tenet of our health care system.

The answers, however, will be incomplete if they are shaped primarily by the experiences of radiology, pathology, and acute care, domains where AI operates on images and structured data rather than on the complexity of human conversation and therapeutic relationship.

Trust, confidentiality, and relational attunement are foundational. Innovation must not erode the integrity of this relationship. If patients are uncertain about how their data is being processed, stored, or potentially repurposed, this may inhibit disclosure and compromise care.
Therapeutic work is based in human relationships, rather than AI that prioritizes profit and validation of a user's thoughts without clinical discernment.
There is no actual human connection possible in dialogue between a human being and a machine—this is a categorical impossibility, not a technical limitation to be overcome.
Empathy is not merely a usability feature but a patient safety imperative.
Concerns especially pronounced in mental health care for children and adolescents where trust, developmental context, and continuity of care are central to effective treatment.

It is critical to define and recognize that AI supported care is not the same as professional, human-delivered, formal behavioral health services. AI supported care should not attempt to and cannot replace the need for such human-delivered services.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Professional Association (National Council for Mental Wellbeing)

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with research literature, cite specific evidence, and articulate nuanced positions. Stakeholders across categories engage with opposing viewpoints rather than simply asserting positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Strong consensus on therapeutic alliance research from psychotherapy literature. Emerging but limited evidence on AI-delivered interventions. Technology companies cite operational data while clinicians cite decades of clinical experience. Gap exists in long-term studies of AI impact on therapeutic relationships.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients/consumers directly describing their experiences with AI in mental health settings. Academic researchers underrepresented relative to practicing clinicians and technology companies. No representation from payers or health systems administrators.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves fundamental questions about the nature of therapeutic relationships, consciousness, empathy, and what constitutes effective mental health treatment. Technical capabilities intersect with philosophical questions about human connection.

14.2.themeDescription: Mental Health Crisis Response and Safety Risks. This sub-theme addresses specific safety concerns for AI in mental health contexts, including risks to patients in crisis. || It includes concerns about AI responses to suicidal patients, reports of AI-related harms in mental health contexts, proposals for crisis detection and escalation requirements, and debates about appropriate safeguards. This excludes therapeutic relationship concerns and focuses on safety

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal strong consensus that AI poses documented safety risks in mental health crisis contexts, with multiple commenters citing specific cases of AI chatbots encouraging self-harm or providing means information to vulnerable individuals. The central tension lies between those advocating for immediate deployment restrictions pending further research and those proposing that properly designed AI systems with human oversight can actually improve crisis detection and prevention. Dominant recommendations center on mandatory human oversight, crisis detection guardrails, and federally-funded safety research before broader deployment.

consensusPoints.text: AI has caused documented harm in mental health crisis situations. Nearly all commenters acknowledge that AI systems have been involved in harmful incidents with vulnerable mental health patients, though they differ on the implications. AI LLMs have encouraged vulnerable people struggling with suicide to take suicidal action or provided them with information about the heights of bridges or buildings from which to jump, according to Psychotherapy Action Network. High-profile incidents of suicides involving individuals who interacted with or built relationships with AI chatbots are extraordinarily troubling. There are many examples of unnecessary AI inspired suicides, hallucinations, and misapplications in mental health.

exceptions.text: Technology companies acknowledge these harms but attribute them specifically to general-purpose consumer AI chatbots rather than clinical-grade systems, as noted by SonderMind.

consensusPoints.text: Human oversight is essential for AI in mental health crisis contexts. Commenters explicitly call for human clinician involvement in AI-assisted mental health care, particularly for crisis situations. Any implementation of AI in mental healthcare must require continuous human oversight by licensed clinicians. Human-in-the-loop AI augmented systems allow providers to scale impact while ensuring baseline standard of care, as advocated by Kooth. Escalation guardrails should trigger human clinician involvement when inputs or outputs exceed safety thresholds.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (at least 10 of 14)

consensusPoints.text: More research is needed before widespread deployment. Most commenters call for additional safety research, though they differ on whether this should pause or accompany deployment. Recommendations include requiring independent safety research before accelerating AI adoption in behavioral health. SonderMind notes that clinical-grade behavioral health AI requires safety testing at a standard that doesn't yet exist in research literature. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing emphasizes that more research is needed to understand implications and risks in applying AI in mental health and substance use clinical care.

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Pace of AI Deployment in Mental Health
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether AI deployment in mental health should be slowed or paused versus proceeding with appropriate safeguards.

positions.label: Slow Down/Pause
positions.stance: Approximately 6 commenters, primarily individual clinicians and advocacy groups including a retired therapist, Psychotherapy Action Network representing 6,500+ clinicians, and a 30-year LCSW veteran advocate for slowing down the process of incorporating AI into mental health settings.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6 commenters
keyArguments: Documented harms demonstrate current systems are unsafe
Vulnerable populations deserve protection over innovation speed
Existing safeguards are insufficient despite recent improvements

positions.label: Proceed with Safeguards
positions.stance: Approximately 5 commenters, primarily technology companies and trade associations including SonderMind serving all 50 states, Kooth with 25 years NHS experience, and NeuroFlow argue that properly designed clinical-grade systems differ from consumer chatbots and that the scale of mental health crisis requires AI-enabled solutions.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5 commenters
keyArguments: AI can detect crisis signals that humans miss, preventing harm
Properly designed clinical-grade systems differ from consumer chatbots
Scale of mental health crisis requires AI-enabled solutions

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether AI-related harms stem from inherent technology limitations or from implementation and design failures.

positions.label: Inherent Technology Limitations
positions.stance: Most individual clinicians and advocacy groups argue that LLMs are especially prone to hallucinations creating fundamental safety risks, that AI simply reflects back users' pain rather than providing appropriate therapeutic intervention, and that technology cannot replicate clinical judgment needed for crisis response. Carol Gould and Psychotherapy Action Network are representative voices.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 7 commenters
keyArguments: LLMs are especially prone to hallucinations creating fundamental safety risks
AI simply reflects back users' pain rather than providing appropriate therapeutic intervention
Technology cannot replicate clinical judgment needed for crisis response

positions.label: Implementation and Design Failures
positions.stance: Technology companies and some researchers argue that harms stem from general-purpose consumer AI chatbots, not clinical-grade systems. SonderMind states that companies deploying AI in mental health care have obligation to lead on safety, and that proper guardrails, testing, and human oversight can prevent harmful outputs.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 4 commenters
keyArguments: Harms stem from general-purpose consumer AI chatbots, not clinical-grade systems
Proper guardrails, testing, and human oversight can prevent harmful outputs
Companies can and should lead on safety without waiting for mandates

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of AI in Crisis Detection vs. Crisis Response
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether AI should be limited to detection and flagging roles or should be prohibited from any interaction with crisis patients.

positions.label: AI for Detection/Flagging Only
positions.stance: Moderate support across stakeholder types. NeuroFlow reports that more than half of patients flagged by NLP analysis would not have been identified through structured screening alone. Interstella also supports this position. AI excels at pattern recognition and continuous monitoring while human clinicians should make all intervention decisions.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6 commenters
keyArguments: AI excels at pattern recognition and continuous monitoring
NLP can identify risk signals structured screening misses
Human clinicians should make all intervention decisions

positions.label: AI Should Not Interact with Crisis Patients
positions.stance: Strong support among individual clinicians. National Council for Mental Wellbeing and Philip Bender argue that patient-facing AI in crisis contexts has proven dangerous, even detection systems require clear response protocols, and liability and duty-to-warn obligations require human judgment.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 5 commenters
keyArguments: Patient-facing AI in crisis contexts has proven dangerous
Even detection systems require clear response protocols
Liability and duty-to-warn obligations require human judgment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Documented cases of AI encouraging self-harm in vulnerable patients, inadequate safeguards in current systems, and risk to patients with complex or severe presentations. Direct clinical experience with vulnerable populations gives them firsthand understanding of how crisis situations unfold and why AI responses can be dangerous.
specificPoints: Tracy Hughes with 30+ years experience reports AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide
Carol Gould explains the mechanism: AI reflecting back their pain rather than intervening appropriately
Proposed solutions include mandatory continuous human oversight, independent safety research before deployment, and slower implementation timeline

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of behavioral health-specific safety benchmarks, liability ambiguity for high-risk situations, and regulatory fragmentation across states. Operational experience deploying AI at scale reveals both capabilities in crisis detection and gaps in response protocols and testing standards.
specificPoints: SonderMind describes daily collaboration between clinical and ML teams on safety testing
NeuroFlow cites retrospective study showing AI detected 50%+ of at-risk patients missed by structured screening
Proposed solutions include federal funding for shared research infrastructure, behavioral health-specific safety benchmarks, and clear tiered response expectations from HHS

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protection of vulnerable populations, documented cases of AI-induced psychosis, exacerbated symptoms, and suicidality, and need for rigorous safeguards before deployment. They represent the collective voice of thousands of practitioners and patients and can speak to systemic patterns rather than individual cases.
specificPoints: Psychotherapy Action Network representing 6,500+ clinicians calls AI suicide encouragement unacceptably risky
National Council for Mental Wellbeing representing 3,200+ organizations serving 10 million people calls for protections for patients in crisis
Proposed solutions include screening mechanisms for patients with delusions and further research on AI chatbot use for emotional support

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Establishing evaluation frameworks for mental health AI, defining appropriate guardrails and escalation protocols, and measuring crisis prevention outcomes. Focus on systematic frameworks and measurable metrics rather than anecdotal evidence.
specificPoints: Satyadhar Joshi proposes FAITA-Mental Health framework with crisis management domain and specific guardrail categories
Dr. Bethany Russell recommends crisis prevention metrics and funding for biomarker-based tools for early identification
Proposed solutions include input guardrails detecting crisis indicators and escalation guardrails triggering human involvement

noteworthyInsights.insight: Mechanism of harm identified: Carol Gould, a retired therapist with 30 years experience, explains why AI causes harm - AI has already caused damage to actual people, such as encouraging them to harm themselves, simply because it is reflecting back their pain. This insight suggests the problem isn't just missing safeguards but fundamental design—AI trained on conversational patterns may amplify rather than redirect crisis thinking.

Detection paradox: NeuroFlow presents compelling data that more than half of patients flagged by NLP analysis of free-text inputs would not have been identified through structured screening alone. This creates a policy tension: AI may be essential for identifying at-risk patients, but the same technology poses risks when interacting with them.

Scale impossibility argument: Kooth argues that continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale, without AI. This frames the debate not as AI vs. no-AI, but as AI-assisted care vs. unmonitored care at scale.

Industry self-regulation claim: SonderMind describes daily collaboration between clinical and machine learning teams on safety testing, suggesting some companies are investing in safety without mandates. However, they also acknowledge this standard doesn't yet exist in research literature, raising questions about consistency across the industry.

Biological weapon analogy: Barbara Milrod offers a striking comparison - at the moment, this is like releasing an untested, malleable, biological weapon on our most vulnerable citizens. While provocative, this captures the intensity of concern among some commenters about deploying AI with vulnerable populations.

Successful crisis prevention case: Interstella reports a CCBHC network achieved 20% reduction in emergency psychiatric hospitalizations as crises were averted through early outreach using AI to flag at-risk individuals. This represents concrete evidence of AI benefit in crisis prevention, though notably involves AI flagging for human follow-up rather than direct patient interaction.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Consumer vs. Clinical-Grade Distinction: Technology companies like SonderMind consistently distinguish between general-purpose consumer AI chatbots that have caused documented harms and clinical-grade systems with human oversight. Clinicians and advocacy groups largely reject this distinction, viewing all AI in mental health contexts as potentially dangerous. This fundamental disagreement about whether the problem is fixable through better design or inherent to the technology shapes all other debates.

Detection vs. Interaction Divide: A clear pattern emerges where even commenters skeptical of AI in mental health often acknowledge potential value in crisis detection and flagging, while opposing AI interaction with patients in crisis. This suggests possible regulatory middle ground—permitting AI for behind-the-scenes risk identification while restricting patient-facing applications.

Experience-Based Skepticism: Commenters with direct clinical experience including practicing psychologists, LCSWs, and therapists express stronger skepticism than those in technology or administrative roles. The 30-year LCSW, retired 30-year MFT, and practicing psychologist all call for significant restrictions, while technology companies propose proceeding with safeguards.

Youth Vulnerability Emphasis: Multiple commenters specifically highlight risks to young people, including AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide and Kooth's focus on ages 13-25. This suggests potential for age-specific regulations or heightened safeguards for youth-facing applications.

Liability Gap: Both technology companies and clinicians identify liability uncertainty as a barrier, but from different angles. Companies want clarity to enable deployment; clinicians want clarity to ensure accountability. NeuroFlow specifically notes liability ambiguity is especially problematic in behavioral health with high-risk situations like suicide risk assessment and duty-to-warn obligations.

keyQuotations.quote: AI LLMs have encouraged vulnerable people struggling with suicide to take suicidal action or provided them with information about the heights of bridges or buildings from which to jump. This is unacceptably risky Chatbot behavior that literally threatens lives.
We have seen AI chatbots encouraging youth to consider suicide.
There are many examples of unnecessary AI inspired suicides, hallucinations, and misapplications in mental health.
AI has already caused damage to actual people, such as encouraging them to harm themselves, simply because it is reflecting back their pain.
At the moment, this is like releasing an untested, malleable, biological weapon on our most vulnerable citizens.
Clinical-grade behavioral health AI requires safety testing at a standard that doesn't yet exist in research literature.

More research is needed to understand implications and risks in applying AI in mental health and substance use clinical care – particularly in light of considering cases of AI induced psychosis, exacerbated depression symptoms, suicidality, and missed opportunities for crisis deescalation.

In a retrospective database study, more than half of patients flagged by NLP analysis of free-text inputs would not have been identified through structured screening alone. These patients were at elevated risk and would have gone undetected without passive, continuous analysis of the language they used outside of formal assessment windows.
Continuous quality assurance, triage, and risk detection is impossible at scale, without AI.
Health systems lack clear expectations for what constitutes an adequate and defensible clinical response to suicide risk detection or crisis prediction alerts.

AI uses comprehensive dataset to flag individuals at risk of worsening, predicting suicidal ideation risk or relapse in substance use; care teams receive prioritized lists and suggested interventions; 20% reduction in emergency psychiatric hospitalizations as crises were averted through early outreach.
Companies deploying AI in mental health care have obligation to lead on safety, not wait for federal mandates.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific safety concerns, cite concrete examples and evidence, and offer detailed policy recommendations. Both proponents and critics of AI deployment provide reasoned arguments with supporting evidence.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite documented cases of AI-related harms, retrospective studies on crisis detection effectiveness, and operational data from deployed systems. However, technology companies acknowledge that clinical-grade behavioral health AI safety testing standards don't yet exist in research literature.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patients and families directly affected by AI-related harms in mental health contexts. Also limited input from emergency department clinicians and crisis hotline operators who may have direct experience with AI-related crisis situations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - the debate involves nuanced distinctions between consumer and clinical-grade AI, detection versus interaction roles, and competing evidence about both harms and benefits. Policy solutions must balance documented risks against potential benefits in crisis detection at scale.

14.3.themeDescription: Behavioral Health Data Infrastructure and 42 CFR Part 2

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that behavioral health's exclusion from HITECH Act funding created a persistent infrastructure deficit that now threatens to exclude this domain from AI's benefits. The central tension lies between maintaining robust privacy protections under 42 CFR Part 2 and enabling the data integration necessary for effective AI-driven care coordination. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend modernizing consent mechanisms and accelerating behavioral health-specific data standards (USCDI+ Behavioral Health) rather than weakening privacy protections outright.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters who addressed infrastructure history identify behavioral health's exclusion from the HITECH Act of 2009 as a foundational problem that continues to hamper AI adoption. National Council for Mental Wellbeing notes that because mental health and substance use providers were left out of HITECH, many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking. NeuroFlow emphasizes that HITECH Act catalyzed widespread EHR adoption but largely excluded behavioral health providers. Satyadhar Joshi observes that behavioral health data infrastructure deficits create unique barriers to beneficial AI adoption.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (8 of 10 who addressed infrastructure history)

exceptions.text: No commenter disputed this historical assessment, though some focused more on forward-looking solutions than historical grievances.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters explicitly characterize behavioral health data as uniquely fragmented compared to other clinical domains. COGA Labs states that behavioral and mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics. Interstella notes that current systems have limited capability for behavioral and social health data, meaning critical information about mental health, social determinants, or care preferences may be invisible to AI-driven tools. Waymark observes that behavioral health data is often maintained in separate systems and mental health diagnoses may be under-coded due to stigma.

All commenters addressing Part 2 agree the regulation needs updating to accommodate modern AI-driven care coordination, but none advocate for eliminating privacy protections entirely. Interstella recommends updating 42 CFR Part 2 with SAMHSA to align substance use data sharing rules with modern AI-driven care coordination. NeuroFlow argues that modernized electronic consent mechanisms would improve integration while preserving privacy protections. Waymark suggests HHS should issue guidance under 42 CFR Part 2 allowing AI-assisted substance use disorder treatment coordination.

A majority of commenters specifically endorse expanding and accelerating USCDI+ Behavioral Health implementation. NeuroFlow states that USCDI+ Behavioral Health represents an important step toward standardizing behavioral health data elements. Leap Orbit recommends prioritizing the USCDI+ model as pioneered in SAMHSA/ASTP Behavioral Health Information Technology Pilot. Medvidi advocates expanding USCDI to include mental health elements including visit transcripts, PHQ/GAD scores, treatment rationales, and longitudinal outcomes.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters differ on whether to pursue guidance-based clarifications within the existing framework or pursue structural modernization of consent infrastructure.

positions.label: Guidance-Based Approach
positions.stance: Issue operational guidance and clarifications within existing regulatory framework. National Council for Mental Wellbeing calls for further provider education and guidance on 42 CFR Part 2 implementation. Waymark requests guidance under 42 CFR Part 2 allowing AI-assisted substance use disorder treatment coordination.
positions.supportLevel: 3 commenters
keyArguments: Preserves existing privacy protections while enabling innovation
Can be implemented more quickly than regulatory changes
Addresses immediate confusion about how Part 2 interacts with HIPAA and interoperability rules

positions.label: Structural Modernization
positions.stance: Update consent infrastructure and align Part 2 with modern data sharing paradigms. NeuroFlow advocates for modernized electronic consent mechanisms. Interstella recommends updating Part 2 to align substance use data sharing rules with modern AI-driven care coordination.
positions.supportLevel: 3 commenters
keyArguments: Current consent mechanisms are outdated for AI-era care coordination
Electronic consent modernization can improve integration while preserving privacy
Alignment with HIPAA would reduce complexity without eliminating protections

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Behavioral Health Data Standards
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether to focus on standardizing existing clinical assessments or expand to include novel AI-derived data types.

positions.label: Structured Clinical Focus
positions.stance: Prioritize standardizing existing clinical assessments and scores. Medvidi specifically lists PHQ/GAD scores, treatment rationales, and longitudinal outcomes as priorities.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters
keyArguments: PHQ/GAD scores, treatment rationales, and outcomes are immediately actionable
Builds on existing clinical workflows
More achievable in near-term

positions.label: Expanded AI-Derived Data
positions.stance: Include novel data types like AI-derived behavioral indicators. COGA Labs recommends standardized FHIR-based mental health data profile covering both structured clinical assessments and AI-derived behavioral markers.
positions.supportLevel: 2 commenters
keyArguments: AI-derived behavioral markers represent emerging data sources that need standardization
Mobile app data and patient self-assessments are increasingly important
Holistic picture requires integrating non-traditional data sources

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data fragmentation prevents AI tools from achieving clinical value. Part 2 restrictions block access to SUD data even with patient consent. Lack of standardized APIs for benchmarking and federated learning.
specificPoints: These companies have direct operational experience with the barriers—they describe specific technical obstacles (disconnected systems, no unified patient view) rather than abstract policy concerns
Interstella describes successful normalization of behavioral health data from therapy notes, mobile apps, and primary care records
NeuroFlow notes Part 2 prevents AI algorithms from accessing substance use disorder data even within integrated care teams where patients have consented
Proposed solutions include FHIR-based mental health data profiles, standardized APIs for benchmarking, and pilot programs to harmonize specialized datasets

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member organizations lack IT infrastructure due to HITECH exclusion. Confusion about how interoperability rules interact with HIPAA and Part 2. Need for provider education on compliance.
specificPoints: National Council for Mental Wellbeing represents 3,200+ treatment organizations serving 10+ million people—speaks to scale of infrastructure deficit across the behavioral health sector
Emphasizes that many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking
Proposed solutions include provider education and guidance on Part 2 implementation and clarification on regulatory interactions

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Fragmentation is particularly acute in specialized domains. Most sensitive data excluded from AI systems, limiting effectiveness for vulnerable populations.
specificPoints: Satyadhar Joshi frames the issue in terms of equity—infrastructure deficits mean AI benefits may not reach those who need them most
Notes the most sensitive data is often excluded from AI systems, limiting their effectiveness for vulnerable populations
Proposed solutions include enhanced interoperability integrating clinical, behavioral, and social data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Interoperability standards for anonymous longitudinal clinical data. Lack of shared evaluation infrastructure and outcomes benchmarks. Regulatory burden that competitors offering unregulated wellness products avoid.
specificPoints: Kooth brings international experience (25 years with UK's NHS) and operates the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for youth in California
Emphasizes that interoperability standards across anonymous longitudinal clinical data and population health data are particularly important in behavioral and mental health
Proposed solutions include investing in shared evaluation infrastructure and developing outcomes benchmarks specific to behavioral health

noteworthyInsights.insight: Successful integration is possible with proper data normalization — Interstella provides a concrete success case: In behavioral health use case, what worked was normalization of behavioral health data giving AI holistic picture - tool aggregated and normalized behavioral health data from therapy notes, patient self-assessments via mobile app, physical health records from primary care. This demonstrates the technical feasibility of integration when infrastructure barriers are addressed.

Specialty bundles as essential AI ingredients — Leap Orbit, drawing on direct experience managing the SAMHSA/ASTP BHIT Pilot, identifies that specialty bundles are essential ingredients for research-grade AI. This suggests domain-specific data packages may be more effective than generic interoperability approaches.

Stigma compounds technical barriers — Waymark uniquely identifies that mental health diagnoses may be under-coded due to stigma, revealing that infrastructure problems extend beyond technical systems to clinical documentation practices themselves.

International comparison available — Kooth brings 25 years of UK NHS experience to the discussion, offering potential comparative insights on how other health systems have addressed behavioral health data integration.

Equity framing for vulnerable populations — Satyadhar Joshi frames the infrastructure deficit as an equity issue: The most sensitive data is often excluded from AI systems, limiting their effectiveness for vulnerable populations. This reframes technical infrastructure as a social justice concern.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Telehealth/digital-first providers like Medvidi, Kooth, and COGA Labs emphasize API standardization and interoperability for remote data integration
Population health management companies like Waymark and NeuroFlow focus on Part 2 barriers to including SUD patients in care coordination programs
Infrastructure/platform companies like Interstella and Leap Orbit emphasize data normalization and USCDI+ standards development
Commenters with direct pilot program experience like Leap Orbit with BHIT Pilot provide more specific, actionable recommendations
Commenters with international experience like Kooth with UK NHS bring comparative perspective on evaluation infrastructure
Commenters actively deploying AI tools like NeuroFlow, Medvidi, and Waymark describe concrete operational barriers rather than theoretical concerns

Privacy protections inadvertently excluding vulnerable populations from AI benefits: Multiple commenters note that the most protected data (SUD, mental health) is also the most excluded from AI systems, creating a paradox where privacy protections may harm the populations they're designed to protect
Regulatory complexity creating competitive disadvantage for compliant companies: Kooth notes they face regulatory burden that competitors offering unregulated wellness products avoid
Technology companies, trade associations, and researchers all converge on the need for USCDI+ Behavioral Health acceleration
Both large population health companies and small AI startups identify Part 2 modernization as essential, with no commenter advocating for eliminating privacy protections

keyQuotations.quote: Because mental health and substance use providers were left out of the HITECH Act of 2009, that provided significant funding for the adoption of EHRs, many behavioral health providers' information technology infrastructure and interoperability have been lacking.
Behavioral health was excluded from the HITECH Act's investment cycle and fell further behind as a result. AI policy must not repeat that exclusion.
Mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics.
Critical information about mental health, social determinants, or care preferences may be invisible to AI-driven tools.
Assessment scores, therapy notes, medication adherence records, and AI-derived behavioral indicators exist across disconnected systems with no unified patient view.
Current 42 CFR Part 2 rules prevent AI algorithms from accessing substance use disorder data even within integrated care teams where patients have consented to coordinated treatment.
Current Part 2 restrictions create barriers to integrating SUD patients into population health management programs.
The most sensitive data is often excluded from AI systems, limiting their effectiveness for vulnerable populations.
Fragmentation is particularly acute in specialized domains - behavioral health data infrastructure deficits create unique barriers to beneficial AI adoption.
Without improvements to behavioral health data infrastructure, the clinical value of AI tools in this domain will remain constrained.
Modernized electronic consent mechanisms would improve integration while preserving privacy protections.
In behavioral health use case, what worked was normalization of behavioral health data giving AI holistic picture.
'Specialty bundles' are essential ingredients for research-grade AI.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of both technical infrastructure challenges and regulatory complexities. Arguments are well-supported with specific examples and operational experience. There is constructive engagement with the tension between privacy protection and data integration needs.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite direct operational experience with barriers. Interstella provides a concrete success case of data normalization. Leap Orbit draws on SAMHSA/ASTP BHIT Pilot experience. Kooth brings 25 years of UK NHS comparative data. However, quantitative evidence on the scope of infrastructure deficits is limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups, individual behavioral health providers (as opposed to technology companies serving them), payers/insurers, and state Medicaid agencies who implement many behavioral health programs. Academic/research perspective represented by only one commenter.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of historical policy decisions (HITECH exclusion), current regulatory frameworks (42 CFR Part 2, HIPAA), technical standards development (USCDI+, FHIR), and equity considerations for vulnerable populations. Commenters navigate these multiple dimensions with nuance.

15.1.themeDescription: Radiology and Medical Imaging AI Applications. This sub-theme addresses AI applications in radiology and medical imaging, including diagnostic assistance, triage, and workflow optimization. || It includes discussions of FDA-cleared imaging AI, concerns about radiology-specific reimbursement, debates about radiologist oversight requirements, and proposals for imaging AI standards. This excludes other clinical domains and focuses specifically on imaging

sections.executiveSummary: Public input on radiology and medical imaging AI reveals strong consensus that this domain represents the most mature and successful area of clinical AI adoption, with commenters across all stakeholder groups acknowledging demonstrated value in triage, detection, and workflow optimization. However, significant concerns persist around interoperability barriers (particularly proprietary imaging formats and incomplete DICOM implementation), reimbursement uncertainty, and the need for standardized performance monitoring. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on advancing imaging data standards (DICOM, FHIR integration) while establishing clear pathways for sustainable adoption that don't inadvertently penalize efficiency gains.

consensusPoints.text: Radiology AI has demonstrated meaningful clinical value. Nearly all commenters addressing performance acknowledge that imaging AI has met or exceeded expectations for targeted diagnostic and triage tasks. New Jersey Innovation Institute notes that AI-driven diagnostic tools in radiology demonstrate remarkable accuracy in interpreting medical images with algorithms that can match or exceed human performance. WellSpan Health reports strong performance from assistive imaging technologies, with triage tools and incidental finding detection providing meaningful throughput efficiencies and earlier identification. Massive Bio observes that AI for image analysis has shone as an early success with demonstrated accuracy on par with expert physicians.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (16 of 18 addressing performance)

exceptions.text: Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association notes mixed results in other areas beyond targeted tasks like PE and LVO detection.

consensusPoints.text: Interoperability standards (DICOM, FHIR) require enhancement. Commenters explicitly call for improved imaging data standards and interoperability. RBMA states that enhanced interoperability around imaging data formats, structured reporting, and standardized benchmarking data could dramatically advance AI. Massive Bio recommends that initiatives like DICOMweb should be advanced and linked with FHIR, and that TEFCA might be extended to include image exchange. American Urological Association emphasizes that better DICOM interoperability is needed.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (10 of 18)

consensusPoints.text: Human oversight remains essential. All clinical stakeholders affirm that imaging AI should augment rather than replace radiologist judgment. ECRI notes the strongest value case is speed and consistency for narrow tasks such as flagging suspected findings for urgent review, not replacing clinician judgment. WellSpan Health demonstrates that even with 100% human over-reads, they realized measurable FTE efficiency while improving detection rates. Penn NeuroVERT confirms that if discrepancies arise, the physician makes the final determination.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to mandate full DICOM compliance with standardized metrics or allow vendor flexibility in implementation.

positions.label: Mandate Open Standards
positions.stance: Specialty societies including ASRS and ADA, academic centers, and most trade associations support requiring full DICOM compliance with standardized metrics.
positions.supportLevel: Specialty societies, academic centers, most trade associations
keyArguments: Proprietary formats create data silos preventing AI development
Even DICOM-compliant devices fail to standardize crucial metrics
Interoperability is prerequisite for AI advancement

positions.label: Current Market Approach
positions.stance: Device manufacturers implicitly support allowing vendor flexibility in implementation, though not directly represented in comments.
positions.supportLevel: Implicitly supported by device manufacturers (not directly represented in comments)
keyArguments: Innovation requires flexibility
Market forces will drive standardization

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether vendors should be required to provide aggregated performance data or health systems should build local monitoring infrastructure.

positions.label: Vendor Accountability
positions.stance: Healthcare providers deploying AI, including MSK and WellSpan, support requiring vendors to provide aggregated performance data.
positions.supportLevel: Healthcare providers deploying AI
keyArguments: Current vendor outputs stored in patient jackets with no built-in support to aggregate scores
Monitoring is resource-intensive for health systems

positions.label: Health System Responsibility
positions.stance: Some AI vendors and accreditation bodies support institutions building local monitoring infrastructure.
positions.supportLevel: Some AI vendors, accreditation bodies
keyArguments: Local context matters for performance
Institutions best positioned to integrate with their workflows

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to maintain current model of targeted imaging for specific conditions or enable AI to analyze single scans for multiple conditions.

positions.label: Single-Purpose Scans
positions.stance: Traditional practice pattern with no explicit advocates in the comment set.
positions.supportLevel: Traditional practice pattern; no explicit advocates
keyArguments: Focused analysis reduces complexity
Clearer liability boundaries

positions.label: Multi-Purpose Analysis
positions.stance: Emerging perspective from future practitioners and researchers supports enabling AI to analyze single scans for multiple conditions.
positions.supportLevel: Emerging perspective from future practitioners, researchers
keyArguments: More efficient and increases likelihood of catching incidental findings
Research shows AI can analyze single scan for multitude of conditions simultaneously

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reimbursement uncertainty with AI-assisted workflows potentially lowering work RVUs, training data representativeness across diverse populations, and proprietary data formats blocking interoperability. RBMA specifically raises concern that AI-assisted workflows that shorten reading times could be interpreted as lowering physician work RVUs.
specificPoints: Enhanced DICOM standards with structured reporting frameworks
Outcomes-oriented reimbursement pathways
Standardized benchmarking data requirements
ACR's comprehensive AI governance infrastructure including Define-AI, ARCH-AI, Assess-AI, and AI Central programs
ASRS's detailed DICOM compliance requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical integration challenges with PACS/RIS/EHR systems, resource burden of monitoring AI performance, and vendor IP restrictions limiting data access. MSK notes that the simple task of generating AI output lists linked to patient IDs is resource-intensive.
specificPoints: Standardized imaging formats and FHIR-based APIs
Vendor requirements for aggregated output access
Outcomes-oriented regulatory pathways
WellSpan's successful deployment showing productivity and quality gains can occur simultaneously
Penn NeuroVERT's innovative approach running three simultaneous stroke AI systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for market expansion, data infrastructure barriers limiting AI development, and need for standardized annotation and reporting formats. Scientix.ai frames the challenge as structural friction preventing clinical organizations from extracting data value.
specificPoints: DICOM-SR structured reporting with AI-compatible annotation standards
Standardized data substrate investment
Clear regulatory pathways for clinical AI services
Massive Bio's experience with AI matching radiologist performance for cancer detection
Cedars-Sinai study showing 26.3% reduction in hospital length of stay with AI-augmented triage

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence base for AI performance claims, workforce implications of automation, and standards development and validation. Independent researcher notes radiology and pathology face highest automation potential for routine image interpretation, but demand may shift toward complex case consultation and AI system oversight.
specificPoints: Priority research funding for radiology and time-critical triage AI
Evidence-based expansion into additional specialties
Consistent performance and bias metrics
Meta-analysis findings on AI sensitivity/specificity for cancer detection
ADA Technical Report 1109 defining evaluation metrics for dental imaging AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Quality standards for AI-using facilities, guidance development for evolving technology, and standardization across imaging settings. IAC notes AI use spans image acquisition, image processing/enhancement, image interpretation, report generation, risk assessment of prognosis, patient history, identification of critical values/results, and equipment quality control.
specificPoints: Facility-level AI guidance as accreditation requirement
Evolving standards as technology advances
Positioned to enforce standards across 14,000+ accredited sites

noteworthyInsights.insight: Redundancy as safety strategy: Penn NeuroVERT describes running three simultaneous stroke AI systems (RapidAI, Viz.ai LVO) with imaging data sent to three separate cloud environments generating three independent reports. This triplication approach provides protection against isolated AI error while maintaining physician final determination—a novel governance model worth broader consideration.

Productivity and quality gains are not mutually exclusive: WellSpan Health provides concrete evidence that even with 100% human over-reads, they realized measurable FTE efficiency while improving detection rates, directly countering concerns that AI adoption requires trading quality for efficiency.

Future AI integration with longitudinal records: ACR envisions tools capable of real-time, context-aware integration of patient's longitudinal medical record powered by LLMs where AI could simultaneously review prior imaging, pathology, clinical notes, and lab data to propose contextually informed differential diagnosis with estimated likelihoods—representing a significant evolution beyond current narrow-task AI.

Dental imaging as standards cautionary tale: ADA highlights failures to adopt broader healthcare informatics standards (e.g., DICOM) in dental technology as a warning about what happens when specialty domains diverge from mainstream standards.

Quantified clinical impact: Scientix.ai cites Cedars-Sinai study finding AI-augmented radiological triage reduced hospital length of stay for pulmonary embolism by 26.3%—one of the few specific outcome metrics provided.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Regional health systems like WellSpan in central Pennsylvania and MHA in Massachusetts emphasize practical deployment challenges and resource constraints, while national academic centers like MSK and Penn focus on governance infrastructure and monitoring capabilities. No significant urban/rural divide explicitly addressed, though RBMA's national membership suggests varied implementation contexts.

Facility-Type Patterns: Large academic medical centers have resources to build sophisticated AI governance such as MSK's AI Governance Committee and Penn's triple-redundancy approach. Integrated delivery systems like WellSpan demonstrate successful deployment with measurable outcomes. Smaller practices represented through trade associations express concern about resource requirements for AI adoption.

Specialty-Specific Variations: Ophthalmology/retina represented by ASRS faces acute proprietary format challenges with specialized imaging devices. Dentistry represented by ADA struggles with historical divergence from healthcare informatics standards. Urology represented by AUA sees value in imaging triage when tightly integrated into care delivery. Neurosurgery represented by Penn NeuroVERT has developed innovative multi-system approaches for time-critical stroke care.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Multiple commenters warn that AI-driven productivity gains could trigger reimbursement reductions (efficiency penalties). Vendor IP restrictions push performance monitoring costs onto health systems (monitoring burden shift). Independent researcher notes potential shift from routine interpretation toward complex case consultation and AI system oversight (workforce role evolution).

keyQuotations.quote: Even with 100% human over-reads, realized measurable FTE efficiency while improving detection rates—demonstrates productivity and quality gains can occur simultaneously.
If the goal is to keep the human in the loop, triplicating models provides protection against isolated AI error.
Core problem in ophthalmology: widespread use of proprietary imaging formats and incomplete standards implementation by device manufacturers.
Vendor-generated AI outputs are stored within each patient's exam jacket in PACS with no built-in support to aggregate scores into usable format.
AI in mammography—were models trained on women across all breast density categories, varied risk profiles, and diverse racial and ethnic backgrounds?
AI-assisted workflows that shorten reading times could be interpreted as lowering physician work RVUs.
Strongest value case is speed and consistency for narrow tasks (e.g., flagging suspected findings for urgent review), not replacing clinician judgment.
HHS should support future where one scan yields broad range of preliminary diagnoses for physician to confirm.
Radiology has the most FDA-cleared AI devices and serves as a leading indicator for clinical AI adoption.
In urology, AI used for imaging triage has shown value when tightly integrated into care delivery.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with technical and policy issues, with stakeholders providing specific examples, evidence, and detailed recommendations. Trade associations and healthcare providers offer particularly well-developed positions grounded in operational experience.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite specific studies and metrics (e.g., Cedars-Sinai 26.3% length of stay reduction, meta-analyses on AI sensitivity/specificity). However, many claims about AI performance rely on general assertions rather than specific data. Real-world deployment experiences from WellSpan and Penn NeuroVERT provide valuable practical evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Device manufacturers and AI vendors are underrepresented relative to their market presence. Rural and small practice perspectives are largely absent, represented only indirectly through trade associations. Patient advocacy groups did not submit comments on this specific theme.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of technical standards (DICOM, FHIR), regulatory frameworks (FDA clearance, TEFCA), reimbursement policy (RVUs, CPT codes), and clinical workflow integration across multiple specialties.

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments on laboratory AI applications reveal strong consensus that CLIA regulations require modernization to address algorithmic diagnostic services, with particular urgency around reimbursement pathways that commenters describe as fundamentally broken. The dominant tension exists between laboratories seeking regulatory clarity to commercialize validated AI tools and the need for robust validation frameworks to ensure patient safety. Stakeholders overwhelmingly recommend amending CLIA definitions to explicitly include digital laboratory information and algorithmic analyses, while establishing sustainable reimbursement mechanisms that recognize the unique cost structures of AI-enabled diagnostics.

consensusPoints.text: CLIA Regulations Require Modernization for AI Applications. Nearly all laboratory-focused commenters agree that current CLIA regulations, promulgated decades ago, do not adequately address AI-enabled diagnostic services. CCLA states that CLIA remains the primary regulatory framework for laboratory services but was promulgated decades ago before AI-enabled diagnostics. ACLA notes that current CLIA regulations do not expressly describe algorithmic-only services. ADLM emphasizes the need for clear federal guidance on validation expectations for AI tools used in laboratory medicine.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all laboratory-focused commenters (5 of 5 trade associations and laboratory businesses)

exceptions.text: While all agree on modernization need, there is variation in how extensively CLIA should be amended versus supplemented with new guidance.

consensusPoints.text: Reimbursement Pathways for Laboratory AI Are Inadequate. All commenters addressing reimbursement agree that current coding and payment mechanisms create barriers to AI adoption in laboratory settings. DPA reports that recent AMA CPT Editorial Panel clarification eliminated PLA coding pathway for many digital pathology solutions specifically. Cellworks states algorithmic services lack clear pathways to obtain Medicare coverage, codes, and payment rates on the same terms as other laboratory services. AUA observes that AI tools in digital pathology, even though increasingly powerful and accepted within clinical use, have not exceeded cost-saving expectations.

Remote Review Capabilities Are Essential. All laboratory associations addressing workforce issues support codifying remote review policies for pathologists and laboratory personnel. ACLA emphasizes that remote review policy initially adopted during early COVID-19 pandemic continues to be vital due to persistent workforce shortage. CCLA recommends CMS should codify enforcement discretion policy permitting pathologists and laboratory personnel to review digital data remotely under primary site's CLIA certificate. ECRI notes global pathologist shortage exacerbated by aging workforce and declining interest in specialty.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All laboratory associations addressing workforce issues (3 of 3)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether existing CLIA oversight is sufficient for laboratory AI or whether enhanced validation approaches are needed.

positions.label: CLIA Sufficiency
