positions.stance: CLIA oversight combined with technical assessment provides adequate framework. Laboratory businesses and some trade associations including Cellworks and ACLA support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Laboratory businesses and some trade associations
keyArguments: CLIA-certified laboratories already conduct rigorous analytical validation
Established review processes such as MolDx provide technical assessment
FDA clearance should not be prerequisite for Medicare coverage

positions.label: Enhanced Validation Needed
positions.stance: AI systems require additional validation approaches beyond traditional CLIA. Professional associations focused on quality such as ADLM support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Professional associations focused on quality
keyArguments: AI systems introduce unique risks around data comparability
Different testing platforms produce different results that AI may misinterpret
Ongoing performance monitoring specifically for AI/ML applications is necessary

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI-enabled services should receive identical reimbursement to traditional procedures or require distinct payment mechanisms.

positions.label: Parity with Traditional
positions.stance: AI-enabled services should receive identical reimbursement to equivalent traditional procedures. Virtual staining and digital pathology companies including Pictor Labs and DPA support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Virtual staining and digital pathology companies
keyArguments: If clinical outcome is equivalent, method should be irrelevant
AI may actually provide superior value through tissue preservation and faster results

positions.label: New Payment Structures
positions.stance: AI services require distinct coding and payment mechanisms reflecting unique cost structures. Digital Pathology Association and CCLA support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Digital Pathology Association, CCLA
keyArguments: Investment and operational costs including image acquisition, computing, and data storage not captured in current reimbursement
Capital-intensive technologies require ongoing investment and continuous model refinement

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether CLIA definition should be expanded to include facilities examining digital laboratory information.

positions.label: Expand Definition
positions.stance: Include facilities examining digital laboratory information. Strong consensus among laboratory associations including ACLA and CCLA.
positions.supportLevel: Strong consensus among laboratory associations
keyArguments: Algorithmic-only services analyzing previously sequenced DNA or digital images represent legitimate laboratory functions
Regulatory ambiguity creates uncertainty

positions.label: Cautious Expansion
positions.stance: No explicit opposition was voiced in the comments, though ADLM's emphasis on validation requirements suggests caution about expanding definitions without corresponding quality safeguards.
keyArguments: Quality safeguards should accompany any definition expansion

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for algorithmic services under CLIA, sustainable reimbursement pathways for AI-enabled diagnostics, and workforce shortage and remote review capabilities. Organizations including ADLM, CCLA, DPA, and ACLA bring deep understanding of operational realities in clinical laboratories with emphasis on how regulatory ambiguity affects day-to-day operations and investment decisions.
specificPoints: Amend CLIA definitions to include digital laboratory information and algorithmic examinations
Codify remote review policies
Create new coding pathways including APC families and demonstration models for AI services
ACLA provided detailed proposed regulatory amendments
DPA called for CMMI demonstration model

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear Medicare coverage pathways blocks commercialization, reimbursement parity with traditional methods, and regulatory uncertainty despite validated technologies. Organizations including Pictor Labs and Cellworks have direct experience with commercialization barriers and can articulate specific patient benefits being delayed by policy gaps.
specificPoints: CMS guidance stating CLIA-certified algorithmic services are eligible for Medicare coverage without FDA clearance
Identical reimbursement for AI-driven and traditional procedures achieving same clinical outcomes
Pictor Labs highlighted tissue preservation benefits
Cellworks described precision oncology validation experience

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clinical utility and cost-effectiveness of AI tools. The American Urological Association provides end-user perspective on whether AI delivers promised value.
specificPoints: Observation that digital pathology AI have not exceeded cost-saving expectations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety implications of AI diagnostics, evidence base for AI applications beyond well-studied cancers, and workforce automation potential. Organizations including ECRI and independent researchers provide evidence-based assessment of AI capabilities and limitations with focus on safety rather than commercial viability.
specificPoints: More research needed on AI-enabled diagnosis beyond gastric, breast, and prostate cancer
ECRI's evidence review showing high sensitivity and specificity for certain malignancies but limited data for others

noteworthyInsights.insight: Tissue Preservation as Underappreciated Benefit: Pictor Labs highlights that virtual staining requires only a single tissue slice, preserving significant biopsy tissue for additional molecular tests. This addresses a concrete clinical problem where several common biopsy locations frequently have inadequate samples triggering repeat sampling including breast, melanoma, colorectal, kidney, lung, and thyroid. The patient burden of repeat biopsies represents a tangible harm that AI could reduce.

Harmonization Funding Gap: ADLM reveals that Congress has provided CDC only $2 million annually since FY 2018 for laboratory test harmonization, which ADLM considers insufficient. This underfunding directly undermines AI reliability, since AI systems assume data comparability that doesn't exist in practice.

Automation Vulnerability Identified: Independent researcher Joshi identifies pathology assistants as having high automation potential for routine pattern recognition, suggesting workforce implications that other commenters do not address directly.

Comprehensive Digital Data Definition: ACLA provides an unusually detailed proposed definition of digital laboratory information encompassing digital images from glass slides, flow cytometry plots, cytogenetic karyograms, chromatographic data, mass spectrometric data, clinical chemistry data, immunological data, hematologic data, electropherograms, gel images, genetic expression data, and array/sequencing data. This specificity could serve as a template for regulatory language.

Evidence of Clinical Utility: ECRI provides balanced evidence assessment noting AI-enabled diagnostic reviews of WSI demonstrate high sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy relative to manual review across various malignancies with substantial agreement between AI-assisted and manual reviews and reduced review times. This independent validation supports adoption while noting evidence gaps.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Regulatory Timing Urgency: Multiple commenters reference imminent deadlines creating pressure for action. ACLA notes enforcement discretion for remote cytology review scheduled to expire in March. DPA references recent AMA CPT Editorial Panel clarification eliminating PLA pathway. This suggests a window for regulatory action that may close without intervention.

Convergence of Workforce and Technology Issues: Three distinct stakeholder types including trade associations, safety organizations, and businesses independently connect pathologist shortage to AI adoption barriers, suggesting this intersection deserves policy attention.

Laboratory vs. Point-of-Care Distinction: Commenters consistently emphasize that laboratory AI faces unique challenges distinct from other clinical AI including CLIA-specific regulatory framework, distinct coding and reimbursement pathways such as PLA and APC, remote review considerations, and data comparability issues across testing platforms.

Validation Framework Tension: A subtle tension exists between laboratories asserting CLIA validation is sufficient such as Cellworks and ACLA versus quality-focused organizations emphasizing AI-specific validation needs such as ADLM. This may reflect different risk tolerances or commercial interests.

Geographic Concentration: California laboratories appear prominently including CCLA and Cellworks in San Jose, with CCLA noting California laboratories operate at forefront of biotechnology and digital health innovation. This suggests potential for state-level regulatory divergence if federal action is delayed.

keyQuotations.quote: Repeat biopsies are a real, recurring source of healthcare cost and patient burden. If a virtually stained slide circumvents a re-biopsy, significant cost is saved, not to mention sparing the patient another invasive procedure, delays, frustration, and anxiety.
AI systems that use laboratory data often assume that test results are comparable across different testing sites and methods. In practice, this is often not the case.
If a stained slide is reimbursed, whether accomplished by approved AI or chemical processes should be unimportant.
Algorithmic services offered lawfully by CLIA-certified laboratories lack clear pathways to obtain Medicare coverage, codes, and payment rates on the same terms as other laboratory services.
Recent AMA CPT Editorial Panel clarification eliminated PLA coding pathway for many digital pathology solutions specifically.
Remote review policy initially adopted during early COVID-19 pandemic continues to be vital due to persistent workforce shortage.
Evidence indicates AI-enabled diagnostic reviews of WSI demonstrate high sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy relative to manual review across various malignancies.
AI tools in digital pathology, even though increasingly powerful and accepted within clinical use, have not exceeded cost-saving expectations.
CLIA remains primary regulatory framework for laboratory services but was promulgated decades ago before AI-enabled diagnostics.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of regulatory frameworks, provide specific technical recommendations, and engage substantively with policy implications. Trade associations offer detailed proposed regulatory language while maintaining constructive tone.

evidenceBase.explanation: Laboratory stakeholders cite specific regulatory provisions, coding pathway changes, and operational experiences. ECRI provides independent evidence review of AI performance. However, quantitative data on cost-effectiveness and patient outcomes remains limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient advocacy groups and individual patients are notably absent from comments. Rural and small laboratory perspectives are underrepresented. Payer perspectives beyond Medicare are not represented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of CLIA regulations, Medicare reimbursement policy, CPT coding processes, FDA oversight questions, and workforce considerations requiring specialized expertise to fully evaluate.

15.3.themeDescription: Oncology and Cancer Care AI Applications. This sub-theme addresses AI applications in cancer care, including clinical trial matching, treatment planning, and precision oncology. || It includes discussions of AI for clinical trial enrollment, concerns about oncology-specific AI challenges, debates about AI in cancer treatment decisions, and proposals for oncology AI support

sections.executiveSummary: Public input on oncology AI applications reveals strong consensus that AI holds transformative potential for cancer care—particularly in clinical trial matching and precision medicine—but commenters uniformly emphasize that structural barriers in reimbursement, regulatory pathways, and healthcare system incentives must be addressed to realize these benefits. The dominant tension lies between the promise of AI to democratize access to cutting-edge cancer care and the risk that without deliberate policy intervention, these advances will concentrate in well-resourced academic centers, widening disparities for the majority of cancer patients treated in community settings. Commenters converge on recommendations for integrating AI into existing oncology payment models, prioritizing community oncology deployment, and funding prospective outcomes research.

consensusPoints.text: AI has significant potential to improve cancer care across the continuum. SITC states AI utilization is an opportunity to accelerate progress across the cancer care continuum. ACS CAN notes AI applications showing promise in cancer care for improving screening and detection, diagnosis, treatment direction and assessment, and personalized medicine. Massive Bio emphasizes AI's ability to dramatically shorten trial enrollment timelines and boost participation rates. Enthusiasm is consistently tempered by calls for maintaining highest standards of patient safety, privacy, and scientific best practices.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All 7 commenters addressing oncology AI express optimism about AI's role in cancer care

consensusPoints.text: Clinical trial matching is a critical, underutilized opportunity for AI. Massive Bio argues clinical trials are among the most underutilized assets in U.S. healthcare and that AI can reverse the structural failure where most patients learn about trials too late. ACS CAN highlights the scale of need: 2.1 million new cancer diagnoses expected in 2026.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Strong agreement among business and advocacy commenters (3 of 7 explicitly address this)

exceptions.text: No commenters oppose AI-enabled trial matching, though some emphasize the need for prospective validation

consensusPoints.text: Existing oncology payment models provide ready platforms for AI integration. ICF notes the Enhancing Oncology Model (EOM) began July 2023, added second cohort July 2025, and represents a ready platform for AI-enabled symptom monitoring and care navigation. VieCure recommends reimbursement pathways should work within existing models such as EOM.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Multiple commenters (3 of 7) specifically reference the Enhancing Oncology Model as an integration pathway

areasOfDebate.topic: Deployment Priority: Academic Centers vs. Community Oncology
areasOfDebate.description: These positions are not mutually exclusive but reflect different emphasis on speed vs. rigor in deployment strategy.

positions.label: Community-First Approach
positions.stance: Prioritize AI deployment in community and rural oncology settings. VieCure CEO explicitly advocates this position, and Massive Bio's nationwide reach implicitly supports it.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly advocated by VieCure; implicitly supported by Massive Bio's nationwide reach
keyArguments: Majority of U.S. cancer patients receive care in community settings
Without aligned incentives, advances will concentrate in large systems, widening disparities
Community practices lack resources to adopt new therapies and AI technologies independently

positions.label: Evidence-First Approach
positions.stance: Focus on rigorous validation before widespread deployment. Cellworks and SITC emphasize this position.
positions.supportLevel: Emphasized by Cellworks and SITC
keyArguments: Algorithmic diagnostics require extensive prospective validation to achieve standard-of-care adoption
Oncotype DX success demonstrates 20+ years of prospective outcomes data can achieve guideline recommendation and nationwide coverage

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether to create specific reimbursement codes for AI-enhanced oncology care or embed AI utilization into existing value-based models.

positions.label: Add-on Payments/Modifiers
positions.stance: Create specific reimbursement codes for AI-enhanced oncology care. VieCure advocates this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Advocated by VieCure
keyArguments: Technology-enabled modifiers for AI tools that measurably enhance guideline concordance
Rewards cost-deflation through sponsor-funded therapies

positions.label: Value-Based Integration
positions.stance: Embed AI utilization into existing APMs and quality programs. Massive Bio and ICF advocate this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Advocated by Massive Bio and ICF
keyArguments: Trial utilization should be integrated into MIPS and APMs
EOM provides ready infrastructure for AI integration

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear Medicare coverage pathways for algorithmic laboratory services, regulatory and coding uncertainty blocking commercialization, and community oncology practices lacking resources for AI adoption. These companies have direct operational experience with AI deployment at scale; Massive Bio has served 160,000+ patients across all 50 states.
specificPoints: AI-generated trial and pathway reports prior to prior authorization
'Gold card' mechanisms for real-time approval
Add-on payments or technology-enabled modifiers for AI tools
Massive Bio's partnership with ACS powering nationwide trial matching
Cellworks' precision oncology tools validated against NCCN guidelines

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety and scientific rigor in AI implementation, gaps in research funding for childhood cancer and comparative effectiveness, and liability and reimbursement clarity for clinicians using AI. These organizations represent frontline clinicians and researchers who will implement AI tools; SITC brings specific expertise in immunotherapy drug development.
specificPoints: Increased NIH investment in AI-driven biomarker discovery
Funding for research areas unsupported by industry
AUA notes prostate cancer risk stratification AI has shown value when tightly integrated into care delivery

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Translating policy into operational requirements and ensuring AI adoption pathways are practical. ICF brings 50+ years supporting federal health agencies with expertise in CMS programs and payment levers.
specificPoints: Leverage EOM as ready platform for AI-enabled symptom monitoring and care navigation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI impacts on cancer screening, diagnosis, treatment access, and coverage decisions, with emphasis on ensuring benefits reach all 18.6 million Americans living with cancer history. ACS CAN emphasizes the scale of cancer burden with 626,000+ deaths expected in 2026.
specificPoints: NIH funding for childhood cancer and comparative effectiveness research

noteworthyInsights.insight: Oncotype DX as validation model - Cellworks provides a detailed case study of how algorithmic diagnostics can achieve standard-of-care adoption: Oncotype DX now informs ~40% of U.S. breast cancer treatment decisions (>90% in relevant subtypes), is Medicare/Medicaid reimbursed nationwide, and is recommended in NCCN clinical guidelines—but this required 20+ years of prospective outcomes data. This suggests a long runway for newer AI tools.

Trial enrollment as cost-deflection strategy - Massive Bio makes an underappreciated economic argument: clinical trials shift costs from payers to sponsors, meaning AI-enabled trial matching could reduce healthcare spending while improving patient access to cutting-edge therapies.

White House Cancer Moonshot recognition - Massive Bio's AI-powered trial pre-screening hubs have been recognized by the White House Cancer Moonshot initiative, suggesting federal-level validation of this approach.

Prostate cancer as integration proof point - AUA notes that AI for prostate cancer risk stratification has shown value when tightly integrated into care delivery, suggesting integration approach matters as much as algorithm quality.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Strong pattern of concern about community vs. academic center disparities. VieCure explicitly warns that advances will concentrate in large systems without policy intervention. Massive Bio's nationwide reach across all 50 states suggests AI can bridge geographic gaps when properly deployed.

Validation timeline tensions - Cellworks' Oncotype DX example (20+ years to standard-of-care) contrasts with urgency expressed by other commenters. Tension exists between rigorous prospective validation and rapid deployment to address immediate patient needs.

Payment model convergence - Multiple commenters (3 of 7) independently identify the Enhancing Oncology Model as a ready platform for AI integration, suggesting EOM may be an emerging consensus vehicle for oncology AI reimbursement.

Unintended consequences identified including prior authorization delays causing patients to miss trial eligibility windows, lack of reimbursement pathways blocking commercialization of validated tools, and resource constraints preventing community practices from adopting beneficial AI.

keyQuotations.quote: Clinical trials are among the most underutilized assets in U.S. healthcare. They provide cutting-edge therapy, shift costs from payers to sponsors, generate real-world evidence, and preserve patient choice. Yet most patients learn about trials too late—after prior authorization delays or disease progression. This is a structural failure, not a technology gap.

Without aligned incentives, advances will concentrate in large systems, widening disparities and gaps in care within community care and thereby jeopardizing the timeliness and quality of care received by the majority of US citizens diagnosed with cancer seen in the community oncology setting.
AI utilization is an opportunity to accelerate progress across the cancer care continuum.
In urology, AI used for prostate cancer risk stratification has shown value when tightly integrated into care delivery.

Oncotype DX informs ~40% of U.S. breast cancer treatment decisions (>90% in relevant subtypes), is Medicare/Medicaid reimbursed nationwide, recommended in NCCN clinical guidelines, with 20+ years of prospective outcomes data.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples, case studies, and quantitative data. Positions are clearly articulated with supporting rationale.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite specific data points, operational experience, and case studies such as Oncotype DX validation history and Massive Bio's patient reach statistics. Some claims would benefit from additional independent validation.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from individual patients, smaller community oncology practices, and payers. Academic medical centers and health systems are not directly represented in the comments analyzed.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of regulatory pathways, reimbursement mechanisms, clinical validation requirements, and healthcare delivery system dynamics

15.4.themeDescription: Surgical and Procedural AI Applications. This sub-theme addresses AI applications in surgical and procedural settings, including robotic surgery, surgical guidance, and procedural planning. || It includes discussions of AI-enabled surgical systems, concerns about auditability in surgical AI, debates about surgeon oversight, and proposals for procedural AI standards. This excludes diagnostic AI and focuses specifically on interventional applications. The defining characteristic is focus on AI that guides physical interventions rather than diagnostic or treatment planning AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments on surgical and procedural AI reveal strong consensus that these systems require enhanced auditability and must maintain human oversight, with commenters uniformly rejecting fully autonomous surgical AI. The central tension lies between enabling innovation in AI-guided procedures while ensuring safety through appropriate hardware and software safeguards. Stakeholders—spanning surgeons, nurses, anesthesia providers, device manufacturers, and technical experts—converge on the need for clear standards that preserve clinician control while allowing AI to augment procedural accuracy and efficiency.

consensusPoints.text: Human-in-the-Loop Control is Non-Negotiable. Nearly all commenters emphasize that surgical AI must augment rather than replace clinician judgment. AANA states that AI is most effective when it enhances clinician expertise, improves consistency, and integrates seamlessly into clinical workflows. MediView XR explicitly notes their platform preserves human-in-the-loop control as a defining characteristic of responsible Physical AI. AANA cites the commercial failure of SEDASYS automated sedation system as evidence that autonomous systems lacked sufficient adaptability to real-world clinical complexity.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (5 of 6 addressing this issue)

exceptions.text: While all agree on human oversight, the degree of oversight and specific mechanisms remain undefined.

consensusPoints.text: Auditability is a Critical Gap in Current Surgical AI Systems. Commenters identify fragmented, incomplete procedural records as a systemic problem requiring regulatory attention. Dr. Binita Ashar notes that when adverse events, unexpected outcomes, or performance questions arise in surgical systems, it is often difficult to reconstruct what actually occurred. Penn NeuroVERT notes that procedural information exists across structured and unstructured fields but is not easily synthesized.

exceptions.text: No commenter argued against enhanced auditability requirements.

consensusPoints.text: AI Integration Should Combine Clinical and Operational Intelligence. Multiple stakeholders agree that the most valuable surgical AI tools integrate multiple data streams rather than functioning in isolation. AANA suggests AI systems that combine physiologic monitoring with throughput and capacity data could provide unified view to support both frontline clinicians and health system leaders. MediView XR describes integration of pre-operative and intra-operative imaging, AI-enabled segmentation, continuous spatial alignment and instrument tracking.

areasOfDebate.topic: Safety Mechanism Design for Surgical AI
areasOfDebate.description: This debate reflects a fundamental tension between engineering-focused safety requirements and clinical workflow integration. The technical commenter raises concerns not addressed by clinical stakeholders, suggesting a potential blind spot in current practice standards.

positions.label: Hardware-First Safety
positions.stance: Physical interlocks must override software controls. Michael Bridges, a technical expert, argues that software response times (20-50ms) are too slow to prevent tissue damage, and that physical hardware can guarantee zero-energy state in 1-2ms while software-only stops are insufficient for actuator control.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly advocated by 1 technical expert; implicitly supported by safety-focused commenters
keyArguments: Software response times (20-50ms) are too slow to prevent tissue damage
Physical hardware can guarantee zero-energy state in 1-2ms
Software-only stops are insufficient for actuator control

positions.label: Software-Integrated Safety
positions.stance: Embedded automation within existing clinical systems is sufficient. AANA describes how rule-based automation in infusion pumps and monitoring systems has long contributed to safer, more efficient anesthesia delivery, and that existing systems work within clinical workflows.
positions.supportLevel: Implicitly supported by clinical stakeholders describing current practice
keyArguments: Rule-based automation in infusion pumps and monitoring systems has long contributed to safer, more efficient anesthesia delivery
Existing systems work within clinical workflows

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of AI Autonomy in Procedural Settings
areasOfDebate.description: The distinction between augmentation and direct influence may be semantic, but it reflects genuine uncertainty about where to draw the line between decision support and procedural guidance.

positions.label: Augmentation Only
positions.stance: AI should support but never direct procedural actions. AANA argues that the SEDASYS failure demonstrates risks of autonomous systems, that clinical complexity requires human adaptability, and that AI integration into clinical reasoning has proven problematic when models attempt to operate autonomously.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of clinical commenters
keyArguments: SEDASYS failure demonstrates risks of autonomous systems
Clinical complexity requires human adaptability
AI integration into clinical reasoning has proven problematic when models attempt to operate autonomously

positions.label: Guided Autonomy
positions.stance: AI can directly influence procedural execution with appropriate safeguards. MediView XR and some academic researchers argue that Physical AI directly influences procedural execution and outcomes, and that real-time spatial guidance enables interventions in resource-constrained environments.
positions.supportLevel: Device manufacturers and some academic researchers
keyArguments: Physical AI directly influences procedural execution and outcomes
Real-time spatial guidance enables interventions in resource-constrained environments

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Auditability gaps; inability to reconstruct adverse events; fragmented data across manufacturers, hospitals, EHRs, and cloud systems. Dr. Binita Ashar emphasizes the system-level complexity of surgical AI—not just the device, but the entire ecosystem of devices, software, cloud platforms, networks, and clinician decision-making in real time. Dr. Ashar brings unique credibility as FACS, MBA, and surgical systems advisor.
specificPoints: Define minimum attributes for auditable procedural records
Elevate auditability as a system requirement for high-risk surgical AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient and staff safety in all phases of perioperative care. AORN is developing the first comprehensive AI guideline specifically for perioperative settings (publication May 2026), using PRISMA systematic review methodology. AORN represents frontline end-users who will implement whatever standards emerge.
specificPoints: Evidence-based guidelines with clinical application examples

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI tools must integrate into existing workflows; autonomous systems have failed when they lack clinical adaptability; CRNAs are sole providers in many rural hospitals. AANA has extensive experience with rule-based automation (infusion pumps, monitoring systems) providing historical context for AI integration and can speak to what has worked and failed. AANA represents 69,000 CRNAs providing 50+ million anesthetics annually—massive scale of potential AI impact.
specificPoints: AI should combine clinical and operational intelligence
Focus on real-time prediction of complications and discharge barriers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity and reimbursement pathways; need for recognition of Physical AI as distinct category. MediView XR emphasizes real-time spatial awareness and instrument tracking as defining features of procedural AI and highlights value in acute and resource-constrained environments.
specificPoints: Recognize Physical AI as distinct from diagnostic AI
Establish appropriate regulatory and reimbursement frameworks

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance barriers, EMR integration challenges, and reimbursement misalignment affecting adoption of beneficial AI tools. Penn NeuroVERT focuses on practical workflow improvements—AI/NLP for procedural detail extraction could help clinicians iterate on technique and refine practice patterns.
specificPoints: Automate extraction of procedural information from existing documentation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Software response times inadequate for preventing tissue damage; current safety mechanisms may be insufficient. Michael Bridges is the only commenter to address hardware-level safety requirements and brings an engineering perspective absent from clinical comments.
specificPoints: Require physical hardware interlocks
Evaluate systems on ability to achieve zero-energy state in 1-2ms

noteworthyInsights.insight: Hardware vs. Software Safety Blind Spot — Technical expert Bridges raises a concern entirely absent from clinical stakeholder comments: that software-based safety mechanisms are fundamentally inadequate for physical interventions. This suggests a potential gap between clinical and engineering perspectives that policymakers should investigate.

Historical Precedent for AI Failure — AANA's citation of SEDASYS provides a concrete, named example of autonomous surgical AI commercial failure, offering policymakers a case study for what not to do.

Proactive vs. Reactive AI — AANA articulates a vision of AI that predicts complications before they occur rather than responding to them—a shift from reactive to proactive care that could fundamentally change surgical safety paradigms.

Physical AI as Distinct Category — MediView XR argues that Physical AI operating in real-time with spatial awareness is fundamentally different from diagnostic AI, suggesting current regulatory categories may be inadequate.

Rural Access Implications — AANA notes that CRNAs are sole anesthesia providers in nearly 100% of rural hospitals in some states, meaning AI policy decisions will disproportionately affect rural healthcare access.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder Type Correlates with Safety Focus: Surgeons focus on system-level auditability, nurses on patient and staff safety protocols, anesthesia providers on workflow integration and clinical adaptability, manufacturers on regulatory clarity and human-in-loop design, and technical experts on hardware-level response times.

Experience with Automation Shapes AI Attitudes: Commenters with extensive experience with existing automation (such as AANA's experience with infusion pumps and monitoring systems) express more nuanced views—acknowledging both benefits and limitations—compared to those focused on emerging AI capabilities.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Without automated extraction, clinicians won't maintain procedural logs even when beneficial. Unclear regulatory pathways may prevent beneficial AI tools from reaching market. AI policies designed for well-resourced settings may not work where CRNAs are sole providers.

Convergence Across Stakeholder Types: Despite different professional perspectives, commenters converge on four points: human oversight is essential, current auditability is inadequate, AI should integrate multiple data streams, and standards are needed but don't yet exist.

keyQuotations.quote: Advanced, AI-enabled and networked surgical systems integrate devices, software, cloud platforms, networks, and clinician decision-making in real time—creating unique auditability challenges.
In physical care, such as robotic surgery, a software loop taking 20 to 50 milliseconds to recognize an error and issue a stop command can result in unintended tissue damage.
AI is most effective when it enhances clinician expertise, improves consistency, and integrates seamlessly into clinical workflows.

Physical AI differs in that it operates in real time, maintains spatial awareness of anatomy and instruments, preserves human-in-the-loop control, and directly influences procedural execution and outcomes.
Early automated sedation systems like SEDASYS failed commercially because they lacked sufficient adaptability to real-world clinical complexity and variability.
When adverse events, unexpected outcomes, or performance questions arise in surgical systems, it is often difficult to reconstruct what actually occurred.
If process were simple and automated, more clinicians would maintain logs to iterate on technique and refine practice patterns.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific technical and clinical issues, cite concrete examples and evidence, and offer actionable recommendations. Stakeholders bring distinct professional expertise and avoid purely rhetorical arguments.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several comments cite specific examples (SEDASYS failure, technical response time requirements) and professional experience, though formal research citations are limited. The evidence is largely experiential and practice-based rather than peer-reviewed.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient advocacy groups and health system administrators are notably absent from the comments. No comments from liability insurers or legal experts who might address accountability frameworks. International perspectives on surgical AI regulation are not represented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of engineering safety requirements, clinical workflow integration, regulatory frameworks, and accountability structures across multiple stakeholder types with different priorities and expertise.

15.5.themeDescription: Primary Care and Chronic Disease Management AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public input reveals strong consensus that AI holds transformative potential for primary care and chronic disease management, with commenters across all stakeholder groups citing evidence of reduced hospitalizations, improved outcomes, and enhanced care coordination. However, significant tensions exist between the promise of AI-enabled care and structural barriers including inadequate reimbursement, fragmented data systems, and chronic underinvestment in primary care infrastructure. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on expanding CMS reimbursement pathways, prioritizing interoperability, and ensuring AI tools are validated for the specific populations they serve—particularly vulnerable groups in long-term care, Medicaid, and community health settings.

consensusPoints.text: AI Can Meaningfully Reduce Hospitalizations and Emergency Department Utilization. TapestryHealth reports 43% reduction in ED visits and 45% reduction in inpatient admissions using AI + clinician model in long-term care settings. Waymark achieved 20.4% reduction in avoidable ED visits and 48.3% reduction in 30-day hospitalizations for Medicaid populations. NeuroFlow documented reductions in ED utilization when measurement-informed behavioral health care was systematically applied in primary care settings.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters providing outcome data (6 of 6)

exceptions.text: ECRI raises important concerns that some widely-used predictive models (e.g., sepsis prediction) have performed substantially worse than expected in real-world evaluation, suggesting outcome claims require independent validation.

consensusPoints.text: Primary Care Remains Chronically Under-Resourced Despite Evidence of Value. AAFP states that despite overwhelming evidence that primary care is the only health care component where increased supply is associated with better population health and improved patient outcomes, the U.S. continues to underinvest. Community Care Cooperative notes that primary care is the only specialty consistently shown to reduce total cost of care through prevention, early detection, and longitudinal chronic disease management yet remains chronically under-resourced. AAFP adds that in many large organizations, primary care is still treated as a loss leader to generate referrals to high-margin specialty and hospital services.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (particularly provider organizations and advocacy groups)

consensusPoints.text: Expanded CMS Reimbursement Pathways Are Essential for AI Adoption. Ellipsis Health recommends expanding CMS reimbursement pathways for AI-supported assessment, monitoring, and outreach, particularly in chronic disease management and transitional care. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi echoes this recommendation. TapestryHealth notes that current payment models financially penalize their efficient AI-driven care model.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Multiple commenters across business, provider, and research sectors (at least 4 explicitly)

consensusPoints.text: AI Must Support Longitudinal, Whole-Person Care Across Disease Stages. Heart Failure Society of America emphasizes that AI-enabled care should address the full heart failure continuum from Stage A (at-risk) through advanced heart failure including surveillance, prevention, early detection, treatment optimization, and advanced care. National MS Society notes AI can integrate diverse data sources including genetic databases, clinical studies, and real-world evidence to improve understanding of disease heterogeneity, progression, and treatment response.

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Approach to AI in Primary Care
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the appropriate level of regulatory oversight for AI tools in primary care settings

positions.label: Light-Touch Regulation
positions.stance: Business commenters including ScriptChain Health and SanLuca AI argue that AI providing nutrition and lifestyle interventions should not be regulated, that bureaucratic barriers create 12-18 month approval delays, and that innovation is stifled by excessive oversight.
positions.supportLevel: Business commenters
keyArguments: AI providing nutrition and lifestyle interventions should not be regulated
Bureaucratic barriers create 12-18 month approval delays
Innovation is stifled by excessive oversight

positions.label: Rigorous Validation Requirements
positions.stance: Independent research organizations like ECRI and patient advocacy groups argue that widely-used AI models have failed real-world validation, additional prospective studies are needed to confirm accuracy, and patient safety requires independent verification before deployment.
positions.supportLevel: Independent research organizations, patient advocacy groups
keyArguments: Widely-used AI models have failed real-world validation
Additional prospective studies needed to confirm accuracy
Patient safety requires independent verification before deployment

areasOfDebate.topic: Priority Disease Areas for AI Investment
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over which chronic disease areas should receive priority AI investment and development focus

positions.label: Cardiovascular/Heart Failure Focus
positions.stance: Heart Failure Society of America and New Jersey Innovation Institute advocate for prioritizing cardiovascular and heart failure AI applications.
positions.supportLevel: Heart Failure Society of America, New Jersey Innovation Institute
keyArguments: 6.7 million Americans affected with 24% lifetime risk
Clear evidence that optimal therapy could prevent 1.19 million deaths globally
Existing ARPA-H investment through ADVOCATE program

positions.label: Diabetes/Metabolic Disease Focus
positions.stance: Individual caregivers and ScriptChain Health advocate for prioritizing diabetes and metabolic disease AI applications.
positions.supportLevel: Individual caregivers, ScriptChain Health
keyArguments: High prevalence of diabetes
180-300 daily health decisions for T1D patients
AI could reduce trial-and-error burden
Opportunity for prevention through nutrition interventions

positions.label: Behavioral Health Integration Focus
positions.stance: NeuroFlow and Ellipsis Health advocate for prioritizing behavioral health integration in primary care AI.
positions.supportLevel: NeuroFlow, Ellipsis Health
keyArguments: Behavioral and psychosocial signals often precede high-cost medical events
Primary care lacks specialty behavioral health expertise
Proven reductions in ED utilization

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over how much autonomous decision-making AI systems should have in primary care delivery

positions.label: AI as Clinician Support
positions.stance: Most provider organizations and advocacy groups including AAFP and HFSA argue AI should surface actionable clinical insights and support population health management while keeping clinicians in control, particularly important in high-risk conditions like heart failure.
positions.supportLevel: Most provider organizations, advocacy groups
keyArguments: AI should surface actionable clinical insights
AI should support population health management while keeping clinicians in control
Particularly important in high-risk conditions like heart failure

positions.label: AI-Enabled Autonomous Care
positions.stance: Some technology companies including Pair Team and Waymark argue that AI-first approaches can address provider shortages, with autonomous systems achieving higher contact rates and enabling scaling to underserved populations.
positions.supportLevel: Some technology companies
keyArguments: AI-first approach can address provider shortages
Autonomous systems achieve higher contact rates (41.2% vs. 10-30% traditional)
Enables scaling to underserved populations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payment model misalignment penalizing efficient AI-driven care; fragmented EMR integration blocking automated referral coordination; need for interoperability standards
specificPoints: Direct operational experience with measurable outcomes—these commenters provide the most specific quantitative evidence (e.g., 43% ED reduction, $3.73:1 ROI, 4.99-5.65 day predictive lead time)
Propose expanding CMS reimbursement pathways and aligning STAR measures with AI-enabled outcomes
TapestryHealth's 80,000+ patient monitoring program demonstrates scalable AI implementation
Waymark serves 175,000+ high-risk Medicaid members
Community Care Cooperative operates across 50+ FQHCs in 15 states

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI tools must be validated for specific disease populations; treatment optimization to reduce trial-and-error prescribing; ensuring AI improves rather than harms vulnerable populations
specificPoints: Emphasis on patient-centered outcomes and the lived experience of managing chronic conditions—particularly the daily decision burden and emotional toll
Propose AI-assisted treatment personalization, education modules for patients and providers, and drug interaction flagging
National MS Society represents 1 million Americans with multiple sclerosis
APDA's Voices of Parkinson's Council provided direct patient input
HFSA focuses on GDMT optimization for heart failure patients

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI adoption barriers for small, independent, rural, and safety-net practices; administrative burden that AI could alleviate or worsen; need for AI to strengthen rather than undermine primary care
specificPoints: AAFP uniquely frames AI through Dr. Barbara Starfield's four key primary care functions: first contact, comprehensiveness, continuity, and coordination
Focus HHS resources on AI tools that reduce documentation burden, support population health management, and improve care coordination
Enable meaningful, low-burden reporting measures
AAFP represents 128,300 family physicians

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Daily decision burden of chronic disease management (180-300 decisions/day for T1D); stress and mental health impacts; variability in individual response to treatment
specificPoints: Deeply personal account of managing disease across multiple family members
Emphasis on the trial-and-error nature of current care and the trauma of disease onset
Propose AI tools to relieve decision-making stress and education modules for patients and providers
Dominick Esposito manages T1D for two teenagers, father, and mother-in-law

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Need for independent validation of AI tools; evidence quality and bias in existing studies; translating diagnostic accuracy to actual patient outcomes
specificPoints: ECRI provides critical counterbalance to industry claims, noting that widely-used tools have failed real-world validation
Propose prospective studies comparing outcomes with and without AI
Independent validation of diagnostic performance required
Prioritize predictive analytics with strong evidence base
ECRI evaluated TREWS and ImmunoScore sepsis tools
NJII cites JAMA Internal Medicine study on heart failure readmission prediction

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty; bureaucratic barriers to health system partnerships; need for clear pathways for AI-enabled nutrition and lifestyle interventions
specificPoints: ICF brings federal contracting experience, proposing specific integration with existing CMS models like Kidney Care Choices
ScriptChain emphasizes Food is Medicine as underexplored AI priority
Propose HHS quotas for public-private partnerships and healthcare reimbursement for nutritional technologies
ScriptChain reports 12-18 month approval delays for health system partnerships

noteworthyInsights.insight: Predictive lead time as key metric - TapestryHealth quantifies that their contactless monitoring provides average predictive lead time of 4.99 to 5.65 days prior to hospitalization event—a concrete, actionable timeframe that could inform standards for AI-enabled early warning systems.

AI outreach dramatically outperforms traditional methods - Waymark reports AI-driven outreach achieves 41.2% contact rate compared to 10-30% for traditional care management, and AI-driven messaging personalization improves response rates by 40-60%. This suggests AI's value may be as much in patient engagement as clinical decision support.

The 180-300 daily decisions burden - Individual caregiver Dominick Esposito provides a striking quantification of chronic disease management burden: people with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day. This frames AI's potential value not just in clinical terms but in quality of life and caregiver sustainability.

Starfield's four functions as AI framework - AAFP uniquely proposes evaluating AI through Dr. Barbara Starfield's four key primary care functions: first contact, comprehensiveness, continuity, and coordination. This provides a patient-centered framework distinct from disease-specific or efficiency metrics.

Behavioral health as upstream predictor - Ellipsis Health and NeuroFlow both emphasize that behavioral, emotional, and psychosocial signals often precede high-cost medical events—suggesting AI investment in behavioral health integration may have outsized returns for chronic disease management.

Crashing into disease as preventable trauma - Dominick Esposito describes how one son was monitored for years after antibody detection before becoming insulin dependent, while noting not everyone who could develop T1D is tested and crashing into T1D is a very traumatic event. This highlights AI's potential role in disease prediction and staged intervention.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Long-term care facilities face unique challenges with high-acuity, low-staffing, multi-chronic populations that differ significantly from ambulatory or acute hospital settings, as noted by TapestryHealth.
FQHCs and community health centers are chronically under-resourced and serve populations at highest risk of harm from AI trained on non-representative data, according to Community Care Cooperative.
Rural settings need AI supporting decision-making about local management versus transfer to higher-acuity care.
Multi-state Medicaid operations face an emerging patchwork of state AI requirements that impede multi-state scaling, as Waymark reports.
Medicaid/Medicare beneficiaries are explicitly identified as populations where AI can solve gaps in access, quality, cost and efficiency, according to Pair Team.
Elder care populations require AI specifically designed for their high-acuity, low-staffing, and multi-chronic nature, as TapestryHealth emphasizes.
Patients with rare/complex conditions like MS and Parkinson's need AI validated by disease specialists to improve care from providers without specialized expertise, according to APDA.
AI could worsen administrative burden if not carefully designed—AAFP notes AI could either alleviate or worsen documentation burden.
Efficient AI-driven care may be financially penalized under current payment models, as TapestryHealth explicitly states.
False alerts from poorly validated AI could cause alert fatigue and missed true cases, according to ECRI's evaluation.

Provider organizations and technology companies align on need for expanded reimbursement and interoperability, while patient advocacy groups and independent researchers align on need for rigorous validation and population-specific testing.

keyQuotations.quote: It is established that people with T1D make 180 to 300 additional health-related decisions a day. We would love a tool to relieve the stress of making these decisions.

Fewer than one in four eligible patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction receive comprehensive quadruple guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT), despite clear evidence of its benefit.

At Kaiser Permanente, patients have said that getting into the nutrition programs are very difficult to get into, and the waitlist can take anywhere from 3-12 months. At that rate, you are closer to your deathbed than anything else.
Unfortunately, not everyone who could develop T1D is tested. And, more unfortunately, 'crashing into' T1D is a very traumatic event for people with this disease and their loved ones.

Despite overwhelming evidence that primary care is only health care component where increased supply is associated with better population health and improved patient outcomes, U.S. continues to underinvest.
In many large organizations, primary care still treated as loss leader to generate referrals to high-margin specialty and hospital services.
Primary care is the only specialty consistently shown to reduce total cost of care through prevention, early detection, and longitudinal chronic disease management.
The highest-cost healthcare events—ED utilization, hospitalizations, readmissions, and unmanaged chronic disease—are often preceded by unaddressed behavioral, physical, or psychosocial signals.
Widely used proprietary sepsis prediction model performed substantially worse than expected in real-world evaluation—missed many true cases and generated false alerts.

AI model can flag heart failure patient on high-dose diuretics whose respiratory rate has subtly increased over 48 hours—a pattern often missed by routine checks but highly predictive of impending fluid overload crisis.
PD patients report medication management feels like trial-and-error, indicating need for AI-assisted treatment optimization.
AI-driven messaging personalization improves response rates by 40-60% compared to generic outreach.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific evidence, quantitative outcomes, and concrete policy recommendations. Multiple stakeholder types provide complementary perspectives with specific data points and real-world implementation experience.

evidenceBase.explanation: Provider and technology company comments include specific quantitative outcomes (e.g., 43% ED reduction, 41.2% contact rates). However, ECRI raises important concerns about independent validation, noting that some widely-used AI tools have failed real-world evaluation despite initial claims.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct input from patients themselves (only 2 comments from one individual caregiver). Rural and safety-net provider perspectives are mentioned but not directly represented. Payer perspectives are notably absent despite significant discussion of reimbursement issues.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves intersecting issues of payment model reform, interoperability standards, clinical validation requirements, and population-specific AI development needs. Stakeholders present nuanced positions that acknowledge both AI's potential and significant implementation barriers.

16.1.themeDescription: Clinical Documentation and Ambient AI Scribes. This sub-theme addresses AI for clinical documentation, including ambient listening tools, AI scribes, and documentation assistance. || It includes discussions of ambient documentation benefits, concerns about documentation accuracy, debates about clinician review requirements, and proposals for documentation AI standards. This excludes billing and scheduling and focuses specifically on clinical documentation

sections.executiveSummary: Public comment on clinical documentation AI reveals remarkable consensus that ambient scribes and documentation tools represent one of healthcare AI's most successful and widely adopted applications, with commenters across all stakeholder groups citing measurable reductions in clinician burnout and documentation time. However, this enthusiasm is tempered by significant concerns about accuracy—including documented hallucinations, specialty-specific content gaps, and the absence of technical mechanisms to verify human authorship. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for continued adoption with enhanced oversight, integration standards, and third-party accreditation rather than restrictive regulation.

consensusPoints.text: Documentation AI is among the most successful clinical AI applications to date. Nearly all commenters characterize ambient documentation tools as meeting or exceeding expectations, representing the clearest success story in clinical AI adoption. SanLuca AI notes documentation burden reduction has met or exceeded expectations with measurable time savings and clinician experience improvements. A Healthcare Consultant describes ambient AI documentation tools as one of most well-evidenced applications of AI in clinical care today. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing reports ambient scribe tools have exceeded performance impact for community behavioral health providers.

exceptions.text: Even strong proponents acknowledge accuracy limitations requiring continued clinician oversight

consensusPoints.text: Documentation burden is a primary driver of clinician burnout. Commenters explicitly link administrative documentation requirements to workforce burnout, positioning ambient AI as a direct intervention for this crisis. Massive Bio reported Mass General Brigham saw about a 40% relative drop in physician burnout during an AI scribe pilot. A Pre-med Student notes administrative overhead from charting is a critical contributor to physician burnout. The Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association observes ambient documentation shows strong early adoption and impact on healthcare provider burden and pajama time.

Human oversight must remain mandatory. All commenters assume or explicitly endorse continued clinician review of AI-generated documentation, with no commenter advocating for fully autonomous documentation. SonderMind emphasizes every AI-generated note should be fully editable by provider before finalization. Community Care Cooperative states such tools must preserve clinician judgment and accountability. A Healthcare Consultant notes AI scribe accuracy varies and frequently requires manual edits, reinforcing need for continued clinician oversight even in well-performing applications.

Documentation AI enables clinicians to focus on patients rather than screens. Commenters frame the primary benefit as restoring the patient-physician relationship by redirecting attention from computer screens to direct patient engagement. A Pre-med Student suggests by integrating such technologies into the clinical workflow, HHS can help redirect the physician's attention back to the patient rather than the computer screen. AAFP notes these tools support more meaningful interactions between patients and physician-led care teams in primary care settings. The American Parkinson Disease Association expresses interest in AI tools that record clinical visits and generate notes without provider typing, enabling increased engagement between provider and patient.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on the appropriate level of regulatory oversight for documentation AI tools

positions.label: Minimal Regulation
positions.stance: Administrative AI tools should face lighter regulatory requirements than clinical decision-making AI. A mental health clinician/developer and AI consulting firms support this view.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of business commenters and several healthcare providers
keyArguments: Documentation tools are lower-risk than diagnostic AI
Over-regulation could slow adoption of proven beneficial technology
Existing clinical oversight provides adequate safeguard

positions.label: Structured Standards
positions.stance: Documentation AI requires specific standards, accreditation, and evidence infrastructure. GLACIS Technologies physician, MHK, and Writerslogic advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Medical specialty associations, safety-focused organizations, and some technology vendors
keyArguments: Documentation errors can propagate through medical records with serious consequences
Hallucinations have been documented
No current mechanism to trace failures or verify authorship

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether indirect value justifies investment or whether federal reimbursement support is needed

positions.label: Indirect Value Sufficient
positions.stance: ROI manifests through retention, burnout reduction, and workforce sustainability rather than direct cost savings. Epic Systems and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association support this view.
positions.supportLevel: EHR vendors, health systems, several business commenters
keyArguments: True ROI of AI scribes appears downstream through happier clinicians less likely to burn out or leave, reducing recruitment and onboarding costs
Difficult to quantify hard savings beyond scribe substitution

positions.label: Reimbursement Needed
positions.stance: Federal incentives and payment reform required to accelerate adoption. A Healthcare consultant, Community Care Cooperative, and AAFP advocate for this position.
positions.supportLevel: Healthcare consultants, trade associations, community health organizations
keyArguments: Early adopters found limited financial ROI initially
Smaller practices and safety-net providers cannot absorb costs without support
Current reimbursement structures don't recognize AI-enabled efficiency

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether seamless EHR integration is essential or standalone tools provide acceptable value

positions.label: Seamless Integration Essential
positions.stance: Tools must function as plug-and-play extensions of existing workflows. The Penn Neurosurgery team and American Society of Hematology strongly support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Academic medical centers, practicing clinicians, specialty associations
keyArguments: Manual transfer of AI outputs negates efficiency gains
If an AI tool does not function as a plug-and-play extension of existing workflows, adoption is unlikely regardless of its theoretical benefits

positions.label: Standalone Tools Acceptable
positions.stance: Free or low-cost tools without full integration still provide value. The Penn Neurosurgery team acknowledges this reality while critiquing it.
positions.supportLevel: Limited explicit support; some acknowledgment of current reality
keyArguments: Doximity tool is free and widely accessible even without EMR integration
Some value better than none during transition period

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Documentation accuracy, workflow integration, time savings, burnout reduction. Direct experience with both benefits and limitations; report specific time savings (80% reduction in note completion, 90 minutes saved daily) alongside accuracy concerns requiring manual edits.
specificPoints: Medvidi reports clinicians edit AI-generated notes in under two minutes instead of 10-15 minutes
InStride Health conducted Wharton-collaborated case study documenting measurable reductions in clinician documentation burden
University of Kansas Health System confirms ambient listening tools show significant success reducing clinician documentation burden
Proposed solutions include prioritizing seamless EHR integration, maintaining human-in-the-loop review, and focusing on tools that genuinely reduce rather than shift administrative burden

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Specialty-specific accuracy gaps, liability exposure, member practice variability, reimbursement alignment. Aggregate member experiences revealing systematic patterns; identify specialty-specific limitations in neurology content gaps and dermatology coding concerns.
specificPoints: American Academy of Neurology reports significant content gaps for neurology among ambient scribes compared to primary care—text generated often fails to distinguish important clinical details like different types of headache or seizures
AAFP supports HHS prioritizing research on AI products that streamline documentation
AMA recommends documentation support that improves accuracy and compliance without encouraging note bloat that obscures clinical meaning
Proposed solutions include specialty-specific validation requirements, integration with prior authorization workflows, and research prioritization for documentation AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity, market adoption barriers, interoperability standards, evidence infrastructure. Technical implementation challenges; identify infrastructure gaps preventing failure analysis; propose specific technical solutions.
specificPoints: GLACIS Technologies physician experienced hallucination on first use: AI stated recommendation for lamotrigine for PTSD which was nowhere in the transcript, with no way to trace the failure
Scientix.ai notes clinicians report spending up to three hours per week correcting or bypassing AI outputs due to missing context
Epic Systems observes true ROI of AI scribes appears downstream through retention rather than direct savings
Proposed solutions include third-party accreditation, evidence infrastructure for tracing AI outputs, open standards for authorship verification, and data substrate investment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence base development, data privacy, informed consent, rural/underserved access. Systematic review of evidence; identification of research gaps; privacy and consent frameworks.
specificPoints: Computing Research Association raises questions about how data is being stored, what data is being used for, and how informed consent can be ensured
RUPRI Health Panel emphasizes rural context where ambient listening to populate health records is a means of coping with workforce shortages
Healthcare consultant cites systematic review showing 9 of 10 studies reported efficiency improvements and 7 of 10 reported positive effects on clinician wellness
Proposed solutions include prioritizing research on documentation AI, addressing data storage and consent questions, and ensuring rural applicability

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Enhanced patient-provider engagement during visits. Patient-centered framing of benefits; focus on relationship quality rather than efficiency metrics.
specificPoints: American Parkinson Disease Association expresses interest in AI tools that record clinical visits and generate notes without provider typing during visit, enabling increased engagement between provider and patient
Proposed solutions include supporting tools that enable providers to focus on patients rather than typing

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Real-world workflow impact, future practice environment, direct patient care time. Ground-level observations of current documentation burden; future workforce perspective.
specificPoints: Pre-med student from shadowing observed doctors spending hours on charting, often extending beyond scheduled clinical shifts
Critical care nurse confirms experience with documentation assistance tools embedded in EHR and identifies reducing documentation burden as one of the most promising AI use cases
Proposed solutions include widespread adoption of ambient listening tools and integration into clinical workflow

noteworthyInsights.insight: First-use hallucination reveals systemic gap: A practicing physician and Chief Medical Officer testing an ambient AI scribe for their healthcare organization experienced a hallucination on the very first use—the AI fabricated a medication recommendation that appeared nowhere in the transcript. More concerning than the error itself was the complete inability to trace what went wrong, suggesting current deployment may be outpacing safety infrastructure.

Specialty-specific performance disparities: The American Academy of Neurology reveals that ambient scribes optimized for primary care encounters systematically underperform in specialty contexts, failing to capture clinically significant distinctions like different types of headache or seizures. This suggests a potential equity issue where specialists and their patients may receive lower-quality AI assistance.

The pajama time paradox: Multiple commenters note that while ambient scribes reduce after-hours documentation work, this doesn't directly offset technology costs. Epic Systems articulates this clearly: less pajama time for providers does not by itself offset the cost of the technology, with true ROI appearing downstream through retention and reduced burnout. This creates a challenge for traditional ROI calculations and reimbursement justification.

Patient advocacy alignment: The sole patient advocacy organization commenting (American Parkinson Disease Association) frames documentation AI benefits entirely through the lens of enhanced patient-provider engagement rather than efficiency metrics—suggesting patient priorities may differ from provider and payer framings.

Quantified time savings: SonderMind provides concrete metrics showing their AI documentation tool reduces note completion time by 80%, saving providers up to 90 minutes per day with 80%+ adoption among eligible providers and over 100,000 notes generated since September 2025. This represents one of the most specific adoption and impact datasets in the comments.

Authorship verification as emerging legal risk: Writerslogic raises a concern no other commenter addresses—the physician signature at the bottom of a note attests presence but not authorship, with no technical mechanism to answer whether a human clinician actually composed documentation or ratified output they did not write. This anticipates future liability challenges.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural contexts: RUPRI Health Panel emphasizes that ambient documentation serves as a means of coping with workforce shortages in rural areas, suggesting different value propositions across settings

Safety-net providers: Community Care Cooperative notes that FQHCs serving underserved populations have expressed strong interest but face unique adoption barriers and risks from tools trained on non-representative data
Academic medical centers report more cautious, limited rollouts with emphasis on integration challenges, as seen in Penn's Dragon Copilot limited to small number of beta users
Telehealth and behavioral health providers report highest adoption rates and most enthusiastic endorsements, possibly due to documentation-heavy nature of mental health encounters
Community oncology: VieCure notes ambient AI scribes are especially valuable in oncology where decision-making and documentation burden is high
Primary care tools appear optimized for and most successful in primary care settings, while neurology shows documented content gaps and accuracy concerns
Note bloat risk: AMA warns documentation AI should not encourage note bloat that obscures clinical meaning
New administrative burdens: Penguin Ai cautions that tools must provide genuine relief for documentation burdens rather than introducing new administrative tasks
Adoption slowdown from safety concerns: GLACIS Technologies warns that without evidence infrastructure, the rational response would be to slow or stop AI adoption entirely

Direct implementation experience correlates with more nuanced views acknowledging both benefits and limitations; vendors with physician leadership like GLACIS and Medvidi provide most detailed accuracy concerns alongside endorsements

keyQuotations.quote: If implemented and managed appropriately, the AAFP believes AI could deepen, not disintermediate, the patient-physician relationship by allowing physicians to spend more time engaging with their patients instead of clicking boxes on a screen.
By integrating such technologies into the clinical workflow, HHS can help redirect the physician's attention back to the patient, rather than the computer screen.

AI-powered clinical documentation tool reduces note completion time by 80%, saving providers up to 90 minutes per day... adopted by 80%+ of eligible providers... generated over 100,000 notes submitted to health insurers.
Mass General Brigham reported about a 40% relative drop in physician burnout during an AI scribe pilot.

The first time I used it, it actually generated a hallucination in the note. It stated that I had recommended lamotrigine for PTSD which was nowhere in the transcript that I was able to go back and check word for word... I had no way to trace back through the system to understand where that failure occurred.

AI-generated notes frequently omit or erroneously report information from patient visit... text generated often fails to distinguish important clinical details like different types of headache or seizures.

The physician signature at the bottom of a note attests presence but not authorship. Under current conditions, no technical mechanism exists to answer the question a court, a regulator, or a patient's family will eventually ask: did a human clinician actually compose this, or did they ratify output they did not write?

In real-world clinical environments, if an AI tool does not function as a plug-and-play extension of existing workflows that creates more opportunity for the clinician to directly engage with the patient, adoption is unlikely, regardless of its theoretical benefits.

Less 'pajama time' for providers, however, does not by itself offset the cost of the technology. The true ROI of AI scribes appears downstream: happier clinicians are less likely to burn out or leave, reducing recruitment and onboarding costs.

Ambient listening to populate health records is a means of coping with workforce shortages... AI tools enabling clinicians to spend less time on administrative tasks would have greatest impact on access to quality care.
Workflow-aware clinical documentation support that measurably reduces administrative burden without introducing silent errors is a high-potential tool category.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific implementation experiences, quantified outcomes, and nuanced acknowledgment of both benefits and limitations. Multiple stakeholders cite systematic reviews and concrete metrics rather than abstract positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Strong anecdotal and organizational evidence with specific metrics (80% time reduction, 40% burnout reduction, 100,000+ notes generated). Systematic review cited showing 9 of 10 studies reporting efficiency improvements. However, limited peer-reviewed clinical trial data and no standardized outcome measures across implementations.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient advocacy perspective limited to single comment from American Parkinson Disease Association. No direct patient voices or consumer advocacy groups represented. Payer perspective notably absent. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives present but limited.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: Moderate complexity with clear consensus on benefits but substantive debate on regulatory approach, ROI measurement, and integration requirements. Technical concerns about traceability and accountability add nuance beyond simple adoption questions.

16.2.themeDescription: Revenue Cycle and Claims Processing AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal deep concern about an emerging asymmetry in healthcare AI: payers are deploying AI to accelerate claim denials while simultaneously contractually prohibiting providers from using AI to respond, creating what multiple commenters call a "bad faith" dynamic. There is strong consensus that AI holds significant promise for reducing administrative burden and fraud, but commenters are sharply divided on whether current payer AI practices constitute legitimate efficiency gains or systematic denial of medically necessary care. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for federal intervention to establish transparency requirements, prohibit discriminatory AI-prohibition clauses, and create accountability mechanisms for AI-driven coverage decisions.

consensusPoints.text: Administrative AI holds significant potential for efficiency gains. Nearly all commenters acknowledge that AI can meaningfully reduce administrative burden in billing, coding, and claims processing. This spans healthcare providers, technology vendors, advocacy groups, and trade associations. The American Medical Association notes AI can streamline eligibility and benefits verification by reducing back and forth required to confirm coverage details. Advocate Health identifies billing, supply chain optimization, and clinical data abstraction as areas of highest potential impact. The American Academy of Dermatology Association states AI empowers dermatologists and coding staff to increase successful claims and results in fewer coding errors, more timely claim submissions, and smoother revenue cycle.

exceptions.text: Even enthusiastic supporters emphasize the need for oversight and human-in-the-loop safeguards.

consensusPoints.text: Payer AI use in coverage decisions requires regulatory oversight. A strong majority of commenters explicitly call for federal oversight of AI used by payers for prior authorization and claims processing, with no commenters defending the current unregulated status quo. CommonSpirit Health warns that black box systems used by payers raise profound ethical, legal, and patient safety concerns. The American Cancer Society Cancer Action Network recommends HHS and CMS should create clear and consistent regulatory guardrails for transparency and accountability in AI used for coverage decisions. The American Society of Retina Specialists urges HHS to restrict or prohibit AI use by payers for prior authorization and punitive physician auditing.

Prior authorization is a critical pain point requiring AI-enabled reform. Most healthcare providers and physician organizations identify prior authorization as a major source of administrative burden and patient harm, with AI positioned as both a potential solution and a potential exacerbator. The AMA states prior authorization is one of the most significant drivers of physician burden and delays in care. Dr. Raymond Kordonowy advocates that prior authorization should be automated with approvals returned in seconds, not days.
