Massive Bio recommends modernizing regulations to recognize AI-based determinations with automatic approval when evidence-based criteria are met.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and physician organizations (at least 8 commenters)

areasOfDebate.topic: Payer AI Prohibition Clauses in Provider Contracts
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether payers should be allowed to contractually restrict providers from using AI in revenue cycle management

positions.label: Ban the Bans
positions.stance: Prohibit payers from contractually restricting provider AI use. University of Kansas Health System calls this a bad faith approach to contracting. WellSpan Health calls for federal ban on payer-mandated AI-prohibition clauses.
positions.supportLevel: Strong support from healthcare providers
keyArguments: Creates fundamental unfairness where payers use high-speed AI to find reasons to deny while forcing providers to use manual, expensive, and slow processes
Broad AI definitions create chilling effect on basic automation
Undermines administrative efficiency

positions.label: No Explicit Defense
positions.stance: No commenters explicitly defended payer AI-prohibition clauses. Payer organizations did not submit comments in this dataset, leaving this perspective unrepresented.
positions.supportLevel: Zero commenters

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Scope of AI in Coverage Decisions
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI should be restricted or regulated in prior authorization and claims denial processes

positions.label: Restrict Payer AI
positions.stance: Limit or prohibit AI use in prior authorization and claims denials. Supported by American Society of Retina Specialists, Global Liver Institute, and The Interstitial Institute.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of healthcare providers and advocacy groups (at least 7 commenters)
keyArguments: Time-sensitive care decisions require clinical nuance
AI lacks transparency and appeal rights
Documented cases of mass denials with minimal human review
94% of physicians report negative clinical outcomes from AI-driven denials

positions.label: Regulate, Don't Prohibit
positions.stance: Allow AI with transparency, oversight, and accountability requirements. MHK recommends third-party accreditation for prior authorization using AI with audit trails demonstrating human-in-the-loop governance. Cooperative Exchange emphasizes their AI is designed for Administrative Acceleration, not Clinical Restriction with HITL overrides hard-coded into the transaction path.
positions.supportLevel: Several technology vendors and some provider organizations (at least 5 commenters)
keyArguments: AI can improve efficiency if properly governed
Human-in-the-loop requirements can address concerns
Third-party accreditation can ensure accountability

areasOfDebate.topic: AI for Fraud Prevention vs. Legitimate Claims Processing
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the appropriate use of AI for fraud detection versus concerns about misuse in denying legitimate claims

positions.label: Aggressive Fraud Prevention
positions.stance: Deploy AI to detect and prevent healthcare fraud at scale. Advanced Interactive Technology Holdings proposes SBIR topics for AI-Powered Real-Time Medicare Claims Validation with greater than 95% detection rate.
positions.supportLevel: Technology vendors and some policy analysts (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Current system loses $85B annually to fraud
AI can achieve 8:1 ROI on prevention
Real-time validation can process 15M+ claims daily

positions.label: Caution on Fraud Framing
positions.stance: Distinguish fraud prevention from legitimate utilization management. Global Liver Institute notes majority of appealed denials are overturned, suggesting many initial decisions are wrong.
positions.supportLevel: Healthcare providers and advocacy groups (implicit in multiple comments)
keyArguments: Fraud prevention rhetoric can mask inappropriate denials of legitimate claims
Majority of appealed denials are overturned, suggesting initial decisions are wrong
Patients bear burden of proving legitimacy

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payer AI-prohibition clauses creating unfair competitive disadvantage; lack of transparency in payer AI denial decisions; administrative burden from manual processes
specificPoints: Direct operational experience with both implementing AI tools and responding to AI-driven denials; see both sides of the AI equation

University of Kansas Health System describes insurance companies adding contract language that specifically prohibits the use of AI in revenue cycle management while using high-speed AI to find reasons to deny those same claims
WellSpan Health calls for federal ban on payer-mandated AI-prohibition clauses
Proposed solutions include federal ban on AI-prohibition clauses, regulatory oversight of payer AI, and standardized API-driven interoperability for prior authorization

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Payer AI downcoding claims without clinical review; liability exposure for AI coding tools; barriers to AI adoption for smaller practices
specificPoints: Represent collective physician experience across specialties; can quantify impacts through member surveys
American Academy of Dermatology reports payers using AI to expedite claims review are increasingly downcoding E/M claims without requesting clinical records
AMA survey shows 61% of physicians report AI is increasing prior authorization denials
Proposed solutions include enhanced regulatory oversight of payer downcoding, post-marketing surveillance of AI coding tools, and standardized interoperability requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI-driven denials harming vulnerable patient populations; embedding historical inequities at scale; lack of transparency and appeal rights
specificPoints: Focus on patient outcomes rather than operational efficiency; emphasize consequences for chronic disease populations
Global Liver Institute warns algorithms trained on historical spending patterns rather than clinical need risk embedding past inequities at scale
ACS CAN cites 2023 Cigna lawsuit alleging AI-based system led to improper denial of hundreds of thousands of pre-approved insurance claims
Proposed solutions include clear regulatory guardrails for AI in coverage decisions, transparency requirements, and accountability mechanisms

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity for AI deployment; reimbursement pathways; interoperability standards; liability frameworks
specificPoints: Technical understanding of AI capabilities and limitations; experience implementing human-in-the-loop safeguards
MHK emphasizes significant concern about batch denials caused by automation and recommends third-party accreditation for prior authorization using AI with audit trails
Cooperative Exchange clarifies their AI is designed for Administrative Acceleration, not Clinical Restriction
Proposed solutions include third-party accreditation programs, CMMI pilot programs for administrative efficiency, and simplified approval pathways with clinical oversight requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance gaps in AI deployment; interpretive drift in medical necessity criteria; lack of accountability architecture
specificPoints: Systems-level analysis of governance failures; documentation of specific algorithmic harms

The Interstitial Institute documents UnitedHealthcare's nH Predict with approximately 90% error rate and Cigna's PXDX algorithm denying over 300,000 payment requests in two months with physicians spending average of 1.2 seconds per claim
Proposed solutions include deterministic semantic frameworks to eliminate interpretation delta and governance architecture for clinical decision boundaries

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Administrative costs and inefficiencies; need for government-owned platforms; point-of-care settlement
specificPoints: Range from pre-medical students to practicing physicians to technology developers; offer fresh perspectives outside institutional constraints
Dr. Kordonowy advocates for point-of-care settlement where patient wallet confirms benefit eligibility, service documented, patient acknowledges, funds transfer immediately
Proposed solutions include government-owned AI platforms for claims verification, automated prior authorization with seconds-not-days turnaround, and point-of-care settlement systems

noteworthyInsights.insight: Payer technology vendor acknowledges denial concerns: MHK, a company that develops AI tools for health plans, explicitly states there is significant concern about batch denials caused by automation and recommends third-party accreditation with overturn rate tracking—a notable admission from an industry insider

Clearinghouse industry distinguishes its role: The Cooperative Exchange, representing 90%+ of healthcare clearinghouses, proactively distinguishes Administrative Acceleration from Clinical Restriction, emphasizing that their AI handles routing and validation rather than coverage decisions—suggesting industry awareness of public concerns about Algorithmic Denial

Quantified review time exposes rubber-stamping: The Interstitial Institute's documentation that Cigna reviewing physicians spent average of 1.2 seconds per claim on 300,000+ denials provides concrete evidence that human oversight can be nominal rather than meaningful

Medicare spending anomaly suggests AI governance gaps: David Bynon's observation that Medicare spending on skin substitutes increased from $256 million to over $10 billion in a five-year period illustrates how AI systems can enable rather than prevent inappropriate spending when governance is inadequate

Pre-medical student offers international comparison: Ryan Kern's observation that U.S. healthcare system has exceptionally high administrative costs compared to international counterparts like Taiwan provides useful benchmarking context for AI efficiency potential

Interpretation delta concept: David Bynon introduces the concept of deterministic semantic fragments as sovereign truth anchors to eliminate the interpretation delta between policy intent and automated enforcement—a technical framework for addressing algorithmic drift in medical necessity criteria

emergingPatterns.pattern: Asymmetry as Central Theme: The most striking pattern across comments is the framing of payer-provider AI access as fundamentally asymmetric. Healthcare providers consistently describe a dynamic where they face AI-powered denial systems while being contractually prohibited from using AI to respond. This asymmetry concern appears in comments from academic health systems like University of Kansas, large integrated systems like WellSpan and CommonSpirit, and trade associations like AMA and AADA.

Facility-Type Patterns: Large health systems like CommonSpirit, Advocate Health, and WellSpan focus on vendor contracting, governance frameworks, and systemic policy changes. Specialty practices in dermatology and retina emphasize specific impacts like downcoding and time-sensitive care delays. Academic medical centers like University of Kansas and Massachusetts hospitals highlight research and innovation implications.

Stakeholder Alignment Across Traditional Divides: Healthcare providers and at least one health plan technology vendor (MHK) express similar concerns about batch denials and algorithmic bias, suggesting potential for cross-stakeholder consensus on governance requirements. This alignment is unusual given typical payer-provider tensions.

Unintended Consequences Identified: University of Kansas notes that broad AI definitions in prohibition clauses could inadvertently prohibit basic automation tools that have been industry standards for years. Multiple commenters note patients abandoning medically necessary treatment rather than navigating denial appeals. Comments suggest current lack of oversight enables both wasteful spending and inappropriate denials.

Geographic Considerations: While most comments take national perspectives, regional health systems like WellSpan in Pennsylvania/Maryland and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association emphasize that smaller and safety-net providers face disproportionate barriers to AI adoption, suggesting rural and underserved areas may be left behind in administrative AI benefits.

keyQuotations.quote: Some insurance companies are now adding language to their provider agreements that specifically prohibits the use of AI in revenue cycle management (RCM). This is a 'bad faith' approach to contracting. It forces health systems to rely on manual, expensive, and slow processes while the payers use high-speed AI to find reasons to deny those same claims.

Without clear guardrails from HHS on the use of AI in utilization management, private payers will likely significantly increase their use of them. This will ultimately limit medically necessary care for patients and further add to already significant physician administrative burden.
There is significant concern about batch denials caused by automation.
Deterministic semantic fragments function as sovereign truth anchors, eliminating the 'interpretation delta' between policy intent and automated enforcement.
While payers increasingly rely on AI for claims processing and denial management, they often restrict providers from using similar tools to improve their own administrative efficiency.
Nearly 1 in 4 physicians has witnessed a serious adverse event linked to delayed or denied care, including hospitalization, disability, or death.
Cigna's PXDX algorithm denied over 300,000 payment requests in two months; reviewing physicians spent average of 1.2 seconds per claim.
AI empowers dermatologists and coding staff to increase successful claims... AI results in fewer coding errors, more timely claim submissions, smoother revenue cycle.
Prior authorization should be automated with approvals returned in seconds, not days. Manual authorization should be the exception.
Clearinghouse AI is designed for 'Administrative Acceleration,' not 'Clinical Restriction.' Human-in-the-loop (HITL) overrides are hard-coded into the transaction path.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific policy issues, cite concrete evidence and statistics, and offer detailed recommendations. Multiple stakeholder types provide complementary perspectives with specific examples and quantified impacts.

evidenceBase.explanation: Comments cite specific statistics (e.g., 93% of physicians report harm, 1.2 seconds average review time), reference lawsuits and documented cases, and draw on member surveys. However, some claims lack primary source citations and payer perspectives are notably absent.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Payer organizations did not submit comments in this dataset, leaving the perspective of health insurers and their rationale for AI-prohibition clauses and AI-driven utilization management unrepresented. This creates a one-sided view of the payer-provider dynamic.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of contract law, healthcare regulation, AI governance, patient safety, and market dynamics with competing stakeholder interests and technical considerations around human-in-the-loop requirements and algorithmic transparency

16.3.themeDescription: Workforce Scheduling and Operational Optimization AI

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments on workforce scheduling and operational optimization AI reveal broad consensus that these tools offer significant value for addressing healthcare's operational challenges, particularly workforce shortages and administrative burden. Commenters—spanning healthcare providers, technology companies, professional associations, and researchers—uniformly support AI deployment in this domain while emphasizing the need for governance guardrails. The central tension lies between leveraging AI's predictive capabilities for system-level planning versus protecting individual workers from surveillance and punitive applications. The dominant recommendation thrust favors public-private partnerships, clear governance frameworks that explicitly prohibit individual performance evaluation, and interoperability standards to enable regional capacity coordination.

consensusPoints.text: AI-driven operational tools demonstrably improve healthcare efficiency. All commenters addressing specific operational applications affirm positive outcomes. Massive Bio reports that predictive algorithms for patient flow and staffing have helped hospitals optimize bed management and reduce length of stay, and AI-driven scheduling tools have cut wait times in operating rooms and imaging departments. Advocate Health's Chief Technology and AI Officer states that strengthening workforce capacity through scheduling, documentation, and radiology tools reduces burden and improves productivity. Medvidi describes successfully deploying AI Receptionist and Modular Agents handling routine follow-up messaging, adherence prompts, and scalable operational support.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All 6 commenters addressing specific operational applications

exceptions.text: All positive assessments come from organizations actively deploying or developing these tools; no frontline worker perspectives on lived experience with scheduling AI are represented.

consensusPoints.text: Workforce attrition represents a critical, quantifiable crisis requiring predictive intervention. Dr. Randhawa provides specific cost data showing nursing turnover costs average hospital $5.7M annually and loss of single high-acuity physician costs $500,000 to more than $1M in replacement costs. For high-revenue specialties, six-month vacancy results in more than $3M in lost clinical capacity.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Strong agreement among those addressing workforce economics

exceptions.text: None explicitly disagree, though the limited comment pool may not capture dissenting views.

consensusPoints.text: Administrative burden in credentialing and onboarding is a significant operational bottleneck. ADA identifies credentialing as one of most administratively burdensome aspects of clinical operations and argues that streamlining primary source verification and onboarding improves access to care and removes barrier for clinicians seeking timely employment opportunities.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope and Governance of Workforce Telemetry
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether workforce AI should be limited to system-level planning only or could include individual-level monitoring

positions.label: System-Level Planning Only
positions.stance: Dr. Randhawa, a neurosurgeon with trauma center experience and healthcare operations MBA, explicitly advocates that Workforce Readiness Telemetry should provide anonymized, aggregate indicators of operational strain, cognitive load, and staffing fragility. Must be explicitly NOT designed for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions—only for system-level planning. Aggregated data enables real-time heat map for mass casualty, pandemic, or geopolitical events to enable regional and national surge prediction.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly advocated by 1 commenter, with no direct opposition in this comment set
keyArguments: Workforce Readiness Telemetry should provide anonymized, aggregate indicators of operational strain, cognitive load, and staffing fragility
Must be explicitly NOT designed for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions—only for system-level planning
Aggregated data enables real-time heat map for mass casualty, pandemic, or geopolitical events to enable regional and national surge prediction

positions.label: No Counter-Position Articulated
positions.stance: The comment set does not include voices advocating for individual-level workforce monitoring or opposing the system-level-only approach. There is an absence of employer/administrator perspectives on whether individual performance data should inform AI-driven scheduling decisions.

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between shared regional capacity data models and facility-level autonomous solutions

positions.label: Shared Capacity Data Utility
positions.stance: Van Pelt's model, referenced by independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi, provides continuously available views of healthcare system capacity including staffing shortages, bed availability, and regional surge conditions, supporting operational optimization across facilities and regions.
positions.supportLevel: Proposed by 1 academic researcher
keyArguments: Van Pelt's model provides continuously available views of healthcare system capacity including staffing shortages, bed availability, and regional surge conditions
Supports operational optimization across facilities and regions

positions.label: Facility-Level Autonomy
positions.stance: Organizations like Advocate Health and Medvidi describe proprietary, internally-deployed solutions with emphasis on organizational control. No explicit opposition to shared data, but focus remains on internal deployments.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in provider comments focusing on internal deployments
keyArguments: Organizations describe proprietary, internally-deployed solutions
No explicit opposition to shared data, but emphasis on organizational control

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reducing administrative burden on clinical staff, optimizing scheduling and capacity utilization, and maintaining human oversight of AI-driven operations. Emphasize practical deployment experience and measurable outcomes; focus on what works operationally rather than theoretical frameworks.
specificPoints: Deploy AI for scheduling, documentation, and patient communication under strict human oversight
Use AI to strengthen workforce capacity through multiple integrated tools
Medvidi's AI Receptionist deployment demonstrates practical application
Advocate Health's FAIR-AI framework provides governance model

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workforce attrition and replacement costs, operational strain and cognitive load on staff, and liability ambiguity around AI-influenced workflows. Only voice explicitly addressing worker protection and the need to prevent AI from being used for surveillance or discipline; brings trauma center and disaster preparedness lens.
specificPoints: Prioritize AI as Operational Radar predicting attrition 30-60 days in advance
Mandate that workforce telemetry cannot be used for individual evaluation
Create regional/national surge prediction capabilities
Dr. Randhawa's detailed cost analysis and governance framework proposal provides comprehensive approach

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Credentialing administrative burden, barriers to timely clinician employment, and standards development for AI in their specialty. Brings standards development expertise (ANSI-accredited) and existing public-private partnership models.
specificPoints: Public-private partnerships for AI-enabled credentialing
End-to-end AI-based credentialing solutions
ADA's collaboration with CAQH and LightSpun.ai for automated primary source verification demonstrates working model

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Demonstrating AI value in operational contexts, regulatory clarity for scaling solutions, and reimbursement pathways. Provides specific operational metrics and outcomes data from deployed systems.
specificPoints: Massive Bio's data on reduced wait times and optimized bed management demonstrates measurable outcomes
Focus on demonstrating current capabilities rather than explicit policy proposals

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Systematic frameworks for AI governance, interoperability and data sharing models, and coordination of multiple AI agents. Synthesizes peer-reviewed literature and proposes architectural frameworks including agentic AI with orchestration.
specificPoints: Shared Capacity Data Utility Model for regional coordination based on Van Pelt's model
Agentic AI architectures with scheduling, triage, and coordination agents

noteworthyInsights.insight: Deflationary infrastructure framing — Dr. Randhawa uniquely frames workforce prediction AI as critical deflationary infrastructure, suggesting these tools could help contain healthcare costs systemically rather than just improving individual facility operations. This economic framing may resonate with policymakers focused on healthcare spending.

Existing public-private partnership model — ADA provides a concrete, already-operational example of AI credentialing collaboration (CAQH + LightSpun.ai) that could serve as a template for other operational AI deployments, demonstrating that cross-sector coordination is achievable.

Agentic AI architecture proposal — Satyadhar Joshi introduces the concept of coordinated AI agents (scheduling, triage, care coordination, predictive analytics, patient outreach) managed by an agent orchestrator, representing a more sophisticated operational vision than single-purpose tools.

Dual-use concern articulation — Dr. Randhawa's explicit call for workforce telemetry to be explicitly NOT designed for individual performance evaluation represents the only comment directly addressing the worker autonomy/surveillance tension, suggesting this concern may be underrepresented in the comment record.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Large integrated health systems like Advocate Health emphasize internal AI development and proprietary frameworks
Specialty-focused providers like Medvidi in telehealth/behavioral health highlight modular, scalable solutions
Professional associations like ADA focus on cross-facility standardization and interoperability
Technology/business leaders emphasize demonstrated outcomes and scaling potential while frontline clinicians emphasize worker protection and governance guardrails
Researchers emphasize architectural frameworks and data sharing models while professional associations emphasize standards and public-private partnerships
Workforce AI designed for optimization could be repurposed for surveillance, and efficiency gains may not translate to worker benefit without explicit protections

Notable absence of comments from labor unions, nursing associations, individual frontline workers (non-physician), patient/family perspectives, and rural healthcare perspectives on operational AI feasibility

keyQuotations.quote: AI tools predicting workforce attrition 30-60 days in advance represent critical deflationary infrastructure
WRT must be explicitly NOT designed for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions—only for system-level planning
Nursing turnover costs average hospital $5.7M annually... loss of single high-acuity physician costs $500,000 to >$1M in replacement costs
Predictive algorithms for patient flow and staffing have helped hospitals optimize bed management and reduce length of stay
Credentialing is one of most administratively burdensome aspects of clinical operations
AI Receptionist and Modular Agents handling routine follow-up messaging, adherence prompts, and scalable operational support under strict human oversight
Aggregated telemetry should create real-time heat map for mass casualty, pandemic, or geopolitical events to enable regional and national surge prediction

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments provide substantive arguments with specific examples and data, but represent a narrow stakeholder range with limited opposing viewpoints

evidenceBase.explanation: Some comments provide specific cost data and operational metrics, while others rely on characterizations without quantitative support. Evidence primarily comes from organizations deploying the tools being discussed.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Overrepresented: Technology developers, health system executives, professional associations. Underrepresented: Frontline workers (nurses, allied health), labor organizations, patients, rural providers, safety-net hospitals. Absent: Explicit opposition to operational AI; concerns about job displacement; worker experience with AI scheduling.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: The consensus and support levels reported should be interpreted cautiously given the small sample size (6 comments) and stakeholder composition. The absence of dissenting voices may reflect comment filtering rather than true consensus.

17.1.themeDescription: Research Funding Priorities and Evidence Gaps. This sub-theme addresses what research HHS should fund and through what mechanisms. || It includes proposals for specific research priorities, debates about appropriate funding mechanisms (grants, contracts, prizes), discussions of implementation science versus technology development, and recommendations for research infrastructure

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate overwhelming consensus that HHS research priorities must shift from algorithm development toward implementation science, real-world validation, and post-deployment monitoring. The dominant tension lies between those advocating for broad, foundational research infrastructure versus those pushing for targeted funding in specific clinical domains (behavioral health, rare diseases, chronic conditions). Across stakeholder types, commenters identify a critical "evidence gap" not in whether AI can work, but in understanding how it performs when deployed at scale across diverse settings—with particular concern for underrepresented populations in rural, safety-net, and pediatric settings.

consensusPoints.text: Implementation Science Must Be Prioritized Over Algorithm Development. Nearly all commenters agree that the research focus should shift from model performance in controlled settings to real-world deployment, workflow integration, and longitudinal outcomes. The Health AI Institute notes that the primary evidence gap is no longer whether AI models can perform discrete tasks, but whether they perform reliably, equitably, and safely over time and across settings. Amir Abrams, former Director of Medical Informatics, argues HHS should fund implementation science, not just model research. Ellipsis Health and independent researchers emphasize that the largest evidence gap is not whether AI can work but how it works when deployed at scale across diverse settings and populations.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (approximately 40+ of 55 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: ScriptChain Health argued that most R&D funding has gone to nonprofits and academic institutions which yields poor results, suggesting funding should flow to commercial entities instead.

consensusPoints.text: Post-Deployment Monitoring and Drift Detection Are Critical Research Gaps. Commenters explicitly call for research into how AI systems perform after deployment, including methods for detecting performance degradation, bias emergence, and model drift. Cooperative Exchange emphasizes research on post-deployment AI behavior in production environments including performance drift, error propagation, system updates, and long-term operational impacts—beyond controlled pilots or single-site studies. The Health AI Institute identifies priority research areas including post-deployment performance monitoring, workflow integration, organizational governance, and economic impact analysis. Cooperative Exchange also recommends HHS fund research on Model Decay—how AI performance degrades over years as medical codes and payer policies shift.

Real-World Economic Evidence Is Lacking and Urgently Needed. Commenters addressing cost-effectiveness agree that current economic analyses are fragmented, inconsistent, and insufficient for informing adoption decisions. Ellipsis Health notes the literature on AI cost impact remains fragmented and inconsistent with limited analysis of avoided utilization, underrepresentation of indirect benefits such as workforce efficiency, and insufficient attention to cost and benefit transfers between stakeholders. The Interstitial Institute observes that cost-benefit literature on clinical AI is methodologically immature. Massive Bio calls for rigorous health economics research on AI—how it shifts costs and benefits across stakeholders.

Equity, Bias Detection, and Diverse Population Validation Are Essential. Commenters emphasize that research must address bias, equity, and performance across diverse populations—particularly underserved groups. Vikki Small, a safety-net health system leader, argues federal grants and contracts should prioritize tools developed with and validated in diverse populations: Medicaid beneficiaries, people with disabilities, limited English proficiency populations, safety-net settings. SANCIAN LLC notes rural and safety-net settings are particularly underrepresented. Massive Bio calls for research into algorithms that detect and correct bias.

Multi-Site, Pragmatic Trials Are Preferred Over Single-Site Studies. Clinical and research commenters call for multi-center, real-world trials rather than retrospective analyses or single-institution pilots. WellSpan Health argues priority research should target multicenter, real-world trials linking AI use to patient-level outcomes, not only process metrics. The Heart Failure Society of America recommends HHS support pragmatic, multi-site randomized trials. ECRI emphasizes that large, multicenter studies comparing AI with traditional methods and reporting patient-oriented outcomes are needed.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of clinical and research commenters (25+)

areasOfDebate.topic: Research Focus: Foundational Infrastructure vs. Targeted Clinical Domains
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether HHS should invest in broad, generalizable research infrastructure or concentrate funding on specific high-impact clinical areas.

positions.label: Broad Infrastructure Investment
positions.stance: Build generalizable research infrastructure, datasets, and evaluation frameworks. The Computing Research Association, Waymark, and Penguin AI support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of academic researchers, trade associations, and large health systems (approximately 25 commenters)
keyArguments: Open datasets and benchmarking platforms level the playing field for all developers
Federated learning infrastructure enables generalization without centralized data
Standardized evaluation methods are prerequisites for meaningful comparison

positions.label: Targeted Disease Focus
positions.stance: Concentrate funding on specific high-impact clinical areas. Steven Zecola (Parkinson's patient), the American College of Radiology, and the National Council for Mental Wellbeing advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Patient advocates, specialty societies, and condition-specific providers (approximately 15 commenters)
keyArguments: Complex, multifactorial conditions (neurodegenerative diseases, rare diseases) have been neglected
Behavioral health lacks adequate evidence base and requires specialized research
Pediatric populations and rare diseases represent market failures requiring federal intervention

areasOfDebate.topic: Funding Mechanisms: Grants vs. Contracts vs. Prizes
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether traditional grant mechanisms or innovation-focused approaches like SBIR and prize competitions are more appropriate for AI research.

positions.label: Traditional Grants
positions.stance: Fund through NIH, AHRQ, and established mechanisms. The Markkula Center for Applied Ethics and the American Urological Association support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Academic institutions, professional societies, most healthcare providers (approximately 30 commenters)
keyArguments: Cooperative agreements enable regional evaluation collaboratives
Grants support independent, unbiased research
Established peer review ensures scientific rigor

positions.label: Innovation Mechanisms
positions.stance: Use SBIR and prize competitions to accelerate development. Advanced Interactive Technology Holdings, ICF Incorporated, and Interstella advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Small businesses, startups, some technology companies (approximately 8 commenters)
keyArguments: SBIRs encourage development of new evaluation methods and tools
Prize competitions can focus attention on specific high-value problems
Commercial incentives drive faster translation

areasOfDebate.topic: Data Access: Open Sharing vs. Controlled Access
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on the appropriate balance between expanding data access for research and maintaining rigorous protections for sensitive health data.

positions.label: Expand Open Access
positions.stance: Provide broader access to de-identified and synthetic datasets. HealthScoreAI, Penguin AI, and ScriptChain Health support this position.
positions.supportLevel: AI developers, startups, some academic researchers (approximately 15 commenters)
keyArguments: Innovators need standardized, high-quality datasets to develop and validate models
Controlled access policies slow research progress
Synthetic data and de-identified datasets can enable innovation while protecting privacy

positions.label: Maintain Protections
positions.stance: Preserve controlled access for sensitive data. The American Society of Retina Specialists warns of potential chilling effects from overly restrictive policies.
positions.supportLevel: Professional societies with imaging data, some academic institutions (approximately 5 commenters)
keyArguments: NIH's proposed Controlled-Access Data Policy has potential chilling effect on imaging-based clinical research
Risk-based approaches should replace blanket restrictions
Professional societies already manage responsible data sharing

areasOfDebate.topic: Behavioral Health AI: Cautious Validation vs. Accelerated Research
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether behavioral health AI requires exceptional scrutiny before deployment or whether the mental health crisis demands faster research action.

positions.label: Rigorous Validation Required
positions.stance: Behavioral health AI requires exceptional scrutiny. The Psychotherapy Action Network and Bianka Hardin emphasize the need for independent validation.
positions.supportLevel: Mental health advocacy groups, some clinicians (approximately 8 commenters)
keyArguments: Evidence base showing behavioral health AI products are effective and safe is lacking
Corporate metrics (downloads, engagement) are not substitutes for clinical effectiveness
LLMs must be tested to ensure they cannot be manipulated into encouraging self-harm

positions.label: Accelerate Research
positions.stance: Mental health crisis demands faster action. SonderMind, InStride Health, and Medvidi advocate for accelerated research approaches.
positions.supportLevel: Digital mental health providers, some health systems (approximately 10 commenters)
keyArguments: Mental health workforce shortage creates urgent need for AI augmentation
Real-world deployment data from existing tools can inform research
Pediatric mental health populations warrant focused attention

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Real-world workflow integration, clinician burden reduction, liability exposure from AI oversight
specificPoints: Research must validate AI as cognitive augmentation, not just automation
Concern about tangible risk that over-reliance on AI might lead to deterioration of essential human clinical skills
Proposed solutions include multi-site pragmatic trials, shared registries for outcome tracking, and research on human-AI teaming
Key examples include WellSpan Health, University of Kansas Health System, and Advocate Health

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Access to validation datasets, regulatory clarity enabling market development, sustainable reimbursement pathways
specificPoints: Identify structural friction in data infrastructure as primary barrier
Scientix.ai emphasizes need for data-quality-at-source protocols
Proposed solutions include open-source benchmarking datasets, federated learning infrastructure, and vendor-agnostic use case repositories
Key examples include Epic Systems, Waymark, and FERZ AI

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member readiness for AI adoption, liability frameworks, specialty-specific research gaps
specificPoints: The American College of Radiology highlights market failures in pediatrics and rare diseases where limited data results in less commercial AI development
Proposed solutions include specialty-specific research priorities, professional society involvement in metric development, and targeted funding for underserved clinical areas
Key examples include American College of Radiology, AORN, and American Academy of Family Physicians

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Research infrastructure funding, access to compliant computing environments, methodological rigor
specificPoints: The Computing Research Association emphasizes need for in-context evaluation that assesses not only trustworthiness but correctness
Proposed solutions include regional evaluation collaboratives, standardized benchmarking datasets, and governance architecture research
Key examples include Computing Research Association, Markkula Center for Applied Ethics, and Interstitial Institute

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient safety, equity across populations, independent validation free from commercial bias
specificPoints: The Psychotherapy Action Network emphasizes that clinical metrics differ from corporate metrics focused on measuring product profitability rather than safety and efficacy
Proposed solutions include independent research separated from product developers, clinician collaboration on appropriate metrics, and focus on underserved populations
Key examples include Psychotherapy Action Network, American Cancer Society CAN, and Heart Failure Society of America

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Disease-specific research gaps, information overload, practical tools for daily management
specificPoints: Steven Zecola highlights that more than 50% of nation's healthcare costs are attributable to patients with multiple chronic illnesses yet research neglects complex conditions
Proposed solutions include prioritizing AI for complex, multifactorial conditions, developing education modules tied to AI, and focusing on rare diseases
Key examples include Steven Zecola (Parkinson's patient) and Dominick Esposito (T1D caregiver)

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance as research priority, not afterthought — The Interstitial Institute argues HHS should prioritize governance infrastructure research—not AI capability research, proposing development of governance failure taxonomy organized by specific architectural absences. This reframes the research agenda from technical performance to institutional accountability.

Degraded-state validation for resilience — Neurosurgeon Pal Randhawa uniquely proposes testing AI reliability under cyber outages, communications latency, mass casualty surges, geopolitical conflict—a perspective informed by disaster preparedness that most commenters overlook.

Model collapse from synthetic data — Scientix.ai warns of research into preventing model collapse where AI models degrade when trained on recursively generated synthetic data rather than high-fidelity, curated records—a technical concern with significant implications for data generation strategies.

Caregiver perspective on information overload — T1D caregiver Dominick Esposito notes that information for individuals about to become or currently insulin-dependent has proliferated—making sense of it for each individual case can be overwhelming, highlighting AI's potential role in synthesis rather than just generation.

NIH data policy as research barrier — The American Society of Retina Specialists warns that NIH's proposed Controlled-Access Data Policy has potential chilling effect on imaging-based clinical research and AI development, noting that mandating controlled access for all retinal imaging will detrimentally slow research progress.

Defense AI governance parallels — DoD data scientist Eli Talbert applies defense AI governance principles to clinical AI, recommending research to measure longitudinal override patterns in deployed systems and develop standardized human-AI coordination metrics—cross-sector expertise rarely represented in health policy comments.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Rural and safety-net settings consistently identified as underrepresented in research. Regional health systems in Massachusetts, Pennsylvania, and Kansas emphasize need for multi-site validation across diverse settings. State-level commenters in California and New Jersey note that fragmented state requirements complicate research generalizability.

Facility-type patterns show academic medical centers emphasizing foundational research infrastructure and methodological rigor, while safety-net providers prioritize equity validation and resource-constrained implementation research. Post-acute and long-term care settings highlight unique infrastructure and workforce challenges. Specialty practices call for specialty-specific research priorities.

Experience correlations reveal frontline clinicians emphasize workflow integration and burden reduction over technical performance, AI developers focus on data access and benchmarking infrastructure, governance consultants prioritize accountability frameworks and failure mode analysis, and patient advocates emphasize disease-specific research gaps and practical utility.

Unintended consequences identified include skill atrophy from AI reliance degrading clinical skills over time, burden shifting where AI oversight creates uncompensated work for clinicians, equity gaps where tools validated in well-resourced settings perform poorly elsewhere, and research distortion where controlled-access data policies inadvertently slow beneficial research.

keyQuotations.quote: The primary evidence gap is no longer whether AI models can perform discrete tasks, but whether they perform reliably, equitably, and safely over time and across settings.
Too often, tools are built and validated in well-resourced systems and then deployed elsewhere with weaker performance and new access barriers.
Most of R&D funding has gone to non profits and academic institutions which yields poor results.
HHS should fund implementation science, not just model research.
Many barriers to adoption reside downstream of algorithmic innovation.

Existing literature typically focuses on technical performance or theoretical cost savings—greater emphasis needed on implementation science: how AI tools change care processes, redistribute work, and affect quality and access in practice.
We need more research on the 'tangible risk' that over-reliance on AI might lead to the deterioration of essential human clinical skills.
Research must validate AI as tool for cognitive augmentation, not just automation.
Primary safety endpoint should be whether AI reduces time-to-decision or increases cognitive burden.
Clinical-grade behavioral health AI requires safety testing at a standard that doesn't yet exist in research literature.
Clinical metrics differ from corporate metrics focused on measuring product profitability rather than safety and efficacy.
HHS should fund studies testing LLMs in behavioral health scenarios to ensure they cannot be manipulated into encouraging self-harm or dangerous behaviors.

The literature on AI cost impact remains fragmented and inconsistent with limited analysis of avoided utilization, underrepresentation of indirect benefits such as workforce efficiency, and insufficient attention to cost and benefit transfers between stakeholders.
Cost-benefit literature on clinical AI is methodologically immature.
HHS should prioritize governance infrastructure research—not AI capability research.
Research on governance, accountability, and monitoring models should be prioritized.

AI is particularly promising for complex, multifactorial conditions including neurodegenerative diseases, autism spectrum disorders, and multiple chronic illnesses where conventional reductionist approaches have failed.
Pediatrics and rare disease care are relatively small markets with limited data, resulting in less commercial AI development.

Investment in federated learning infrastructure represents highest priority on research front—would enable AI systems to generalize across heterogeneous care settings without requiring centralized data aggregation.
Research into preventing 'model collapse' where AI models degrade when trained on recursively generated synthetic data rather than high-fidelity, curated records.
Synthetic data and LLM evaluations (one LLM assessing another) are insufficient as evidence of external validity, safety, and value.
Today's literature is niche and organization-dependent with limited ability to report outcomes absent robust analytics.
Current benchmarks are largely static and single-turn, focusing narrowly on clinical decision-making rather than care delivery more broadly.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of research methodology, implementation science, and evidence gaps. Stakeholders across categories provide substantive, well-reasoned arguments with specific recommendations and citations to existing literature.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters frequently cite specific evidence gaps and methodological limitations in existing research. Many reference concrete examples from their own experience or published literature, though some claims about research priorities lack empirical support.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Individual patients and caregivers are underrepresented (only 3 comments) relative to industry and professional stakeholders. Rural providers and safety-net systems are mentioned as underrepresented in research but also appear underrepresented among commenters themselves. International perspectives and comparative health systems research are largely absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves nuanced distinctions between research types (implementation vs. capability), funding mechanisms (grants vs. prizes vs. SBIR), and data access models (open vs. controlled). Commenters demonstrate awareness of tradeoffs and unintended consequences across multiple dimensions.

17.2.themeDescription: Public-Private Partnerships and Innovation Sandboxes. This sub-theme addresses mechanisms for federal collaboration with private sector on AI innovation, including demonstration projects, sandboxes, and partnerships. || It includes proposals for AI sandboxes and pilot programs, debates about appropriate public-private partnership structures, discussions of CMMI demonstration models, and recommendations for innovation support

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that federal-private sector collaboration is essential for accelerating responsible AI adoption in healthcare, with virtually all 42 commenters supporting some form of partnership mechanism. The dominant tension lies not in whether to partner, but in how—specifically, debates over the appropriate balance between regulatory flexibility and safety oversight, whether to prioritize pilots or operational-scale demonstrations, and how to structure partnerships to benefit smaller innovators and safety-net providers rather than only well-resourced organizations. The overwhelming recommendation thrust favors leveraging existing authorities (CRADAs, CMMI demonstrations, cooperative agreements) rather than creating new regulatory structures, with strong emphasis on shared infrastructure, validation datasets, and assess once, reuse many models.

consensusPoints.text: Federal partnership mechanisms can de-risk AI adoption and accelerate responsible innovation. Nearly all commenters agree that public-private collaboration is necessary and beneficial for advancing clinical AI. This consensus spans healthcare providers, technology companies, trade associations, and academic researchers. Representative examples include Massive Bio stating that federal partnership can de-risk AI adoption and accelerate responsible innovation, Ellipsis Health noting that early federal participation accelerates learning, builds evidence, and lowers barriers for broader market adoption, and BlueHalo emphasizing that HHS is well positioned to convene multi-institution, vendor-neutral partnerships and establish shared expectations.

exceptions.text: AAFP strongly agrees with Administration that voluntary, private sector coalitions can move more quickly than federal government in many instances, but government's perspective and support is still very much needed and wanted—suggesting partnership should complement, not replace, private initiative.

consensusPoints.text: Existing federal authorities (CRADAs, CMMI, cooperative agreements) are sufficient and preferred. A strong majority of commenters explicitly recommend leveraging existing mechanisms rather than creating new regulatory structures. CRADAs, CMMI demonstration models, cooperative agreements, and prize competitions are the most frequently cited vehicles. David Bynon noted that HHS could advance this approach through existing vehicles—such as the AI Governance Board, CMS sub-regulatory guidance, or limited voluntary demonstrations coordinated with CMMI—without requiring new statutory authority. BlueHalo emphasized that CRADAs and cooperative agreements provide flexible mechanisms for collaboration between government, industry, and research organizations. Specific mechanisms cited include CRADAs (9 commenters), CMMI demonstrations (8 commenters), cooperative agreements (12 commenters), prize competitions (10 commenters), and SBIR/STTR grants (4 commenters).

Shared infrastructure and validation datasets are critical public goods. Nearly all commenters addressing technical needs emphasize that federally-supported shared resources—benchmark datasets, testing frameworks, evaluation protocols—would reduce duplicative effort and lower barriers for all innovators. Health AI Institute proposed creating an AI Evaluation Commons as shared national resource supporting standardized benchmarks, AI-relevant data standards, post-deployment performance metrics, and failure-mode reporting. American Urological Association suggested a nationwide network of AI assurance laboratories, supported but not operated by HHS, would allow industry-driven testing results to be shared transparently and reused across health systems. NeuroFlow noted that federally supported validation infrastructure would reduce duplicative review burdens and create common benchmarks.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing technical needs (approximately 25 of 42)

consensusPoints.text: Time-limited programs with structured evaluation are the preferred sandbox model. Most commenters supporting sandboxes emphasize that regulatory flexibility must be paired with mandatory evidence collection, monitoring, and defined endpoints. Interstella recommended time-limited programs with strong monitoring to demonstrate safe ways to adjust regulations in the long run. ICF proposed creating time-limited AI adoption pilots with clear guardrails, aligned reimbursement and reporting, targeted compliance flexibilities. SANCIAN LLC emphasized enabling pilots with real-world evidence requirements and mandatory evidence collection.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Partnership Focus: Pilots vs. Operational Scale
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether federal partnerships should prioritize controlled pilot programs or move directly to operational-scale demonstrations.

positions.label: Operational Scale First
positions.stance: Shift from pilots to full-scale demonstrations. Massive Bio argues that HHS should prioritize execution-ready models and shift from pilots to operational-scale demonstrations, stating that Massive Bio operates today as national infrastructure—not a pilot or an app—and stands ready to partner with HHS to implement this vision now.
positions.supportLevel: 4-5 commenters, primarily established technology companies
keyArguments: Pilots generate limited evidence
Operational-scale demonstrations prove real-world viability
Execution-ready models exist today

positions.label: Pilots and Sandboxes First
positions.stance: Start with controlled, time-limited experiments. Interstella and WellSpan Health advocate for preserving room for innovation sandboxes and provisional scaling to allow developers to iterate and build evidence without halting progress.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (~25), including health systems, trade associations, and smaller innovators
keyArguments: Pilots generate evidence safely
Sandboxes allow iteration without premature scaling
Smaller organizations need lower-risk entry points

areasOfDebate.topic: Partnership Beneficiaries: Large vs. Small Innovators
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether partnership mechanisms should prioritize accessibility for startups and small companies or focus on proven, scalable organizations.

positions.label: Broad Access Priority
positions.stance: Prioritize mechanisms accessible to startups and small companies. ScriptChain Health argues that a large number of startups should receive funding and that there needs to be a strong alliance between the private sector and public sector with an accelerated approval process instead of waiting 9-12 months. Bethany Russell proposes not only funding the large developers but taking a VC approach and offering seed money to thousands of companies in the form of tax credits, noting some companies might just hit it out of the park.
positions.supportLevel: 8-10 commenters, primarily smaller technology companies and individual researchers
keyArguments: Current processes favor large, well-resourced organizations
Startups face 12-18 month approval delays
VC-style approach could identify breakthrough innovations

positions.label: Established Partners Focus
positions.stance: Focus on proven, scalable organizations. Massive Bio and FastHealth Corporation emphasize working with organizations that have demonstrated operational capability.
positions.supportLevel: 5-6 commenters, primarily larger technology companies and established health systems
keyArguments: Operational-scale partners can demonstrate impact faster
Proven track records reduce risk
Infrastructure already exists

areasOfDebate.description: Disagreement over whether the federal government should only convene and fund partnerships or actively build and operate shared infrastructure.

positions.label: Convener/Funder Only
positions.stance: Government should support but not operate infrastructure. Providence argues that financial and administrative burden should not fall to federal government. American Urological Association proposes a nationwide network of AI assurance laboratories, supported but not operated by HHS.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (~20 commenters), including trade associations and health systems
keyArguments: Private sector moves faster
Financial burden should not fall to federal government
Industry-driven testing maintains flexibility

positions.label: Active Federal Infrastructure
positions.stance: Government should build and maintain shared resources. Pair Team argues that HHS should invest in national, open-source infrastructure through federal funding to private sector and academia to create a shared foundation for all innovators. Scientix.ai proposes building shared, cloud-agnostic infrastructure through OneHHS allowing agencies like FDA, CDC, and CMS to reuse validated AI components.
positions.supportLevel: 6-8 commenters, primarily academic researchers and some technology companies
keyArguments: Market failures in shared infrastructure
Federal leadership ensures equity and access
Reduces duplicative investment

areasOfDebate.topic: AI Application Focus: Clinical Decision vs. Measurement/Administrative
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether partnerships should prioritize clinical decision support AI or focus first on measurement and administrative applications.

positions.label: Clinical Decision Support
positions.stance: Prioritize AI that directly supports clinical decisions. Health AI Institute, ACR, and Massive Bio emphasize clinical applications as having the greatest potential for patient impact.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (~30), including most health systems and specialty societies
keyArguments: Greatest potential for patient impact
Most urgent need for governance frameworks
Highest stakes require most attention

positions.label: Measurement/Administrative First
positions.stance: Focus on observability and administrative AI before clinical automation. Keith Mountjoy argues that public-private pilot programs should focus on AI-enabled measurement and observability technologies rather than clinical decision automation. Cooperative Exchange notes their members process over 6 billion transactions annually and could leverage CMMI authorities to test AI-enabled administrative and operational innovations.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters, primarily individual researchers and clearinghouse organizations
keyArguments: Lower risk entry point
Measurement enhances rather than replaces clinical judgment
Administrative AI can demonstrate value without patient safety concerns

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability clarity, reimbursement alignment, integration with existing workflows, resource constraints for smaller/safety-net providers
specificPoints: Emphasize need for pilots in under-resourced settings and express willingness to serve as implementation partners and share real-world evidence
WellSpan Health advocates for preserving room for innovation sandboxes and provisional scaling
Community Care Cooperative supports collaboratives that pair early-stage developers with safety-net providers using ATTUNE-like models
Advocate Health calls for pilot programs testing AI applications in under-resourced settings including rural and safety net providers
Propose CMMI demonstrations with outcomes-based rewards, multi-site evaluations, and learning collaboratives

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity, accelerated approval pathways, access to validation datasets, reimbursement for AI-enabled services
specificPoints: Emphasize execution-readiness and desire to move beyond pilots; highlight specific technical solutions they can offer; express frustration with bureaucratic delays
Massive Bio advocates shifting from pilots to operational-scale demonstrations
FERZ AI proposes deploying LASO(f) and DELIA in hospital pilots to generate real-world evidence
COGA Labs recommends establishing an AI Health Sandbox modeled on UK MHRA AI Airlock and Singapore programs
Propose CRADAs for dataset development, prize competitions for specific challenges, sandbox programs with provisional authorization, and assess once, reuse many certification models

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member diversity (large and small organizations), sustainable adoption pathways, avoiding unfunded mandates, maintaining professional standards
specificPoints: Balance innovation enthusiasm with caution about implementation burden on members; emphasize need for federal support without federal operation
AAFP states it is crucial for ASTP/ONC to partner more closely with private sector coalitions on AI—both financially and intellectually
ACR recommends strategic public-private partnerships and CRADAs focused on clinically meaningful radiology applications
ADA notes HHS can accelerate AI integration by establishing a practice network with ADA's leadership in standards
Propose public-private partnerships for testing infrastructure, industry-driven accreditation with federal recognition, and demonstration projects with shared learning

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence generation, workforce training, research-to-practice translation, maintaining scientific rigor
specificPoints: Emphasize university expertise in training and credentialing; highlight need for implementation science; propose pilot-to-standard approaches

Computing Research Association encourages industry-academia partnerships across AI and clinical care, noting university researchers have expertise in training and credentialing that private sector could benefit from
Health AI Institute proposes creating National Clinical AI Sandbox Program as time-limited program enabling real-world deployment under defined regulatory and reimbursement flexibility
Propose industry-academia partnerships, SBIR/STTR guidance on accreditation, and technical communities for iterative standard development

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Framework adoption, governance methodology validation, access for independent innovators
specificPoints: Often propose specific proprietary frameworks or methodologies; emphasize deterministic/auditable approaches; offer to brief federal officials directly
David Bynon proposes supporting a federal reference implementation for a complex benefit program such as Medicare Part D
Satyadhar Joshi recommends reducing administrative friction and duplication through mutual recognition of credible private-sector accreditation programs
Propose demonstration projects validating specific methodologies, cooperative agreements for framework testing, and recognition of private-sector accreditation

noteworthyInsights.insight: International sandbox models as templates — COGA Labs specifically recommends modeling U.S. approach on UK (MHRA AI Airlock) and Singapore programs, suggesting international precedents could accelerate domestic framework development.

Pilot-to-Standard flywheel concept — Leap Orbit articulates how rapidly iterating FHIR IGs through technical communities feeds a flywheel of AI adoption and provides bedrock of consistent AI-ready data for autonomous agentic AI systems—connecting infrastructure investment to long-term AI capability.

Concrete pilot success story — Interstella provides specific evidence: academic hospital tests AI-assisted triage system in emergency department with guardrails; AI suggestion not final, nurses could override, outcomes tracked; improved throughput, high-risk patients seen 10 minutes faster on average, no critical cases missed, AI caught subtle presentations of sepsis earlier than standard triage.

VC-style funding approach — Bethany Russell, a practicing clinician and AI developer, proposes unconventional approach: take VC approach and offer seed money to thousands of companies in the form of tax credits, some companies might just hit it out of the park—suggesting portfolio approach to innovation funding.

Clearinghouse industry as untapped partner — Cooperative Exchange notes their members process over 6 billion transactions annually and could serve as industry resource to CMS in support of government and private sector collaborative initiatives—highlighting administrative AI opportunities often overlooked in clinical AI discussions.

Measurement-first philosophy — Keith Mountjoy offers contrarian view that pilots should focus on AI-enabled measurement and observability technologies rather than clinical decision automation, arguing for lower-risk entry points that enhance observability and explainability.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Regional health systems like WellSpan in Pennsylvania/Maryland, Providence across 7 western states, and MHA in Massachusetts emphasize multi-state regulatory complexity and need for federal preemption or harmonization. Rural-focused commenters including FastHealth and Advocate Health specifically call for pilots in under-resourced settings.

Organization Size Patterns: Large organizations like Providence, Advocate Health, and ACR emphasize need for federal convening authority but resist federal operation of infrastructure. Small/startup organizations like ScriptChain, COGA Labs, and Bethany Russell emphasize access barriers, approval delays, and need for VC-style broad funding. Mid-size organizations like Ellipsis Health, NeuroFlow, and Waymark focus on CMMI demonstrations and payment model alignment.

Specialty-Specific Patterns: Radiology (ACR) emphasizes post-deployment monitoring and shared output monitoring infrastructure. Mental/Behavioral Health commenters including Medvidi, InStride, NeuroFlow, and Bethany Russell emphasize shortage areas, telehealth integration, and specialty-specific benchmarks. Oncology (Massive Bio) emphasizes operational-scale readiness and clinical trial matching. Primary Care (AAFP, C3) emphasizes small practice constraints and safety-net provider needs.

Mechanism Preference Patterns: Technology companies strongly favor CRADAs and prize competitions. Health systems strongly favor CMMI demonstrations and cooperative agreements. Trade associations favor public-private partnerships with industry-driven testing. Academic researchers favor SBIR/STTR and technical communities.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Multiple commenters note that without equity-focused design, partnerships will primarily benefit well-resourced organizations, potentially widening healthcare disparities. Several note that pilot programs without clear pathways to permanent adoption create pilot purgatory. Trade associations warn that unfunded mandates for participation could exclude smaller members.

keyQuotations.quote: Massive Bio operates today as national infrastructure—not a pilot or an app—and stands ready to partner with HHS to implement this vision now.
There needs to be a strong alliance between the private sector and public sector with an accelerated approval process instead of waiting 9-12 months to know if you are approved.
Not only fund the large developers, take VC approach and offer seed money to thousands of companies in the form of tax credits, some companies might just 'hit it out of the park.'

HHS is well positioned to convene multi-institution, vendor-neutral partnerships and establish shared expectations... By prioritizing partnerships emphasizing evaluation, monitoring, governance, and interoperability, HHS can foster an ecosystem in which AI tools are not only developed but sustained and trusted in clinical care.
Structure of shared evaluation reduces redundant, one-off evaluations, improves data confidence, and creates scalable pathway from research validation to deployment in cost-efficient manner.
Lessons learned through CRADAs can inform broader policy and programmatic decisions - creating a feedback loop between experimentation and guidance.

Strongly agrees with Administration that voluntary, private sector coalitions can move more quickly than federal government in many instances, but government's perspective and support is still very much needed and wanted.
Financial and administrative burden should not fall to federal government.
Time-limited programs with strong monitoring to demonstrate safe ways to adjust regulations in the long run.

AI suggestion not final, nurses could override, outcomes tracked; improved throughput, high-risk patients seen 10 minutes faster on average, no critical cases missed, AI caught subtle presentations of sepsis earlier than standard triage.

C3 and the community health centers it supports stand ready to partner with HHS on future pilots, learning collaboratives, and stakeholder engagement to inform implementation efforts with on-the-ground experience from primary care and community health settings.

HHS could advance this approach through existing vehicles—such as the AI Governance Board, CMS sub-regulatory guidance, or limited voluntary demonstrations coordinated with CMMI—without requiring new statutory authority or model-specific regulation.
Establish an 'AI Health Sandbox' modeled on UK (MHRA AI Airlock) and Singapore programs that would grant provisional authorization for AI health tools to operate in supervised clinical environments.
Rapidly iterating FHIR IGs through technical communities feeds 'flywheel' of AI adoption and provides bedrock of consistent 'AI-ready' data for autonomous agentic AI systems.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of federal partnership mechanisms, cite specific existing authorities and precedents, and provide concrete implementation recommendations. Debate is substantive and focused on how rather than whether to pursue partnerships.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite specific examples including international sandbox models (UK MHRA AI Airlock, Singapore), existing federal programs (N3C, HRSA UDS+ UTC, ARPA-H ADVOCATE), and concrete pilot outcomes (Interstella's ED triage example). However, quantitative evidence of partnership effectiveness remains limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups, payers/insurers, and state/local government perspectives. Safety-net provider voices are present but underrepresented relative to well-resourced health systems and technology companies.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - commenters engage with nuanced tradeoffs between regulatory flexibility and safety oversight, pilot scale versus operational deployment, and equity considerations in partnership design. Multiple viable approaches are articulated with thoughtful consideration of implementation challenges.

18.1.themeDescription: Patient Trust Barriers and Acceptance Factors. This sub-theme addresses factors affecting patient trust in AI and willingness to accept AI involvement in their care. || It includes discussions of patient trust barriers, reports of patient attitudes toward AI, debates about building patient confidence, and recommendations for trust-building approaches. This excludes patient rights and focuses on attitudes and acceptance

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal broad consensus that patient trust is foundational—not optional—for successful AI adoption in healthcare, with commenters across all stakeholder groups identifying transparency, human oversight, and demonstrated safety as essential trust-building elements. The central tension lies between urgency to deploy beneficial AI tools and recognition that premature or poorly governed deployment could trigger trust erosion that sets back adoption for years. Dominant recommendations center on positioning AI as supporting rather than replacing clinicians, investing in rigorous validation frameworks, and engaging trusted community sources for patient education.

consensusPoints.text: Trust is a prerequisite, not a barrier, to AI adoption. Commenters frame patient trust as foundational infrastructure rather than a soft outcome or obstacle to overcome. ECRI states definitively that public trust is not a barrier to innovation but a prerequisite for success at scale. ACS CAN emphasizes that patients and providers have low trust that AI will be used responsibly, and this trust is essential for the transformative potential of AI advances to be realized. SANCIAN LLC recommends HHS should make patient trust a core success metric—not a soft outcome, but the foundation of sustainable adoption.

exceptions.text: One individual commenter expressed high existing trust in AI, rating ChatGPT better than 80% of human doctors, suggesting a minority of patients may already be receptive without additional trust-building measures.

consensusPoints.text: Transparency about AI use is essential for trust. Commenters explicitly identify transparency—about when, how, and why AI is used—as a core trust-building requirement. American Urological Association recommends building trust through transparency about when and how AI is used. Global Liver Institute cites patient concerns about lack of transparency around when and how AI is used. Kooth identifies that lack of trust from patients/end users is driven by AI models lacking explainability or transparency.

Human oversight must remain visible and meaningful. Clinical and advocacy commenters emphasize that patients need assurance AI supports rather than replaces human judgment. NeuroFlow states that when AI is positioned as infrastructure supporting rather than replacing licensed professionals, it can enhance safety, access, and continuity while preserving the clinician-patient relationship. Ellipsis Health recommends AI must be positioned and designed as an extension of the care team, with clear escalation to humans and visible clinician involvement. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi notes patients consistently express concern that increasing reliance on automated systems may erode human connection central to effective, compassionate health care.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all clinical and advocacy commenters (approximately 18 of 20 in these categories)

consensusPoints.text: Privacy and data security concerns are universal patient worries. Commenters addressing specific patient concerns identify privacy and data security as primary trust barriers. Dr. Bethany Russell identifies patient concerns about privacy including fear of data breaches or discriminatory use of mental health information. American Society of Hematology reports patients cite privacy concerns about how protected health data is stored and used. Computing Research Association notes majority of Americans do not trust use of AI in their healthcare—concerned about security of data, AI hallucinations, model poisoning, etc.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing specific patient concerns (approximately 12 of 15)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether patients are fundamentally skeptical of AI requiring significant trust-building versus whether latent demand exists among patients who actively seek AI tools when properly designed.

positions.label: Trust Deficit Crisis
positions.stance: Most patients are skeptical and require significant trust-building. ACS CAN advocacy group, Computing Research Association, and American Society of Hematology represent this view.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 75% of commenters (24 of 32)
keyArguments: Only approximately 19% of adults expect AI to improve care according to Interstella
Majority of Americans do not trust AI in healthcare according to CRA
Patients show hesitancy that creates implementation barriers according to ASH

positions.label: Latent Demand Exists
positions.stance: Patients actively seek AI tools when properly designed. COGA Labs, Penn NeuroVERT, and individual patients represent this view, primarily technology companies.
positions.supportLevel: Small minority (3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: Users actively seek AI tools that provide objective, data-driven indicators according to COGA Labs
Patients express gratitude and enthusiasm for AI detection tools according to Penn NeuroVERT
Strong demand exists if tools are transparent about methodology

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between prioritizing robust validation and governance before deployment versus building trust through visible successes and publicized benefits.

positions.label: Rigor First
positions.stance: Robust validation and governance must precede broad deployment. CJM Strategic Consulting, ECRI, and ACS CAN represent this view, especially advocacy groups and independent consultants.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (approximately 18 of 32)
