keyArguments: Reducing oversight prematurely risks high-profile failures that would set back public confidence according to Morrow
Trust erodes incrementally when governance is insufficient
Rigorous standards are prerequisites for patient trust that sustains adoption

positions.label: Demonstrate Wins
positions.stance: Build trust through visible successes and publicized benefits. Interstella, Medvidi, and caregiver Dominick Esposito represent this view.
positions.supportLevel: Several business commenters (approximately 6-8)
keyArguments: Need to raise expectations by delivering and publicizing wins according to Interstella
AI scribes have helped providers become comfortable with AI through small steps according to Esposito
Patients value faster access, reduced wait times, providers who appear more engaged

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether patients need comprehensive disclosure of all AI involvement versus balanced notification that avoids overload while maintaining awareness.

positions.label: Comprehensive Disclosure
positions.stance: Patients need full awareness of AI involvement. American Academy of Sleep Medicine and Global Liver Institute represent this view, primarily advocacy groups and professional societies.
positions.supportLevel: Most advocacy groups and professional societies (approximately 10 commenters)
keyArguments: Without clear disclosure and meaningful explanation, trust in both technology and healthcare system may be undermined according to AASM
Transparency is foundational to trust

positions.label: Balanced Notification
positions.stance: Avoid notification overload while maintaining awareness. University of Kansas Health System represents this view.
positions.supportLevel: Several health systems (3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: Patients want awareness of AI use but not notification overload according to University of Kansas
Patient comfort varies based on level of trust in provider and perceived benefit

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining therapeutic relationships while integrating AI, patient hesitancy creating implementation barriers, and balancing transparency with notification fatigue. Providers observe that patient trust is often mediated through trust in the individual clinician—patient comfort with AI tools like ambient documentation varies based on level of trust in provider according to University of Kansas.
specificPoints: Clear communication about how AI supports rather than replaces clinicians
Educational resources helping clinicians communicate data protections
Thoughtful design and transparent quality reporting
WellSpan Health notes need for thoughtful design, education, and transparent quality reporting to address trust issues
InStride Health highlights concerns especially pronounced in mental health care for children and adolescents

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Trust deficit limiting market adoption, need for regulatory clarity to demonstrate trustworthiness, and balancing innovation speed with governance requirements. Technology companies emphasize that trust is a technical problem with technical solutions—deterministic governance, cryptographic audit trails, and explainable AI can bridge the gap according to FERZ AI.
specificPoints: Deterministic governance infrastructure for verifiable AI behavior
Positioning AI as care team extension with clear human escalation
Public education campaigns about governed AI systems
Ellipsis Health warns AI that patients avoid or distrust introduces risk, regardless of technical accuracy
ICF proposes investing in trustworthy AI R&D to foster social license

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protecting vulnerable populations from AI-related harms, ensuring patient voice in AI development, and addressing concerns about loss of human connection. Advocacy groups emphasize that trust-building must come from trusted community sources, not just healthcare institutions—educational efforts should come from trusted sources and focused offerings from peers in formal and informal settings may help gain trust according to National MS Society.
specificPoints: Patient-focused product development meetings
Community ambassadors and peer educators
Formal public engagement processes for AI implementation
ACS CAN emphasizes implementation through formal process with public engagement essential for creating trust
Global Liver Institute calls for transparency, shared decision-making, and patient-centered metrics

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preserving clinician-patient relationship, liability implications of AI use, and member education on communicating AI to patients. Professional societies note that trust is often ignored because it's hard to develop policies for an ambiguous term according to ACC, calling for concrete, actionable trust-building frameworks.
specificPoints: Transparent, clinically validated AI systems
Opt-out options for nonessential AI uses
Educational resources for clinician-patient communication
AMA notes limited public trust is inhibiting rapid adoption
AUA recommends maintaining clinician oversight and offering opt-out options

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence base for trust-building interventions, public communication about AI safety measures, and long-term consequences of trust erosion. Researchers warn of systemic consequences—without these steps in place, individuals could lose trust in AI and, by extension, in the healthcare infrastructure as a whole, leading to lower engagement and, consequently, worse health outcomes according to CRA.
specificPoints: Research into patient understanding and trust
Partnerships between AI researchers and communications professionals
Rigorous validation aligned with NIST AI RMF
CRA recommends AI and clinical care researchers partner with communications and public health professionals

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reliability of AI for high-stakes decisions, desire for both innovation and safety assurance, and provider skepticism limiting access to beneficial tools. Caregivers articulate the emotional tension between hope and caution—faces dichotomy as parent: desperately wants AI tool but would be cautious without knowing it was tested and vetted in standardized, rigorous fashion according to Esposito.
specificPoints: Standardized, rigorous testing visible to patients
Small incremental steps to build provider and patient comfort
Clear evidence of benefit before adoption
Caregiver Esposito notes AI scribes used by endocrinologists have helped the medical professional community become comfortable with AI—demonstrating small steps can build trust

noteworthyInsights.insight: Trust as engagement mechanism: Ellipsis Health offers a safety-focused reframing that AI systems that patients trust and engage with consistently are more likely to detect emerging concerns and less likely to miss critical signals. This positions trust not just as adoption enabler but as clinical safety mechanism.

Incremental trust-building through familiar tools: Caregiver Dominick Esposito observes that AI scribes used by endocrinologists have helped the medical professional community become comfortable with AI—demonstrating small steps can build trust. This suggests low-stakes administrative AI may serve as trust-building gateway to clinical AI.

User frustration with current alternatives: COGA Labs reports surprising finding that users express frustration with subjective self-report tools (PHQ-9, GAD-7) that depend on memory and self-awareness and actively seek AI tools that provide objective, data-driven indicators. This suggests some patients may trust AI more than traditional assessment methods.

Trust mediated through provider relationship: University of Kansas Health System notes patient comfort with AI tools varies based on level of trust in provider and perceived benefit to their care—suggesting provider endorsement may be more powerful trust signal than institutional or regulatory assurance.

Caregiver emotional tension: Parent Dominick Esposito articulates the psychological complexity: desperately wants AI tool but would be cautious without knowing it was tested and vetted in standardized, rigorous fashion. This captures the simultaneous hope and fear that characterizes patient/caregiver attitudes.

Contrarian high-trust perspective: Individual commenter Sergey Alexashenko rates ChatGPT better than 80% of human doctors I can go to. It's never out of network. While an outlier, this suggests some patients may already have higher trust in AI than in accessible human providers—a perspective absent from institutional commenters.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Clinical Context Variations: Mental health and pediatric settings show heightened trust concerns due to relationship-centrality and vulnerability according to InStride Health, Dr. Russell, and NeuroFlow. Acute/emergency settings like stroke triage show more positive patient reception when AI enables faster, life-saving interventions according to Penn NeuroVERT. Chronic disease management shows mixed patterns—caregivers desperate for help but cautious about reliability according to Esposito.

Stakeholder Type Correlations: Technology companies emphasize technical solutions to trust including governance infrastructure and explainability. Advocacy groups emphasize community engagement and patient voice. Providers emphasize communication and relationship-mediated trust. Researchers emphasize systemic consequences and evidence needs.

Trust Transfer Dynamics: Multiple commenters suggest trust flows through existing relationships: provider-to-patient, community organization-to-member, peer-to-peer. Institutional trust in HHS and health systems may be necessary but insufficient. Trusted sources and ambassadors repeatedly mentioned as trust conduits.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Notification overload could paradoxically reduce trust by creating fatigue according to University of Kansas. Incidental findings from AI detection may increase anxiety rather than reassurance according to Penn NeuroVERT. Overly cautious governance could delay beneficial tools, eroding trust in system's ability to deliver innovation. High-profile failures from premature deployment could set back entire field according to Morrow and ECRI.

Provider Trust as Gatekeeper: Several commenters note provider skepticism limits patient access to AI tools. Esposito reports providers are skeptical that tools are just going to be another expense. Building provider trust may be prerequisite to building patient trust.

keyQuotations.quote: Public trust is not a barrier to innovation; it is a prerequisite for success at scale.

Patients and providers have low trust that AI will be used responsibly and that health care systems will protect patients from AI-related harms—and this trust is essential for the transformative potential of AI advances to be realized.
The promise of AI in clinical care is immense. The barrier is trust. Deterministic governance infrastructure can bridge that gap—transforming AI from a liability concern into a verified asset.

Insufficient attention to governance and compliance when AI innovations enter clinical workflows acts as a silent killer of adoption: trust does not collapse in a single event but erodes incrementally, degrading patient confidence in AI-assisted care until adoption stalls and the root cause is difficult to trace.
Without these steps in place, individuals could lose trust in AI and, by extension, in the healthcare infrastructure as a whole, leading to lower engagement and, consequently, worse health outcomes.
AI that patients avoid or distrust introduces risk, regardless of technical accuracy.
Will AI be used to avoid talking to me, rather than to support me?

Users express frustration with subjective self-report tools (PHQ-9, GAD-7) that depend on memory and self-awareness. They actively seek AI tools that provide objective, data-driven indicators of their mental and physical state.

By investing in trustworthy AI R&D, HHS will foster the foundational 'social license' for AI in healthcare—building public trust and providing the guardrails so innovation can proceed more rapidly without undue risk.
ChatGPT is already a better doctor than 80% of human doctors I can go to. It's never out of network.
Faces dichotomy as parent: desperately wants AI tool but would be cautious without knowing it was tested and vetted in standardized, rigorous fashion.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of trust dynamics with specific evidence, concrete recommendations, and acknowledgment of tradeoffs. Multiple stakeholder types engage substantively with the complexity of building patient trust while enabling innovation.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters cite surveys, patient feedback, clinical experience, and research findings. Some quantitative data referenced (e.g., 19% of adults expect AI to improve care). However, much evidence is anecdotal or based on organizational experience rather than peer-reviewed studies.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited direct patient voice—only 3 individual patient/caregiver comments among 32 total. Most patient perspectives are reported secondhand by providers, technology companies, and advocacy groups. Perspectives from historically marginalized communities underrepresented despite concerns about bias affecting these populations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High—commenters recognize multiple interacting factors including provider trust as gatekeeper, trust transfer through relationships, clinical context variations, and potential unintended consequences of both over- and under-regulation.

18.2.themeDescription: Patient Concerns About Privacy, Bias, and Human Connection

sections.executiveSummary: Across 42 comments from diverse stakeholders, there is remarkable consensus that patients harbor significant concerns about privacy, algorithmic bias, loss of human connection, and unclear accountability when AI is used in their healthcare. While virtually all commenters acknowledge these concerns as legitimate and requiring policy attention, they diverge on whether current safeguards are adequate and how aggressively to regulate. The dominant recommendation thrust emphasizes transparency, human oversight preservation, and patient-centered disclosure mechanisms—with business stakeholders notably validating rather than dismissing patient fears.

consensusPoints.text: Privacy and data security are universal patient concerns. Nearly all commenters identify privacy as a primary or top-tier patient concern about healthcare AI, cutting across every stakeholder type—from AI vendors to patient advocates to professional associations. Massive Bio notes that the foremost patient concern is 'What happens to my data?' as patients want to know their personal details and medical history remain confidential. The American Urological Association confirms that privacy, data security, and reliability are significant patient concerns, with secondary uses of their data concerning patients. New Jersey Innovation Institute highlights that the risk of data breaches is a significant concern as healthcare organizations are prime targets for cyberattacks. COGA Labs provides empirical validation showing that data access and control consistently ranks as top concern across their user base—ahead of accuracy, cost, or usability.

Fear of losing human connection is pervasive. A strong majority of commenters report that patients worry AI will make healthcare impersonal or replace human judgment and empathy. Massive Bio notes that many patients fear healthcare could become impersonal if AI takes on too much—they want assurance that AI is an assistant, not a replacement. Kooth reports that the central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection. HealthScoreAI observes that patients and caregivers are concerned that increased reliance on AI could erode human connection central to healing. This concern is particularly acute in mental health and behavioral health settings where therapeutic relationships are foundational.

Algorithmic bias and equity concerns are widely recognized. Most commenters identify patient concerns about AI perpetuating or amplifying existing healthcare disparities, with particular emphasis on historically underserved populations. SANCIAN LLC notes patient concerns include bias and exclusion, specifically fear of discrimination. NeuroFlow emphasizes that patients want assurance that AI systems have been validated for populations that look like them—rural patients and Veterans should not receive tools trained exclusively on urban academic medical center data. Ellipsis Health reports that patients worry AI may misinterpret them, perform worse for certain populations, or miss context that a human would understand.

Accountability gaps when AI is wrong are a core worry. Most commenters report patient concerns about unclear responsibility and lack of recourse when AI-influenced decisions cause harm. EHY Consulting identifies unclear accountability when AI influences clinical decisions or access to services. WellSpan Health notes accountability—specifically if something goes wrong, who is responsible—as a key concern. ECRI frames the central patient question as: Is AI safe, transparent, and accountable? Does someone remain clearly responsible when AI influences care?

Transparency about AI use is expected. A strong majority note that patients want to know when AI is being used in their care and how it influences decisions. Ellipsis Health observes that patients often do not know when AI is being used, what decisions it influences, or how their information is interpreted. Global Liver Institute reports patients are concerned about lack of transparency around when and how AI is used. The American Urological Association confirms that lack of transparency about when AI is used is a significant concern.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether existing frameworks like HIPAA are sufficient for AI-specific privacy risks or require significant updates.

positions.label: HIPAA Insufficient
positions.stance: Current frameworks are inadequate for AI-specific risks. COGA Labs, NeuroFlow, and Citizens' Council for Health Freedom argue that AI enables novel data aggregation and secondary uses beyond HIPAA's original scope, that behavioral health data requires heightened protections, and that patients want explicit control over retention and secondary use.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 60% of commenters addressing privacy
keyArguments: AI enables novel data aggregation and secondary uses beyond HIPAA's original scope
Behavioral health data requires heightened protections
Patients want explicit control over retention and secondary use

positions.label: HIPAA Adaptable
positions.stance: Existing frameworks can accommodate AI with guidance. Cooperative Exchange and HIMSS EHRA, primarily larger health systems and EHR vendors, argue that HIPAA provides foundational protections, additional guidance rather than new law is needed, and fragmented state requirements create compliance burden.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 25% of commenters, primarily larger health systems and EHR vendors
keyArguments: HIPAA provides foundational protections
Additional guidance rather than new law is needed
Fragmented state requirements create compliance burden

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate whether patients should be informed whenever AI influences care or whether disclosure should be proportionate to AI's role and risk level.

positions.label: Comprehensive Disclosure
positions.stance: Patients should know whenever AI influences care. Global Liver Institute, AORN, and Health AI Institute argue that informed consent requires transparency, patients cannot exercise autonomy without knowledge, and disclosure builds trust.
positions.supportLevel: Most patient advocacy groups, many clinicians, some AI vendors
keyArguments: Informed consent requires transparency
Patients cannot exercise autonomy without knowledge
Disclosure builds trust

positions.label: Contextual Disclosure
positions.stance: Disclosure should be proportionate to AI's role and risk. Some health systems and technology companies argue that over-disclosure may cause unnecessary anxiety, not all AI use is clinically significant, and disclosure requirements could slow beneficial adoption. University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT notes that more information does not necessarily translate into clarity.
positions.supportLevel: Some health systems, technology companies
keyArguments: Over-disclosure may cause unnecessary anxiety
Not all AI use is clinically significant
Disclosure requirements could slow beneficial adoption

areasOfDebate.description: Stakeholders debate whether AI fundamentally cannot replace human empathy or whether it can augment human connection by freeing clinicians for more meaningful interaction.

positions.label: Cannot Replace Empathy
positions.stance: AI has a fundamental limitation requiring human oversight. HealthScoreAI argues that AI can simulate but not genuinely experience empathy, healing requires authentic human connection, and moral responsibility cannot be delegated to algorithms.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority across stakeholder types
keyArguments: AI can simulate but not genuinely experience empathy
Healing requires authentic human connection
Moral responsibility cannot be delegated to algorithms

positions.label: Can Augment Connection
positions.stance: Technology can free clinicians for more meaningful interaction. Interstella and InStride Health argue that AI handling administrative burden allows more face time, AI can ensure consistent follow-up, and technology extends reach to underserved populations.
positions.supportLevel: Several AI vendors and health systems
keyArguments: AI handling administrative burden allows more face time
AI can ensure consistent follow-up
Technology extends reach to underserved populations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory clarity to address patient concerns without stifling innovation; need for frameworks that distinguish responsible actors from bad actors; reimbursement pathways that reward patient-centered design
specificPoints: AI vendors notably validate rather than dismiss patient concerns—many cite their own user research confirming privacy and human connection as top patient priorities, suggesting market incentives may align with patient protection
Proposed solutions include plain-language disclosures, privacy-preserving technologies (e.g., contactless radar vs. cameras), ongoing bias monitoring, and human-in-the-loop design
COGA Labs provides empirical data showing privacy outranks accuracy and cost in user priorities
TapestryHealth offers privacy-preserving monitoring as solution to 'Safety vs. Surveillance' tension

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability exposure for members when AI fails; uncompensated oversight work; preservation of professional autonomy and patient relationships
specificPoints: Emphasize that patient concerns directly affect member practice—if patients don't trust AI, they withhold information or avoid providers, undermining care quality
Proposed solutions include strong privacy and security protections, independent validation requirements, clear accountability frameworks, and public monitoring for safety and equity
American Urological Association details specific patient hopes alongside fears
American Academy of Sleep Medicine emphasizes validation across diverse populations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Operational challenges of addressing patient concerns while implementing AI; liability when AI influences decisions; integration with clinical workflows
specificPoints: Direct experience with patient reactions—both anxiety about AI and frustration when AI-generated information lacks clinical relevance
Proposed solutions include patient education, transparent disclosure practices, human oversight preservation, and validation for local populations
WellSpan Health provides comprehensive taxonomy of patient concerns from implementation experience
University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT notes patient anxiety around incidental findings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Protection of vulnerable populations; ensuring AI doesn't devalue people with chronic conditions or disabilities; maintaining human connection in care
specificPoints: Represent patient voice directly; emphasize that concerns are not hypothetical but reflect lived experience with healthcare system failures
Proposed solutions include mandatory transparency, strong privacy protections, accountability mechanisms, and coverage without increased patient costs
American Parkinson Disease Association raises specific concerns about insurance coverage and out-of-pocket costs
Global Liver Institute warns about AI devaluing chronic disease patients

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Evidence base for addressing patient concerns; systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives; technical solutions to privacy and bias
specificPoints: Provide empirical grounding for patient concerns; note that majority of Americans express concern about AI security and hallucinations
Proposed solutions include personalized patient portals, culturally appropriate communication, and research-backed governance frameworks
Computing Research Association cites majority American concern about AI security
Satyadhar Joshi identifies privacy, consent, autonomy, and equity as central ethical challenges

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Reliability and safety of AI tools affecting family members; efficient vetting processes that provide confidence; cost implications
specificPoints: Bring visceral, daily experience of managing chronic conditions and the stress of uncertainty about AI reliability
Proposed solutions include standardized, rigorous testing; patient-centered vetting processes; timely and cost-efficient evaluation
Dominick Esposito as T1D caregiver emphasizes need for confidence in tool safety and reliability

noteworthyInsights.insight: Privacy outranks accuracy in patient priorities — COGA Labs provides empirical user research showing data access and control consistently ranks as top concern across user base—ahead of accuracy, cost, or usability. This challenges assumptions that patients primarily worry about AI getting things wrong.

'Safety vs. Surveillance' tension in long-term care — TapestryHealth articulates a nuanced dilemma: Families want the safety of continuous monitoring ('never alone'), but residents fear the indignity of cameras ('Big Brother'). This frames patient concerns as legitimate trade-offs rather than irrational fears.

Patient-generated AI content creates new clinical dynamics — University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT observes patients bringing printed outputs from LLMs, treated similarly to information from internet searches, and notes these encounters can be constructive and facilitate shared understanding, particularly among technically literate patients.

Trust deficit has concrete behavioral consequences — Caroline Morrow warns that if patients don't trust AI, they will avoid providers who use it, withhold information from AI-enabled systems, or seek redundant second opinions—directly undermining AI's promised benefits.

Youth specifically fear loss of human connection — Kooth, operating the nation's first statewide free digital mental health service for youth, reports this as the central concern that both youth and practitioners have.

Patients want AI to solve real problems, not just avoid harms — Interstella notes patients want faster appointments, better communication, fewer errors, lower costs—suggesting policy should address both concerns and aspirations.

AI vendors validate patient concerns through market research — Multiple AI companies (COGA Labs, Ellipsis Health, Massive Bio) cite their own user research confirming patient concerns, suggesting market incentives may naturally align with patient protection when companies listen to users.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Setting-Specific Concern Intensity: Mental health and behavioral health settings show heightened concern about human connection and privacy of sensitive information (Kooth, NeuroFlow, InStride Health, Medvidi). Long-term care settings emphasize dignity and surveillance concerns (TapestryHealth, AORN). Acute care and surgical settings focus more on accuracy and accountability (WellSpan, AORN, MediView XR).

Population-Specific Vulnerability: Multiple commenters identify heightened concerns for historically underserved populations, rural patients, Veterans, youth, elderly, people with chronic conditions, and those with limited digital access. American Parkinson Disease Association and Global Liver Institute specifically warn about AI devaluing chronic disease patients.

Vendor-Patient Alignment: AI vendors largely validate rather than dismiss patient concerns. This may reflect genuine user research informing product development, recognition that trust is prerequisite for adoption, or strategic positioning to support reasonable regulation that disadvantages less responsible competitors.

Concern Clustering: Privacy, transparency, and accountability concerns frequently appear together—suggesting patients view these as interconnected rather than separate issues. Human connection concerns often paired with bias concerns—patients worry AI will both depersonalize care AND treat them unfairly.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Over-disclosure may cause patient anxiety (University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT). Lack of trust leads to information withholding, provider avoidance, and redundant care (Caroline Morrow). AI-generated incidental findings may create unnecessary concern orthogonal to clinical relevance.

keyQuotations.quote: Foremost patient concern is 'What happens to my data?' - they want to know that their personal details and medical history remain confidential

AI will have access to this data, also without patient consent, for purposes the patient may not support, and potentially to directly impact the patient's care in ways that could be harmful, insufficient, or generated through AI hallucinations.
Data access and control consistently ranks as top concern across user base—ahead of accuracy, cost, or usability
The central concern that both youth and practitioners have is around loss of human connection
AI can simulate empathy through language and tone but cannot genuinely experience empathy, shared suffering, or moral responsibility
Families want the safety of continuous monitoring ('never alone'), but residents fear the indignity of cameras ('Big Brother').

If patients do not trust AI-assisted care, they will avoid providers who use it, withhold information from AI-enabled systems, or seek redundant second opinions, all of which undermine the cost savings and outcome improvements that clinical AI promises.
Central patient question: Is AI safe, transparent, and accountable? Does someone remain clearly responsible when AI influences care?
There is significant concern about batch denials caused by automation.

Patients want assurance that AI systems have been validated for populations that look like them - rural patients and Veterans should not receive tools trained exclusively on urban academic medical center data

Can we have an efficient process to vet tools that is timely, is patient-centered, and cost-efficient so that we can provide families confidence that the tools being rolled out into any clinical care setting are safe and reliable?
I would be cautious about any tool that purported to be a solution without knowing that it was tested and vetted in a standardized and rigorous fashion.

Medical deepfakes can convince patients to follow harmful advice, delay care, or abandon evidence-based treatment... Deepfakes exploit trusted cues (physician's voice, image, credentials), creating a uniquely high-risk form of consumer deception in health care
Patients want AI to solve real problems: faster appointments, better communication, fewer errors, lower costs

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with patient concerns, drawing on empirical user research, clinical experience, and systematic analysis. Stakeholders across categories provide specific examples and evidence rather than abstract assertions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite user research, surveys, and implementation experience. COGA Labs provides empirical data on patient priorities. Computing Research Association cites majority American concern statistics. However, some claims about patient concerns rely on anecdotal observation rather than systematic research.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Individual patient voices are underrepresented (only 2 of 42 comments), with most patient perspective information filtered through providers, vendors, and advocacy groups. Rural and underserved population perspectives are discussed but not directly represented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The analysis reveals interconnected concerns (privacy-transparency-accountability clustering) and nuanced trade-offs (safety vs. surveillance, comprehensive vs. contextual disclosure) that resist simple policy solutions.

18.3.themeDescription: Patient Involvement in AI Governance and Design

sections.executiveSummary: There is strong consensus across all commenters—spanning advocacy groups, healthcare providers, businesses, and researchers—that patients must be meaningfully involved as partners throughout the AI development lifecycle, not merely as end recipients of AI-enabled care. The primary tension lies not in whether to involve patients, but in how to operationalize this involvement effectively, with commenters proposing various mechanisms from advisory panels to human-centered design approaches. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to establish formal expectations and structured opportunities for patient engagement at every stage from design through post-market evaluation.

consensusPoints.text: Patients should be partners, not passive recipients, in AI development. National Multiple Sclerosis Society states that individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease, holding a wealth of information that can inform the design of novel therapeutics and emerging technologies. An independent researcher advocates for a partnership approach treating workers, patients, and communities as collaborators rather than passive recipients of technological change. Global Liver Institute explicitly states patient and caregiver experience must be incorporated into design and evaluation of AI tools.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Universal agreement across all 7 comments addressing this theme

consensusPoints.text: Patient involvement should span the entire AI lifecycle. Global Liver Institute calls for patient input throughout AI lifecycle, from development and testing to deployment and post-market evaluation. National MS Society recommends engagement early and often throughout development and throughout model development process. Interstella emphasizes co-design of AI solutions from the outset.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Strong majority (at least 4 of 7 commenters explicitly address lifecycle-wide engagement)

consensusPoints.text: HHS should take an active role in facilitating patient engagement. National MS Society recommends HHS should facilitate focus groups and patient-focused product development meetings and hold series of stakeholder feedback opportunities. Global Liver Institute states HHS has opportunity to set expectations for patient and caregiver input throughout AI lifecycle. ICF Incorporated recommends HHS should support HCD approaches including intentional human-to-human engagement.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (6 of 7) direct recommendations specifically to HHS

areasOfDebate.description: While all commenters agree patients should be involved, they propose different structural approaches. These approaches are not mutually exclusive, and several commenters advocate for multiple mechanisms simultaneously. The debate is more about emphasis than opposition.

positions.label: Formal Advisory Panels
positions.stance: Establish standing patient review bodies. National MS Society proposes review process by advisory panel of individuals with lived experience. APDA demonstrates this model through their Voices of Parkinson's Council.
positions.supportLevel: 2 commenters explicitly advocate this approach
keyArguments: Provides structured, ongoing input
Ensures lived experience informs decisions systematically
Creates accountability

positions.label: Human-Centered Design Integration
positions.stance: Embed patients in design methodology. ICF Incorporated advocates Human Centered Design to position care providers and patients at center of evaluation method design. Interstella recommends co-design of AI solutions.
positions.supportLevel: 2 commenters emphasize this approach
keyArguments: Positions patients at center of evaluation alongside providers
Builds trust through intentional engagement
Accelerates adoption

positions.label: Ongoing Stakeholder Sessions
positions.stance: Regular feedback opportunities. National MS Society calls for series of stakeholder feedback opportunities. Interstella recommends soliciting patient input on priority use cases.
positions.supportLevel: 2 commenters propose this mechanism
keyArguments: Allows iterative input
Enables HHS to share strategic plans and gather responses
Creates dialogue rather than one-time consultation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patients are rarely meaningfully involved in current AI governance. AI systems may prioritize efficiency over patient needs without lived experience input. Underrepresented communities face heightened risks of exclusion. Advocacy groups uniquely emphasize that patients with chronic conditions possess irreplaceable expertise about their diseases that cannot be substituted by clinical or technical knowledge alone.
specificPoints: Formal advisory panels with disease-specific expertise
Focus groups and patient-focused product development meetings
Targeted engagement of underrepresented communities
National MS Society demonstrates sophisticated understanding of both scientific rigor and social/ethical alignment
APDA provides working model through Voices of Parkinson's Council
Global Liver Institute articulates risk of systems that prioritize efficiency or cost savings while overlooking real-world impacts

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Building patient trust in AI solutions. Making AI outputs transparent and acceptable to lay users. Accelerating adoption through engagement. Business commenters frame patient involvement as both an ethical imperative and a practical necessity for successful AI deployment.
specificPoints: Co-design approaches
Research on transparency for lay users
Human-centered design methodologies
Interstella connects patient involvement to trust-building
ICF Incorporated emphasizes intentional human-to-human engagement to build trust and accelerate adoption

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Maintaining clinician involvement in significant clinical decisions. Protecting patient autonomy. Provider perspective emphasizes patient choice and clinician oversight as complementary safeguards.
specificPoints: Allow patients to opt out of AI-assisted care without penalty
Maintain clinician involvement in all significant clinical decisions
Dr. Bethany Russell brings dual perspective as both practicing clinician and AI developer

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring partnership approach rather than passive recipient model. Patient transparency as core element of safe AI implementation. Academic framing positions patient involvement within broader governance frameworks.
specificPoints: Partnership approach treating patients as collaborators
Transparency frameworks referencing Sancian LLC framework
Satyadhar Joshi synthesizes stakeholder perspectives through systematic analysis

noteworthyInsights.insight: APDA demonstrates that patient advisory councils can effectively inform organizational programming, research, policy, and resource development—providing a replicable template for AI governance structures.

National MS Society articulates that patient input serves two distinct functions: ensuring scientific rigor AND ensuring social/ethical alignment—suggesting patient involvement is not merely about preferences but about substantive quality improvement.
ICF Incorporated makes the strategic observation that human-centered design approaches can accelerate adoption—framing patient involvement not as a barrier to innovation but as an enabler of it.
Multiple commenters explicitly include carepartners and caregivers alongside patients, recognizing the broader ecosystem of lived experience that informs care decisions.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Disease-Specific Expertise Model: Advocacy organizations representing specific conditions (MS, Parkinson's, liver disease) consistently argue that disease-specific patient expertise is essential and irreplaceable. This suggests a potential framework where AI governance includes condition-specific patient panels rather than generic patient representation.

Lifecycle Integration Pattern: Commenters consistently reject one-time consultation models in favor of continuous engagement. The pattern suggests patient involvement should be embedded in organizational processes rather than treated as a compliance checkbox.

Trust-Building as Shared Priority: Both business and advocacy commenters identify trust as a central concern, though from different angles (adoption vs. protection). This convergence suggests trust-building through patient involvement could serve as a unifying policy objective.

Underrepresentation Awareness: Multiple commenters specifically flag the need to engage underrepresented communities. This pattern indicates awareness that generic patient involvement may reproduce existing disparities without intentional inclusion efforts.

keyQuotations.quote: Individuals living with chronic conditions are the experts on their disease. These individuals, along with their carepartners and loved ones, hold a wealth of information that can inform the design of novel therapeutics and emerging technologies to support care delivery.
When patient voices and lived experience are absent, systems may prioritize efficiency or cost savings while overlooking real-world impacts on access, understanding, and trust.
Partnership approach—treating workers, patients, communities as collaborators rather than passive recipients of technological change—offers most promising path.
HHS has opportunity to set expectations for patient and caregiver input throughout AI lifecycle, from development and testing to deployment and post-market evaluation.
Must engage individuals with lived experience of MS, especially from underrepresented communities, throughout model development process.
HHS should support HCD approaches including intentional human-to-human engagement to build trust and accelerate adoption.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated understanding of both the ethical imperatives and practical considerations for patient involvement. Arguments are well-reasoned and supported by specific examples and organizational experience.

evidenceBase.explanation: While commenters provide compelling arguments and some organizational examples (such as APDA's Voices of Parkinson's Council), there is limited empirical evidence cited regarding the effectiveness of specific patient engagement mechanisms in AI governance contexts.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: The comment set lacks direct input from individual patients themselves; all patient-focused perspectives come through advocacy organizations. Additionally, there is limited representation from smaller healthcare providers or rural health systems that may face different challenges in implementing patient engagement mechanisms.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: Moderate - The core question of whether to involve patients shows clear consensus, but the implementation mechanisms present nuanced trade-offs that require careful consideration of organizational capacity, resource constraints, and diverse patient population needs.

2.1.themeDescription: Developer and Vendor Liability for Algorithmic Defects. This sub-theme specifically addresses arguments that AI developers should bear primary responsibility for defects in their products, including proposals for product liability frameworks. || It includes arguments that developers have superior knowledge of AI architecture and failure modes, concerns about vendors using contractual disclaimers to shift all risk to providers, proposals for mandatory indemnification requirements, and comparisons to pharmaceutical product liability

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across healthcare providers, professional associations, and technology companies that AI developers should bear meaningful responsibility for defects in their products, particularly for "black box" systems where clinicians cannot inspect or validate underlying algorithms. The central tension lies not in whether developers should be liable, but in how to structure shared accountability frameworks that align responsibility with control while avoiding contracting impasses that could slow beneficial AI adoption. Commenters strongly favor regulatory intervention through model contract language, mandatory indemnification requirements, and potentially novel compensation mechanisms analogous to vaccine injury programs.

consensusPoints.text: Developers possess superior knowledge justifying accountability. AI developers have exclusive access to system architecture, training data, and failure modes that end-users cannot inspect or control. CommonSpirit Health notes that vendors possess exclusive knowledge of underlying architecture, training data, and potential failure points justifying shared accountability. Health IT End-Users Alliance states developers are often best positioned to prevent harm due to their knowledge of development, function, and intended use. AMA notes that underlying algorithms are "black box" meaning physicians and patients cannot fully understand the logic leading to outputs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 12 of 18 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: AMA acknowledges that with complex machine learning algorithms, not even developers can always easily ascertain where mistakes are made, suggesting some inherent limits to developer knowledge.

consensusPoints.text: Current vendor contracts inappropriately shift all risk to providers. InStride Health notes vendor contracts typically disclaim clinical liability while clinicians and provider organizations remain fully responsible for care decisions. WellSpan Health observes current contracting practices shift disproportionate liability to providers while vendors use opaque practices. ECRI finds contracts often lack meaningful indemnity for clinical harm, hallucinated content, or latent model defects. No commenters defended current vendor disclaimer practices as appropriate or sufficient.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of healthcare provider commenters (at least 6 of 8 provider organizations)

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians should not bear liability for undetectable algorithmic errors. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association argues if a reasonable clinician could not have known that an AI output was erroneous, and it was reasonable to rely on that output based on the developer's representations, the accountability for resulting harm should rest with the AI developer. American Society of Retina Specialists states physicians should not be held accountable for errors attributable to algorithm design, training data deficiencies, or vendor-controlled system updates. American Academy of Sleep Medicine notes clinicians should not be held liable for defects in tools they did not design, train, or control.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Broad agreement across professional associations and providers

consensusPoints.text: Regulatory intervention is necessary—self-regulation is insufficient. Most commenters explicitly or implicitly call for HHS action rather than relying on market forces or voluntary standards. Psychotherapy Action Network argues leaving regulation and oversight to corporations will not promote consumer safety, privacy, or effective care. Multiple associations call for HHS to develop model contract language, strengthen regulatory guardrails, or issue formal guidance.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters agree developers should bear liability but differ on the specific mechanism for allocating responsibility between developers and deployers.

positions.label: Strict Product Liability
positions.stance: Developers control product design and should be accountable like pharmaceutical manufacturers. Psychotherapy Action Network and American Academy of Sleep Medicine argue this creates proper incentives for safety investment and that corporations must be held accountable for products they develop and sell.
positions.supportLevel: Several professional associations and advocacy groups
keyArguments: Developers control product design and should be accountable like pharmaceutical manufacturers
Creates proper incentives for safety investment
Corporations must be held accountable for products they develop and sell

positions.label: Shared Proportional Liability
positions.stance: CommonSpirit Health, California Clinical Laboratory Association, and SonderMind argue responsibility should align with control over specific aspects of deployment, noting both developers and deployers have roles in ensuring safe use and this approach avoids contracting impasses that could slow adoption.
positions.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and some technology companies
keyArguments: Responsibility should align with control over specific aspects of deployment
Both developers and deployers have roles in ensuring safe use
Avoids contracting impasses that could slow adoption

positions.label: Insurance/Compensation Fund
positions.stance: Massive Bio and Interstella explicitly propose novel mechanisms analogous to the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, arguing this could resolve contracting impasses between hospitals and vendors and spread risk across the industry rather than creating winner-take-all liability battles.
positions.supportLevel: At least 2 commenters explicitly propose novel mechanisms
keyArguments: Analogous to National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program
Could resolve contracting impasses between hospitals and vendors
Spreads risk across industry rather than creating winner-take-all liability battles

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether developer liability should encompass comprehensive accountability for safety, accuracy, and security, or focus more narrowly on clinical performance defects.

positions.label: Comprehensive Accountability
positions.stance: American Academy of Family Physicians and Health IT End-Users Alliance argue developers have direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure, and that end-users cannot inspect or control AI internals. They frame security vulnerabilities as developer-created defects.
positions.supportLevel: Multiple professional associations, particularly those focused on cybersecurity
keyArguments: Developers have direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure
End-users cannot inspect or control AI internals
Security vulnerabilities are developer-created defects

positions.label: Clinical Performance Focus
positions.stance: American College of Cardiology argues developers should be liable for specific medical diagnosis, treatment, and management use cases, noting existing models like IDx-DR and Doctronic demonstrate feasibility of developer-assumed liability for defined use cases.
positions.supportLevel: Some clinical specialty societies
keyArguments: Developers should be liable for specific medical diagnosis, treatment, and management use cases
Existing models (IDx-DR, Doctronic) demonstrate feasibility of developer-assumed liability for defined use cases

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether HHS should provide model contract language and best practices or impose mandatory requirements with strengthened enforcement.

positions.label: Model Contract Language
positions.stance: Massive Bio, ECRI, and WellSpan Health support model contract language and best practices, arguing this provides standardization without rigid mandates, reduces transaction costs and contracting friction, and allows flexibility for different risk profiles.
positions.supportLevel: Broad support across provider and vendor commenters
keyArguments: Provides standardization without rigid mandates
Reduces transaction costs and contracting friction
Allows flexibility for different risk profiles

positions.label: Mandatory Requirements
positions.stance: American Academy of Family Physicians and American Dental Association argue voluntary approaches are insufficient to change vendor behavior, calling for enforceable cybersecurity and accountability requirements and strengthened HIPAA requirements for vendors.
positions.supportLevel: Several professional associations
keyArguments: Voluntary approaches insufficient to change vendor behavior
Need enforceable cybersecurity and accountability requirements
HIPAA requirements should be strengthened for vendors

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physicians bearing all liability for tools they cannot inspect or control, "black box" algorithms preventing meaningful validation, and perverse incentives discouraging AI adoption if physicians bear all risk. AMA emphasizes that if developers have no accountability, there will be very little incentive for physicians to meaningfully engage with them.
specificPoints: Developer liability for undetectable errors
Framework encouraging developers to assume liability for specific use cases
Strengthened regulatory guardrails

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor contracts disclaiming all clinical liability, disproportionate liability burden on providers, and lack of transparency about AI system limitations and updates. These organizations have direct operational experience with vendor contracting practices and can identify specific contractual gaps.
specificPoints: Shared-risk contracting models
Standardized Business Associate Agreement requirements
Mandatory vendor disclosure of bias, harm, or negative findings post-deployment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Contracting impasses between hospitals seeking indemnification and vendors wary of open-ended liability, need for regulatory clarity to enable responsible deployment, and balancing accountability with innovation incentives. These commenters acknowledge tension from the vendor side and some are willing to accept liability with appropriate frameworks.
specificPoints: Model contract language from HHS
Insurance pools or compensation funds
Liability insurance requirements for developers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor disclaimers laundering responsibility away from developers, system designs that prevent meaningful human intervention while disclaiming responsibility, and lack of meaningful indemnity for clinical harm or latent defects. These commenters focus on structural and design-level accountability mechanisms.
specificPoints: Anti-evasion design requirements
Model contract clauses with realistic indemnification
Vendor obligations for safety incidents and vulnerability disclosure

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Corporate accountability for products developed and sold, consumer safety, privacy, and effective care. Psychotherapy Action Network frames the issue as fundamental corporate responsibility rather than technical liability allocation.
specificPoints: Corporations must be held accountable
Self-regulation rejected as insufficient

noteworthyInsights.insight: Multiple commenters draw explicit parallels to pharmaceutical product liability, suggesting AI should follow similar accountability frameworks. Massive Bio specifically proposes a compensation fund analogous to the National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program.

Successful developer-assumed liability models already exist. American College of Cardiology identifies IDx-DR (LumineticsCore) and Doctronic as examples where developers have successfully utilized liability insurance to protect against harm from autonomous AI tools, demonstrating this approach is feasible, not theoretical.

Independent researcher Kanav Jain introduces a novel concept of anti-evasion design requirements that prevent vendors from disclaiming responsibility while their system design prevents meaningful human intervention. This addresses the structural contradiction where vendors claim tools are decision support while designing systems that preclude meaningful human override.

AMA articulates a fundamental trust paradox: Claims that physicians should never rely on the outputs of the technology would seem to obviate the need for utilizing the tool in the first place. If physicians must independently verify every AI output, the efficiency gains disappear.

American Dental Association identifies a regulatory blind spot: Administrative tasks not supported by practice management or health record systems are often performed through workaround applications that may not require business associate agreements, shifting compliance accountability to clinicians.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder alignment across traditional divides: Unusually, technology companies and healthcare providers largely agree on the need for clearer liability frameworks—they differ primarily on mechanism rather than principle. This suggests potential for consensus-based solutions.

Specialty-specific concerns: Retina specialists (ASRS) have direct experience with first FDA-cleared AI device and emphasize vendor-controlled system updates as liability concern. Sleep medicine (AASM) emphasizes long-term therapy decisions driven by AI diagnostics. Mental health providers (PsiAN, InStride, SonderMind) emphasize unique risks in behavioral health AI.

Size and resource disparities: Multiple commenters note that smaller providers, rural facilities, and safety-net organizations face disproportionate challenges in negotiating favorable vendor contracts or conducting independent AI validation. This suggests liability frameworks should account for provider capacity.

Cybersecurity as liability issue: AAFP, Health IT End-Users Alliance, and ADA frame cybersecurity vulnerabilities as a species of product defect, arguing developers should bear responsibility for security failures just as they would for clinical performance failures.

Post-deployment accountability gap: CommonSpirit Health and others emphasize that liability should extend to post-deployment discoveries of bias or harm, with mandatory disclosure requirements—suggesting current frameworks focus too heavily on pre-market approval and not enough on ongoing accountability.

keyQuotations.quote: Should developers of AI technologies have no accountability for the performance of their tool, there will be very little incentive for physicians to meaningfully engage with them, knowing they bear all the risk for its performance. Claims that physicians should never rely on the outputs of the technology would seem to obviate the need for utilizing the tool in the first place.

If a reasonable clinician could not have known – or had no reason to know – that an AI output was erroneous, and it was reasonable to rely on that output based on the developer's representations, the accountability for resulting harm to patients should rest with the AI developer.
AI developers, not end-users, are best positioned to mitigate and respond to cyberattacks given their direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure.
Corporations must be held accountable for products they develop and sell. Leaving regulation and oversight to corporations will not promote consumer safety, privacy, or effective care.
Hospitals often seek indemnification from AI vendors for any harm caused by their software, but vendors are wary of open-ended liability.
Vendor disclaimers are being used to launder responsibility away from developers.

Rapid expansion of AI tools places significant responsibility on providers to judge validation and performance of products, implement and maintain privacy and cybersecurity measures, and assess vendor reliability.
IDx-DR (LumineticsCore) and Doctronic have successfully utilized liability insurance to protect against harm from autonomous AI tools.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of liability frameworks, draw on concrete examples and analogies, and engage substantively with the tension between accountability and innovation. Multiple stakeholder types provide detailed, well-reasoned positions with specific recommendations.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters cite specific examples of existing liability models (IDx-DR, Doctronic), reference established frameworks (National Vaccine Injury Compensation Program, pharmaceutical liability), and draw on direct operational experience with vendor contracting. Some claims about market dynamics are asserted rather than documented.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from AI developers themselves defending current disclaimer practices. No comments from liability insurers who would be central to proposed insurance pool mechanisms. Small and rural providers are discussed but few direct comments from these organizations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of product liability law, healthcare regulation, contract law, cybersecurity, and emerging technology governance. Commenters recognize inherent tensions between accountability and innovation incentives.

2.2.themeDescription: Clinician Liability and Safe Harbor Proposals. This sub-theme addresses when individual clinicians should be protected from liability for AI-assisted decisions, including proposals for safe harbor conditions. || It includes arguments that clinicians following validated AI recommendations in good faith should be protected, concerns about expanded malpractice exposure from AI adoption, debates about whether AI recommendations could become the standard of care, and proposals for safe harbors tied to documented governance practices. This excludes developer liability and focuses on protecting individual practitioners. The key distinction is focus on professional liability rather than organizational or product liability

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that clinicians currently face an untenable "no-win" liability situation when using AI tools—potentially liable both for following and for overriding AI recommendations—creating a significant barrier to beneficial AI adoption. While nearly all stakeholders agree that some form of safe harbor protection is needed, they diverge on the conditions, scope, and whether liability should remain primarily with clinicians or be shared with AI developers. The dominant recommendation thrust favors federal guidance establishing safe harbors tied to documented governance practices, validated AI tools, and good-faith clinical oversight, though the precise allocation of responsibility between clinicians, organizations, and developers remains actively debated.

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians face an untenable "no-win" liability position where they face liability exposure whether they follow or override AI recommendations, creating adoption hesitancy. The AMA states that assigning liability to physicians on the basis they should not depend on AI outputs while also exposing them to penalties for doing so places them in an untenable, no-win position. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association notes that holding healthcare providers liable both for relying on AI and for exercising independent judgment against AI recommendations places them in an untenable position. ACC notes great uncertainty exists on potential risks and liability when clinicians deviate from AI-enabled software outputs.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 15 of 24 comments explicitly addressing this)

exceptions.text: While all acknowledge the problem, some commenters such as CJM Strategic Consulting argue this is not fundamentally different from existing liability for misinterpreting diagnostic tools—the AI is simply another instrument.

consensusPoints.text: Safe harbor protections are needed for clinicians using AI tools appropriately. Massive Bio recommends clarifying that clinicians remain the final authority, but providing legal safe harbors for providers who follow recommendations from HHS-certified or validated AI tools. AADA recommends establishing safe harbor that clarifies and limits physician liability when AI is used as support tool in cases of documented clinical oversight, compliance protocols, and diagnostics. WellSpan Health recommends clarifying safe-harbor conditions for providers following approved governance practices.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (at least 16 of 24)

exceptions.text: No commenters explicitly opposed safe harbors, though some emphasized conditions and limitations.

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians should retain final decision-making authority, with AI serving in an advisory capacity. InStride Health states that AI-generated outputs should be advisory and require human review without shifting accountability away from licensed professionals. Massive Bio emphasizes that clinicians remain the final authority and thus primarily liable for AI-assisted decisions. ACC notes that reiterating that AI tools inform rather than dictate decision-making will help ensure clinicians remain in charge.

Liability uncertainty is a significant barrier to AI adoption, even when tools could benefit patients. New Jersey Innovation Institute notes that if providers fear being held accountable for decisions influenced by AI they may be reluctant to implement AI tools in their practice. SonderMind describes a chilling effect where providers avoid beneficial AI tools entirely rather than accept uninsurable risk. Penn NeuroVERT reports physicians are hesitant to use tools when effectively assuming all liability unless tool is clearly helpful or demonstrably improves outcomes.

areasOfDebate.topic: Allocation of Liability Between Clinicians and Developers
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether clinicians should retain primary responsibility as decision-makers or whether responsibility should be distributed among clinicians, organizations, and developers.

positions.label: Clinician-Centric Liability
positions.stance: Clinicians retain primary responsibility as the decision-maker. CJM Strategic Consulting argues that AI tool is an instrument and clinician is the decision-maker, no different from clinician who misinterprets diagnostic image or disregards contraindication. Bethany Russell, Ph.D. states that clinicians using AI decision support as intended retain professional liability.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6-8 commenters, primarily individual clinicians and some healthcare consultants
keyArguments: AI is simply another diagnostic instrument; clinicians have always been responsible for interpreting tools
Professional standards already govern clinical judgment regardless of information source
Clinicians choose to use AI and must exercise appropriate oversight

positions.label: Shared/Distributed Liability
positions.stance: Responsibility should be allocated among clinicians, organizations, and developers. AAFP recommends shared liability frameworks should appropriately allocate responsibility among physicians, health care organizations, and AI developers. AMA argues that if a reasonable physician did not know or have reason to know an AI error occurred when it should have been reasonable to rely on the outputs, the developer should be accountable for resulting harm. SonderMind states that provider should bear responsibility for clinical decision to use AI tool and standard of care, not for technical failures they cannot control.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters, primarily professional associations and health systems
keyArguments: Clinicians cannot control, detect, or fix technical failures in AI systems
Standard malpractice insurance doesn't cover third-party software failures
Developers have superior knowledge of system limitations and should share accountability

areasOfDebate.topic: Whether AI Recommendations Should Become Standard of Care
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI outputs should remain advisory or could become presumptive standards of care.

positions.label: AI Should Not Define Standard
positions.stance: AI outputs should remain advisory, not presumptive standards. ACC states that AI/ML-driven solutions should not be considered or presumed as the new standard of care. ASRS argues physicians should retain clinical judgment and oversight but not be penalized for reasonable reliance on validated tools or choosing not to use AI where standards unsettled. AASM warns that if AI use becomes standard of care, practices lacking financial resources to adopt may face disproportionate legal exposure.
positions.supportLevel: At least 4 commenters explicitly address this, all opposing AI as standard of care
keyArguments: Treating AI as standard of care would undermine clinical autonomy
Practices lacking resources to adopt AI would face disproportionate legal exposure
AI tools are not yet mature enough to establish care standards

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether safe harbors should be broad (covering any validated/certified AI tool) or conditional (tied to specific governance practices).

positions.label: Broad Safe Harbors
positions.stance: Protection for using any validated/certified AI tool as intended. ATA Action recommends HHS establish safe harbors for those who deploy AI tools in accordance with FDA authorization, labeling, cybersecurity expectations, and recognized governance frameworks. NCPDP states clinicians using CMS-approved, validated AI tools should have safe-harbor protections. Interstella suggests considering a qualified AI safe harbor where if an AI tool meets HHS-endorsed standards, providers using it as intended could have mitigated liability.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters
keyArguments: Reduces adoption barriers and encourages responsible innovation
Aligns with existing FDA authorization frameworks
Provides clarity needed for clinical decision-making

positions.label: Conditional Safe Harbors
positions.stance: Protection tied to specific governance practices, documentation, and oversight. AADA recommends establishing safe harbor that clarifies and limits physician liability when AI is used as support tool in cases of documented clinical oversight, compliance protocols, and diagnostics. WellSpan Health recommends clarifying safe-harbor conditions for providers following approved governance practices. Satyadhar Joshi recommends creating safe harbors for organizations implementing defined governance practices.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6-8 commenters
keyArguments: Ensures accountability and quality assurance
Prevents blanket immunity that could reduce vigilance
Aligns protection with responsible implementation

areasOfDebate.description: Whether clinicians need explicit protection when exercising independent judgment against AI recommendations.

positions.label: Explicit Protection Needed
positions.stance: Clinicians should be protected when exercising independent judgment against AI. AFSCME argues that disregarding AI recommendations should not be basis for employer discipline of health care workers or licensing board disciplinary proceedings. Satyadhar Joshi notes clinicians face unresolved questions about what protections exist when they override AI recommendations that later prove correct. ASRS states physicians should not be penalized for reasonable reliance on validated tools or choosing not to use AI where standards unsettled.
positions.supportLevel: At least 5 commenters explicitly address this
keyArguments: Clinical judgment must remain paramount
Fear of liability for overriding AI undermines professional autonomy
Workers should not face employer discipline for exercising judgment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Members face liability exposure from both following and overriding AI, lack of clear federal guidance creates practice uncertainty, and potential for AI to become de facto standard of care without adequate validation. These associations represent large physician constituencies with direct malpractice exposure and emphasize that existing malpractice frameworks were not designed for AI-assisted care. They are concerned about resource disparities where smaller practices may face disproportionate exposure.
specificPoints: Federal safe harbor guidance tied to validated tools and documented oversight
Shared liability frameworks allocating responsibility among clinicians, organizations, and developers
Collaboration with DOJ, OIG, and state medical societies on model legislation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Operational uncertainty about liability when deploying AI across clinical workflows, fear of legal exposure creating barriers to adoption of beneficial tools, and state-by-state regulatory fragmentation complicating multi-state operations. These organizations experience implementing AI at scale across diverse care settings and face both organizational and individual clinician liability considerations, encountering practical challenges with vendor contracting and insurance coverage.
specificPoints: HHS regulations affirming reasonable reliance on validated tools does not create expanded liability
Model liability allocation frameworks distinguishing clinical decisions from technical failures
Safe harbors tied to risk-appropriate validation, monitoring, and transparency

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Frontline accountability for AI-influenced decisions in high-stakes care, hesitancy to adopt tools when assuming full liability burden, and need for clarity on professional responsibility boundaries. These commenters have direct experience with liability ambiguity in clinical practice and understand practical workflow implications of AI integration, though some view AI as simply another diagnostic instrument within existing frameworks.
specificPoints: Time-limited federal safe harbors during declared emergencies proposed by Dr. Randhawa
Semi-indemnification or shared-responsibility models recommended by Penn NeuroVERT
Guidance confirming clinicians retain professional liability when using AI as intended

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty impeding market development and adoption, need for clear frameworks to enable responsible innovation, and fraud and abuse law implications for AI-driven care coordination. These companies develop and deploy AI tools, understand technical capabilities and limitations, face both developer liability questions and provider adoption barriers, and have experience with state legislative efforts to assign liability.
specificPoints: Qualified AI safe harbors for tools meeting HHS-endorsed standards
Clarification that transparent administrative tools are not Predictive DSIs
OIG guidance on care coordination safe harbor application to AI-driven engagement

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Healthcare workers may face employer discipline for overriding AI, licensing board consequences for exercising professional judgment, and protection of worker autonomy and job security. AFSCME represents frontline workers who may have less institutional power than physicians and focuses on employment consequences, not just malpractice liability, emphasizing worker protections beyond traditional liability frameworks.
specificPoints: Establish that disregarding AI recommendations cannot be basis for employer discipline
Establish that disregarding AI recommendations cannot be basis for licensing board proceedings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance frameworks for responsible AI implementation, adoption barriers created by liability uncertainty, and need for evidence-based policy development. These institutions provide systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives and peer-reviewed literature, have direct clinical research experience with AI in specialized workflows such as stroke triage, and focus on innovation ecosystem health.
specificPoints: Safe harbors tied to defined governance practices
Semi-indemnification frameworks for appropriate AI use
Clear liability frameworks to enable innovation

noteworthyInsights.insight: Insurance gap for AI-related liability: SonderMind identifies a critical gap noting that standard malpractice insurance covers clinical negligence, not third-party software failures. This suggests current insurance products may not adequately cover AI-related risks, creating uninsurable exposure for clinicians.

Emergency-specific safe harbors: Neurosurgeon Dr. Randhawa proposes time-limited federal safe-harbor mechanisms during declared staffing emergencies, recognizing that crisis conditions may warrant different liability frameworks than routine care.

Fraud and abuse implications for AI-driven engagement: Waymark raises an underexplored issue noting that when AI systems optimize member engagement strategies, questions arise about whether personalized outreach constitutes remuneration that could influence referrals. This extends liability concerns beyond malpractice into fraud and abuse territory.

Administrative AI as distinct category: Penguin Ai argues that clarifying transparent administrative tools are not Predictive DSIs creates a safe harbor encouraging market to adopt audit-ready automation immediately, suggesting different liability frameworks may be appropriate for clinical vs. administrative AI.

State legislative activity creating urgency: SonderMind reports being actively engaged with state legislatures on proposed bills holding providers fully responsible for all aspects of AI system performance, indicating that absent federal action, states are moving forward with potentially inconsistent approaches.

Resource equity concerns: AASM notes that if AI use becomes standard of care, practices lacking financial resources to adopt may face disproportionate legal exposure, highlighting how liability frameworks could exacerbate healthcare disparities.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic/Jurisdictional Variations: State-by-state regulatory fragmentation is forcing multi-state providers to make difficult operational decisions. SonderMind reports excluding some states from AI offerings due to inconsistent requirements. MHA (Massachusetts) and regional health systems emphasize need for federal preemption or harmonization. AAFP recommends collaboration with NCSL and state medical societies on model legislation.
