positions.supportLevel: 1 commenter
keyArguments: Patients want smart, real-time, low cost, and maximally effective apps
Current TEFCA/IAS barriers favor incumbents

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters differ on whether to frame patient rights as ownership or custodianship/control

positions.label: Ownership Model
positions.stance: A patient advocate, researcher, and physician use ownership language, arguing that patients own their data as property. Providers currently treat portal data as their own, and ownership establishes clear legal rights.
positions.supportLevel: 3 of 6 commenters
keyArguments: Providers currently treat portal data as their own
Ownership establishes clear legal rights

positions.label: Custodianship/Control Model
positions.stance: A software architect and health IT company prefer custodianship/control framing, focusing on practical access and direction of data flows rather than property rights debates.
positions.supportLevel: 2 of 6 commenters
keyArguments: Focuses on practical access and direction of data flows rather than property rights debates

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Administrative burden of current systems; restoring patient-physician relationship primacy over institutional interests
specificPoints: Only stakeholder to explicitly connect patient data control to payment reform and administrative simplification
Dr. Kordonowy proposes AI could generate prior authorization packets, submit notifications automatically, produce cost transparency, and execute payment upon service confirmation
Proposed solutions include federal rulemaking for Patient Record Custodianship Rights, Patient Health Wallet standard, and AI as patient-authorized agent for administrative tasks

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Incumbent EHR vendor lock-in; barriers to innovation; ensuring market forces can operate on portable data
specificPoints: Emphasize that patient control enables business innovation and competition, not just individual rights
1upHealth argues patients should get AI-optimized medical advice in forms they choose rather than being bound to provider portals
Proposed solutions include aggregate record access, level playing field for AI tools, and removal of TEFCA/IAS barriers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Glacial pace of healthcare R&D; providers treating patient data as their own; lack of informed consent
specificPoints: Only stakeholder to frame data ownership in context of rare/complex disease research needs
24-year Parkinson's patient notes patients with rare diseases have near 0% chance of relief under current procedures
Proposed solutions include national standards for patient-facing data collection preserving ownership and informed consent while enabling longitudinal tracking

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Incomplete records degrading AI safety, quality, and equity; need for structural rather than portal-based solutions
specificPoints: Most detailed technical architecture proposal; explicitly distinguishes patient-designated routing from consumer app strategies
Emphasizes patients do not need to use an app for this to work - designation occurs at registration with minimal friction

Proposed solutions include one-time patient designation of certified longitudinal endpoint and persistent routing pointer that is patient-controlled, privacy-preserving, and designed to avoid enabling unauthorized tracking

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI governance; clear protections for patient data
specificPoints: Frames patient ownership within broader AI governance framework
Independent researcher with international credentials
Patients should retain ownership with clear protections

noteworthyInsights.insight: Patient control doesn't require patient engagement - A software architect makes crucial distinction that patient-designated routing is not a consumer portal strategy and patients do not need to use an app for this to work. This reframes patient control from active consumer engagement to passive structural protection.

Existing designation models provide precedent - Patients already make durable healthcare designations (insurance plans, pharmacies, PCPs, consent forms) suggesting patient-designated endpoints follow established patterns rather than requiring new behaviors.

Routing pointer architecture preserves privacy - Technical insight that the pointer should say where the longitudinal record lives, not what is in it offers elegant solution to tension between accessibility and privacy.

Patient motivation as market force - HealthScoreAI uniquely frames patients as most motivated stakeholders to demand portability, suggesting patient control could harness consumer pressure to solve problems regulation alone cannot.

Chronic disease perspective on urgency - A 24-year Parkinson's patient provides visceral context: patients with rare diseases have near 0% chance of relief under current systems, framing data control as life-or-death rather than abstract rights issue.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Convergence Across Stakeholder Types - Notably, providers, patients, businesses, and technical experts all arrive at similar conclusions about patient control despite different starting points and interests. This cross-stakeholder alignment is unusual and suggests genuine consensus rather than interest-group positioning.

Technical Sophistication Gradient - Comments range from principled statements about patients owning their data to detailed technical architectures including routing pointers and wallet standards. Policymakers may need to bridge between these levels of specificity.

AI as Enabler, Not Threat - Unlike many healthcare AI discussions focused on risks, these commenters uniformly frame AI as beneficial when combined with patient-controlled data. Patient control is positioned as the safeguard that makes AI safe, not as protection against AI.

Implicit Critique of TEFCA/Current Infrastructure - Multiple commenters implicitly or explicitly critique current interoperability approaches as insufficient or captured by incumbents, suggesting patient-centered approaches as alternative path.

Free-Market and Patient-Rights Alignment - Unusual coalition emerges between free-market healthcare advocates and patient rights perspectives, both finding common ground in patient control as solution to institutional capture.

keyQuotations.quote: Patient-designated routing improves equity by making longitudinal completeness a default expectation across sites rather than a privilege of being 'in the right health system.'
keyQuotations.sourceType: Software Architect/Former Director of Medical Informatics

keyQuotations.quote: This restores medicine to its proper relationship: patient and doctor first, institutions second.
Each patient owns his or her data - providers do not own the data despite treating portal information as their own for research purposes.
Patients are most motivated stakeholders to demand portability, continuity, and meaningful use of their data.
Patients want apps that are smart, real-time, low cost, and maximally effective - they do not want to be bound to incumbent 'portals.'
The routing pointer should say 'where the longitudinal record lives,' not 'what is in it' - access to content remains consent-governed and auditable.
Critically: patients do not need to 'use an app' for this to work.
Only parties with actual right to patient data are the patient, their providers, and their payer.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments span multiple stakeholder types with substantive technical and policy proposals. Cross-stakeholder alignment suggests genuine consensus rather than interest-group positioning.

evidenceBase.explanation: Comments include personal experience, technical expertise, and business perspectives, but limited empirical data or research citations. Technical proposals are detailed but largely theoretical.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: No hospital administrators, large health systems, EHR vendors, or privacy advocacy organizations are represented. Geographic diversity is limited to primarily national scope with Florida and Virginia representation. The absence of opposing voices concerned about patient capacity to manage data, liability issues, or security risks of decentralization may skew toward pro-patient-control perspectives.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - Technical architecture proposals require bridging between principled statements and detailed implementation specifications

4.2.themeDescription: Laboratory Test Harmonization Across Platforms. This sub-theme specifically addresses the lack of standardization in laboratory test results across platforms and its impact on AI development and deployment. || It includes concerns that different testing platforms produce different numeric results for the same analyte, proposals for increased CDC funding for harmonization efforts, and arguments that this variability degrades AI model performance when data is pooled across institutions. This excludes other data quality issues and focuses specifically on laboratory result comparability

sections.executiveSummary: There is unanimous agreement among commenters that laboratory test variability across platforms represents a fundamental barrier to AI development in healthcare, with all three submissions identifying this as a critical infrastructure problem rather than a minor technical inconvenience. The central tension lies between the scientific complexity of the problem—where mathematical conversion between measurement systems is often invalid, not merely imprecise—and the inadequate federal funding currently allocated to address it. Commenters converge on recommending increased CDC funding and implementation of comprehensive harmonization infrastructure including standardized calibration protocols, method-specific semantic identifiers, and continuous quality monitoring.

consensusPoints.text: All commenters identify laboratory data variability as a primary obstacle to healthcare AI development and deployment. ADLM explicitly states this is one of the leading barriers to AI use in healthcare settings. Independent researcher Joshi confirms laboratory data heterogeneity is a leading barrier to AI use in healthcare. HealthFramework's Jelatancev demonstrates through technical examples how this variability renders longitudinal analysis invalid.

All commenters agree that simple mathematical conversion between units does not solve the underlying comparability problem. Jelatancev states definitively that unit conversion alone is insufficient when the measurement procedure differs. ADLM notes that each value may be accurate within a specific measurement system, but numeric differences between systems cause problems. Joshi references the need for metadata inclusion for test methods and validation parameters beyond simple numeric conversion.

All commenters identify degraded AI model performance when data is pooled across institutions as a direct consequence of non-harmonization. ADLM warns that non-harmonized laboratory results degrade model performance when data are pooled across institutions. Joshi echoes that different testing platforms produce varying numeric results for the same analyte, degrading model performance when data is pooled across institutions. Jelatancev provides concrete example of triglyceride values from different laboratories leading to AI-generated insights that may appear contradictory.

areasOfDebate.description: Given the limited number of comments, explicit debates are minimal. However, implicit differences in emphasis emerge between focusing on semantic infrastructure versus federal funding and standards. These approaches are complementary rather than mutually exclusive, representing different entry points to the same problem.

positions.label: Semantic Infrastructure First
positions.stance: Focus on upstream semantic validation and method-specific coding. HealthFramework CEO emphasizes that non-comparable results require distinct LOINC codes, a governed semantic validation layer is needed upstream, and transformation logic must be stabilized at point of reuse.
positions.supportLevel: Emphasized by technology entrepreneur (1 of 3)
keyArguments: Non-comparable results require distinct LOINC codes
Governed semantic validation layer needed upstream
Transformation logic must be stabilized at point of reuse

positions.label: Federal Funding & Standards
positions.stance: Prioritize increased government investment in harmonization programs. ADLM and independent researcher Joshi emphasize that current $2M annual CDC funding is insufficient, standardized calibration protocols are needed across assay systems, and validation requirements across diverse clinical settings are required.
positions.supportLevel: Emphasized by trade association and academic researcher (2 of 3)
keyArguments: Current $2M annual CDC funding is insufficient
Need standardized calibration protocols across assay systems
Require validation requirements across diverse clinical settings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Association for Diagnostics & Laboratory Medicine (ADLM) raises concerns about insufficient federal funding for harmonization efforts, systematic errors introduced into risk scores and clinical decision support systems, and difficulty evaluating AI performance across diverse health systems.
specificPoints: Only commenter to quantify current federal investment ($2M annually since FY 2018) and explicitly call for increased FY27 budget allocation
Represents laboratory professionals who must validate and monitor AI tools under CLIA regulations
Proposes additional HHS funding beyond current CDC allocation
Bridges the gap between technical laboratory operations and AI deployment challenges

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: HealthFramework (Daniela Jelatancev, CEO) raises concerns about scientific invalidity of comparing results across different measurement procedures, specific high-risk analytes (enzyme activity vs. mass, immunoassay vs. mass spectrometry), and misinterpretation of longitudinal trends.
specificPoints: Only commenter to provide detailed technical taxonomy of problematic measurement types (CK-MB, insulin, FSH, testosterone)
Emphasizes that comparison is invalid not merely imprecise
Proposes method-specific semantic differentiation, governed hybrid semantic validation layer upstream, and distinct LOINC codes for non-comparable results
Frames the problem as requiring semantic infrastructure rather than just standardization protocols

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Satyadhar Joshi, Independent Researcher, raises concerns about laboratory data heterogeneity as barrier to AI and the need for comprehensive infrastructure approach.
specificPoints: Synthesizes ADLM's proposed Laboratory Data Harmonization Infrastructure into five concrete components
Provides systematic framework drawing on peer-reviewed literature

Proposes standardized calibration protocols, metadata inclusion for test methods, uniform sample collection/reporting guidelines, validation requirements across settings, and continuous quality monitoring (daily QC, proficiency testing, trend analysis)
Offers most comprehensive operational framework for implementation

noteworthyInsights.insight: Technical precision matters for policy: HealthFramework's Jelatancev makes the crucial distinction that cross-platform comparison is invalid rather than merely imprecise—a framing that elevates this from a quality improvement issue to a patient safety concern requiring regulatory attention.

Comprehensive infrastructure framework: Independent researcher Joshi synthesizes ADLM's proposed Laboratory Data Harmonization Infrastructure into five actionable components, providing policymakers with a ready-made implementation roadmap spanning calibration, metadata, collection, validation, and monitoring.

Upstream vs. downstream solutions: Jelatancev uniquely argues that semantic validation must occur upstream at the point of data creation rather than attempting to reconcile incompatible data downstream—an architectural insight with significant implications for where regulatory requirements should be placed.

Specific high-risk analytes identified: The comment provides a taxonomy of particularly problematic measurement types that could inform prioritization of harmonization efforts: enzyme activity measurements, biological potency assays, and immunoassay vs. mass spectrometry comparisons.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Cross-sector consensus: Unusual alignment between business, trade association, and academic perspectives suggests this is a well-recognized technical problem rather than a contested policy question. The debate is not whether to act, but how to act and at what scale.

Implicit acknowledgment of resource constraints: While only ADLM explicitly quantifies the funding gap, all commenters propose solutions that would require significant investment—suggesting shared recognition that current resources are inadequate.

High expertise level: All three comments demonstrate deep technical understanding of laboratory science and AI systems, suggesting this issue has not yet reached broader public awareness but is well-understood by affected professionals.

Multi-pronged approach emerging: Comments collectively outline a comprehensive solution spanning semantic infrastructure (Jelatancev), federal funding (ADLM), and operational protocols (Joshi)—suggesting effective policy will require action across multiple domains simultaneously.

keyQuotations.quote: AI systems that use laboratory data often assume that test results are comparable across different testing sites and methods. In practice, this is often not the case.
In such cases, longitudinal comparison is not merely imprecise—it is invalid.
Laboratory data heterogeneity is a leading barrier to AI use in healthcare.

In laboratory contexts involving enzyme activity versus mass (e.g., CK-MB), biological potency (e.g., insulin, FSH), or immunoassay-based versus mass spectrometry-based results (e.g., testosterone), mathematical conversion is scientifically invalid.

A patient whose triglyceride values are reported over time by different laboratories—one using mg/dL, another using mmol/L, with differing reference ranges and assay context—without explicit normalization rules and provenance, longitudinal trends may be misinterpreted, clinical thresholds may be applied inconsistently, and AI-generated insights may appear contradictory.
Non-harmonized results introduce systematic errors into risk scores and clinical decision support systems that depend on numeric thresholds.
Congress has provided CDC with $2 million annually since FY 2018 to work on laboratory test harmonization.
Apply method-specific semantic differentiation before longitudinal analysis occurs to improve precision and consistency over time.

discourseQuality.explanation: All three comments demonstrate deep technical understanding of laboratory science and AI systems, with specific examples, quantified data, and actionable recommendations.

evidenceBase.explanation: Comments cite specific analyte examples, congressional appropriations history, peer-reviewed literature, and operational experience under CLIA regulations.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: This issue has not yet reached broader public awareness; comments represent only affected professionals (trade association, technology entrepreneur, academic researcher) with no patient advocacy or general public perspectives.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High technical complexity requiring specialized knowledge of laboratory science, measurement systems, and AI model development.

4.3.themeDescription: Semantic Interoperability and Data Meaning Preservation

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types that current interoperability standards successfully enable data movement but fundamentally fail to preserve data meaning—a gap that poses serious risks for AI systems making clinical and administrative decisions. Commenters spanning healthcare technology vendors, professional associations, academic researchers, and individual experts converge on the diagnosis while offering varied but complementary solutions, from semantic validation layers to standardized terminologies to ontological harmonization research. The dominant thrust is that semantic interoperability must be treated as a regulatory and technical priority, not merely a "nice-to-have" enhancement, because AI systems will amplify the consequences of inconsistent data interpretation at scale.

consensusPoints.text: Data movement without data meaning is insufficient for AI. Nearly all commenters explicitly state that current interoperability standards enable syntactic exchange but fail to preserve semantic meaning, creating fundamental barriers to safe and effective AI deployment. This consensus spans trade associations (AMA, ADLM, ADA), technology vendors (Interstella, Penguin Ai, HealthFramework), academic researchers (Health AI Institute, SKAF), and individual experts (Amir Abrams, David Bynon).

AI systems will amplify semantic inconsistencies with harmful consequences. Commenters warn that AI systems will draw incorrect conclusions from data that appears complete but lacks necessary context, with patient safety and clinical accuracy at stake.

Standardized terminologies and coding systems are essential foundations. Commenters agree that consistent terminologies, coding systems, and vocabulary standards are prerequisites for semantic interoperability.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters propose different technical and policy approaches to achieving semantic interoperability, ranging from validation mechanisms to research investments to regulatory mandates.

positions.label: Semantic Validation Layers
positions.stance: Deploy explicit validation mechanisms at multiple points in the data pipeline to ensure semantic consistency before AI consumption. HealthFramework and David Bynon/TPI advocate for deterministic processing for unit normalization, reference alignment, and semantic validation with explicit provenance and audit logs.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters, primarily technology vendors and architects
keyArguments: Deterministic processing for unit normalization, reference alignment, and semantic validation with explicit provenance and audit logs
Validation can occur at source systems, before FHIR publication, or downstream before AI consumption
Provides measurable, auditable semantic quality

positions.label: Ontological Harmonization Research
positions.stance: Fund research into scalable methods for resolving semantic inconsistencies across disparate data systems. Scientix.ai and SKAF emphasize that hybrid mapping systems coupling classical lexical matchers with LLMs show substantial improvements.
positions.supportLevel: 3 commenters, primarily research-oriented organizations
keyArguments: Hybrid mapping systems coupling classical lexical matchers with LLMs show substantial improvements
Addresses root cause rather than symptoms
Enables AI models to reason from consistent definitions

positions.label: Mandatory Standardized Reporting
positions.stance: Require specific quantitative metrics and definitions through regulatory mandate. ASRS and Cooperative Exchange argue that voluntary adoption has failed to achieve consistency.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters, primarily specialty associations and clearinghouses
keyArguments: Voluntary adoption has failed to achieve consistency
Clinical metrics like visual acuity lack standardized definitions
Administrative terms like "medical necessity" vary by payer, preventing AI scalability

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters differ on whether semantic standardization should focus primarily on clinical data, expand to additional data types, or prioritize administrative logic.

positions.label: Clinical Data Focus
positions.stance: Prioritize semantic standardization for core clinical concepts (diagnoses, labs, medications, procedures). AMA and ADLM emphasize this approach for greatest immediate patient safety impact.
positions.supportLevel: 6 commenters, primarily clinical associations and health systems
keyArguments: Greatest immediate patient safety impact
Builds on existing standards infrastructure
Most mature terminology systems available

positions.label: Expanded Data Types
positions.stance: Extend semantic standards to behavioral health, social determinants, device data, and unstructured content. Interstella and Leap Orbit argue that critical information about mental health, social determinants, or care preferences may be invisible to AI-driven tools.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters, primarily technology vendors and innovation-focused organizations
keyArguments: Critical information about mental health, social determinants, or care preferences may be invisible to AI-driven tools
Current systems handle structured data but have limited capability for unstructured data
Comprehensive patient understanding requires broader data integration

positions.label: Administrative Logic Standardization
positions.stance: Create standardized "Administrative Knowledge Graphs" and national digital dictionaries for administrative logic. Cooperative Exchange and David Bynon argue that if every payer uses different definitions, AI cannot scale.
positions.supportLevel: 3 commenters, primarily clearinghouses and benefits specialists
keyArguments: If every payer uses different definitions, AI cannot scale
CMS definitions are regulatory, not published in machine-readable form
Administrative AI requires deterministic semantic substrate

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inconsistent data interpretation undermining member trust in AI, proprietary formats creating costly integration burdens, and lack of harmonization across testing platforms and clinical metrics. Their members face direct operational consequences—physicians cannot trust AI that performs unpredictably across sites; laboratories cannot validate AI when the same analyte produces different results on different platforms.
specificPoints: Mandate consistent use of standardized, clinically validated code sets
Require uniform reporting of specific quantitative metrics
Develop domain-specific terminology standards
AMA's concern that AI performance will vary unpredictably across sites
ADLM's observation that different testing platforms producing different numeric results for the same analyte means AI cannot correctly interpret data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Custom integration requirements for every AI tool, lack of plug-and-play interoperability, and semantic inconsistencies across EHR systems, claims databases, and research repositories. They provide concrete technical examples of how semantic gaps manifest in implementation—proprietary JSON formats, inconsistent code mapping, missing provenance metadata.
specificPoints: Expand FHIR Provenance resources for AI-extracted data
Fund ontological harmonization research
Deploy semantic validation layers with audit logs
Penguin Ai's vivid illustration: If every AI vendor reads a PDF and maps 'heart attack' to different codes, hospital IT must build custom 'plumbing' for every AI tool

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clinical data lacking provenance, context, labeling, and longitudinal linkage; need for interoperable frameworks representing scientific knowledge; and AI hallucinations stemming from inconsistent terminology. They focus on foundational infrastructure and research needs rather than immediate operational concerns.
specificPoints: Adopt EBMonFHIR Implementation Guide for scientific knowledge exchange
Standardize glossary terms for AI interpretation
Treat semantic quality as measurable and improvable through operationalization
Health AI Institute noting that clinical data often lacks provenance, context, labeling, and longitudinal linkage required for scalable AI evaluation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Coding variation, normalization drift, incomplete reconciliation; current interoperability standards not encoding authoritative policy interpretation; and episodic exchange masking semantic quality problems. They bring deep technical implementation experience and argue that longitudinal data aggregation will expose semantic problems that have been hidden by episodic exchange patterns.
specificPoints: Treat semantic challenges as 'forcing function' for improvement
Implement deterministic semantic substrate alongside FHIR exchange
Modernize glossary terms for AI interpretation
Amir Abrams arguing that operationalizing longitudinal aggregation makes semantic quality measurable and improvable

noteworthyInsights.insight: Semantic problems are currently masked by episodic exchange — Software architect Amir Abrams offers the counterintuitive insight that implementing longitudinal data routing will reveal semantic interoperability problems that episodic exchange currently hides, and this should be treated as a 'forcing function' for improvement rather than a reason to avoid reform.

FHIR is intentionally semantically neutral — David Bynon points out that current APIs and data services are 'intentionally neutral with respect to semantic meaning,' suggesting the gap is by design rather than oversight, and proposing a 'deterministic semantic substrate' as a complementary layer rather than replacement.

Specialty bundles as 'research-grade AI' ingredients — Leap Orbit draws on direct experience with the SAMHSA/ASTP Behavioral Health IT Pilot to argue that USCDI+ specialty bundles provide the 'structured context that LLMs and agentic AI struggle to infer reliably,' positioning standardization as enabling rather than constraining AI capability.

Glossary alignment as compliance function — David Bynon/TPI reframes terminology standardization from a content refinement task to a compliance function, noting that 'AI hallucinations often stem from inconsistent Medicare terminology' and that publishers must adapt definitions 'for AI interpretation.'

Hybrid mapping systems showing promise — Scientix.ai reports that 'research into hybrid mapping systems—coupling classical lexical matchers with LLMs—has shown substantial improvements in aligning complex medical ontologies,' suggesting emerging technical solutions to the harmonization challenge.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Cross-Stakeholder Alignment: Unlike many regulatory topics, there is striking alignment between technology vendors, clinical associations, and academic researchers on both the diagnosis (semantic interoperability gap) and general direction of solutions (standardization, validation, harmonization). This suggests potential for coalition-building around policy recommendations.

Domain-Specific Manifestations: The semantic interoperability challenge manifests differently across specialties—laboratory medicine faces different platforms producing different numeric results for same analyte; ophthalmology has inconsistent metrics reporting; dentistry struggles with non-conformance to standard data models and proprietary APIs; administrative contexts face payer-specific definitions for terms like 'medical necessity.'

Implementation Experience Informing Policy: Several commenters draw on direct implementation experience to inform recommendations, including Leap Orbit's SAMHSA/ASTP Behavioral Health IT Pilot experience, David Bynon's Medicare.org platform rebuild, and ADA's CDT inclusion in USCDI v5.

Tension Between Voluntary and Mandatory Approaches: While most commenters support standardization, there is implicit tension between market-driven, voluntary adoption (Leap Orbit's 'USCDI+ as market-driven resource') and regulatory mandates (Cooperative Exchange's 'HHS should mandate a national digital dictionary').

Unintended Consequence: Longitudinal Aggregation Exposing Hidden Problems: Multiple commenters suggest that moving toward longitudinal data aggregation will reveal semantic quality problems that have been masked by episodic exchange patterns—a potential short-term disruption that could drive long-term improvement.

keyQuotations.quote: Interoperability that enables data movement but not data meaning limits AI impact.
It is not enough to move data; the AI must understand it. We need standardized 'Administrative Knowledge Graphs'. If every payer uses a different definition for 'medical necessity,' AI cannot scale.
Without strong semantic standardization, AI performance will vary unpredictably across sites, undermining physician trust and increasing patient risk.
Different testing platforms producing different numeric results for the same analyte means AI cannot correctly interpret data even when it appears complete.

The receiving EHR system doesn't speak English; it speaks in discrete codes (SNOMED, ICD-10). If every AI vendor reads a .pdf and maps 'heart attack' to a different code, or outputs the data in a proprietary JSON format, the hospital's IT team has to build custom 'plumbing' for every single AI tool.
FHIR provides flexible mechanisms to capture structured measurements, attribution, and contextual metadata, while leaving transformation logic and semantic interpretation to implementers.
Treat semantic interoperability challenges as a forcing function rather than a reason to avoid reform—operationalizing longitudinal aggregation makes semantic quality measurable and improvable.
A deterministic semantic substrate should be understood not as a replacement for FHIR-based exchange, but as the semantic payload carried alongside it.
Even when using standardized formats, devices fail to standardize crucial metrics reporting (central subfield thickness, GA lesion size, subretinal fluid volume).

Outstanding data quality issues due to non-conformance to standard data models and terminology...proliferation of proprietary APIs in dental technology forces industry to rely on extensive mapping to standards-based APIs by costly third-party data brokers.
AI models may reason from conflicting data due to semantic inconsistencies across EHR systems, claims databases, and research repositories.
Clinical data often lacks provenance, context, labeling, and longitudinal linkage required for scalable AI evaluation and deployment.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters demonstrate sophisticated technical understanding of semantic interoperability challenges, provide concrete examples from implementation experience, and offer constructive solutions rather than merely identifying problems. The discourse reflects genuine expertise across multiple stakeholder types.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific technical examples, implementation experiences (SAMHSA/ASTP pilot, Medicare.org rebuild), and research findings (hybrid mapping systems). However, quantitative evidence of harm from semantic inconsistencies is largely anecdotal rather than systematically documented.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from payers, health plans, and patient advocacy groups. The perspective of smaller healthcare organizations and rural providers on semantic interoperability challenges is also underrepresented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High technical complexity requiring understanding of healthcare data standards (FHIR, SNOMED, ICD-10), AI/ML systems, and the distinction between syntactic and semantic interoperability. Policy implications span regulatory mandates, standards development, and research funding.

4.4.themeDescription: Instrument Drift and Hardware Metadata Tracking

sections.executiveSummary: There is unanimous agreement among the small but technically expert group of commenters (3 total) that instrument drift and hardware changes pose a serious, underappreciated risk to AI diagnostic accuracy. Commenters with direct data engineering and laboratory medicine experience warn that this is a well-documented failure mode in other industries that the current regulatory framework overlooks. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on mandatory metadata provenance requirements and continuous monitoring protocols to detect hardware-induced performance degradation before patient harm occurs.

consensusPoints.text: All three commenters agree that changes in medical equipment—including firmware updates, calibration shifts, and hardware replacements—can silently degrade AI model performance in ways that are difficult or impossible to detect through standard monitoring. A senior data engineer warns that an 'accelerated' AI model lacking rigid provenance checks will likely produce diagnostic errors that are statistically undetectable until patient harm occurs. ADLM notes that changes in analytical instrument performance or patient characteristics over time may degrade their ability to produce accurate diagnostic results even when AI models remain constant. An independent researcher echoes that silent instrument drift can cause undetectable diagnostic errors.

exceptions.text: While all agree on the risk, only the industry expert provides detailed technical mechanisms explaining why this occurs (hardware noise profiles being misinterpreted as biological signals).

consensusPoints.text: All commenters concur that AI models are sensitive to specific hardware configurations and cannot be assumed to generalize across equipment changes without validation. ADLM emphasizes that if an AI model is based on data from one method and environment and applied in a different setting without adjustment, it may misinterpret results. The independent researcher states directly that AI models are sensitive to specific hardware configurations.

areasOfDebate.description: Given the limited number of comments (3), no significant areas of disagreement emerged. All commenters align on both the problem identification and general solution direction. However, there are variations in specificity of proposed solutions.

positions.label: Detailed Technical Mandates
positions.stance: The industry expert proposes specific technical requirements including mandatory Device ID and Firmware Version tracking, plus explicit protocols to distinguish biological from instrument drift.
positions.supportLevel: 1 of 3 commenters
keyArguments: Engineering precision is necessary
Vague requirements will not prevent harm

positions.label: Framework-Level Guidance
positions.stance: ADLM and the independent researcher reference broader frameworks (metadata inclusion, continuous quality monitoring) without prescribing specific technical implementations.
positions.supportLevel: 2 of 3 commenters
keyArguments: Flexibility allows adaptation to different laboratory contexts

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Hardware changes creating statistically undetectable diagnostic errors; AI models misinterpreting hardware noise as biological signal
specificPoints: Brings direct experience from industrial genomics where 'Batch Effects' are a daily operational challenge in petabyte-scale pipelines
Frames this as a solved problem in other industries that healthcare has not yet addressed
Proposes mandatory 'Instrument-Agnostic Validation' with two components: Metadata Provenance and Drift Normalization protocols

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inconsistent calibration across laboratories; different pre-analytical protocols affecting how values reflect physiology; AI models trained in one environment failing in different settings
specificPoints: Represents the clinical laboratory professionals who will be responsible for validating and monitoring AI tools under CLIA regulations
Emphasizes the operational reality of laboratory variability
Proposes metadata inclusion for test methods and validation parameters along with continuous quality monitoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Silent instrument drift causing undetectable errors; hardware configuration sensitivity of AI models
specificPoints: Synthesizes concerns from other stakeholders
References ADLM framework as a model approach
Endorses ADLM framework for metadata inclusion and continuous monitoring

noteworthyInsights.insight: Cross-industry knowledge transfer opportunity: The data engineering expert reveals that this problem is well-understood and actively managed in industrial genomics under the term 'Batch Effects,' suggesting healthcare AI could adopt existing solutions rather than developing new ones from scratch.

The invisibility problem: Multiple commenters emphasize that the most dangerous aspect of instrument drift is its undetectability—errors accumulate silently until patient harm occurs, making traditional quality assurance approaches insufficient.

Regulatory blind spot identified: The industry expert explicitly characterizes silent instrument drift as 'a fundamental engineering failure mode that the RFI overlooks,' suggesting this theme may be underweighted in current policy discussions despite its technical significance.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Experience Correlations: Commenters with hands-on data engineering experience provide the most technically specific recommendations, while those with policy or organizational perspectives focus on framework-level solutions. This suggests implementation details may need to come from technical practitioners rather than policy bodies.

Cross-Domain Validation: The convergence of concerns from industrial genomics, clinical laboratory medicine, and academic AI governance research suggests this is a genuine technical risk rather than a niche concern—three different fields independently identify the same failure mode.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Routine hospital equipment upgrades (firmware updates, hardware replacements) could inadvertently degrade AI diagnostic performance without any awareness by clinical staff, creating a hidden patient safety risk embedded in normal maintenance activities.

keyQuotations.quote: If a hospital upgrades its MRI firmware or sequencing hardware, an 'accelerated' AI model lacking rigid provenance checks will likely produce diagnostic errors that are statistically undetectable until patient harm occurs.
The model interprets hardware noise profile as biological signal when hardware changes.
Silent instrument drift is a fundamental engineering failure mode that the RFI overlooks.
If an AI model is based on data from one method and environment and applied in a different setting without adjustment, it may misinterpret results or fail to identify important variations.
Inconsistent calibration and different pre-analytical protocols can change how a laboratory value reflects underlying physiology.
In industrial genomics, this problem is called 'Batch Effects' in production environments.

discourseQuality.explanation: All three commenters demonstrate significant technical expertise and their concerns are mutually reinforcing across different professional domains.

evidenceBase.explanation: Evidence cited includes industrial genomics experience with Batch Effects and laboratory medicine experience with calibration variability, though empirical data specific to healthcare AI is limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: This analysis is based on only 3 comments with relevant content on this theme. The small sample size means quantitative statements should be interpreted cautiously, minority perspectives may not be represented, and the apparent consensus may reflect selection bias rather than true stakeholder agreement.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High technical complexity requiring cross-domain expertise in data engineering, laboratory medicine, and AI systems to fully address.

4.5.themeDescription: FHIR Implementation and Bidirectional API Access. This sub-theme specifically addresses implementation of FHIR-based APIs for AI integration, including read and write capabilities. || It includes concerns about EHR vendors restricting API access, proposals for mandating write-back capabilities, debates about API performance requirements, and arguments that current read-only access limits AI utility

sections.executiveSummary: There is strong consensus across all stakeholder types (30 of 30 comments) that FHIR-based APIs are essential infrastructure for clinical AI integration, with particular emphasis on the need for bulk data access and consistent implementation. The central tension lies between the current "read-only" reality of FHIR implementation and the widespread demand for bidirectional (write-back) capabilities, with commenters identifying EHR vendor control over write access as the primary barrier to AI adoption. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to mandate standardized, affordable bidirectional API access while limiting vendor fees and proprietary restrictions that currently fragment the market.

consensusPoints.text: FHIR is the foundational standard for clinical AI integration. Nearly all commenters explicitly endorse FHIR as the essential interoperability standard for AI in healthcare. This consensus spans business, academic, provider, and professional association stakeholders. Cyberhill Partners states that FHIR-native connectivity with EHR systems is foundational to clinical AI platforms. Scientix.ai notes that when data flows through standardized interfaces, specialized AI tools can be deployed across different health systems without prohibitive cost of custom integrations. The American Medical Association emphasizes that FHIR-based APIs can be the practical bridge between EHRs and trustworthy AI if implemented consistently.

exceptions.text: While all support FHIR, several commenters including 1upHealth criticize TEFCA's document-based approach as incompatible with FHIR's API-driven model.

consensusPoints.text: Bulk FHIR data access is critical for population-level AI and research. Commenters emphasize bulk FHIR capabilities as essential for training AI models and conducting population health analytics. Massive Bio states that bulk FHIR data access is crucial for research and population-level AI. Leap Orbit recommends prioritizing Bulk FHIR for population-level datasets to fuel large-scale research ensuring AI models trained on representative, cross-system data. SANCIAN LLC identifies FHIR-based clinical data with bulk access for population AI as a priority data type.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters addressing data access (at least 8 explicitly)

consensusPoints.text: Current integration barriers disproportionately harm smaller and innovative organizations. Commenters agree that current API implementation creates competitive advantages for large incumbents while locking out smaller innovators and under-resourced providers. COGA Labs notes this concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology. The American Society of Hematology emphasizes that integration challenges are especially problematic for smaller or under-resourced community practices and rural institutions lacking integration resources. Scientix.ai argues that standardized APIs lower barrier to entry for smaller innovators.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing market dynamics (approximately 10 of 30)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether to mandate EHR vendors to provide standardized, affordable write-back capabilities versus implementing write-back cautiously with governance safeguards.

positions.label: Mandate Write Access
positions.stance: Require EHR vendors to provide standardized, affordable write-back capabilities. Penguin Ai, AAFP, and AUA strongly advocate for this position.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of AI vendors and several professional associations (approximately 8-10 commenters)
keyArguments: Read-only access relegates AI to side-car applications that fragment workflows
Without write-back, clinicians face swivel-chair processes that negate efficiency gains
Current asymmetry maintains vendor moats that stifle innovation

positions.label: Cautious Implementation
positions.stance: Support write-back where appropriate with governance safeguards. AMA and AUA express this more measured position.
positions.supportLevel: Some professional associations and health systems (approximately 3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: Write access requires clear governance and accountability frameworks
Security and patient safety concerns must be addressed before broad write access
ONC certification for deeper in-system integration needed to ensure trust

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to regulate what EHR vendors can charge for API access versus relying on market-based approaches through standardization.

positions.label: Limit Vendor Fees
positions.stance: Regulate what EHR vendors can charge for API access. Penguin Ai and COGA Labs advocate for fee limitations.
positions.supportLevel: Several AI vendors and technology companies (approximately 4-5 commenters)
keyArguments: High integration costs lock out smaller innovators
Per-integration costs favor incumbents over best technology
Fees for write access create artificial barriers

positions.label: Market-Based Approach
positions.stance: Focus on standardization rather than price controls. WellSpan Health and Scientix.ai implicitly support this approach through emphasis on standardization.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in several comments emphasizing standardization over fee regulation
keyArguments: Standardized APIs naturally reduce costs through competition
Focus should be on technical requirements, not pricing

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to prioritize RESTful FHIR APIs and move away from TEFCA's document-based architecture versus supporting complementary approaches.

positions.label: Prioritize RESTful FHIR
positions.stance: Move away from TEFCA's document-based architecture. 1upHealth explicitly advocates for this position.
positions.supportLevel: At least 2 commenters explicitly; several others implicitly through emphasis on modern APIs
keyArguments: TEFCA cannot move large amounts of data needed for robust AI
Document flows are brittle, expensive, slow, and insecure
Modern internet economy demonstrates superiority of JSON/REST transactions

positions.label: Complementary Approaches
positions.stance: Support both TEFCA and FHIR APIs for different use cases. SanLuca AI suggests this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Most commenters do not explicitly address this tension
keyArguments: Different use cases may require different approaches
Transition tools needed for legacy systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Custom integration costs for each health system, vendor lock-in and proprietary barriers, and lack of write-back capabilities limiting product utility. Penguin Ai, COGA Labs, and Scientix.ai emphasize that current barriers favor companies with existing relationships over those with superior technology.
specificPoints: Describe specific technical requirements including latency, throughput, and event-driven architectures
Propose standardized API marketplaces to reduce per-integration costs
Advocate for mandated bidirectional access and fee limitations on legacy vendors
Recommend certification requirements for plug-in flexibility

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workflow integration for practicing clinicians, barriers affecting smaller and rural practices, and vendor lock-in limiting tool choice. AMA, AAFP, and ASH represent frontline users who must work within existing EHR systems.
specificPoints: Emphasize that theoretical benefits are meaningless without seamless workflow integration
Recommend expanding CEHRT requirements to include write-based APIs
Call for enforcing FHIR standards for specialty-specific data elements
Advocate for interoperability standards to promote competition

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Legacy platform interoperability, integration costs and vendor control, and practical workflow disruption. WellSpan Health, Penn Neurosurgery, and MHA provide direct operational experience with implementation challenges.
specificPoints: Can speak to real-world friction between AI tools and clinical workflows
Propose standardized, low-cost FHIR exchanges
Recommend strengthening certification requirements for third-party integration
Call for transition tools for legacy EHR implementations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data access for AI training and research, standards development and adoption, and cross-institutional compatibility. SKAF and Satyadhar Joshi focus on expanding FHIR beyond clinical data to scientific knowledge exchange.
specificPoints: Propose specific technical specifications for data pipelines
Recommend adopting EBMonFHIR Implementation Guide
Call for providing synthetic or test FHIR datasets for AI benchmarking
Suggest specific performance requirements including latency under 100ms and throughput over 10K events per second

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Medical society adoption of FHIR APIs, patient-controlled data access, and evolution of FHIR capabilities. David Rocha and Dr. Kordonowy propose innovative applications like Patient Health Wallets and graph query language integration.
specificPoints: Recommend medical societies collectively adopt FHIR APIs
Propose defining patient wallet standards interfacing via FHIR
Suggest aligning FHIR evolution with ISO graph query standards

noteworthyInsights.insight: Patient Health Wallet concept: Dr. Kordonowy proposes defining a Patient Health Wallet standard interfacing via FHIR APIs that would store longitudinal records, consent directives, and payment credentials, describing it as the operating system for AI in health care - a patient-centric architecture that could fundamentally restructure data ownership.

Graph query language evolution: David Rocha suggests aligning FHIR with ISO 39075 graph query language standard, noting that GQL on FHIR with AI will be useful to reason over care and relationship aware clinical reasoning - pointing to next-generation capabilities beyond current REST-based approaches.

LLM-powered legacy data conversion: Leap Orbit proposes a Structured Insight Extractor initiative using LLMs to convert legacy semi-structured data (CCDA summaries, lab reports) into high-fidelity FHIR resources - an innovative approach to the transition problem that leverages AI to enable AI.

Medical society API gap: David Rocha identifies an overlooked barrier noting that clinical guidelines, care plans, treatment algorithms, appropriate use criteria, care pathways, and living guidelines from medical societies are not currently accessible via FHIR APIs - suggesting that even with perfect EHR integration, AI lacks access to the clinical knowledge it needs.

TEFCA architectural critique: 1upHealth provides striking comparison noting that the cellphone Internet economy does billions of JSON transactions daily by comparison to TEFCA's antiquated architecture and that TEFCA proponents have claimed for 5 years it will move to FHIR yet TEFCA has not performed a single bona fide API transaction using FHIR.

Specific performance benchmarks: Satyadhar Joshi provides concrete technical specifications stating that data pipeline architectures should include real-time ingestion via Kafka/FHIR with latency under 100ms and throughput over 10K events per second - offering actionable standards for policy requirements.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic and Setting Variations: Urban academic medical centers like Penn and WellSpan describe vendor lock-in as the primary barrier, while professional associations representing rural and small practices such as AAFP and ASH emphasize resource constraints and integration costs. State-level organizations like MHA and NJII focus on standardization to enable cross-system deployment.

Facility-Type Patterns: Large health systems have resources to negotiate custom integrations but face vendor control. Small practices lack both negotiating power and technical capacity. Specialty practices in areas like retina, dental, and sleep medicine face additional challenges with specialty-specific data elements not yet standardized in FHIR.

Business Model Correlations: Established AI vendors with health system relationships like NeuroFlow and Massive Bio emphasize governance and certification. Newer and smaller AI companies like COGA Labs and Penguin Ai emphasize fee limitations and marketplace access. Infrastructure providers like Scientix.ai, Leap Orbit, and 1upHealth emphasize data quality and standardization as prerequisites.

Workflow fragmentation paradox: AI tools intended to reduce burden actually increase it when lacking write access, as clinicians must manually transfer outputs. Penn Neurosurgery and Penguin Ai highlight this unintended consequence.

Innovation concentration: Interoperability mandates that exist in principle but remain vendor-controlled in practice inadvertently favor incumbents. Penn Neurosurgery and COGA Labs identify this unintended consequence.
Specialty data gaps: General FHIR adoption may leave specialty-specific data elements like visual acuity and dental records in proprietary formats. ASRS and ADA raise this concern.

Temporal Pattern: Multiple commenters note that promises of FHIR adoption have been made for years without full realization. 1upHealth notes TEFCA has been promising FHIR for 5 years, and general sentiment indicates FHIR adoption is growing but incomplete.

keyQuotations.quote: In real-world clinical environments, if an AI tool does not function as a plug-and-play extension of existing workflows that creates more opportunity for the clinician to directly engage with the patient, adoption is unlikely, regardless of its theoretical benefits.
AI tools that cannot be cleanly embedded into existing EHR workflows or that rely on proprietary integrations create barriers and disincentivize adoption and scalability.
This concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology.
Dominant EMR vendor (Epic) makes innovation difficult - companies must integrate directly into Epic's ecosystem or be effectively excluded.
Large EHR and RCM vendors maintain technical and contractual moats restricting third-party AI from pushing insights directly into native workflows.
Interoperability standards (FHIR and USCDI) have succeeded in unlocking 'read' access to patient records...market remains constrained by severe asymmetry in 'write' access.
Without affordable, standardized write-back capabilities, AI cannot achieve primary goal of reducing administrative burden.
FHIR-based APIs can be the practical bridge between EHRs and trustworthy AI if implemented consistently, support writeback where appropriate, and are paired with clear governance and accountability.
When data flows through standardized interfaces, specialized AI tools can be deployed across different health systems without prohibitive cost of custom integrations.
GQL on FHIR with AI will be useful to reason over care and relationship aware clinical reasoning.
The cellphone Internet economy does billions of JSON transactions DAILY by comparison to TEFCA's antiquated architecture.
TEFCA proponents have claimed for 5 years it will move to FHIR yet TEFCA has not performed a single bona fide API transaction using FHIR.

Doximity tool is free and widely accessible but lacks EMR integration - clinicians must record conversation, permit transcription to cloud, then manually paste output into EMR - manual transfer diminishes efficiency such that traditional dictation or typing becomes equally efficient.
Integration challenges are especially problematic for smaller or under-resourced community practices and rural institutions lacking integration resources.
Standard FHIR profiles for specific needs could let AI modules operate across many hospitals with minimal tweaks.
Synergy of embedding dental-specific needs in FHIR data model makes dental data more readily available for AI modeling.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated technical understanding of FHIR implementation challenges, with specific examples, concrete recommendations, and clear articulation of trade-offs. Multiple stakeholder types engage substantively with the same core issues from different perspectives.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters frequently cite specific real-world examples (Doximity integration challenges, Epic ecosystem barriers) and some provide concrete technical specifications. However, quantitative data on integration costs or adoption rates is limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: EHR vendors themselves are notably absent from the comment record, leaving their perspective on write-access barriers and fee structures unrepresented. Patient advocacy groups are also underrepresented relative to technology vendors and professional associations.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves technical architecture decisions (REST vs. document-based), market dynamics (vendor control, fee structures), regulatory mechanisms (certification requirements, procurement conditions), and clinical workflow considerations that interact in complex ways.

4.6.themeDescription: USCDI Expansion for AI-Relevant Data Elements. This sub-theme addresses proposals for expanding the US Core Data for Interoperability to include data elements necessary for AI applications. || It includes calls for adding behavioral health data, social determinants of health, administrative data, and AI governance artifacts to USCDI; debates about prioritization of new data elements; and concerns about implementation burden

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate strong consensus that the current USCDI scope is insufficient for AI applications, with virtually all stakeholders calling for expansion to include behavioral health data, social determinants of health, administrative/financial data, and specialty-specific elements. The primary tension lies not in whether to expand, but in prioritization—with behavioral health and SDOH emerging as top priorities while administrative data advocates push for equal attention. Commenters consistently emphasize that data standardization is foundational to AI development, with one commenter noting expansion would "widen market opportunities, fuel research, and accelerate AI development."

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters explicitly or implicitly argue that USCDI must expand to support AI use cases effectively. Penguin Ai states directly that current USCDI scope is insufficient for many AI applications, particularly administrative workflows. SANCIAN LLC recommends USCDI expansion should prioritize AI-relevant data elements. Interstella calls to expand required data elements to include more data types useful for AI.

exceptions.text: No commenter explicitly opposes expansion, though implementation burden concerns are implied in calls for minimal viable indicators.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters specifically identify Social Determinants of Health (SDOH) as a priority data category for USCDI expansion. Waymark identifies priority data elements including housing stability, food insecurity, transportation access, and social isolation. Massive Bio argues that standards such as the HL7 Gravity Project to record and share SDOH in health records should be advanced. SANCIAN LLC lists social determinants of health among priority data types.

Multiple commenters emphasize behavioral health as a priority domain, with consensus that it remains uniquely fragmented. COGA Labs states that behavioral and mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics. Medvidi recommends expanding USCDI to include mental health elements including visit transcripts, PHQ/GAD scores, treatment rationales, and longitudinal outcomes. NeuroFlow identifies structured symptom assessments for depression, anxiety, and PTSD, suicide risk indicators, and longitudinal treatment response data as most consequential.

Most commenters addressing clinical data needs call for inclusion of patient-generated health data including wearables and patient-reported outcomes. AMA states that data should be available from both inside the clinic and from wearables, remote patient monitoring, and hospital-at-home environments. AUA lists wearable device data and patient-reported outcomes among high-value data types. SANCIAN LLC prioritizes patient-reported outcomes captured outside encounters.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters addressing clinical data needs (at least 5 of 14)

areasOfDebate.topic: Prioritization of Data Domains for Expansion
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether clinical and specialty-specific data elements should take priority over administrative and financial data elements in USCDI expansion efforts.

positions.label: Clinical-First Approach
positions.stance: Medical specialty societies and clinical AI developers prioritize clinical and specialty-specific data elements, arguing that clinical data directly impacts patient care and safety. AMA, AUA, ADA, and NeuroFlow emphasize that specialty domains have unique data needs not addressed by general USCDI, and existing frameworks like mCODE and USCDI+ Behavioral Health provide ready templates.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 6 commenters
keyArguments: Clinical data directly impacts patient care and safety
Specialty domains (oncology, urology, dentistry, behavioral health) have unique data needs not addressed by general USCDI
Existing frameworks like mCODE and USCDI+ Behavioral Health provide ready templates

positions.label: Administrative-Equal Approach
positions.stance: Administrative AI vendors and some healthcare technology companies argue that administrative and financial data deserves equal priority. Penguin Ai and AMA note that administrative burden is a major driver of healthcare costs and clinician burnout, and that prior authorization and claims data currently exist as unstructured text or proprietary codes.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 3 commenters
keyArguments: Administrative burden is a major driver of healthcare costs and clinician burnout
Prior authorization and claims data currently exist as unstructured text or proprietary codes
Administrative AI cannot function without standardized financial data

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to develop domain-specific USCDI+ extensions for specialty areas or focus on expanding the core USCDI standard.

positions.label: Specialty Bundle Model
positions.stance: Specialty societies and interoperability vendors like Leap Orbit and ADA advocate for developing domain-specific USCDI+ extensions that provide predictable data floors for AI developers. They argue that specialty bundles lower barriers to entry for innovative clinical tools and are essential ingredients for research-grade AI.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 4 commenters
keyArguments: Specialty bundles provide predictable data requirements for AI developers
Lowers barrier to entry for innovative clinical tools
Experience shows specialty bundles are essential ingredients for research-grade AI

positions.label: Core Expansion Model
positions.stance: Commenters like Penguin Ai focusing on USCDI v7 changes advocate for expanding core USCDI rather than proliferating USCDI+ domains, arguing that core standardization ensures universal adoption and reduces fragmentation across specialty domains.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 2 commenters
keyArguments: Core standardization ensures universal adoption
Reduces fragmentation across specialty domains
Administrative data should be in core USCDI, not relegated to plus categories

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to include system-level operational and workforce data in USCDI or maintain focus on patient-level clinical data.

positions.label: Operational/Workforce Data
positions.stance: Dr. Pal Randhawa, a frontline clinician, proposes including system-level operational indicators such as Workforce Readiness Telemetry, arguing that current frameworks focus on clinical outputs rather than upstream operational inputs. This data could enable proactive system planning and must be explicitly designed not for individual performance evaluation.
positions.supportLevel: Single commenter
keyArguments: Current frameworks focus on clinical outputs, not upstream operational inputs
Workforce strain indicators could enable proactive system planning
Must be explicitly designed NOT for individual performance evaluation

positions.label: Traditional Clinical Focus
positions.stance: The majority of commenters implicitly support maintaining focus on patient-level clinical data, arguing that patient care is the primary purpose of health data standards and that operational data raises privacy and labor relations concerns.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (implicit)
keyArguments: Patient care is the primary purpose of health data standards
Operational data raises privacy and labor relations concerns
Existing frameworks already address clinical needs

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Specialty-specific data elements not adequately represented in current USCDI, need for longitudinal clinical data (labs, vitals, medications, outcomes), and integration of imaging and structured reports.
specificPoints: Emphasize the breadth of data types needed across the care continuum—from operative notes to discharge summaries to referrals
Propose developing USCDI+ specialty domains for dental and behavioral health
AMA provides comprehensive list spanning clinical and administrative data
ADA offers direct assistance to ONC in developing dental domain

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data fragmentation impedes AI development and deployment, lack of standardized administrative data creates barriers to automation, and behavioral health data gaps prevent longitudinal monitoring.
specificPoints: Emphasize market and innovation implications—standardization widens market opportunities, fuels research, and accelerates AI development
Propose creating standardized FHIR-based profiles for mental health data
Penguin Ai provides detailed proposal for administrative data standardization
COGA Labs calls for unified mental health data profile

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workforce strain and operational fragility not captured in current data standards, need for system-level planning data that precedes clinical events.
specificPoints: Only commenter to propose entirely new data category (Workforce Readiness Telemetry) focused on operational rather than clinical data
Proposes adoption of WRT as interoperability standard with explicit safeguards against individual performance evaluation
Recommends leveraging existing metadata streams for minimal implementation burden

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data gaps in plus categories (behavioral health, sensitive environmental factors) and need for predictable data requirements to support AI tool development.
specificPoints: Experience implementing federal health IT initiatives provides practical insight into what works
Leap Orbit's experience with SAMHSA/ASTP BHIT Pilot demonstrates value of specialty bundles
Prioritize USCDI+ model for specialty care with voluntary data floors for predictability

noteworthyInsights.insight: Frontline neurosurgeon Dr. Randhawa proposes Workforce Readiness Telemetry (WRT) as an entirely new interoperability standard focused on upstream operational inputs that precede clinical events—a perspective absent from other comments and representing a unique clinician viewpoint on system-level data needs.

NeuroFlow highlights emerging evidence that passively collected data from smartphones and wearables can serve as objective proxies for mental health status—mobility patterns, sleep duration, and heart rate variability can detect symptom changes and predict deterioration. This suggests USCDI expansion should anticipate novel data sources beyond traditional clinical capture.

ADA explicitly offers to assist ONC in developing a dental USCDI+ domain, demonstrating that specialty societies are prepared to actively contribute to standards development rather than simply advocate for inclusion.

Interstella articulates the economic case for expansion: enhanced interoperability would widen market opportunities, fuel research, and accelerate AI development—framing standardization as an innovation enabler rather than compliance burden.

Dr. Randhawa's WRT proposal includes explicit safeguards: data must be explicitly designed NOT for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions—only for system-level planning and readiness assessment. This anticipates labor relations concerns before they arise.

emergingPatterns.pattern: AI/technology companies consistently emphasize data standardization as foundational to their business models and innovation capacity, while medical specialty societies focus on comprehensive clinical data elements across the care continuum.
Behavioral health stakeholders (both providers and technology companies like COGA Labs, Medvidi, and NeuroFlow) show strongest consensus on the urgency of their domain's data gaps.

Commenters rarely advocate for single data types; instead, they propose comprehensive lists spanning clinical, administrative, and patient-generated categories. SDOH appears across stakeholder types as a cross-cutting priority.

Administrative data advocacy comes primarily from technology companies like Penguin Ai rather than provider organizations, while larger organizations and specialty societies favor USCDI+ domain-specific extensions.

Multiple commenters note that without standardization, AI development will proceed with fragmented, proprietary data—potentially creating interoperability problems that are harder to solve later. Leap Orbit warns that data gaps in plus categories may undermine the voluntary USCDI+ model's effectiveness.

keyQuotations.quote: Mental health data remains the most fragmented domain in clinical informatics.
Prior Authorization Criteria, Payer Denial Reasons, and Claim Status metadata currently often exist as unstructured text or proprietary codes.
Enhanced interoperability would 'widen market opportunities, fuel research, and accelerate AI development.'
When certified EHR systems support consistent capture and API-based exchange of behavioral health measures, data completeness improves.
WRT must be explicitly designed NOT for individual performance evaluation, disciplinary action, or employment decisions—only for system-level planning and readiness assessment.
'Plus' categories (behavioral health, sensitive environmental factors) often hampered by data gaps.
Specialty bundles are essential ingredients for research-grade AI.
Establishing dental domain in USCDI could support agency-wide initiatives and further support interoperability and facilitate AI development.
Data should be available from both inside the clinic and from wearables, remote patient monitoring, and hospital-at-home environments.

Passively collected data from smartphones and wearables can serve as objective proxies for mental health status—mobility patterns, sleep duration, and heart rate variability can detect symptom changes and predict deterioration.
Current USCDI scope is insufficient for many AI applications, particularly administrative workflows.
Assessment scores, therapy notes, medication adherence records, and AI-derived behavioral indicators exist across disconnected systems with no unified patient view.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide detailed, substantive recommendations with specific data elements identified. Arguments are well-reasoned and supported by practical experience. Multiple stakeholder types engage constructively with the topic.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters cite practical implementation experience (e.g., Leap Orbit's SAMHSA/ASTP BHIT Pilot) and emerging research (e.g., NeuroFlow on passive data collection), but quantitative evidence of impact is limited. Most arguments rely on logical reasoning about AI data requirements.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from payers, health plans, and patient advocacy groups. No comments from EHR vendors on implementation feasibility. Rural and safety-net provider perspectives absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves technical standards development, multiple competing priorities across stakeholder types, and tension between core USCDI expansion versus USCDI+ domain proliferation.

4.7.themeDescription: TEFCA and National Health Information Exchange Adequacy

sections.executiveSummary: Public sentiment on TEFCA's role in supporting AI applications is sharply divided, with a fundamental tension between stakeholders who view TEFCA as a promising foundation requiring expansion (life sciences, pharmacy, and some health IT firms) and those who characterize it as fundamentally flawed infrastructure that constitutes information blocking (competing data platform providers). The dominant recommendation thrust centers on expanding TEFCA's scope to support research and AI use cases, though one commenter argues for abandoning TEFCA entirely in favor of modern API-based approaches.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters agree that national health information exchange infrastructure is consequential for AI development, though they disagree sharply on whether TEFCA helps or hinders this goal. Veeva Systems notes enhanced interoperability would accelerate responsible AI development; 1upHealth argues TEFCA's antiquated architecture cannot move large amounts of data needed for robust AI; Leap Orbit proposes AI agents to enhance TEFCA functionality.

exceptions.text: Agreement on importance does not translate to agreement on approach—views range from expansion to abandonment.

consensusPoints.text: Most commenters acknowledge that existing interoperability frameworks, including TEFCA, have room for improvement in supporting research, innovation, and evidence generation. Veeva Systems states opportunities remain to better support innovation, research, and evidence generation; NCPDP supports continued funding of TEFCA as an important part of research and development.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether TEFCA should be expanded and enhanced or abandoned entirely as information blocking.

positions.label: Expand and Enhance
positions.stance: Life sciences technology provider Veeva Systems, pharmacy standards organization NCPDP, and AI-first health IT firm Leap Orbit support building upon TEFCA's foundation.
positions.supportLevel: 3 of 4 commenters
keyArguments: TEFCA could enable AI-powered identification of diverse patient populations for clinical trials
Expanding TEFCA to support research exchange purposes would enable secure, standardized data access
Increased TEFCA participation, including pharmacy, will naturally expand care team interoperability

positions.label: Abandon as Information Blocking
positions.stance: 1upHealth, a competing data platform provider with MD leadership, argues TEFCA should be abandoned entirely.
positions.supportLevel: 1 of 4 commenters, but articulated with significant technical detail
keyArguments: TEFCA's document-based architecture fundamentally interferes with data access, exchange, and use—meeting the Cures Act definition of information blocking
Ancient AOL/Yahoo era protocols cannot handle data volumes required for AI
Security model relies on faux-HIPAA permitted uses rather than modern cryptographic approaches
