areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether TEFCA's security model is adequate with enhancements or fundamentally compromised.

positions.label: Adequate with Enhancements
positions.stance: Implicit support from commenters who propose building on TEFCA, including Leap Orbit's proposals for automated consent solutions and AI agents.
positions.supportLevel: Implicit in 3 of 4 commenters
keyArguments: Automated, standards-based consent solutions can make TEFCA consent-aware
AI agents can filter data to present only relevant, authorized information

positions.label: Fundamentally Compromised
positions.stance: 1upHealth argues the security model is fundamentally flawed.
positions.supportLevel: 1 of 4 commenters
keyArguments: QHINs engaged in multiple lawsuits around wholesale misappropriation of patient data
Resold data almost invariably been subsidized by taxpayers
Security model is brittle compared to robust public key cryptography and zero-trust security

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Fragmentation between clinical care and clinical research; limited data access for AI-enabled research activities
specificPoints: Emphasizes TEFCA's potential to address long-standing challenges in trial participation and data representativeness by reaching community-based and rural care settings
Proposes expanding TEFCA to explicitly support research exchange purposes
Envisions bidirectional flow where research insights translate back into care delivery

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Divergent views—one sees opportunity for AI enhancement of TEFCA, another sees TEFCA as obstacle to AI
specificPoints: Technical implementation expertise shapes opposing conclusions about TEFCA's viability
Leap Orbit proposes deploying TEFCA Agentic Orchestrator with autonomous AI agents for real-time, consent-aware data surfacing
1upHealth advocates abandoning TEFCA's brittle, expensive, slow, and insecure document flows entirely

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Continued funding and expansion of TEFCA participation
specificPoints: Pharmacy sector inclusion as pathway to broader care team interoperability
Proposes maintaining TEFCA funding and expanding pharmacy participation

noteworthyInsights.insight: 1upHealth makes the provocative legal argument that TEFCA itself constitutes information blocking under the 2016 Cures Act definition—interfering with the access, exchange and use of patient data—turning the regulatory framework against the government's own infrastructure initiative.

Veeva Systems articulates a future state where insights from clinical research and real-world evidence flow back into care delivery through AI-enabled clinical decision support reflecting up-to-date evidence and real-world patient populations—a vision of continuous learning health systems enabled by enhanced TEFCA.

Leap Orbit proposes using AI to solve TEFCA's challenges rather than replacing TEFCA—autonomous agents would filter massive volumes of network-exchanged data to present only relevant, authorized information to clinicians.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Commenters whose business models compete with or are independent of TEFCA infrastructure (1upHealth) express fundamental opposition, while those whose models could integrate with or build upon TEFCA (Veeva, Leap Orbit, NCPDP members) support expansion.

The most technically detailed critique comes from the commenter opposing TEFCA (1upHealth), citing specific architectural limitations, security protocols, and alternative approaches. Supporters tend toward higher-level policy recommendations.
Multiple commenters identify consent management as a key challenge for AI-enabled data exchange, with Leap Orbit specifically proposing automated consent solutions as enabler for AI adoption.

Veeva Systems uniquely emphasizes TEFCA's potential to reach community-based and rural care settings for more representative AI training data and clinical trial populations—a health equity dimension not raised by other commenters.

keyQuotations.quote: The 2016 Cures Act defines information blocking as interfering with the 'access, exchange and use' of patient data – TEFCA does all three.
TEFCA's antiquated architecture and baroque protocols cannot move large amounts of data needed for robust AI.

This end run around HIPAA is so brittle that TEFCA participants – the same QHINs which are supposed to vet other participants – are currently engaged in multiple lawsuits around their wholesale misappropriation of patient data.
TEFCA could support AI-enabled identification of more diverse and representative patient populations across community-based and rural care settings.
In future state, stronger interoperability could enable insights from clinical research and real-world evidence to be more rapidly translated back into care delivery.
Incentivizing automated, standards-based consent solutions accelerates AI adoption and provides scalable, real-time mechanism ensuring TEFCA care delivery is always 'consent-aware.'
As participation in TEFCA increases, including pharmacy participation, expansion in interoperability of the care team should be a natural outcome.

discourseQuality.explanation: The discourse ranges from high-level policy recommendations to detailed technical critiques, with the most technically sophisticated arguments coming from the opposing position.

evidenceBase.explanation: Analysis is based on only 4 comments with relevant content on this theme, limiting the ability to quantify consensus or identify statistically meaningful patterns.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: The small sample includes no patient/consumer voices, healthcare provider organizations, or government entities—perspectives that would be essential for comprehensive policy analysis.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: The sharp divergence between commenters may reflect genuine industry disagreement or may be amplified by the small sample size and the direct business interests of the commenting organizations.

4.8.themeDescription: EHR Vendor Data Practices and Information Blocking

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal near-universal concern that a small number of dominant EHR vendors are creating significant barriers to AI innovation through restrictive data access policies, proprietary formats, and contractual limitations. Commenters spanning healthcare providers, AI developers, professional associations, and academic researchers converge on the view that current vendor practices constitute de facto information blocking that undermines the intent of the 21st Century Cures Act. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for stronger federal enforcement, mandatory equal access for third-party applications, and treating EHR systems more like regulated utilities than proprietary platforms.

consensusPoints.text: EHR vendor market concentration creates innovation bottlenecks. Commenters identify the near-monopoly status of major EHR vendors as a fundamental barrier to AI advancement. Advocate Health describes a near monopoly among few major EHR vendors that limits innovation and slows progress toward data-driven care. Providence characterizes the EHR industry as currently standing as near monopoly with limited competition among handful of large companies. 1upHealth states the biggest barrier to private sector AI innovation is that most clinical data is held by few EHR vendors and only available to whatever AI programs those vendors decide to allow.

exceptions.text: While all agree on the problem, commenters differ on whether the solution requires utility-style regulation or simply stronger enforcement of existing interoperability rules.

consensusPoints.text: Current vendor practices disadvantage third-party AI developers. Commenters explicitly describe how EHR vendors use technical, contractual, or financial mechanisms to favor their own solutions over third-party innovations. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association reports large EHR vendors are using control of workflows and APIs to disadvantage third party apps and innovation. Penguin Ai describes how vendors maintain technical and contractual moats restricting third-party AI from pushing insights directly into native workflows. COGA Labs notes each health system integration requires custom engineering work that favors large incumbents over innovative startups.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (11 of 14)

consensusPoints.text: Federal intervention is necessary to address vendor barriers. All commenters call for some form of federal action, with most specifically requesting HHS leadership to enforce existing rules or establish new requirements. Advocate Health states federal leadership essential to prevent interoperability barriers created by EHR vendors. AAFP supports robust enforcement of information blocking provisions, particularly for EHR developers imposing unnecessary technical, contractual, or administrative barriers. University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT team argues regulation forcing more open-platform environment would significantly accelerate innovation.

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether EHR vendors should face utility-style comprehensive regulation or targeted enforcement of existing Cures Act provisions.

positions.label: Utility-Style Regulation
positions.stance: Treat EHRs as regulated utilities with comprehensive access requirements. HealthScoreAI and 1upHealth explicitly advocate this approach, arguing that full interoperability would eliminate vendor lock-in, that EHR transitions reflect rent-seeking behavior rather than meaningful advances in care delivery, and that taxpayers funded most clinical data collection.
positions.supportLevel: 3-4 commenters explicitly advocate this approach
keyArguments: Full interoperability would eliminate vendor lock-in
EHR transitions reflect rent-seeking behavior rather than meaningful advances in care delivery
Taxpayers funded most clinical data collection

positions.label: Targeted Enforcement
positions.stance: Strengthen enforcement of existing Cures Act provisions without fundamental restructuring. AMA, AAFP, and Massive Bio favor this more incremental approach, arguing that existing information blocking rules provide adequate framework, focus should be on consistent API access and standardized implementation, and regulatory overreach should be avoided.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of commenters (8-10)
keyArguments: Existing information blocking rules provide adequate framework
Focus on consistent API access and standardized implementation
Avoid regulatory overreach

areasOfDebate.topic: Intellectual Property Rights Over AI Models
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether health systems should own AI models developed from their patient data versus vendor IP claims.

positions.label: Provider Ownership
positions.stance: Health systems should own AI models developed from their patient data. Memorial Sloan Kettering explicitly raises this concern, arguing that health systems legally own patient data, vendor IP claims create a chilling effect on innovation, and internal development is discouraged when ownership cannot be guaranteed.
positions.supportLevel: Explicitly raised by 2 commenters, implicitly supported by several others
keyArguments: Health systems legally own patient data
Vendor IP claims create chilling effect on innovation
Internal development discouraged when ownership cannot be guaranteed

areasOfDebate.topic: API Access Model: Read vs. Write Capabilities
areasOfDebate.description: Technical debate over whether third parties need full write access to EHR workflows or should focus first on standardized read access.

positions.label: Full Write Access Required
positions.stance: Third parties need ability to push data into EHR workflows, not just read. Penguin Ai and 1upHealth specifically address this technical distinction, arguing that read-only access relegates AI to dashboards and side applications, forces swivel-chair workflows, and limits clinical utility.
positions.supportLevel: 2-3 commenters specifically address this technical distinction
keyArguments: Read-only access relegates AI to dashboards and side applications
Forces swivel-chair workflows
Limits clinical utility

positions.label: Standardized Read Access First
positions.stance: Focus on consistent FHIR implementation before expanding scope. AMA and AAFP prioritize this as foundational step, arguing that current API access is inconsistent across vendors, practices are forced into costly custom builds, and a baseline is needed before expanding.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters prioritize this as foundational step
keyArguments: Current API access inconsistent across vendors
Practices forced into costly custom builds
Need baseline before expanding

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Inability to access and use their own patient data for AI development, vendor control over data export and integration capabilities, and cost of AI add-ons from EHR vendors. Large health systems with significant IT resources still face substantial barriers—if they struggle, smaller providers have no chance. Even MSK, a world-renowned cancer center, identifies restrictive foreground IP rights as common in EMR and PACS company standard contracts.
specificPoints: Require equal technical access for third-party applications
Dismantle contractual IP barriers
Include baseline AI functionality in core EHR products
Open market to all developers to keep prices down
Even large systems like Advocate Health face constraints on accessing, processing, and using their own data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Per-integration costs that lock out smaller innovators, asymmetric access (read vs. write capabilities), and vendor-controlled ecosystems that favor incumbents. These companies experience vendor barriers as existential business threats and describe a market where success depends more on existing health system relationships than on building superior technology.
specificPoints: Standardized API marketplaces for FDA-authorized AI tools
Certification criteria limiting fees for write access
Require patient portals to run through public APIs
Enforce information blocking rules against both vendors and providers using them

COGA Labs states per-integration cost currently locks out smaller innovators from the market and concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physician workflow disruption from fragmented AI integration, proprietary data formats preventing tool comparison, and barriers affecting small, independent, and rural practices disproportionately. These organizations represent frontline clinicians who must work within whatever systems are available and emphasize that vendor lock-in ultimately harms patient care by preventing adoption of better tools.
specificPoints: Consistent FHIR profile implementation
Robust enforcement of information blocking provisions
EHR functionality supporting third-party application ecosystems
Incentives for full adoption of interoperable standards

ASRS notes the core problem in ophthalmology: widespread use of proprietary imaging formats and incomplete standards implementation by device manufacturers with high costs and contractual barriers to extracting data from EHRs and imaging platforms

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Dominant vendor Epic creates ecosystem dependency, expanding beyond Epic requires unrealistic cost burdens for startups, and interoperability mandates exist in principle but remain highly vendor-controlled in practice. Academic researchers see both the clinical and innovation sides—they develop AI tools and must deploy them in real clinical workflows, observing that regulatory intent is not matching market reality.
specificPoints: Strengthen 45 CFR Part 170 Subpart C to allow third-party integration without proprietary barriers
Push EMRs toward open-ended innovation platforms with AI plug-in capabilities
Dominant EMR vendor Epic makes innovation difficult - companies must integrate directly into Epic's ecosystem or be effectively excluded

noteworthyInsights.insight: 1upHealth makes the provocative point that the vast bulk of hidden data has been paid for by US taxpayers, suggesting public investment should yield public benefit rather than vendor profit. This reframes the debate from private market dynamics to public accountability.

1upHealth identifies an unexpected consequence: current policy has spawned vendor Payer Platforms where EHR vendors use private APIs to help providers and payers game higher Star ratings. This suggests vendor data control creates perverse incentives beyond just blocking innovation.

MSK reveals that even world-class institutions are discouraged from developing AI because vendor contracts claim ownership of resulting models. This represents a hidden cost of current practices that may not be visible to regulators.

ASRS exposes how imaging vendors claim standards compliance while maintaining proprietary control: vendors should not be permitted to claim DICOM adoption simply for file transfer capabilities. This technical insight reveals how standards can be nominally met while practically circumvented.

HealthScoreAI draws a cautionary parallel: hospital migrations from Meditech to Cerner to Epic over two decades reflect vendor consolidation and rent-seeking behavior rather than meaningful advances in care delivery or outcomes. This suggests AI policy should learn from EHR history.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Large health systems like Advocate Health, Providence, and MSK emphasize their inability to use their own data despite significant resources, suggesting the problem is structural rather than capacity-based. Smaller providers are represented primarily through professional associations like AAFP and MHA who emphasize disproportionate burden on resource-limited settings.

Unusual alignment between healthcare providers and AI companies—both identify EHR vendors as the common barrier. Professional associations serve as bridge voices, representing clinician concerns while advocating for market competition.

Multiple commenters distinguish between technical barriers (proprietary formats, inconsistent APIs) and contractual barriers (IP claims, restrictive licensing). Both require different regulatory responses—technical issues need standards enforcement; contractual issues need policy intervention.

Vendor control creates swivel-chair workflows that increase rather than decrease clinician burden. IP restrictions discourage internal AI development at institutions best positioned to create clinically relevant tools. Star rating gaming suggests vendor platforms may be optimizing for metrics rather than outcomes.

Ophthalmology represented by ASRS faces unique challenges with proprietary imaging formats not fully addressed by EHR interoperability rules. Sleep medicine represented by AASM emphasizes need for cross-vendor validity to support continuity of care. Oncology represented by Massive Bio and MSK highlights clinical trial matching as use case blocked by data access barriers.

keyQuotations.quote: This concentrates AI innovation among companies with existing health system relationships rather than those building the best technology.

This creates a chilling effect on innovation. Internal efforts within hospital systems are discouraged from developing novel AI models, since ownership cannot be guaranteed due to these restrictive contract conditions.
Dominant EMR vendor (Epic) makes innovation difficult - companies must integrate directly into Epic's ecosystem or be effectively excluded.
Biggest barrier to private sector AI innovation is that most clinical data is held by few EHR vendors and only available to whatever AI programs those vendors decide to allow.
In genuinely interoperable environment, EHR platforms would function more like regulated utilities rather than proprietary lock-in systems.
EHR vendors should not be permitted to keep AI away from providers unable to afford add-on after add-on.
External data release to potential competition is heavily gated by cumbersome procedures, disproportionate costs, ancient protocols, and outright throttling of data flows.
'Pull vs. Push' asymmetry relegates new AI vendors to 'read-only' data supporting workflows, dashboards, or side-car applications.
Even large systems like Advocate Health face constraints on accessing, processing, and using their own data.
These transitions reflect vendor consolidation and rent-seeking behavior rather than meaningful advances in care delivery or outcomes.
Interoperability mandates exist in principle but integration remains highly vendor-controlled in practice.
Large EHR vendors are using control of workflows and APIs to disadvantage third party apps and innovation.

AI developers should be able to compete on performance and safety (not bespoke EHR integrations) by building against consistent FHIR profiles and implementation guidance, reducing integration costs and vendor lock-in.
Regulation forcing more open-platform environment would significantly accelerate innovation.
Any patient data including scheduling not available at same performance level to outside competitive products should be considered information-blocking.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of technical, regulatory, and market dynamics. Stakeholders provide specific examples and evidence rather than general complaints. Multiple commenters distinguish between different types of barriers and propose targeted solutions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters cite specific experiences with vendor practices, contractual terms, and integration challenges. Large health systems and AI companies provide concrete examples of barriers encountered. However, quantitative data on costs and market impacts is limited.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: No commenters defended vendor IP claims or current vendor practices, which may reflect selection bias in who submitted comments on this theme. EHR vendor perspectives are notably absent from the comment record. Small independent practices are represented only through professional associations rather than direct comments.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of technical standards (FHIR, APIs), regulatory frameworks (21st Century Cures Act, information blocking rules), market dynamics (vendor consolidation), intellectual property law, and clinical workflow considerations.

4.9.themeDescription: Data Access for AI Training and Validation Datasets. This sub-theme addresses challenges in obtaining representative datasets for AI model development and validation. || It includes concerns about limited access to diverse patient populations, proposals for shared benchmarking datasets, debates about synthetic data as an alternative to real patient data, and calls for federated learning infrastructure

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types that limited access to diverse, representative datasets is a fundamental barrier to developing safe and equitable healthcare AI, with commenters uniformly calling for federal action to create shared benchmarking datasets and federated learning infrastructure. The central tension lies not in whether to expand data access, but in how to do so—with debates centering on synthetic versus real patient data, centralized repositories versus federated approaches, and the appropriate balance between privacy protection and research utility. The dominant recommendation thrust calls for HHS to actively coordinate creation of de-identified or synthetic datasets, fund federated learning networks accessible to smaller innovators, and reform policies that inadvertently block legitimate AI development while maintaining robust privacy protections.

consensusPoints.text: Representative datasets are essential for equitable AI performance. Nearly all commenters explicitly identify limited access to diverse patient populations as a critical barrier to developing AI that works fairly across all groups. ADLM notes that one of the biggest challenges facing AI is ensuring data used for healthcare decisions is representative of the population, as studies have demonstrated such imbalances can cause models to systematically underestimate risk or misclassify disease in certain populations. SANCIAN LLC emphasizes that equity gaps from non-representative training data worsen disparities in underserved populations, while NeuroFlow observes that current validation datasets often underrepresent rural populations, Veterans, and communities of color.

exceptions.text: While all agree on the problem, commenters differ on whether the solution requires centralized data repositories or distributed federated approaches.

consensusPoints.text: Federal government should create or coordinate shared benchmarking datasets. A strong majority explicitly call for HHS to develop, fund, or coordinate creation of standardized evaluation datasets. Massive Bio suggests HHS could coordinate creation of reference datasets that are continuously updated via interoperable feeds from diverse sources. Interstella recommends funding development of an open benchmarking platform for AI tools on standard datasets with metrics like bias, robustness, and generalizability. ICF states that government should build benchmark datasets or sponsor their development.

exceptions.text: No commenters oppose this concept, though SanLuca AI emphasizes that datasets must include appropriate subgroup reporting and drift checks.

consensusPoints.text: Federated learning deserves significant federal investment. Most commenters specifically endorse federated learning as a privacy-preserving approach to collaborative AI development. AORN describes federated learning as a strategy for anonymizing data and training AI models through decentralized data repositories that can enhance accessibility to datasets and improve privacy. Independent Researcher Satyadhar Joshi notes that federated learning enables collaborative model training across institutions without centralizing sensitive patient data. COGA Labs calls for creation of a permanent, HIPAA-compliant federated learning network accessible to small companies, not just academic medical centers.

Current data access barriers disproportionately harm smaller innovators. Multiple commenters across business and research sectors agree that existing barriers create an uneven playing field favoring large incumbents. COGA Labs notes that the combination of HIPAA's minimum necessary standard, fragmented data systems, and proprietary formats makes it disproportionately expensive for small companies to access sufficient clinical training data. Interstella calls for lowering barriers for startups or researchers to train and evaluate algorithms. Dr. Bethany Russell observes that restrictive data sharing policies prevent AI model development and validation, and lack of standardized evaluation frameworks creates prohibitive validation costs.

areasOfDebate.topic: Synthetic Data vs. Real Patient Data
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether synthetic data can adequately support AI development or whether real patient data remains essential for robust AI systems.

positions.label: Synthetic Data Advocates
positions.stance: Synthetic data can adequately support AI development while eliminating privacy risks. Cyberhill Partners, California Clinical Laboratory Association, and Cooperative Exchange argue that synthetic data eliminates privacy concerns entirely, enables broader sharing, can be designed to ensure representativeness, and that powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters
keyArguments: Eliminates privacy concerns entirely
Enables broader sharing
Can be designed to ensure representativeness
Powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data

positions.label: Real Data Prioritizers
positions.stance: Synthetic data has limitations and real patient data remains essential for robust AI. HealthScoreAI, Atropos Health, and Veeva Systems argue that synthetic data may not capture rare conditions or complex interactions, longitudinal outcomes require real data, and most impactful AI applications require longitudinal clinical data, genetic information, and linked claims data.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 12-15 commenters
keyArguments: Synthetic data may not capture rare conditions or complex interactions
Longitudinal outcomes require real data
Most impactful AI applications require longitudinal clinical data, genetic information, and linked claims data

positions.label: Hybrid Approach
positions.stance: Both synthetic and real data have roles depending on use case. Interstella and American Dental Association implicitly support using synthetic data for initial development and benchmarking while reserving real data for validation, recognizing different needs at different stages.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters
keyArguments: Synthetic for initial development and benchmarking
Real data for validation
Different needs at different stages

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether national data repositories or distributed federated approaches would better serve AI development needs while protecting privacy.

positions.label: Centralized Repository Supporters
positions.stance: National data repositories would accelerate innovation. Interstella, American Urological Association, and Penguin Ai argue for easier access for all developers, consistent data quality, enabling direct comparison across tools, and creation of a national repository of de-identified health records.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10-12 commenters
keyArguments: Easier access for all developers
Consistent data quality
Enables direct comparison across tools
National repository of de-identified health records

positions.label: Federated Infrastructure Advocates
positions.stance: Distributed approaches better protect privacy while enabling collaboration. COGA Labs, Massive Bio, and Independent Researcher Satyadhar Joshi argue that data never leaves institutional control, reduces privacy risk, enables participation without full data sharing, and that enhanced interoperability need not mean centralized data.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 15-18 commenters
keyArguments: Data never leaves institutional control
Reduces privacy risk
Enables participation without full data sharing
Enhanced interoperability need not mean centralized data

areasOfDebate.topic: HIPAA as Barrier vs. HIPAA as Appropriate Safeguard
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether current HIPAA interpretation unnecessarily blocks legitimate AI development or whether privacy-preserving approaches can achieve goals without weakening protections.

positions.label: HIPAA Reform Needed
positions.stance: Current HIPAA interpretation unnecessarily blocks legitimate AI development. Massive Bio, New Jersey Innovation Institute, and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association argue that federated learning should not be considered a HIPAA disclosure, current interpretation creates unnecessary friction, and health systems should be allowed to participate in federated model training without it being considered a HIPAA disclosure.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8-10 commenters
keyArguments: Federated learning should not be considered a HIPAA disclosure
Current interpretation creates unnecessary friction
Allow health systems to participate in federated model training without it being considered a HIPAA disclosure

positions.label: Privacy Protections Essential
positions.stance: Privacy-preserving approaches can achieve goals without weakening HIPAA. Cyberhill Partners and Independent Researcher Satyadhar Joshi argue that technical solutions exist, differential privacy and secure aggregation can enable collaboration, and privacy-preserving approaches leveraging metadata, aggregations, and synthetic data can deliver actionable intelligence.
positions.supportLevel: Several commenters
keyArguments: Technical solutions exist
Differential privacy and secure aggregation can enable collaboration
Privacy-preserving approaches leveraging metadata, aggregations, and synthetic data can deliver actionable intelligence

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether the proposed NIH policy would harm imaging-based AI research.

positions.label: Withdraw/Modify Policy
positions.stance: The American Society of Retina Specialists strongly opposes the proposed policy, arguing it would have a chilling effect on imaging data dissemination, particularly in retina, and that current professional society sharing arrangements work well but would not meet proposed requirements.
positions.supportLevel: At least one major specialty society strongly opposes
keyArguments: Chilling effect on imaging data dissemination, particularly in retina
Current professional society sharing arrangements work well but would not meet proposed requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Access to diverse training data; prohibitive costs of site-specific data mapping; lack of standardized benchmarking tools; competitive disadvantage for smaller companies. They emphasize the scalability trap—every new AI deployment requires custom, site-specific data mapping creating significant training debt, as noted by Leap Orbit.
specificPoints: Federal coordination of benchmark datasets needed
Federated learning networks should be established
CRADAs for dataset development would help
National Golden Provider Record initiative proposed
COGA Labs notes that NIH's N3C project demonstrated value of multi-institutional collaboration—a standing equivalent for health AI would be transformative

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member readiness for AI adoption; ensuring AI works across diverse practice settings; protecting patient safety while enabling innovation. They bring specialty-specific insights—ASRS notes that the first FDA-cleared AI device was for diabetic retinopathy screening yet their members face nearly unworkable barriers to creating aggregated datasets.
specificPoints: Multi-institutional data sharing needed
Specialty-specific validation datasets required
Professional society facilitation of data collection networks proposed
American Dental Association offers to facilitate collaborations to operationalize practice network for efficiently gathering annotated training data

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Underfunded research infrastructure; lack of compliant computing environments; barriers to accessing anonymized health data. Computing Research Association emphasizes need for better/compliant infrastructure (example: NAIRR-Secure) and notes researchers need health systems to collaborate for better data contextualization.
specificPoints: Regulatory reforms for broader anonymized data access needed
Investment in secure research infrastructure required
Privacy-preserving cryptography should be supported
University of Pennsylvania neurosurgery team describes running three different stroke AI systems simultaneously to increase robustness—demonstrating practical multi-model approaches

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Current interoperability requirements don't support population-level data aggregation; validation costs; integration with existing workflows. Advocate Health notes that APIs do not reliably support population-level data aggregation needed for model training—a technical limitation not emphasized by other stakeholders.
specificPoints: Privacy-preserving data enclaves needed
Common telemetry schemas required
Reduced friction in data-use agreements essential
Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association calls for enabling privacy-preserving data enclaves and de-identified datasets for external validation

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Equity gaps from non-representative data; need for standardized evaluation frameworks. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi provides specific technical requirements: privacy budget ε<2 and model accuracy within 5% of centralized.
specificPoints: Benchmark datasets for fairness needed
Shared testbeds for rural and safety-net settings required
Dr. Bethany Russell calls for cooperative agreements establishing AI evaluation networks with shared data contributing to public knowledge

noteworthyInsights.insight: Multi-model redundancy as safety strategy — University of Pennsylvania neurosurgery team describes running three different stroke AI systems simultaneously from the same CT scanner, with physicians making final determinations when results diverge. If the goal is to keep the human in the loop, triplicating models provides protection against isolated AI error. This practical approach to AI safety through redundancy is rarely discussed in policy contexts.

Privacy-preserving AI is already working — Cyberhill Partners reports their Cerebro Platform relies on metadata and aggregations rather than Protected Health Information, demonstrating that powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data. This challenges assumptions that AI development necessarily requires access to raw patient data.

The scalability trap concept — Leap Orbit articulates a critical barrier: every new AI deployment requires custom, site-specific data mapping creating significant training debt. Their proposed solution—a National Golden Provider Record to resolve provider identity at point of ingestion—would solve the scalability trap for the market by providing AI developers a predictable foundation of accurate provider data.

Quality measurement as data infrastructure lever — 1upHealth proposes a novel forcing function: CMS should zero out payments for Star rating measures unless data obtained through public API with each data element tagged with metadata of public API call. This would modernize entire quality measurement landscape through AI while creating secondary benefits for data access.

Specific technical parameters for federated learning — Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi provides unusually specific technical requirements: privacy budget ε<2 and model accuracy within 5% of centralized. This level of technical specificity could inform actual implementation standards.

Professional societies as data infrastructure — American Dental Association offers to facilitate collaborations to operationalize practice network for efficiently gathering annotated training data, suggesting professional societies could serve as trusted intermediaries for data collection—a model that could scale across specialties.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Geographic Variations: Rural populations consistently identified as underrepresented in training data. State-level variation in data access noted by multi-state operators like Waymark. California-specific initiatives like Kooth's Soluna service demonstrate state-level innovation that could inform federal approaches.

Facility-Type Patterns: Safety-net settings identified as having thinnest evidence base. Academic medical centers have advantages in data access that smaller providers and startups lack. Large health systems like Advocate Health (69 hospitals) face different challenges than smaller organizations—they have data but face API limitations for population-level aggregation.

Stakeholder Role Correlations: AI developers consistently emphasize competitive barriers and call for democratized data access. Trade associations balance member interests with patient safety concerns. Academic researchers emphasize infrastructure gaps and funding needs. Healthcare providers focus on practical implementation challenges and workflow integration.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Current interoperability requirements focused on individual patient access inadvertently constrain population-level AI development. HIPAA's minimum necessary standard, while protective, creates disproportionate burden on smaller innovators. Proposed NIH controlled-access policy could undermine existing successful data-sharing arrangements. Lack of standardized benchmarks leads to duplicative validation efforts across organizations.

Cross-Stakeholder Alignment: Unusual alignment between AI vendors and healthcare providers on need for federated infrastructure. Both large health systems and small startups identify data access as critical barrier, though from different angles. Professional associations across specialties (urology, dentistry, retina, oncology, perioperative nursing) converge on need for specialty-specific validation datasets.

keyQuotations.quote: One of the biggest challenges facing AI is ensuring data used for healthcare decisions is representative of the population.
Single institutions may lack sufficient data to detect rare failures or performance issues in underrepresented populations.

Our experience demonstrates that powerful healthcare AI does not require training models on confidential patient data. Privacy-preserving approaches leveraging metadata, aggregations, and synthetic data can deliver actionable intelligence while fully respecting patient privacy.
Enhanced interoperability need not mean centralized data—federated learning infrastructure deserves investment.

The combination of HIPAA's minimum necessary standard, fragmented data systems, and proprietary formats makes it disproportionately expensive for small companies to access sufficient clinical training data.
NIH's N3C project demonstrated value of multi-institutional collaboration—a standing equivalent for health AI would be transformative.
If the goal is to keep the human in the loop, triplicating models provides protection against isolated AI error.
Moving from custom code to repeatable pipelines is key to making clinical AI deflationary and scalable.

Approximately 12 million Americans are dually eligible for Medicare and Medicaid—AI development for this population faces significant data integration barriers including fragmented data sources, inconsistent identifiers, regulatory complexity, and D-SNP limitations on data sharing.
Current validation datasets often underrepresent rural populations, Veterans, and communities of color.
Equity gaps from non-representative training data worsen disparities in underserved populations.
Studies of health AI tools have demonstrated such imbalances can cause models to systematically underestimate risk or misclassify disease in certain populations.
Federally supported validation infrastructure would reduce duplicative review burdens and create common benchmarks.
Single-site studies cannot answer the questions policymakers and payers need answered about AI performance at population scale.

It is nearly unworkable for retina specialists and researchers to create large, aggregated, high-quality datasets essential for robust AI due to proprietary formats and incomplete standards implementation.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of technical, regulatory, and practical challenges. Stakeholders provide specific examples, cite evidence, and offer concrete recommendations rather than vague concerns. Multiple commenters reference specific technical parameters, existing programs like NIH's N3C, and real-world implementation experiences.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite studies demonstrating AI bias in underrepresented populations, reference specific programs and initiatives, and draw on direct operational experience. However, quantitative data on the scope of data access barriers is limited, with most evidence being qualitative or anecdotal.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient and consumer advocacy perspectives are notably absent from this comment set. The discourse is dominated by technology developers, healthcare providers, and professional associations, with limited direct input from the populations most affected by AI bias or data access decisions.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves intersecting technical (federated learning, synthetic data, privacy-preserving computation), regulatory (HIPAA, NIH policies), and equity considerations that require sophisticated policy responses balancing multiple competing interests.

5.1.themeDescription: HIPAA Applicability and Modernization for AI Systems. This sub-theme addresses how HIPAA applies to AI systems and whether updates are needed to address AI-specific risks. || It includes debates about whether AI vendors are covered entities or business associates, proposals for extending HIPAA-like protections to non-covered AI developers, concerns about gaps in current HIPAA coverage, and calls for guidance on AI-specific HIPAA compliance

sections.executiveSummary: There is near-universal consensus among commenters that HIPAA requires modernization to address AI-specific risks, with particular concern about regulatory gaps that allow AI vendors to operate outside traditional HIPAA protections. The central tension lies between those seeking clearer guidance within existing frameworks versus those advocating for expanded regulatory coverage to new entities. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend that HHS issue specific guidance on AI-related HIPAA compliance, with strong support for extending privacy obligations to AI vendors handling health data regardless of their current covered entity status.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters agree that HIPAA was not designed for AI and requires updates to address AI-specific risks. This consensus spans healthcare providers, AI vendors, trade associations, and advocacy groups. Cyberhill Partners states the current regulatory framework does not adequately address AI-specific PHI risks; Advocate Health notes many AI developers, data brokers, and digital health platforms operate outside HIPAA scope; NeuroFlow observes HIPAA's definition of covered entities does not adequately address the range of digital health and AI tools now operating in clinical contexts.

exceptions.text: Citizens' Council for Health Freedom takes a fundamentally different view, arguing HIPAA itself is flawed as a data security rule rather than a privacy rule that already permits too much data sharing.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters identify regulatory ambiguity as a significant barrier to responsible AI development and deployment. Massive Bio states uncertainty about how HIPAA applies to AI creates barriers to innovation; Cyberhill Partners notes organizations face uncertainty about minimum necessary standards for AI, audit trail requirements for AI-generated content, and liability when AI inadvertently discloses PHI; Penguin Ai reports ambiguity regarding data ingestion for AI improvement leads to implementation paralysis.

Nearly all commenters call for HHS to provide clearer guidance on HIPAA compliance for AI systems, though they differ on specific areas requiring clarification. AUA recommends HHS should issue HIPAA guidance clarifying de-identification and minimum-necessary standards for AI development, validation, and post-deployment monitoring; SanLuca AI requests guidance on HIPAA permitted uses/disclosures baseline, de-identification standard safe harbor/expert determination, and security/technical safeguards expectations for AI vendors as Business Associates.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of HIPAA Coverage for AI Vendors
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI vendors handling health data should be subject to the same obligations as covered entities or whether the existing Business Associate framework should be strengthened.

positions.label: Extend Full Coverage
positions.stance: AI vendors handling health data should be subject to same obligations as covered entities. AAPA states AI technology vendors must be treated as HIPAA-covered entities; CommonSpirit Health argues extending same privacy obligations and penalties to AI vendors would help ensure appropriate use of patient data; Health IT End-Users Alliance asserts AI companies dealing with health data should be held to same standards as HIPAA-covered entities.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority—approximately 15 of 28 commenters, including most healthcare providers and trade associations
keyArguments: Current gaps leave patient data unprotected when processed by AI systems
Healthcare organizations bear disproportionate compliance burden while vendors escape accountability
Patient trust requires consistent protections regardless of who handles data

positions.label: Strengthen BAA Framework
positions.stance: Work within existing Business Associate structure with enhanced requirements. AMA recommends adaptations to HITECH Act's Business Associate Agreement policies to include baseline transparency, safety, and security requirements; Interstella suggests streamlining compliance with template Business Associate Agreements for AI vendors; Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association requests HIPAA guidance clarifying BAAs, model-training boundaries, de-identification, and prohibited secondary uses.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support—approximately 8 of 28 commenters, primarily AI vendors and some trade associations
keyArguments: Existing BAA framework provides foundation that can be adapted
Creating new covered entity categories may be legally complex
Clearer guidance within current structure could resolve most issues

areasOfDebate.topic: Data Use for AI Model Training
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to enable pathways for using health data in AI development with safeguards versus maintaining strict limits on data use for AI training.

positions.label: Enable Training with Safeguards
positions.stance: Clarify pathways for using health data in AI development. Massive Bio recommends allowing health systems to participate in federated model training without it being considered a HIPAA disclosure; University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT requests guidance to clarify use of deidentified information in model training under §164.514.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of AI vendors and academic institutions—approximately 12 of 28 commenters
keyArguments: AI development requires access to real-world data to ensure safety and effectiveness
Federated learning and de-identification can protect privacy while enabling innovation
Current restrictions create competitive disadvantage for U.S. healthcare AI

positions.label: Prioritize Privacy Protection
positions.stance: Maintain strict limits on data use for AI training. Citizens' Council for Health Freedom calls to restore medical privacy rights eliminated under federal HIPAA privacy rule; an anonymous psychologist emphasizes patients often don't know that their data isn't protected when using AI mental health tools.
positions.supportLevel: Minority but vocal—approximately 5 of 28 commenters, primarily advocacy groups and individual clinicians
keyArguments: Patients often don't understand or consent to AI training uses
De-identification may not adequately protect against re-identification
Commercial interests should not override patient privacy

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether HHS should issue interpretive guidance within existing regulatory framework or pursue formal rule updates through rulemaking.

positions.label: Issue Guidance First
positions.stance: Provide interpretive guidance within existing regulatory framework. WellSpan Health requests HHS should clarify HIPAA obligations under 45 CFR §164.508 for AI-related disclosure and consent with clear safe harbors; Cyberhill Partners argues clear technical safeguard standards would reduce compliance uncertainty and unlock private sector investment.
positions.supportLevel: Majority—approximately 18 of 28 commenters
keyArguments: Faster to implement than formal rulemaking
Can address immediate uncertainty while longer-term solutions develop
Allows flexibility as technology evolves

positions.label: Formal Rule Updates
positions.stance: Revise HIPAA regulations through formal rulemaking. Bethany Russell recommends revising 45 CFR Part 164 (HIPAA Privacy and Security Rules) to explicitly address AI-enabled clinical tools; Scientix.ai calls to modernize HIPAA for AI-era data governance.
positions.supportLevel: Significant minority—approximately 8 of 28 commenters
keyArguments: Guidance lacks enforcement teeth
Fundamental gaps require regulatory changes
Formal rules provide clearer legal standing

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Bearing disproportionate compliance burden while AI vendors escape accountability; institutional variation in HIPAA interpretation creating operational inconsistencies; patient consent frameworks inadequate for AI context. Healthcare providers emphasize the practical operational challenges of implementing AI under current HIPAA uncertainty—they must make real-time decisions about AI deployment without clear regulatory guidance, creating risk exposure.
specificPoints: CommonSpirit Health notes current privacy laws primarily place responsibility on covered entity (healthcare organization) for maintaining patient privacy
University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT observes that regulatory silence renders such decisions institutional, creating discontinuities between health systems
Proposed solutions include extending privacy obligations to AI vendors, issuing clear guidance on cloud-based AI processing, and establishing safe harbors for AI-related consent

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty creating barriers to market entry and scaling; lack of clear standards for de-identification and model training; inconsistent interpretation of BAA requirements across health systems. AI vendors emphasize that regulatory ambiguity affects investment decisions and product development timelines—they need predictable compliance pathways to build sustainable businesses.
specificPoints: Ellipsis Health notes organizations often apply overly restrictive interpretations of privacy requirements, limiting AI functionality or data integration even when patient consent and safeguards are in place
Penguin Ai recommends clear guidance incentivizing architectural privacy (scrubbing data at the edge) rather than contractual privacy (BAAs alone)
Proposed solutions include template BAAs for AI vendors, clear guidance on cloud-based AI under HIPAA, standards for de-identified data use in training, and architectural privacy approaches

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Members facing uncertainty about HIPAA obligations for AI tools; third-party AI vendors not regulated same as covered entities; need for practical compliance guidance their members can implement. Trade associations represent collective voice of practitioners who must navigate AI adoption decisions daily—they emphasize need for actionable, practical guidance rather than abstract principles.
specificPoints: AAFP expresses being deeply concerned about inadequacy of current protections for tools developed outside HIPAA guidance

ADA notes administrative tasks not supported by practice management or health record systems often performed through workaround applications that may not require business associate agreement, shifting compliance accountability to clinicians
Proposed solutions include strengthening BAA requirements, issuing guidance on de-identification standards, and clarifying that AI vendors handling PHI should meet same standards as covered entities

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient privacy not adequately protected by current framework; AI mental health tools operating outside HIPAA; patients unaware their data lacks protection. Advocacy groups center patient rights and autonomy, emphasizing that regulatory gaps create real harm to vulnerable populations who may not understand the privacy implications of AI tools.
specificPoints: Psychotherapy Action Network states AI chatbots (mental health specific or generalized models) are not HIPAA compliant and user/patient privacy not protected by federal law
Anonymous psychologist emphasizes patients often don't know that their data isn't protected when using AI mental health tools outside HIPAA coverage
Proposed solutions include establishing enforceable federal privacy standards covering AI tools outside HIPAA, restoring patient consent requirements, and ensuring uniform federal standards

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: HIPAA compliance complicates data sharing essential for AI research; ambiguity around data retention and model training; need for practical guidance on HIPAA-compliant AI architectures. Academic institutions balance dual roles as healthcare providers and researchers—they see both clinical implementation challenges and research barriers created by regulatory uncertainty.
specificPoints: New Jersey Innovation Institute notes HIPAA compliance complicates data-sharing processes essential for training AI algorithms
ECRI identifies ambiguity around data retention, model training on user data, potential data transfer outside U.S. complicates compliance and risk
Proposed solutions include updated guidance on acceptable AI tool architectures, clarification on de-identified data use, and practical guidance on BAAs for LLM vendors

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor exemptions from HIPAA protections; mental health AI tools lacking privacy protections; need to expand HIPAA definitions to include AI data pipelines. Individual commenters often bring technical expertise or direct patient experience that institutional commenters may not emphasize—they highlight specific gaps and loopholes.
specificPoints: Christopher Woodall argues all inputs to healthcare AI systems must receive full HIPAA protection—current regulatory ambiguity allowing vendor exemptions must be closed
Ty Greenhalgh recommends AI Models and Pipelines should be considered Relevant Electronic Information Systems under the HIPAA Security Rule

Proposed solutions include closing loopholes allowing vendor HIPAA exemptions, extending protections to all AI inputs including biometric and genomic data, and expanding definition of Relevant Electronic Information Systems

noteworthyInsights.insight: Penguin Ai, founded by executives from UnitedHealth Group, Optum, and Kaiser Permanente, proposes a paradigm shift from relying on BAAs to requiring architectural privacy (scrubbing data at the edge)—a technical approach that could fundamentally change how AI systems handle PHI.

Citizens' Council for Health Freedom offers a contrarian view that HIPAA rule, properly understood, is a data security rule rather than a privacy rule. It permits confidential patient data to be shared broadly without patient consent by covered entities. This challenges the premise of many other comments seeking to extend HIPAA protections.

Massive Bio, which has served 160,000+ cancer patients, proposes allowing health systems to participate in federated model training without it being considered a HIPAA disclosure—a technical solution that could enable AI development while keeping data within covered entities.

Cybersecurity professional Ty Greenhalgh proposes expanding HIPAA's definition of REIS to include data pipelines that feed AI models and requiring specific versioning of AI models and their data training sources—a novel approach to bringing AI infrastructure under existing regulatory frameworks.

The American Dental Association identifies a specific gap where administrative tasks not supported by practice management or health record systems often performed through workaround applications that may not require business associate agreement, shifting compliance accountability to clinicians—highlighting how current gaps create unfair burden on individual practitioners.

HealthScoreAI raises the novel concern that consumer-generated data can be transformed into PHI through association with covered entity or business associate, recommending that once data is handled by covered entity or business associate for clinical or operational purposes, it must be treated consistently as PHI.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Limited geographic variation observed in this theme; concerns about HIPAA applicability appear consistent across national, regional, and state-level commenters. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association and New Jersey Innovation Institute represent state-level perspectives but align with national concerns.

Large integrated health systems such as CommonSpirit, Advocate Health, and WellSpan emphasize vendor accountability and extending HIPAA obligations, while AI vendors emphasize need for clearer guidance and template BAAs to reduce compliance uncertainty.

Academic medical centers like University of Pennsylvania highlight institutional variation in interpretation creating operational inconsistencies, while mental health-focused organizations such as PsiAN express heightened concern about gaps in coverage for behavioral health AI tools.

Commenters with direct AI deployment experience such as Advocate Health, WellSpan, and Ellipsis Health tend to emphasize practical operational challenges, while trade associations representing frontline clinicians like AAFP, ADA, and AAPA emphasize that compliance burden falls disproportionately on practitioners.

Unintended consequences identified include overly restrictive interpretations limiting AI functionality, compliance accountability shifting to clinicians due to gaps in vendor coverage, regulatory uncertainty slowing beneficial AI development, and institutional discontinuities in AI implementation.

keyQuotations.quote: Many AI developers, data brokers, and digital health platforms operate outside HIPAA scope.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Healthcare Provider - Chief Technology and AI Officer

keyQuotations.quote: Current regulatory ambiguity allowing vendor exemptions must be closed.
HIPAA's definition of covered entities does not adequately address the range of digital health and AI tools now operating in clinical contexts.
Uncertainty about how HIPAA applies to AI creates barriers to innovation.
Ambiguity regarding data 'ingestion' for AI improvement leads to implementation paralysis.
Organizations face uncertainty about minimum necessary standards for AI, audit trail requirements for AI-generated content, and liability when AI inadvertently discloses PHI.
This regulatory silence renders such decisions institutional, creating discontinuities between health systems.
Without foundational privacy requirements, healthcare organizations risk severe erosion of patient confidence and potential data misuse.
AI chatbots (mental health specific or generalized models) are not HIPAA compliant. User/patient privacy not protected by federal law.
Patients often don't know that their data isn't protected when using AI mental health tools outside HIPAA coverage.
Many mental health AI tools fall outside HIPAA, meaning sensitive patient data may be left unprotected.
Clear technical safeguard standards would reduce compliance uncertainty and unlock private sector investment.
Extending same privacy obligations and penalties to AI vendors would help ensure appropriate use of patient data.
Clear guidance incentivizing architectural privacy (scrubbing data at the edge) rather than contractual privacy (BAAs alone).
Once data is handled by covered entity or business associate for clinical or operational purposes, it must be treated consistently as PHI and remain subject to HIPAA safeguards.
The HIPAA rule, properly understood, is a data security rule rather than a privacy rule. It permits confidential patient data to be shared broadly without patient consent by 'covered entities.'
While HIPAA provides a strong foundation, challenges remain around appropriate data minimization, transparency in patient-facing AI interactions, and secondary use of derived insights.

45 CFR Parts 164 Subparts C and E permit PHI use for treatment, payment, and healthcare operations but make no mention of cloud-based processing/storage, ambient transcription, or perpetual AI model refinement.
AI 'Models and Pipelines' should be considered Relevant Electronic Information Systems under the HIPAA Security Rule.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across stakeholder types provide substantive, well-reasoned arguments with specific regulatory citations and concrete examples. The debate reflects genuine engagement with complex policy tradeoffs rather than superficial advocacy.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific regulatory provisions (45 CFR sections), operational experiences, and concrete examples of regulatory gaps. Some claims about patient harm or innovation barriers would benefit from additional empirical support.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups focused on data access rights (as opposed to privacy protection), international perspectives on cross-border data flows, and smaller healthcare practices that may face different implementation challenges than large health systems.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves intersecting legal, technical, and operational considerations with significant disagreement about both problem definition and appropriate solutions.

5.2.themeDescription: De-identification Standards and Re-identification Risks. This sub-theme addresses concerns about AI's ability to re-identify supposedly de-identified data and implications for data use in AI development. || It includes concerns that AI can re-identify individuals from de-identified datasets, proposals for updating de-identification standards to account for AI capabilities, debates about safe harbor versus expert determination methods, and arguments for preserving de-identification permissions that enable AI research

sections.executiveSummary: Commenters broadly agree that current de-identification standards require updating to address AI's enhanced re-identification capabilities, with near-universal recognition that the 2012 guidance predates modern AI technologies. However, significant tension exists between those prioritizing stronger protections against re-identification risks and those emphasizing the need to preserve data access pathways that enable beneficial AI research. The dominant recommendation thrust favors updated guidance that balances privacy protection with continued innovation, particularly through clarified safe harbors, risk-based approaches, and technical safeguards like differential privacy.

consensusPoints.text: Current de-identification standards need updating for the AI era. Nearly all commenters addressing this directly agree that existing de-identification guidance is outdated and fails to account for AI capabilities. Waymark explicitly notes the 2012 de-identification guidance was issued before widespread deployment of AI and machine learning technologies. Massive Bio states de-identification standards need updating to account for AI capabilities. Cyberhill Partners observes AI presents novel privacy risks including potential for re-identification of de-identified data.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (11 of 14 addressing this directly)

exceptions.text: ACLA urges caution, arguing the administration should continue fostering use of de-identified information and not disrupt balance between genetic information and de-identification under Privacy Rule.

consensusPoints.text: AI can re-identify individuals from supposedly de-identified data. A strong majority of commenters acknowledge this technical reality as a foundational concern requiring regulatory response. AORN provides specific evidence that with as few as four pieces of data, a person may be reidentified, with that data not being exclusive to the items identified in the current HIPAA safe harbor list. Cyberhill Partners warns AI systems can generate or infer Protected Health Information even from de-identified inputs. Mental health counselor Mark O'Brien notes even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification or misuse.

Regulatory clarity is needed for AI training on de-identified data. Most commenters call for clearer guidance on how de-identification rules apply to AI development contexts. AUA recommends HHS should issue HIPAA guidance clarifying de-identification and minimum-necessary standards for AI development, validation, and post-deployment monitoring. Scientix.ai calls to clarify de-identification standards for AI training datasets under HIPAA Privacy Rule. Massive Bio requests clarity on how de-identification rules apply to complex data like genomic or free-text data used in AI.

areasOfDebate.topic: Approach to Data Access for AI Research
areasOfDebate.description: Tension between prioritizing stronger protections against re-identification risks versus preserving data access pathways that enable beneficial AI research.

positions.label: Strengthen Protections
positions.stance: Prioritize updating standards to address re-identification risks before enabling broader data sharing. Healthcare providers, nursing associations, and mental health professionals support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Healthcare providers, nursing associations, mental health professionals
keyArguments: Four data points can re-identify individuals; current safe harbor list is insufficient
AI can infer PHI even from de-identified inputs
Mental health data requires heightened protection due to sensitivity

positions.label: Preserve Access Pathways
positions.stance: Maintain or expand de-identification permissions to enable beneficial AI research. Clinical laboratories, specialty medical societies, and AI companies support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Clinical laboratories, specialty medical societies, AI companies
keyArguments: De-identified datasets have been instrumental in medical advancements
Overly restrictive requirements will detrimentally slow research progress
Current balance between privacy and research access should not be disrupted

areasOfDebate.topic: Safe Harbor vs. Expert Determination Methods
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to expand safe harbor provisions for AI-related uses or update the expert determination method to account for AI capabilities.

positions.label: Expand Safe Harbor
positions.stance: Create explicit safe harbors for AI-related uses of de-identified data. Majority of business commenters support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of business commenters (4 of 6 businesses)
keyArguments: Some covered entities are overly conservative, fearing any data sharing might violate HIPAA
Safe harbor for Privacy-by-Design AI architectures would enable responsible innovation
Clear safe harbors for bona fide R&D would reduce uncertainty

positions.label: Update Expert Determination
positions.stance: Modernize expert determination method to account for AI capabilities. Healthcare technology companies and some trade associations support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Healthcare technology companies, some trade associations
keyArguments: Expert determination analyses must account for AI re-identification risks
Risk-based approaches more appropriate than universal controlled-access requirements
Current uncertainty about whether older de-identification methods remain valid

areasOfDebate.topic: Universal vs. Risk-Based Data Access Controls
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to require controlled access for all potentially re-identifiable data or tailor access requirements to specific data types and contexts.

positions.label: Universal Controlled Access
positions.stance: Require controlled access for all potentially re-identifiable data. This position is implied by NIH's stance as referenced by ASRS.
positions.supportLevel: Implied by NIH position
keyArguments: User registration alone does not constitute controlled access
Comprehensive protections needed given AI capabilities

positions.label: Risk-Based Approach
positions.stance: Tailor access requirements to specific data types and contexts. Medical specialty societies and research-focused organizations support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Medical specialty societies, research-focused organizations
keyArguments: Mandating controlled access for all such imaging will detrimentally slow research progress
Professional societies can develop workable, context-specific policies
Existing data sharing practices through society portals provide robust protections

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory uncertainty impeding AI development; overly conservative interpretation of HIPAA by covered entities; need for clear pathways to use de-identified data for training
specificPoints: Current ambiguity creates business risk and slows beneficial innovation
Technical solutions like privacy-by-design and edge processing could enable safe data use
Interstella notes some covered entities are overly conservative, fearing any data sharing might violate HIPAA
Penguin Ai proposes safe harbor for Privacy-by-Design AI architectures using automated de-identification and edge-processing to scrub PHI before data enters model training environment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Balancing member needs for data access with patient privacy; ensuring standards are workable in clinical and research contexts; avoiding one-size-fits-all mandates
specificPoints: Bring specialty-specific knowledge about data types including retinal imaging, genetic information, and urologic data
ASRS warns that universal controlled-access requirements would undermine existing research data sharing through professional portals like the Retina Image Bank
ACLA emphasizes that inclusion of genetic information in de-identified datasets has been instrumental in medical advancements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient trust and therapeutic relationships; vulnerability of sensitive data especially mental health; practical implementation of privacy protections
specificPoints: Frontline experience with patient concerns about data privacy
Understanding of how privacy breaches could undermine care relationships
Mental health counselor Mark O'Brien expresses concern that even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification or misuse
Dr. Russell recommends de-identification standards should account for re-identification risks in AI model training datasets

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Technical adequacy of privacy protections; evidence-based approaches to data governance
specificPoints: Offers specific technical solutions grounded in privacy research
Satyadhar Joshi recommends differential privacy ensures aggregated models cannot be reverse-engineered to extract patient-level information through noise injection with privacy budget ε<2

noteworthyInsights.insight: Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi provides concrete technical parameters for differential privacy (ε<2), offering policymakers a specific, implementable standard rather than abstract principles.

ASRS reveals that existing, well-functioning research data sharing through professional portals like the Retina Image Bank may be disrupted by new requirements, highlighting unintended consequences of overly broad mandates.

Cyberhill Partners identifies a novel concern—AI systems can generate or infer PHI from de-identified inputs—suggesting that traditional de-identification may be insufficient even when technically compliant.
AORN notes that minimum necessary actions to protect health information may vary according to AI model type and architecture, suggesting one-size-fits-all approaches may be inappropriate.

ACLA highlights that genetic information occupies a unique position—PHI only if individually identifiable—and that laboratories have achieved exponential AI-enabled medicine advancements through large de-identified datasets, providing a concrete success story for balanced approaches.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Different data types present distinct re-identification challenges requiring tailored approaches: genetic/genomic data emphasized by ACLA and Massive Bio, imaging data detailed by ASRS with specific re-identification pathways, mental health data highlighted by Mark O'Brien for heightened sensitivity, and free-text data noted by Massive Bio for particular challenges with unstructured clinical notes.

Stakeholder role correlations show AI companies consistently emphasize safe harbors and reduced uncertainty, professional societies balance member research needs with patient protection, individual providers prioritize patient trust and therapeutic relationships, and trade associations defend existing practices while acknowledging need for updates.

Commenters divide between those proposing technical solutions such as differential privacy, privacy-by-design architectures, and edge processing versus policy solutions including updated guidance, expanded safe harbors, and risk-based frameworks, with some advocating hybrid approaches embedding technical standards in regulatory safe harbors.

Unintended consequences identified include universal controlled-access requirements potentially disrupting existing professional society data sharing as noted by ASRS, overly conservative interpretations already chilling beneficial research per Interstella and Waymark, and new requirements potentially invalidating previously compliant de-identification methods.

keyQuotations.quote: With as few as four (4) pieces of data, a person may be reidentified, with that data not being exclusive to the items identified in paragraphs (b)(2)(i)(A) through (b)(2)(i)(R).
AI systems can generate or infer Protected Health Information even from de-identified inputs, and the current regulatory framework does not adequately address this risk.

The 2012 de-identification guidance was issued before widespread deployment of AI and machine learning technologies. This creates uncertainty for covered entities and business associates about: whether data de-identified using older methods remains de-identified under current technology, how to conduct Expert Determination analyses that account for AI re-identification risks, when AI training and validation constitute permitted uses of de-identified or limited data sets, and what safeguards are necessary when sharing de-identified data with AI vendors.
Mandating controlled access for all such imaging will detrimentally slow research progress.
Inclusion of genetic information in de-identified datasets has been instrumental in medical advancements.
Even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification or misuse.
Differential privacy ensures aggregated models cannot be reverse-engineered to extract patient-level information through noise injection with privacy budget ε<2.
Provide specific safe harbor for 'Privacy-by-Design' AI architectures using automated de-identification and edge-processing to scrub PHI before data enters model training environment.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific technical details and concrete examples. Multiple stakeholder perspectives are represented with clear articulation of competing interests and proposed solutions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite specific research findings (e.g., four-data-point re-identification), technical standards (differential privacy parameters), and real-world examples (Retina Image Bank). However, some claims about AI capabilities lack specific citations.
