analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups, individual patients, and smaller healthcare providers. Academic/research perspective represented by only one commenter. No comments from health plans or insurers on this specific topic.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves technical privacy concepts, regulatory interpretation, and balancing competing policy objectives across multiple data types and use cases.

5.3.themeDescription: Secondary Use of Patient Data for AI Training

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal deep concern about the commercialization of patient data for AI training without adequate consent, transparency, or patient benefit. While there is near-universal agreement that current frameworks inadequately address AI-specific data uses, commenters divide sharply on solutions—ranging from strict consent requirements and data use prohibitions to calls for clearer safe harbors enabling de-identified data use for innovation. The dominant thrust is toward requiring explicit governance of "learning accumulation" (how operational data becomes AI capability), with healthcare providers particularly alarmed that vendors are using clinical feedback mechanisms to harvest training data without compensation or disclosure.

consensusPoints.text: Nearly all commenters agree that existing privacy frameworks do not adequately address AI-specific data uses, particularly the distinction between care delivery and model training. EHY Consulting notes that privacy/security frameworks that govern access and disclosure do not explicitly govern learning accumulation or model improvement using operational clinical data. Interstella observes that privacy rules like HIPAA can make it difficult to aggregate and share data needed for robust AI models. ECRI highlights that ambiguity around model training on user data complicates compliance and risk.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (18 of 21 addressing this issue)

exceptions.text: ACLA argues current de-identification standards are sufficient and should be leveraged more aggressively for AI R&D.

consensusPoints.text: A strong majority of commenters explicitly call for enhanced patient visibility, consent mechanisms, or opt-out rights regarding AI training uses. Christopher Woodall emphasizes that patients have zero visibility into where their health data goes or who accesses it. COGA Labs notes that users want explicit control over who sees data, retention periods, and secondary use. Health IT End-Users Alliance recommends prohibiting secondary use of data without consent.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (14 of 21)

consensusPoints.text: Most healthcare providers and trade associations agree that contracts must explicitly address AI training restrictions. CommonSpirit Health states that all vendors should have well-defined, enforceable mechanisms for data purging once data has served its agreed-upon purpose. ECRI recommends model contract clauses covering data use and retention limits including 'no training on our data' unless explicit. ICF calls for model contracting guidance including prohibitions on unauthorized model training.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and trade associations (8 of 10 provider/association comments)

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters are divided on what level of consent should be required before patient data can be used for AI model training.

positions.label: Strict Consent Mandate
positions.stance: Require explicit patient authorization before any data use for AI training. This position is supported primarily by advocacy groups, individual providers, and patient-focused organizations including Citizens' Council for Health Freedom, Christopher Woodall, and Mark O'Brien LCPC.
positions.supportLevel: 7 commenters
keyArguments: Data memorized in model parameters—potentially forever creates permanent privacy exposure
Patients may not support purposes their data is used for
Commercial exploitation without patient knowledge violates trust

positions.label: De-identification Safe Harbor
positions.stance: Allow unrestricted use of properly de-identified data for AI development. This position is supported primarily by laboratories, technology companies, and some health systems including ACLA, Waymark, and Interstella.
positions.supportLevel: 5 commenters
keyArguments: De-identified data has enabled exponential AI-enabled medicine advancements
Strict consent requirements would impede innovation and population health analytics
Properly de-identified data poses minimal privacy risk

positions.label: Tiered Consent Framework
positions.stance: Develop standardized consent options allowing patient choice across use categories. This position is supported primarily by AI companies and consulting firms including Massive Bio, COGA Labs, and Leap Orbit.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters
keyArguments: Patients willing to share data but want explicit control over secondary use
Standard frameworks would reduce compliance burden while respecting autonomy
Consent-Awareness capabilities should be incentivized through reimbursement

areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether healthcare organizations should be compensated when their data improves vendor models versus focusing on enabling data sharing for innovation.

positions.label: Clinicians as Unpaid Labor
positions.stance: Healthcare organizations should be compensated when their data improves vendor models. This position is supported primarily by major health systems including Memorial Sloan Kettering and CommonSpirit Health.
positions.supportLevel: 3 commenters
keyArguments: Vendors use model monitoring as pretense to collect labeled data for retraining proprietary weights without remuneration
Clinical judgments are being monetized without compensation
Feedback tools should only benefit the licensing health system

positions.label: Innovation Enablement
positions.stance: Focus on enabling data sharing rather than restricting it. This position is supported primarily by technology companies and laboratories including Penguin Ai, ACLA, and Scientix.ai.
positions.supportLevel: 4 commenters
keyArguments: Overly restrictive approaches create implementation paralysis
High-value data excluded from AI training to avoid compliance risk
Clear safe harbors would accelerate beneficial AI development

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor exploitation of clinical feedback for proprietary training; lack of control over data once shared; compliance uncertainty. Providers see themselves as unwitting data suppliers whose clinical expertise is being harvested and monetized by vendors without compensation or even disclosure.
specificPoints: Mandatory de-identification before training use
Explicit contractual prohibitions on AI training
Feedback tools redesigned to benefit only the licensing organization
MSK provides detailed account of vendors using model monitoring as pretense for data harvesting
CommonSpirit Health calls for enforceable mechanisms for data purging

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory ambiguity creating implementation paralysis; need for clear safe harbors; competitive disadvantage from unclear rules. Companies emphasize that learning accumulation is the true competitive asset—whoever controls the data feedback loop controls the market.
specificPoints: Privacy-by-Design safe harbors
Clear guidance on de-identification for AI training
FHIR-native consent management incentives
EHY Consulting offers framework distinguishing care delivery versus model improvement
Penguin Ai calls for Privacy-by-Design safe harbors

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member uncertainty about HIPAA compliance; need for model contract language; state-level regulatory patchwork. Associations bridge provider and vendor concerns, seeking practical frameworks that enable innovation while protecting members from liability.
specificPoints: Model BAA language
Clear HIPAA guidance on AI training uses
Disclosure requirements for AI developers
MHA calls to clarify HIPAA-consistent BAAs, de-identification, and restrictions on vendor model training
Health IT End-Users Alliance recommends prohibiting secondary use of data without consent

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Patient autonomy; lack of meaningful consent; AI access to data for purposes patients may not support. Frames the issue as fundamental to the doctor-patient relationship and medical privacy rights.
specificPoints: Explicit patient consent requirements
Restrictions on AI data access
CCHF warns that AI will have access to data without patient consent for purposes the patient may not support

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data permanence in model weights; third-party cloud exposure; patient audit rights. Technical understanding of how AI systems actually process and retain data, combined with clinical sensitivity to therapeutic relationships.
specificPoints: Right to delete data from training sets and model weights
Audit rights for patients
Consent before third-party transfer
Christopher Woodall provides technical explanation that data gets memorized in model parameters potentially forever
Mark O'Brien raises concern about commercial exploitation of sensitive health data in psychotherapy

noteworthyInsights.insight: Learning accumulation as strategic vulnerability — EHY Consulting offers a sophisticated framework distinguishing between data access (governed by HIPAA) and learning accumulation (currently ungoverned), arguing that the most durable advantage—and most consequential vulnerability—often lies not in the AI model or the molecule, but in control over the learning system. This reframes the policy question from privacy protection to competitive strategy.

User research on consent preferences — COGA Labs provides rare empirical evidence: User research found users willing to share AI-derived health insights with care providers but want explicit control over secondary use. This suggests patients are not categorically opposed to data sharing but demand agency.

Feedback tools as data harvesting mechanisms — MSK's AI Governance Committee reveals a specific vendor practice where quality assurance feedback tools are actually designed to collect labeled training data, turning clinicians into unpaid annotators. This concrete example illustrates abstract concerns about commercial exploitation.

Consent-Awareness as reimbursable service — Leap Orbit proposes a novel financial mechanism: reimbursement codes for AI tools demonstrating ability to programmatically respect granular patient restrictions in real-time. This would create market incentives for privacy-protective design.

SUD data de-identification clarification — Waymark raises a specific regulatory gap: whether substance use disorder records de-identified per HIPAA standards may be used for AI training without consent under 42 CFR Part 2. This highlights how overlapping regulatory frameworks create additional uncertainty.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Large academic medical centers like MSK and CommonSpirit focus on vendor exploitation and data ownership, viewing themselves as data-rich targets for commercial harvesting
Technology companies emphasize regulatory clarity and safe harbors, framing ambiguity as the primary barrier to beneficial innovation
Individual practitioners express more fundamental concerns about patient trust and therapeutic relationships
Commenters with technical AI expertise emphasize the permanence of training data in model weights and the need to govern derived artifacts
Data exclusion as unintended consequence: Multiple commenters note that compliance uncertainty leads organizations to exclude high-value data from AI training entirely, potentially harming innovation
Vendor lock-in concern: EHY Consulting warns that vendors accumulating learning advantages through clinical data create dependency relationships that may be difficult to escape
Quality assurance avoidance: MSK notes that fear of data harvesting may discourage providers from using feedback tools that could improve AI safety

keyQuotations.quote: This turns clinicians into unpaid annotators, helping the parent company's internal model development rather than providing oversight.
When patient data enters an AI model for training, it gets memorized in model parameters—potentially forever.

The most durable advantage—and the most consequential vulnerability—often lies not in the AI model or the molecule, but in control over the learning system: the workflow-generated data, outcomes, failure modes, and process metadata that enable iterative optimization over time.

AI will have access to this data, also without patient consent, for purposes the patient may not support, and potentially to directly impact the patient's care in ways that could be harmful, insufficient, or generated through AI hallucinations.
Patients have zero visibility into where their health data goes or who accesses it.

Laboratories have contributed exponential AI-enabled medicine advancements through large de-identified datasets including more precise identification of cancer origin and better understanding and predicting patient response to specific treatments.
Ambiguity regarding data 'ingestion' for AI improvement leads to implementation paralysis.
User research found users willing to share AI-derived health insights with care providers but want explicit control over secondary use.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of technical, legal, and ethical dimensions. Multiple stakeholders provide concrete examples and specific policy recommendations rather than general complaints. The debate reflects genuine engagement with tradeoffs between innovation and privacy protection.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite specific experiences (MSK's vendor practices, COGA Labs' user research) but most arguments are based on logical reasoning rather than empirical data. Limited quantitative evidence on actual harms or benefits from current practices.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from patient advocacy groups (only 1 comment). No direct patient voices in the sample. Geographic representation limited to a few state-level associations. Small and rural healthcare providers underrepresented.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The issue involves intersecting technical (AI training mechanics, de-identification), legal (HIPAA, 42 CFR Part 2, state laws), economic (data ownership, compensation), and ethical (consent, autonomy) dimensions that commenters navigate with varying degrees of sophistication.

5.4.themeDescription: AI-Specific Cybersecurity Threats and Vulnerabilities. This sub-theme addresses security vulnerabilities specific to AI systems, including adversarial attacks, prompt injection, and data pipeline security. || It includes concerns about AI-specific attack vectors, proposals for AI security standards, debates about responsibility for AI cybersecurity, and discussions of emerging threats like autonomous agentic malware

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal strong consensus that AI introduces novel attack surfaces requiring security frameworks beyond traditional HIPAA protections, with particular alarm about autonomous agentic malware and prompt injection attacks that operate at speeds exceeding human response capabilities. The central tension lies between holding AI developers primarily responsible for cybersecurity (favored by healthcare providers and end-users) versus distributing responsibility across the ecosystem. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend HHS develop AI-specific security standards, incentivize defensive AI capabilities, and shift liability toward developers who control system architecture.

consensusPoints.text: AI introduces fundamentally novel attack surfaces that current frameworks do not address. This consensus spans cybersecurity professionals, healthcare providers, trade associations, and researchers. Representative examples include independent researchers noting that AI introduces novel attack surfaces that current frameworks do not address, the American Urological Association identifying AI-specific attack vectors including adversarial attacks, prompt injection, and data pipeline security vulnerabilities, and ECRI warning that tool integrations (browser, plugins, AI agents) expand attack surface and create new routes for data exfiltration or manipulation.

exceptions.text: No commenter argued that existing HIPAA security frameworks are sufficient for AI-specific threats.

consensusPoints.text: Prompt injection is a recognized, active threat requiring specific countermeasures, with both theoretical concerns and real-world examples cited. Commenters note that attackers generate novel prompt injections for every stage of an attack to bypass standard IOC monitoring, that prompt injection is a real security and privacy attack vector for AI systems that requires active management, and that safety-focused red teaming should include prompt injection and tool hijacking.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (8 of 15)

consensusPoints.text: Healthcare providers should not bear sole liability for sophisticated AI-driven attacks. Providers argue they should not be penalized when they are victims of sophisticated, AI-driven attacks, that holding end-users liable for breaches or system failures they cannot control would undermine trust and deter adoption, and that cybersecurity policy should be incentive-based to reward providers for maintaining reasonable, diligent adherence to evolving best practices.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most healthcare providers and end-user organizations (6 of 7 provider/end-user comments)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether AI developers should bear primary responsibility for securing systems versus distributing responsibility across the ecosystem including developers, providers, and cloud partners.

positions.label: Developer-Primary Responsibility
positions.stance: AI developers should bear primary responsibility for securing systems. The Health IT End-Users Alliance, AAFP, and University of Kansas Health System advocate this position, arguing that developers have direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure that end-users lack.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of healthcare providers and end-user organizations (6 of 15 commenters)
keyArguments: Developers have direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure that end-users lack
End-users often lack visibility into system vulnerabilities or control over defenses
Vendor liability caps (e.g., one year of service fees) ignore massive downstream risk

positions.label: Shared Ecosystem Responsibility
positions.stance: Security requires coordinated controls across developers, providers, and cloud partners. EHY Consulting and Cyberhill Partners advocate this position, emphasizing third-party and subcontractor risk management.
positions.supportLevel: Minority position, primarily from consulting/business commenters (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Third-party and subcontractor risk (cloud hosting, analytics, support) requires audit rights and contractual controls
Security-by-design must be enforceable across the supply chain
Multi-layered guardrail patterns require implementation at multiple points

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether defensive systems must match attacker speed through autonomous AI or whether multi-layered guardrails with human escalation remain essential.

positions.label: AI-Driven Autonomous Defense
positions.stance: Defensive systems must match attacker speed through autonomous AI. Ty Greenhalgh argues that the threat landscape has shifted to AI vs. AI and that clinical care cannot be protected by human-speed manual responses.
positions.supportLevel: Strongly advocated by cybersecurity specialists (2 commenters with deep technical expertise)
keyArguments: The threat landscape has shifted to AI vs. AI - clinical care cannot be protected by human-speed manual responses
A policy that relies on human intervention for AI-driven threats is a policy that accepts failure
Attackers use agentic AI to automate lateral movement at speeds humans cannot match

positions.label: Human-in-the-Loop Guardrails
positions.stance: Multi-layered guardrails with human escalation remain essential. Satyadhar Joshi and Cyberhill Partners advocate for guardrail patterns that include escalation triggering human involvement and pre-deployment red team exercises.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support from researchers and some providers (3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: Guardrail patterns should include escalation guardrails triggering human involvement
Pre-deployment evaluation should include red team exercises to identify failure modes
Human oversight remains necessary for clinical decision validation

areasOfDebate.topic: Regulatory Approach: Prescriptive Standards vs. Incentive-Based Frameworks
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether HHS should mandate specific AI security controls or reward best practices rather than penalize breaches.

positions.label: Prescriptive Security Standards
positions.stance: HHS should mandate specific AI security controls. EHY Consulting, ECRI, and Cyberhill Partners advocate for enforceable security-by-design requirements and minimum security controls for AI-enabled programs.
positions.supportLevel: Moderate support across stakeholder types (5-6 commenters)
keyArguments: Security-by-design requirements should be enforceable
Need minimum security controls for AI-enabled programs with ability to influence patient care
Updates to HIPAA Security Rule technical safeguards needed for AI-specific threats

positions.label: Incentive-Based Frameworks
positions.stance: Reward best practices rather than penalize breaches. WellSpan Health and University of Kansas Health System argue that policies should reward providers for implementing recognized cybersecurity best practices since sophisticated attacks can overwhelm even well-prepared organizations.
positions.supportLevel: Favored by healthcare providers (3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: Policies should reward providers for implementing recognized cybersecurity best practices
Sophisticated attacks can overwhelm even well-prepared organizations
Punitive approaches deter adoption and undermine trust

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Speed of AI-driven attacks exceeding human response capability; inadequacy of traditional scanning and monitoring approaches; emergence of autonomous agentic malware. Only group emphasizing the AI vs. AI paradigm shift and specific attack techniques (BodySnatcher, ForcedLeak, Silver Fox).
specificPoints: Continuous Threat Exposure Management (CTEM) needed to address AI threats
AI-driven inventory systems required to discover AI models and dependencies in real-time
Autonomous detection and response capabilities essential
HHS reimbursement incentives needed for AI-based defensive solutions
Ty Greenhalgh provides detailed technical analysis of agentic malware capabilities

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Liability exposure for attacks beyond their control; vendor liability caps that ignore downstream risk; need for incentive-based rather than punitive cybersecurity policy. Bring operational reality of implementing AI security while managing clinical care and direct experience with vendor contracting limitations.
specificPoints: Safe harbors needed for providers following best practices
Modernization of Stark Law/Anti-Kickback Statute for cybersecurity collaboration
Developer-primary liability frameworks preferred
University of Kansas Health System highlights vendor liability caps
WellSpan Health advocates incentive-based policy

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member physicians lack visibility into AI system vulnerabilities; emerging threats from quantum computing; need for consistent baseline cybersecurity standards. Represent frontline clinicians who depend on secure tools but cannot evaluate or control security architecture.
specificPoints: Developer responsibility for securing systems essential
ONC certification for security standards recommended
Alignment with federal health data protections needed
AAFP emphasizes family physicians' dependence on secure-by-design tools
American College of Cardiology raises quantum computing threats

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Third-party and subcontractor risk; cloud and remote access security; need for enforceable security-by-design requirements. Bring experience implementing secure AI in highly regulated environments including DoD and Intelligence Community.
specificPoints: Zero-trust architecture recommended
Red team exercises essential for pre-deployment evaluation
Identity/least privilege/logging controls needed
Audit rights for subcontractors required
R&D in federated learning and differential privacy recommended
Cyberhill Partners brings DoD/IC security expertise
EHY Consulting emphasizes subcontractor controls

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Data breach risks eroding trust; AI-specific attack vectors; need for tailored privacy and security standards. Focus on independent evaluation of AI safety and systematic analysis of stakeholder perspectives.
specificPoints: Multi-layered guardrail patterns recommended
Safety-focused red teaming including prompt injection testing essential
AI-specific security standards for healthcare needed
ECRI provides detailed red teaming recommendations
Satyadhar Joshi proposes guardrail architecture

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: AI systems process healthcare data in plaintext during inference; encryption overhead makes clinical deployment infeasible; data exposure through third-party cloud providers. Deep technical understanding of computational limitations of current privacy-preserving technologies.
specificPoints: Current privacy-preserving encryption imposes 100-1000x computational overhead
Fundamental tension between security and clinical feasibility cannot be solved through policy alone
Christopher Woodall highlights computational overhead limitations

noteworthyInsights.insight: Medvidi, a telehealth provider, reports already managing prompt injection risks using guardrails that review AI before and after invocation - demonstrating that practical solutions exist and are being deployed by forward-thinking organizations.

Cyberhill Partners brings experience from Department of Defense and Intelligence Community deployments, suggesting healthcare could benefit from security architectures proven in national security contexts, including zero-trust principles and HIPAA-aligned AI deployments.

Christopher Woodall identifies a critical gap that no other commenter addresses: current privacy-preserving encryption imposes 100-1000x computational overhead, creating a fundamental tension between security and clinical feasibility that cannot be solved through policy alone.

Ty Greenhalgh reframes the entire cybersecurity challenge as AI vs. AI, arguing that the threat landscape has fundamentally changed in ways that make human-speed responses obsolete - a perspective that challenges traditional security governance models.

University of Kansas Health System highlights a specific market failure: vendor liability caps at one year of service fees while providers face potentially unlimited downstream risk, creating misaligned incentives that policy could address.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Healthcare providers consistently emphasize liability protection and incentive-based approaches, reflecting their position as potential breach victims with limited control over AI system architecture.
Cybersecurity specialists focus on technical threat evolution and autonomous defensive capabilities, bringing urgency about attack speed that other stakeholders may underappreciate.
Trade associations bridge clinical and technical concerns, emphasizing that frontline physicians lack visibility into security vulnerabilities.
Commenters with DoD/IC experience advocate for zero-trust architecture and red teaming, reflecting security practices from high-stakes environments.
Commenters with direct AI deployment experience provide concrete examples of managing risks in practice, grounding theoretical concerns in operational reality.
Punitive liability frameworks could deter AI adoption, particularly among smaller providers who cannot absorb breach costs.
Vendor liability caps create moral hazard where developers have insufficient incentive to invest in security.
Traditional scanning approaches may provide false assurance when AI assets exist in ephemeral containers and cloud environments.
Multiple commenters note that existing frameworks (HIPAA, NIST) were not designed for AI-specific threats, creating a regulatory gap that attackers can exploit while defenders wait for guidance.

keyQuotations.quote: A policy that relies on human intervention for AI-driven threats is a policy that accepts failure.
AI developers, not end-users, are best positioned to mitigate and respond to cyberattacks given their direct access to system architecture, code, and infrastructure.
Holding end-users liable for breaches or system failures they cannot control would undermine trust and deter adoption.
AI vendors cap liability for data breaches at very low amounts (e.g., one year of service fees), ignoring massive downstream risk.
Current AI systems process healthcare data in plaintext during inference - data sits exposed in memory, model weights, and system logs.
The threat landscape has shifted to 'AI vs. AI' - clinical care cannot be protected by human-speed manual responses.
Attackers generate novel prompt injections for every stage of an attack to bypass standard IOC monitoring.
Family physicians depend on AI tools that are secure by design but often lack visibility into system vulnerabilities or control over defenses.
Quantum computing and advanced computational techniques threaten to nullify existing privacy and security techniques.
MEDvidi already manages prompt injection risks using guardrails that review AI before and after invocation.
Sophisticated AI-driven attacks can overwhelm even well-prepared organizations.
Third-party and subcontractor risk (cloud hosting, analytics, support), especially when access paths are opaque.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated technical understanding of AI-specific threats, with cybersecurity specialists providing detailed analysis of attack vectors and healthcare providers offering concrete operational perspectives. Debate is substantive and evidence-based.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite specific real-world examples (ServiceNow BodySnatcher flaw, Salesforce ForcedLeak exploit, Silver Fox group), technical specifications (100-1000x encryption overhead), and direct operational experience. Some claims rely on general awareness rather than specific evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from AI developers/vendors who would be most affected by developer-primary liability proposals. No comments from smaller healthcare practices that may face different challenges than large health systems. Patient advocacy perspectives absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - involves intersection of rapidly evolving AI technology, cybersecurity threat landscape, healthcare operations, and regulatory frameworks. Fundamental technical limitations (encryption overhead) may constrain policy options.

5.5.themeDescription: Sensitive Health Information and Enhanced Protections. This sub-theme addresses heightened privacy concerns for particularly sensitive health information in AI contexts, including behavioral health, substance use, and genetic data. || It includes concerns about 42 CFR Part 2 restrictions limiting AI access to substance use data, debates about genetic information privacy in AI training, proposals for enhanced protections for mental health data, and arguments that sensitivity should inform AI governance requirements. This excludes general PHI and focuses on categories requiring special protection

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal strong consensus that current 42 CFR Part 2 restrictions on substance use disorder (SUD) data create significant barriers to AI-enabled care coordination, with nearly all commenters (10 of 11 addressing this issue) calling for modernization while preserving privacy protections. The central tension lies between AI technology companies and healthcare providers who seek data access to improve care for vulnerable populations, and behavioral health clinicians who emphasize that confidentiality is foundational to treatment itself. Commenters broadly recommend aligning Part 2 with HIPAA, modernizing consent infrastructure, and developing specialized guidance for behavioral health AI applications—though they diverge on how much access is appropriate and what safeguards are sufficient.

consensusPoints.text: 42 CFR Part 2 Restrictions Create Barriers to AI-Enabled Care. Nearly all commenters addressing substance use data agree that current Part 2 restrictions limit AI's ability to support coordinated care for patients with substance use disorders. Waymark notes that current Part 2 restrictions create barriers to integrating SUD patients into population health management programs. NeuroFlow observes that current 42 CFR Part 2 rules prevent AI algorithms from accessing substance use disorder data even within integrated care teams where patients have consented to coordinated treatment. Massive Bio similarly notes that 42 CFR Part 2 restrictions limit AI access to substance use data, creating barriers.

exceptions.text: While agreeing restrictions create barriers, behavioral health clinicians emphasize these barriers exist for important reasons and any changes must preserve core privacy protections.

consensusPoints.text: Mental Health Data Carries Heightened Privacy Risks Requiring Special Consideration. All commenters addressing mental health data acknowledge that behavioral health information requires enhanced protections beyond standard PHI. Medvidi notes that mental health conversational data carries heightened privacy risks. Mark O'Brien, LCPC emphasizes that behavioral health settings involve uniquely sensitive disclosures: trauma histories, suicidal ideation, substance use, and deeply personal relational information. Dr. Bethany Russell observes that patients have fear of data breaches or discriminatory use of mental health information.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: All commenters addressing mental health data (5 of 5)

consensusPoints.text: Modernization Should Preserve Privacy While Enabling Care Coordination. A strong majority of commenters explicitly frame their recommendations as balancing access with protection, not eliminating safeguards. Dr. Russell recommends modernizing 42 CFR Part 2 to permit AI-enabled care coordination while maintaining privacy protections. SanLuca AI calls for modernizing operational guidance for Part 2 data to enable safe, consented exchange for whole-person AI support. NeuroFlow notes that modernized electronic consent mechanisms would improve integration while preserving privacy protections.

areasOfDebate.topic: Appropriate Level of AI Access to Sensitive Behavioral Health Data
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters diverge on whether to expand AI access to behavioral health data or maintain strong restrictions given the unique sensitivity of this information.

positions.label: Enable Integration
positions.stance: Align Part 2 with HIPAA to allow AI-driven care coordination. Technology companies and integrated care providers including Waymark, NeuroFlow, and Massive Bio advocate for this position.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority of technology companies and integrated care providers (7 commenters)
keyArguments: Patients with co-occurring conditions are highest-risk and stand to benefit most from AI
Excluding SUD data compromises AI model precision for vulnerable populations
February 2024 SAMHSA/OCR alignment creates opportunity to address remaining gaps

positions.label: Preserve Restrictions
positions.stance: Maintain strong barriers given unique sensitivity of behavioral health data. Behavioral health clinicians including an anonymous psychologist and Mark O'Brien, LCPC advocate for this position.
positions.supportLevel: Behavioral health clinicians (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Confidentiality is the structural condition that makes treatment possible
Many AI mental health tools operate outside HIPAA, leaving data unprotected
Even de-identified mental health data may be vulnerable to re-identification

areasOfDebate.topic: De-Identified Data Use for AI Training
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether de-identified SUD and mental health data should be available for AI model development.

positions.label: Permit Training Use
positions.stance: De-identified SUD data should be available for AI model development. Waymark argues that data de-identified per HIPAA standards should be usable without consent and that AI outputs from de-identified data should not constitute Part 2 records unless identifying specific patients.
positions.supportLevel: At least 2 commenters explicitly advocate this position
keyArguments: Data de-identified per HIPAA standards (45 CFR 164.514(b)) should be usable without consent
AI outputs from de-identified data should not constitute Part 2 records unless identifying specific patients

positions.label: Exercise Caution
positions.stance: De-identification may be insufficient protection for mental health data. Mark O'Brien and an anonymous psychologist raise concerns about re-identification risks with detailed behavioral health narratives.
positions.supportLevel: At least 2 behavioral health clinicians
keyArguments: Re-identification risks are heightened with detailed behavioral health narratives
Patients often don't know their mental health data isn't protected

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Regulatory barriers preventing AI access to complete clinical records, lack of standardized consent orchestration across systems, and compliance risk leading to exclusion of high-value data from AI training. These companies frame the issue as AI effectiveness being compromised for exactly the patients who would benefit most—those with complex, co-occurring conditions.
specificPoints: NeuroFlow notes that when AI risk stratification models cannot access complete clinical records, their precision is compromised for exactly the patients who stand to benefit most
Leap Orbit observes that high-value data is often excluded from AI training sets and real-time clinical feeds to avoid compliance risk

Proposed solutions include aligning Part 2 with HIPAA, modernizing electronic consent infrastructure, issuing specific guidance on BAA requirements for AI vendors, and pilot programs to research harmonization of specialized datasets

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preservation of therapeutic confidentiality as foundational to treatment, heightened re-identification risks with detailed mental health narratives, and AI tools operating outside HIPAA protections. These clinicians emphasize that privacy in mental health is not merely a preference but a clinical necessity—without it, patients will not disclose information essential to treatment.
specificPoints: An anonymous psychologist states that confidentiality is not a preference—it is the structural condition that makes treatment possible
Proposed solutions include special regulatory consideration for behavioral health and psychotherapy, enhanced patient education about data protections, and maintaining strong consent requirements

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member organizations need education on 42 CFR Part 2 implementation, youth-specific privacy protections for child-facing technology, and historical exclusion from HITECH Act funding creating infrastructure gaps. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing represents 3,200+ behavioral health organizations serving 10 million+ individuals and emphasizes workforce and infrastructure challenges unique to the behavioral health sector.
specificPoints: Provider education and guidance on Part 2 implementation is needed
Youth-specific privacy and safety protections should be developed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Behavioral health data maintained in separate systems, mental health diagnoses under-coded due to stigma, and difficulty applying AI for outcome measurement in mental health. These providers operate at the intersection of behavioral and physical health and see firsthand how data silos impede whole-person care.
specificPoints: Waymark notes their care teams include therapists delivering behavioral health interventions, and their AI systems support behavioral health care coordination
Proposed solutions include updating Part 2 with SAMHSA to align with modern AI-driven care coordination and mapping AI practices to existing regulations including Part 2

noteworthyInsights.insight: Timing opportunity identified: Waymark notes that with the February 2024 SAMHSA/OCR final rule aligning Part 2 with HIPAA and compliance date of February 16, 2026 now upon us, this is the appropriate moment to address remaining gaps the final rule did not resolve. This suggests a regulatory window for action.

Structural vs. preferential framing: An anonymous psychologist reframes the privacy debate by arguing confidentiality is not a patient preference but the structural condition that makes treatment possible—suggesting that weakening protections could undermine treatment effectiveness itself, not just patient comfort.

Stigma-driven under-coding: Waymark identifies that mental health diagnoses may be under-coded due to stigma, revealing that data quality issues compound access restrictions—even when data is technically available, it may be incomplete.

Consent infrastructure as technical barrier: Leap Orbit identifies that the absence of a standardized, automated way to orchestrate consent across systems is a technical infrastructure problem, not just a policy problem—suggesting that regulatory changes alone may be insufficient without accompanying technical standards.

Detailed regulatory proposals: Waymark provides unusually specific guidance requests, including clarification that AI-generated outputs derived solely from de-identified data do not constitute Part 2 records unless they identify specific patients—offering policymakers concrete language to consider.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Stakeholder Type Correlates with Position: Technology companies and integrated care providers consistently advocate for expanded access while behavioral health clinicians consistently emphasize preservation of protections. This split suggests the debate may benefit from cross-stakeholder dialogue to find middle ground.

Sophistication of Regulatory Proposals Varies: Larger organizations like Waymark and NeuroFlow provide detailed, specific regulatory recommendations while individual clinicians express concerns but offer fewer concrete solutions. This may reflect differential access to legal/regulatory expertise.

Consent Modernization as Potential Common Ground: Both access advocates and privacy advocates reference consent mechanisms. Modernized electronic consent infrastructure could potentially satisfy both camps, emerging as a potential area for productive policy development.

Youth Populations Identified as Requiring Special Attention: The National Council for Mental Wellbeing specifically calls out the need for youth-specific privacy and safety protections. This population is mentioned only once but represents a distinct regulatory consideration.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Multiple commenters note that strict privacy protections may paradoxically harm the populations they're designed to protect by excluding them from AI-enabled care improvements. This creates a policy tension between protection and access that requires careful balancing.

keyQuotations.quote: Confidentiality is not a preference — it is the structural condition that makes treatment possible.
When AI risk stratification models cannot access complete clinical records, their precision is compromised for exactly the patients who stand to benefit most.
Behavioral health settings involve uniquely sensitive disclosures: trauma histories, suicidal ideation, substance use, and deeply personal relational information.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Behavioral Health Clinician (Licensed Clinical Professional Counselor)

keyQuotations.quote: Patients with co-occurring substance use and mental health conditions represent some of the highest-risk, highest-cost individuals.
No standardized, automated way to orchestrate consent across systems means high-value data often excluded from AI training sets and real-time clinical feeds to avoid compliance risk.
With compliance date of February 16, 2026 now upon us, this is appropriate moment to address remaining gaps the final rule did not resolve.

Many AI mental health tools operate outside HIPAA, leaving sensitive behavioral health data unprotected... Patients often don't know their mental health data isn't protected when using these AI tools.
Modernize 42 CFR Part 2 (Substance Use Disorder Records) to permit AI-enabled care coordination while maintaining privacy protections.
Introduction of AI systems into psychotherapy sessions creates heightened risks whether for documentation, clinical decision support, or conversational interaction.
keyQuotations.sourceType: Behavioral Health Clinician (Licensed Clinical Professional Counselor)

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific regulatory frameworks (42 CFR Part 2, HIPAA), cite concrete examples and evidence, and offer detailed policy recommendations. Both sides of the debate present reasoned arguments grounded in their professional experience and expertise.

evidenceBase.explanation: Commenters reference specific regulations, compliance dates, and real-world operational challenges. Technology companies provide detailed use cases while clinicians draw on clinical experience. However, quantitative data on outcomes is limited, with most evidence being experiential or operational rather than research-based.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Individual patients with substance use disorders or mental health conditions are not directly represented in the comments. The debate is primarily between those who provide services (clinicians, technology companies, integrated care providers) rather than those who receive them. Additionally, smaller behavioral health practices and community mental health centers may be underrepresented compared to larger technology companies.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The issue involves multiple intersecting regulatory frameworks (42 CFR Part 2, HIPAA, state laws), technical infrastructure challenges (consent orchestration, data de-identification), clinical considerations (therapeutic relationship, treatment effectiveness), and ethical tensions (protection vs. access for vulnerable populations). The February 2024 SAMHSA/OCR alignment adds a time-sensitive regulatory dimension.

6.1.themeDescription: Fee-for-Service Efficiency Penalty and Misaligned Incentives

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all 35 commenters that fee-for-service payment models create a fundamental structural barrier to AI adoption by penalizing efficiency gains and rewarding volume over outcomes. Commenters spanning healthcare providers, AI vendors, professional associations, and academic researchers uniformly describe an "efficiency penalty" where AI tools that improve care quality, reduce unnecessary utilization, or streamline workflows paradoxically reduce revenue. The dominant recommendation thrust is a transition toward value-based payment models, though commenters diverge on implementation specifics and timeline, with some questioning whether even value-based arrangements adequately align incentives given patient churn across payers.

consensusPoints.text: Fee-for-service fundamentally misaligns incentives for AI adoption. Nearly all commenters explicitly identify fee-for-service as creating structural disincentives for AI adoption, spanning every stakeholder category represented. WellSpan Health states that any productivity gains from AI would be punitive under fee-for-service models, creating a self-defeating cycle. AAPA notes that efficiency gains may be unrewarded or penalized, creating a disincentive for adoption even when AI may improve quality and patient outcomes. AAFP believes that the fee-for-service model will continue to drive the creation of AI tools to maximize utilization, instead of AI tools to actually improve patient care.

exceptions.text: Epic Systems suggests fee-for-service reimbursement for AI could quickly become costly and is unnecessary for AI with proven ROI, implying the problem may be overstated for certain AI applications.

consensusPoints.text: The "efficiency penalty" is real and documented across clinical settings. A strong majority of commenters describe specific mechanisms by which AI-driven efficiency reduces revenue, with multiple commenters using the exact phrase "efficiency penalty." TapestryHealth notes that if AI allows a clinician to review a patient's status in 2 minutes instead of 20, the provider is financially penalized. RBMA warns that if AI improves efficiency or reduces interpretive effort, it may paradoxically threaten reimbursement, discouraging adoption even when patient care improves. NeuroFlow explains that preventing a psychiatric hospitalization or reducing emergency department utilization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than the providers who made the investment.

Prevention and care coordination are systematically undervalued. Most commenters note that AI's greatest value often lies in prevention, early detection, and care coordination—activities poorly compensated under fee-for-service. Ellipsis Health observes that in fee-for-service environments, preventing a hospitalization or ED visit often reduces revenue rather than creating it. Interstella states that fee-for-service penalizes prevention and care coordination, and the efficiency penalty means better AI equals lower payment. Dr. Bethany Russell notes that preventative care is undercompensated relative to crisis management.

Investment-benefit misalignment undermines sustainability. Nearly all commenters addressing financial sustainability identify a core problem: organizations investing in AI are not those capturing its financial benefits. Health AI Institute repeatedly observes that the organizations investing in AI are not those capturing its financial benefits, undermining sustainability even when clinical value is evident. SanLuca AI notes that financial ROI can lag or shift across stakeholders. AUA states that costs sit with clinics but savings accrue to payers or the broader system.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing financial sustainability (18+ of 35)

areasOfDebate.topic: Adequacy of Value-Based Payment as a Solution
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters debate whether transitioning to value-based payment models will adequately resolve the misalignment between AI investment and financial returns.

positions.label: Value-Based Transition
positions.stance: Shift to outcome-based models will resolve misalignment. Massive Bio, Interstella, and Penguin Ai are among the strong majority endorsing this direction.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (25+ commenters)
keyArguments: Rewards outcomes rather than volume, aligning incentives with AI's value proposition
Creates financial motivation to invest in prevention and efficiency
Allows organizations to capture benefits of reduced utilization

positions.label: Value-Based Skepticism
positions.stance: Even value-based models have structural limitations. HealthScoreAI raises concerns about fundamental limitations in value-based arrangements.
positions.supportLevel: Small but notable minority (2-3 commenters)
keyArguments: Patient churn (5-6 year average enrollment) means payers don't bear true long-term risk
Annual benefit design changes allow indirect shedding of high-risk members
Only entity that consistently bears true long-term financial risk is HHS itself

areasOfDebate.topic: Role of Per-Use Reimbursement for AI
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters disagree on whether AI tools require ongoing per-use reimbursement or primarily need support through initial scaling periods.

positions.label: Ongoing Reimbursement Needed
positions.stance: AI tools require sustainable payment pathways. RBMA and Providence emphasize the need for ongoing reimbursement mechanisms.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of provider and vendor commenters
keyArguments: Practice costs increase due to AI licensing, integration, validation, and cybersecurity
Current reimbursement formulas don't reflect these new expenses
Without payment pathways, adoption will stall

positions.label: Initial Support Sufficient
positions.stance: Organizations primarily need help through scaling period. Epic Systems, a major EHR vendor, argues that ongoing per-use payment is unnecessary for proven AI.
positions.supportLevel: Limited (1-2 commenters)
keyArguments: Fee-for-service reimbursement for AI could quickly become costly
AI with proven ROI doesn't need ongoing per-use payment
Support through initial scaling period is the primary need

areasOfDebate.topic: Whether AI Actually Delivers Efficiency Gains
areasOfDebate.description: Commenters differ on whether AI reliably delivers efficiency gains or whether oversight requirements may negate those gains.

positions.label: Efficiency Gains Real
positions.stance: AI demonstrably reduces time and improves workflows. TapestryHealth and Medvidi provide examples of concrete efficiency improvements.
positions.supportLevel: Most commenters assume this premise
keyArguments: AI scribes reduce documentation time
AI triage accelerates clinical workflows
Efficiency is precisely why the penalty exists

positions.label: Efficiency Gains Uncertain
positions.stance: AI may require additional clinician oversight that negates efficiency gains. AASM and Markkula Center for Applied Ethics raise concerns about uncompensated verification labor.
positions.supportLevel: Small minority (3-4 commenters)
keyArguments: When AI outputs are unreliable, clinicians spend additional unbilled time reviewing
AI improving quality but requiring verification effort may penalize organizations
This time-intensive oversight represents uncompensated labor

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Direct revenue loss from efficiency gains, inability to justify AI investment when ROI accrues elsewhere, and shorter visits leading to lower billing codes. Providers experience the efficiency penalty firsthand—they see improved care but reduced revenue simultaneously.
specificPoints: WellSpan Health describes a self-defeating cycle where productivity gains are punitive
TapestryHealth quantifies the penalty: reviewing a patient's status in 2 minutes instead of 20 results in financial penalty
Providence notes visits presumably get shorter over time, but this results in lower reimbursement
Proposed solutions include value-based payment transition, add-on payment models for AI utilization, and CMS examination of sustainable payment rates

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Market barriers preventing adoption of beneficial tools, unclear reimbursement pathways for efficiency-focused AI, and financial ROI lagging or shifting across stakeholders. Vendors see the demand for their products constrained not by clinical value but by payment model limitations.
specificPoints: Ellipsis Health notes health systems struggle to operationalize or scale these tools because the financial upside accrues slowly or to a different stakeholder
NeuroFlow provides detailed analysis of transfer effects where cost reductions accrue to payers rather than providers
Massive Bio reports AI scribes had limited financial ROI initially despite improving clinician experience
Proposed solutions include transition to value-based models and rewarding AI that reduces documentation burden and expands access

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member practices cannot absorb AI costs without aligned incentives, RVU-based payment penalizes AI-assisted efficiency, and payer assumptions about automatic efficiency gains. Associations represent collective experience across thousands of practitioners, providing systemic rather than anecdotal evidence.
specificPoints: RBMA (2,000+ members) warns innovation that improves quality and safety may be financially penalized
AAFP (128,300 members) states FFS will continue to drive the creation of AI tools to maximize utilization
AMA raises concern about redistributive trends from digital health technology payments
Proposed solutions include ensuring reimbursement is not reduced based on efficiency assumptions and amending legislation mandating RVU redistribution for AI use

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Structural barriers that coding changes cannot overcome, ethical implications of penalizing quality improvement, and evidence base for policy decisions. Academic commenters frame the issue in terms of systemic design flaws and unintended consequences.
specificPoints: Penn NeuroVERT team recommends specific regulatory model (42 CFR §412.87) as a framework for add-on payments
Markkula Center frames the ethical dimension: organizations are penalized financially when AI improves quality
Health AI Institute documents repeated observation of investment-benefit misalignment across clinical settings
Proposed solutions include add-on payment models similar to New Technology Add-on Payment framework

noteworthyInsights.insight: Patient churn undermines value-based assumptions. HealthScoreAI offers a sophisticated critique that even value-based models may not solve the problem because average enrollment duration in employer-sponsored coverage and Medicare Advantage plans is often 5-6 years, meaning costs of underinvestment in prevention or suboptimal care are frequently externalized to future payers. This suggests HHS itself may be the only entity bearing true long-term risk.

The "acute becomes chronic" dynamic. Dr. Bethany Russell, a mental health clinician and AI developer, observes that under fee-for-service, the acute intervention becomes a chronic intervention—a powerful framing of how payment models shape clinical trajectories.

Quantified supply cost precedent. AMA provides concrete data on unintended consequences: Currently 94 medical supply items embedded in codes with purchase price over $500, representing $1.53 billion in direct costs and 21% of all direct practice expense medical supply costs in non-facility setting. This precedent raises concerns about similar redistributive effects if AI is paid within MPFS.

Behavioral health's unique vulnerability. SonderMind, operating AI tools across all 50 states, notes that AI tools designed to reduce visit frequency or provide continuous care effectively lower billable volume—a particularly acute problem in behavioral health where continuous engagement may be more valuable than episodic visits.

The verification paradox. Markkula Center for Applied Ethics identifies a subtle but important dynamic: If AI saves time, adoption is straightforward; if AI improves quality but requires additional clinician effort to verify safely, organizations may be penalized financially. This suggests the safest AI implementations may be the least financially viable.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Specialty-Specific Variations: Radiology faces particular vulnerability because RVUs are based on assumptions about time and cognitive effort—AI that shortens reading times directly threatens reimbursement (RBMA). Behavioral health commenters including SonderMind, NeuroFlow, Dr. Russell, and Medvidi emphasize that continuous care models are especially penalized because current CPT codes don't capture between-session monitoring or proactive outreach. Sleep medicine (AASM) highlights the oversight burden where AI outputs require significant validation time.

Facility-Type Patterns: Large integrated systems like WellSpan and Providence can articulate the problem clearly but still struggle to justify AI investment. Community practices face existential barriers—thin margins make AI adoption impossible without payment reform (VieCure, AAFP members). Post-acute care experiences the efficiency penalty acutely in remote monitoring contexts (TapestryHealth).

Experience Correlations: Commenters with direct AI deployment experience including SonderMind, NeuroFlow, TapestryHealth, and Medvidi provide the most specific descriptions of the efficiency penalty. Professional associations representing large memberships (AAFP, RBMA, AAPA, AMA) frame concerns in systemic terms. Academic researchers including Health AI Institute, Penn NeuroVERT, and Markkula Center offer theoretical frameworks and policy analogies.

Unintended Consequences Identified: AI development distortion where FFS will drive creation of AI tools to maximize utilization rather than improve care (AAFP). Workflow preservation creating incentive to maintain slower, manual workflows even when AI is available (TapestryHealth). Cost shifting where technology developer costs shift to frontline providers through uncompensated oversight labor (AASM). Equity widening as under-resourced settings fall further behind while well-resourced systems capture AI benefits (VieCure, AAFP).

keyQuotations.quote: Any productivity gains that result from the use of AI would be punitive under the fee-for-service models, creating a self-defeating cycle to the adoption of AI.
If AI allows a clinician to review a patient's status in 2 minutes instead of 20, the provider is financially penalized.
This creates a policy dilemma: innovation that improves quality and safety may be financially penalized.
We repeatedly observe that the organizations investing in AI are not those capturing its financial benefits, undermining sustainability even when clinical value is evident.

Preventing a psychiatric hospitalization or reducing emergency department utilization improves outcomes and lowers total cost of care, but in fee-for-service models that value accrues to payers rather than the providers who made the investment.
The AAFP believes that the fee-for-service (FFS) model will continue to drive the creation of AI tools to maximize utilization, instead of AI tools to actually improve patient care.
The acute intervention becomes a chronic intervention under current fee-for-service models.
This time-intensive oversight represents uncompensated labor and shifts costs from technology developers to frontline providers.
If AI improves quality but requires additional clinician effort to verify safely, organizations may be penalized financially.
When efficiency translates directly to financial benefit, adoption surges.
Only entity that consistently bears true long-term financial risk is HHS itself, which ultimately absorbs downstream consequences.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder categories demonstrate sophisticated understanding of payment model dynamics and provide specific, evidence-based arguments. The discourse includes concrete examples, quantified impacts, and nuanced policy analysis rather than general complaints.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters provide specific examples from their operational experience, including quantified time savings, membership data, and cost figures. However, most evidence is anecdotal or based on organizational experience rather than peer-reviewed research or systematic studies.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from payers and insurers who would provide the counterpoint perspective on value capture. Also minimal input from patients or patient advocacy organizations on how payment model limitations affect their care access and quality.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The issue involves interconnected dynamics between payment models, investment incentives, stakeholder benefit distribution, and long-term health outcomes. Commenters identify multiple feedback loops and unintended consequences that resist simple solutions.

6.2.themeDescription: Value-Based Payment Models and AI Alignment

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types that value-based payment models represent the most critical policy lever for accelerating responsible AI adoption in healthcare. Commenters universally agree that fee-for-service creates perverse incentives that penalize AI-driven efficiency, while value-based arrangements naturally align financial incentives with AI's ability to improve outcomes and reduce costs. The primary tensions center not on whether to pursue value-based AI integration, but on implementation specifics: how to measure AI's contribution to value, how to address the capital-versus-savings mismatch, and how to ensure smaller and safety-net providers can participate equitably.

consensusPoints.text: Value-based payment is the most important policy lever for AI adoption. Nearly all commenters explicitly endorse value-based payment models as essential for aligning AI incentives with improved outcomes. This consensus spans AI developers, health systems, professional associations, and academic researchers. Massive Bio states that value-based payment is the most important policy lever for accelerating AI adoption. Interstella notes that under APMs like accountable care or Medicare Advantage, the business case for AI is stronger. AAFP argues that value-based payment will drive the market to adopt AI solutions that drive quality care and cost-effectiveness. This position is shared by specialty societies (AUA, HFSA), trade associations (RBMA, AAPA, EHRA), health systems (WellSpan), behavioral health providers (SonderMind, InStride Health), and AI developers across clinical and administrative domains.

Current fee-for-service models create perverse incentives that penalize AI efficiency. Commenters identify the fundamental misalignment between volume-based payment and AI's value proposition. Multiple commenters note that AI's deflationary benefits—reducing unnecessary services, preventing hospitalizations, improving efficiency—are financially punished under FFS. RBMA warns that without payment reform, AI risks becoming a victim of its own success. TapestryHealth argues that payment models must reward Capacity (patients managed safely) rather than Minutes (time spent documenting). SonderMind notes that current reimbursement models economically penalize their AI-enabled continuous care approach.

CMMI demonstration projects should be a primary vehicle for testing AI in value-based models. Most commenters recommending specific policy mechanisms identify CMMI as the appropriate venue for piloting AI integration into value-based care. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association recommends launching CMMI demonstrations rewarding outcomes-validated AI use-cases with equity adjustments for rural/safety-net providers. SonderMind suggests CMS should consider creating CMMI demonstration models specifically for behavioral health AI. ICF recommends hardwiring an AI capability track into high-leverage CMMI models.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters recommending specific policy mechanisms (18+ comments)

consensusPoints.text: AI costs should be allowable within value-based payment structures. Commenters addressing cost allocation agree that AI licensing, validation, monitoring, and infrastructure costs should be recognized as legitimate practice expenses within APMs. Health AI Institute recommends allowing AI costs within value-based models. AUA argues that AI licensing, validation, updates, and monitoring should be treated as allowable practice expense inputs when tied to clinical services. SANCIAN LLC proposes enabling value-based AI add-ons through payment adjustments under MSSP, ACO REACH, and bundled payments when AI contributes to outcomes.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing cost allocation (12+ comments)

areasOfDebate.topic: How to Measure AI's Contribution to Value
areasOfDebate.description: Debate centers on whether AI reimbursement should be tied directly to measurable clinical outcomes or recognized as a quality improvement activity regardless of attributable outcomes.

positions.label: Outcome-Linked Metrics
positions.stance: Tie AI reimbursement directly to measurable clinical outcomes. Dr. Bethany Russell proposes detailed Outcome-Linked Payment Tiers with specific metrics. HFSA advocates payment approaches that reward demonstrated clinical utility.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of clinical providers and specialty societies (approximately 60% of commenters)
keyArguments: Creates accountability
Generates evidence base
Aligns with VBC principles
Prevents adoption of ineffective tools

positions.label: Process-Based Recognition
positions.stance: Recognize AI deployment as quality improvement activity regardless of attributable outcomes. Massive Bio suggests allowing Medicare Advantage plans to count investments in AI tools as quality improvement activities in their Medical Loss Ratio calculations. AUA proposes an improvement activity category for responsible AI deployment.
positions.supportLevel: Several AI developers and health plans (approximately 25% of commenters)
keyArguments: Attribution is technically difficult
Savings are often delayed or indirect
Process measures enable faster adoption

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether value-based frameworks should prioritize clinical AI that directly improves patient outcomes or also include administrative efficiency gains.

positions.label: Clinical AI Focus
positions.stance: Prioritize AI that directly improves clinical outcomes. VieCure focuses on clinical and operational value of validated, clinician-in-the-loop AI. NeuroFlow emphasizes early detection and measurement-informed care.
positions.supportLevel: Most clinical providers and specialty societies
keyArguments: Clinical outcomes are measurable
Patient safety is paramount
Clinical AI has clearest value proposition

positions.label: Administrative AI Inclusion
positions.stance: Include administrative efficiency gains in value-based frameworks. Penguin Ai proposes a Shared Savings for Administrative Efficiency model. MHK advocates for AI-enabled reductions in administrative burden.
positions.supportLevel: AI developers focused on RCM and operations (approximately 30% of commenters)
keyArguments: Administrative burden is massive cost driver
Efficiency gains free resources for care
Administrative AI has faster ROI

areasOfDebate.topic: Speed of Transition to Value-Based Models
areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether to accelerate aggressively toward value-based care or ensure adequate infrastructure and evidence before broad expansion.

positions.label: Accelerate Aggressively
positions.stance: Rapid transition to VBC is essential for AI adoption. New Jersey Innovation Institute argues that slow transition to value-based care models limits financial incentives for providers to adopt AI. AAFP calls to accelerate transition to meaningful VBP models.
positions.supportLevel: Most AI developers and innovation-focused organizations
keyArguments: Current pace is too slow
Market signals are needed now
Evidence generation requires scale

positions.label: Measured Transition
positions.stance: Ensure adequate infrastructure and evidence before broad expansion. Health AI Institute recommends time-limited reimbursement pathways to evaluate real-world performance, equity, and cost impacts prior to permanent coverage decisions.
positions.supportLevel: Some health systems and provider associations (minority view)
keyArguments: Premature transition could harm vulnerable providers
Evidence base is still developing
Implementation challenges are real

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lack of clear reimbursement pathways; inability to demonstrate ROI to health system customers; attribution challenges for AI-driven savings. They experience the chicken-and-egg problem where health systems won't invest without reimbursement clarity, but evidence can't be generated without deployment.
specificPoints: Ellipsis Health notes that even in value-based arrangements, savings are frequently delayed, indirect, or difficult to attribute, making it challenging for health systems to justify near-term investment
Medvidi observes that when efficiency translates directly to financial benefit, adoption surges
Proposed solutions include CMMI pilots, shared savings models, MA quality activity recognition, and outcome-linked payment tiers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Capital-versus-operational cost mismatch; need to absorb AI's deflationary benefits; maintaining financial viability during transition. They have direct operational experience with the tension between AI efficiency gains and volume-based revenue.
specificPoints: TapestryHealth identifies the Capital vs. Operational mismatch: AI requires upfront Capital Expense (sensors), but savings (reduced hospitalizations) accrue to Payer (Medicare), not Facility
WellSpan recommends starting AI incentive programs where value-based models already track quality (mortality, readmissions, HAIs)
Proposed solutions include shared savings provisions, bundled payments, and starting incentives where quality metrics already exist

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Member financial viability; ensuring AI doesn't undermine practice economics; appropriate recognition of clinician contributions. They represent diverse membership including small practices and safety-net providers who may lack resources for AI adoption.
specificPoints: RBMA warns that aligning reimbursement with quality, accountability, and long-term value is essential to realizing AI's potential without undermining the economic foundations of medical practice
AAPA emphasizes that value-based, population-based, and shared-savings payment models should be prioritized
Proposed solutions include broader VBC adoption, bundled payments, MIPS integration, and allowable practice expense recognition

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: VBC adoption in behavioral health lags physical medicine; unique measurement challenges; crisis prevention value not captured. They operate in a specialty where outcomes are harder to measure and value-based penetration is significantly lower.
specificPoints: SonderMind notes that VBC adoption in behavioral health remains significantly lower than in physical medicine, leaving innovators without a viable commercial pathway
NeuroFlow argues that evidence increasingly suggests AI-enabled behavioral health integration produces the strongest financial signal in risk-bearing or accountable care environments
Proposed solutions include behavioral health-specific CMMI demonstrations, incentive payments for acute episode reduction, and MA STARS weighting toward behavioral outcomes

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Implementation complexity; need for clear regulatory pathways; infrastructure requirements. They have a cross-cutting view of barriers across multiple health systems and payers.
specificPoints: ICF provides detailed recommendations for integrating AI into LEAD, Kidney Care Choices, EOM, and TEAM models
Cyberhill Partners recommends shared savings provisions for AI-driven efficiency gains under Medicare Shared Savings Program
Proposed solutions include specific CMMI model modifications, certification requirements tied to APMs, and data integrity scoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: State-level variation; managed care contract structures; FMAP incentives; social determinants integration. They serve highest-need populations where AI-driven care coordination may have greatest impact but faces unique payment barriers.
specificPoints: Waymark recommends CMS should issue guidance under 42 CFR Part 438 on performance-based payments for AI-driven population health management
Waymark suggests enhanced federal matching (increased FMAP percentages) for states that adopt value-based contract structures
Proposed solutions include CMS guidance on performance-based payments under 42 CFR Part 438, enhanced FMAP for VBC adoption, and risk adjustment methodology updates

noteworthyInsights.insight: The deflationary paradox of AI under FFS: RBMA crystallizes a fundamental tension that without payment reform, AI risks becoming a victim of its own success. This insight—that AI's efficiency gains are financially punished under volume-based payment—appears across multiple comments and represents a core policy challenge.

Capacity vs. Minutes framework: TapestryHealth offers a compelling reframe that payment should reward Capacity (patients managed safely) rather than Minutes (time spent documenting). This conceptual shift could inform broader payment reform discussions.

Behavioral health as leading indicator: NeuroFlow argues that AI-enabled behavioral health integration produces the strongest financial signal in risk-bearing or accountable care environments, suggesting behavioral health could serve as a proving ground for AI-VBC integration.

Detailed outcome metrics for mental health AI: Dr. Bethany Russell provides unusually specific measurement framework including symptom reduction on validated instruments, functional improvement in work/social functioning/quality of life, crisis prevention (reduction in ED visits, hospitalizations, suicide attempts), treatment engagement (retention rates, session attendance), and recovery maintenance at 6 and 12-month follow-up.

Existing model integration opportunities: ICF provides detailed, model-specific recommendations for integrating AI into LEAD, Kidney Care Choices, EOM, and TEAM, demonstrating that AI capability tracks could be hardwired into existing CMMI infrastructure rather than requiring entirely new programs.

Data infrastructure as VBC prerequisite: Leap Orbit uniquely emphasizes that trusted data foundation impacts payers, providers, and patients, proposing Data Integrity Scores in VBC benchmarks—highlighting that AI-enabled VBC requires foundational data quality investments.

Medicaid-specific FMAP lever: Waymark identifies enhanced federal matching as an underutilized tool, noting that enhanced federal matching (increased FMAP percentages) for states that adopt value-based contract structures could accelerate this transition.

Real-world efficiency evidence: Leap Orbit cites concrete savings where one national payer saved estimated $240,000 per year automating CMS 4I reporting, reducing a four-week manual process to under 48 hours.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Care Setting Variations: Post-acute/long-term care faces unique capital-savings mismatch where facilities bear sensor costs but Medicare captures hospitalization savings (TapestryHealth). Behavioral health providers (SonderMind, NeuroFlow, InStride Health) identify systematic underinvestment due to lower VBC penetration and measurement challenges. Oncology has existing models (EOM) as frameworks for AI integration (Massive Bio, VieCure).
