specificPoints: Operate at the intersection of AI development and clinical deployment; understand both technical capabilities and healthcare workflow integration
Support inventory requirements that create market clarity
Massive Bio, serving 160,000+ cancer patients, emphasizes that organizations need visibility into what AI is deployed

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Sustainable adoption pathways; protecting patients while enabling innovation
specificPoints: Represents 40,000+ physicians who are primary end-users of clinical AI; has invested in AI governance infrastructure including Define-AI, ARCH-AI, and Assess-AI programs
Recommends algorithm inventories as low-burden, auditable building blocks
American College of Radiology's Data Science Institute experience informs their recommendation for practical, scalable approaches

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Ensuring all AI tools affecting patient care are tracked, including non-device tools like LLMs
specificPoints: Focuses on safety implications rather than technical or business considerations; emphasizes regulatory enforcement mechanisms
ECRI recommends healthcare organizations should maintain an AI inventory including non-device tools like LLMs as a condition of CMS program participation

noteworthyInsights.insight: Federal agencies struggle with the same problem: GAO found two included use cases later determined not to be AI at all in federal inventories—suggesting the definitional challenge of what constitutes AI compounds the tracking problem even for sophisticated organizations.

Market leader visibility paradox: Humana implemented one of most structurally forward-looking decisions by embedding Plan IDs directly into plan names—yet aggregators still dominate visibility because structured machine-readable surfaces weren't published. This illustrates how good internal practices fail without external discoverability.

The 9% cliff for small organizations: The dramatic drop in AI monitoring capability from 48% to 9% for smaller organizations suggests any inventory mandate must include scaled implementation support or risk creating a two-tier system where patients at smaller facilities face unmonitored AI.

AIBOM as vulnerability response enabler: The cybersecurity expert's recommendation for AI Bill of Materials draws an implicit parallel to software supply chain security practices, suggesting healthcare could adapt existing frameworks from other sectors.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Clear evidence of capability stratification by organization size: monitoring drops from 48% to 9% for smaller organizations. This suggests any regulatory approach must account for resource disparities or risk widening the safety gap.

Technical vs. Governance Framing divergence: Cybersecurity professionals frame inventory as a technical discovery problem requiring automated solutions. Governance researchers frame it as an accountability architecture problem requiring certification structures. Professional associations frame it as a practical adoption problem requiring low-burden approaches. These framings suggest different but potentially complementary intervention points.

Static inventory requirements may create false compliance—organizations check boxes without achieving actual visibility. Overly burdensome requirements may drive AI use underground or to unregulated tools. Focus on device-classified AI may miss significant risks from non-device tools like LLMs.

Cross-sector learning opportunities: Multiple commenters reference frameworks from other domains including software supply chain (AIBOM), data architecture (Plan-ID), and federal IT governance (GAO audits). This suggests healthcare AI inventory could benefit from adapting proven approaches rather than building from scratch.

keyQuotations.quote: Only 22% of hospital leaders report high confidence they could produce a complete AI audit trail within 30 days if requested by a regulator
GAO audit found 15 of 20 federal agency AI inventories contained incomplete or inaccurate data; two included use cases later determined not to be AI at all
AI software 'lives' in ephemeral containers, cloud environments, and embedded firmware - static inventories cannot keep pace
Traditional periodic scanning is insufficient for AI assets
75% of organizations have adopted AI usage policies, but only 36% have implemented formal governance frameworks
Only 48% actively monitor production AI systems for accuracy, drift, or misuse — drops to 9% among smaller organizations
Just 10% of organizations use automated monitoring to detect AI capabilities within existing software
Covered Entities require automated, AI-driven inventory system that discovers AI models and their dependencies in real-time
Encourage adoption of algorithm inventories as low-burden, auditable building blocks
Healthcare organizations should maintain an AI inventory including non-device tools like LLMs as a condition of CMS program participation

HHS should develop a Clinical AI Accountability Registry - federal-level enumeration of clinical AI use cases, accountability architecture each deploying institution has certified, and measurement evidence supporting that certification
70–90% of Plan-ID queries surface aggregator pages, not carrier pages
The 100% inventory requirement should include specific versioning of AI models and their data training sources, not just physical servers

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific statistics, documented audit findings, and concrete implementation proposals. Technical and governance perspectives are well-articulated with clear reasoning.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite quantitative data including GAO audit findings, industry survey statistics on monitoring rates, and specific percentages on governance implementation gaps. Evidence spans federal audits, industry surveys, and operational experience.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from smaller healthcare organizations who face the most significant capability gaps (9% monitoring rate). Patient advocacy groups and frontline clinical staff perspectives are underrepresented relative to technical and governance experts.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The debate involves multiple intersecting dimensions including technical architecture (ephemeral vs. static systems), regulatory approach (mandatory vs. voluntary), scope definition (technical detail vs. accountability focus), and implementation feasibility across organization sizes.

8.3.themeDescription: Vendor Evaluation and Procurement Processes. This sub-theme addresses how organizations should evaluate and procure AI tools, including vendor assessment, contracting, and due diligence. || It includes concerns about inadequate vendor evaluation processes, proposals for standardized evaluation frameworks, debates about appropriate due diligence requirements, and reports of lengthy procurement cycles. This excludes governance structures and focuses on acquisition processes. The key distinction is focus on selecting AI tools rather than governing their use

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types that current AI procurement processes are fundamentally broken, with 12-18 month timelines cited repeatedly as a critical barrier to beneficial AI adoption. The central tension lies between the need for thorough due diligence (security, clinical validation, liability protection) and the urgent desire to accelerate adoption through standardization. Commenters overwhelmingly support federal action to create standardized evaluation frameworks, model contract language, and "assess once, reuse many times" approaches that would reduce duplicative reviews while maintaining safety standards.

consensusPoints.text: Procurement timelines are unacceptably long and impede beneficial AI adoption. Nearly all commenters explicitly cite procurement delays as a critical barrier, with timelines consistently reported at 6-18 months. Onboard AI reports 12+ month AI Committee review cycles being added onto existing, onerous AI Procurement processes. ScriptChain Health describes 12-18 months before partnerships can start. SANCIAN LLC notes Procurement and IT security cycles add 6-18 months to adoption timelines. Cyberhill Partners confirms healthcare purchasing cycles average 12-18 months.

exceptions.text: No commenter argues that current timelines are appropriate or necessary for safety.

consensusPoints.text: Duplicative reviews across organizations waste resources without improving safety. Cooperative Exchange warns of 'death by a thousand audits' currently stifling innovation. Ellipsis Health calls to discourage duplicative reviews that add cost without improving safety. NeuroFlow observes organizations conduct bespoke reviews independently, extending procurement timelines significantly.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (at least 10)

consensusPoints.text: Standardized evaluation frameworks and model contract language are needed. Massive Bio recommends HHS could develop model contract language or best practices for AI procurement. EHY Consulting calls for model contract language and procurement checklists. ECRI recommends standard procurement and contracting language with model contract clauses. ScriptChain Health proposes standard contract agreement for health systems, plans, and vendors.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (at least 12 explicitly recommending federal action)

consensusPoints.text: Security and compliance reviews are major bottlenecks separate from clinical evaluation. NeuroFlow notes a tool that passes clinical review may sit in IT security review for months. Cyberhill Partners cites security and compliance review bottlenecks. SANCIAN LLC identifies legal review delays from lack of AI expertise.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI vendor certification should be mandatory or voluntary/recognition-based

positions.label: Mandatory Certification
positions.stance: Require formal accreditation or certification of AI developers/products. AORN, Dr. Emilie Maxie, and AAFP support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Several professional associations and some providers
keyArguments: Incentivizes evidence-informed and ethically grounded practices
Helps clinicians understand what has been reviewed vs. not
Enhances trust and adoption by establishing industry standards

positions.label: Voluntary Recognition
positions.stance: Encourage mutual recognition of private accreditation without mandates. Ellipsis Health and ICF support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Most AI vendors and consulting firms
keyArguments: Maintains flexibility and innovation pace
Allows market-driven quality signals
Reduces regulatory burden while still providing assurance

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether evaluation should be federally centralized or distributed with portability

positions.label: Federal Centralization
positions.stance: HHS should establish centralized evaluation infrastructure modeled on FedRAMP. ICF, Cooperative Exchange, and The Interstitial Institute support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Several large organizations and consulting firms (at least 5 commenters)
keyArguments: "Assess once, reuse many times" eliminates redundancy
Creates consistent standards across the ecosystem
Reduces burden on individual organizations

positions.label: Distributed Portability
positions.stance: Allow evaluations to be portable across organizations without federal centralization. Ellipsis Health and NeuroFlow support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Several vendors and health systems
keyArguments: Preserves organizational autonomy
Faster to implement than new federal infrastructure
Leverages existing private-sector expertise

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over the extent of disclosure requirements for AI vendors

positions.label: Comprehensive Disclosure
positions.stance: Require extensive technical and performance transparency. WellSpan Health, AMA, and ECRI support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Most health systems, professional associations, and safety organizations
keyArguments: Enables informed purchasing decisions
Addresses power imbalances between vendors and buyers
Essential for ongoing monitoring and accountability

positions.label: Balanced Disclosure
positions.stance: Transparency should be meaningful but not burdensome. HIMSS EHRA notes customers sometimes require safeguards, suggesting variability is appropriate.
positions.supportLevel: Some vendors express implicit concern about over-regulation
keyArguments: Excessive requirements could slow innovation
Proprietary information protection matters
Focus should be on actionable information

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Lengthy procurement cycles (12-18 months) blocking market access, duplicative reviews across health systems, and lack of clear ROI metrics to support purchasing decisions. They experience the procurement process from the selling side and see how governance uncertainty creates unpredictable timelines even when clinical value is demonstrated.
specificPoints: ScriptChain Health proposes HHS maintain a preferred list of health plans and systems willing to adopt AI products
Atropos Health calls for standardized governance and contracting frameworks
Proposed solutions include model contract language, preferred vendor lists, accelerated pathways, and portability of evaluations

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Vendor opacity and black-box contracting practices, resource burden of conducting independent evaluations, and power imbalances with large AI vendors. They must balance clinical enthusiasm against IT security, compliance, and financial constraints while navigating multi-stakeholder internal approval processes.
specificPoints: WellSpan Health advocates for shared-risk contracting models aligning vendor accountability with claimed performance
Providence describes developing a standardized risk stratification process to examine and improve AI tools before clinical integration
Proposed solutions include standardized BAA requirements, shared-risk contracting models, and third-party accreditation to reduce internal evaluation burden

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Members in small/independent practices lack resources for sophisticated evaluation, need for transparency that enables comparison across products, and ensuring primary care and specialty perspectives inform standards. They represent diverse membership including both large systems and small practices and can serve as intermediaries for evaluation and standard-setting.
specificPoints: AMA emphasizes need for standardized format for disclosures across industry participants especially for physicians in small practices or underserved areas who lack evaluation resources
ACR suggests a role for professional/trade associations to help inform administrative decision-making

Proposed solutions include standardized disclosure formats, role for professional associations in administrative decision-making, and certification requirements including usability and data governance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Governance uncertainty creating procurement bottlenecks, contracts lacking essential provisions (auditability, update controls, data boundaries), and need for organizational readiness assessment before procurement. They see patterns across multiple client organizations and understand both technical and organizational dimensions of procurement challenges.
specificPoints: EHY Consulting warns of contracts that do not specify auditability, model-update controls, data-use boundaries, or subcontractor constraints

Satyadhar Joshi recommends AI ReadyCheck assessment evaluating organizational readiness baseline including data governance, workflow optimization, change management capacity, and leadership alignment before technology procurement
Proposed solutions include governance data standards, procurement checklists with security-by-design requirements, and organizational readiness assessments before technology procurement

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Compounding regulatory exposure from fragmented state requirements and disproportionate audit burden as intermediaries. They operate at the intersection of all healthcare ecosystem stakeholders and face multiplicative compliance requirements.
specificPoints: Cooperative Exchange proposes that if clearinghouse AI infrastructure is certified by NIST-aligned federal body, that certification should satisfy all state and payer-specific audit requirements
Proposed solutions include NIST-aligned reciprocity framework, audit once rely many times model, and credentialing standards for AI assessors

noteworthyInsights.insight: Governance before tooling: Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi argues decision clarity should come before tooling — organizations should establish governance infrastructure before evaluating specific products, reversing the typical sequence where tool selection drives governance development.

IT controls adoption pace more than clinical enthusiasm: NeuroFlow observes that Chief Information Officers, information security teams, and compliance leaders often determine the pace of adoption more than clinical enthusiasm does — highlighting that clinical validation is necessary but not sufficient for adoption.

Vendor-controlled evaluation environments limit comparison: BlueHalo notes AI tools are often evaluated within vendor-controlled or site-specific environments - this limits independent assessment and comparison — identifying a structural barrier to informed purchasing that standardization alone cannot address.

Y Combinator model for healthcare needs: ScriptChain Health proposes that Health Plans and Health Systems should publish top 10 needs of specific hospitals for advanced technology, similar to Y Combinator's approach — an innovative demand-signaling mechanism to align vendor development with actual needs.

Four Tests Standard for governance claims: FERZ AI proposes specific technical tests (Stop Test, Ownership Test, Replay Test, Escalation Test) to evaluate vendor governance claims, noting AI governance market includes many solutions marketed with terms lacking precise technical definitions.

Honest AI labeling saves evaluation resources: Providence notes that when software is described as powered by AI, it triggers internal review — emphasizing the importance of honest labeling so resources are not spent analyzing tools that don't require robust review.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Organizational Size Correlations: Large health systems like Providence and WellSpan have developed internal evaluation processes but still find them burdensome. Small practices and independent physicians lack resources for any meaningful evaluation as emphasized by AMA, AAFP, and ADA. This creates a two-tier system where AI adoption concentrates in well-resourced organizations.

Stakeholder Role Patterns: Vendors emphasize timeline delays and market access barriers. Providers emphasize vendor opacity and power imbalances. Professional associations emphasize member resource constraints and need for standardization. Consultants emphasize governance architecture gaps.

Sequential Bottleneck Pattern: Multiple commenters identify a sequential review process where tools must pass through multiple independent gates: clinical/medical review, IT security review, legal/compliance review, and financial/ROI review. Each gate operates independently, and delays compound. NeuroFlow explicitly notes a tool that passes clinical review may sit in IT security review for months.

Unintended Consequences Identified: BlueHalo warns that fragmentation reinforces vendor lock-in as organizations cannot easily compare alternatives. Cooperative Exchange warns current audit burden is stifling innovation. Multiple commenters note that procurement complexity disproportionately affects smaller and under-resourced organizations.

Geographic/Jurisdictional Patterns: Cooperative Exchange emphasizes that clearinghouses face compounding regulatory and liability exposure from fragmented state AI requirements. Providence operating across 7 states notes challenges navigating inconsistent state AI regulations. This suggests multi-state organizations face multiplicative compliance burdens.

keyQuotations.quote: AI developers are increasingly stagnated by 12+ month AI Committee review cycles being added onto existing, onerous AI Procurement processes.

If a clearinghouse's AI infrastructure is certified by a NIST-aligned federal body, that certification should satisfy all state and payer-specific audit requirements, eliminating the 'death by a thousand audits' currently stifling innovation.

AI vendors capable of delivering genuine clinical value cannot cross the procurement threshold because institutional buyers lack governance architecture to accept accountability for what the system does.
Independent physician practices often lack bargaining leverage to negotiate robust contractual protections with developers and third parties.
Vendors use opaque contracting practices and 'black-box' data silos.

Chief Information Officers, information security teams, and compliance leaders control integration, data access, cybersecurity review, and regulatory risk assessment — often determine the pace of adoption more than clinical enthusiasm does.
AI tools are often evaluated within vendor-controlled or site-specific environments — this limits independent assessment and comparison.
Procurement realities: contracts that do not specify auditability, model-update controls, data-use boundaries, or subcontractor constraints.
Without third-party accreditation, health care providers must invest their own time and funds to determine whether each AI tool is safe.
Dental practices increasingly rely on third-party vendors for critical systems but face gaps in federal regulatory space to assist providers making complex technology decisions.

Reducing administrative friction by creating a systematic approach to portability of evaluation and accreditation programs assists organizations in making decisions supported by repeatable data using consistent review standards.
Per patient/use pricing significantly impacts financial considerations in procurement.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate sophisticated understanding of procurement challenges with specific evidence, concrete timelines, and constructive recommendations. Stakeholders across types engage substantively with the issues rather than offering generic positions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Multiple commenters cite specific timelines (12-18 months), concrete examples of procurement bottlenecks, and real-world experiences. Professional associations and health systems provide institutional perspectives grounded in member/organizational experience.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Limited representation from payers/insurers on procurement perspectives. Small/rural provider voices are represented primarily through professional associations rather than direct comment. Patient/consumer perspectives on procurement implications are absent.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: The comments reveal a multi-layered procurement ecosystem with sequential bottlenecks (clinical, IT, legal, financial), jurisdictional fragmentation (state requirements), and stakeholder power imbalances. Solutions require addressing structural issues rather than simple process improvements.

8.4.themeDescription: Change Management and Clinical Workflow Integration

sections.executiveSummary: Public commenters demonstrate near-universal consensus that workflow integration and change management are critical determinants of AI success in healthcare—often more important than technical performance. The dominant concern across all stakeholder types is that even well-designed AI tools fail when they add cognitive burden, disrupt established clinical routines, or exist outside existing EHR workflows. Commenters strongly recommend human-centered design approaches, clinician involvement in implementation, and federal support for organizational readiness, though debate exists about whether to prioritize standardized frameworks versus flexible, context-specific approaches.

consensusPoints.text: Workflow fit determines AI success more than technical performance. AI tools fail in practice when poorly integrated into clinical workflows, regardless of their analytical capabilities. EHY Consulting notes that workflow misfit is the dominant barrier—AI systems that perform well in development can fail in practice when they add documentation burden, disrupt clinician cognitive flow, or create ambiguous responsibility at the point of care. ICF Incorporated emphasizes that even a highly accurate algorithm will fail in practice if it is difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines. Penn Neurosurgery observes that in real-world clinical environments, if an AI tool does not function as a plug-and-play extension of existing workflows, adoption is unlikely regardless of its theoretical benefits.

exceptions.text: No commenters argued that technical performance alone is sufficient; the consensus is remarkably uniform across business, academic, provider, and trade association perspectives.

consensusPoints.text: AI tools outside EHR workflows face adoption failure. EHR integration is a critical success factor, with tools requiring separate portals or manual workarounds consistently failing. BlueHalo notes that when AI outputs exist outside of the EHR environment and require clinicians to leave established workflows to access or interpret them, it creates resistance. Ellipsis Health observes that AI solutions operating outside existing clinical workflows and requiring duplicate systems or manual workarounds frequently fail to scale. AMA emphasizes the biggest upside comes from making it easier for physicians to use AI as a native part of clinical care, embedded within EHR workflows, rather than forcing physicians to step outside the EHR into separate tools.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: A strong majority of commenters (18+ of 30)

consensusPoints.text: Change management is systematically underinvested. Inadequate organizational investment in change management is a root cause of implementation failures. SANCIAN LLC notes that organizations underestimate transformation required, leading to failed implementations that erode confidence. Ellipsis Health observes that organizations under workforce strain may struggle to allocate resources to change management efforts. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association identifies limited operational bandwidth for safe rollout including training, change management, and post-implementation monitoring.

Clinician involvement in design and implementation is essential. AAFP notes that the absence of meaningful clinician input contributes to technologies poorly aligned with clinical workflows and real-world needs, undermining adoption, effectiveness, and physician buy-in. SANCIAN LLC recommends workflow co-design with clinician input. ICF Incorporated emphasizes that involving clinicians in design decisions builds trust and accelerates adoption.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing implementation processes (20+ of 30)

areasOfDebate.description: Debate exists between developing universal standardized frameworks versus prioritizing local adaptation and human-centered design approaches.

positions.label: Standardized Frameworks
positions.stance: Develop universal toolkits, templates, and assessment protocols. SANCIAN LLC proposes AI ReadyCheck assessment. Health AI Institute recommends Organizational AI Readiness Grants. ACR suggests workflow documentation and training as low-burden, auditable building blocks.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 40% of commenters, particularly consultants and think tanks
keyArguments: Provides consistent baseline for evaluation across organizations
Reduces duplication of effort; smaller organizations lack capacity to develop their own
Enables benchmarking and comparison across implementations

positions.label: Context-Specific Approaches
positions.stance: Prioritize local adaptation and human-centered design. ICF emphasizes direct observation of clinicians using AI tools and one-on-one interviews to uncover tacit knowledge and context-specific decision cues. Scientix.ai warns that AI implementations built on official workflow fail in practice because they are trained on a fiction.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 35% of commenters, particularly providers and implementation consultants
keyArguments: One-size-fits-all ignores specialty-specific and setting-specific needs
Actual workflows differ from documented workflows; local mapping essential
Human-centered design requires direct observation and iterative refinement

areasOfDebate.description: Tension between allowing voluntary early adoption with iterative refinement versus emphasizing thorough validation before expansion.

positions.label: Phased/Opt-In Models
positions.stance: Allow voluntary early adoption with iterative refinement. Penn Neurosurgery advocates allowing clinicians to opt in voluntarily to facilitate innovation without system-wide disruption. Interstella notes their pilot approach allowed safe failure and iteration.
positions.supportLevel: Several academic and provider commenters
keyArguments: Enables faster real-world learning without system-wide disruption
Pilot approaches allow safe failure and iteration
Builds evidence base before broader rollout

positions.label: Cautious Scaling
positions.stance: Emphasize thorough validation before expansion. MHA warns of risk that immature deployments erode clinician trust. Scientix.ai criticizes ever-growing portfolios of successful pilots that never scale.
positions.supportLevel: Several trade associations and health systems
keyArguments: Immature deployments erode clinician trust
Perpetual pilot programs waste resources without achieving scale
Need explicit safety metrics before broader deployment

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Integration complexity with legacy EHR systems, administrative hurdles (procurement, IT support) slowing deployments, and need for clear pathways to demonstrate value. Vendors emphasize that their tools succeed when tightly scoped and integrated but fail when workflow integration is immature; they see themselves as partners in change management, not just technology suppliers.
specificPoints: Research on best practices from successful and failed implementations
Workflow transformation guidance for transitioning to agentic systems
Incentives for organizations to map actual operational workflows before procurement
Massive Bio's Reticulum Nexus platform reduced hundreds of hours of manual effort to auditable, human-in-the-loop AI workflow
Medvidi's ambient documentation provides incremental updates every minute reducing editing time from 10-15 minutes to under 2 minutes

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Clinician burden and cognitive load from poorly designed tools, fragmented workflows and duplicative documentation, and absence of clinician input in tool design. They represent frontline users who experience implementation failures daily and emphasize that members frequently describe shortcomings in digital tools that fail to integrate with existing workflows.
specificPoints: Advisory panels including primary care physicians
AI strategy tied to clinical priorities
Workflow documentation and training as auditable building blocks
AUA reports AI tools met or exceeded expectations when narrowly scoped, integrated into workflow, and evaluated in real-world settings in urology
AASM describes fragmented workflows, duplicative documentation, and increased administrative burden from poorly integrated tools

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Limited operational bandwidth for safe rollout, training requirements and workflow disruption during implementation, and risk of eroding clinician trust through premature deployment. They have direct operational experience with both successes and failures and can identify specific failure modes such as WellSpan's example of an automated agent confirming an appointment that was never scheduled.
specificPoints: Leadership buy-in tied to clear benefits for frontline staff
Bounded deployments with explicit accountability architecture
Human-in-the-loop, clinically governed deployment models
InStride Health's Wharton case study documented improvements in clinician experience through a human-in-the-loop, clinically governed AI deployment model

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Sociotechnical conditions determining success beyond tool availability, lack of infrastructure for iterative deployment models, and need for implementation research examining organizational characteristics. They focus on systematic analysis and evidence generation, emphasizing that success depends on sociotechnical conditions, workflow integration, and sustained operational capacity rather than tool availability alone.
specificPoints: Implementation toolkits (workflow mapping, minimum training, governance templates)
Demonstration projects with explicit safety metrics
Phased, opt-in deployment models with faster feedback cycles
Penn Neurosurgery describes Dragon Copilot beta release as constructive shift toward iterative refinement but notes broader opt-in participation would allow more rapid real-world learning

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Handoff to end users as most common failure point, cultural transformation and workflow ownership, and leaders lacking digital fluency investing in disconnected pilots. They have a cross-organizational view of implementation patterns and can identify systemic issues across multiple deployments.
specificPoints: Deliberate workflow design, education, and change management
AI ReadyCheck assessments before technology procurement
Mapping actual operational workflows including informal workarounds
Lumenex Advisory describes experience where downstream teams resisted adoption because new workflows were viewed as extra steps—the long-term value was not immediately visible to end users

noteworthyInsights.insight: The flywheel effect of incremental success - ICF Incorporated proposes an incremental, evolutionary approach to create successes and build confidence—creates flywheel effect building momentum, suggesting that small wins can compound into broader organizational transformation.

AI as capacity infrastructure - Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi introduces Sancian LLC's AI SoftLife philosophy: AI as capacity infrastructure that reduces cognitive burden, protects clinicians' energy, sustains humane care delivery—measures success through cognitive load reduction, time returned to patient care, and clinician trust. This reframes AI success metrics away from technical accuracy toward human impact.

The fiction of official workflows - Scientix.ai offers a striking insight that AI implementations built on official workflow fail in practice because trained on a fiction, highlighting the gap between documented processes and actual clinical practice that undermines many implementations.

Leadership buy-in through frontline benefit - Interstella describes how a chief nursing officer championed it after seeing it could reduce staff stress, demonstrating that leadership adoption follows when benefits to frontline workers are visible and concrete.

Governance architecture alignment - The Interstitial Institute provides a sophisticated framework: successful AI deployments feature AI system's scope is bounded, named clinical party is responsible for outputs, defined measurement specification with threshold, and consequence mechanism built into workflow rather than appended to it.

Real-time documentation as workflow enabler - Medvidi describes how their ambient documentation provides incremental updates every minute so practitioners don't wait until end of visit, reducing editing time from 10-15 minutes to under 2 minutes—a concrete example of workflow-native design.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Large academic medical centers such as Penn and InStride Health describe more sophisticated pilot programs and iterative deployment models.
Regional health systems such as WellSpan and MHA members emphasize operational bandwidth constraints and change management challenges.
Safety-net and rural providers mentioned as disproportionately affected by resource constraints but underrepresented in direct comments.
Radiology emerges as a relative success story, with multiple commenters including AUA, WellSpan, and ACR citing imaging triage and cancer detection as examples of well-integrated AI.
Mental/behavioral health providers such as InStride Health, Medvidi, and NeuroFlow emphasize human-in-the-loop models and bounded use cases.
Primary care representatives like AAFP emphasize the unique challenges of small, independent practices with limited IT resources.
Trust erosion cascade: Failed implementations don't just fail—they create lasting resistance to future AI adoption.
Pilot proliferation: Well-intentioned pilot programs can become perpetual without scaling, consuming resources without delivering value.
Shadow workflows: AI tools that don't integrate into EHRs may create parallel documentation systems that fragment care records.

keyQuotations.quote: In real-world clinical environments, if an AI tool does not function as a plug-and-play extension of existing workflows that creates more opportunity for the clinician to directly engage with the patient, adoption is unlikely, regardless of its theoretical benefits.

If AI tools and solutions are intuitive, reduce human effort, and demonstrably enhance care delivery, providers are far more likely to embrace them. Conversely, even a highly accurate algorithm will fail in practice if it is difficult to use or disrupts proven clinical routines.
Integration and change management are as critical as model performance.
Without deliberate workflow design, education, and change management, AI tools risk being perceived as additive burden rather than enabling infrastructure.
AI that adds cognitive burden rather than reducing it creates friction regardless of technical performance. Exhausted care teams cannot absorb poorly designed tools.
Organizations underestimate transformation required, leading to failed implementations that erode confidence.
AI implementations built on official workflow fail in practice because trained on a fiction.

Radiology AI system deployed to flag potential findings for radiologist review with defined escalation thresholds and explicit tracking of alert-to-review intervals succeeds because governance architecture and clinical workflow are aligned—AI narrows the decision space; human retains authority over the decision.

Administration can accelerate transformation by aligning federal policy levers around three goals: make AI integrate into routine workflows (not separate portals or 'shadow processes' that add clicks), enable competition on performance and burden reduction, protect trust with baseline privacy, security, and transparency expectations proportionate to risk.
Absence of meaningful clinician input contributes to technologies poorly aligned with clinical workflows and real-world needs, undermining adoption, effectiveness, and physician buy-in.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters across all stakeholder types provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific examples from implementation experience. The discourse demonstrates sophisticated understanding of sociotechnical challenges and avoids simplistic solutions.

evidenceBase.explanation: Many commenters cite specific implementation experiences, case studies, and concrete examples of both successes and failures. Vendors provide quantitative metrics (e.g., time savings), while trade associations aggregate member experiences. Some claims about broader patterns lack systematic evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Safety-net and rural providers are mentioned as disproportionately affected by resource constraints but are underrepresented in direct comments. Patient perspectives on workflow changes are largely absent. Small independent practices have limited direct representation despite being identified as particularly challenged.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The comments reveal sophisticated understanding that AI implementation success depends on interconnected technical, organizational, cultural, and resource factors. Commenters recognize that solutions must address multiple dimensions simultaneously rather than focusing on single interventions.

9.1.themeDescription: Clinician Involvement in AI Development and Governance

sections.executiveSummary: There is remarkable consensus across all stakeholder types—from professional medical associations to AI developers to health systems—that clinicians must be meaningfully involved throughout the AI lifecycle, from initial design through deployment and ongoing monitoring. The central tension is not whether clinicians should be involved, but how to operationalize this involvement given the persistent gap between stated intentions and actual practice. Commenters overwhelmingly recommend establishing formal governance structures with clinician representation, mandating participatory design approaches, and recognizing that AI tools developed without adequate clinical input consistently fail to meet real-world needs and may introduce safety risks.

consensusPoints.text: Clinician involvement is essential for safe, effective AI. Nearly all commenters explicitly state that clinical expertise is indispensable for developing AI tools that are safe, effective, and aligned with real-world workflows. The American Medical Association states that clinical expertise is indispensable in ensuring these tools are safe, effective, and trustworthy. The Computing Research Association emphasizes that clinicians need to be key stakeholders in any AI application as they are the ones using it to impact patient health. Independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi notes that AI tools designed without adequate clinical input fail to meet clinical needs and may introduce safety risks.

exceptions.text: While all agree on the principle, several commenters note that current practice falls far short of this ideal.

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians are currently underrepresented in AI decision-making. Commenters explicitly identify a gap between the importance of clinician input and actual inclusion in AI development and governance processes. The Health IT End-Users Alliance states that end-users are often included at the end of the policymaking and technology development process, not the beginning, which leads to technologies and policies that do not meet their intended goals. AAFP reports that their survey with Rock Health found many primary care physicians reported little to no involvement in decision-making processes. The Computing Research Association notes that core users (practicing physicians, nurses, etc.) are often not sufficiently consulted on what would best support their work.

AI adoption depends on clinician buy-in. Clinician engagement is not just ethically important but practically necessary for successful AI adoption. Ellipsis Health observes that AI that lacks nursing and care management buy-in rarely moves beyond pilot phase, regardless of technical performance. AORN states that primary influencers of AI adoption are intended end-users. Massive Bio notes that clinical champions—respected physicians or nurse leaders who advocate for an AI tool—can significantly sway their peers.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters addressing implementation (12 of 18)

consensusPoints.text: Formal governance structures with clinician representation are needed. Commenters recommend establishing formal mechanisms—advisory panels, oversight committees, or governance councils—that include clinician leadership. Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association recommends that AI governance function should include clinician leadership. Independent researcher Joshi calls for cross-functional AI governance councils that should include representation from clinical, technical, compliance, and patient safety leadership. AAFP urges HHS to establish advisory panels including primary care physicians to ensure AI tools align with real-world clinical workflows and patient needs.

areasOfDebate.topic: Scope of Clinician Types to Include
areasOfDebate.description: This is less a sharp disagreement than a difference in emphasis. No commenter explicitly excludes other clinical roles; rather, professional associations naturally advocate for their members' inclusion. The emerging consensus appears to favor multidisciplinary involvement.

positions.label: Physician-Centric Focus
positions.stance: Emphasize physician leadership in AI governance. Several major medical associations including AMA, ACC, AAFP, AUA, and AADA support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Several major medical associations
keyArguments: Physicians bear ultimate clinical responsibility
Specialty expertise is essential for defining clinically meaningful endpoints
Physicians face direct liability exposure

positions.label: Broader Clinical Team
positions.stance: Include nursing, care management, and non-physician providers. Nursing associations, AI developers, health systems, and mental health organizations support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Nursing associations, AI developers, health systems, mental health organizations
keyArguments: Nurses and care managers often have outsized influence on adoption decisions
Non-licensed providers play essential roles in behavioral health
Frontline staff understand workflow realities

areasOfDebate.topic: Timing and Depth of Clinician Engagement
areasOfDebate.description: These positions are largely complementary rather than contradictory. The debate centers on implementation feasibility rather than principle.

positions.label: Early and Continuous
positions.stance: Clinicians must be involved from initial design through post-market surveillance. Health IT End-Users Alliance, AORN, and ACC are representative voices for this position.
positions.supportLevel: Strong majority (at least 12 commenters)
keyArguments: Late-stage inclusion leads to poor workflow fit
Participatory design from the beginning prevents costly redesigns
Ongoing monitoring requires clinical judgment

positions.label: Pragmatic Checkpoints
positions.stance: Advisory panels and feedback channels at key decision points. Some AI developers and consulting firms support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Some AI developers and consulting firms
keyArguments: Continuous engagement may be impractical
Structured feedback mechanisms can capture clinical input efficiently
Formalized review processes ensure accountability

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether governance should prioritize active clinicians or balance multiple disciplines.

positions.label: Active Clinicians Essential
positions.stance: Governance must include clinicians with substantial ongoing clinical practice. Academic medical centers and some professional associations support this view.
positions.supportLevel: Academic medical centers, some professional associations
keyArguments: Clinicians removed from active practice lose touch with workflow realities
IT or executive-dominated governance creates blind spots

positions.label: Multidisciplinary Balance
positions.stance: Include clinical, technical, compliance, and patient safety leadership together. Researchers, consulting firms, and some health systems support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Researchers, consulting firms, some health systems
keyArguments: AI governance requires diverse expertise
Technical and regulatory knowledge is also essential
Balanced representation prevents any single perspective from dominating

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Physicians and nurses being excluded from AI decisions that affect their practice; liability exposure when using AI tools they had no role in designing; AI tools misaligned with clinical workflows and specialty-specific needs. Organizations include AMA, AAFP, ACC, AUA, AADA, AORN, and National Council for Mental Wellbeing.
specificPoints: Specialty societies often carry the most influence on adopting medical technologies and should be formally included in defining clinically meaningful endpoints
Survey data shows physicians report little to no involvement in decision-making processes, highlighting the gap between stated intentions and actual practice
Propose establishing advisory panels with practicing physicians across specialties
Recommend embedding clinicians throughout the AI lifecycle—design, validation, implementation, and post-market surveillance
AAFP's Primary Care Innovation Network serves as a model for physician-led mechanism connecting frontline primary care teams with AI and digital health solution developers

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Building clinician trust is essential for product adoption and sustainability; clinician skepticism about AI overriding their judgment; need for regulatory clarity on clinician involvement requirements. Organizations include Massive Bio, Ellipsis Health, Interstella, ICF, and SANCIAN LLC.
specificPoints: Nursing leaders, care management directors, and population health executives exert outsized influence on whether AI-enabled care tools are adopted and sustained
Building trust took training and showing AI was there to augment, not replace, expertise
Propose formalizing user feedback channels and oversight committees
Support human-centered design approaches including direct observation and listening sessions
Interstella's behavioral health use case demonstrates how clinician skepticism was overcome through training and demonstration

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation challenges when AI tools don't fit clinical workflows; need for clinical champions to drive successful adoption; governance structures that may be dominated by IT or executive leadership. Organizations include WellSpan Health and Massachusetts Health & Hospital Association.
specificPoints: Adoption hinges on clinical and implementation champions with demonstrated organizational POCs
Comments co-signed by Chief Medical Informatics Officers, lending clinical credibility to governance recommendations
AI governance function should include clinician leadership
Support clinical AI credentialing programs to train healthcare professionals to evaluate, implement, and oversee AI systems

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Persistent gap between developers and diversity of real-world clinical users; governance structures dominated by non-clinical leadership becoming disconnected from frontline realities; need for participatory design from the very beginning. Organizations include Computing Research Association, University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT, and independent researcher Satyadhar Joshi.
specificPoints: Significant variability exists among users compared to other industries with blind spots regarding patient diversity between specialties and variability of real-world clinical workflows
Active clinician champions who maintain substantial clinical effort are essential to balanced governance
Recommend Human-AI Collaboration as a priority research area
Call for cross-functional governance councils with worker representation
Emphasize robust discussions with direct users and stakeholders from the very beginning

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: End-users included too late in development processes; technologies and policies that don't meet intended goals due to insufficient input; need for clear expectations on training, competency, and oversight. Organizations include HIMSS EHR Association and Health IT End-Users Alliance.
specificPoints: End-users are at the forefront of AI use and well-equipped to collaborate on common principles
Recommend bringing developers, policymakers, and end-users together from project outset
Organizations need policies clarifying training, competency, and oversight expectations

noteworthyInsights.insight: Ellipsis Health provides a striking insight that AI that lacks nursing and care management buy-in rarely moves beyond pilot phase, regardless of technical performance—highlighting that technical excellence is insufficient without clinical engagement.
Massive Bio identifies the Chief Medical Informatics Officer as critical in evaluating AI tools and persuading other clinicians, suggesting a key leverage point for governance structures.

AUA notes that professional medical specialty societies, especially trade associations, often carry the most influence on adopting medical technologies—an insight that could inform HHS engagement strategies.

University of Pennsylvania NeuroVERT makes the nuanced point that governance requires active clinician champions who maintain substantial clinical effort—not just clinicians by title, but those currently practicing.

ICF emphasizes that human-centered design approaches can uncover tacit knowledge, context-specific decision cues, early signs of misunderstanding or misuse—knowledge that surveys and formal feedback mechanisms may miss.

AAFP's Primary Care Innovation Network represents a concrete example of creating sustainable, physician-led mechanism connecting frontline primary care teams with AI and digital health solution developers.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Specialty-Specific Concerns: Different clinical specialties emphasize different aspects of clinician involvement. Perioperative nurses (AORN) focus on workflow integration in surgical settings. Dermatologists (AADA) emphasize privacy concerns given their work across diverse services. Neurosurgeons (NeuroVERT) highlight time-critical stroke workflows. Primary care physicians (AAFP) stress the unique needs of small, independent, and rural practices.

Organizational Role Patterns: CMIOs and clinical informatics leaders are identified as key bridge figures between clinical and technical domains. Nursing and care management leaders are recognized as having outsized influence on adoption decisions, yet may be underrepresented in formal governance. Thought leaders and chief scientific officers within specialty societies are identified as key influencers.

Implementation Stage Variations: Organizations with active AI deployments (WellSpan, Interstella) emphasize practical trust-building and champion development. Professional associations focus more on formal governance structures and policy requirements. Academic researchers emphasize participatory design methodology.

Unintended Consequences Identified: Late-stage clinician inclusion leads to technologies that don't meet intended goals. Governance dominated by non-clinical leadership creates disconnection from frontline realities. Advisory panels that don't capture user variability may provide false assurance of clinical input.

keyQuotations.quote: End-users are often included at the end of the policymaking and technology development process, not the beginning, which leads to technologies and policies that do not meet their intended goals.
Clinicians should be full partners in preparing for the future of AI that intersects their respective practice settings.
AI that lacks nursing and care management buy-in rarely moves beyond pilot phase, regardless of technical performance.
When governance structures are dominated by IT or executive leadership removed from active practice, decision-making risks becoming disconnected from frontline workflow realities.
Physicians must play a central role in ethical development, deployment, and utilization of AI technologies in health care.
Clinical champions—respected physicians or nurse leaders who advocate for an AI tool—can significantly sway their peers.
Absence of meaningful clinician input contributes to technologies poorly aligned with clinical workflows and real-world needs, undermining adoption, effectiveness, and physician buy-in.
Clinicians need to be key stakeholders in any AI application (especially embodied AI) to clinical care—they are the ones using it to impact patient health.
Developers may convene advisory panels but significant variability exists among users compared to other industries.
Imperative that providers of all types (licensed and non-licensed) are involved from the beginning.
In behavioral health use case, some clinicians were skeptical, worried AI might override their judgment—building trust took training and showing AI was there to augment, not replace, expertise.
Successful development, implementation, and maintenance require early and intentional interdisciplinary collaboration among technology developers and end-users.

discourseQuality.explanation: Comments demonstrate substantive engagement with specific governance mechanisms, cite concrete examples and survey data, and offer constructive recommendations rather than merely expressing opposition or support.

evidenceBase.explanation: Several commenters cite survey data (AAFP/Rock Health), organizational experience reports, and specific implementation case studies. However, some claims about clinician exclusion rely more on professional experience than systematic evidence.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient and caregiver voices are notably absent from direct commentary, though several commenters advocate for their inclusion. Small and rural practice perspectives are mentioned but underrepresented in direct submissions. Non-physician clinical roles (nursing, care management) are discussed but fewer direct submissions from these groups.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - The theme involves multiple interacting factors including governance structures, development processes, adoption dynamics, and specialty-specific considerations. Commenters recognize that solutions must address both formal mechanisms and cultural/practical barriers to meaningful clinician involvement.

9.2.themeDescription: Workforce Training and AI Literacy Requirements. This sub-theme addresses the need for training healthcare workers to effectively use and oversee AI systems. || It includes proposals for AI competency requirements, debates about what training is needed, concerns about workforce readiness gaps, and calls for integrating AI education into clinical training programs. This excludes clinician governance involvement and focuses on skills development

sections.executiveSummary: There is strong consensus across all stakeholder types that healthcare workforce AI literacy is critically deficient and represents a fundamental barrier to safe, effective AI adoption. Commenters broadly agree that training must span career stages—from academic curricula to continuing education—but debate centers on who should bear responsibility for developing and funding these programs, with tensions between calls for federal mandates versus voluntary professional society-led approaches. The dominant recommendation thrust favors multi-channel credentialing programs, federal funding through HRSA and AHRQ, and collaboration with professional societies to develop role-specific competency frameworks.

consensusPoints.text: Current workforce AI literacy is inadequate for safe AI deployment. This concern spans healthcare providers, professional associations, technology vendors, and academic researchers. SonderMind notes that clinicians are trained to practice medicine, not to evaluate how AI is designed, validated, or deployed. Scientix.ai reports that 40% of IT leaders indicate staff are unequipped for AI adoption. Interstella observes that many hospitals lack personnel with AI expertise such as data scientists and informaticists.

exceptions.text: The AMA acknowledges that expecting all physicians to deeply understand AI-enabled technologies is unreasonable, suggesting a tiered approach to competency expectations.

consensusPoints.text: AI education must be integrated across career stages, from pre-professional academic programs through continuing education for practicing clinicians. Satyadhar Joshi proposes an education framework spanning medical/nursing students learning AI fundamentals, residents/trainees developing critical evaluation and AI tool proficiency, practicing clinicians addressing ethical considerations and workflow integration, and clinical leadership focusing on governance frameworks, bias detection, and strategic planning. Ryan Kern, a Stanford pre-med student, argues that AI must become a core component of clinical training for successful and safe integration. The National Council for Mental Wellbeing emphasizes that information and education at colleges and universities while someone is becoming a provider is also important.

Training must address AI limitations, not just capabilities, including hallucinations, bias, and appropriate trust calibration. The University of Kansas Health System emphasizes that training must equip healthcare professionals to trust but verify. Ryan Kern stresses that training must include deep understanding of AI limitations including potential to hallucinate or produce convincingly incorrect answers. The University of Kansas Health System also recommends that HHS should invest in tailored education for clinicians to help them recognize AI limitations such as hallucinations or algorithmic bias.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Most commenters emphasizing training content (14 of 27)

consensusPoints.text: Professional societies should play a central role in training development. Massive Bio recommends supporting grants to medical specialty societies to create continuing education and credentialing programs on AI. Pictor Labs calls for intensive interaction between HHS and professional societies like USCAP/DPA for continued education. AAFP urges ASTP/ONC to prioritize development of AI-focused educational and training resources for the healthcare workforce, developed in collaboration with specialty societies.

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether all clinicians should achieve baseline AI competency or whether different competency levels should apply to different roles.

positions.label: Universal Proficiency
positions.stance: All clinicians should achieve baseline AI competency. AORN, Satyadhar Joshi, and Ryan Kern represent this view.
positions.supportLevel: Majority of professional associations, academic commenters, and technology vendors (approximately 15 of 27)
keyArguments: Patient safety requires all clinicians to evaluate AI outputs
AI will be ubiquitous in clinical workflows
Liability concerns demand competent oversight

positions.label: Tiered Expectations
positions.stance: Different competency levels for different roles. The AMA and Cyberhill Partners support this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 4 of 27
keyArguments: Unreasonable to expect all physicians to deeply understand AI
Developers should make information accessible to physicians of varying AI experience
Specialized roles like clinical informaticists can bridge gaps

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether HHS should develop and fund training programs directly, professional societies should lead with federal support, or AI developers should provide training.

positions.label: Federal Leadership
positions.stance: HHS should develop and fund training programs directly. AAPA, New Jersey Innovation Institute, and Interstella support this position.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 10 of 27 commenters, particularly technology vendors and some healthcare providers
keyArguments: Ensures consistency across settings
Addresses resource disparities
Can leverage existing federal programs like HRSA and AHRQ

positions.label: Professional Society Leadership
positions.stance: Specialty societies should lead with federal support. Massive Bio, AAFP, and Pictor Labs advocate for this approach.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8 of 27 commenters, particularly professional associations
keyArguments: Societies have domain expertise
Existing CME infrastructure can be leveraged
Profession-specific needs require tailored approaches

positions.label: Vendor Responsibility
positions.stance: AI developers should provide accessible training. The AMA and EHRA support this view.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 3 of 27
keyArguments: Developers best understand their tools
Reduces burden on clinicians
Ensures training matches product capabilities

areasOfDebate.description: Debate over whether AI competency should be required for practice/accreditation or encouraged through voluntary and incentivized approaches.

positions.label: Mandatory Requirements
positions.stance: Require AI competency for practice/accreditation. AORN argues that competency should be assessed before using any AI-enabled technology. New Jersey Innovation Institute and Pictor Labs also support mandatory approaches, with Pictor Labs recommending inclusion of modern technology use for hospital accreditation.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 8 of 27 commenters
keyArguments: Ensures universal adoption
Patient safety imperative
Creates accountability

positions.label: Voluntary Approaches
positions.stance: Encourage through funding, recognition, and support. AAFP, Epic, and Interstella favor this approach, with Interstella suggesting that ONC workforce programs could highlight certified hospitals as leaders.
positions.supportLevel: Approximately 12 of 27 commenters
keyArguments: Avoids adding regulatory burden
Respects practice autonomy
Allows flexibility for different settings

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Workforce readiness gaps limiting safe adoption; need for profession-specific training; administrative burden of implementation. They emphasize leveraging existing CME and accreditation infrastructure and highlight that different specialties have distinct AI use cases requiring tailored education.
specificPoints: Federal grants to specialty societies for training development
Integration with existing credentialing systems
Collaboration between HHS and professional organizations
AAFP calls for concise and actionable resources for small practices
ADA notes that extensive training and workforce development challenges are amplified by workforce shortage

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Training barriers slowing AI adoption and market growth; lack of specialized talent in healthcare organizations; need for AI fluency among non-technical stakeholders. They emphasize infrastructure and governance knowledge gaps beyond clinical AI use and highlight need for new roles like AI operations manager and clinical informaticists.
specificPoints: Federal funding for role-based training
Support for internal expertise development to reduce vendor dependence
Peer-to-peer learning networks
Scientix.ai notes that leadership frequently underestimates need for specialized roles such as information architects and data governance leads
Epic calls for neutral, trusted education tailored to different stakeholder groups

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Practical implementation challenges; risk of clinical skill deterioration; need for trust but verify capabilities. They bring direct operational experience with AI deployment and concern about balancing training investment with clinical demands.
specificPoints: Clinical AI credentialing programs
Education and implementation supports
Research on human-AI teaming
University of Kansas Health System raises concern about tangible risk of skill deterioration
WellSpan calls for credentialing programs essential for safe deployment, informed adoption decisions, and long-term governance

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Comprehensive competency frameworks; career-stage-appropriate education; ethical reasoning alongside technical skills. They take a systematic approach to curriculum development with emphasis on simulation environments and interdisciplinary programs.
specificPoints: Detailed competency frameworks spanning technical literacy, critical evaluation, ethical awareness, workflow integration, patient communication, and continuous learning
Satyadhar Joshi proposes comprehensive education framework across career stages
Renee Pope calls for role-based credentialing for facility staff responsible for AI supervision and monitoring

stakeholderPerspectives.primaryConcerns: Preparing next generation of physicians for AI-integrated practice; training to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool. Brings patient and future practitioner viewpoint with emphasis on prompt engineering and critical parsing skills.
specificPoints: Integration of AI education into clinical training
Training on limitations and error reduction
Ryan Kern offers pragmatic framing that the goal should not be to wait for completely error-free AI but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool

noteworthyInsights.insight: A pre-medical student offers a pragmatic framing that the goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool—a perspective that may resonate more with policymakers than calls for perfect AI.

MediView XR highlights that Mixed Reality environments allow clinicians to understand spatial relationships rather than memorizing steps, accelerating skill acquisition and improving retention—suggesting AI infrastructure itself can address training challenges.

AAFP uniquely identifies that limited AI literacy among both end-users and purchasers creates hesitancy, highlighting that training needs extend beyond clinical users to those making procurement decisions.

Scientix.ai notes that leadership frequently underestimates need for specialized roles such as information architects and data governance leads—suggesting training must address organizational and infrastructure competencies, not just clinical AI use.

National Council for Mental Wellbeing emphasizes that providers must furnish psychoeducation on tool use for patients prior to implementation—expanding the training mandate to include patient communication skills.

SonderMind, operating at scale with 100,000+ AI-generated notes, observes that very few high-quality, practical education offerings exist despite their extensive deployment—suggesting even organizations successfully using AI struggle to find adequate training resources.

emergingPatterns.pattern: Setting-type variations are emerging, with Renee Pope emphasizing need for training tailored to PALTC roles including CNA, nursing, and administration, recognizing different workforce composition and constraints. AAFP specifically requests resources for practices with limited resources to evaluate and implement AI. National Council for Mental Wellbeing notes members were excluded from HITECH Act funding, creating historical technology adoption gaps.

Role-based competency differentiation is emerging across multiple commenters, with clinical leadership needing governance frameworks, strategic planning, and bias detection; frontline clinicians needing workflow integration, output validation, and patient communication; technical staff needing data governance, model monitoring, and implementation science; and administrative staff needing procurement evaluation and vendor management.

Tension between speed and thoroughness is evident, with technology vendors emphasizing training as an adoption barrier and implicitly favoring faster, lighter-touch approaches, while professional associations and health systems emphasize comprehensive competency assessment suggesting more rigorous requirements. This tension reflects underlying debate about whether AI adoption should be accelerated or carefully gated.

Unintended consequences are being identified: Massive Bio notes that training requirements themselves may become adoption barriers; ADA observes that workforce shortages compound training challenges since fewer staff means less time for training; University of Kansas Health System warns that training to use AI may inadvertently reduce foundational clinical skills through over-reliance.

keyQuotations.quote: The goal should not be to wait for a completely error-free AI, which may never be realized, but to train clinicians to use AI as a powerful, imperfect tool.
We need more research on the 'tangible risk' that over-reliance on AI might lead to the deterioration of essential human clinical skills.
Clinicians are trained to practice medicine, not to evaluate how AI is designed, validated, or deployed.
Mixed Reality environments allow clinicians to understand spatial relationships rather than memorizing steps, accelerating skill acquisition and improving retention.
While some physicians will develop significant AI expertise, expecting all physicians to deeply understand AI-enabled technologies is unreasonable.
Family physicians want and need to know about new AI and digital health products but are overburdened by system inefficiencies with limited ability to seek out these tools.
40% of IT leaders report staff are unequipped for AI adoption.

Administrative hurdles stem from lack of understanding of 'AI' technology, conflating general purpose conversational AI with made-for-narrow-purpose, human-in-the-loop AI systems with strict guardrails.
Technology adoption depends on human understanding and trust, not just technical readiness.
Critical deficit in specialized talent capable of managing data ingestion pipelines and model monitoring results in implementation paralysis.
Competency should be assessed before using any AI-enabled technology.

Resources should be concise and actionable—point busy practice owners and technology champions to highest impact AI use cases and outline steps practices can take to assess, implement, and utilize new AI capabilities.
Training curricula could incorporate anonymized examples to teach clinicians how to interpret AI confidence scores, recognize performance drift, and exercise effective oversight.

discourseQuality.explanation: Commenters provide substantive, evidence-based arguments with specific recommendations. Multiple stakeholder types engage constructively with the topic, offering concrete proposals rather than vague concerns. The debate reflects genuine policy tensions rather than mere advocacy.

evidenceBase.explanation: Some commenters cite specific data points such as the 40% IT leader statistic from Scientix.ai, while others rely on professional experience and anecdotal observations. There is limited citation of peer-reviewed research on AI training effectiveness, though practical implementation experience is well-represented.

analyticalNotes.representationGaps: Patient and consumer perspectives are notably absent from the training discussion. Rural and safety-net provider voices are mentioned but not directly represented. International perspectives on AI workforce training approaches are not discussed.
analyticalNotes.complexityLevel: High - the topic involves multiple intersecting policy domains including education accreditation, professional licensing, federal funding mechanisms, and healthcare delivery system constraints. Commenters recognize these interdependencies and propose multi-faceted solutions.

9.3.themeDescription: Professional Autonomy and Human-in-the-Loop Requirements. This sub-theme addresses concerns about maintaining clinician judgment and authority when AI is involved in care decisions. || It includes debates about human-in-the-loop requirements, concerns about automation bias and over-reliance on AI, proposals for ensuring clinicians can override AI recommendations, and arguments that AI should augment rather than replace clinical judgment

sections.executiveSummary: Public comments reveal near-universal consensus that AI must augment rather than replace clinical judgment, with clinicians retaining final decision-making authority—a position articulated across all stakeholder types from individual practitioners to major medical associations. However, significant debate exists around how to operationalize meaningful human oversight, with tensions between those advocating for strict, prescriptive requirements and those seeking flexible, outcome-oriented approaches that can scale across different clinical contexts. The dominant recommendation thrust centers on establishing clear regulatory frameworks that define functional (not merely nominal) human oversight, require override capabilities with logging, and protect clinicians who exercise professional judgment to deviate from AI recommendations.

consensusPoints.text: AI Must Augment, Not Replace, Clinical Judgment. Nearly all commenters explicitly endorse the principle that AI should support rather than substitute for human clinical decision-making. This consensus spans healthcare providers, technology companies, professional associations, and patient advocacy groups. The Association of periOperative Registered Nurses states that AI cannot and must never be a replacement for human clinical judgement, emphasizing that human expertise, empathy and accountability required for clinical decision-making cannot be replicated. HIMSS EHR Association argues AI should be designed to augment informed clinical judgment, not substitute for licensed clinical professionals. Medvidi, Inc. affirms that clinicians retain final decision-making authority.
consensusPoints.supportLevel: Nearly all commenters (45+ of 52 substantive comments)

exceptions.text: No commenter explicitly advocated for autonomous AI clinical decision-making, though ATA Action suggested that as AI systems demonstrate reliability within defined parameters, policy should permit appropriate integrated clinical deployment—implying potential future expansion of AI autonomy.

consensusPoints.text: Clinicians Must Retain Override Authority. A strong majority of commenters emphasize that clinicians must have the ability to override AI recommendations, with many specifying this should be a functional capability rather than a nominal checkbox. AFSCME states all health care workers must be able to override AI recommendations when the individual believes it's not appropriate for the patient. Kanav Jain argues human oversight should be defined as functional override and rollback capability, not merely a nominal review step. HIMSS EHR Association emphasizes clinicians must be able to contextualize decisions or override outputs as appropriate.

exceptions.text: Several commenters note that override capability alone is insufficient without tracking and analysis of override patterns.

consensusPoints.text: Automation Bias Is a Recognized Patient Safety Risk. Most commenters addressing implementation concerns identify automation bias—the tendency to defer to AI outputs without independent verification—as a material patient safety risk requiring active mitigation. Caroline Morrow notes automation bias presents material patient safety risk as clinicians may defer to AI output without exercising independent judgment, particularly under time pressure or when the tool's reasoning is opaque. ECRI warns that LLM outputs can be very convincing even when inaccurate, creating automation bias and subtle influence even when clinicians think they're not being influenced.
